### Q1. Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional Requirements of an embedded system. Give two examples of each. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Functional Requirements (FRs):** Specify the *specific functions* or *behaviors* the system must perform.
  - *Example 1:* "The system shall sample the temperature sensor every 100 ms."
  - *Example 2:* "The system shall display the current heart rate on the OLED."

- **Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs):** Specify the *quality attributes* and *constraints*.
  - *Example 1:* "The system must consume less than 50 mW average power."
  - *Example 2:* "The system boot time must be under 2 seconds."

| Aspect | Functional | Non-Functional |
|---|---|---|
| Question answered | *What* does it do? | *How well* does it do it? |
| Testability | Direct function test | Often measurable metric |
| Examples | Read sensor, send SMS | Latency, MTBF, cost |

---

### Q2. List and briefly explain any **five** design metrics that must be captured during the Requirements phase of an embedded system. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **NRE Cost (Non-Recurring Engineering Cost):** One-time cost to design the system, including engineer time, EDA tools, and masks.
2. **Unit Cost:** Per-device manufacturing cost, which decreases with higher production volume.
3. **Performance:** Measured in MIPS, latency, or throughput — how fast the system responds.
4. **Power Consumption:** Average or peak power in mW/W, critical for battery-operated devices.
5. **Size / Form Factor:** Physical dimensions (mm³) and weight, critical for wearables and IoT nodes.

*(Optional 6th: Reliability / MTBF, Flexibility, Time-to-Market, Safety.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the complete **Requirements Engineering Process** for an embedded system. List its five sub-activities and describe the output of each. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** For a new IoT-based **air quality monitoring node**, identify and justify **six design metrics** that you would specify as non-functional requirements. Compute the **crossover volume** between an ASIC implementation (NRE = ₹8,00,000, Unit Cost = ₹80) and an FPGA implementation (NRE = ₹0, Unit Cost = ₹240). `[7 Marks]`

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Requirements Engineering Process — 5 Sub-Activities:**

1. **Elicitation** — Interview stakeholders, observe use cases, run workshops. *Output:* Raw, informal list of user needs.
2. **Specification** — Convert raw needs into the **SRS** (Software Requirements Specification) using the "shall" template, UML use cases, or DFA. *Output:* Formal, testable requirement statements.
3. **Analysis** — Detect conflicts, redundancies, and feasibility issues. Negotiate trade-offs (e.g., performance vs. cost). *Output:* Prioritized, conflict-free requirement set.
4. **Validation** — Conduct reviews, walkthroughs, and prototype demonstrations with the customer. *Output:* Customer sign-off / acceptance test cases.
5. **Management (Traceability)** — Maintain a **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** mapping each requirement → design → code → test. *Output:* Live, auditable RTM throughout project lifecycle.

**(b) Six NFRs for IoT Air Quality Node:**

| # | Metric | Justified Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Power** | Solar + battery, must run 1 year on a single charge → ≤ 5 mW average |
| 2 | **Size** | Must fit in 5 cm × 5 cm enclosure for pole mounting |
| 3 | **Cost** | Mass deployment (1000s of nodes) → unit cost ≤ ₹100 |
| 4 | **Reliability** | Outdoor, unattended → MTBF ≥ 50,000 hours |
| 5 | **Connectivity** | Must use LoRaWAN for 2 km range, latency ≤ 10 s |
| 6 | **Sampling Rate** | Air quality data every 60 s (NFR, not a function count) |

**Crossover Volume Calculation:**

$$V^* = \dfrac{NRE_1 - NRE_2}{C_2 - C_1} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000 - 0}{240 - 80}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^* &= \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{160} \\
&= 5{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{V^* = 5{,}000\ \text{units}}$$

**Interpretation:** For volumes **above 5,000**, the **ASIC** is cheaper. For volumes **below 5,000**, the **FPGA** wins. For a city-wide pilot of 2,000 nodes, choose FPGA; for full-state deployment of 50,000 nodes, choose ASIC.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **role of a Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** in embedded system design. How does it help in V&V (Verification & Validation)? `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** A battery-powered wearable health monitor operates at $V_{dd} = 1.8\ \text{V}$, $f = 40\ \text{MHz}$, effective switched capacitance $C = 8\ \text{pF}$, activity factor $\alpha = 0.25$. Compute the **average power** and the **battery life** in hours, given a 200 mAh Li-ion battery at 1.8 V. `[7 Marks]`

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) RTM Explanation:**

A **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** is a grid (typically a table) that maps every requirement ID to its corresponding design module, code unit, and test case. It is the **backbone of traceability** in any safety-critical embedded project.

**Structure of an RTM:**

| Req ID | Description | Design Module | Code File | Test Case ID | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R001 | Read temperature every 100 ms | Temp driver | `temp.c` | TC001 | Pass |
| R002 | Display on OLED | OLED driver | `oled.c` | TC002 | Pass |
| R003 | Send BLE notification | BLE stack | `ble.c` | TC003 | Fail |

**Role in V&V:**
- **Verification:** "Are we building the product *right*?" — RTM ensures every requirement has a corresponding test case. If R005 has no test case, verification is incomplete.
- **Validation:** "Are we building the *right* product?" — RTM allows customer walkthroughs to confirm every original need is satisfied.
- **Change Management:** When a requirement is added/changed, RTM instantly reveals which design, code, and test artifacts must be updated.

**(b) Power and Battery Life Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Average power equation.**

$$P_{avg} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= 0.25 \times (8 \times 10^{-12}) \times (1.8)^2 \times (40 \times 10^6) \\
&= 0.25 \times 8 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.24 \times 4 \times 10^{7} \\
&= 0.25 \times 8 \times 3.24 \times 4 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 25.92 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{W} \\
&= 2.592 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{P_{avg} \approx 0.259\ \text{mW}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute battery energy in Joules.**

$$E_{batt} = V \times I \times t = 1.8 \times (200 \times 10^{-3}) \times 3600\ \text{s}$$

$$E_{batt} = 1.8 \times 0.2 \times 3600 = 1296\ \text{J}$$

**Step 4 — Compute battery life.**

$$t_{life} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{P_{avg}} = \dfrac{1296}{2.592 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{s}$$

Convert to hours:

$$t_{life} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{6}}{3600} \approx 1388.9\ \text{hours}$$

Convert to days:

$$t_{life} = \dfrac{1388.9}{24} \approx 57.9\ \text{days}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Battery Life} \approx 1389\ \text{hours} \approx 58\ \text{days}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the activity factor $\alpha$** — students often write $P = CV^2f$ without $\alpha$, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in battery life** — battery capacity is in **mAh**, but energy is in **Joules**. Convert $mAh \times V$ correctly: $200\ mAh \times 1.8\ V = 360\ mWh = 1.296\ kWh$... wait, check: $0.2 \times 1.8 = 0.36\ \text{Wh} = 1296\ \text{J}$ ✓
> 3. **Do not round intermediate steps** — keep 4 significant figures until the final answer.
> 4. **For Part A questions**, students often confuse *Functional* with *Non-Functional*. Always answer "*What*" vs. "*How well*."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Requirements**

- ✅ **Requirement** = Statement of what the system must do (FR) and how well (NFR).
- ✅ **FR vs. NFR:** FR = function; NFR = quality attribute (power, cost, size, perf, reliability).
- ✅ **5 Stages of RE:** **Elicit → Specify → Analyze → Validate → Manage (ESAVM).**
- ✅ **SRS / SyRS** is the output of the specification stage.
- ✅ **RTM (Requirements Traceability Matrix)** is used for change management and V&V.
- ✅ **NRE Cost** = fixed one-time design cost; **Unit Cost** = per-device cost.
- ✅ **Crossover Volume** $V^* = \dfrac{NRE_1 - NRE_2}{C_2 - C_1}$.
- ✅ **Power equation:** $P = \alpha C V_{dd}^2 f$; **Energy per op:** $E = C V_{dd}^2$.
- ✅ **Reliability equation:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; **MTBF** = $1/\lambda$.
- ✅ **Performance** is the inverse of execution time: $Perf \propto 1/T_{exec}$.
- ✅ **Yield** (Poisson) = $e^{-DA}$, where $D$ = defect density, $A$ = chip area.
- ✅ **Trade-off triangle:** Performance ↔ Power ↔ Cost (you can optimize at most 2).
- ✅ **Use-Case diagrams** are the standard UML tool for visualizing FRs.
- ✅ **Real-time deadlines** are part of NFRs — they define timing constraints.

> **End of Notes — PBEVT504 — Module 1 — Requirements**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Embedded System Design**, **Specification** is the rigorous, unambiguous, and complete description of *what* an embedded system must do and *under what constraints* it must operate — formulated **before** any architectural decisions, hardware selection, or implementation begins. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PBEVT504, Module 1), specification is the foundational phase of the embedded design flow that captures the **system's requirements, behavior, interfaces, timing constraints, and performance metrics** in a machine-verifiable and human-readable form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> *"Specification is the process of precisely stating the functional, non-functional, and interface requirements of an embedded system such that the design team has a verifiable contract against which the final product is validated."*

The specification phase typically produces the following artifacts:

| Artifact | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| **Requirements Document (SRS)** | Captures stakeholder needs and high-level goals |
| **Functional Specification (FS)** | Defines WHAT the system does — inputs, outputs, behavior |
| **Design Specification (DS)** | Defines HOW the system will be built — architecture, components |
| **Interface Specification (IS)** | Defines the interaction contract between modules and the external world |
| **Performance/Timing Specification** | Defines deadlines, throughput, power, memory budgets |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architectural Blueprint" Intuition

Think of specification like the **architectural blueprint of a house** before construction begins:

- A **client** says: *"I want a 3-bedroom home with solar power, under ₹50 lakh, completed in 12 months."*
- An **architect** converts this wish-list into a **formal blueprint** showing: room dimensions, plumbing routes, electrical wiring, ventilation, structural load limits, and material specifications.
- The **builder** (embedded system designer) cannot lay a single brick (write a single line of code) until the blueprint is approved.

In embedded systems, the **client** is the *customer/stakeholder*, the **architect** is the *system engineer doing specification*, and the **builder** is the *hardware/software implementation team*. If the blueprint (specification) is vague, the house (embedded product) will collapse, run over budget, or miss deadlines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Metrics You Must Memorize:**
> - A good specification is **Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked (by importance), Verifiable, Modifiable, and Traceable** — collectively known as the **8 quality attributes of a good specification** (IEEE 830 / KTU 2024 Module 1).

## 1.3 Types of Specifications in Embedded Systems

A KTU 2024 examiner expects you to classify specifications into the following hierarchy:

1. **User-Level Specification** — A high-level, natural-language description of the system's purpose (e.g., *"An anti-lock Braking System (ABS) for a car must prevent wheel lockup during hard braking"*).
2. **System-Level Specification** — A black-box description of the system's behavior in terms of inputs, outputs, and reactions to events, *without* exposing internal structure.
3. **Device-Level Specification** — A detailed description of the internal modules (CPU, memory, sensors, actuators) and their interconnections.
4. **Formal Specification** — A mathematically precise description using formal languages (e.g., **Statecharts, Z-notation, VDM, Process Algebras**) that allows **proof of correctness** and **automated verification**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** If a question asks *"List the types of specifications"*, the **3-tier classification** (User → System → Device) plus **Formal vs. Informal** is the highest-marks answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 4-Phase Specification Process (Step-by-Step Logic)

The KTU 2024 syllabus prescribes a structured **4-phase specification process** for embedded systems. Each phase has a clear input, transformation, and output:

### Phase 1 — Requirements Elicitation
- **Input:** Stakeholder interviews, market research, regulatory standards.
- **Transformation:** Translate informal needs into structured **User Requirements (UR)**.
- **Output:** *Natural-language requirements list, prioritized by MoSCoW (Must/Should/Could/Won't).*

### Phase 2 — Functional Decomposition
- **Input:** Approved user requirements.
- **Transformation:** Break the system into **use cases**, **scenarios**, and **external interactions** (no design yet).
- **Output:** **Use-Case Diagram**, **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)**, and **Context Diagram**.

### Phase 3 — Behavioral & Temporal Modeling
- **Input:** Use cases from Phase 2.
- **Transformation:** Model *how* the system reacts to events over time using state machines, sequence diagrams, and timing diagrams.
- **Output:** **Statechart / FSM diagram**, **Sequence Diagram**, **Timing Constraints Table**.

### Phase 4 — Verification & Validation of Specification
- **Input:** All artifacts from Phases 1–3.
- **Transformation:** Apply **reviews, walkthroughs, simulation, and formal model checking** to confirm correctness, completeness, and consistency.
- **Output:** **Validated Specification Document** signed off as the "design contract."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Each Phase Matters:**
> Skipping Phase 1 leads to building the *wrong* product. Skipping Phase 2 leads to a monolithic, untestable design. Skipping Phase 3 leads to missed deadlines (the #1 cause of embedded system failure). Skipping Phase 4 leads to defects propagating into hardware, which are **10×–100× costlier to fix** than software defects.

## 2.2 Formal vs. Informal Specification — A Comparative Study

| Dimension | Informal Specification | Semi-Formal Specification | Formal Specification |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| **Notation** | Natural language, bullet points | UML, Statecharts, SysML | Z, VDM, CSP, LTL, CTL |
| **Precision** | Low (ambiguity present) | Medium (graphical rigor) | High (mathematical rigor) |
| **Verifiability** | Manual review only | Model simulation | Model checking, theorem proving |
| **Industry Use** | Small teams, prototypes | Most KTU/industry projects | Safety-critical (aerospace, medical, automotive ISO 26262) |
| **Tool Support** | None | MATLAB/Simulink, Rhapsody, Enterprise Architect | SPIN, NuSMV, Isabelle, Coq |
| **Learning Curve** | Trivial | Moderate | Steep |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since Specification is largely a *conceptual and design* topic, the "formulas" below are **notational frameworks, metrics, and structural rules** that KTU examiners expect you to write down.

### A. The 8 Quality Attributes of a Good Specification (IEEE 830)

| # | Attribute | Definition (write verbatim in exams) |
|---|-----------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Correct** | Every stated requirement accurately represents a stakeholder need |
| 2 | **Unambiguous** | Each requirement has exactly one interpretation |
| 3 | **Complete** | All possible inputs, states, and responses are covered |
| 4 | **Consistent** | No requirement conflicts with another |
| 5 | **Ranked** | Each requirement has a priority tag (MoSCoW) |
| 6 | **Verifiable** | A test can be designed to check each requirement |
| 7 | **Modifiable** | Changes can be made while preserving structure |
| 8 | **Traceable** | Every requirement maps to its source and to design/test artifacts |

### B. The MoSCoW Prioritization Rule

$$
P(r) = \begin{cases} \text{MUST} & \text{if } r \in R_{\text{critical}} \text{ (system fails without it)} \\ \text{SHOULD} & \text{if } r \in R_{\text{important}} \text{ (high value but workaround exists)} \\ \text{COULD} & \text{if } r \in R_{\text{desirable}} \text{ (only if time/resources permit)} \\ \text{WON'T (this release)} & \text{if } r \text{ is explicitly deferred} \end{cases}
$$

### C. Specification Completeness Metric (Examiner Favorite)

A **traceability matrix** requirement coverage is given by:

$$
C_{\text{spec}} = \frac{\vert R_{\text{covered}} \vert}{\vert R_{\text{total}} \vert} \times 100\%
$$

where $\vert R_{\text{covered}} \vert$ is the count of requirements with at least one linked design element, and $\vert R_{\text{total}} \vert$ is the total number of requirements. KTU expects $C_{\text{spec}} \geq 95\%$ before design sign-off.

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not use the pipe `|` character inside markdown table cells.** This rule (Anti-Breakout Rule) is critical. Always write `\vert` in LaTeX.

### D. Hard Real-Time Deadline Constraint

For a real-time task $\tau_i$ with worst-case execution time $C_i$, period $T_i$, and deadline $D_i$:

$$
\forall i : \quad D_i \leq T_i \quad \wedge \quad C_i \leq D_i
$$

This is the **schedulability constraint** that *must* appear in the specification of any hard real-time embedded system.

### E. Power Specification (Embedded)

Average power dissipation is constrained as:

$$
P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T V(t) \cdot I(t)\, dt \;\leq\; P_{\text{budget}}
$$

A specification must declare $P_{\text{budget}}$ (e.g., **$P_{\text{budget}} = 500\,\text{mW}$** for a wearable).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Specification is **not academic filler** — it is the single highest-ROI activity in embedded design:

- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** A specification error in a brake-by-wire ECU can lead to fatalities. Formal specification is mandatory.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304):** Insulin pump specifications must be unambiguous, complete, and traceable — *your grade depends on this in the exam*.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C):** Avionics specification must be at Design Assurance Level (DAL) A — formal methods required.
- **IoT/Consumer:** Even a smartwatch spec must declare sampling rate, latency, power budget, and memory footprint *before* hardware is chosen.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation: From User Need to Formal Specification

Below is the **complete, examiner-grade derivation** of how a vague user need is progressively refined into a formal, verifiable specification. **Every step is shown explicitly — no skipping.**

**Step 0 — Stakeholder Statement (Informal Need):**
*"We need a fire alarm system for our office building that detects smoke quickly and alerts occupants."*

**Step 1 — Extract User Requirements (UR):**

List each need as an independent, testable sentence.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UR}_1 &: \text{ The system shall detect smoke within 5 seconds of occurrence.} \\
\text{UR}_2 &: \text{ The system shall sound an audible alarm at } \geq 85\,\text{dB}. \\
\text{UR}_3 &: \text{ The system shall send an SMS to the building manager.} \\
\text{UR}_4 &: \text{ The system shall operate on battery for } \geq 24 \text{ hours during power failure.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Functional Specification (FS):**

Each UR is rewritten using the *shall* syntax with measurable values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FS}_1 &: \text{ IF } (\text{smoke\_sensor} > T_{\text{threshold}}) \text{ THEN } \text{trigger alarm within } 5\,\text{s} \\
\text{FS}_2 &: \text{ ALARM\_OUTPUT\_VOLUME } \geq 85\,\text{dB} \text{ at } 1\,\text{m distance} \\
\text{FS}_3 &: \text{ GSM\_MODULE.send(SMS, "FIRE\_ALARM", manager\_number)} \\
\text{FS}_4 &: \text{ BATTERY\_CAPACITY } \geq I_{\text{avg}} \times 24\,\text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add Non-Functional (Quality) Specifications:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NF}_1 &: \text{ Reliability (MTBF)} \geq 50{,}000 \text{ hours} \\
\text{NF}_2 &: \text{ Operating temperature} : -10^{\circ}\text{C to } +60^{\circ}\text{C} \\
\text{NF}_3 &: \text{ Power consumption (standby)} \leq 50\,\text{mW} \\
\text{NF}_4 &: \text{ Response time} \leq 5\,\text{s} \text{ (hard real-time constraint)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Define Interface Specification (IS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IS}_1 &: \text{ Sensor Input: Analog voltage } 0\text{–}5\,\text{V from MQ-2 sensor} \\
\text{IS}_2 &: \text{ Actuator Output: GPIO pin controlling piezoelectric buzzer} \\
\text{IS}_3 &: \text{ Communication: UART @ 9600 baud to GSM module} \\
\text{IS}_4 &: \text{ User Interface: LED indicators (Green=OK, Red=ALARM)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Behavioral Specification using FSM:**

Define the system states and transitions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{States} &: S = \{S_{\text{IDLE}},\; S_{\text{MONITOR}},\; S_{\text{ALARM}},\; S_{\text{BATTERY\_LOW}}\} \\
\text{Initial State} &: S_0 = S_{\text{IDLE}} \\
\text{Transition } \delta &: S_{\text{MONITOR}} \xrightarrow{\text{smoke} > T} S_{\text{ALARM}} \\
\text{Transition } \delta &: S_{\text{ALARM}} \xrightarrow{\text{reset button}} S_{\text{MONITOR}} \\
\text{Transition } \delta &: S_{\text{MONITOR}} \xrightarrow{V_{\text{bat}} < 3.3\,\text{V}} S_{\text{BATTERY\_LOW}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification of the Specification:**

Apply the **8 quality attributes** checklist:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Correct?} && \text{YES — meets all stakeholder needs.} \\
&\text{Unambiguous?} && \text{YES — every clause has measurable thresholds.} \\
&\text{Complete?} && \text{YES — sensor, alarm, comms, power, UI all covered.} \\
&\text{Consistent?} && \text{YES — no conflicting deadlines or thresholds.} \\
&\text{Ranked?} && \text{UR}_1, \text{UR}_2, \text{UR}_3 = \text{MUST; UR}_4 = \text{SHOULD.} \\
&\text{Verifiable?} && \text{YES — every clause can be tested (e.g., dB meter, stopwatch).} \\
&\text{Modifiable?} && \text{YES — modular structure allows change without cascade.} \\
&\text{Traceable?} && \text{YES — each FS maps back to a UR and forward to a test case.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Specification Completeness:**

Suppose $R_{\text{total}} = 12$ requirements, $R_{\text{covered}} = 12$:

$$
C_{\text{spec}} = \frac{\vert 12 \vert}{\vert 12 \vert} \times 100\% = 100\% \;\;\geq\; 95\% \quad \therefore \text{ Specification APPROVED.}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Specification Method Selection

Use the matrix below to **justify** which specification method to use for a given project (a common 7-mark exam question).

| Project Class | Example | Recommended Method | Justification |
|---------------|---------|--------------------|---------------|
| Toy / Hobby | LED blinker | Informal (natural language) | Low risk, single developer, throwaway code |
| Consumer IoT | Smart bulb | Semi-formal (UML Statechart) | Multiple states, moderate cost if buggy |
| Industrial Control | PLC for assembly line | Semi-formal + structured text | IEC 61131-3 mandated |
| Automotive (non-safety) | Infotainment | Semi-formal (SysML) | Complex UI, multiple subsystems |
| Automotive (safety, ASIL-D) | Brake-by-wire ECU | **Formal (Z / SCADE / Event-B)** | Lives depend on correctness |
| Medical (Class III) | Insulin pump | **Formal + Traceability matrix** | FDA / IEC 62304 requires it |
| Aerospace (DAL-A) | Flight control | **Formal + theorem proving** | DO-178C Level A compliance |

## 3.3 Derived Heuristic: When to Choose Formal Specification

$$
\text{Choose Formal Specification} \iff (\text{Safety Critical} \;\lor\; \text{Regulatory Mandated} \;\lor\; \text{Cost of Bug} > 10^6 \text{ USD})
$$

$$
\text{Choose Semi-Formal Specification} \iff (\text{Complex} \;\land\; \text{Team Size} > 5 \;\land\; \text{Market-driven})
$$

$$
\text{Choose Informal Specification} \iff (\text{Prototype} \;\lor\; \text{Team Size} \leq 2 \;\lor\; \text{Lifecycle} < 6 \text{ months})
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Specification Phase within the Embedded Design V-Model

This diagram shows **where Specification sits** in the standard embedded system design flow (left-to-right V-Model). It is a high-frequency KTU question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Stakeholder Needs] --> B[User Requirements UR]
    B --> C[Functional Specification FS]
    B --> D[Non-Functional Specification NFR]
    C --> E[Interface Specification IS]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Behavioral Specification FSM]
    F --> G[Validation of Spec]
    G --> H{Spec Approved}
    H -- Yes --> I[Architecture Design]
    H -- No --> B
    I --> J[Hardware Design]
    I --> K[Software Design]
    J --> L[Integration]
    K --> L
    L --> M[System Testing]
    M --> N[Deployment]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — The 4-Phase Specification Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Requirements Elicitation] -->|MoSCoW prioritized UR| P2[Phase 2: Functional Decomposition]
    P2 -->|Use Cases, DFD, Context Diagram| P3[Phase 3: Behavioral and Temporal Modeling]
    P3 -->|Statechart, Sequence, Timing| P4[Phase 4: Verification and Validation]
    P4 -->|Reviews, Model Checking| P5[Validated Specification Document]
    P4 -.->|Defects Found| P1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Machine — Generic Embedded System Specification States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> MONITOR : Power ON
    MONITOR --> ALARM : sensor > threshold AND deadline met
    MONITOR --> BATLOW : battery < 3.3V
    ALARM --> MONITOR : user reset AND acknowledge
    BATLOW --> SAFE : battery charged AND system test passed
    SAFE --> MONITOR : operator clears
    ALARM --> [*] : system shutdown
    BATLOW --> [*] : system shutdown
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Specification Artifacts & Their Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UR[User Requirements] --> FS[Functional Specification]
    UR --> NFR[Non-Functional Specification]
    FS --> IS[Interface Specification]
    NFR --> IS
    FS --> BS[Behavioral Specification]
    IS --> BS
    BS --> TM[Traceability Matrix]
    UR --> TM
    FS --> TM
    NFR --> TM
    IS --> TM
    TM --> VAL[Validation Report]
    VAL --> APPROVED{Spec Approved}
```

## 4.5 Explanation of Each Block

- **Block A (Stakeholder Needs):** Raw input — interviews, market studies.
- **Block B (User Requirements):** First formal artifact — written in plain English with priority tags.
- **Block C (Functional Spec):** Describes inputs, outputs, and behavior *without* architecture.
- **Block D (Non-Functional Spec):** Captures performance, power, reliability, safety metrics.
- **Block E (Interface Spec):** Defines the boundary contracts (sensors, actuators, buses, APIs).
- **Block F (Behavioral Spec):** Uses FSM / Statecharts to model dynamic behavior.
- **Block G (Validation):** Reviews + model checking + simulation.
- **Block H (Decision):** Approval gate before architecture design begins.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Q: Define the term "Specification" in the context of embedded system design. List the eight quality attributes of a good specification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Specification is a precise, complete, and unambiguous description of *what* an embedded system must do and *under what constraints* it must operate, formulated before any design or implementation begins. It serves as the contract between the customer and the design team.

> **Eight Quality Attributes (1.5 marks):** A good specification must be Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked (by importance), Verifiable, Modifiable, and Traceable — as per IEEE 830 standard adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam]`

**Q: Differentiate between formal and informal specification methods. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Informal Specification (1.5 marks):** Uses natural language and/or simple diagrams; easy to write, but ambiguous and not machine-verifiable. *Example:* A plain English description of an anti-lock Braking System (ABS) — *"The system should apply brakes to prevent wheel lock."*

> **Formal Specification (1.5 marks):** Uses mathematically precise notations such as Z-notation, VDM, Statecharts, or temporal logic; supports automated model checking and proof of correctness. *Example:* A Statechart for ABS specifying all states, transitions, guards, and actions in a graphical, executable form.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**Q: Specification of Embedded Systems**
(a) Explain in detail the different types of specifications used in embedded system design. (7 marks)
(b) Describe the 4-phase specification process with a suitable example. (7 marks)

---

**Model Answer for (a) — Types of Specifications (7 marks):**

The following types of specifications are used in embedded system design, classified by *level of abstraction* and *degree of formalism*:

1. **User-Level Specification (1 mark):** A high-level, natural-language statement of the system's purpose from the end-user's perspective. *Example:* *"A wearable heart-rate monitor shall display BPM in real time."*

2. **System-Level Specification (1 mark):** A black-box description of the system's behavior in terms of inputs, outputs, and reactions to events, without exposing internal structure. *Example:* *"When the input BPM > 120, the output shall be a yellow LED alert."*

3. **Device-Level Specification (1 mark):** A detailed description of the internal modules (microcontroller, memory, sensors, actuators, buses) and their interconnections. *Example:* *"STM32F4 MCU connected to MAX30102 sensor via I2C at 400 kHz."*

4. **Informal Specification (1 mark):** Uses natural language, bullet points, and simple diagrams; suited for small teams and prototypes.

5. **Semi-Formal Specification (1 mark):** Uses UML, SysML, or Statecharts; provides graphical rigor and is widely used in industry.

6. **Formal Specification (1 mark):** Uses mathematical notations (Z, VDM, CSP, LTL); enables automated model checking. Used in safety-critical domains (aerospace, medical).

7. **Interface Specification (0.5 mark):** Defines the contracts at module boundaries (e.g., APIs, bus protocols, GPIO mappings).

8. **Performance / Timing Specification (0.5 mark):** States deadlines, throughput, jitter, latency, power, and memory budgets as numerical constraints.

---

**Model Answer for (b) — 4-Phase Specification Process (7 marks):**

The 4-phase specification process, as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus, is as follows:

**Phase 1 — Requirements Elicitation (2 marks):** Stakeholder needs are gathered through interviews, market research, and regulatory standards, and translated into structured User Requirements (UR). Each UR is prioritized using the **MoSCoW** method (Must, Should, Could, Won't).

**Phase 2 — Functional Decomposition (2 marks):** The system is decomposed into use cases, scenarios, and external interactions. The outputs are **Use-Case Diagrams**, **Data Flow Diagrams (DFD)**, and **Context Diagrams** — all of which describe WHAT the system does, not HOW.

**Phase 3 — Behavioral and Temporal Modeling (2 marks):** The dynamic behavior of the system is modeled using **Statecharts / FSMs**, **Sequence Diagrams**, and **Timing Diagrams**. This phase defines deadlines, response times, and event-driven transitions.

**Phase 4 — Verification and Validation of Specification (1 mark):** The specification is reviewed, simulated, and (if formal) model-checked. The output is a **Validated Specification Document** signed off as the design contract, after which architecture design can begin.

> **Example (Fire Alarm System, optional addition):** UR₁ = detect smoke within 5s; UR₂ = sound alarm ≥ 85 dB; UR₃ = SMS to manager; Phase 2 yields a use-case "Trigger Alarm"; Phase 3 yields a state machine with states IDLE → MONITOR → ALARM; Phase 4 validates against the 8 quality attributes and computes $C_{\text{spec}} = 100\%$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Formal Methods and Specification Quality**
(a) Discuss the 8 quality attributes of a good specification with examples. (7 marks)
(b) With a neat diagram, explain the role of specification in the embedded system design flow (V-Model). (7 marks)

---

**Model Answer for (a) — 8 Quality Attributes with Examples (7 marks):**

| # | Attribute | Example (Smart Thermostat) |
|---|-----------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Correct** | The requirement "Display current temperature" matches the user's actual need. |
| 2 | **Unambiguous** | "Display temperature within ±0.5 °C" — not "display temperature accurately." |
| 3 | **Complete** | All modes (Heating, Cooling, OFF, Sleep) are defined; no missing states. |
| 4 | **Consistent** | "Auto-mode operates between 18–25 °C" does not conflict with "Heating activates below 20 °C." |
| 5 | **Ranked** | Auto-mode = MUST; Wi-Fi connectivity = SHOULD; Voice control = COULD. |
| 6 | **Verifiable** | "Response time < 2 s" — verifiable with a stopwatch. |
| 7 | **Modifiable** | Changing the temperature range from [18,25] to [20,24] requires editing only one section. |
| 8 | **Traceable** | Each requirement has an ID (REQ-001) traceable to design (DES-001) and test (TST-001). |

(Marks: 0.75 per attribute × 8 = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for the table format or any one real-world example.)

---

**Model Answer for (b) — Role of Specification in the V-Model (7 marks):**

The **V-Model** is the standard embedded system design flow where each development phase on the left side has a corresponding testing phase on the right side, and **Specification sits at the very top of the V**.

**Phases of the V-Model (1 mark per phase = 6 marks; introduction = 1 mark):**

1. **Requirements Specification (top-left of V):** Stakeholder needs → User Requirements Document.
2. **System / Functional Specification:** Defines inputs, outputs, and behavior.
3. **Architecture Specification:** High-level block diagram of hardware/software partitioning.
4. **Detailed Design Specification:** Module-level design.
5. **Implementation (bottom of V):** Coding and hardware fabrication.
6. **Unit Testing (right side, bottom-up):** Tests individual modules.
7. **Integration Testing:** Tests module interactions.
8. **System Testing (right side, top):** Validates the complete system against the **Requirements Specification**.
9. **Acceptance Testing (top-right):** Validates against user needs.

> **Key Point (carry 1 mark):** The Specification is the **single source of truth** — every test on the right side of the V must trace back to a requirement on the left side via the **Traceability Matrix**. Without a validated specification, the V-Model collapses.

**Suggested ASCII V-Model Diagram (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Requirements Spec} \longleftrightarrow \text{Acceptance Test} \\
&\qquad \swarrow \qquad \qquad \qquad \nwarrow \\
&\text{System Spec} \longleftrightarrow \text{System Test} \\
&\qquad \swarrow \qquad \qquad \qquad \nwarrow \\
&\text{Architecture Spec} \longleftrightarrow \text{Integration Test} \\
&\qquad \swarrow \qquad \qquad \qquad \nwarrow \\
&\text{Detailed Design} \longleftrightarrow \text{Unit Test} \\
&\qquad \qquad \downarrow \qquad \\
&\qquad \text{Implementation}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Specification questions:**
> 1. **Conflating Specification with Design.** Specification = *what*; Design = *how*. Writing "we will use an ARM Cortex-M4" in a specification question = **−2 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Listing only 3–4 quality attributes** instead of all 8. Examiners award marks per attribute; missing 4 = losing 50% of the marks on that question.
> 3. **Skipping the example.** A question worth 7 marks *expects* a worked example (e.g., ABS, fire alarm, pacemaker). No example = **−2 to −3 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting MoSCoW.** "Ranked" is a quality attribute, and MoSCoW is the standard method. Omitting it = **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Writing informal text in a formal-spec question.** If the question says "formal specification," do NOT write English paragraphs — use Statechart / Z-notation / mathematical predicates.
> 6. **Not drawing the V-Model.** A 7-mark question that says "with a neat diagram" gives **1 mark** for the diagram itself. Skipping it is a free-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorize before the exam):**

- ✅ Specification = *what* the system does; **NOT** how it does it.
- ✅ The 8 quality attributes: **Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable.**
- ✅ MoSCoW prioritization: **Must, Should, Could, Won't** (this release).
- ✅ 3-tier classification: **User-Level → System-Level → Device-Level.**
- ✅ 3 formalism levels: **Informal → Semi-Formal → Formal.**
- ✅ 4-phase process: **Elicitation → Decomposition → Behavioral Modeling → Verification.**
- ✅ The **Traceability Matrix** links UR → FS → Design → Test.
- ✅ Completeness metric: $C_{\text{spec}} = \frac{\vert R_{\text{covered}} \vert}{\vert R_{\text{total}} \vert} \times 100\% \geq 95\%$.
- ✅ Hard real-time constraint: $C_i \leq D_i \leq T_i$.
- ✅ Use **Statecharts / FSMs** for behavioral specification.
- ✅ Use **DFDs** and **Use-Case diagrams** for functional decomposition.
- ✅ Specification sits at the **top of the V-Model** and drives all subsequent testing.
- ✅ Formal specification is **mandatory** for safety-critical systems (ISO 26262, IEC 62304, DO-178C).
- ✅ A specification error caught at design time costs **10× less** than one caught after deployment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Architecture Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Architecture Design in Embedded Systems?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Architecture Design** of an embedded system is the systematic process of defining the *abstract structural organization* of the system — its processors, memory, I/O devices, communication buses, and hardware/software partitioning — *before* the detailed hardware logic and software code are developed. It is the **bridge between specifications and implementation**, capturing the "what" and "how" of the system's hardware and software interaction at a high level of abstraction.

In simpler words: when you design a house, you don't start by laying bricks. You first draw the **floor plan** — which rooms exist, where the kitchen is, how the wiring flows. **Architecture design is the "floor plan" of an embedded system.** It defines the major building blocks, how they connect, and what each block does.

## 1.2 Why Architecture Design Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> - KTU frequently asks comparison questions (Von Neumann vs Harvard, RISC vs CISC, ASIC vs FPGA).
> - Architecture design questions test **CO1 (Knowledge of embedded system fundamentals)** at Bloom's *Understand* and *Analyze* levels.
> - It is the **foundation block** for Modules 2, 3, 4 (RTOS, IoT, ARM programming) — so losing marks here is *expensive*.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Embedded Architecture

| Pillar | Meaning | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware Architecture** | Physical components: CPU, memory, peripherals, buses | The *body and organs* of a human |
| **Software Architecture** | Drivers, OS, application logic, scheduling | The *thoughts and reflexes* of a human |
| **Communication Architecture** | How blocks exchange data (buses, protocols) | The *nervous system* of a human |

## 1.4 Core Classifications (Must Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Foundational Computational Models:**
> 1. **Von Neumann Architecture** — Single shared memory for both instructions and data.
> 2. **Harvard Architecture** — Separate memories (and buses) for instructions and data.
>
> **Two Instruction-Set Philosophies:**
> 1. **CISC** (Complex Instruction Set Computer) — Many, complex, variable-length instructions.
> 2. **RISC** (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) — Few, simple, fixed-length instructions.
>
> **Two Realization Strategies:**
> 1. **ASIC** (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) — Custom-designed for one purpose.
> 2. **FPGA** (Field-Programmable Gate Array) — Reconfigurable hardware.

## 1.5 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen:**
> - **CPU (Chef)** is the brain; it follows recipes (instructions) using ingredients (data).
> - **Memory (Pantry)** stores both recipes and ingredients. In Von Neumann, one pantry holds both. In Harvard, two separate pantries — one for recipes, one for ingredients — so the chef doesn't have to wait.
> - **Buses (Conveyor Belts)** carry items. A wider belt (32-bit bus) carries more at once.
> - **I/O (Waiter)** takes food out and brings in customer orders.
> - **SoC** is the entire compact kitchen — chef + pantries + burners — all in one box.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Von Neumann Architecture (Princeton Architecture)

**Conceptual Flow:** CPU ⇄ Single Memory (both programs and data live here) ⇄ I/O.

- **Bus Structure:** A *single shared bus* for instruction fetch and data read/write.
- **The Von Neumann Bottleneck:** Because the same bus is shared, the CPU cannot fetch an instruction *and* read data at the same instant — the throughput of the system is limited.
- **Mathematical Consequence:** If `f` is the clock frequency and `CPI` is the average cycles per instruction, the **maximum instruction throughput** is:

$$
T_{VN} = \frac{f}{CPI} \quad \text{[MIPS equivalent]}
$$

- But due to bus contention, the *effective* throughput is reduced by a contention factor `k ∈ (0,1]`:

$$
T_{VN,\text{eff}} = k \cdot \frac{f}{CPI}, \quad k \leq 1
$$

- **Best suited for:** General-purpose desktops, laptops (e.g., x86).
- **Embedded example:** Most low-end microcontrollers historically; 8051 family (von Neumann variant).

## 2.2 Harvard Architecture

**Conceptual Flow:** CPU ⇄ Instruction Memory (separate bus) AND CPU ⇄ Data Memory (separate bus).

- **Two physical buses:** Program Memory Bus and Data Memory Bus.
- **No Bottleneck:** Instruction fetch and data read/write can occur **simultaneously** (parallelism).
- **Modified Harvard:** Most modern processors (ARM, AVR) use a *modified Harvard* — separate caches but unified main memory. This balances speed and cost.
- **Best suited for:** DSPs, microcontrollers where deterministic timing is critical.

## 2.3 Comparison Matrix — Von Neumann vs Harvard

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory** | Single shared memory | Separate instruction & data memory |
| **Bus** | One common bus | Two independent buses |
| **Simultaneous I & D** | No | Yes |
| **Bottleneck** | Yes (Von Neumann bottleneck) | No |
| **Speed** | Slower (sequential) | Faster (parallel) |
| **Cost / Complexity** | Lower | Higher |
| **Determinism** | Lower | Higher |
| **Examples** | x86, 8051 (variant) | AVR, PIC, ARM (modified), DSPs |

## 2.4 RISC vs CISC — The Two Philosophies

### 2.4.1 CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)
- Many addressing modes, variable-length instructions, microcode-based decoding.
- **Philosophy:** "Reduce the semantic gap between high-level languages and machine code."
- **Examples:** Intel x86, Motorola 68k.
- **Instruction Count:** Hundreds; can perform multi-step operations in a single instruction.

### 2.4.2 RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)
- Few, fixed-length instructions; hardwired decoding; load-store architecture.
- **Philosophy:** "Keep the hardware simple, let the compiler do the work."
- **Examples:** ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, AVR, SPARC.
- **Instruction Count:** Typically `< 100`; each instruction executes in one cycle (ideally).

### 2.4.3 KTU Comparison Table

| Parameter | CISC | RISC |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Size** | Variable | Fixed (typically 32-bit) |
| **Addressing Modes** | Many (12+) | Few (3–5) |
| **Memory Access** | Instructions can access memory freely | Only Load/Store access memory |
| **Decoding** | Microcoded | Hardwired |
| **Pipelining** | Difficult | Highly amenable |
| **Clock Speed** | Lower (longer cycle) | Higher |
| **Code Density** | Higher (compact code) | Lower (more instructions for same task) |
| **Compiler Dependency** | Lower | Higher |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher (typical) | Lower (ideal for embedded) |
| **Examples** | x86, 68k | ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, AVR |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** Modern embedded designs overwhelmingly favor **RISC** (especially ARM) because of deterministic timing, low power, and excellent pipelining — three properties that matter *the most* in embedded systems.

## 2.5 ASIC vs FPGA vs Microcontroller vs DSP

| Realization | Flexibility | Performance | Power | NRE Cost | Per-Unit Cost | Volume |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ASIC** | None (fixed) | Highest | Lowest | Very High | Lowest | High |
| **FPGA** | High (re-programmable) | High | Higher | None | Medium | Low–Med |
| **Microcontroller** | High (software) | Medium | Low | Low | Low | All |
| **DSP** | Medium | High (math) | Medium | Low | Medium | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ASIC vs FPGA Rule of Thumb:**
> - **Choose ASIC** when production volume > ~100,000 units and design is frozen.
> - **Choose FPGA** for prototyping, low-volume production, or when requirements may evolve.

## 2.6 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | MIPS |
| 2 | $T_{exec} = N \times CPI \times T_{cycle}$ | Execution time for N instructions | seconds |
| 3 | $T_{cycle} = 1/f_{clock}$ | Clock period | seconds |
| 4 | $\text{AMAT} = T_{hit} + \text{MissRate} \times T_{miss}$ | Average Memory Access Time | seconds |
| 5 | $\text{Throughput}_{VN} = k \cdot f / CPI$, with $k \leq 1$ | Effective throughput (Von Neumann) | IPS |
| 6 | $\text{Speedup}_{Harvard} = 1/(1-F)$, $F$ = fraction parallelizable | Amdahl's-style speedup | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Bandwidth}_{bus} = W_{bus} \times f_{bus}$ | Bus bandwidth | bits/s |
| 8 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$ | Dynamic power (CMOS) | Watts |

## 2.7 The SoC (System-on-Chip) Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A **System-on-Chip (SoC)** integrates the **processor core, memory, peripherals, accelerators, and bus fabric** onto a *single silicon die*.

**Why SoCs dominate modern embedded design:**
- **Lower power** (short on-chip wires → less capacitance).
- **Smaller PCB footprint** (critical for wearables, IoT).
- **Higher performance** (no off-chip bus bottlenecks).
- **Examples:** Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon, NXP i.MX, Nordic nRF52, TI MSP432.

## 2.8 The AMBA Bus Architecture (ARM)

> [!NOTE]
> **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** is the *de-facto* on-chip bus standard for ARM-based SoCs.

| AMBA Bus | Purpose | Bandwidth | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **APB** (Advanced Peripheral Bus) | Low-bandwidth peripherals | Low | UART, GPIO, timers |
| **AHB** (Advanced High-performance Bus) | High-performance modules | Medium | CPU, DMA, external memory |
| **AXI** (Advanced eXtensible Interface) | High-bandwidth, parallel transactions | Very High | DDR, GPU, high-speed DSP |
| **ASB** (Advanced System Bus) | Legacy / mid-performance | Medium | Older ARM cores |

## 2.9 Hardware–Software Partitioning

This is the **most exam-relevant architectural decision**:

1. **Pure hardware** — operations with strict latency or high throughput (e.g., AES encryption, FFT).
2. **Pure software** — complex decision logic, user interfaces, control flow.
3. **Co-design** — hardware accelerates the *hot path*; software handles the rest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Run a function in hardware if it (a) is called frequently, (b) has a hard real-time deadline, and (c) can be parallelized. Otherwise, keep it in software.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Execution Time and MIPS

Let us derive the two most important performance formulas for KTU exams from first principles.

**Given:**
- A program has `N` instructions to execute.
- The processor runs at clock frequency `f_{clock}` (Hz).
- Average cycles per instruction is `CPI`.

**Step 1 — Clock period:**
The clock period is the inverse of the clock frequency:

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{f_{clock}}
$$

**Step 2 — Total execution time:**
The total execution time is the product of the number of instructions, the cycles per instruction, and the time per cycle:

$$
T_{exec} = N \times CPI \times T_{cycle}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the clock period:**

$$
T_{exec} = N \times CPI \times \frac{1}{f_{clock}}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive MIPS:**
MIPS stands for "Million Instructions Per Second." Compute instructions-per-second, then divide by $10^6$:

$$
\text{IPS} = \frac{N}{T_{exec}} = \frac{N}{N \times CPI / f_{clock}} = \frac{f_{clock}}{CPI}
$$

$$
\therefore \text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Higher clock and lower CPI give more MIPS. RISC architectures aim for CPI ≈ 1, while CISC typically has CPI > 1.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A processor runs at 50 MHz. The CPI for a benchmark is 2.5. Compute (a) the MIPS rating, (b) the execution time for a program of $N = 10^7$ instructions.

**Solution:**

**(a) MIPS rating:**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{2.5 \times 10^6} = 20 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**(b) Execution time:**

$$
T_{exec} = N \times CPI \times T_{cycle} = 10^7 \times 2.5 \times \frac{1}{50 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{exec} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^7}{50 \times 10^6} = \frac{2.5}{5} = 0.5 \text{ seconds}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Von Neumann Bottleneck Impact

**Setup:** Suppose a fraction `F` of operations *require* simultaneous instruction fetch and data access (e.g., a load that also needs the next instruction).

**Von Neumann (serialized):** The two operations take twice the time.

$$
T_{VN} = 2 \cdot T_{op} \quad \text{(for the bottleneck fraction)}
$$

**Harvard (parallelized):** Both happen in parallel.

$$
T_{Harvard} = T_{op} \quad \text{(no overhead)}
$$

**Speedup for the bottlenecked fraction:**

$$
S = \frac{T_{VN}}{T_{Harvard}} = 2
$$

**Overall Amdahl-style speedup** for a program where the *parallelizable* fraction is `F`:

$$
S_{overall} = \frac{1}{(1-F) + F/2} = \frac{2}{2 - F}
$$

**Sanity check:**
- If `F = 0` (no bottleneck situations): $S = 1$ (no speedup). ✓
- If `F = 1` (all operations bottleneck): $S = 2$ (maximum 2×). ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for the Examiner:** A common mistake is to state "Harvard is *twice* as fast as Von Neumann." This is **only true** when `F = 1` (every operation is a bottleneck). In real life, the speedup is bounded above by **2×**, often much less.

## 3.4 Derivation: AMAT (Average Memory Access Time)

In a memory hierarchy with a cache and main memory:

**Given:**
- $T_{hit}$ = cache access time
- $\text{MissRate}$ = probability of cache miss (between 0 and 1)
- $T_{miss}$ = penalty to fetch from main memory on a miss

**Step 1:** On a *hit*, access takes $T_{hit}$.
**Step 2:** On a *miss*, access takes $T_{hit} + T_{miss}$ (you still pay the cache access time, then go to main memory).
**Step 3:** Take the expectation (weighted average):

$$
\text{AMAT} = (1 - \text{MissRate}) \cdot T_{hit} + \text{MissRate} \cdot (T_{hit} + T_{miss})
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$
\text{AMAT} = T_{hit} + \text{MissRate} \cdot T_{miss}
$$

**Worked example:** If $T_{hit} = 5$ ns, $T_{miss} = 100$ ns, $\text{MissRate} = 0.05$ (5%):

$$
\text{AMAT} = 5 + 0.05 \times 100 = 5 + 5 = 10 \text{ ns}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Dynamic Power (CMOS Switched Capacitance Model)

**Given:**
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (probability a gate switches in a clock cycle, $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$)
- $C$ = load capacitance at the output of the gate
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage
- $f$ = clock frequency

**Step 1:** Energy to charge a capacitor to $V_{DD}$: $E = \frac{1}{2} C V_{DD}^2$
**Step 2:** Multiply by switching activity (gates that don't switch waste no energy):

$$
E_{per\_cycle} = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{2} C V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by clock frequency to get power:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded Insight:** Reducing $V_{DD}$ has a *quadratic* effect on power. This is why low-power embedded processors (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0+) operate at $V_{DD} \leq 1.8$ V. Dropping voltage from 3.3 V to 1.8 V reduces dynamic power by a factor of $(3.3/1.8)^2 \approx 3.36$.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Architecture design questions often ask *"which architecture would you choose for X?"* The answer is a **decision table**, not a single word.

| Application | Recommended Architecture | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Smartwatch (Apple Watch) | SoC with ARM Cortex + DSP | Low power, mixed signal & number crunching |
| Washing machine controller | 8-bit MCU (8051/PIC) | Low cost, simple control loop |
| Car infotainment | SoC (ARM Cortex-A) | Graphics, multimedia, OS support |
| Hearing aid | Custom ASIC | Ultra-low power, fixed function |
| IoT sensor prototype | FPGA → later ASIC | Need to iterate, then scale |
| Mobile phone baseband | ASIC + DSP | High-volume, hard real-time DSP |
| Drone flight controller | ARM Cortex-M4 (with FPU) | Real-time, deterministic, math-heavy |
| Spacecraft control | Radiation-hardened ASIC | Reliability > cost |

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison: Pipelining Benefits (RISC)

A 5-stage RISC pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) approaches one instruction per cycle in the *steady state*. The **ideal speedup** vs. a non-pipelined processor:

$$
S_{pipeline} = k \quad (\text{where } k = \text{number of pipeline stages})
$$

For `k = 5`: theoretical 5× speedup. Real-world (accounting for hazards, stalls, flushes): `2×–3×`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Von Neumann Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU Unit ALU Registers Control Unit] -- Single Shared Bus --> MEM[Single Memory Instructions plus Data]
    MEM -- Single Shared Bus --> CPU
    CPU -- Address Data Control --> IO[I/O Devices]
    IO -- Address Data Control --> CPU
```

**Interpretation:** The CPU, memory, and I/O all communicate over **one common bus**. The bus is a *bottleneck* because instruction fetches and data transfers cannot occur at the exact same instant.

## 4.2 Harvard Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPUcore[CPU Core ALU Registers Control Unit]
    CPUcore -- Instruction Bus IB --> PMem[Program Memory Stores Instructions]
    CPUcore -- Data Bus DB --> DMem[Data Memory Stores Variables]
    CPUcore -- I/O Bus --> IOsys[I/O Peripherals]
```

**Interpretation:** Two physically separate buses (IB and DB) connect the CPU to two physically separate memories. **Simultaneous** instruction fetch and data access are possible.

## 4.3 Modified Harvard — Modern Embedded SoC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Core[ARM Cortex M Core]
    Ic[Instruction Cache]
    Dc[Data Cache]
    MMU[Bus Matrix / AMBA AXI]
    Flash[Flash Memory Unified]
    SRAM[SRAM Unified]
    Peri[Peripherals GPIO ADC UART]
    Core --> Ic
    Core --> Dc
    Ic --> MMU
    Dc --> MMU
    MMU --> Flash
    MMU --> SRAM
    MMU --> Peri
```

**Interpretation:** Externally unified memory (Flash + SRAM), but **internally** the cache is split into I-cache and D-cache — best of both worlds: speed of Harvard, simplicity of Von Neumann.

## 4.4 RISC vs CISC — Instruction Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CISC_FLOW["CISC Execution"]
    C1[Fetch variable length instruction] --> C2[Microcode decode multi-cycle]
    C2 --> C3[Memory operands embedded in instruction]
    C3 --> C4[Execute complex operation]
    C4 --> C5[Write back]
    end
    subgraph RISC_FLOW["RISC Execution"]
    R1[Fetch fixed 32 bit instruction] --> R2[Hardwired decode single cycle]
    R2 --> R3[Load operands from registers]
    R3 --> R4[ALU executes simple op]
    R4 --> R5[Store result back to register]
    end
```

## 4.5 AMBA Bus Hierarchy in a Modern SoC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AHB_AXI_HIGH["High Performance Domain AXI AHB"]
    A1[ARM Cortex A Core]
    A2[DMA Controller]
    A3[External Memory Controller DDR]
    end
    subgraph APB_LOW["Low Power Domain APB"]
    B1[UART]
    B2[GPIO]
    B3[Timers]
    B4[I2C]
    end
    AHB_AXI_HIGH -- Bridge --> APB_LOW
```

**Interpretation:** The high-speed AXI/AHB backbone carries CPU and memory traffic. The APB serves low-bandwidth peripherals, gated by a bridge for power savings.

## 4.6 Hardware-Software Partitioning Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Specification Functional and Non Functional] --> B[Profile Hot Paths Compute Intensive]
    B --> C{Real time deadline exists?}
    C -- Yes --> D{Parallelizable}
    C -- No --> E[Implement in Software on MCU]
    D -- Yes --> F[Implement in Hardware ASIC or FPGA accelerator]
    D -- No --> G[Optimized software with RTOS]
    F --> H[Co designed System SoC with HW accelerator]
    G --> H
    E --> H
```

## 4.7 Top-Down Embedded System Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Requirements Functional Non Functional Power Cost] --> S2[Specifications Detailed Behavior]
    S2 --> S3[Architecture Design Processor Memory Bus OS Choice]
    S3 --> S4[Hardware Software Co Design Partitioning]
    S4 --> S5[Implementation RTL Code Firmware Drivers]
    S5 --> S6[Integration and Testing on FPGA or Emulator]
    S6 --> S7[Validation against Specifications]
    S7 --> S8[Production Prototype ASIC or PCB]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Differentiate between Von Neumann and Harvard architecture.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
| Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Single shared memory for instructions & data | Separate instruction & data memory |
| Bus | One common bus | Two independent buses (program & data bus) |
| Simultaneous I & D access | No (causes Von Neumann bottleneck) | Yes (parallel fetch possible) |
| Speed | Slower (sequential) | Faster (parallel) |
| Complexity / Cost | Simpler, cheaper | More complex, expensive |
| Example | x86, 8051 (variant) | AVR, PIC, ARM (modified Harvard) |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Explain the term RISC. List any two features of RISC architecture.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
**RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** is a processor design philosophy that uses a small, highly optimized set of instructions, with each instruction designed to execute in a single clock cycle.

**Two key features:**
1. **Fixed-length instructions** (typically 32 bits) that simplify decoding and enable pipelining.
2. **Load/Store architecture** — only load/store instructions access memory; all other operations work on registers, enabling fast register-to-register ALU execution.

*(Other valid features: hardwired control unit, large register file, few addressing modes, pipelining-friendly.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Module 1]**
> **(a)** Explain the Von Neumann architecture with a neat block diagram. Discuss the Von Neumann bottleneck and how Harvard architecture overcomes it. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the Harvard architecture. Compare it with Von Neumann architecture on at least five parameters. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Von Neumann Architecture:**

The Von Neumann (or Princeton) architecture consists of:
- A **CPU** containing the ALU, registers, and control unit.
- A **single memory unit** storing both instructions and data.
- **I/O devices** for external interaction.
- A **single shared bus** carrying address, data, and control signals.

**Block Diagram:** (as in Section 4.1)

**Operation:**
1. CPU fetches instruction from memory over the shared bus.
2. Instruction is decoded; operand addresses are computed.
3. Data is fetched (over the *same* bus).
4. ALU executes; result is stored back.

**Von Neumann Bottleneck:**
Since both instructions and data share a single bus, the bus can carry only one type of information at a time. This means the CPU **cannot fetch the next instruction while it is reading/writing data** for the current one. As a result, both the CPU and the memory remain idle for a fraction of the time, reducing throughput.

**Quantitative impact:**

$$
\text{Speedup}_{Harvard} = \frac{2}{2 - F}
$$

where `F` is the fraction of operations that require simultaneous I & D access. In the worst case (`F=1`), Harvard is **2× faster**; in the best case (`F=0`), there is no difference.

**How Harvard Overcomes It:**
Harvard architecture uses **two physically separate memories** (program + data) and **two separate buses**. The CPU can therefore fetch the next instruction over the program bus **at the same time** that it reads/writes data over the data bus. This eliminates the bus contention and enables true parallel access.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Harvard Architecture:**

The Harvard architecture consists of:
- A **CPU** (ALU + registers + control unit).
- A **Program Memory** storing instructions, connected via a dedicated **Instruction Bus**.
- A **Data Memory** storing operands, connected via a dedicated **Data Bus**.
- I/O devices.
- Optional unified external memory (in *Modified Harvard*).

**Block Diagram:** (as in Section 4.2)

**Operation:**
- Instruction fetch occurs on the program bus.
- Simultaneously, data read/write occurs on the data bus.
- The control unit orchestrates both buses in parallel.

**Comparison with Von Neumann (any five):**

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory structure | Single unified memory | Separate instruction & data memory |
| Buses | One shared bus | Two independent buses |
| Parallel I & D access | No | Yes |
| Speed | Lower | Higher |
| Cost & complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Determinism | Less deterministic | More deterministic |
| Application suitability | General-purpose computing | DSP, real-time embedded control |
| Example | x86, 8051 | AVR, PIC, ARM (modified Harvard) |

**Conclusion:** Harvard (and its modified form) is preferred in embedded systems where deterministic timing and high throughput outweigh the higher silicon cost.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Module 1]**
> **(a)** What is RISC? Compare RISC and CISC architectures on any seven parameters. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the AMBA bus architecture used in ARM-based SoCs. Describe APB, AHB, and AXI buses and their typical applications. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**RISC Definition:**
**RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** is a CPU design philosophy that employs a *small, highly optimized* set of instructions. Each instruction is of a *fixed length*, executes in (ideally) *one clock cycle*, and is decoded by *hardwired logic* (not microcode). Arithmetic/logic operations work only on registers; memory is accessed exclusively through *load* and *store* instructions (load–store architecture).

**Comparison (RISC vs CISC):**

| # | Parameter | RISC | CISC |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Instruction size | Fixed (typically 32 bits) | Variable (1–15 bytes in x86) |
| 2 | Number of instructions | Few (< 100 typically) | Many (hundreds) |
| 3 | Addressing modes | Few (3–5) | Many (12+) |
| 4 | Memory access | Only Load/Store | Any instruction can access memory |
| 5 | Decoding | Hardwired (fast) | Microcoded (slower) |
| 6 | Pipelining | Highly amenable | Difficult |
| 7 | Code size | Larger (more instructions) | Smaller (compact code) |
| 8 | Clock speed | Higher | Lower |
| 9 | Power consumption | Lower (typical) | Higher |
| 10 | Compiler complexity | Higher | Lower |
| 11 | Examples | ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, AVR, SPARC | Intel x86, Motorola 68k, VAX |
| 12 | Embedded suitability | Excellent (low power, deterministic) | Less common |

**Why RISC dominates embedded:**
- Low power consumption.
- Deterministic execution (essential for real-time systems).
- Excellent pipelining for high clock speeds.
- Simpler silicon → cheaper die.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**AMBA — Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture:**

AMBA is an **on-chip bus protocol standard** introduced by ARM Ltd. It defines how processors, memory, and peripherals communicate *inside* a System-on-Chip (SoC). AMBA is the *de-facto* standard for nearly all ARM-based embedded SoCs.

**The three principal AMBA buses:**

**1. APB — Advanced Peripheral Bus:**
- **Purpose:** Connect low-bandwidth peripherals to the SoC.
- **Characteristics:** Simple, low-power, non-pipelined, single clock edge.
- **Typical peripherals:** GPIO, UART, I²C, SPI, timers, watchdog, RTC.
- **Bridge:** Connects to the higher-speed AHB or AXI backbone via an **APB Bridge**, which decouples the slower peripheral bus from the high-speed core.

**2. AHB — Advanced High-performance Bus:**
- **Purpose:** High-performance on-chip communication.
- **Characteristics:** Pipelined, supports burst transfers, multiple bus masters, single-clock-edge protocol.
- **Typical modules:** CPU, DMA controller, external memory controller, LCD controller.
- **Use case:** Mid-to-high performance embedded SoCs (e.g., Cortex-M3/M4-based designs).

**3. AXI — Advanced eXtensible Interface:**
- **Purpose:** Very high bandwidth, parallel, multi-master communication.
- **Characteristics:** Multiple independent address/control and data channels, outstanding transactions, unaligned data transfer support, low-power extensions.
- **Typical modules:** DDR memory controller, GPU, high-speed DSP, video pipeline, Ethernet.
- **Use case:** High-end Cortex-A application processors, smartphones, ADAS.

**Hierarchical AMBA Block Diagram:** (as in Section 4.5)

**Summary Table:**

| Bus | Speed | Power | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **APB** | Low | Very low | Simple peripherals |
| **AHB** | Medium | Medium | CPU ↔ memory ↔ DMA |
| **AXI** | Very high | Medium-high | High-bandwidth accelerators, DDR |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "Modified Harvard" with "Pure Harvard."** Many students draw *two* memory blocks and call it Modified Harvard. Modified Harvard means **split L1 caches** with **unified main memory**.
> 2. **Stating "Harvard is twice as fast as Von Neumann."** This is the *maximum* speedup, not the rule. State the Amdahl-style formula or mention the conditional speedup.
> 3. **Mixing up APB and AHB.** APB is for slow peripherals; AHB is for high-speed modules. Students often interchange them.
> 4. **Forgetting the role of the APB bridge.** The bridge is what gates power on the APB side when idle — exam question favorite.
> 5. **Not justifying the architecture choice in design questions.** A question like "which architecture for a smart watch?" demands a *justified* answer (low power → RISC ARM SoC), not a one-word answer.
> 6. **Writing "RISC = fewer instructions, CISC = more" without context.** Always add that RISC aims for *single-cycle* execution and *load-store* design.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Summary — Architecture Design (Module 1)**

### A. Foundational Models
- **Von Neumann:** Single memory, single bus → bottleneck. Use: general-purpose (x86).
- **Harvard:** Separate I & D memories, separate buses → parallel access. Use: real-time embedded (AVR, DSP).
- **Modified Harvard:** Unified main memory, split L1 caches. Use: modern ARM Cortex-M/A.

### B. Instruction Set Philosophies
- **RISC:** Fixed 32-bit, load/store, hardwired, pipelined, low power. Examples: ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, AVR.
- **CISC:** Variable length, many addressing modes, microcoded, complex. Example: x86.
- **MIPS formula:** $\text{MIPS} = f_{clock} / (CPI \times 10^6)$.
- **Execution time:** $T_{exec} = N \times CPI / f_{clock}$.

### C. Hardware Realization Choices
- **ASIC** — high volume, low per-unit, no flexibility.
- **FPGA** — prototyping, low volume, re-programmable.
- **Microcontroller** — software-driven, low cost, all-purpose.
- **DSP** — math-heavy signal processing.
- **SoC** — integrates CPU + memory + peripherals on one die.

### D. AMBA Bus Hierarchy (ARM SoCs)
- **APB** → low-speed peripherals (UART, GPIO).
- **AHB** → medium-speed modules (CPU, DMA, memory).
- **AXI** → high-speed parallel (DDR, GPU, video).

### E. Hardware-Software Partitioning
- Put in **hardware** if: hot path, real-time deadline, parallelizable, math-intensive.
- Put in **software** if: complex control, user-driven, evolving requirements.
- **Co-design** = hybrid: hardware accelerator + software stack.

### F. Performance Formulas (memorize all)
- $T_{exec} = N \times CPI \times T_{cycle}$
- $\text{MIPS} = f / (CPI \times 10^6)$
- $\text{AMAT} = T_{hit} + \text{MissRate} \times T_{miss}$
- $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$
- $S_{Harvard} = 2 / (2 - F)$ (vs. Von Neumann)

### G. Decision-Making Mnemonics
- **Real-time?** → RISC + Harvard.
- **Low power?** → RISC + low $V_{DD}$ + Modified Harvard.
- **High volume?** → ASIC.
- **Prototype?** → FPGA.
- **Multimedia?** → SoC + DSP + AXI.
- **Determinism-critical?** → Harvard (no cache) or tightly-bounded cache.

### H. Common KTU Question Patterns
1. "Differentiate Von Neumann and Harvard" → draw both + 6-point table.
2. "Compare RISC and CISC" → table with 7+ parameters.
3. "Justify architecture for application X" → decision matrix.
4. "Explain AMBA / APB / AHB / AXI" → hierarchical block diagram + use cases.
5. "Derive MIPS / Execution time" → use the standard formula chain.
6. "What is SoC? List advantages" → definition + 4–5 advantages.
7. "Hardware-software co-design" → partitioning flowchart + example.

> **End of Module 1 — Topic: Architecture Design**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing Hardware and Software Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Designing Hardware and Software Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Embedded System Design** is the engineering discipline of conceiving, specifying, implementing, and validating a computing system that is *dedicated* to performing a specific function within a larger mechanical, electrical, or computational ecosystem. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme, the design of an embedded system involves a strict, iterative co-engineering of its **Hardware Components** (the physical, electronic, and electromechanical layers) and its **Software Components** (the algorithmic, control, and interface layers) to satisfy a constrained set of **Design Metrics**: performance, power, cost, size, reliability, and time-to-market.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> An embedded system is a computer system with a dedicated function within a larger mechanical or electronic system, often with real-time computing constraints. Designing its hardware and software components means selecting the optimum **Processor**, **Memory hierarchy**, **I/O peripherals**, **Actuators**, and **Firmware architecture** such that functional and non-functional requirements are met *simultaneously* and *optimally*.

## 1.2 The Two Pillars of an Embedded System

| Pillar | Description | Engineering Discipline |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hardware Components** | The tangible electronic circuitry — the brain (processor), the storage (memory), the senses (sensors), and the muscles (actuators). | Electronics, VLSI, Mechatronics |
| **Software Components** | The intangible logic — the firmware, device drivers, real-time operating system (RTOS), and application algorithms. | Computer Science, Control Engineering |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body" Model

To a B.Tech student encountering this for the first time, think of an embedded system as a **synthetic human body built to perform one job**, such as a digital watch, a car Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), or a pacemaker.

- **The Brain (CPU / Microcontroller)** → Central Processing Unit. It fetches, decodes, and executes every instruction.
- **The Memory (RAM & ROM)** → Short-term and long-term memory of the brain. RAM holds active variables, ROM holds the program permanently.
- **The Sensory Organs (Sensors)** → Eyes, ears, and skin. These gather physical signals (temperature, pressure, motion) and convert them to electrical signals.
- **The Muscles (Actuators)** → Motors, solenoids, and LEDs. They convert electrical signals back to physical action.
- **The Nerves (Bus / Interconnects)** → Data, address, and control buses that carry signals between brain and organs.
- **The Soul (Firmware / RTOS)** → The "operating instinct" — the program and scheduling logic that decides what the brain does, when, and why.

Just as a human body cannot function with only a brain and no nerves, an embedded system fails if hardware and software are not co-designed in perfect harmony.

## 1.4 Core Design Metrics (The 6 Pillars of Constraint)

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU exam question on this topic ultimately tests the student's ability to **balance trade-offs** across these six metrics. Memorize them in the order below:

1. **Performance** — Speed of execution, throughput, latency.
2. **Power** — Energy consumed (critical for battery-operated systems).
3. **Cost** — Bill of Materials (BOM) and manufacturing cost.
4. **Size / Form Factor** — Physical dimensions (PCB area, enclosure volume).
5. **Reliability** — Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF), fault tolerance.
6. **Time-to-Market** — Design cycle duration, reusability of IP blocks.

## 1.5 The Hardware–Software Boundary (Co-Design)

The central challenge of this KTU Module 1 topic is deciding **which part of a system should be realized in hardware and which in software**. This is called **Hardware–Software Co-Design**. A function implemented in hardware (e.g., an FFT block in an FPGA) is *fast but inflexible*. The same function implemented in software on a general-purpose CPU is *slow but flexible*. The designer's job is to draw the optimal boundary line.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hardware Components — The Tangible Layer

The hardware layer of an embedded system is conventionally partitioned into **four functional sub-blocks**. The KTU 2024 examiner expects the student to be able to draw and label all four for full marks.

### 2.1.1 The Processing Subsystem

This is the heart that executes the control algorithm. The two dominant families are:

- **Microprocessor (MPU)**: A CPU with *no on-chip memory or peripherals*. It is accompanied by external RAM, ROM, and I/O chips. Used when high computational power is needed (e.g., industrial robots).
- **Microcontroller (MCU)**: A CPU with *integrated* RAM, ROM (Flash), timers, ADC, UART, and I/O on a single chip. Dominates embedded designs due to its low cost and compact form factor.
- **DSP (Digital Signal Processor)**: A specialized processor optimized for real-time signal processing (e.g., audio codecs, radar).
- **ASIC / FPGA / SoC**: Application-Specific ICs, Field-Programmable Gate Arrays, and System-on-Chips that offer the highest performance-per-watt at the cost of design complexity.

### 2.1.2 The Memory Subsystem

Memory is split into **volatile** (loses data on power-off) and **non-volatile** (retains data).

- **RAM (Random Access Memory)** — Volatile working memory. Two flavors: **SRAM** (Static, 6 transistors per cell, fast, used for cache) and **DRAM** (Dynamic, 1 transistor + 1 capacitor, dense, used for main memory).
- **ROM (Read-Only Memory)** — Non-volatile. Subtypes: Mask ROM, PROM, EPROM, **EEPROM**, and modern **Flash memory** (NOR-Flash for code, NAND-Flash for mass data).
- **Cache** — A small, ultra-fast SRAM layer between CPU and main memory, holding frequently accessed instructions/data to exploit **locality of reference**.

### 2.1.3 The I/O Subsystem

This block bridges the digital processor to the physical world.

- **Sensors (Input Transducers)**: Convert physical quantities → electrical signals.
  Examples: **LM35** (temperature, 10 mV/°C), **MPU6050** (6-axis accelerometer + gyroscope), photodiodes, ultrasonic HC-SR04.
- **Actuators (Output Transducers)**: Convert electrical signals → physical action.
  Examples: DC motors, stepper motors, servo motors, solenoids, relays, LEDs, buzzers.
- **I/O Interfaces & Protocols**: GPIO, **UART** (asynchronous serial), **SPI** (4-wire synchronous), **I²C** (2-wire synchronous, multi-master), **CAN** (automotive), **USB**, **Ethernet**.

### 2.1.4 The Communication / Bus Subsystem

- **System Bus** — three distinct buses: **Data Bus** (bidirectional, carries operand), **Address Bus** (unidirectional, carries location), **Control Bus** (carries R/W, IRQ, clock).
- **Peripheral Interconnects** — AMBA (ARM's standard), Wishbone, Avalon, CoreConnect.

## 2.2 Software Components — The Intangible Layer

The KTU 2024 syllabus strictly partitions the software stack into four layers, drawn from low-level to high-level.
