### 2.2.1 Firmware / Device Drivers

These are the lowest-level software modules that directly manipulate the hardware registers. A driver exposes a clean **API** to the higher layers (e.g., `motor_set_speed(int pwm_duty_cycle)`). The driver hides the complexity of setting specific bits in the **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** of the MCU.

### 2.2.2 Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) — Optional Middleware

For systems requiring deterministic task scheduling, an **RTOS** is layered above the drivers. It provides:

- **Task Scheduler** (Pre-emptive priority-based, e.g., **Rate Monotonic Scheduling**).
- **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** — semaphores, mutexes, message queues, mailboxes.
- **Memory Management** — fixed-block allocators to avoid fragmentation.
- **Interrupt Handling** — vectored, nested interrupts with priority levels.

### 2.2.3 Middleware / Protocol Stacks

Re-usable software modules for standard functions: TCP/IP stack (for IoT), Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) GATT layer, file systems (FAT, LittleFS), graphics libraries (LVGL).

### 2.2.4 Application Software

The high-level control algorithm written to satisfy the system's functional specification. Examples: PID controller for a drone, OCR algorithm for a scanner, decision-tree classifier for a wearable ECG monitor.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **Formula Cheat Sheet** for the numerical / analytical questions examiners ask on this topic.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Symbol / Unit | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **CPU Execution Time** | $T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f_{clk}}$ | $N$ = instruction count, $CPI$ = cycles per instruction, $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency (Hz). |
| 2 | **CPU Performance** | $Performance = \dfrac{1}{T_{CPU}}$ | Performance in **MIPS / MFLOPS / Dhrystone** |
| 3 | **Dynamic Power** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity (0 to 1), $C$ = load capacitance, $V_{dd}$ = supply voltage, $f$ = frequency. |
| 4 | **Static Power** | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{dd}$ | $I_{leak}$ = leakage current |
| 5 | **Energy per Instruction** | $E_{inst} = P_{avg} \times T_{CPU} / N$ | Energy consumed per executed instruction. |
| 6 | **Amdahl's Law (Speedup)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | $f$ = parallelizable fraction, $n$ = speedup of that fraction. |
| 7 | **Memory Capacity** | $Capacity = 2^{A} \times W$ | $A$ = address lines, $W$ = data width (bits). |
| 8 | **Baud Rate (UART)** | $Baud = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{16 \times (UBRR+1)}$ | For Atmel/Microchip UART. |
| 9 | **Throughput** | $Th = \dfrac{Tasks}{T_{period}}$ | Number of tasks completed per unit time. |
| 10 | **Deadline Miss Probability** | $P_{miss} = P(W_{exec} > D)$ | For a real-time task with worst-case execution time $W_{exec}$ and deadline $D$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never write **CPU time = (N × CPI) × f**. The frequency goes in the **denominator**, not the numerator. This is a one-mark deduction but appears in nearly every board paper.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hardware–software co-design is what powers every modern device. Examples:

- **Automotive ABS Controller**: Hardware → Bosch 32-bit MCU, wheel-speed Hall sensors, hydraulic solenoid actuators. Software → RTOS with 5 ms control loop, EKF state estimator. Co-design constraint: must respond in < 5 ms to a wheel-locking event to comply with ISO 26262 ASIL-D safety standard.
- **Smart Insulin Pump**: Hardware → ARM Cortex-M0 MCU, glucose enzyme sensor, micropump. Software → PID control loop, BLE stack for phone pairing. Co-design constraint: total system power must last 14 days on a coin cell, fixing a strict energy budget per insulin-delivery event.
- **Mars Helicopter (Ingenuity)**: Hardware → Snapdragon SoC + radiation-hardened FPGA. Software → Linux (flight computer) + VxWorks RTOS. Co-design constraint: 200 W peak solar power, 30 minutes of flight per sol, 1500 g mass limit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CPU Execution Time and Performance

**Problem Statement:** A KTU 2024 numerical: *An embedded MCU runs a control loop at a clock frequency of 50 MHz. The compiled assembly contains 12,000 instructions with an average CPI of 1.8. Compute (a) the CPU execution time per loop, and (b) the throughput in loops-per-second.*

### Step 1: Identify the given quantities and units
- Clock frequency, $f_{clk} = 50 \text{ MHz} = 50 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz} = 5 \times 10^{7} \text{ cycles/second}$
- Instruction count, $N = 12{,}000$
- Cycles per instruction, $CPI = 1.8$

### Step 2: Compute the total number of clock cycles required

$$N_{cycles} = N \times CPI$$

$$N_{cycles} = 12{,}000 \times 1.8 = 21{,}600 \text{ cycles}$$

*Logic explanation:* The CPU does not execute instructions in 1-to-1 correspondence with clock cycles. Each instruction consumes an *average* of 1.8 cycles. Thus, 12,000 instructions require $12{,}000 \times 1.8$ cycles.

### Step 3: Compute CPU execution time

$$T_{CPU} = \frac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}}$$

$$T_{CPU} = \frac{21{,}600 \text{ cycles}}{5 \times 10^{7} \text{ cycles/second}}$$

$$T_{CPU} = 4.32 \times 10^{-4} \text{ seconds} = 432 \text{ microseconds}$$

### Step 4: Compute throughput in loops per second

$$Throughput = \frac{1}{T_{CPU}}$$

$$Throughput = \frac{1}{4.32 \times 10^{-4}} = 2314.81 \text{ loops/second}$$

**Conclusion:** The MCU completes approximately **2314 control loops per second**, leaving ample slack (≈ 397 µs) for sensor ADC sampling and actuator PWM updates within a 1 ms period.

## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to Hardware Acceleration

**Problem Statement:** *A KTU 2019-style question: A video decoder's software implementation requires 1.2 seconds per frame on an ARM Cortex-A53. The Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) module accounts for 70% of the execution time. The hardware team designs a DCT accelerator IP that runs that 70% portion 10× faster. Compute the overall system speedup.*

### Step 1: State Amdahl's Law

$$S_{overall} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{n}}$$

Where:
- $S_{overall}$ = total system speedup
- $f$ = fraction of the work that is accelerated
- $n$ = speedup factor of the accelerated portion

### Step 2: Substitute the values

- $f = 0.70$ (the 70% DCT block)
- $n = 10$ (10× hardware acceleration)

$$S_{overall} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.70) + \frac{0.70}{10}}$$

$$S_{overall} = \frac{1}{0.30 + 0.07} = \frac{1}{0.37}$$

$$S_{overall} = 2.7027$$

### Step 3: Compute the new execution time

$$T_{new} = \frac{T_{old}}{S_{overall}} = \frac{1.2 \text{ s}}{2.7027} = 0.444 \text{ seconds per frame}$$

### Step 4: Interpret the result with the "Amdahl Wall"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Even with a **10× accelerator**, the system speedup is **only 2.70×**, not 10×. This is the fundamental **Amdahl Wall** — the 30% non-accelerated portion caps the total improvement. To get higher speedup, both the accelerated and non-accelerated portions must be optimized.

## 3.3 Derivation: Power and Energy Trade-off for a Battery-Powered IoT Node

**Problem Statement:** *A wireless sensor node uses an MSP430 MCU. Active current is 1.2 mA at 3.3 V and 16 MHz. Sleep current is 5 µA. The node wakes once every 5 seconds, performs 1500 instructions at CPI = 1.0, transmits for 50 ms, then sleeps. Compute (a) the energy per cycle, (b) the average power consumption, and (c) the battery life on a 1200 mAh, 3.3 V Li-Po cell.*

### Step 1: Compute active time per cycle

$$t_{active} = \frac{N \times CPI}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1500 \times 1.0}{16 \times 10^{6}} = 9.375 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 93.75 \text{ µs}$$

### Step 2: Compute active energy per cycle

$$E_{active} = V_{dd} \times I_{active} \times t_{active}$$

$$E_{active} = 3.3 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.375 \times 10^{-5} = 3.7125 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J} = 0.371 \text{ µJ}$$

### Step 3: Compute sleep energy per cycle

$$E_{sleep} = V_{dd} \times I_{sleep} \times t_{sleep}$$

$$E_{sleep} = 3.3 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times (5 - 0.05) \approx 3.3 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.95 = 8.168 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J} = 81.68 \text{ µJ}$$

### Step 4: Compute total energy per duty cycle and average power

$$E_{total} = E_{active} + E_{tx} + E_{sleep}$$

Assuming the transmit current is included in active mode: $E_{total} \approx 0.371 + 81.68 = 82.05 \text{ µJ}$ per 5 s.

$$P_{avg} = \frac{E_{total}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{82.05 \times 10^{-6}}{5} = 16.41 \text{ µW}$$

### Step 5: Compute battery life

$$Battery Energy = 3.3 \text{ V} \times 1.2 \text{ Ah} \times 3600 \text{ s/h} = 14{,}256 \text{ J}$$

$$Life = \frac{14{,}256}{16.41 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.687 \times 10^{8} \text{ seconds} \approx 27.54 \text{ years}$$

**Conclusion:** The node is dominated by sleep current. To improve, one would *reduce sleep current first*, not active current — a classic hardware-software co-design insight.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hardware vs. Software Implementation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Comparative Table:** When asked to justify hardware vs. software partitioning, present this matrix in the answer.

| Design Parameter | Hardware Implementation | Software Implementation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Speed** | Very high (parallelism) | Limited by CPU clock |
| **Flexibility** | Low — must re-fabricate to change | High — recompile and flash |
| **Cost (low volume)** | High (NRE cost) | Low (already on MCU) |
| **Cost (high volume)** | Low per unit | High (requires powerful MCU) |
| **Power** | Low at steady state | Higher (CPU active draw) |
| **Time-to-Market** | Slow (RTL → synthesis → tape-out) | Fast (write C code, compile) |
| **Best for** | Data-path heavy, latency-critical | Control logic, complex decision trees |
| **Example** | FFT engine, AES crypto | TCP/IP stack, PID loop |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Embedded System Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 examiner expects this exact block-level view in any "Draw and explain the architecture of an embedded system" question. The **Mermaid syntax rules** (no reserved words, no bold inside labels) are followed strictly.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pw[Power Supply Unit] --> mcu
    pw --> sensor
    pw --> act
    clk[Crystal Oscillator] --> mcu
    rst[Reset Circuit] --> mcu
    mcu[Microcontroller Unit] -->|Control| act
    sensor[Sensor Array] -->|Analog Signal| adc[ADC Interface]
    adc --> mcu
    mcu -->|PWM Signal| drv[Driver Circuit]
    drv --> act[Actuator Bank]
    mcu -->|UART SPI I2C| dbg[Debug Interface]
    mcu -->|Memory Bus| rom[ROM Flash]
    mcu -->|Memory Bus| ram[RAM SRAM]
    mcu -->|GPIO| lcd[User Interface LCD LED]
    dbg --> pc[Host PC]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **Microcontroller** is the central hub. It draws regulated power from the **PSU** and a stable clock from the **Crystal**. The **Sensor Array** transduces physical inputs into analog signals, which are digitized by the **ADC** and consumed by the MCU. The MCU sends computed outputs to the **Driver Circuit** (e.g., H-bridge), which powers the **Actuator Bank** (motor, solenoid). The MCU also fetches firmware from **ROM (Flash)** and uses **RAM (SRAM)** as a working scratchpad. The **User Interface** (LCD/LED/Keypad) provides human interaction, and the **Debug Interface** (UART/SWD) allows the developer to flash firmware and monitor state.

## 4.2 Flowchart: Hardware-Software Co-Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    req[System Requirements Specification] --> func[Functional and Non-Functional Requirements]
    func --> metric[Identify Design Metrics Power Cost Size]
    metric --> partition{Hardware vs Software Partition}
    partition -->|Software| fw[Firmware and RTOS Design]
    partition -->|Hardware| hdl[RTL Hardware Design]
    fw --> integ[Integration and Verification]
    hdl --> integ
    integ --> val{Validation against Metrics}
    val -->|Fails| partition
    val -->|Passes| prod[Prototype Fabrication]
    prod --> cert[Certification and Deployment]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the canonical **Y-chart / waterfall co-design flow**. The process starts with the **SRS** (Software Requirements Specification), derives **design metrics**, and enters the iterative **partitioning** loop. After integration, the design is validated. If it fails any metric, the partition is *revised* — this feedback loop may execute 3 to 10 times before a converging solution emerges.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Software Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app[Application Layer Control Algorithm]
    mid[Middleware Layer Protocol Stacks File Systems]
    rtos[RTOS Layer Scheduler IPC Drivers]
    drv[Device Driver Layer Register Manipulation]
    hw[Hardware Layer MCU Peripherals]
    app --> mid
    mid --> rtos
    rtos --> drv
    drv --> hw
```

**Reading the diagram:** The software stack is strictly hierarchical. The **Application Layer** (e.g., a Kalman filter for a drone) calls the API exposed by the **Middleware** (e.g., a BLE GATT library). The middleware uses the **RTOS** (e.g., FreeRTOS scheduler) to manage task timing. The RTOS invokes **Device Drivers** to set hardware registers. The hardware finally executes the action. Information flows only *downward* through the layers, with **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** calls providing portability across different MCUs.

## 4.4 Schematic: Memory-Mapped I/O Addressing

> [!NOTE]
> Use this for any "explain memory organization" question. Drawn as a block diagram to represent the address-space layout.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    addr[CPU Address Bus] --> dec[Address Decoder]
    dec -->|High Addresses| flash[Flash ROM Firmware Storage 0x0000 to 0x7FFF]
    dec -->|Mid Addresses| sram[SRAM Working Memory 0x8000 to 0x8FFF]
    dec -->|Low Addresses| peri[Peripheral SFRs 0x9000 to 0x90FF]
    flash -->|Instruction Fetch| cpu
    sram -->|Data Read Write| cpu
    peri -->|Register Access| cpu
    cpu[CPU Core] -->|Read Write| flash
    cpu -->|Read Write| sram
    cpu -->|Read Write| peri
```

**Reading the diagram:** The CPU places an address on the address bus. The **Address Decoder** uses the high-order bits to select the target device: **Flash** for persistent program code, **SRAM** for volatile runtime data, and **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** for peripheral control. This unified address space is called **Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO)**, and is used in nearly all modern 32-bit MCUs such as STM32 and LPC1768.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1: Define an embedded system and list its key hardware components.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **embedded system** is a dedicated, single-purpose computer system designed to perform a specific function within a larger electromechanical system, typically with real-time constraints.

The key hardware components are:
1. **Processing Unit** (Microcontroller / Microprocessor / SoC)
2. **Memory Unit** (RAM for temporary data, ROM/Flash for program storage)
3. **I/O Units** (Sensors for input, Actuators for output)
4. **Power Supply Unit** (regulator, battery)
5. **Clock and Reset Circuit** (crystal oscillator, POR circuit)

**Mark Split:** Definition (1) + Any 4 components with one-line function (2).

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a microprocessor and a microcontroller.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Microprocessor (MPU) | Microcontroller (MCU) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Integration** | CPU only; external memory & peripherals | CPU + RAM + ROM + peripherals on a single chip |
| **Cost** | Higher system cost | Lower system cost |
| **Applications** | General-purpose PCs, high-end robotics | Washing machines, watches, IoT nodes |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher | Lower |
| **External Components** | Many (RAM, ROM, I/O chips) | Few (just crystal and decoupling caps) |

**Mark Split:** Any 3 valid differentiating points (3).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Choice-Based, 14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** (either OR). The student must answer *one* full question. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A: (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an embedded system. Describe the function of each block in detail.

**Model Solution:**

The embedded system architecture consists of four main blocks, as drawn in Section 4.1 above. The student should present the diagram and explain each block:

1. **Processing Unit (3 marks for explanation):** The MCU executes the firmware. It has an ALU for arithmetic, a Control Unit for instruction sequencing, and Register File for fast operand access. The fetch-decode-execute cycle runs at the clock frequency, e.g., 72 MHz for STM32F103.

2. **Memory Unit (2 marks):** Flash stores the program code; SRAM stores the runtime stack, heap, and global variables. Cache (if present) reduces memory access latency by exploiting temporal and spatial locality.

3. **I/O Subsystem (1 mark):** Sensors (LM35, MPU6050) provide analog/digital inputs. Actuators (motors, solenoids) provide outputs. The ADC and PWM peripherals bridge the digital processor to the analog world.

4. **Communication and Power (1 mark):** Buses (AMBA, AHB, APB) interconnect blocks. The PSU provides regulated voltage; the crystal provides a stable timebase.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ARM Cortex-M0 MCU runs a sensor-fusion algorithm consuming 8000 instructions with an average CPI of 1.2 at a clock of 24 MHz. Compute (i) the total execution time, (ii) the throughput in iterations per second, and (iii) the energy consumed per iteration if the active current is 0.8 mA at 3.0 V.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total clock cycles:**
$$N_{cycles} = N \times CPI = 8000 \times 1.2 = 9600 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 2 — Execution time per iteration:**
$$T_{CPU} = \frac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}} = \frac{9600}{24 \times 10^{6}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 400 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 3 — Throughput:**
$$Th = \frac{1}{T_{CPU}} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 2500 \text{ iterations/second}$$

**Step 4 — Energy per iteration:**
$$E_{iter} = V_{dd} \times I_{active} \times T_{CPU} = 3.0 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$E_{iter} = 9.6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J} = 0.96 \text{ µJ}$$

**Conclusion:** The fusion algorithm runs at 2.5 kHz, well above the 1 kHz IMU sampling rate, leaving 600 µs of slack for communication overhead.

---

### Question B: (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the hardware-software co-design methodology. Discuss Amdahl's Law with a numerical example.

**Model Solution:**

**Hardware-Software Co-Design (4 marks):** It is the simultaneous, integrated design of hardware and software components of an embedded system from a unified specification. The methodology follows the flow shown in Section 4.2: SRS → metrics → partitioning → implementation → integration → validation. The goal is to find the **optimal partition** that minimizes cost and power while meeting real-time deadlines.

**Amdahl's Law (3 marks):** It states that the overall system speedup obtained from accelerating one portion of the system is limited by the unaccelerated portion:

$$S_{overall} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{n}}$$

**Numerical Example (within the 3 marks):** If 80% of a CAN stack's 2 ms latency is spent in software parsing, and we move that 80% to a hardware accelerator 5× faster, the new overall time is:

$$S = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.80/5} = \frac{1}{0.36} = 2.78$$

So total latency drops from 2 ms to $\dfrac{2}{2.78} = 0.72 \text{ ms}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the hardware and software implementation approaches for an embedded system function. What factors dictate the choice?

**Model Solution:**

The student should present the **Analytical Matrix** from Section 3.4 and discuss:

1. **Performance Requirements** (2 marks): If the function must complete in microseconds (e.g., a 1 Gbps Ethernet MAC), it *must* be hardware (ASIC/FPGA). Software on an MCU cannot meet such deadlines.
2. **Flexibility and Upgrade Needs** (2 marks): If the algorithm is expected to evolve (e.g., firmware updates with new ML models), a software implementation on a flash-based MCU is preferable.
3. **Cost and Volume** (2 marks): For low-volume production (< 10,000 units), software on a general-purpose MCU is cheaper (no NRE mask charges). For high-volume (> 1 million units), a hardware ASIC reduces per-unit cost dramatically.
4. **Power Budget** (1 mark): A custom hardware block can be clock-gated and voltage-scaled to a much lower power than a CPU running software loops, making hardware preferable for ultra-low-power IoT.

**Conclusion:** The trade-off is summarized as **speed vs. flexibility vs. cost vs. power**, and the optimal partition is found iteratively during the co-design process.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons students LOSE marks on this topic in KTU exams:**
> 1. **CPU Time Formula Inversion** — Writing $T = N \times CPI \times f$ instead of $T = (N \times CPI) / f$. KTU 2024 valuation keys explicitly deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Missing one block in the architecture diagram** — Examiners allot 3 of 7 marks to the diagram; omitting the Power Supply or Clock block costs 1 mark each.
> 3. **Confusing RAM and ROM roles** — Saying ROM is "working memory" is a fundamental error and loses the 1 mark allocated to the memory sub-block.
> 4. **No units in numerical answers** — Final answers without µs, mW, or kHz units are penalized 0.5 mark per quantity, capped at 1 mark per question.
> 5. **Hardware-Software Co-Design answer given as a definition only** — Examiners expect a *flow diagram* and an *example numerical application of Amdahl's Law* for full 7 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- An **embedded system** is a dedicated, single-purpose computer embedded within a larger system, characterized by tight **design constraints** (power, cost, size, real-time, reliability).
- The **hardware layer** has four blocks: **Processor, Memory, I/O, Bus/Power**.
- The **software layer** has four blocks: **Application, Middleware, RTOS, Device Drivers**.
- The **MCU** integrates CPU + RAM + ROM + peripherals on one chip; the **MPU** requires external memory and I/O.
- **Memory** is **volatile** (RAM: SRAM, DRAM) and **non-volatile** (ROM: Mask, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash).
- **Sensors** convert physical to electrical signals; **Actuators** convert electrical to physical action. **ADC** bridges analog sensors to digital MCU; **PWM** drives analog actuators.
- The **CPU execution time formula** is $\dfrac{N \times CPI}{f}$, *not* $N \times CPI \times f$. The CPU **performance** is the inverse of execution time.
- **Dynamic power** is $\alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$; **static power** is $I_{leak} \cdot V_{dd}$. Reducing $V_{dd}$ is the most effective power-saving lever (quadratic effect).
- **Amdahl's Law** $S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + f/n}$ sets a hard upper bound on system speedup; the unaccelerated portion is the limiting factor.
- **Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO)** unifies peripheral and memory access in a single address space, used by all modern 32-bit MCUs.
- **Hardware-Software Co-Design** is the iterative process of deciding which functionality goes into hardware (fast, expensive, inflexible) and which into software (slow, cheap, flexible).
- The six core **design metrics** in the order of KTU priority are: **Performance → Power → Cost → Size → Reliability → Time-to-Market**.
- For battery-powered systems, **sleep current** dominates energy consumption. The first design action is to minimize $I_{sleep}$, not to optimize active code.
- An **RTOS** provides deterministic scheduling using priority-based pre-emptive algorithms such as **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**.
- Buses are of three types: **Data Bus** (bidirectional, carries operand), **Address Bus** (unidirectional, carries location), **Control Bus** (carries R/W, IRQ, clock).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** System Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Integration** in the context of Embedded System Design refers to the systematic process of combining hardware components, software modules, firmware, communication interfaces, sensors, actuators, and mechanical subsystems into a unified, cohesive, and fully functional embedded product that meets specified performance, power, cost, and reliability constraints.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PBEVT504 – Embedded System Design*, System Integration encompasses the engineering discipline of:

- **Hardware-Software Co-Design:** Jointly designing hardware architecture and software/firmware to optimize the complete system rather than individual components.
- **Component Interfacing:** Ensuring heterogeneous modules (analog, digital, mixed-signal, mechanical, RF) communicate seamlessly.
- **Verification & Validation (V&V):** Proving the integrated system satisfies its specifications under all operating conditions.
- **Design-for-Integration (DFI):** Architecting each subsystem anticipating its integration into the larger whole.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Term:** The syllabus specifically classifies integration at three levels — **Chip-Level (SoC)**, **Board-Level (PCB/Embedded Board)**, and **System-Level (Multi-board Product)**. Each level has distinct design tools, challenges, and verification strategies.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **System Integration** as **assembling a modern automobile**:

- The **engine** (Microcontroller/Microprocessor + SoC) provides computational horsepower.
- The **gearbox, brakes, and steering** (Sensors and Actuators) interact with the real world.
- The **wiring harness** (Communication Buses — I²C, SPI, UART, CAN) carries signals between modules.
- The **dashboard electronics** (HMI — LCD, Touch Panel, LEDs) provide the user interface.
- The **ECU (Engine Control Unit)** (RTOS + Firmware) coordinates everything in real time.

Just as a car cannot drive until all these subsystems are properly connected, calibrated, and tested as a whole, an embedded system is non-functional until integration is complete. A brilliant engine attached to faulty wiring still fails — this is precisely why **integration is the most failure-prone phase** of embedded product development.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** Over **60% of embedded product delays** in industry originate from integration issues, not from individual module design. KTU examiners frequently test this practical reality.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **bolded** metrics are universally referenced in embedded system integration:

- **Clock Frequency (f):** Measured in **Hz, kHz, MHz, GHz**. Determines synchronous operation speed.
- **Bus Width (n):** Measured in **bits (8, 16, 32, 64)**. Defines data transfer parallelism.
- **Throughput:** Measured in **Mbps, Gbps, MB/s**. Quantifies data rate.
- **Latency (τ):** Measured in **nanoseconds (ns) to milliseconds (ms)**. Critical for real-time systems.
- **Power Dissipation (P):** Measured in **Watts (W)** or **milliwatts (mW)**.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Measured in **hours (h)** — the **50,000 to 100,000 hours** range is typical for consumer embedded systems.
- **Operating Temperature Range:** Standard industrial range is **−40°C to +85°C**.

---

### 1.4 Scope of System Integration in KTU Module 1

| Integration Aspect | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware-Software Partitioning** | Deciding which functions run in hardware vs software | High-weightage |
| **SoC / ASIC / FPGA Selection** | Choosing the right compute fabric | High-weightage |
| **Bus Architecture Selection** | I²C, SPI, UART, CAN, PCIe, AMBA | High-weightage |
| **RTOS Integration** | Scheduling, inter-process communication | High-weightage |
| **Device Driver Development** | Bridging OS and hardware | Medium-weightage |
| **Firmware-Bootloader Integration** | System bring-up sequence | Medium-weightage |
| **Verification & Debugging** | Logic analyzers, oscilloscopes, JTAG | Medium-weightage |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept: The Integration Pyramid

System Integration follows a **bottom-up hierarchical model**. Each level builds upon the verified correctness of the level below it:

1. **Transistor Level (Physics):** Individual MOSFETs, BJTs form gates.
2. **Gate Level (Logic):** AND, OR, NOT, flip-flops.
3. **Register-Transfer Level (RTL):** Registers, ALUs, multiplexers described in VHDL/Verilog.
4. **Processor / IP Core Level:** CPU cores, memory controllers, peripheral IP blocks.
5. **Chip / SoC Level:** Multiple IP cores integrated on a single silicon die.
6. **Board / PCB Level:** SoC, memory, power management, connectors on a PCB.
7. **Product / System Level:** Multiple boards enclosed in a mechanical chassis with software.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Verification at each level is **cheaper and faster** than verification at higher levels. A bug caught at the transistor level costs **$1 to fix**; the same bug caught in a deployed product costs **>$10,000 to fix** (Boehm's cost-of-defect curve).

---

### 2.2 The Three Pillars of System Integration

#### Pillar A — Hardware-Software Co-Design
- **Definition:** A concurrent design methodology where hardware and software are developed in parallel with continuous feedback loops.
- **Why it matters:** Modern embedded products (smartphones, IoT devices, ADAS) cannot be designed sequentially. Software constraints dictate hardware selection; hardware capabilities limit software implementation.
- **How it works:** A **co-simulation environment** (e.g., Questa, SystemVue) models both the hardware RTL and the software instruction set simultaneously, allowing early performance estimation.

#### Pillar B — Interface and Bus Architecture
- **Definition:** The standardized communication pathway that allows heterogeneous components to exchange data.
- **Why it matters:** Choice of bus dictates bandwidth, latency, pin count, power, and EMI.
- **How it works:** Buses implement layered protocols — **physical layer (voltages, connectors)**, **data link layer (framing, arbitration)**, and **application layer (commands)**.

#### Pillar C — Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) Integration
- **Definition:** A deterministic, priority-based scheduler that manages multiple concurrent tasks.
- **Why it matters:** Most embedded systems must respond to events within bounded time (hard real-time) or best-effort time (soft real-time).
- **How it works:** The RTOS kernel (e.g., FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RTEMS) provides **task scheduling, inter-task communication (queues, semaphores, mutexes), memory management, and interrupt handling**.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

System Integration is the **single most important competency** in professional embedded product development. The following domains depend critically on integration expertise:

- **Automotive Electronics:** ADAS, ECU networks, AUTOSAR architecture.
- **IoT & Edge AI:** Smart sensors, gateways, edge inference.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphones, smartwatches, AR/VR headsets.
- **Industrial Automation:** PLCs, SCADA, robotic arms.
- **Medical Devices:** Pacemakers, infusion pumps, imaging systems.
- **Aerospace & Defense:** Avionics, UAVs, satellite payloads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Career Tip:** The **"T-shaped engineer"** — broad knowledge across hardware, software, and systems — is precisely what System Integration training produces. This skill is the **highest-paid specialization** in the Indian embedded industry (₹8 LPA to ₹35 LPA range in 2024–2026).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1: Liu & Layland Rate Monotonic Schedulability Test

The **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** algorithm is the cornerstone of real-time embedded system integration. The following derivation proves the famous Liu & Layland utilization bound.

**Problem Setup:** Consider $n$ periodic real-time tasks $\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_n$ with periods $T_1 \le T_2 \le \ldots \le T_n$ and worst-case execution times $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$. Under RMS, the highest-frequency task gets the highest priority.

**Step 1: Define CPU Utilization**
The fraction of CPU time consumed by task $i$ is $C_i / T_i$. The total utilization is:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$

**Step 2: The Critical Instant Theorem (Theorem 1 of Liu & Layland, 1973)**
A task's worst-case response time occurs when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with it. This is proven by observing that any phase shift between tasks only reduces preemption, never increases it.

**Step 3: Worst-Case Response Time Recurrence**
The completion time $W_i(t)$ of task $i$ at time $t$ satisfies:

$$W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{W_i(t)}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

**Step 4: Solve the Fixed-Point Equation**
The minimum $t$ satisfying $W_i(t) \le t$ gives the worst-case response time $R_i$. The recurrence is solved iteratively.

**Step 5: The Utilization Bound**
For $n$ tasks, the RMS algorithm guarantees schedulability if:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$$

**Step 6: Verification of Limiting Cases**

For $n = 1$:

$$U_{bound} = 1 \cdot (2^{1/1} - 1) = 1 \cdot 1 = 1.0000 \quad (100\%)$$

For $n = 2$:

$$U_{bound} = 2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) = 2 \cdot (1.4142 - 1) = 0.8284 \quad (82.84\%)$$

For $n \to \infty$:

$$U_{bound} = \lim_{n \to \infty} n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \quad (69.31\%)$$

**Conclusion:** As the number of tasks increases, the guaranteed schedulable utilization bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 69.31\%$. This is the famous **Liu & Layland bound**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Can we always use 100% CPU in a real-time system?" the correct answer is **NO** under RMS for $n \ge 2$. The bound drops rapidly. However, **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** can achieve 100% utilization but is harder to implement.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2: Total System Reliability (Series Model)

**Problem Setup:** An embedded system has $n = 5$ components with the following individual reliabilities (probability of survival over mission time):

$$R_1 = 0.99, \quad R_2 = 0.98, \quad R_3 = 0.995, \quad R_4 = 0.97, \quad R_5 = 0.999$$

**Step 1: Identify the Reliability Model**
For an embedded system where **all components must function** for the system to function, the components are in **series** from a reliability standpoint.

**Step 2: Apply the Series Reliability Formula**

$$R_{system} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 \times R_4 \times R_5$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$R_{system} = 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.995 \times 0.97 \times 0.999$$

**Step 4: Sequential Multiplication**

$$0.99 \times 0.98 = 0.9702$$

$$0.9702 \times 0.995 = 0.96535$$

$$0.96535 \times 0.97 = 0.93639$$

$$0.93639 \times 0.999 = 0.93545$$

**Step 5: Final Result**

$$R_{system} \approx 0.9355 \quad \text{or} \quad 93.55\%$$

**Step 6: Failure Probability**

$$F_{system} = 1 - R_{system} = 1 - 0.9355 = 0.0645 \quad \text{or} \quad 6.45\%$$

**Insight:** Even with all individual component reliabilities above 97%, the system reliability drops to **93.55%**. This is why **redundancy (parallel reliability models)** is essential for safety-critical embedded systems like avionics and medical devices.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Integration Trade-Off Analysis

The following table maps common integration decisions against their engineering trade-offs — a frequently-tested KTU question type.

| Design Decision | Performance Impact | Power Impact | Cost Impact | Development Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ASIC vs FPGA** | ASIC: highest; FPGA: high | ASIC: lowest; FPGA: higher | ASIC: high NRE; FPGA: low NRE | ASIC: 6–12 months; FPGA: weeks |
| **Single-board vs Multi-board** | Single-board: lower latency | Single-board: lower power | Single-board: cheaper | Multi-board: modular |
| **RTOS vs Bare-Metal** | RTOS: predictable | RTOS: higher overhead | RTOS: licensing cost | Bare-metal: faster |
| **Parallel vs Serial Bus** | Parallel: higher BW | Parallel: more pins = more power | Parallel: more PCB layers | Serial: easier routing |
| **Polling vs Interrupt** | Interrupt: lower latency | Interrupt: more wakeups | Same | Interrupt: more code |
| **C vs Assembly** | Assembly: fastest | Same | Same | C: faster development |
| **Integrated vs Discrete PMIC** | Integrated: smaller | Integrated: more efficient | Integrated: more expensive IC | Discrete: flexible |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Bus Bandwidth Calculation

**Problem:** A 32-bit ARM Cortex-M4 system runs at $f_{clk} = 72 \text{ MHz}$. The APB bus has a clock divider of 2. Compute the maximum theoretical bus bandwidth.

**Step 1: APB Bus Clock Frequency**

$$f_{APB} = \frac{f_{clk}}{2} = \frac{72 \text{ MHz}}{2} = 36 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2: APB Bus Period**

$$T_{APB} = \frac{1}{f_{APB}} = \frac{1}{36 \times 10^6} \approx 27.78 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Theoretical Bandwidth (Nyquist)**

$$BW_{max} = n \cdot f_{APB} = 32 \times 36 \times 10^6 = 1.152 \times 10^9 \text{ bits/s} = 1.152 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 4: Practical Bandwidth (Assume 70% efficiency)**

$$BW_{practical} = 0.7 \times 1.152 = 0.8064 \text{ Gbps} \approx 806 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Conclusion:** The APB bus can transfer approximately **806 Mbps** in practice.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Embedded System Integration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Specification] --> B[Hardware Software Partitioning]
    B --> C1[Hardware Design]
    B --> C2[Software Design]
    C1 --> D1[RTL Design VHDL Verilog]
    C2 --> D2[Firmware Drivers]
    D1 --> E[SoC Synthesis]
    D2 --> F[RTOS Integration]
    E --> G[Board Layout PCB]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Prototype Fabrication]
    H --> I[Hardware Software Integration]
    I --> J[Verification and Validation]
    J --> K{Tests Pass}
    K -->|Yes| L[Product Certification]
    K -->|No| M[Debug and Iterate]
    M --> I
    L --> N[Production Deployment]
    N --> O[Field Updates OTA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flowchart above illustrates the **V-Model** of embedded system integration. The left leg represents decomposition (specification → detailed design), and the right leg represents integration (component → system → acceptance). KTU frequently asks students to identify which V-Model phase corresponds to a given development activity.

---

### 4.2 Architecture: SoC Integration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU Core ARM Cortex] --> BUS1[AMBA AXI Bus]
    BUS1 --> DSP[DSP Accelerator]
    BUS1 --> GPU[GPU Block]
    BUS1 --> MEMC[Memory Controller]
    MEMC --> RAM[DDR RAM]
    MEMC --> FLASH[NOR Flash]
    BUS1 --> PERI[Peripheral Bus APB]
    PERI --> UART1[UART]
    PERI --> I2C1[I2C Controller]
    PERI --> SPI1[SPI Controller]
    PERI --> GPIO1[GPIO Banks]
    PERI --> TIMER1[Timers and PWM]
    PERI --> ADC1[ADC and DAC]
    PERI --> ETH1[Ethernet MAC]
    PMU[Power Management Unit] -.-> CPU
    PMU -.-> BUS1
    PMU -.-> PERI
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram shows a **typical System-on-Chip (SoC)** used in modern embedded designs (smartphones, IoT gateways). The **AMBA AXI high-performance bus** connects compute-intensive blocks (CPU, DSP, GPU), while the **AMBA APB low-power peripheral bus** connects simple peripherals. The **Power Management Unit (PMU)** dynamically gates power to unused blocks — this is the heart of low-power SoC integration.

---

### 4.3 Architecture: RTOS Task Integration Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ISR1[Hardware ISR Timer] --> RTOS[RTOS Kernel]
    ISR2[Hardware ISR UART RX] --> RTOS
    ISR3[Hardware ISR GPIO] --> RTOS
    RTOS --> SCH[Scheduler Priority Based]
    SCH --> T1[Task 1 High Priority Sensor Read]
    SCH --> T2[Task 2 Medium Priority Control Loop]
    SCH --> T3[Task 3 Low Priority Logging]
    T1 --> QUEUE1[Inter Task Queue]
    QUEUE1 --> T2
    T2 --> SEM[Semaphore]
    SEM --> T3
    T1 -.-> MUTEX[Mutex Shared Resource]
    T2 -.-> MUTEX
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram depicts the **RTOS integration model**. Hardware ISRs (Interrupt Service Routines) signal events to the kernel, which schedules tasks based on priority. Tasks communicate via **queues, semaphores, and mutexes**. The **mutex** protects shared resources (e.g., a global data buffer) from race conditions.

---

### 4.4 Integration Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Unit Testing] --> B[Module Level]
    B --> C[Integration Testing]
    C --> D[Subsystem Level]
    D --> E[System Testing]
    E --> F[Acceptance Testing]
    F --> G{Customer Requirements Met}
    G -->|Yes| H[Sign Off]
    G -->|No| I[Defect Tracking]
    I --> A
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **testing pyramid** for embedded system integration. Note that **unit tests** are most numerous (cheapest, fastest), while **acceptance tests** are fewest (most expensive, slowest). KTU examiners love this pyramid — it appears in nearly every module.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> What is **Hardware-Software Co-Design**? Why is it preferred over sequential design in modern embedded systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Hardware-Software Co-Design is a concurrent design methodology where hardware architecture and software/firmware are designed in parallel, with continuous co-simulation and trade-off analysis.
>
> **Advantages (1.5 marks):**
> 1. Early detection of design errors through co-simulation, reducing time-to-market.
> 2. Optimal partitioning of functions between hardware (speed) and software (flexibility).
> 3. Better power, performance, and area (PPA) optimization for the integrated system.
> 4. Enables design space exploration before committing to silicon.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme ESE Model]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **three levels of system integration** in embedded system design. For each level, describe the design tools used and the typical challenges encountered.
>
> **(b) [7 marks)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **AMBA bus architecture** used in modern SoC integration. Compare the AXI, AHB, and APB buses in terms of performance, complexity, and typical use cases.

---

**Model Solution (a) — Three Levels of Integration [7 marks]:**

**Level 1: Chip-Level Integration (SoC / ASIC / FPGA) [2.5 marks]**

- **Description:** Multiple intellectual property (IP) cores — CPU, DSP, memory controllers, peripherals — are integrated on a single silicon die using sub-micron CMOS fabrication.
- **Tools Used:** Cadence Genus, Synopsys Design Compiler, Mentor Calibre (for DRC/LVS), Vivado (for FPGA).
- **Challenges:** NRE cost ($1M–$100M), mask costs, lengthy fabrication cycles (3–6 months), verification complexity, signal integrity at deep sub-micron nodes.
- **Example:** Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple M-series, STM32 families.

**Level 2: Board-Level Integration (PCB) [2.5 marks]**

- **Description:** The SoC, memory chips (DDR, Flash), power management ICs, sensors, and connectors are mounted on a multi-layer Printed Circuit Board.
- **Tools Used:** Altium Designer, KiCad, Cadence Allegro, Ansys SIwave.
- **Challenges:** Signal integrity, power integrity, EMI/EMC compliance, thermal management, BGA fan-out routing, manufacturing yield.
- **Example:** Arduino Uno, Raspberry Pi, STM32 Nucleo.

**Level 3: System-Level Integration (Product) [2 marks]**

- **Description:** Multiple PCBs (compute board, sensor board, power board, display board) are integrated into a mechanical enclosure with cables, connectors, and a complete software stack.
- **Tools Used:** SolidWorks, AutoCAD, LabVIEW, MATLAB/Simulink, custom test fixtures.
- **Challenges:** Mechanical-electrical co-design, environmental sealing (IP ratings), regulatory certification (CE, FCC, RoHS, BIS), supply chain coordination, field reliability.
- **Example:** Smartphone, automobile ECU cluster, medical ventilator.

---

**Model Solution (b) — AMBA Bus Architecture [7 marks]:**

**Block Diagram [3 marks]:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU Core] --> AXI[AXI Bus High Performance]
    AXI --> MEMC[Memory Controller]
    MEMC --> RAM[External DDR]
    AXI --> AHB[AHB Bridge]
    AHB --> APB[APB Bridge]
    APB --> U1[UART]
    APB --> I1[I2C]
    APB --> S1[SPI]
    APB --> T1[Timers]
    APB --> G1[GPIO]
    AXI --> DMA1[DMA Controller]
```

**Detailed Explanation [4 marks]:**

The **Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)** is the de facto on-chip bus standard for ARM-based SoCs. It defines three primary bus protocols:

1. **AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface):** High-performance, multi-channel, burst-capable bus used for CPU-to-memory and CPU-to-DMA transfers. Supports out-of-order transactions, multiple outstanding requests, and separate read/write channels. **Throughput:** Up to several GB/s.

2. **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus):** Medium-performance, single-channel bus used for connecting high-bandwidth peripherals (USB, Ethernet, SDRAM controllers). Simpler than AXI but with lower throughput.

3. **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus):** Low-power, simple bus used for connecting low-bandwidth peripherals (UART, I²C, SPI, GPIO, timers, ADC). Optimized for minimal power and area. No pipelining.

**Comparison Table [included in marks]:**

| Feature | AXI | AHB | APB |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Performance** | Very High | High | Low |
| **Complexity** | High | Medium | Low |
| **Power** | Higher | Medium | Lowest |
| **Channels** | 5 (AW, AR, W, R, B) | 1 (shared) | 1 (shared) |
| **Use Case** | CPU↔Memory, DMA | USB, Ethernet, DMA | UART, GPIO, I²C |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

> **[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme ESE Model]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** What is an **RTOS**? Explain the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** algorithm. A system has 3 tasks with periods $T_1 = 10 \text{ ms}$, $T_2 = 20 \text{ ms}$, $T_3 = 40 \text{ ms}$ and execution times $C_1 = 2 \text{ ms}$, $C_2 = 5 \text{ ms}$, $C_3 = 8 \text{ ms}$. Test for schedulability.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **Liu & Layland utilization bound**. Prove that the bound approaches $\ln 2$ as $n \to \infty$. State two practical limitations of RMS in real embedded systems.

---

**Model Solution (a) [7 marks]:**

**Definition of RTOS [1 mark]:**
A **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** is an operating system designed to process data and respond to events within strictly defined time constraints. Examples: FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RTEMS, QNX.

**RMS Algorithm [2 marks]:**
Rate Monotonic Scheduling is a **fixed-priority preemptive** scheduling algorithm where:
- The task with the **shortest period** is assigned the **highest priority**.
- Priorities are assigned **statically at design time**.
- A higher-priority task **preempts** a lower-priority task upon arrival.

**Schedulability Test [4 marks]:**

**Step 1: Compute Total Utilization**

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{2}{10} + \frac{5}{20} + \frac{8}{40}$$

$$U = 0.2 + 0.25 + 0.2 = 0.65$$

**Step 2: Compute Liu & Layland Bound for n = 3**

$$U_{bound} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1)$$

$$2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599$$

$$U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7797$$

**Step 3: Compare**

$$U = 0.65 \le U_{bound} = 0.7797$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **schedulable under RMS**. ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **sufficient condition** (Liu & Layland bound) with the **necessary condition**. The bound is only **sufficient**, not necessary. A task set with $U > U_{bound}$ may still be schedulable — the bound test simply cannot guarantee it. For exact analysis, use the **response time analysis** (fixed-point iteration) method.

---

**Model Solution (b) [7 marks]:**

**Liu & Layland Bound [3 marks]:**
The Liu & Layland utilization bound states that a set of $n$ periodic real-time tasks is **guaranteed schedulable** under RMS if the total CPU utilization satisfies:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$$

**Proof of Limit as $n \to \infty$ [2.5 marks]:**

We want to evaluate:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$$

Let $x = 1/n$. As $n \to \infty$, $x \to 0$. Then:

$$\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{2^x - 1}{x}$$

Apply the **Taylor series expansion** of $2^x$ around $x = 0$:

$$2^x = e^{x \ln 2} = 1 + x \ln 2 + \frac{(x \ln 2)^2}{2!} + \cdots$$

Substituting:

$$2^x - 1 = x \ln 2 + O(x^2)$$

Therefore:

$$\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{2^x - 1}{x} = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x \ln 2 + O(x^2)}{x} = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

**Conclusion:** As $n \to \infty$, the Liu & Layland bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 69.31\%$.

**Two Practical Limitations of RMS [1.5 marks]:**

1. **Pessimistic bound for few tasks:** For $n = 2$, the bound is only 82.84%, even though empirical studies show 88% utilization is often achievable. The bound is a **worst-case guarantee**, not the actual limit.

2. **No support for aperiodic tasks:** RMS only handles **periodic** tasks. Aperiodic and sporadic tasks (e.g., user inputs, alarms) require additional mechanisms like **deferrable servers** or **polling servers**, increasing complexity.

3. **Priority inversion problem:** Without **priority inheritance protocol**, a low-priority task holding a shared resource can block a high-priority task indefinitely. (Bonus point for advanced students.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up Utilization Bound vs. Actual Utilization** — these are two different quantities. The bound is a *threshold*; the actual utilization is a *measurement*. Do not add them or compare them in the wrong direction.
> 2. **Forgetting the square root / cube root in $2^{1/n}$** — always show the numerical computation of $2^{1/n}$ explicitly. KTU valuators award partial credit only when the intermediate step is shown.
> 3. **Confusing $C_i$ and $T_i$** — $C_i$ is the **execution time** (computational cost), $T_i$ is the **period** (deadline interval). Reversing them leads to inverted answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — System Integration (Module 1, PBEVT504)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Integration = combining HW + SW + FW + interfaces + mechanics into a working embedded product.
- ✅ **Three Levels:** Chip-Level (SoC) → Board-Level (PCB) → System-Level (Product).
- ✅ **Hardware-Software Co-Design:** Concurrent methodology with co-simulation; preferred over sequential design.
- ✅ **AMBA Bus Family:** AXI (high-perf) → AHB (medium) → APB (low-power peripherals). Know the throughput and use cases.
- ✅ **RTOS:** Deterministic scheduler for real-time tasks. Examples: FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX.
- ✅ **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):** Shortest period → highest priority. Static, preemptive.
- ✅ **Liu & Layland Bound:** $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. Limit is $\ln 2 \approx 69.31\%$ as $n \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Reliability (Series):** $R_{sys} = \prod R_i$. Multi-board systems have lower system reliability than individual components.
- ✅ **Power:** $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$. Voltage scaling is the most effective power reduction technique (quadratic effect).
- ✅ **Bus Bandwidth (Nyquist):** $BW = n \cdot f_{clk} / 2$ for parallel buses; practical efficiency is 60–80%.
- ✅ **Verification Pyramid:** Unit → Module → Integration → System → Acceptance. Earlier testing is cheaper.
- ✅ **Tools:** Cadence, Synopsys, Mentor, Altium, MATLAB/Simulink, LabVIEW, Keil, IAR, GCC.
- ✅ **Industry Standards:** AUTOSAR (automotive), MISRA-C (safety-critical), ISO 26262 (functional safety), IEC 61508 (industrial), FCC/CE (regulatory).
- ✅ **Common Interview Question:** "What is the difference between verification and validation?" — *Verification = Are we building the product right? Validation = Are we building the right product?*
- ✅ **Time-to-Market:** Integration phase typically consumes **40–60%** of total project effort in commercial embedded systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formalisms for System Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Formalisms for System Design # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are "Formalisms" in System Design? In **Embedded System Design**, a *formalism* is a precise, mathematical (or semi-mathematical) notation used to **describe, analyze, simulate, and verify** the behavior and structure of a system **before** any hardware is fabricated or code is committed to ROM. KTU 2024 Scheme treats formalisms as the *contract* between specification and implementation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Formalisms for System Design* are abstract models — State Machines, Dataflow Graphs, Petri Nets, Process Networks, and Discrete-Event Systems — that capture the functional, temporal, and concurrent behavior of an embedded system in an unambiguous, executable form. ## 1.2 Why Are Formalisms Needed? (The "Blue-Print" Analogy) Imagine you ask a mason to build a house. Without a **blueprint**, the mason *might* build something — but it will be ad-hoc, full of ambiguity, and impossible to verify against your requirements. The blueprint is **not** the house; it is a *formal description* of the house. In embedded systems: | Without a Formalism | With a Formalism | |---|---| | Behavior described in English → ambiguous | Behavior in FSM / Petri Net → unambiguous | | Concurrency bugs discovered on hardware | Deadlock, livelock detected *pre-silicon* | | Hardware/software split decided late | Split is a *direct consequence* of model partitioning | | Validation = "compile and pray" | Validation = mathematical proof / reachability analysis | > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus groups formalisms into three families: **(1) Sequential Models** (State Machines), **(2) Concurrent / Communicating Models** (CSP, Process Networks, Petri Nets), and **(3) Data-Driven Models** (Dataflow Graphs, SDF). ## 1.3 The Five Pillars Covered in Module 1 1. **Finite State Machine (FSM)** — Mealy & Moore variants. 2. **Concurrent Process / Communicating Sequential Processes (CSP)** — Hoare's formalism. 3. **Dataflow Graph / Synchronous Data Flow (SDF)** — Kahn-style networks. 4. **Petri Net** — Bipartite graphs with tokens. 5. **Discrete Event System (DEVS)** — Event-driven hierarchical models. Each of these answers one of three design questions: - *What happens next?* → **FSM** - *When does it happen?* → **Discrete Event / Time-Triggered** - *How do parallel blocks cooperate?* → **Petri Net / CSP / Dataflow** # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Finite State Machine (FSM) — The Core Sequential Model ### 2.1.1 Formal Definition A deterministic FSM is the 5-tuple: $$ M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F) $$ where: - $Q$ — finite, non-empty **set of states**. - $\Sigma$ — finite, non-empty **input alphabet**. - $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ — **transition function** (total function for a *complete* FSM). - $q_0 \in Q$ — **initial state**. - $F \subseteq Q$ — set of **accepting / final states**. ### 2.1.2 Mealy vs. Moore — Output Logic | Aspect | **Mealy Machine** | **Moore Machine** | |---|---|---| | Output depends on | Current state **and** current input | Current state **only** | | Output function | $\lambda : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow \Gamma$ | $\lambda : Q \rightarrow \Gamma$ | | Latency | Output available *immediately* with input change | Output lags by one clock | | States required for same function | Fewer | Generally more | | Glitch-sensitivity | Higher (combinational path) | Lower (registered) | | Typical KTU use | UART receivers, traffic controllers | Watchdog timers, elevator controllers | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Theorem (KTU 2024 — High Weightage):** *Every Mealy machine can be converted to an equivalent Moore machine, and vice versa, by a deterministic state-splitting procedure.* ## 2.2 Concurrent Process Model & CSP A **concurrent process** is an independent sequential unit that communicates with its peers through **channels**. CSP (Hoare, 1978) formalizes this with primitives: $$ P \;::=\; \text{STOP} \mid a \rightarrow P \mid P \;\|\; Q \mid P \;[\;c \mid c'\;]\; Q \mid b ?x \rightarrow P \mid b !e \rightarrow P $$ Where: - $a \rightarrow P$ — prefix: perform event $a$, then behave as $P$. - $P \| Q$ — **interleaving** parallelism. - $P [\,c \mid c'\,] Q$ — parallel composition with channel renaming on $c$. - $b ?x$ — receive $x$ on channel $b$. - $b !e$ — send expression $e$ on channel $b$. The **trace semantics** of a process $P$ is the set: $$ traces(P) = \{\, \sigma \in \Sigma^{*} : P \xrightarrow{\sigma} P' \,\} $$ ## 2.3 Dataflow Graph & Synchronous Data Flow (SDF) A dataflow graph is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where: - $V$ = *actors* (computational kernels). - $E \subseteq V \times V$ = *edges* carrying data tokens. In **SDF**, each actor $v$ consumes and produces a *fixed, statically known* number of tokens per firing: $$ \text{cons}(v, e) \in \mathbb{N}, \qquad \text{prod}(v, e) \in \mathbb{N} $$ A **repetition vector** $q \in \mathbb{N}^{|V|}$ satisfies the **balance equation**: $$ \text{For every edge } e = (u \rightarrow v): \quad q(u) \cdot \text{prod}(u, e) \;=\; q(v) \cdot \text{cons}(v, e) $$ If a non-trivial integer solution $q > 0$ exists, the SDF graph is **consistent** and can be scheduled infinitely without buffer overflow. ## 2.4 Petri Net A Petri Net is the 4-tuple (or 5-tuple with markings): $$ N = (P, T, F, M_0) $$ - $P$ — finite set of **places** (drawn as circles, model *conditions*). - $T$ — finite set of **transitions** (drawn as bars, model *events*). - $F \subseteq (P \times T) \cup (T \times P)$ — **flow relation** (directed arcs). - $M_0 : P \rightarrow \mathbb{N}_0$ — **initial marking** (token distribution). A transition $t \in T$ is **enabled** under marking $M$ iff: $$ \forall p \in \,^{\bullet}t : \; M(p) \;\geq\; 1 $$ On firing, the new marking is $M'$: $$ M'(p) = M(p) - W(p,t) + W(t,p) \quad \text{for all } p \in P $$ where $W$ is the arc-weight function ($W = 1$ for ordinary nets). ### 2.4.1 Key Behavioral Properties (KTU Favourite!) | Property | Definition | Why It Matters | |---|---|---| | **Reachability** | $\exists$ sequence $\sigma : M_0 \xrightarrow{\sigma} M$ | Verifies a desired state is achievable | | **Liveness** | Every transition can fire from *some* reachable marking | No "dead" code paths | | **Boundedness** | $\exists k : \forall M \in [M_0\rangle, M(p) \leq k \; \forall p$ | Guarantees finite memory | | **Deadlock-freeness** | $\forall M \in [M_0\rangle, \exists t \in T$ enabled | No system hang | ## 2.5 Discrete Event System (DEVS) DEVS separates **state changes (events)** from **continuous time** and is hierarchical. A primitive DEVS atom: $$ DEVS = (X, Y, S, \delta_{int}, \delta_{ext}, \lambda, t_a) $$ - $X$ — input event set. - $Y$ — output event set. - $S$ — state set. - $\delta_{int} : S \rightarrow S$ — **internal** transition (no input event). - $\delta_{ext} : Q \times X \rightarrow S$ — **external** transition on input, where $Q = \{ (s, e) \mid s \in S, \; 0 \le e < t_a(s) \}$ is the *elapsed-time* state. - $\lambda : S \rightarrow Y$ — output function. - $t_a : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{0,\infty}$ — time-advance function. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat-Sheet | # | Formula / Definition | Symbol / Domain | Used In | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | FSM tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | $Q$ finite, $\Sigma$ finite | Sequential modeling | | 2 | Mealy output $\lambda(s, a)$ | $Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow \Gamma$ | Reactive controllers | | 3 | Moore output $\lambda(s)$ | $Q \rightarrow \Gamma$ | Glitch-free controllers | | 4 | CSP trace set $traces(P)$ | $\Sigma^{*}$ | Concurrency proofs | | 5 | SDF balance $q(u)\!\cdot\!\text{prod}(u,e) = q(v)\!\cdot\!\text{cons}(v,e)$ | $\mathbb{N}^{|V|}$ | Throughput analysis | | 6 | Petri enabled $\forall p \in {^{\bullet}t}, M(p) \geq 1$ | $\mathbb{N}_0^{|P|}$ | Concurrency / shared resources | | 7 | Petri firing $M'(p) = M(p) - W(p,t) + W(t,p)$ | $\mathbb{N}_0^{|P|}$ | State evolution | | 8 | DEVS elapsed-time $0 \le e < t_a(s)$ | $\mathbb{R}_{0,\infty}$ | Real-time simulation | | 9 | State count bound (Moore from Mealy) $\vert Q_{Moore} \vert \le \vert Q_{Mealy} \vert \cdot \vert \Gamma \vert$ | Cardinality | Model conversion | | 10 | Reachability set $\vert M_0 \rangle$ | $\mathcal{P}(M)$ | Model checking | > [!NOTE] > Notice the recurring theme: **tokens / states / events are the three mathematical atoms** of all five formalisms. KTU questions often test the *isomorphism* between them. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive ECUs (Bosch, Continental):** Designed in EAST-ADL → internally modeled as timed automata + dataflow. - **Avionics (DO-178C, ARINC 653):** Architecture modeled as communicating sequential processes mapped to time/space partitions. - **DSP & SDR:** Synchronous Data Flow graphs are the *de-facto* specification in GNU Radio. - **PLC / Industrial Automation:** IEC 61131-3 *SFC* (Sequential Function Chart) is essentially a Petri Net notation. - **Network-on-Chip (NoC) verification:** Petri nets model arbitration to prove liveness of the bus. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section converts the abstract theory above into **solved KTU-style analytical problems** with full algebraic working. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mealy-to-Moore Conversion (Full KTU 14-Mark Pattern) ### Statement Given a Mealy machine $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, \lambda, q_0)$ construct the equivalent Moore machine $M' = (Q', \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta', \lambda', q_0')$. ### Step-by-Step Construction **Step 1 — Partition the output alphabet.** For each Mealy state $q \in Q$, collect the set of outputs that can be generated on *outgoing* transitions: $$ \text{Out}(q) \;=\; \bigl\{\, \lambda(q, a) \;\big\vert\; a \in \Sigma,\; \delta(q,a) \text{ defined} \,\bigr\} $$ **Step 2 — Split each state.** Replace every $q$ by one Moore state per distinct outgoing output: $$ Q' \;=\; \bigl\{\, (q, \gamma) \;\big\vert\; q \in Q,\; \gamma \in \text{Out}(q) \,\bigr\} $$ **Step 3 — Define Moore output.** The output of a Moore state is its second coordinate: $$ \lambda'((q, \gamma)) \;=\; \gamma $$ **Step 4 — Define Moore transition.** For input $a$ in the original Mealy machine, look up the next state $q' = \delta(q,a)$ and its associated output $\lambda(q,a) = \gamma$: $$ \delta'((q, \gamma), a) \;=\; (\delta(q,a),\; \lambda(\delta(q,a),\, a)) $$ **Step 5 — Initial state.** The initial state of the Moore machine corresponds to $q_0$ with the output of its first transition: $$ q_0' \;=\; (q_0,\; \lambda(q_0, a_0)) \quad \text{where } a_0 \text{ is the first input symbol} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example Consider the Mealy machine with two states $\{A, B\}$, $\Sigma = \{0,1\}$, $\Gamma = \{x, y\}$: | Current | Input 0 | Input 1 | |---|---|---| | A | B / x | A / y | | B | A / y | B / x | Apply the construction: - $\text{Out}(A) = \{x, y\}$, $\text{Out}(B) = \{y, x\} = \{x, y\}$. - $Q' = \{(A,x), (A,y), (B,x), (B,y)\}$. - $\delta'((A,x), 0) = (B, x)$ because $\delta(A,0) = B$ and $\lambda(B,0) = y$ — wait, recheck: $\lambda(A,0) = x$, so transition on 0 produces $x$ *now*, but the destination state $B$ on 0 will produce $y$ on its *own* next 0-input. The Moore destination is therefore $(B, y)$: $$ \delta'((A,x), 0) = (\delta(A,0), \lambda(\delta(A,0), 0)) = (B, y) $$ - Continuing the full table: | Moore State | Out $\lambda'$ | $\delta'$ on 0 | $\delta'$ on 1 | |---|---|---|---| | (A, x) | x | (B, y) | (A, y) | | (A, y) | y | (B, y) | (A, y) | | (B, x) | x | (A, y) | (B, y) | | (B, y) | y | (A, y) | (B, y) | ### State Reduction Step Notice (A, y) and (B, y) are equivalent (same outputs, same transitions). Merging yields the **minimal** Moore machine with **3 states**: $\{(A,x), (A,y), (B,x)\}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > The bound $\vert Q_{Moore} \vert \le \vert Q_{Mealy} \vert \cdot \vert \Gamma \vert$ from §2.6 is now visible: $2 \times 2 = 4$ possible Moore states, reduced to 3 after minimisation. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SDF Repetition Vector (Matrix Form) For an SDF graph, the balance equations are linear. Write them in matrix form. Let $\Gamma(v, e)$ be the *production matrix* and $\Psi(v, e)$ the *consumption matrix*. The balance equation for edge $e = (u \to v)$: $$ \Gamma(u, e) \cdot q(u) - \Psi(v, e) \cdot q(v) \;=\; 0 $$ Stacking all edges into the **topology matrix** $T \in \mathbb{Z}^{|E| \times |V|}$: $$ T \cdot q \;=\; 0 \quad \in \mathbb{N}^{|E|} $$ The repetition vector is the **kernel** (null space) of $T$ restricted to the positive orthant. ### Worked Example A 2-actor SDF graph: actor 1 → 2 (prod=2, cons=1), actor 2 → 1 (prod=3, cons=1). $$ T = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -3 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T \cdot q = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 2q_1 = q_2,\; 3q_1 = q_2 $$ The two equations are inconsistent — wait, this means **no non-trivial solution exists** and the SDF is *inconsistent* (which is correct for prod=2,cons=1 vs prod=3,cons=1: the rates don't balance). Fix the second edge to prod=2 instead: $$ T = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;\Rightarrow\; 2q_1 = q_2 $$ Choosing the **smallest integer** $q_1 = 1$ gives $q = [1, 2]^T$ — actor 2 must fire **twice** for every firing of actor 1. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Petri Net Reachability Tree (Coverability) For an unbounded Petri net, we build the **coverability tree**: 1. Initial marking $M_0$ is the root. 2. Apply each *enabled* transition; record the new marking as a child. 3. If a new marking $M'$ has a component strictly greater than its ancestor $M$ (component-wise), replace that component with $\omega$ (the *unbounded* symbol). 4. Stop when a marking has already appeared (cycle detected) or is a duplicate of an $\omega$-marked ancestor. ### Worked Mini-Example Net with one place $p_1$, one transition $t_1$ (consumes 1 from $p_1$, produces 1 to $p_1$), initial marking $M_0 = (1)$. - $t_1$ is enabled; firing gives $M_1 = (1)$ — **same as root**, so we mark the node as a *self-loop* and stop. - Net is **bounded** by 1, **live** (transition can always fire), **deadlock-free**. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What? | Design Question | Recommended Formalism | KTU Justification | |---|---|---| | "Is the controller reactive to inputs?" | **Mealy FSM** | Output depends on input | | "Must output be glitch-free / registered?" | **Moore FSM** | Output only on state change | | "How do concurrent tasks synchronize?" | **Petri Net** | Native notion of place, token, condition | | "How to model a DSP pipeline?" | **SDF Graph** | Static token rates, schedulable | | "How to verify no deadlock in comm channels?" | **CSP** | Process algebra, trace equivalence | | "Real-time event-driven simulation?" | **DEVS** | Explicit time advance, hierarchical | | "Mixed control + dataflow?" | **Hybrid** (FSM + SDF) | Used in industrial EAST-ADL | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Formalism Choice The following Mermaid diagram captures the **decision flow** an embedded designer follows when picking a formalism — this is exactly the question KTU 2024 poses in 14-mark problems. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: New Embedded Design] --> B{Is behavior purely sequential?} B -- Yes --> C{Does output depend on input?} B -- No --> D{Is interaction concurrent?} C -- Yes --> E[Mealy FSM] C -- No --> F[Moore FSM] D -- Yes --> G{Static or dynamic data rates?} G -- Static --> H[SDF Dataflow Graph] G -- Dynamic --> I[Kahn Process Network] D -- No --> J{Is timing critical?} J -- Yes --> K[Timed Petri Net / DEVS] J -- No --> L[Ordinary Petri Net] E --> M[Convert to Moore if glitch-sensitive] F --> N[Minimize using equivalence partitioning] H --> O[Solve balance equation for repetition vector] I --> P[Verify deadlock-freeness via Kahn's theorem] K --> Q[Compute coverability tree] L --> R[Check liveness and boundedness] ``` ## 4.2 Generic FSM State Diagram (Worked Example) A **car-anti-theft alarm** in Mealy style — the canonical KTU textbook example. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Disarmed Disarmed --> Armed : key_lock / silent Armed --> Disarmed : key_unlock / beep Armed --> Triggered : motion / siren_on Triggered --> Disarmed : key_unlock / beep_long Triggered --> Armed : timer_30s / silent ``` Notice every transition is annotated `event / output` — the **Mealy signature**. ## 4.3 Petri Net Schematic — Producer / Consumer (Bounded Buffer) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Producer ready
3 tokens] -- in --> T1 T1 -- out --> P2[Buffer slot
capacity 2] P2 -- in --> T2 T2 -- out --> P3[Consumer ready
0 tokens] style P1 fill:#f9f,stroke:#333 style P2 fill:#bbf,stroke:#333 style P3 fill:#bfb,stroke:#333 ``` Interpretation: - **$P_1$** holds 3 tokens: producer can fire 3 times before blocking. - **$P_2$** holds the free buffer slots (initially 2). $T_1$ needs both a token in $P_1$ *and* a free slot in $P_2$. - **$P_3$** accumulates "consumed" items. - This net is **bounded, live, and deadlock-free**. ## 4.4 SDF Graph — 3-Stage DSP Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Actor A
FFT 64-pt] -- 4 tokens --> B[Actor B
Multiply by window] B -- 2 tokens --> C[Actor C
Magnitude] C -- 1 token --> A A -- 1 token --> C ``` Balance equations (selecting the cycle A→B→C→A and the bypass A→C): - A→B: $q_A \cdot 4 = q_B \cdot 1 \Rightarrow q_B = 4q_A$ - B→C: $q_B \cdot 2 = q_C \cdot 1 \Rightarrow q_C = 2q_B = 8q_A$ - C→A: $q_C \cdot 1 = q_A \cdot 1 \Rightarrow q_C = q_A$ — **conflict!** The bypass and the A→B→C→A cycle give contradictory rates. Resolving requires **buffering edges** or splitting the bypass into a separate graph. This is a typical KTU question on SDF consistency. ## 4.5 DEVS Hierarchical Block — Weather Station Sensor ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CoupledDEVS[WeatherStation Coupled Model] direction TB T[TempSensor DEVS
ta = 5s] --> Aggregator P[PressureSensor DEVS
ta = 10s] --> Aggregator H[HumiditySensor DEVS
ta = 30s] --> Aggregator end Aggregator --> Radio[RadioTransmitter DEVS
ta = 60s] Aggregator --> LCD[LCDDisplay DEVS
ta = 1s] ``` The **coupled DEVS** wraps three primitives with different time advances and routes their output events to a multiplexer. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Differentiate between Mealy and Moore machines. Mention one advantage of each. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Mealy | Moore | |---|---|---| | Output depends on | State **and** input | State **only** | | Output function | $\lambda : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow \Gamma$ | $\lambda : Q \rightarrow \Gamma$ | | Number of states | Generally fewer | Generally more | | Output timing | Combinational, immediate | Registered, one-cycle delay | | Glitch sensitivity | Higher | Lower | - *Mealy advantage:* Fewer states → simpler hardware. - *Moore advantage:* Output is glitch-free → preferred in safety-critical controllers (e.g., airbag ECU). *(3 marks: definition 1, table 1, advantage 1)* ### Q2. Define a Petri Net. What is the significance of "liveness" in a Petri Net model? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **Model Answer:** A **Petri Net** is the 4-tuple $N = (P, T, F, M_0)$ where $P$ is a set of *places*, $T$ a set of *transitions*, $F \subseteq (P \times T) \cup (T \times P)$ the *flow relation*, and $M_0$ the *initial marking* assigning tokens to places. **Liveness** means that from *every* reachable marking, every transition $t \in T$ is *potentially* fireable through some sequence. A live Petri Net guarantees that no part of the modeled system becomes permanently "stuck" — a critical property for embedded controllers (e.g., a real-time scheduler where every task must eventually get CPU time). *(3 marks: tuple 1, marking 1, liveness with significance 1)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Module 1):** Students often *describe* formalisms in plain English but **forget to draw the corresponding state diagram / Petri net / SDF graph**. KTU awards 4–5 marks purely for the *diagram*. Always include a labeled diagram with **states, transitions, inputs, and outputs explicitly marked**. --- ### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Q. (a)** Design a Mealy FSM for a 2-bit up-down counter that counts in the sequence 00 → 01 → 10 → 11 → 00 when the input $X = 1$ and reverse counts 00 → 11 → 10 → 01 → 00 when $X = 0$. Output $Z$ should be 1 when the present state is 11 (up) or 00 (down). Draw the state diagram and state table. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** Convert the above Mealy machine into an equivalent Moore machine. Show the conversion steps and the resulting state table. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Identify states.** The four count values are the states: $S_0 = 00$, $S_1 = 01$, $S_2 = 10$, $S_3 = 11$. **Step 2 — State Table.** | Present State | $X = 0$ (Down) | $X = 1$ (Up) | |---|---|---| | 00 | 11 / $Z=1$ | 01 / $Z=0$ | | 01 | 00 / $Z=0$ | 10 / $Z=0$ | | 10 | 01 / $Z=0$ | 11 / $Z=0$ | | 11 | 10 / $Z=0$ | 00 / $Z=1$ | **Step 3 — State Diagram.** ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> S0_00 S0_00 : S0 (00) S1_01 : S1 (01) S2_10 : S2 (10) S3_11 : S3 (11) S0_00 -- 0/Z=1 --> S3_11 S0_00 -- 1/Z=0 --> S1_01 S1_01 -- 0/Z=0 --> S0_00 S1_01 -- 1/Z=0 --> S2_10 S2_10 -- 0/Z=0 --> S1_01 S2_10 -- 1/Z=0 --> S3_11 S3_11 -- 0/Z=0 --> S2_10 S3_11 -- 1/Z=1 --> S0_00 ``` #### Model Solution — Part (b) Apply the Mealy → Moore procedure of §3.1. **Step 1 — Output sets per state.** - $\text{Out}(S_0) = \{1, 0\}$ - $\text{Out}(S_1) = \{0\}$ - $\text{Out}(S_2) = \{0\}$ - $\text{Out}(S_3) = \{0, 1\}$ **Step 2 — Split states.** $S_1, S_2$ need not split. $S_0$ becomes $\{S_{0a}, S_{0b}\}$, $S_3$ becomes $\{S_{3a}, S_{3b}\}$. | Moore State | Output $\lambda'$ | $\delta'$ on 0 | $\delta'$ on 1 | |---|---|---|---| | $S_{0a}$ (1) | 1 | $S_{3b}$ | $S_{1}$ | | $S_{0b}$ (0) | 0 | $S_{3b}$ | $S_{1}$ | | $S_1$ (0) | 0 | $S_{0a}$ | $S_2$ | | $S_2$ (0) | 0 | $S_1$ | $S_{3a}$ | | $S_{3a}$ (0) | 0 | $S_2$ | $S_{0a}$ | | $S_{3b}$ (1) | 1 | $S_2$ | $S_{0a}$ | **Step 3 — Equivalence reduction.** $S_1$ and $S_2$ both output 0, both go to $\{S_{0a}, S_3\}$ on inputs 0,1 in the same pattern — they are equivalent. Merge → $S_{12}$. **Final Minimal Moore Machine:** 4 states $\{S_{0a}, S_{0b}, S_{12}, S_{3a}\}$, $\vert Q_{Moore}^{min} \vert = 4 \le \vert Q_{Mealy} \vert \cdot \vert \Gamma \vert = 4 \times 2 = 8$. ✓ *(Award 7 marks: state table 2, diagram 2, conversion logic 2, minimisation 1)* --- ### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Q. (a)** Define a Petri Net. For the Petri Net shown below, determine: - (i) The initial marking - (ii) All enabled transitions at the initial marking - (iii) The reachability set starting from the initial marking (show all reachable markings). ```mermaid flowchart LR P1((P1
1 token)) -- 1 --> T1[T1] T1 -- 1 --> P2((P2
0 tokens)) T1 -- 1 --> P3((P3
2 tokens)) P3 -- 1 --> T2[T2] T2 -- 1 --> P1 P2 -- 1 --> T3[T3] T3 -- 1 --> P4((P4
0 tokens)) ``` **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** Explain the **Synchronous Data Flow (SDF)** model. Given the SDF graph below, compute the repetition vector and comment on consistency. **(7 marks)** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Actor A] -- 3 tokens --> B[Actor B] B -- 2 tokens --> C[Actor C] C -- 1 token --> A ``` #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Petri Net definition** (as in Q2 of Part A). **Step 2 — Initial marking.** $$ M_0 = (M(P_1), M(P_2), M(P_3), M(P_4)) = (1, 0, 2, 0) $$ **Step 3 — Enabled transitions.** A transition $t$ is enabled iff every input place has at least one token. - $T_1$ needs $P_1 \geq 1$: satisfied. **Enabled**. - $T_2$ needs $P_3 \geq 1$: satisfied (has 2). **Enabled**. - $T_3$ needs $P_2 \geq 1$: **not** satisfied (0). Disabled. **Step 4 — Reachability.** - Fire $T_1$ → $M_1 = (0, 1, 3, 0)$. Now $T_3$ is enabled, $T_2$ still enabled, $T_1$ disabled. - Fire $T_2$ from $M_0$ → $M_2 = (2, 0, 1, 0)$. $T_1$ enabled, $T_2$ enabled. - Fire $T_1$ from $M_1$ → $M_3 = (0, 2, 4, 0)$. $T_2, T_3$ enabled. - Continue: $T_3$ from $M_1$ → $M_4 = (0, 0, 3, 1)$. Now only $T_2$ enabled. - Fire $T_2$ from $M_4$ → $M_5 = (1, 0, 2, 1) = M_0 \cup \{P_4\}$. The net is **unbounded in $P_3$** (tokens grow by 1 each cycle through $T_1$). The full reachability set is: $$ [M_0\rangle = \{(1, 0, 2+2k, k) \mid k \geq 0\} \cup \{(0, j, 2+2k, k) \mid j \geq 1, k \geq 0\} $$ which is **infinite** — the net is **unbounded**, **live** (every transition can fire infinitely often), but contains an **unbounded place** $P_3$. *(7 marks: definition 1, $M_0$ 1, enabled 1, reachability 3, comment 1)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — SDF model explanation.** A Synchronous Data Flow graph is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where each actor $v$ consumes a *fixed* number $\text{cons}(v, e)$ of tokens from each input edge $e$ and produces a *fixed* number $\text{prod}(v, e)$ of tokens to each output edge per firing. These rates are *constants*, allowing static analysis (scheduling, buffer sizing) before code generation. SDF is widely used in DSP and streaming applications. **Step 2 — Balance equations.** - Edge $A \to B$: $\text{prod}(A, AB) = 3$, $\text{cons}(B, AB) = 1$. Equation: $3 q_A = 1 q_B \Rightarrow q_B = 3 q_A$. - Edge $B \to C$: $\text{prod}(B, BC) = 2$, $\text{cons}(C, BC) = 1$. Equation: $2 q_B = 1 q_C \Rightarrow q_C = 2 q_B = 6 q_A$. - Edge $C \to A$: $\text{prod}(C, CA) = 1$, $\text{cons}(A, CA) = 1$. Equation: $1 q_C = 1 q_A \Rightarrow q_C = q_A$. **Step 3 — Solve.** The first two equations give $q_C = 6 q_A$, the third gives $q_C = q_A$. These are **inconsistent** (no finite positive solution except $q_A = 0$). **Step 4 — Verdict.** The SDF graph is **inconsistent**. To fix it, either (i) change production/consumption rates, or (ii) add a *delay* edge (initial token) of appropriate value. *(7 marks: explanation 2, balance equations 2, solution 2, verdict 1)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings (Common Pitfalls) > [!WARNING] > 1. **Forgetting the diagram** — KTU awards 30–35% marks for the *graphical* representation. A textual state table alone loses 3–4 marks. > 2. **Confusing "reachability" with "coverability"** — reachability asks if a *specific* marking is reachable; coverability asks if an *upper bound* is exceeded. Petri Net answers differ. > 3. **Mixing Mealy and Moore notation** — once you commit to Mealy, outputs go on transitions; once you commit to Moore, outputs go on states.
