The five common examples studied in the KTU 2024 syllabus are: **(1) Digital Still Camera, (2) Smart Mobile Phone, (3) Automotive ABS/Engine Control Unit, (4) Washing Machine Controller, and (5) Smart Card / RFID Tag**. Each example is chosen to highlight specific design pillars — single-function vs multi-function, hard real-time vs soft real-time, networked vs isolated, and energy-constrained vs performance-constrained. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for the example list + 1 mark for the design-pillar mapping.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Module 1]**
*Identify the five main hardware subsystems in a typical digital camera design. State the role of each in one sentence.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The five main hardware subsystems of a digital camera are: **(1) Image Sensor (CMOS/CCD)** — captures the optical scene and converts photons into raw Bayer-pattern digital pixels; **(2) Image Signal Processor (ISP)** — performs demosaicing, white balancing, and noise reduction in hardware; **(3) Processor Core (MCU/SoC)** — executes the firmware, runs the user interface, and controls all peripherals via a system bus; **(4) Memory Subsystem (NOR Flash + SDRAM + SD Card)** — stores the firmware, holds the working frame buffer, and provides long-term image storage; **(5) Communication and I/O (USB, LCD, keypad, SDIO)** — provides the user interface and connectivity to external hosts. *(½ mark per subsystem = 2½ marks; + ½ mark for the integrated "bus" mention.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Choose ONE — 14 Marks)

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *Hardware-Software Partitioning and Numerical Design*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted from Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **digital still camera** as an embedded system. Clearly identify the roles of the **image sensor, ISP, DMA, processor, memory, RTOS, and storage**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A digital camera must capture **4000 × 3000 pixel** images at **12 bits/pixel** (raw Bayer), compress them with a JPEG encoder (assume **10:1 compression**), and write the result to an SD card within **1 second**. The processor runs at **200 MHz** with an average **CPI of 1.5**, and JPEG encoding requires **30 million instructions** per image. Compute:
  (i) the raw image size in MB,
  (ii) the JPEG file size in MB,
  (iii) the time taken to compress one image,
  (iv) the CPU utilisation, and
  (v) the required DMA bandwidth (assume a 30 fps preview). *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Block Diagram (4 marks) + Role explanation (3 marks):**

> *(Draw the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.2 — "Image-Capture Data-Flow Inside a Digital Camera". A neatly hand-drawn version gets full 4 marks for diagram.)*

**Role of each block:**
- **Image Sensor (CMOS)** — converts the optical image into a stream of raw 12-bit Bayer pixels. *(½ mark)*
- **ISP** — performs on-the-fly image processing (demosaic, noise reduction, gamma, white balance) to convert Bayer into a viewable YUV/RGB image. *(½ mark)*
- **DMA** — moves the large pixel stream from the sensor/ISP into the DRAM frame buffer **without CPU intervention**, freeing the CPU for compression and UI. *(½ mark)*
- **Processor (MCU/SoC)** — runs the RTOS, executes JPEG encoding, file-system writes, and user-interface logic. *(½ mark)*
- **Memory** — NOR Flash holds the firmware; SDRAM holds the frame buffer; SD card stores compressed images. *(½ mark)*
- **RTOS** — schedules the four concurrent tasks (preview, capture, compression, storage) with hard real-time deadlines. *(½ mark)*
- **Storage (SD card)** — long-term, removable, FAT32-formatted bulk storage for the JPEG files. *(½ mark)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Raw image size (2 marks):**

$$
M_{raw} = 4000 \times 3000 \times 12 \;\text{bits} = 1.44 \times 10^{8} \;\text{bits} = 18 \;\text{MB}
$$

**(ii) JPEG file size (1 mark):**

$$
M_{JPEG} = \dfrac{18}{10} = 1.8 \;\text{MB}
$$

**(iii) Compression time (1.5 marks):**

$$
t_{exec} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^{6} \times 1.5}{200 \times 10^{6}} = 0.225 \;\text{s} = 225 \;\text{ms}
$$

**(iv) CPU utilisation (1 mark):**

$$
U = \dfrac{0.225}{1.0} \times 100\% = 22.5\%
$$

**(v) DMA bandwidth (1.5 marks):**

$$
BW = \dfrac{18 \;\text{MB}}{33 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{s}} \approx 545 \;\text{MB/s}
$$

> **Conclusion (½ mark):** The design easily meets the 1-second deadline; the CPU has 77.5% spare bandwidth to handle USB, UI, and file system. A 200 MHz Cortex-M4 class processor is sufficient; a hardware JPEG block is *not* mandatory.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Comparison and Design Justification*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **digital camera** and **smart mobile phone** as embedded system design examples under the following heads: processor class, operating system, memory, power budget, real-time constraints, and connectivity. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Justify, with quantitative reasoning, why **JPEG encoding should be implemented in hardware** for a mid-range DSC but **in software (or as a hybrid)** for a high-end smart phone. Support your argument with one numeric example for each case. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per row + 0.5 for the concluding remark):**

| Design Head | Digital Camera | Smart Phone |
|-------------|----------------|-------------|
| Processor | Cortex-M4 single core, 100 – 200 MHz | Cortex-A multi-core, 1.5 – 3 GHz |
| OS / RTOS | Bare-metal or FreeRTOS (single function) | Android / iOS / Linux (multitasking) |
| Memory | 64 MB SDRAM, 8 – 32 GB SD | 4 – 16 GB LPDDR5, 128 GB – 1 TB UFS |
| Power | 1 – 3 W, optimised for shots-per-charge | 0.5 – 5 W, optimised for thermals |
| Real-time | Hard (shutter-to-save < 500 ms) | Soft (UI < 16 ms = 60 Hz) |
| Connectivity | USB 2.0, optional Wi-Fi | Wi-Fi 6, 5G, BT, NFC, GPS, USB-C |

> **Concluding remark (½ mark):** The DSC is a *single-function, hard-real-time, energy-constrained* device, while the smart phone is a *multi-function, soft-real-time, general-purpose* device. This contrast is the single biggest design driver.

**(b) Hardware-vs-Software Justification (7 marks):**

**Mid-Range DSC case (3.5 marks):**
- The DSC has only one ARM Cortex-M4 running at 200 MHz, *no GPU*, *no NPU*.
- From Section 3.1, software JPEG takes 225 ms — this is acceptable for a single photo, but the camera must simultaneously run the **preview task at 30 fps** (33 ms per frame) and the **auto-focus task** (interrupt-driven, must complete in < 100 ms).
- A single-core software JPEG would *block* the preview, causing visible stutter on the LCD.
- A **hardware JPEG block** completes the encode in ~5 ms, leaving 220 ms of headroom for other tasks and **dropping CPU utilisation from 22.5% (compressing) to 0%**.
- The **+1 USD silicon cost** is justified because the alternative is a *slower, multi-core MCU costing +3 USD*.

**High-End Smart Phone case (3.5 marks):**
- The phone has a 2.8 GHz octa-core CPU, a GPU, an NPU, and 8 GB of LPDDR5 — total memory bandwidth > 50 GB/s.
- JPEG encoding on one CPU core completes in ~5 ms — equal to the hardware block — *but software is free, updatable over-the-air, and supports multiple codecs (HEIF, AVIF, RAW)*.
- The 1 USD "saved" silicon area is better used for an **NPU** that runs AI scene detection, which a hardware JPEG cannot do.
- Therefore, **software (or hybrid NEON-accelerated)** JPEG is the right choice.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. *Do not* just write "hardware is faster" — KTU expects you to **show the numbers** (225 ms vs 5 ms) and **justify the silicon cost trade-off** (1 USD vs 3 USD).
> 2. *Do not* confuse **JPEG hardware block** with **JPEG accelerator software library** (e.g., libjpeg-turbo). The former is *silicon*, the latter is *optimised code running on the CPU*.
> 3. *Do not* forget to mention the **memory bandwidth** constraint. Software JPEG reads and writes the frame buffer repeatedly; a 540 MB/s DMA stream is mandatory.
> 4. *Do not* state the formula for compression time without converting units. KTU strictly penalises unit mismatches (e.g., writing MHz × instructions = seconds without a $\times 10^{6}$ factor).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] An **embedded system design example** is a real product (camera, phone, ECU) used to teach the *co-design* of hardware and software.
- [x] The **five universal blocks** are: Processor, Memory, I/O, Communication, Power/Clock/Reset.
- [x] The **three buses** are: Address, Data, Control.
- [x] The **five design pillars** are: Speed, Memory, Power, Latency, Cost.
- [x] A **12 MP raw image** is **18 MB**; a **JPEG** is **~1.8 MB** (10:1 ratio).
- [x] The **sensor data rate** for 1080p @ 30 fps is **~93 MB/s**; for 4K @ 30 fps it is **~373 MB/s**.
- [x] The **JPEG compression time** on a 200 MHz / CPI 1.5 CPU is **225 ms** for a 12 MP image.
- [x] **Hardware JPEG** is chosen for hard real-time, single-function devices (mid-range DSC).
- [x] **Software JPEG** is chosen for flexible, multi-function devices (smart phone with spare CPU cycles).
- [x] **DMA bandwidth** must be computed using $BW = F_{size} / t_{transfer}$.
- [x] **CPU utilisation** in an RTOS is $U = t_{exec} / T_{deadline} \times 100\%$.
- [x] The **DSC vs Phone** matrix is the most-asked 7-mark comparison — memorise it.
- [x] **RTOS** is needed only when multiple tasks with deadlines must share one CPU; bare-metal suffices for one task.
- [x] **MIPI CSI-2** is the standard high-speed image-sensor interface; **SDIO** is the standard SD-card interface.
- [x] **Boot sequence**: Boot ROM → Bootloader → Main firmware in Flash → Execute in SDRAM.
- [x] **Power consumption** of CMOS logic is $P = \alpha C V^{2} f$ — reducing $V$ is the most effective lever.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Mnemonic — "SMILE-P":**
> **S**ensors → **M**emory → **I**/O → **L**ogic (CPU) → **E**xecution (RTOS) → **P**ower. If you remember SMILE-P, you can draw the block diagram of *any* embedded system design example in the KTU exam, even if the product is unfamiliar.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded Product Development Cycle (EDLC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Embedded Product Development Cycle (EDLC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Embedded Product Development Life Cycle (EDLC)** is a structured, multi-phase engineering framework that governs the conceptualization, design, implementation, validation, deployment, and end-of-life management of an embedded computing product. It integrates **hardware-software co-design**, **firmware development**, **verification & validation (V&V)**, and **lifecycle maintenance** within an iterative engineering workflow aligned to the constraints of time-to-market, cost, reliability, and certification.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, EDLC is treated as a **process-management model** that explicitly accounts for the **co-existence of hardware (HW), software (SW), and firmware (FW)** on a single PCB/platform. Unlike generic software engineering life cycles, EDLC demands **simultaneous progress tracking** on mechanical, electronic, and algorithmic tracks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine you are opening a **cloud-kitchen restaurant**:
- **Requirements Phase** — The menu is decided (features, cost limits, delivery time).
- **Architecture Phase** — The kitchen layout is planned (where stoves, fridges, packaging stations go).
- **HW/SW Partitioning** — Deciding which tasks are done by the chef (hardware/firmware) and which by the order-management app (high-level software).
- **Implementation** — Actually cooking and packing orders.
- **Testing** — Tasting the food, checking hygiene, getting customer feedback.
- **Deployment** — Orders start rolling in.
- **Maintenance & Upgrades** — Adding new menu items, fixing bugs, replacing worn-out stoves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Just like a kitchen cannot be redesigned after the restaurant opens, an embedded product's architecture is the most critical decision — fixing architecture bugs AFTER deployment can cost 50×–200× more than fixing them at the design stage.** This is the heart of EDLC philosophy.

## 1.3 Why EDLC Differs from Generic SDLC

| Attribute | Generic SDLC | Embedded EDLC |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware | Optional/Separate | **Integral & Co-developed** |
| Real-time deadlines | Often soft | **Often hard (μs–ms deadlines)** |
| Memory constraints | Usually abundant | **Severe (kB to few MB)** |
| Power budget | Not critical | **Strict (mW to W)** |
| Debugging | Software debuggers | **JTAG, SWD, Logic Analyzers, Oscilloscopes** |
| Update model | Over-the-air patches | **Field Firmware Updates (FFU) with rollback** |
| Failure consequence | Often recoverable | **May cause safety hazard / liability** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** Embedded systems frequently operate in **safety-critical** domains (automotive ABS, medical ventilators, aviation). Hence the KTU 2024 syllabus mandates that the EDLC explicitly include **Verification & Validation (V&V)** as a separate, traceable phase.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Eight Canonical Phases of EDLC

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes the following sequential (yet iterative) phases:

### Phase 1 — Product Specification (Requirements Engineering)
- Capture **functional** and **non-functional** requirements.
- Use **Use-Case diagrams**, **User Stories**, and **EARS (Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax)** notation.
- Define constraints: power, cost, MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures), response latency, EMI/EMC limits.
- Output: **SRS (Software Requirement Specification)** + **HRS (Hardware Requirement Specification)**.

### Phase 2 — System Architecture & Hardware–Software Partitioning
- Decide what runs on **hardware (ASIC/FPGA)**, what runs on **firmware (bare-metal or RTOS)**, and what runs on **application software**.
- Apply **Co-Design principles** — joint optimization across HW/SW boundaries.
- Choose processor class: **MCU vs. MPU vs. DSP vs. SoC**.
- Output: **System Architecture Document (SAD)** + **Partitioning Table**.

### Phase 3 — Detailed Hardware & Software Design
- **Hardware:** Schematic capture, PCB layout, BOM optimization, signal integrity, power-tree design.
- **Software:** Module decomposition, RTOS task graph, state-machine design, data-flow design.
- Output: **Schematics, Gerbers, Software Design Document (SDD)**.

### Phase 4 — Implementation (HW Fabrication & SW Coding)
- PCB fabrication, soldering, prototype assembly.
- Firmware coding in C/C++/Assembly with version control (Git).
- Continuous integration (CI) with **unit tests** and **static analysis (e.g., MISRA-C)**.

### Phase 5 — Integration & Testing
- **Unit Testing** → **Integration Testing** → **System Testing** → **Acceptance Testing**.
- Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) testing.
- Compliance testing: **CE, FCC, RoHS, AEC-Q100 (automotive), IEC 60601 (medical)**.

### Phase 6 — Validation & Verification (V&V)
- **V-model traceability:** every requirement must link to a test case.
- Formal methods (model checking) for safety-critical systems.
- FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) + FTA (Fault Tree Analysis).

### Phase 7 — Deployment & Manufacturing
- Design for **Manufacturability (DFM)** and **Testability (DFT)**.
- Pilot production run → DVT (Design Validation Test) → PVT (Production Validation Test) → MP (Mass Production).
- Generate **production test fixtures** and **flash programmers**.

### Phase 8 — Maintenance, Upgrades & End-of-Life (EOL)
- **Bug fixes**, **security patches (CVE remediation)**, **feature upgrades**.
- **Obsolescence management** for components (e.g., when a key IC becomes EOL, re-spin needed).
- EOL: secure decommissioning, data wiping, recycling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Iterative nature:** In practice, EDLC is **not strictly linear**. Phases 1–4 are revisited during prototyping, and Phases 5–8 feed back into Phase 1 for the **next product generation (N+1)**.

## 2.2 EDLC Process Models (Variants)

### 2.2.1 Waterfall Model
Linear, phase-gated. Suitable for **well-understood, low-risk** products with frozen requirements. **Disadvantage:** late discovery of design errors.

### 2.2.2 Incremental / Staged Delivery
Product delivered in **increments** (e.g., v1.0 → v1.1 → v2.0). Each increment adds features.

### 2.2.3 Spiral Model (Boehm)
Combines waterfall + iterative risk analysis. **Best for high-risk embedded projects** (aerospace, medical).

### 2.2.4 V-Model (Verification & Validation Model) ⭐ *Most used in Embedded*
Extension of waterfall where **each development phase has a corresponding testing phase**:

| Development Phase (left arm) | ↔ | Verification Phase (right arm) |
|---|---|---|
| Requirements | ↔ | **Acceptance Testing** |
| System Design | ↔ | **System Testing** |
| Module Design | ↔ | **Integration Testing** |
| Coding | ↔ | **Unit Testing** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The **V-Model** is the most-tested model in KTU board exams for EDLC questions. Memorize the left-to-right traceability mapping above.

### 2.2.5 Agile / Scrum (Adapted for Embedded)
Use of **2-week sprints** with **continuous hardware-software integration**. Modern automotive (e.g., Tesla, Bosch) and IoT companies use a hybrid: **Agile firmware + waterfall hardware milestones** ("**Dual-Track Agile**").

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the key cost/risk equations frequently asked in KTU 2024 ESE questions on EDLC. **All equations use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table syntax.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Real-World Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defect Cost Multiplier (Boehm's Rule)** | $C_{fix} \approx 10^{\left(\log_{10}(N) + 1\right)}$ or empirically $C_{fix_{phase}} = k \cdot C_{fix_{design}}$ | $C$ = cost to fix; $N$ = phase index; $k$ = **10×–200×** | Justifies early testing. |
| 2 | **1–10–100 Rule (Gartner)** | Design phase = **\$1**, Build phase = **\$10**, Post-release = **\$100** | Cost per defect | Used in QA budgeting. |
| 3 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\sum t_{operating}}{N_{failures}}$ | $t$ = hours; $N$ = failure count | Reliability spec. |
| 4 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$ | MTTR = Mean Time To Repair | SLA definition. |
| 5 | **FITs (Failures In Time)** | $\text{FIT} = \dfrac{10^{9}}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Failures per $10^9$ device-hours | IC reliability metric. |
| 6 | **Code Coverage (Statement)** | $C_{stmt} = \dfrac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $S$ = statement count | Testing metric. |
| 7 | **Branch Coverage** | $C_{branch} = \dfrac{B_{taken}}{B_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $B$ = branch count | Testing metric. |
| 8 | **ROM/RAM Budget Check** | $S_{ROM} + S_{RAM} \leq S_{MCU}$ | Bytes | Resource partitioning. |
| 9 | **Power Budget (Avg)** | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} P_i \cdot t_i}{\sum_{i} t_i}$ | Watts, time (s) | Battery-life estimation. |
| 10 | **Schedule Slip Risk (PERT)** | $T_E = \dfrac{T_O + 4T_M + T_P}{6}$ | $T_O$=optimistic, $T_M$=most-likely, $T_P$=pessimistic | Project management. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Formula 2 (1–10–100 Rule) is the most-quoted KTU exam metric — it numerically justifies why EDLC mandates **early testing** rather than deferring it to the end.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Boehm's Defect Cost Multiplier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation shows **why embedded projects must invest in early-phase testing** — a favourite 7-mark KTU question.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

Let the cost to fix a defect at the **design phase** be $C_0$. Empirical data from large-scale embedded projects (Boehm, 1981; McConnell, 1996) shows that cost multiplier grows **exponentially** with each successive phase the defect survives:

$$
C_n = C_0 \cdot r^{\,n}
$$

where:
- $C_n$ = cost to fix a defect discovered at phase $n$
- $C_0$ = baseline cost at the design phase
- $r$ = empirical growth factor (typically $r = 10$ for critical embedded systems, $r = 5$ for desktop software)
- $n$ = phase index (0 = design, 1 = implementation, 2 = integration, 3 = system test, 4 = post-release)

**Step 1 — Substitute $n = 4$ (post-release):**

$$
C_4 = C_0 \cdot 10^{4} = 10\,000 \cdot C_0
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to multiples of design-phase cost:**

$$
\frac{C_4}{C_0} = 10^4 = 10\,000\times
$$

**Step 3 — Apply safety-critical correction factor** (for automotive DO-178C / IEC 61508 systems):

$$
C_{safety} = C_n \cdot \alpha, \quad \alpha \in [5, 20]
$$

**Step 4 — Final critical-system multiplier:**

$$
C_{critical,4} = C_0 \cdot 10^{4} \cdot \alpha
$$

For $\alpha = 20$:

$$
C_{critical,4} = 200\,000 \cdot C_0 = 200\,000\times
$$

This is the source of the famous **"200× rule"** for safety-critical embedded systems.

### 3.1.2 Numerical Worked Example

Suppose a defect costs **₹500** to fix at design stage. Calculate the cost of the same defect if it survives until **post-release** in a **medical device** ($\alpha = 15$).

**Step 1:** Identify the multiplier for post-release ($n = 4$).

$$
r^{n} = 10^{4} = 10\,000
$$

**Step 2:** Compute raw cost.

$$
C_4 = 500 \times 10\,000 = 5\,000\,000 \text{ (₹50 lakh)}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply safety multiplier.

$$
C_{safety,4} = 5\,000\,000 \times 15 = 75\,000\,000 \text{ (₹7.5 crore)}
$$

**Conclusion:** A defect that would cost **₹500** to fix at the design table becomes a **₹7.5 crore** liability if discovered post-release — including potential **FDA recall cost** and **legal damages**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** The 2014 Toyota unintended-acceleration recall was traced to **software defects in the ETCS (Electronic Throttle Control System)** that escaped early-phase testing. The recall cost was estimated at **\$3+ billion** — a textbook example of Boehm's multiplier in action.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — EDLC Phase vs. Activity Type

| EDLC Phase | HW Activity | SW Activity | Documentation Artifact | V&V Activity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Specification | Sensor/actuator list | Feature list | SRS, HRS | Requirement review |
| 2. Architecture | Block diagram, BOM | Task graph, OS choice | SAD, Partitioning table | Architecture review |
| 3. Detailed Design | Schematics, Layout | State machines, APIs | SDD, Schematic PDF | Design review (DR) |
| 4. Implementation | PCB fab, soldering | Code, unit tests | Git commits, CI logs | Static analysis, MISRA |
| 5. Integration | Bring-up, JTAG debug | Module integration | Integration test report | HIL testing |
| 6. V&V | EMC/EMI chamber | Functional tests | Traceability matrix | System acceptance test |
| 7. Deployment | Pilot run | Flash programming | Production test plan | DVT/PVT reports |
| 8. Maintenance | Field returns, RMA | OTA updates | ECN (Engineering Change Notice) | Regression testing |

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Process Models

| Model | Strength | Weakness | Best Fit for Embedded |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Waterfall** | Simple, predictable | Late error discovery | Small, well-defined projects |
| **Incremental** | Early value delivery | Architecture drift | Consumer electronics with roadmap |
| **Spiral** | Strong risk management | Expensive, slow | Aerospace, defence |
| **V-Model** | Strong V&V traceability | Rigid, not agile | Automotive (ISO 26262), Medical (IEC 62304) |
| **Agile/Scrum** | Adaptable, fast | Requires CI/CD maturity | IoT startups, modern automotive |
| **Dual-Track Agile** | Combines HW rigor + SW agility | Coordination overhead | Modern automotive (Bosch, Continental) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 EDLC Master Flow (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> P1Spec
    P1Spec : P1 Specification and Requirements
    P1Spec --> P2Arch : Approved SRS and HRS
    P2Arch : P2 System Architecture and HW SW Partition
    P2Arch --> P3Design : SAD Signed Off
    P3Design : P3 Detailed HW and SW Design
    P3Design --> P4Impl : Design Review Passed
    P4Impl : P4 Implementation and Prototyping
    P4Impl --> P5Integ : Prototype Built
    P5Integ : P5 Integration and Unit Testing
    P5Integ --> P6Val : Modules Integrated
    P6Val : P6 Validation and System Testing
    P6Val --> P7Deploy : Compliance Approved
    P7Deploy : P7 Deployment and Manufacturing
    P7Deploy --> P8Maint : Mass Production
    P8Maint : P8 Maintenance Upgrades EOL
    P8Maint --> [*]
    P4Impl --> P3Design : Design Error Found
    P5Integ --> P2Arch : Architecture Bug Found
    P6Val --> P5Integ : Integration Regression
    P8Maint --> P1Spec : Next Gen N plus 1
```

## 4.2 V-Model Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    req[Requirements] --> sys[System Design]
    sys --> mod[Module Design]
    mod --> code[Coding]
    code --> unit[Unit Testing]
    unit --> integ[Integration Testing]
    integ --> syst[System Testing]
    syst --> acc[Acceptance Testing]
    req -. traceability .-> acc
    sys -. traceability .-> syst
    mod -. traceability .-> integ
    code -. traceability .-> unit
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Dashed arrows represent **traceability links** — every test case on the right must be traceable back to a design artefact on the left. This is the essence of **ISO 26262** (automotive) and **IEC 62304** (medical) compliance.

## 4.3 HW–SW Co-Design Partitioning Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[System Requirement] --> decide{HW or SW Implementation}
    decide -->|Latency Critical, Parallelism| hw[Hardware Implementation]
    decide -->|Flexibility, Complex Logic| sw[Software Implementation]
    decide -->|Mixed, Reconfigurable| fpga[FPGA Implementation]
    hw --> integ[Integrated System]
    sw --> integ
    fpga --> integ
    integ --> test[Validation]
    test --> pass{Quality Acceptable}
    pass -->|Yes| release[Product Release]
    pass -->|No| decide
```

## 4.4 Risk-Reduction Iteration in Spiral Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    s0[Cycle 0 Concept] --> r1[Risk Analysis 1]
    r1 --> p1[Prototype 1]
    p1 --> e1[Evaluation 1]
    e1 --> s1[Cycle 1 Refined Requirements]
    s1 --> r2[Risk Analysis 2]
    r2 --> p2[Prototype 2]
    p2 --> e2[Evaluation 2]
    e2 --> s2[Cycle 2 Detailed Design]
    s2 --> r3[Risk Analysis 3]
    r3 --> p3[Prototype 3]
    p3 --> e3[Evaluation 3]
    e3 --> final[Final Product]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not confuse EDLC with generic SDLC** — always mention *hardware-software co-design* and *real-time constraints* at least once. Marks are cut for generic answers.
> 2. **In V-Model questions**, examiners expect the **exact 4-pair mapping** (Requirements↔Acceptance, etc.) — students lose 2–3 marks for a vague "left-right testing" answer.
> 3. **For "phases of EDLC"**, you must list **all eight phases** in order. Skipping "Maintenance & EOL" is a recurring deduction.
> 4. **Numerical/derivation questions** (Boehm's rule, MTBF, 1–10–100 rule) require **substituted numerical values with units** — partial credit is only given when units are shown.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Embedded Product Development Life Cycle (EDLC)". List any four major phases of EDLC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EDLC Definition (1.5 marks):** EDLC is a structured, multi-phase engineering framework that governs the design, development, testing, deployment, and maintenance of an embedded computing product, integrating hardware, firmware, and software co-design within constraints of cost, time, power, and reliability.

**Four major phases (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Product Specification & Requirements Engineering** — capture functional/non-functional requirements.
2. **System Architecture & HW–SW Partitioning** — decide hardware vs. software implementation.
3. **Implementation & Prototyping** — PCB fabrication, firmware coding, unit testing.
4. **Testing, Validation & Deployment** — integration testing, compliance, mass production.

*(Any four of the eight phases accepted; full marks only if "phases" are clearly named.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between the Waterfall model and the V-Model of embedded system development.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Waterfall (1.5 marks) | V-Model (1.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| Testing | Performed **only at the end** | **Parallel** to each development phase |
| Error detection | **Late**, expensive fixes | **Early**, due to traceability |
| Documentation | Sequential | **Strong bidirectional traceability** |
| Suitability | Simple, low-risk projects | **Safety-critical** (automotive, medical) |
| Feedback | Minimal | Strong, from test phases back to design |

---

## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions on EDLC carry an **internal choice**. Both alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) are provided below. Answer **either** Q-A **or** Q-B.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — V-Model & Boehm's Rule

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **V-Model** of embedded system development with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages over the waterfall model.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A defect costs **₹1,000** to fix at the design stage of an embedded project. Using **Boehm's defect cost multiplier rule (with growth factor $r = 10$)**, calculate the cost of fixing the same defect if it is detected at:
  (i) the **implementation** stage, and
  (ii) the **post-release** stage.
  (iii) Comment on the implications for an **automotive safety-critical** system with a safety multiplier of $\alpha = 12$.

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**V-Model Definition (1.5 marks):**
The V-Model is an extension of the waterfall model where each development phase on the left arm of the "V" is paired with a corresponding verification/validation phase on the right arm. It establishes a **bidirectional traceability** between design and testing.

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to V-Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2.)*

**Four Pairings (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. Requirements ↔ Acceptance Testing
2. System Design ↔ System Testing
3. Module Design ↔ Integration Testing
4. Coding ↔ Unit Testing

**Advantages over Waterfall (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Early defect detection** — testing is planned in parallel, not deferred.
2. **Strong traceability** — every requirement has a testable acceptance criterion (mandatory for ISO 26262, IEC 62304).
3. **Better risk management** — particularly suited to safety-critical embedded domains (automotive ABS, avionics, medical implants).

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $C_0 = 1\,000$, $r = 10$, $\alpha = 12$.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
C_n = C_0 \cdot r^{\,n}
$$

**Step 1 — Implementation stage ($n = 1$):** *(2 marks)*

$$
C_1 = 1\,000 \times 10^{1} = 1\,000 \times 10 = 10\,000
$$

$$
\boxed{C_1 = \text{₹}10\,000}
$$

**Step 2 — Post-release stage ($n = 4$):** *(2 marks)*

$$
C_4 = 1\,000 \times 10^{4} = 1\,000 \times 10\,000 = 10\,000\,000
$$

$$
\boxed{C_4 = \text{₹}1 \text{ crore} \;(10^{7})}
$$

**Step 3 — Safety-critical correction ($\alpha = 12$):** *(2 marks)*

$$
C_{safety,4} = 10\,000\,000 \times 12 = 120\,000\,000
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{safety,4} = \text{₹}12 \text{ crore}}
$$

**Conclusion (0 marks — included above):** A defect that costs **₹1,000** at design becomes a **₹12 crore** liability in an automotive safety-critical context. This is why **MISRA-C compliance, code reviews, and HIL testing are non-negotiable** in the V-Model workflow.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Process Models & 1–10–100 Rule

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare any **three EDLC process models** (Waterfall, Spiral, V-Model, Agile) in terms of: risk handling, defect-detection timing, suitability for embedded systems, and documentation rigor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An embedded medical device has **40 defects** detected in total across its development life cycle. Of these, **30** are detected at the **design review** stage and **10** are detected **post-release**. Using the **1–10–100 rule**, compute the **total cost of defects** if the design-phase fix cost is **₹500 per defect**. Comment on the result.

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Comparison table (5 marks — distributed across four criteria × three models):**

| Criterion (×3 models) | Waterfall (≈1.7 marks) | Spiral (≈1.7 marks) | V-Model (≈1.7 marks) | Agile (≈1.7 marks) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Risk handling | Poor — issues at end | **Excellent** — explicit risk analysis each cycle | Good — but rigid | Good in fast-changing scope |
| Defect detection timing | **Late** | **Early** (each loop) | **Early** (parallel V-V) | Continuous (CI/CD) |
| Suitability for embedded | Low (consumer toys only) | High (aerospace) | **High** (automotive, medical) | Moderate (modern IoT) |
| Documentation rigor | High but static | Medium | **Very high** (traceability) | Medium — living docs |

**Conclusion (2 marks):** The V-Model is the most widely adopted in **safety-critical embedded** (ISO 26262, IEC 62304), while the **Spiral model** dominates **high-risk aerospace/defence**. Agile (with hardware-waterfall hybrid) is rising in **modern IoT/automotive**.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Given (1 mark):**
- Total defects = 40
- Design-phase defects = 30
- Post-release defects = 10
- Design-phase fix cost = ₹500/defect

**Apply 1–10–100 rule (2 marks):**

| Phase | Cost Multiplier | Cost per Defect |
|---|---|---|
| Design | 1× | ₹500 |
| Build | 10× | ₹5,000 |
| Post-release | 100× | ₹50,000 |

**Compute total cost (3 marks):**

$$
C_{design} = 30 \times 500 = 15\,000
$$

$$
C_{post} = 10 \times 50\,000 = 5\,00\,000
$$

$$
C_{total} = 15\,000 + 5\,00\,000 = 5\,15\,000
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{total} = \text{₹}5{,}15{,}000 \;(\approx \text{₹}5.15 \text{ lakh})}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Even though only **25% of defects** (10/40) escaped to post-release, they account for **97% of total defect cost** (₹5,00,000 / ₹5,15,000). This numerically proves the **1–10–100 rule** and reinforces the EDLC principle of **front-loading testing effort**.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1 / EDLC**

- ✅ **EDLC = 8 phases**: Specification → Architecture → Detailed Design → Implementation → Integration → V&V → Deployment → Maintenance & EOL.
- ✅ **EDLC ≠ SDLC** — always mention **HW/SW co-design** and **real-time constraints**.
- ✅ **V-Model** is the **most-tested** model in KTU exams — memorize the **4-pair left↔right mapping**.
- ✅ **Boehm's Rule:** $C_n = C_0 \cdot r^{\,n}$ with $r = 10$ → **10,000× cost growth** by post-release.
- ✅ **1–10–100 Rule:** Design = **\$1**, Build = **\$10**, Post-release = **\$100** per defect.
- ✅ **MTBF** = total operating hours ÷ number of failures; **FIT** = $10^9$ / MTBF.
- ✅ **Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).
- ✅ **Spiral model** = best for **high-risk / aerospace** projects; combines waterfall + risk analysis.
- ✅ **Agile in embedded** = "Dual-Track Agile" — waterfall hardware + agile firmware.
- ✅ Always show **units** (₹, hours, %) in numerical answers to earn full marks.
- ✅ **Safety-critical multiplier ($\alpha$)** in Boehm's formula: $\alpha = 5$–$20$ for automotive/medical — yields the **200× ceiling rule**.
- ✅ **Traceability** (requirement ↔ test) is the **soul of V-Model** — mention this in every V-Model answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Phases of EDLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Different Phases of EDLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is EDLC?

**EDLC** stands for the **Embedded Design Life Cycle** (also called the Embedded System Development Life Cycle). It is the structured, systematic, and iterative set of engineering phases that engineers follow to conceive, design, implement, test, deploy, and maintain an embedded system — from the day an idea is born on paper until the product retires in the field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> The **Embedded Design Life Cycle (EDLC)** is defined as a *systematic, phased engineering methodology* that governs the technical and managerial activities required to transform a set of customer/stakeholder requirements into a fully operational, validated, and field-deployed embedded system. It is a superset of the classic software SDLC, extended to include **hardware co-design, firmware development, real-time constraints, and hardware-software integration**.

## 1.2 The Phases at a Glance

The KTU 2024 Scheme groups the EDLC into **six core phases** (some texts use 5 or 7, but the modular breakdown for this course is 6):

1. **Requirements & Feasibility Phase**
2. **System Specification Phase**
3. **Architecture & Hardware–Software Partitioning Phase**
4. **Component Design (Hardware & Software) Phase**
5. **Implementation, Integration & Testing Phase**
6. **Deployment, Maintenance & Evolution Phase**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The House-Building Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Think of building a *smart home* from scratch.
> - You don't pour concrete on day one. First, you ask the family **what they need** (3 bedrooms? solar? smart locks?) — that's the *Requirements Phase*.
> - Then an architect drafts the *blueprint* with exact measurements — that's the *Specification Phase*.
> - Next, the architect decides which parts are *brick walls* (hardware) and which are *the smart wiring/software* (firmware) — that's the *Architecture & Partitioning Phase*.
> - The electrician and civil engineer then *build* their respective parts in detail — that's the *Component Design Phase*.
> - Plumbers, electricians, and carpenters *assemble* the house, and inspectors *test* every switch and pipe — that's the *Implementation & Testing Phase*.
> - Finally, the family *moves in*, repaints, fixes leaks, and upgrades to solar — that's the *Deployment & Maintenance Phase*.

The EDLC is *exactly* this disciplined journey, but for an embedded product (say, a smart wearable or a car's ABS controller).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the EDLC matter in KTU exams?**
> Because the Examiner will test whether you can:
> (a) **Name** each phase in order.
> (b) **Explain** the *deliverable* (output) of each phase.
> (c) **Distinguish** between adjacent phases (e.g., Specification vs. Design).
> (d) **Justify** why the cycle is often **iterative** (the "V-Model" or "Spiral" notion).

## 1.4 High-Yield Exam Keywords (Memorize These)

- **Stakeholder** — anyone who benefits from or is affected by the system (customer, end-user, regulatory body).
- **Deliverable** — a tangible document/product produced at the *end* of a phase.
- **Iterative Cycle** — returning to earlier phases as new information is found.
- **Co-design** — designing hardware and software *together* (not sequentially).
- **Verification vs. Validation** — *Are we building the product right?* (Verification) vs. *Are we building the right product?* (Validation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phase 1 — Requirements & Feasibility Phase

**Purpose:** Understand *what* the customer wants and *whether* it is even buildable (technically, legally, financially).

**Key Activities:**
- Elicit requirements from stakeholders via interviews, surveys, use-cases.
- Identify **functional requirements** (what the system *does*) and **non-functional requirements** (performance, power, cost, safety, reliability, security).
- Conduct **feasibility analysis** across four dimensions:
  1. *Technical feasibility* — Can existing technology meet the spec?
  2. *Economic feasibility* — Is the ROI acceptable?
  3. *Legal/Regulatory feasibility* — Does it meet standards (e.g., FCC, CE, ISO 26262)?
  4. *Operational feasibility* — Will end-users actually adopt it?

**Deliverables:**
- *Requirements Document* / *Marketing Requirements Document (MRD)*
- *Feasibility Report*

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** A misjudged feasibility study killed the *Google Glass* consumer edition (2015) — the team misjudged social and operational feasibility even though the technology worked.

---

## 2.2 Phase 2 — System Specification Phase

**Purpose:** Convert informal "wants" into **measurable, testable, unambiguous engineering specifications**.

**Key Activities:**
- Write a **System Requirements Specification (SRS)** following IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148 standards.
- Specify inputs, outputs, behavior, performance metrics (latency, throughput, jitter), interfaces, and design constraints.
- Identify **constraints** (e.g., *"must run on a 32-bit ARM Cortex-M0 with ≤ 16 KB RAM"*).
- Generate **use-case diagrams**, **sequence diagrams**, and **state-machine models**.

**Deliverables:**
- *System Requirements Specification (SRS)* document
- *Use-case models* and *timing diagrams*

**Two flavors of specification:**
- *Functional Specification* — what the system *does*.
- *Non-functional Specification* — how *well* it does it (timing, power, EMI/EMC, MTBF).

---

## 2.3 Phase 3 — Architecture & Hardware–Software Partitioning Phase

**Purpose:** Decide the *big picture* — which functions will be implemented in **hardware** (ASIC, FPGA, dedicated peripherals) and which in **software** (firmware on a microcontroller/processor).

**Key Activities:**
- **Hardware–Software Co-design:** Decide the boundary between custom logic and programmable code.
- Select the **processor** (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4, RISC-V, DSP, custom ASIC).
- Choose the **RTOS** (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RT-Linux) or *super-loop/bare-metal* approach.
- Define **system architecture** (block diagram of CPU, memory, buses — AMBA, AHB, APB — peripherals).
- Plan the **memory map**, **boot sequence**, and **power-management policy**.

**Deliverables:**
- *System Architecture Document*
- *Hardware–Software Partitioning Table*
- *Block-level schematic*

---

## 2.4 Phase 4 — Component Design Phase

**Purpose:** Detailed engineering of the two parallel tracks.

### 4A. Hardware Design
- **Schematic capture** (using tools like KiCad, Altium, OrCAD).
- **PCB layout** (signal integrity, EMI/EMC, thermal).
- **Power-supply design** (LDO, buck/boost converters).
- **Component selection** (passive & active).
- **Mechanical design** (enclosure, IP rating, connectors).

### 4B. Software/Firmware Design
- **Algorithm design** (state machines, dataflow, control loops).
- **Module decomposition** (drivers, HAL, middleware, application).
- **Detailed timing analysis** (WCET — Worst-Case Execution Time).
- **Coding in C/C++/Assembly** for the target MCU.
- **Code optimization** for size, speed, and power.

**Deliverables:**
- *Hardware:* Schematics, BOM, Gerbers, Netlist.
- *Software:* Source code, header files, build system (Makefile/CMake), static-analysis report.

---

## 2.5 Phase 5 — Implementation, Integration & Testing Phase

**Purpose:** Build the prototype and **verify/validate** every layer.

**Key Activities:**
- **Implementation:** PCB fabrication, soldering, firmware flashing.
- **Integration:** Combine hardware + software incrementally (bottom-up or top-down).
- **Testing Hierarchy (the V-Model mapping):**

| Test Level | Verifies | Tools |
|------------|----------|-------|
| Unit Test | Individual functions/modules | Unity, CMock, Google Test |
| Integration Test | Module interactions | Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) |
| System Test | Full system vs. SRS | Oscilloscope, logic analyzer |
| Acceptance Test | End-user satisfaction | Beta testing, field trials |

- **Debugging:** JTAG/SWD debuggers, GDB, logic analyzers, oscilloscopes.
- **Performance profiling:** Power consumption, CPU load, memory footprint.

**Deliverables:**
- *Working prototype* + *Test Reports* + *Bug-Tracking logs*

---

## 2.6 Phase 6 — Deployment, Maintenance & Evolution Phase

**Purpose:** Ship the product, fix field issues, and add features.

**Key Activities:**
- **Manufacturing hand-off** (DFM — Design for Manufacturing).
- **Firmware Over-the-Air (OTA) updates.**
- **Field monitoring** (telemetry, logs, crash reports).
- **Corrective maintenance** (bug fixes), **adaptive maintenance** (new OS/hardware), **perfective maintenance** (new features), **preventive maintenance** (re-architecting).
- **End-of-Life (EOL)** planning — component obsolescence, spare-parts strategy.

**Deliverables:**
- *Production-ready product*
- *User manuals, service manuals*
- *Maintenance logs and version-controlled firmware*

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Reference Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The EDLC is **methodological**, not mathematical. However, KTU frequently tests *quantitative trade-off reasoning*. Memorize the following relationships.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $$\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$ | hours | Reliability metric, Phase 1 (non-functional req.) |
| 2 | **Availability (A)** | $$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$$ | % | MTTR = Mean Time To Repair |
| 3 | **Power Budget (Dynamic)** | $$P = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$$ | Watts (W) | $\alpha$ = switching activity, $C$ = load capacitance, $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage, $f$ = clock frequency |
| 4 | **CPU Utilization (U)** | $$U = \frac{T_{\text{busy}}}{T_{\text{busy}} + T_{\text{idle}}}$$ | dimensionless (0–1) | RTOS scheduling metric |
| 5 | **Deadline Miss Ratio (DMR)** | $$\text{DMR} = \frac{N_{\text{missed}}}{N_{\text{total tasks}}}$$ | dimensionless | Hard real-time systems must have DMR = 0 |
| 6 | **Response Time (R)** | $$R = T_{\text{arrival}} - T_{\text{completion}}$$ | ms / µs | Lower is better for control loops |
| 7 | **Cost-of-Ownership (TCO)** | $$\text{TCO} = C_{\text{dev}} + C_{\text{prod}} + C_{\text{maintain}} + C_{\text{disposal}}$$ | ₹ / $ | Used in feasibility study (Phase 1) |
| 8 | **Code Coverage** | $$\text{Cov} = \frac{\text{Lines Executed}}{\text{Total Lines}} \times 100\%$$ | % | Phase 5 metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** In markdown tables, never use the bare `|` symbol for absolute value. Use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table parser. (Already done in the table above.)

---

## 2.8 Why an Iterative Life Cycle?

> [!NOTE]
> A **waterfall** EDLC works for *well-understood* problems (e.g., a digital thermometer).
> An **iterative/spiral** EDLC is needed when:
> - Requirements are *unclear* or *evolving* (R&D-heavy products).
> - Hardware and software must be *co-simulated* before any silicon is taped out.
> - Regulatory certification (e.g., **ISO 26262** for automotive) demands traceability across *every* phase.

KTU expects you to know the difference between **Waterfall**, **V-Model**, and **Spiral/Iterative** EDLCs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Selecting a Processor Using the EDLC

**Problem Statement (typical 14-mark KTU style):**
A startup wants to build a **wearable heart-rate monitor** that:
- Samples PPG sensor at **200 Hz**.
- Runs an FFT-based heart-rate algorithm.
- Displays BPM on an OLED.
- Must run **> 7 days** on a 100 mAh coin-cell battery.
- Cost < ₹1500 BOM.

**Derive, phase-by-phase, what the engineering team does.**

---

### Step 1 — Requirements & Feasibility (Phase 1)

**Functional Requirements (FR):**
- FR1: Sample PPG at 200 Hz.
- FR2: Compute HR via FFT.
- FR3: Display on OLED via I²C.
- FR4: Store 24 h of HR data in flash.

**Non-Functional Requirements (NFR):**
- NFR1: Battery life ≥ 7 days (energy budget = **100 mAh / (7×24 h) ≈ 0.595 mAh per hour**).
- NFR2: Cost BOM ≤ ₹1500.
- NFR3: Latency (sensor-to-display) ≤ **500 ms**.
- NFR4: Weight ≤ 30 g.

**Feasibility Decision:** *Technically feasible* — modern 32-bit MCUs can do FFT at 200 Hz. *Economically* marginal. *Operationally* — heart-rate wearables are a proven market.

**Mathematical Justification of Battery Budget:**

$$
E_{\text{day}} = \frac{100 \text{ mAh}}{7} \approx 14.28 \text{ mAh/day}
$$

$$
P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{day}} \times 3.7 \text{ V}}{24 \text{ h}} = \frac{14.28 \times 3.7}{24} \text{ mW} \approx 2.20 \text{ mW}
$$

> **Logic Row 1:** The feasibility engineer computes the average power budget to determine if any off-the-shelf MCU + sensor combo can realistically meet this energy envelope. If $P_{\text{avg}} < 5$ mW, the project is feasible with low-power MCUs like Cortex-M0+.

---

### Step 2 — System Specification (Phase 2)

Convert the above into measurable specs.

| Parameter | Specification | Test Method |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| Sample rate | 200 Hz ± 1% | Oscilloscope on ADC pin |
| HR accuracy | ±2 BPM | Clinical comparison |
| Display refresh | 1 Hz | Visual + timing |
| Latency | ≤ 500 ms | GPIO toggle + scope |
| Battery life | ≥ 7 days | Bench test |

**Use-Case Example:** *"When user taps the OLED, the system must wake from sleep and display BPM within 200 ms."*

---

### Step 3 — Architecture & HW-SW Partitioning (Phase 3)

The team decides:

| Function | Implementation | Justification |
|----------|----------------|---------------|
| ADC sampling | **Hardware (DMA + Timer)** | Offloads CPU, enables deep sleep |
| FFT (256-point) | **Software (CMSIS-DSP library)** | Cortex-M4F has single-cycle MAC |
| Display rendering | **Software (frame-buffer in RAM)** | OLED is slow I²C, no need for GPU |
| Heart-rate algorithm | **Software (state machine)** | Needs flexibility for tuning |

**Processor Selection Decision Matrix:**

$$
\text{Score} = w_1 \cdot \text{Perf} + w_2 \cdot \text{Power} + w_3 \cdot \text{Cost} + w_4 \cdot \text{Toolchain}
$$

With weights $w_1 = 0.35, w_2 = 0.30, w_3 = 0.20, w_4 = 0.15$, the team scores three candidates (out of 10):

| MCU | Perf | Power | Cost | Toolchain | Weighted Score |
|-----|------|-------|------|-----------|----------------|
| STM32L0 (Cortex-M0+) | 6 | 9 | 8 | 9 | $0.35(6) + 0.30(9) + 0.20(8) + 0.15(9) = 7.55$ |
| STM32L4 (Cortex-M4F) | 9 | 7 | 7 | 9 | $0.35(9) + 0.30(7) + 0.20(7) + 0.15(9) = 7.90$ |
| nRF52832 (Cortex-M4F + BLE) | 9 | 6 | 6 | 8 | $0.35(9) + 0.30(6) + 0.20(6) + 0.15(8) = 7.25$ |

> **Logic Row 2:** The highest weighted score is **STM32L4** → chosen.

**Memory-Map Design:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Flash} &= 256 \text{ KB} \\
&\to 180 \text{ KB Firmware} \\
&\to 64 \text{ KB Logging buffer} \\
&\to 12 \text{ KB Bootloader} \\
\text{RAM} &= 64 \text{ KB} \\
&\to 8 \text{ KB Stack} \\
&\to 16 \text{ KB FFT buffer} \\
&\to 8 \text{ KB Display buffer} \\
&\to 32 \text{ KB FreeRTOS heap}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Row 3:** Memory budgets are allocated *before* writing any code. This is the output of Phase 3.

---

### Step 4 — Component Design (Phase 4)

**Hardware Design (key equations):**

For the I²C bus pull-up resistor:

$$
R_{\text{pullup}} = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{OL}}{I_{\text{OL}}}
$$

With $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, $V_{OL} = 0.4$ V, $I_{\text{OL}} = 3$ mA:

$$
R_{\text{pullup}} = \frac{3.3 - 0.4}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = 966.67 \, \Omega
$$

> **Logic Row 4:** A standard 1 kΩ pull-up is chosen.

**Software Design — FFT Computation Time:**

A 256-point radix-2 FFT on Cortex-M4F takes roughly $256 \cdot \log_2(256) = 2048$ butterfly operations. At 16 MIPS/MHz × 32 MHz = 512 MIPS, and assuming ~10 cycles per butterfly:

$$
T_{\text{FFT}} = \frac{2048 \times 10}{32 \times 10^6} = 640 \, \mu s
$$

> **Logic Row 5:** Since the period at 200 Hz is $1/200 = 5$ ms, the FFT leaves 4.36 ms of slack — easily meeting real-time deadlines.

**RTOS Decision:**
- DMR (Deadline Miss Ratio) must be 0 for a medical device.
- FreeRTOS with preemptive scheduling is selected.

---

### Step 5 — Implementation, Integration & Testing (Phase 5)

**Test Case Example (Unit Test for FFT module):**

$$
\text{Code Coverage} = \frac{\text{Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100\%
$$

Target: ≥ 90% line coverage, 100% branch coverage for safety-critical paths (per ISO 62304 for medical software).

**Integration Test Setup:**

$$
\text{Setup} = \text{MCU board} + \text{PPG sensor} + \text{Oscilloscope on INT pin}
$$

**Acceptance Test:**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%
$$

If MTBF = 50,000 h and MTTR = 1 h, availability $A = 99.998\%$.

---

### Step 6 — Deployment & Maintenance (Phase 6)

**OTA Update Strategy:**

$$
\text{Update Size} = 64 \text{ KB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{\text{OTA}} = \frac{64 \times 8}{50 \times 10^3} = 10.24 \text{ s (via BLE)}
$$

**Field Failure Analysis (per 1000 units shipped):**

$$
\text{FIT Rate} = \frac{\text{Failures}}{\text{Device-Hours}} \times 10^9
$$

If 2 units fail in 1 year (8760 h) of continuous operation per unit:

$$
\text{FIT} = \frac{2}{1000 \times 8760} \times 10^9 = 228 \text{ FIT}
$$

> **Logic Row 6:** A FIT of 228 is acceptable for consumer wearables (industrial safety requires < 10 FIT).

---

## 3.2 Summary Matrix — EDLC Phases vs. Engineering Activities

| Phase | Primary Input | Primary Output | Key Engineering Activity | KTU Bloom Level |
|-------|--------------|----------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| 1. Requirements & Feasibility | Stakeholder needs | MRD, Feasibility Report | Elicitation, 4-D Feasibility | **Understand, Analyze** |
| 2. Specification | MRD | SRS, Use-cases | Measurable spec writing | **Apply, Analyze** |
| 3. Architecture & Partitioning | SRS | Block diagram, HW/SW table | Co-design, MCU selection | **Apply, Evaluate** |
| 4. Component Design | Architecture doc | Schematics, Source code | Schematic capture, coding | **Create, Apply** |
| 5. Implementation & Testing | Designs | Working prototype, Test report | Fabrication, debugging, V&V | **Evaluate, Create** |
| 6. Deployment & Maintenance | Prototype | Shipped product, firmware updates | DFM, OTA, monitoring | **Evaluate, Create** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Six-Phase EDLC Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PHASE 1: Requirements and Feasibility"] --> B["PHASE 2: System Specification"]
    B --> C["PHASE 3: Architecture and HW SW Partitioning"]
    C --> D["PHASE 4: Component Design"]
    D --> E["PHASE 5: Implementation Integration and Testing"]
    E --> F["PHASE 6: Deployment Maintenance and Evolution"]
    F -.->|"Feedback Loop"| A
    F -.->|"Bug fixes / new features"| C
    E -.->|"HW SW co simulation issues"| B

    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show the *forward* (waterfall) progression. Dotted arrows show *iterative feedback* — a defining feature of real EDLC practice.

---

## 4.2 The V-Model Mapping (Verification Across Phases)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Requirements"] --- T6["Acceptance Test"]
    P2["System Spec"] --- T5["System Test"]
    P3["Architecture"] --- T4["Integration Test"]
    P4["Module Design"] --- T3["Unit Test"]
    P5["Implementation"] --- T2["Code Review"]
    P6["Coding Detail"] --- T1["Static Analysis"]

    style P1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style P4 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style P5 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style P6 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style T1 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style T2 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style T3 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style T4 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style T5 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style T6 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
```

> **Key insight:** Each development phase on the **left descending arm** has a corresponding testing phase on the **right ascending arm**. This is the **V-Model** — the most tested framework in KTU exams.

---

## 4.3 Hardware–Software Co-Design Partitioning (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph hw["HARDWARE LAYER"]
        h1["Sensor Interface ADC"]
        h2["DMA Controller"]
        h3["Memory Controller Flash and RAM"]
        h4["I2C SPI UART Peripherals"]
        h5["Clock and Power Management Unit"]
    end

    subgraph sw["SOFTWARE LAYER"]
        s1["Bootloader"]
        s2["Hardware Abstraction Layer HAL"]
        s3["Device Drivers"]
        s4["RTOS Kernel"]
        s5["Middleware DSP Libraries"]
        s6["Application Layer State Machines"]
    end

    h1 --> s3
    h2 --> s3
    h3 --> s3
    h4 --> s3
    h5 --> s4
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6

    style h1 fill:#ffe4b5
    style h2 fill:#ffe4b5
    style h3 fill:#ffe4b5
    style h4 fill:#ffe4b5
    style h5 fill:#ffe4b5
    style s1 fill:#e0ffff
    style s2 fill:#e0ffff
    style s3 fill:#e0ffff
    style s4 fill:#e0ffff
    style s5 fill:#e0ffff
    style s6 fill:#e0ffff
```

> This is the **output of Phase 3 (Architecture & Partitioning)** — the blueprint that Phase 4 designers expand into detailed schematics and code.

---

## 4.4 Iterative Spiral Model (Alternative EDLC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Spiral 1: Concept and Feasibility"] --> S2["Spiral 2: Prototype and Risk Analysis"]
    S2 --> S3["Spiral 3: Detailed Design and V&V"]
    S3 --> S4["Spiral 4: Production and Maintenance"]

    S1 -.- r1["Risk: Technology unproven"]
    S2 -.- r2["Risk: Performance vs Power"]
    S3 -.- r3["Risk: Certification IEC 61508"]
    S4 -.- r4["Risk: Component obsolescence"]

    style S1 fill:#ffbb78
    style S2 fill:#ffbb78
    style S3 fill:#ffbb78
    style S4 fill:#ffbb78
    style r1 fill:#ff9896
    style r2 fill:#ff9896
    style r3 fill:#ff9896
    style r4 fill:#ff9896
```

> Use this **Spiral Model** diagram if the exam question asks *"Compare EDLC models for an R&D-heavy embedded project."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
**Define the Embedded Design Life Cycle (EDLC). List any four of its phases.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Embedded Design Life Cycle (EDLC) is a structured sequence of engineering activities used to design, develop, test, and deploy an embedded system from initial concept to end-of-life. **(1 mark)**
The four phases are:
1. Requirements and Feasibility Analysis **(0.5 mark)**
2. System Specification **(0.5 mark)**
3. Architecture and Hardware–Software Partitioning **(0.5 mark)**
4. Component Design / Implementation & Testing **(0.5 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**
**Differentiate between Verification and Validation in the context of the EDLC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| Question answered | *"Are we building the product right?"* **(1 mark)** | *"Are we building the right product?"* **(1 mark)** |
| Phase in EDLC | Mostly in Phase 5 (testing) | Spans Phases 1–6 (continuous) |
| Example | Unit test, code review | Beta testing against user needs |

**(0.5 mark)** for the example and **(0.5 mark)** for the "spans all phases" continuity point.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six phases of the Embedded Design Life Cycle**, with one real-world deliverable produced at the end of each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **Waterfall, V-Model, and Spiral** approaches to the EDLC. For a **safety-critical automotive airbag controller**, justify which model is most appropriate. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Six Phases of EDLC (7 marks — 1 mark per phase + 0.17 for deliverable justification):**

1. **Requirements & Feasibility:** Gather functional and non-functional requirements; perform technical/economic/legal/operational feasibility. *Deliverable:* Marketing Requirements Document (MRD) + Feasibility Report. **(1 mark)**
2. **System Specification:** Convert requirements into measurable, testable specifications. *Deliverable:* System Requirements Specification (SRS) per IEEE 29148. **(1 mark)**
3. **Architecture & HW–SW Partitioning:** Decide the high-level structure; partition functions into hardware and software. *Deliverable:* System Architecture Document + HW/SW partitioning table. **(1 mark)**
4. **Component Design:** Detailed design of schematics, PCB layout, firmware modules, drivers, algorithms. *Deliverable:* Schematics, BOM, Gerbers, and source code. **(1 mark)**
5. **Implementation, Integration & Testing:** Fabrication, firmware flashing, unit/integration/system/acceptance testing. *Deliverable:* Working prototype + Test Report. **(1 mark)**
6. **Deployment, Maintenance & Evolution:** Manufacturing hand-off, OTA updates, field monitoring, EOL planning. *Deliverable:* Production-ready product + Maintenance manuals. **(1 mark)**
7. **Iterative feedback** loops exist between all phases — EDLC is not strictly linear. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Comparison of EDLC Models (7 marks):**

| Model | Structure | Strength | Weakness | When to Use |
|-------|-----------|----------|----------|-------------|
| **Waterfall** | Linear, sequential | Simple, easy to manage | Late discovery of bugs | Well-understood, stable requirements |
| **V-Model** | V-shaped; each dev phase has a matching test phase | Strong verification, traceability | Still mostly sequential | **Safety-critical systems** |
| **Spiral/Iterative** | Cyclical, risk-driven | Handles uncertainty, continuous refinement | Costly, complex management | R&D-heavy, evolving specs |

**Justification for Airbag Controller (3 marks):**
- The system is **safety-critical** — failure can cause death.
- Regulatory standards (**ISO 26262 ASIL-D**) demand *full traceability* from requirement → test.
- The V-Model is best because it enforces a **1-to-1 mapping** between every specification (left arm) and a corresponding test (right arm), which is exactly what auditors verify.
- A spiral model would be inappropriate because the airbag domain is *mature* — requirements are well-known (no need for iterative risk exploration).
- A pure waterfall would lack the explicit test linkage required by ISO 26262.
- **Conclusion:** V-Model with strict configuration management and formal verification is the industry standard (Bosch, Continental, ZF all use it for airbag ECUs).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Hardware–Software Co-design** in the EDLC. Why is this phase critical for embedded systems but largely absent from generic software SDLC? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company is designing a **battery-powered IoT soil-moisture sensor** that must last 5 years on 2× AA batteries, send data via LoRa every 15 minutes, and cost < ₹500 BOM. Walk through **Phases 1, 2, and 3** of the EDLC for this product, including all numerical energy/power calculations. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Role of HW–SW Co-design (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** HW–SW co-design is the *concurrent* design of hardware and software, deciding which functions are realized in silicon (ASIC, FPGA, peripherals) and which in firmware (running on a CPU/MCU).
2. **Why critical for embedded (3 marks):**
   - Embedded systems are **resource-constrained** (limited CPU, RAM, power). Splitting HW/SW *optimally* decides whether the product is feasible at all.
   - **Performance:** Time-critical loops (motor control, signal processing) often *need* hardware acceleration.
   - **Power:** Keeping a function in hardware can let the CPU sleep more (saving µA).
   - **Cost & Time-to-Market:** Re-using a peripheral saves firmware development time and PCB area.
3. **Why largely absent in generic SDLC (2 marks):**
   - Generic software SDLC assumes a fixed, pre-existing hardware platform (e.g., a desktop PC). There is no "partitioning" decision.
   - Embedded systems are *built from the transistor up* — the hardware *is* part of the deliverable.
4. **Deliverables of Co-design phase (1 mark):** Partitioning table, block diagram, processor selection rationale, RTOS/bare-metal decision, memory map.

**(b) Phases 1–3 for the IoT Soil Sensor (7 marks):**

**Phase 1: Requirements & Feasibility (2 marks)**

- **FR1:** Sample soil moisture every 15 min.
- **FR2:** Transmit via LoRa to gateway.
- **FR3:** 5-year battery life on 2× AA (≈ 2500 mAh @ 3 V).
- **NFR1:** Cost BOM ≤ ₹500.
- **NFR2:** Operating temperature 0–60 °C.

**Energy Budget Calculation:**

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2500 \text{ mAh}}{5 \times 365 \times 24 \text{ h}} = \frac{2500}{43800} \approx 57 \, \mu A
$$

> **Logic:** The entire system — sleep, sense, transmit — must average ≤ 57 µA. This is *aggressive* but achievable with low-power MCUs (STM32L0, MSP430).

**Feasibility:** Technically feasible; economically tight; operationally proven (crop monitoring is a known use-case).

**Phase 2: System Specification (2 marks)**

| Parameter | Spec |
|-----------|------|
| Moisture accuracy | ±3% VWC |
| LoRa payload | 12 bytes |
| TX interval | 15 min ± 1 s |
| Latency (sense-to-TX) | < 1 s |
| Sleep current | < 5 µA |
| TX peak current | < 100 mA (within LoRa spec) |

**Phase 3: Architecture & Partitioning (3 marks)**

| Function | Implementation |
|----------|----------------|
| Moisture sensing | **Hardware** — capacitive sensor with analog front-end |
| ADC conversion | **Hardware peripheral** (12-bit SAR ADC) |
| LoRa TX | **Hardware module** (SX1276) controlled by SPI |
| LoRaWAN stack | **Software** (firmware on MCU) |
| Scheduling | **Software** (RTC wake-up interrupt + state machine) |
| Encryption | **Hardware AES accelerator** (in MCU) |

**Processor Choice:** STM32L0 (Cortex-M0+) — 32 µA/MHz active, 0.5 µA standby. Sufficient for the workload, fits the power budget.

**RTOS vs Bare-metal:** Bare-metal super-loop with RTC interrupts — saves ~3 KB of code and ~2 µA vs FreeRTOS for this simple workload.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** "Specification" with "Design." Specification = *what*, Design = *how*. Mixing them costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Always quantify** when discussing non-functional requirements (latency in *ms*, power in *mW*, cost in *₹*). Vague answers lose marks.
> 3. **Sketch diagrams** wherever possible — even a rough block diagram of HW/SW partitioning earns 1–2 extra marks.
> 4. **Mention iterations.** Stating "EDLC is purely waterfall" is a common *wrong* answer. Always clarify that feedback loops exist.
> 5. **Name tools** (e.g., KiCad, Altium, GCC, FreeRTOS, JTAG) where appropriate — it shows industrial awareness that KTU examiners reward.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as a 5-minute revision sheet before walking into the exam hall.**

- ✅ **EDLC = Embedded Design Life Cycle** — a phased methodology for embedded product development.
- ✅ **Six Phases:** Requirements → Specification → Architecture & Partitioning → Component Design → Implementation & Testing → Deployment & Maintenance.
- ✅ **Phase 1 Output:** Marketing Requirements Document (MRD) + Feasibility Report.
- ✅ **Phase 2 Output:** System Requirements Specification (SRS) — measurable, testable.
- ✅ **Phase 3 Output:** Architecture document + HW/SW partitioning table + block diagram.
- ✅ **Phase 4 Output:** Schematics (HW) + Source code (SW).
- ✅ **Phase 5 Output:** Working prototype + Test report (unit, integration, system, acceptance).
- ✅ **Phase 6 Output:** Manufactured product + Maintenance plan + OTA strategy.
- ✅ **HW–SW Co-design** is the *defining* feature of EDLC vs generic SDLC.
- ✅ **Verification** = building the product right; **Validation** = building the right product.
- ✅ **Waterfall** = simple, rigid; **V-Model** = safety-critical, testable; **Spiral** = R&D, risk-driven.
- ✅ **Iterative loops** exist between *every* phase — EDLC is rarely a straight line.
- ✅ **Key metrics to know:** MTBF, MTTR, Availability, Power Budget, CPU Utilization, DMR, Code Coverage.
- ✅ **Real-world standards:** ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 61508 (industrial), ISO 13485 (medical), DO-178C (avionics).
- ✅ **Co-design decision** must consider: performance, power, cost, time-to-market, reusability, safety certification.
- ✅ **For 14-mark answers:** Always include a labeled diagram, a table comparing options, and a numerical justification.
- ✅ **For 3-mark answers:** Definition + 4 bullet points is the safest, highest-scoring format.

> **End of Notes — Different Phases of EDLC (Module 1)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
