<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Embedded System Development Life Cycle (EDLC)** refers to the structured, phased methodology that governs the end-to-end creation of an embedded system — from the initial articulation of a real-world problem to the final deployment, maintenance, and eventual decommissioning of the hardware-software product. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the EDLC is treated as the *master framework* under which all downstream design activities (architecture selection, RTOS integration, firmware coding, verification, etc.) are organized, scheduled, and validated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> "EDLC Models" specifically covers the *process models* used to organize the life cycle. The KTU syllabus emphasizes **four canonical models**:
> 1. The **Waterfall Model**
> 2. The **Incremental / Build-and-Fix Model**
> 3. The **Spiral Model**
> 4. The **V-Model** (Validation and Verification Model)
> 
> Students must be able to *draw, compare, justify, and select* an appropriate model for a given embedded project.

An **EDLC model** is therefore a *theoretical abstraction* — a roadmap that prescribes:
- The **sequence** of phases (Requirement → Design → Implementation → Testing → Maintenance).
- The **deliverables** of each phase.
- The **feedback loops** that permit re-visiting earlier stages.
- The **risk-handling strategy** (critical for embedded products where late-stage hardware re-spins are extremely expensive).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Risk:**
> In EDLC terminology, *risk* is defined as the probability that a design decision made today will cause an unrecoverable failure (cost, schedule, or technical) tomorrow. The chief purpose of an EDLC model is to *manage, contain, and surface* such risks as early as possible.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Building a House"

Imagine you are commissioned to build a house in Kerala for a client.

- **If you use the Waterfall model**, you first complete the architectural drawings, then the foundation, then the walls, then the roof, and finally the interiors. You *never* go back. The client sees the finished house only at the end.
- **If you use the Incremental model**, you build a small core (one room + kitchen) first, hand it over, then extend with bedrooms, then the upper floor — each increment is a *working subset* of the final house.
- **If you use the Spiral model**, you build a rough model, show it to the client, gather feedback, identify risks (e.g., soil stability, monsoon damage), then build a slightly better version. With each loop, the house gets closer to the ideal.
- **If you use the V-Model**, for every drawing/plan you create on the way down, there is a matching test/inspection on the way up. The foundation test corresponds to the foundation plan, the structural test to the structural drawing, and so on.

**The same logic governs embedded systems.** The "house" is your *final product* (e.g., a wearable health monitor or an automotive ECU), and the "construction phases" are your *engineering phases* (requirement capture, hardware-software partitioning, firmware coding, integration, validation, etc.).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition to remember:** An EDLC model is essentially a *time-management and risk-management discipline* imposed on engineering creativity. Without it, an embedded project collapses under its own complexity.

---

## 1.3 Why EDLC Models are Non-Negotiable in Embedded Systems

Embedded systems are fundamentally different from generic software:
- They are **hardware-software co-designed** — meaning a *firmware bug* discovered after PCB fabrication may cost **lakhs of rupees** in re-spin expenses.
- They have **real-time constraints** — a missed deadline is a system failure, not just a slowdown.
- They are **safety-critical** in domains like automotive, medical, and aerospace — a defect can endanger human life.
- Their **components are sourced globally** (ARM cores, RTOS, sensors) and must integrate seamlessly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Metric — Defect Cost Multiplier:**
> A defect caught at the *requirement phase* costs **1×** to fix.
> A defect caught at the *design phase* costs **5× to 10×**.
> A defect caught at the *integration phase* costs **50× to 100×**.
> A defect caught at the *field-deployment phase* costs **500× to 1000×**.
> 
> This geometric escalation is the single greatest justification for adopting a *structured* EDLC model rather than ad-hoc coding.

The **bold** constants/metrics above should be memorized — KTU examiners frequently award marks to students who can quote *quantified* justifications for model selection.

---

## 1.4 Generic Phases Common to All EDLC Models

Although each model structures the phases differently, the **phases themselves are universal**. Every embedded project, regardless of model, must address these **six core phases**:

1. **Requirement Engineering** — Capturing functional and non-functional needs (power, latency, memory, cost).
2. **Specification** — Producing an unambiguous, testable document (the "contract" between teams).
3. **Architecture & Design** — Hardware/software partitioning, selection of MCU/MPU, RTOS choice, communication bus selection.
4. **Implementation / Coding** — Firmware development in C/C++, HDL coding for FPGA/ASIC, PCB layout.
5. **Integration & Testing** — Unit testing, module testing, hardware-software integration, system-level validation.
6. **Maintenance & Evolution** — Field updates, bug fixes, feature additions, end-of-life planning.

> [!NOTE]
> The **art** of selecting an EDLC model lies in deciding *how these phases are ordered, overlapped, iterated, and verified*. The next sections decode this art.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical EDLC Models — Phased Breakdown

### Model 1: The Waterfall Model

The **Waterfall Model** is the *oldest and most rigid* of all life cycle models, originally borrowed from conventional engineering (construction, manufacturing). It enforces a **strictly linear, non-overlapping sequence** of phases. Once a phase is "signed off," the project *cascades* into the next phase — like a waterfall.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. **Requirement Analysis & Specification Phase**
   - All user needs, regulatory standards (e.g., ISO 26262 for automotive, IEC 62304 for medical), and constraints are gathered.
   - Deliverable: *Software Requirement Specification (SRS) document* + *Hardware Requirement Specification (HRS)*.
2. **System & Architecture Design Phase**
   - Hardware-software partitioning. Block diagrams. MCU/MPU selection. Bus topology (I²C, SPI, CAN, UART).
   - Deliverable: *System Design Document (SDD)* + *Schematic sketches*.
3. **Implementation / Coding Phase**
   - Firmware written in C/C++/Assembly. HDL code (Verilog/VHDL) for any FPGA/ASIC portion. PCB designed in EDA tools (KiCad, Altium).
   - Deliverable: *Source code* + *Gerber files*.
4. **Integration & Testing Phase**
   - Modules combined. Unit tests, integration tests, system tests.
   - Deliverable: *Test reports* + *bug logs*.
5. **Deployment & Maintenance Phase**
   - Product released to the field. Bugs are patched. New features added.
   - Deliverable: *Service packs*, *firmware updates*, *End-of-Life (EOL) notice*.

**The "Why" Behind the Waterfall:**
- It provides **predictable milestones**, easy project tracking, and clean documentation.
- It is well-suited for **small, well-understood, low-risk** embedded products (e.g., a simple digital thermometer).

**The "Why Not" — Limitations:**
- No room for customer feedback after the requirement phase.
- Late discovery of design defects is **catastrophic** (the 1000× cost multiplier).
- Assumes that *all* requirements can be captured perfectly *upfront* — almost never true for cutting-edge embedded products.

---

### Model 2: The Incremental Model (Build-and-Fix / Staged Delivery)

The **Incremental Model** breaks the final product into a series of *working slices*, each of which adds a *portion of the full functionality*. Each slice passes through a *mini-waterfall* (mini-requirement → mini-design → mini-code → mini-test), and the slices are integrated cumulatively.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. **System Decomposition** — The complete system is partitioned into *N* functional increments (e.g., Increment 1 = power management; Increment 2 = sensor acquisition; Increment 3 = wireless communication).
2. **Increment 1 (Core Skeleton)** — A *minimal* working version of the system is built. It might be just the boot loader and a "hello world" LED blink on the target MCU. Fully tested.
3. **Increment 2** — Adds a new feature (e.g., temperature sensing via I²C). Designed, coded, integrated with Increment 1, tested.
4. **Increment 3, 4, …** — Each adds a slice of functionality.
5. **Final Integration** — All increments are merged into the *final product*. Final system-level validation is performed.

**The "Why" Behind the Incremental Model:**
- The customer *sees a working product early* — even if it does only 20% of the final job.
- Defects in an early increment are caught *while its scope is small*.
- Parallel development teams can work on different increments simultaneously.

**The "Why Not" — Limitations:**
- Requires *excellent upfront architecture*, otherwise increments collide.
- Total cost can be *higher* than waterfall because of repeated integration overhead.
- Suitable for projects with **clear, stable high-level requirements** but evolving low-level details.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "ICE":** The Incremental model is best for projects where the requirements are **I**dentified, the architecture is **C**lear, and the scope is **E**xtendable.

---

### Model 3: The Spiral Model (Boehm, 1988)

The **Spiral Model**, proposed by *Barry W. Boehm* in 1988, is the **most risk-aware** of all EDLC models. It explicitly incorporates **risk analysis** as a first-class activity in every cycle. Each loop of the spiral adds *refinement* to the product while *reducing* the residual risk.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step — The Four Quadrants of Each Spiral Loop:**

1. **Quadrant 1 — Planning (North):** Define objectives, alternatives, and constraints for the current cycle. Decide what feature/subsystem to address in this loop.
2. **Quadrant 2 — Risk Analysis (East):** Identify and resolve risks. Techniques include simulation, prototyping, benchmark studies, "spike" solutions, and expert review.
3. **Quadrant 3 — Engineering (South):** Develop and verify the product increment. Conventional design + coding + testing.
4. **Quadrant 4 — Customer Evaluation (West):** The current increment is shown to the customer/user. Feedback is captured. Plans for the next loop are refined.

**The "Why" Behind the Spiral:**
- **Risk-driven.** High-risk issues are attacked *first*, not buried in late stages.
- Combines the **linearity of waterfall** (within each loop) with the **iteration of incremental** (across loops).
- Customer is engaged *continuously*.

**The "Why Not" — Limitations:**
- **Expensive** — risk analysis at every loop consumes time and skilled manpower.
- Demands **expertise in risk assessment**, which is rare.
- Not suitable for *low-risk commodity* projects (over-engineering).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:**
> The Spiral Model is the **preferred choice for large, complex, safety-critical, or research-grade embedded systems** — e.g., avionics flight controllers, MRI firmware, satellite payload software. The high upfront cost of risk analysis is *more than offset* by the avoided cost of late-stage hardware re-spins.

---

### Model 4: The V-Model (Validation & Verification Model)

The **V-Model** is an **extension of the Waterfall** in which the testing phases are explicitly *mapped* to the corresponding development phases. The "V" shape comes from the development phases descending on the left arm and the corresponding testing phases ascending on the right arm.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step (Left Arm = Development, Right Arm = Testing):**

1. **User Requirement (Top of V)** ↔ **Acceptance Testing (Top of V, right)**
2. **System Requirements** ↔ **System Testing**
3. **Architecture Design** ↔ **Integration Testing**
4. **Module Design** ↔ **Module / Unit Testing**
5. **Coding (Bottom of V)** ↔ **Code Reviews, Static Analysis**

**The "Why" Behind the V-Model:**
- Each development phase has a *predefined, corresponding test*. This enforces **traceability** (every requirement has a test).
- Defects are caught at the *matching level* of abstraction.
- Ideal for **safety-critical** domains (automotive, medical, industrial) where certification bodies (ISO 26262, DO-178C, IEC 61508) demand traceable V&V evidence.

**The "Why Not" — Limitations:**
- Still **rigid and sequential** — like waterfall, it resists change mid-project.
- No explicit iteration loop for *prototyping* or *risk analysis*.
- Not ideal for projects with **evolving requirements**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "V is for Verification":** The left arm is **Verification** ("Are we building the product *right*?"). The right arm is **Validation** ("Are we building the *right* product?").

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet — EDLC Model Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No mathematical formulas exist for EDLC models** (it is a process-engineering topic). Instead, the table below serves as the **formula-equivalent cheat sheet** for KTU viva and written exams. Note: pipe characters inside cell content are replaced with semicolons or "and" to comply with markdown table rules.

| **Attribute** | **Waterfall** | **Incremental** | **Spiral** | **V-Model** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Strictly linear, sequential | Linear within each increment, sequential across increments | Cyclical, risk-driven loops | V-shaped, linear with parallel test arms |
| **Customer Feedback** | Only at the end (after deployment) | After every increment | Continuous (every loop) | Only at acceptance testing |
| **Risk Handling** | Implicit, late discovery | Moderate, per-increment | Explicit, first-class activity | Implicit, matched to design phase |
| **Requirement Stability** | Requires highly stable, frozen requirements | Allows partial evolution | Tolerates high uncertainty | Requires stable, testable requirements |
| **Cost of Late Change** | Catastrophic (up to 1000×) | Moderate (contained to an increment) | Low (absorbed by next loop) | High (changes ripple up the V) |
| **Documentation** | Very heavy; one final SRS | Moderate; per-increment | Heavy on risk documentation | Very heavy; full V&V traceability matrix |
| **Time to First Working Version** | Long (full cycle) | Short (after first increment) | Moderate (after first loop) | Long (full cycle, but tested incrementally on the right arm) |
| **Best Suited For** | Small, well-understood projects | Mid-size, evolving-feature products | Large, complex, high-risk, R&D systems | Safety-critical, certification-bound systems |
| **Worst Suited For** | Evolving or R&D projects | Projects with unclear overall architecture | Low-budget, low-risk commodity products | Projects with volatile requirements |
| **Origin / Inventor** | Winston Royce (1970, refined) | Harlan Mills; later codified in IEEE | Barry W. Boehm (1988) | German federal administration; later adopted in automotive and aerospace |
| **Domain Examples** | Digital thermometer, calculator firmware | Smartwatch firmware, IoT device with progressive OTA features | Satellite payload software, autonomous vehicle R&D | Airbag ECU, infusion pump, railway signaling |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of EDLC Models

| **Industry Vertical** | **Typical EDLC Model Used** | **Why** |
|---|---|---|
| **Consumer IoT (smart bulbs, fitness bands)** | Incremental | Short time-to-market, evolving feature set via OTA updates |
| **Automotive ECU (ABS, airbag)** | V-Model (with Spiral overlay) | ISO 26262 mandates traceable V&V; risk is paramount |
| **Avionics** | V-Model + DO-178C compliance | Certification-driven, zero-defect tolerance |
| **Medical devices (pacemakers, MRI)** | V-Model + IEC 62304 | Patient safety; regulatory audits |
| **Research prototypes, robotics R&D** | Spiral | High technical uncertainty; need risk prototyping |
| **Simple industrial controllers** | Waterfall | Stable specs, low complexity, low cost pressure |
| **Smartphone firmware** | Incremental + Agile hybrids | Rapid feature rollouts, app ecosystem evolution |

> [!NOTE]
> In modern KTU exam answers, the most common mistake students make is to *unconditionally* recommend the Spiral Model for everything because it "handles risk." Examiners deduct marks if the student **does not justify** why a simpler model is insufficient. Always match the model to the *project's risk profile, requirement stability, and certification needs*.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Comparative Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis — Selecting the Right EDLC Model

Since EDLC is a *process* topic, the "derivations" here are replaced by **decision-tree style analytical walkthroughs** that KTU examiners love to test. Below is an exhaustive, line-by-line analysis a student should internalize.

### Analytical Walkthrough — "Which Model Should I Pick?"

**Step 1: Identify the Requirement Stability Index (RSI).**
- If requirements are *frozen and complete* → proceed to Step 2a.
- If requirements are *partially known* → proceed to Step 2b.
- If requirements are *highly volatile or exploratory* → proceed to Step 2c.

**Step 2a: Frozen Requirements + Low Risk + Small Project.**
- Choose **Waterfall**.
- Justification: Simplest model; full documentation; predictable schedule.

**Step 2b: Partially Known Requirements + Moderate Risk + Medium Project.**
- Choose **Incremental**.
- Justification: Allows working software early; defects localized to an increment.

**Step 2c: Volatile Requirements + High Risk + Large Project.**
- Choose **Spiral**.
- Justification: Explicit risk analysis absorbs the uncertainty; customer continuously engaged.

**Step 3: Check for Certification Constraints.**
- If the product must be **ISO 26262, IEC 62304, DO-178C, or IEC 61508 certified** → choose **V-Model** (often overlaid with Spiral risk loops).
- Justification: Certification auditors demand *traceable, paired V&V evidence* — only the V-Model formally provides this.

**Step 4: Decision Output.**

$$
\text{Selected Model} = f(\text{RSI},\ \text{Risk Profile},\ \text{Certification Requirement})
$$

> This is the *meta-decision function* the examiner expects in a "justify the model" answer.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis — Constructing a V-Model Traceability Matrix

The **V-Model's** most distinctive deliverable is the **Traceability Matrix** — a table that links every requirement to its design element, its code, and its test. Below is the exhaustive, KTU-expected construction.

**Step 1: List the requirements (from SRS).**
- REQ-001: System shall measure temperature every 100 ms.
- REQ-002: System shall display temperature on LCD.
- REQ-003: System shall trigger alarm if temperature > 60 °C.

**Step 2: For each requirement, list the architectural component that satisfies it.**
- REQ-001 → Temperature Sensor Driver (LM35 via ADC).
- REQ-002 → LCD Driver (HD44780 via GPIO).
- REQ-003 → Alarm Subsystem (Buzzer via GPIO + Threshold Logic in firmware).

**Step 3: For each architectural component, list the source code module.**
- REQ-001 → `temp_sensor.c`, `adc_driver.c`.
- REQ-002 → `lcd_driver.c`, `gpio_driver.c`.
- REQ-003 → `alarm.c`, `threshold.c`.

**Step 4: For each source module, list the corresponding test case.**
- REQ-001 → Unit test `test_temp_sensor.c` + Integration test `test_sensor_lcd_integration.c`.
- REQ-002 → Unit test `test_lcd_driver.c` + System test `test_lcd_display_quality.c`.
- REQ-003 → Unit test `test_alarm_threshold.c` + System test `test_alarm_e2e.c`.

**Step 5: Compile the matrix.**

$$
\text{Traceability} = \{(\text{Req}_i,\ \text{Design}_i,\ \text{Code}_i,\ \text{Test}_i)\ \vert\ i = 1, 2, \dots, N\}
$$

This symbolic notation is a **formal way of expressing the V-Model's traceability principle** — every requirement is *uniquely* linked to exactly one design element, one code module, and one test case.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Strengths vs. Weaknesses

The following *analytical matrix* is a high-yield revision artifact. Each cell is a KTU-valid exam point.

| **EDLC Model** | **Top 3 Strengths** | **Top 3 Weaknesses** | **KTU-Recommended Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Waterfall** | 1. Simple to manage; 2. Clear milestones; 3. Excellent documentation discipline | 1. No iteration; 2. Late defect discovery; 3. Assumes perfect upfront requirements | Stable, small, low-risk embedded products |
| **Incremental** | 1. Early working version; 2. Customer feedback is timely; 3. Defects localized | 1. Needs strong upfront architecture; 2. Integration overhead; 3. Total cost can be high | Mid-size products with stable high-level needs |
| **Spiral** | 1. Risk-driven; 2. Combines waterfall and incremental virtues; 3. Customer in the loop | 1. Expensive; 2. Needs risk-analysis expertise; 3. Can over-engineer simple projects | Large, complex, R&D-grade embedded systems |
| **V-Model** | 1. Traceable V&V; 2. Ideal for certification; 3. Parallel dev-and-test planning | 1. Rigid like waterfall; 2. No late requirement change; 3. Heavy documentation load | Safety-critical, regulated embedded products |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight for KTU Answers:**
> Modern embedded projects rarely use a *pure* model. Industry practice is to **overlay models** — e.g., a Spiral-driven overall flow with V-Model-style V&V at each loop, delivered as Increments. KTU examiners award extra marks to students who acknowledge this *hybrid reality* in their model-selection justifications.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Risk-Analysis Procedure (Spiral Model)

Because the Spiral model is the *most analytical* of the four, examiners frequently ask for its risk-analysis procedure. Here is the exhaustive 6-step protocol:

**Step 1: Identify the risk sources** (technical, schedule, cost, regulatory, supply-chain).

**Step 2: Estimate probability of occurrence** (Low / Medium / High — quantitative via expert judgment or historical data).

**Step 3: Estimate impact severity** (Low / Medium / High — quantified in rupees, days, or human-safety terms).

**Step 4: Compute the Risk Exposure:**

$$
\text{Risk Exposure} = \text{Probability} \times \text{Impact}
$$

For example: Probability = **0.3**, Impact = ₹10,00,000 → Risk Exposure = **₹3,00,000**.

**Step 5: Decide the mitigation strategy** (Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept).

**Step 6: Re-evaluate at the next spiral loop** using the same equation. The metric should *decrease* with each loop if the model is being executed correctly.

This **Probability × Impact = Exposure** formula is a **standard project-management tool** that KTU examiners expect students to be able to *write down and apply* in a numerical example.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Waterfall Model — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Phase 1: Requirement Analysis and Specification"]:::phase
    node2["Phase 2: System and Architecture Design"]:::phase
    node3["Phase 3: Implementation and Coding"]:::phase
    node4["Phase 4: Integration and Testing"]:::phase
    node5["Phase 5: Deployment and Maintenance"]:::phase
    node6["End Product Released"]:::endNode
    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    classDef phase fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a2540
    classDef endNode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a3622
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Information and deliverables flow *strictly downward*. There is no backward arrow — once a phase is complete, the project does not return to it. This rigidity is the defining feature of the Waterfall.

---

## 4.2 Incremental Model — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["System Decomposition: Identify N Increments"]:::decomp
    inc1["Increment 1: Core Skeleton (Boot, LED)"]:::incBlock
    inc2["Increment 2: Sensor Acquisition (I2C, ADC)"]:::incBlock
    inc3["Increment 3: Wireless Communication (BLE, WiFi)"]:::incBlock
    inc4["Increment 4: UI and Display (LCD, Touch)"]:::incBlock
    final["Final Integration and System Validation"]:::finalNode
    release["Final Product Release"]:::endNode
    nodeA --> inc1
    nodeA --> inc2
    nodeA --> inc3
    nodeA --> inc4
    inc1 --> final
    inc2 --> final
    inc3 --> final
    inc4 --> final
    final --> release
    classDef decomp fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#997404,stroke-width:2px,color:#665108
    classDef incBlock fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a2540
    classDef finalNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,stroke-width:2px,color:#58151c
    classDef endNode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a3622
```

**Reading the Diagram:** A common *root* (System Decomposition) spawns N parallel increments. Each increment is independently developed. They all converge into a *Final Integration* node. The advantage is that the customer can use Increment 1 while Increment 2 is still in development.

---

## 4.3 Spiral Model — Mermaid Cyclical Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    loop1["Loop 1: Planning - Risk Analysis - Engineering - Customer Eval"]:::loop
    loop2["Loop 2: Refined Planning - Reduced Risk - More Features - Customer Eval"]:::loop
    loop3["Loop 3: Final Planning - Minimal Risk - Complete Features - Final Sign Off"]:::loop
    release["Final Product Deployment"]:::endNode
    loop1 --> loop2
    loop2 --> loop3
    loop3 --> release
    loop1 -. risk reduced .-> loop2
    loop2 -. risk further reduced .-> loop3
    classDef loop fill:#e0cffc,stroke:#5f3dc4,stroke-width:2px,color:#2e1065
    classDef endNode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a3622
```

> [!NOTE]
> The original Spiral model is a *radial* diagram with four quadrants. The above Mermaid adaptation preserves the *cyclical and progressive-risk-reduction* semantics in a linear layout that is safe to compile. Each successive loop addresses higher risk and adds more functionality.

---

## 4.4 V-Model — Mermaid V-Shape Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    req["User Requirements"]:::topNode
    sysReq["System Requirements"]:::leftArm
    arch["Architecture Design"]:::leftArm
    mod["Module Design"]:::leftArm
    code["Coding (Firmware and HDL)"]:::bottomNode
    unitT["Module and Unit Testing"]:::rightArm
    intT["Integration Testing"]:::rightArm
    sysT["System Testing"]:::rightArm
    accT["Acceptance Testing"]:::topNode
    req --> sysReq
    sysReq --> arch
    arch --> mod
    mod --> code
    code --> unitT
    unitT --> intT
    intT --> sysT
    sysT --> accT
    sysReq -. verification link .-> sysT
    arch -. verification link .-> intT
    mod -. verification link .-> unitT
    classDef topNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#997404,stroke-width:2px,color:#665108
    classDef leftArm fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a2540
    classDef bottomNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,stroke-width:2px,color:#58151c
    classDef rightArm fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a3622
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The left arm shows *development* phases descending from User Requirements to Coding. The right arm shows *testing* phases ascending from Unit Testing to Acceptance Testing. The dotted "verification link" lines connect each development phase to its **corresponding test phase** — this is the defining feature of the V-Model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dotted verification links are not optional. If a student draws the V-Model *without* these links, the KTU examiner will mark the answer as incomplete.

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Selection Logic

For a synthesis-level understanding, the following diagram shows how an embedded system architect navigates from project attributes to model selection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: New Embedded Project"]:::startNode
    cert{"Certification required? (ISO, IEC, DO)"}:::decision
    risk{"High technical risk or volatile requirements?"}:::decision
    size{"Project size and team scale?"}:::decision
    small["Small and stable"]:::leaf
    medium["Medium with evolving features"]:::leaf
    large["Large and high risk"]:::leaf
    vmodel["Choose V-Model (with Spiral overlay)"]:::choice
    spiral["Choose Spiral Model"]:::choice
    incr["Choose Incremental Model"]:::choice
    water["Choose Waterfall Model"]:::choice
    start --> cert
    cert -- Yes --> vmodel
    cert -- No --> risk
    risk -- Yes --> spiral
    risk -- No --> size
    size -- Small --> water
    size -- Medium --> incr
    size -- Large and R and D --> spiral
    classDef startNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#997404,stroke-width:2px,color:#665108
    classDef decision fill:#e0cffc,stroke:#5f3dc4,stroke-width:2px,color:#2e1065
    classDef leaf fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a2540
    classDef choice fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a3622
```

This functional architecture is a *high-yield* KTU revision artifact: it lets a student answer any "select and justify a model" question by walking the decision tree.
