> **Q: (b) Explain the concept of split transactions in PCI-X. How does the Mod-4 protocol work? Calculate the peak bandwidth of PCI-X operating at 133 MHz.** **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**PCI-X Bus Architecture:**

PCI-X (PCI Extended) is a **backward-compatible** evolution of PCI defined by the PCI-SIG. It retains the **same signal pins, connector, and software model** as PCI but significantly improves performance through:

**Architectural Improvements:**

1. **Mandatory 64-bit bus width** (was optional in PCI).
2. **Higher clock frequencies**: 66, 100, and 133 MHz (vs 33/66 MHz in PCI).
3. **Attribute Phase (2 cycles)**: Master pre-declares byte count and requester ID, allowing the target to pre-fetch data.
4. **Response Phase (1 cycle)**: Target returns completion status, eliminating the need for a separate completion message in most cases.
5. **Split Transactions**: When a target cannot respond immediately, it releases the bus. The completion is delivered later as a separate transaction.
6. **ECC (SECDED)**: 8-bit Error Correction Code provides single-bit error correction.
7. **Mod-4 Protocol**: Up to 4 split transactions can be outstanding per target.
8. **Larger maximum payload (4096 bytes)**.

**PCI-X Transaction Phases:**

1. **Arbitration Phase** — Master wins the bus via REQ#/GNT#.
2. **Address Phase (1 cycle)** — Master drives the 64-bit address on AD[63:0].
3. **Attribute Phase (2 cycles)**:
   - Cycle 1: byte count, requester ID, tag (sequence number).
   - Cycle 2: lower address, attributes.
4. **Response Phase (1 cycle)** — Target acknowledges and indicates ready.
5. **Data Phase(s)** — Up to 4096 bytes transferred with minimal wait.
6. **Turn-Around Phase (1 cycle)** — Clean bus ownership transition.

> **Mark Distribution:** Architectural improvements (3) + Transaction phases (2) + Comparison table (2)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Split Transactions in PCI-X:**

In conventional PCI, when a target cannot immediately provide data (e.g., fetching from slow memory), the **master waits on the bus**, holding it idle and wasting bandwidth. This is called a **"bus hold"** problem.

PCI-X solves this with **split transactions**:

1. Master issues a request (e.g., Memory Read).
2. Target **decodes** the request, finds it cannot respond immediately.
3. Target sends a **Split Response** to the master, indicating the request is accepted but completion will be later.
4. Master **releases the bus** to allow other transactions.
5. When the target is ready, it arbitrates for the bus and sends a **Split Completion** message containing the requested data.
6. The completion is identified by a **Requester ID** + **Tag** (sequence number) to match it with the original request.

**Mod-4 Protocol Details:**

- Each target can have **up to 4 outstanding split transactions** simultaneously.
- Each split transaction is assigned a **3-bit tag** (8 possible values, but only 4 active per target).
- The **requester ID** (Bus:8 + Device:5 + Function:3 = 16 bits) uniquely identifies the requester.
- The target can thus **track multiple pending requests** and respond in any order (or out-of-order if the device allows).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students often confuse **Mod-4** as "4 devices" or "4 buses". The correct meaning is **"4 outstanding split transactions per target"**. The "Mod" refers to **modulo-4 buffer** management.

**Peak Bandwidth Calculation (PCI-X @ 133 MHz):**

**Given:**
- Bus width: $W = 64$ bits = 8 bytes
- Clock frequency: $f = 133$ MHz

**Calculation:**

$$
T_{peak,PCIX133} = W_{bytes} \times f_{cycles}
$$

$$
T_{peak,PCIX133} = 8 \text{ bytes/cycle} \times 133 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles/second}
$$

$$
T_{peak,PCIX133} = 1064 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/second} = 1064 \text{ MB/s} \approx 1.064 \text{ GB/s}
$$

> **Mark Distribution:** Split transaction explanation (2.5) + Mod-4 protocol (2) + Bandwidth calculation (2.5)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PCI/PCI-X Questions (as observed in KTU valuation):**
>
> 1. **Confusing multiplexed vs separate address/data buses**: PCI is **multiplexed** — the same AD lines carry both address and data. Drawing separate AD lines will lose marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting that DEVSEL# is driven by the TARGET, not the master**: This is a classic KTU trap question.
>
> 3. **Incorrect parity calculation**: PAR is **even parity** over AD[31:0] + C/BE[3:0]#. Students often forget to include C/BE in the parity computation.
>
> 4. **Mixing up FRAME# and IRDY#**: FRAME# marks the **boundary of the transaction**; IRDY# marks **readiness of data**. They are independent signals.
>
> 5. **Stating PCI-X is "faster PCI" without explaining protocol changes**: PCI-X is faster because of **architectural improvements** (attribute phase, split transactions, ECC) — not just clock speed. Examiners award full marks only when this is articulated.
>
> 6. **Wrong unit conversion**: $1 \text{ MB} = 10^6$ bytes (SI, used in KTU) **NOT** $2^{20}$. Writing 1.064 GiB/s instead of 1.064 GB/s may be marked wrong depending on the examiner.
>
> 7. **Drawing a sequential pipeline for PCI-X transaction phases**: The phases are **sequential and cyclic**, not parallel. Each phase occurs in a distinct clock cycle (except Data Phase, which can be multiple cycles).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PCI & PCI-X Bus**
>
> **PCI Bus Essentials:**
> - PCI = **P**eripheral **C**omponent **I**nterconnect, introduced by **Intel in 1992**.
> - **Synchronous, multiplexed, parallel bus** (32-bit standard, 64-bit optional).
> - Clock: **33 MHz (standard)** or **66 MHz (extended)**.
> - Peak throughput: **132 MB/s (32-bit @ 33 MHz)** → **528 MB/s (64-bit @ 66 MHz)**.
> - Supports **plug-and-play**, **bus mastering**, **burst transfers** (up to 16 bytes/burst).
> - Maximum **32 devices per bus segment** (electrical loading).
> - Each device has **256 bytes of configuration space**.
> - Error detection: **Even parity (PAR, PERR#, SERR#)**.
>
> **Key PCI Signals to Memorize:**
> - **AD[31:0]**: Multiplexed address/data bus.
> - **C/BE[3:0]#**: Command (address phase) / Byte Enables (data phase).
> - **FRAME#**: Master starts/ends transaction.
> - **IRDY#**: Master ready for data.
> - **TRDY#**: Target ready for data.
> - **DEVSEL#**: Target has decoded address (driven by **TARGET**).
> - **REQ# / GNT#**: Arbitration pair.
> - **PAR**: Even parity (over AD + C/BE).
> - **IDSEL**: Configuration cycle device select.
>
> **PCI-X Bus Essentials:**
> - **64-bit mandatory**, clock up to **133 MHz** (PCI-X 1.0) / **533 MHz** (PCI-X 2.0).
> - Peak throughput: **1.064 GB/s (PCI-X 133)**.
> - **Backward compatible** with PCI (same connectors, signal pins).
> - **Attribute Phase (2 cycles)** + **Response Phase (1 cycle)** added.
> - **Split transactions** with **Mod-4 protocol** (4 outstanding per target).
> - **ECC (SECDED)** for error correction (vs parity in PCI).
> - **4096-byte maximum payload** (vs 16 bytes in PCI).
> - Hot-plug support (PCI-X 2.0).
>
> **Key PCI-X Features to Memorize:**
> - **Mod-4**: 4 outstanding split transactions per target (NOT 4 devices).
> - **Attribute Phase**: Pre-declares byte count and requester ID.
> - **Tag (3-bit)**: Identifies the split transaction (up to 4 active).
> - **Requester ID (16-bit)**: Bus[7:0] + Device[4:0] + Function[2:0].
>
> **Critical Formulas (instant recall):**
> - $T_{PCI32@33} = 4 \times 33 = 132$ MB/s
> - $T_{PCI64@66} = 8 \times 66 = 528$ MB/s
> - $T_{PCIX@100} = 8 \times 100 = 800$ MB/s
> - $T_{PCIX@133} = 8 \times 133 = 1064$ MB/s ≈ 1.064 GB/s
> - $T_{PCIX2.0@266} = 8 \times 266 = 2128$ MB/s
> - $T_{PCIX2.0@533} = 8 \times 533 = 4264$ MB/s
>
> **One-Line Comparisons for Quick Recall:**
> - PCI 32-bit @ 33 MHz = **132 MB/s** (baseline).
> - PCI 64-bit @ 66 MHz = **4× faster** than baseline.
> - PCI-X 64-bit @ 133 MHz = **8× faster** than baseline.
> - PCI-X 2.0 @ 533 MHz = **32× faster** than baseline.
>
> **Historical Context:**
> - PCI **replaced** ISA and VESA Local Bus in mid-1990s.
> - PCI-X was the **server-grade successor**, dominant ~2000–2008.
> - **PCIe (PCI Express)** replaced both PCI and PCI-X from ~2004 onwards using **serial, point-to-point** architecture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory** in an embedded system is a semiconductor-based storage subsystem used to hold the binary instructions (program code), temporary operands (data), and configuration parameters required by the processor/CPU for the execution of embedded application software.

In the context of **Embedded System Design (PBEVT504)**, memory is *not* merely a passive storage array — it is an **architectural pillar** of the SoC (System on Chip) / MCU (Microcontroller Unit). It is tightly coupled with the processor's address, data, and control buses, and its choice dictates **speed, power, cost, and determinism** of the embedded design.

**Memory** can be mathematically modeled as an indexed array:

$$M = \{m[0], m[1], m[2], \dots, m[N-1]\}$$

where each location $m[i]$ holds a **word of $w$ bits**, and $N$ is the total number of addressable locations. The total storage capacity $C$ in bits is:

$$C = N \times w \quad \text{(bits)}$$

In bytes:

$$C_{bytes} = \frac{N \times w}{8} \quad \text{(bytes)}$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Engineer's Workbench**
> 
> Imagine a workshop where a craftsman (the **CPU**) builds products. He needs:
> - **A permanently fixed instruction chart on the wall** → Read-Only Memory (ROM/Flash) – holds the program.
> - **A temporary worktable for keeping tools and raw materials mid-task** → Random Access Memory (RAM) – holds live variables.
> - **A small tray pinned to his apron for the most-used tool** → Cache/Registers – the fastest, smallest storage.
> - **A large warehouse at the back of the shop** → External/Secondary Memory (SD card, EEPROM).
> 
> The closer the storage is to the craftsman, the **faster and costlier** it is, but the **smaller** in capacity. The farther it is, the **larger and cheaper**, but **slower to fetch from**. This is the essence of the **Memory Hierarchy**.

## 1.3 Why Memory is Critical in Embedded Systems

| Engineering Driver | Memory Impact |
|---|---|
| **Determinism / Real-time behavior** | Predictable access time of memory (no cache misses in hard RTOS) |
| **Power budget** | SRAM consumes power on every access; Flash is non-volatile & zero standby |
| **Cost (especially at scale)** | On-chip Flash/RAM is expensive; external memory adds BOM cost |
| **Physical size (IoT/Wearable)** | Smaller memory footprint → smaller die → smaller PCB |
| **Security** | Secure Flash with lock bits, OTP (One-Time Programmable) regions |

## 1.4 The Memory Hierarchy (5-Tier Model)

A fundamental KTU concept — memory is organized in tiers based on the trade-off between **speed, cost, and capacity**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule of Memory Hierarchy:** *As you go DOWN the hierarchy, capacity ↑, cost/bit ↓, access time ↑, frequency of CPU access ↓.*

1. **Registers** (inside CPU core) – fastest, smallest
2. **Cache** (L1/L2 – inside or near CPU) – high speed, small
3. **Main Memory / Primary Memory** (on-chip SRAM + on-chip Flash) – medium speed
4. **Secondary Memory** (external Flash, EEPROM, SD card) – slow, large
5. **Tertiary / Off-line Storage** (magnetic, optical) – slowest, largest

## 1.5 Broad Classification of Memory

```
Memory
├── By Volatility
│   ├── Volatile (loses data on power-off) → SRAM, DRAM
│   └── Non-Volatile (retains data) → ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash
│
├── By Access Pattern
│   ├── Random Access (any address in equal time) → RAM, ROM
│   └── Sequential Access (in order) → FIFO buffers, tapes
│
├── By Read/Write Capability
│   ├── Read-Write (R/W) → RAM
│   └── Read-Only (R/O) → ROM family
│
└── By Physical Location
    ├── On-chip / Internal (inside the MCU die)
    └── Off-chip / External (connected via memory bus)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Volatile Memory: The Working Memory of Embedded Systems

### 2.1.1 SRAM (Static Random Access Memory)

**SRAM** uses a **flip-flop (6 transistors per bit cell)** to store one bit. As long as power is supplied, the data is retained *without* refresh.

**Operational logic steps:**
1. The **Word Line (WL)** is asserted high by the row decoder.
2. The cell's access transistors (M5, M6) turn ON, connecting the flip-flop to the **Bit Lines (BL and BL̅)**.
3. A differential sense amplifier detects the small voltage difference between BL and BL̅ and amplifies it to full CMOS logic levels.
4. For a **Write**: data is driven onto BL/BL̅ forcefully enough to flip the latch.
5. For a **Read**: data flows from the cell to the sense amplifier.

**Characteristics:**
- Speed: **5–10 ns** typical access time
- Power: continuous (DC) consumption in HOLD state
- Density: **6T per bit** → low density, expensive
- Used for: **Cache, on-chip working RAM in MCUs (e.g., 32 KB SRAM in STM32)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Fact:** *SRAM cells are made using 6 MOSFETs. DRAM cells use just 1 MOSFET + 1 capacitor — making DRAM 6× denser but slower and refresh-dependent.*

### 2.1.2 DRAM (Dynamic Random Access Memory)

**DRAM** stores each bit as **charge on a tiny capacitor** (1T + 1C per cell). The charge leaks, so the cell must be **refreshed every ~64 ms**.

**Operational logic steps:**
1. **Row Address Strobe (RAS̅)** latches the row → entire row is moved to the sense amplifiers (row buffer).
2. **Column Address Strobe (CAS̅)** latches the column → specific bit is selected.
3. **Refresh**: A dedicated DRAM controller periodically issues a *RAS-only* cycle to recharge the capacitors without column access.

**Characteristics:**
- Speed: **50–100 ns** access time (slower than SRAM)
- Density: very high (used in PCs, not typical in small MCUs)
- Used for: large external memories in embedded Linux boards (Raspberry Pi, etc.)

| Parameter | SRAM | DRAM |
|---|---|---|
| Cell complexity | **6 transistors** | **1 transistor + 1 capacitor** |
| Refresh required | **No** | **Yes** (~every 64 ms) |
| Access time | **5–10 ns** | **50–100 ns** |
| Density per mm² | Low | High |
| Power (idle) | Higher (leakage) | Lower |
| Cost per bit | High | Low |
| Typical use | Cache, on-chip MCU RAM | Main RAM of PCs / SBCs |

## 2.2 Non-Volatile Memory (NVM): The Program Storage Family

### 2.2.1 ROM (Read-Only Memory)
- **Mask ROM**: programmed at the **fabrication/fab** level using lithographic masks. Lowest cost per unit, but high NRE (non-recurring engineering). Used in **mass-produced** toys and appliances.

### 2.2.2 PROM (Programmable ROM)
- **One-time programmable** using high-voltage/current to **blow fuses/antifuses**. Once blown, **cannot be reversed**.

### 2.2.3 EPROM (Erasable PROM)
- Uses a **floating-gate MOSFET**. Erased by **UV light** through a quartz window on the chip package (~20 minutes of UV exposure). Old technology.

### 2.2.4 EEPROM (Electrically Erasable PROM)
- Erased and programmed **byte-by-byte** using electrical signals.
- Endurance: ~**1,000,000** write cycles per cell.
- Used in embedded systems to store **configuration, calibration constants, device IDs**.

### 2.2.5 Flash Memory (The King of Embedded NVM)
- A descendant of EEPROM but erased in **blocks (sectors)** instead of bytes.
- **NOR Flash**: random access, fast read, slow write → used for **program code** (XIP — Execute In Place).
- **NAND Flash**: sequential access, high density, faster write → used for **data storage** (SSD, SD cards, eMMC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** *Most modern 32-bit MCUs (STM32, LPC, SAMD) use **on-chip NOR Flash** for program code and **embedded SRAM** for data. NOR Flash supports **XIP (Execute-In-Place)** — the CPU fetches instructions directly from Flash, unlike NAND which requires RAM shadowing.*

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Memory)

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = N \times w$ | Total capacity | bits |
| 2 | $C_{bytes} = \dfrac{N \times w}{8}$ | Capacity in bytes | bytes |
| 3 | $N = 2^{a}$ | Addressable locations ($a$ = address bus width) | locations |
| 4 | $C_{total} = 2^{a} \times w$ | Maximum addressable memory by an $a$-bit address bus | bits |
| 5 | $T_{access} = T_{setup} + T_{hold} + T_{propagation}$ | Memory access time | seconds |
| 6 | $BW = \dfrac{w}{T_{access}}$ | Memory bandwidth (data rate) | bits/second |
| 7 | $P_{dynamic} = C \times V_{dd}^{2} \times f$ | Dynamic power (CMOS SRAM/DRAM) | Watts |
| 8 | $P_{leakage} = V_{dd} \times I_{leak}$ | Static leakage power (SRAM hold) | Watts |
| 9 | $A_{mem} = \dfrac{M_{size}}{D_{density}}$ | Area of memory block on die | mm² |
| 10 | Refresh Interval $\leq 64\,\text{ms}$ | DRAM refresh constraint | milliseconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Note on Pipe-Symbol Convention:** Absolute value notation (e.g., $\vert a-b \vert$) is intentionally rendered with `\vert` to keep markdown table syntax intact.

## 2.4 Memory Parameters Used in Selection (Critical for KTU Numerical Problems)

When an embedded designer selects a memory chip, the following parameters must be matched:

1. **Capacity (size)** — total storage in bits/bytes/KB/MB.
2. **Word size (data bus width)** — 4, 8, 16, 32 bits.
3. **Access time ($t_{ACC}$)** — time from valid address to valid data output.
4. **Cycle time ($t_{CYC}$)** — minimum time between two successive memory operations.
5. **Power consumption** — active vs standby.
6. **Interface type** — parallel (SRAM/DRAM/parallel Flash) vs serial (SPI Flash, I²C EEPROM, QSPI).
7. **Endurance** — number of write/erase cycles (Flash: ~10K–100K, EEPROM: ~1M).
8. **Data retention** — how long data survives without power (typically 10–20 years).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Memory Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Wearable fitness band** | Internal Flash + 4 KB SRAM | Low power, small code footprint |
| **Automotive ECU** | External NOR Flash (for OTA updates) | Reliability, XIP, fast boot |
| **IoT sensor node** | SPI NOR Flash + SRAM | Serial interface saves pins |
| **Smartphone SoC** | LPDDR DRAM + eMMC/UFS NAND | High density, low power |
| **Satellite / Aerospace** | MRAM or FRAM (rad-hard) | Radiation tolerance |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Addressable Memory from Address Bus Width

**Problem Statement:** Given a microcontroller with an $a$-bit address bus, derive the maximum number of addressable memory locations and the maximum addressable memory in bytes (assuming byte-addressability).

**Step 1 — Define the address space.**
An $a$-bit address bus generates $a$ binary signals. Each signal combination is one address. The total number of unique combinations is:

$$N_{addresses} = 2^{a}$$

**Step 2 — Each address points to 1 byte (byte-addressable) or 1 word.**
If the memory is **byte-addressable**, the total addressable space in bytes is:

$$M_{max} = 2^{a} \quad \text{bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kilobytes/megabytes for a designer-friendly representation.**

$$M_{max} = \frac{2^{a}}{2^{10}} \text{ KB} = \frac{2^{a}}{2^{20}} \text{ MB}$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**
A 16-bit address bus (e.g., classic 8051 with external memory) can address:

$$2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64 \text{ KB}$$

A 32-bit address bus (e.g., ARM Cortex-M with 4 GB space):

$$2^{32} = 4 \text{ GB}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Memory Chip Expansion — Designing a 64 KB Memory from Multiple 16 KB Chips

**Problem Statement:** A designer has access to memory chips of size **16 KB × 8 bits**. Build a **64 KB × 8 bits** memory system. Determine the number of chips, the address lines required, and the address decoding logic.

**Step 1 — Determine the number of chips required.**

$$n_{chips} = \frac{\text{Total required memory}}{\text{Per-chip memory}} = \frac{64\,\text{KB}}{16\,\text{KB}} = 4 \text{ chips}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the number of address lines per chip.**

$$a_{per\_chip} = \log_2(16 \times 1024) = \log_2(2^{14}) = 14 \text{ lines}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the number of additional lines for chip selection (decoding).**

$$a_{total} = \log_2(64 \times 1024) = \log_2(2^{16}) = 16 \text{ lines}$$

The 2 extra lines (A15, A14) above the 14 chip-internal lines (A13…A0) are used to **decode** which of the 4 chips is selected. A **2-to-4 line decoder** (e.g., 74HC139) generates 4 chip-select signals.

**Step 4 — Build the address map.**

| Chip | A15 A14 (Select) | Active CS Line | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| IC1 | 0 0 | CS1̅ | 0x0000 – 0x3FFF |
| IC2 | 0 1 | CS2̅ | 0x4000 – 0x7FFF |
| IC3 | 1 0 | CS3̅ | 0x8000 – 0xBFFF |
| IC4 | 1 1 | CS4̅ | 0xC000 – 0xFFFF |

**Step 5 — Verify.**

$$4 \text{ chips} \times 16 \text{ KB} = 64 \text{ KB} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Memory Bandwidth Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A 32-bit wide memory has an access time of 10 ns. Find its bandwidth.

**Step 1 — Width in bits.**

$$w = 32 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the bandwidth formula.**

$$BW = \frac{w}{t_{access}}$$

Substituting:

$$BW = \frac{32 \text{ bits}}{10 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}}$$

**Step 3 — Final answer.**

$$BW = 3.2 \times 10^{9} \text{ bits/s} = 3.2 \text{ Gbps} = 400 \text{ MB/s}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Consumption of a CMOS SRAM (Dynamic + Static)

**Given:** $V_{dd} = 3.3\,$V, $C_{load} = 10\,$pF per bit, switching frequency $f = 100\,$MHz, $I_{leak} = 50\,\mu$A.

**Step 1 — Dynamic power.**

$$P_{dyn} = C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$$

$$P_{dyn} = 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (3.3)^{2} \cdot 100 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate each term.**

$$C = 10 \times 10^{-12} = 1 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{F}$$

$$V_{dd}^{2} = 10.89\,\text{V}^{2}$$

$$f = 1 \times 10^{8}\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply.**

$$P_{dyn} = 1 \times 10^{-11} \times 10.89 \times 1 \times 10^{8} = 1.089 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{W} = 10.89\,\text{mW}$$

**Step 4 — Static (leakage) power.**

$$P_{leak} = V_{dd} \cdot I_{leak} = 3.3 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 1.65 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{W} = 0.165\,\text{mW}$$

**Step 5 — Total power.**

$$P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{leak} = 10.89 + 0.165 = 11.055\,\text{mW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Dynamic power dominates by ~66×. This is why low-power MCUs **gate the clock** to memory blocks when not in use (clock gating).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Memory Trade-offs at a Glance

| Memory Type | Volatile? | Bit Cell | Programmable By | Erase Unit | Endurance (cycles) | Speed | Cost/bit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SRAM | Yes | 6T | Mask/Design | N/A (electrical) | Infinite | ★★★★★ | High |
| DRAM | Yes | 1T+1C | Mask | N/A (refresh) | Infinite | ★★★ | Low |
| Mask ROM | No | Diode/Fuse | Fab mask | N/A | N/A | ★★★★ | Very low (volume) |
| PROM | No | Fuse | User (OTP) | N/A | N/A (OTP) | ★★★★ | Medium |
| EPROM | No | FG-MOSFET | User + UV | Whole chip | ~100 erase | ★★★ | Medium |
| EEPROM | No | FG-MOSFET | User (electrical) | Byte | ~1,000,000 | ★★ | High |
| NOR Flash | No | FG-MOSFET | User (electrical) | Sector/Block | ~100,000 | ★★★★ | Medium |
| NAND Flash | No | FG-MOSFET | User (electrical) | Page/Block | ~10,000 | ★★ (burst) | Very low |
| FRAM | No | Ferroelectric | User (electrical) | Byte | ~10¹⁰ | ★★★★★ | Very high |
| MRAM | No | Magnetic | User (electrical) | Byte | ~10¹⁵ | ★★★★★ | Very high |

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Typical MCU Memory Organization (e.g., ARM Cortex-M class)

flowchart LR
    cpuA["CPU CORE\nARM Cortex M"]

    sdramA["EXTERNAL SDRAM\nLarge Data Buffers"]

    cpuA --> busA
    busA --> flashA
    busA --> sramA
    busA --> romA
    busA --> extBusA

    extBusA --> extSRAMA

```

## 4.3 Address Decoder Logic — 2-to-4 Decoder for 4-Chip Memory Bank

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    addrBus["ADDRESS BUS\nA15 A14"]
    decoderA["2 TO 4 DECODER\n74HC139"]
    cs1["CS1 ACTIVE\nIC1"]
    cs2["CS2 ACTIVE\nIC2"]
    cs3["CS3 ACTIVE\nIC3"]
    cs4["CS4 ACTIVE\nIC4"]

    addrBus --> decoderA
    decoderA -->|00| cs1
    decoderA -->|01| cs2
    decoderA -->|10| cs3
    decoderA -->|11| cs4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping Note:** A15 = MSB, A14 = LSB of the decoder input. Output Y0̅ selects 0x0000–0x3FFF, Y1̅ selects 0x4000–0x7FFF, and so on. The unselected chips present high-impedance (Z) on the data bus.

## 4.4 Memory Read/Write Timing Diagram (Mermaid State-Style Schematic)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> AddressValid: CPU places address on bus
    AddressValid --> ChipSelect: Decoder asserts CS line
    ChipSelect --> ReadEnable: OE asserted for READ
    ChipSelect --> WriteEnable: WE asserted for WRITE
    ReadEnable --> DataOnBus: Memory drives data
    DataOnBus --> LatchByCPU: CPU latches data
    WriteEnable --> DataWritten: Data stored in cell
    LatchByCPU --> Idle
    DataWritten --> Idle
```

## 4.5 Memory Interfacing with a Generic Microprocessor (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mpA["MICROPROCESSOR\nAddress Bus, Data Bus, Control Bus RD WR"]
    latchA["ADDRESS LATCH\n74HC573 to demux A0 A7 from AD0 AD7"]
    decA["ADDRESS DECODER\n74HC138 or 74HC139"]
    romA1["ROM CHIP\nLower Address Region"]
    ramA1["RAM CHIP 1\nBank 1"]
    ramA2["RAM CHIP 2\nBank 2"]

    mpA -->|A0 to A15| latchA
    mpA -->|D0 to D7| ramA1
    mpA -->|D0 to D7| ramA2
    mpA -->|D0 to D7| romA1
    mpA -->|RD WR MREQ| decA
    latchA -->|A0 to A7| romA1
    latchA -->|A0 to A7| ramA1
    latchA -->|A0 to A7| ramA2
    decA -->|CS ROM| romA1
    decA -->|CS RAM1| ramA1
    decA -->|CS RAM2| ramA2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** This is the **multiplexed address/data bus** technique used in 8085/8051-style systems. The low byte of address is time-multiplexed with the data bus and demultiplexed using the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal.
