## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SRAM | DRAM |
|---|---|---|
| Cell structure | **6 transistors** per bit | **1 transistor + 1 capacitor** per bit |
| Refresh | **Not required** | **Refresh required** every ~64 ms |
| Density | Lower (6T per cell) | Higher (1T+1C per cell) |
| Speed (access time) | **Faster** (~5–10 ns) | **Slower** (~50–100 ns) |
| Cost per bit | **High** | **Low** |
| Typical use | Cache, MCU on-chip RAM | Main memory of PCs/SBCs |
| Power consumption | Higher (leakage) | Lower (with refresh bursts) |

*(1 mark for any 3 distinct differences; 1 mark for table neatness; 1 mark for application/use distinction)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by Execute-In-Place (XIP)? Why is NOR Flash suitable for XIP while NAND Flash is not? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**XIP Definition:** *Execute-In-Place is a memory feature that allows the CPU to fetch and execute program instructions **directly from the non-volatile memory**, without the need to first copy (shadow) them into RAM.*

**Why NOR Flash supports XIP:**
1. NOR Flash has a **random-access architecture** — any byte can be read in equal time.
2. It provides a **standard memory bus interface** (parallel address + data lines).
3. **Fast read access time** (~70–100 ns) comparable to SRAM.

**Why NAND Flash does NOT support XIP:**
1. NAND is **page/block oriented** — data is read in pages (typically 2 KB or 4 KB), not byte-by-byte.
2. It has an **I/O interface** (not a parallel address bus), so the CPU cannot fetch instructions sequentially without a controller.
3. NAND contains **bad blocks** and requires an **Error Correction Code (ECC)** engine.
4. Therefore, NAND data must first be loaded into RAM using a **Flash controller / FTL**, then executed from RAM.

*(1 mark for XIP definition, 1 mark for NOR suitability, 1 mark for NAND limitation)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Memory Organization & Address Decoding**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set A]**

**(a)** A microcontroller has a **20-bit address bus** and a **16-bit data bus**. Compute:
(i) The maximum addressable memory locations.
(ii) The maximum addressable memory in bytes and MB.
(iii) The maximum data transfer size in one bus cycle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a memory system with a total capacity of **64 KB × 16 bits** using **16 KB × 8-bit** memory chips. Show the number of chips required, address line allocation, and chip-select decoding logic. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Given:** $a = 20$ bits, $w = 16$ bits.

**Step 1 — Maximum addressable locations.**

$$N = 2^{a} = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 = 1 \text{ M locations}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum addressable memory in bytes.**

$$M = N \times \frac{w}{8} = 1{,}048{,}576 \times 2 = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to MB.**

$$M = \frac{2{,}097{,}152}{2^{20}} = 2 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum data transfer per bus cycle.**

$$\text{Data per cycle} = 16 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ bytes per access}$$

**Step 5 — Final Summary.**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Addressable locations | 1,048,576 |
| Addressable memory | 2 MB |
| Data per bus cycle | 2 bytes (16 bits) |

---

### **Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Step 1 — Number of chips needed for word width 16 bits from 8-bit chips.**

$$n_{width} = \frac{16}{8} = 2 \text{ chips in parallel for one 16-bit word}$$

**Step 2 — Number of chips for 64 KB capacity.**

$$n_{capacity} = \frac{64\,\text{KB}}{16\,\text{KB}} = 4 \text{ groups}$$

**Step 3 — Total chips.**

$$n_{total} = n_{width} \times n_{capacity} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ chips of 16 KB × 8 bits}$$

**Step 4 — Address lines per chip.**

$$a_{per\_chip} = \log_2(16{,}384) = \log_2(2^{14}) = 14 \text{ lines (A0 to A13)}$$

**Step 5 — Address lines for inter-group decoding.**

$$a_{total} = \log_2(65{,}536) = \log_2(2^{16}) = 16 \text{ lines (A0 to A15)}$$

**Extra lines for decoder:** $a_{15}, a_{14}$ → **2-to-4 decoder (74HC139)**.

**Step 6 — Address Map.**

| Group | A15 A14 | CS Line | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Group 1 | 0 0 | CS1̅ | 0x0000 – 0x3FFF |
| Group 2 | 0 1 | CS2̅ | 0x4000 – 0x7FFF |
| Group 3 | 1 0 | CS3̅ | 0x8000 – 0xBFFF |
| Group 4 | 1 1 | CS4̅ | 0xC000 – 0xFFFF |

**Step 7 — Each group has 2 chips (low byte + high byte of 16-bit word).**

| Within Group | Chip Role | Data Lines Connected |
|---|---|---|
| Chip A | Low byte (D0–D7) | D0–D7 |
| Chip B | High byte (D8–D15) | D8–D15 |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Memory Types & Trade-offs**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set B]**

**(a)** Compare the **NOR Flash** and **NAND Flash** architectures in terms of cell structure, access method, read/write/erase granularity, endurance, and application suitability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wearable health-monitoring IoT device needs to store **8 MB of historical data** with low power and high reliability. It has a **128 KB on-chip Flash** and **32 KB on-chip SRAM**. Propose a memory architecture (internal + external) with justification. Compute the data retention requirement and the average power budget if the device writes **1 MB of data per day** and is battery-powered. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution to Q.B (a):**

| Parameter | NOR Flash | NAND Flash |
|---|---|---|
| Cell structure | Floating-gate MOSFET, parallel to bit line | Floating-gate MOSFET, in series (string) |
| Access method | **Random byte access** | **Page-based** (typically 2 KB/4 KB) |
| Read speed | Fast (~70 ns random read) | Slower per byte; fast in burst |
| Write granularity | Byte / Word | Page |
| Erase granularity | **Sector/Block** (e.g., 4–64 KB) | **Block** (e.g., 128–512 KB) |
| Endurance | ~100,000 cycles | ~10,000 cycles |
| Density | Lower | Higher (smaller cell) |
| XIP support | **Yes** | No (requires RAM shadow) |
| Bad block management | Not required | **Required** (factory-marked bad blocks) |
| ECC | Not required | **Mandatory** |
| Typical application | Program code in MCUs, BIOS, firmware | SSD, eMMC, SD card, USB drives |

---

### **Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify internal memory insufficiency.**
- 8 MB of historical data >> 128 KB on-chip Flash.
- An **external memory** is required.

**Step 2 — Memory choice.**
- Use **external SPI NOR Flash** (e.g., W25Q64, 8 MB) for non-volatile storage.
- Or **external SPI FRAM** if high endurance and byte-level writes are required.
- Use **32 KB on-chip SRAM** as a **write buffer** to batch data, then flush to external Flash in larger page writes (reduces write amplification).

**Step 3 — Address map (simplified).**

| Region | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Internal Flash | 128 KB | Bootloader + application code |
| Internal SRAM | 32 KB | Stack, heap, write buffer |
| External SPI Flash | 8 MB | Historical sensor data log |

**Step 4 — Data retention estimation.**
Industrial-grade SPI NOR Flash typically guarantees **20 years of data retention** at 85 °C and 10 years at 125 °C. For wearable (~body temp ~37 °C), 20 years is conservative. **No re-write needed** within device lifetime for retention; only endurance matters.

**Step 5 — Endurance check.**
- 1 MB/day × 365 days = 365 MB/year.
- Wear-leveling across 8 MB: every location written once every 8 MB / 365 MB/year ≈ **0.022 years** ≈ **8 days**.
- With even basic wear-leveling, each cell written ~45 times/year.
- SPI NOR Flash (100 K endurance) → device life ≈ 100,000 / 45 ≈ **2,222 years** theoretical.
- **Conclusion:** Endurance is **not a problem** with write buffering + wear leveling.

**Step 6 — Power budget (illustrative).**
- Active write current (SPI Flash): ~15 mA @ 3.3 V
- Write time per MB (assuming 1 MB/s SPI write speed): ~1 s
- Energy per write: $15 \text{ mA} \times 3.3 \text{ V} \times 1 \text{ s} = 49.5 \text{ mJ}$
- Daily energy for 1 MB: **~49.5 mJ/day = 0.00005 Wh/day**
- On a 300 mAh @ 3.7 V Li-Po (1.11 Wh battery): **~22,000 days of data writing** (dominated by sensor/MCU active power, not memory).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully!)**
> 
> 1. **Confusing byte-addressable with word-addressable memory:** KTU questions often ask "max memory in bytes" — confirm byte-addressability before dividing by 8.
> 2. **Wrong number of chips in expansion problems:** Students forget the **width multiplication** (e.g., 8-bit chips used to build 16-bit data bus require 2× chips, not just 1× for capacity).
> 3. **Mixing NOR and NAND properties:** NOR = XIP + random read; NAND = block + needs ECC. Don't swap them in the answer.
> 4. **Skipping the address map table:** KTU examiners allocate **at least 2 marks** to the address map in decoding problems — never omit it.
> 5. **Forgetting the refresh requirement** in DRAM answer — instant 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Reporting capacity in bits without conversion to bytes/KB/MB** — always include the unit and the conversion.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Memory capacity formula:** $C = N \times w$ bits, where $N = 2^{a}$ (number of locations) and $w$ = word width.
- ✅ **Maximum addressable space** with an $a$-bit address bus = $2^{a}$ bytes (byte-addressable).
- ✅ **SRAM** = 6T cell, no refresh, fast, used for cache & on-chip MCU RAM.
- ✅ **DRAM** = 1T+1C cell, needs refresh every ~64 ms, dense, used for PC/SBC main memory.
- ✅ **Mask ROM** = programmed at fab; **PROM** = OTP by user; **EPROM** = UV-erase; **EEPROM** = byte-electrical-erase; **Flash** = block-electrical-erase.
- ✅ **NOR Flash** → XIP, random read, program code. **NAND Flash** → sequential page, data storage, needs ECC & bad-block management.
- ✅ **Memory hierarchy:** Register > Cache > Main > Secondary > Tertiary (top to bottom: speed ↓, capacity ↑, cost/bit ↓).
- ✅ **Chip-select decoding** uses extra address lines + decoder (74HC138 / 74HC139) to map memory banks.
- ✅ **Address map table** is mandatory in expansion problems — always show base & end addresses.
- ✅ **Memory access time** = $t_{AA}$ (address to output) in datasheet; bandwidth $BW = w / t_{access}$.
- ✅ **Volatility:** SRAM & DRAM are volatile; all ROM family + Flash + FRAM + MRAM are non-volatile.
- ✅ **Endurance order (high → low):** MRAM > FRAM > EEPROM > NOR Flash > NAND Flash > DRAM/SRAM (infinite).
- ✅ **FRAM & MRAM** are emerging — high endurance, byte-writable, low power, used in metering, medical implants, aerospace.
- ✅ **Power dissipation in CMOS memory:** $P = C V_{dd}^{2} f + V_{dd} I_{leak}$.
- ✅ **SPI/I²C memories** save pins; **parallel memories** are faster — design trade-off.
- ✅ **XIP (Execute-In-Place)** = CPU runs code directly from NVM — supported only by parallel NOR Flash (and some SPI NOR with XIP mode).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Devices and Systems - ROM-Flash
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Memory Devices and Systems - ROM-Flash

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Memory Devices and Systems — ROM & Flash

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Read-Only Memory (ROM)** is a class of non-volatile semiconductor memory in which data is permanently or semi-permanently stored and can only be read during normal operation. **Flash Memory** is an advanced form of Electrically Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory (EEPROM) that uses a single transistor cell architecture, enabling high-density, block-erasable, non-volatile storage in embedded systems.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of memory devices like a **library system**:

- **ROM** is like a **printed reference book** — the information is already engraved by the publisher (manufacturer), and you can only *read* it. You cannot write new content with a normal pen. However, certain ROMs (like Flash) act like **whiteboard notebooks** — you can erase a chapter and rewrite it, but the process is slower than just flipping pages.
- **Flash Memory** is the **modern whiteboard** in every embedded device — it stores the bootloader, firmware, and even user data (like the songs on your MP3 player or the apps on your router). It keeps data **even when power is off** (non-volatile), unlike your brain's short-term memory (RAM).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:**
> - **RAM** = Volatile (data lost on power-off) → Used for temporary workspace.
> - **ROM/Flash** = Non-Volatile (data retained without power) → Used for permanent firmware storage.

### Classification of ROM Family (KTU Syllabus Focus)

| Memory Type | Programmability | Erasability | Typical Use Case |
|-------------|-----------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Mask ROM** | Factory programmed | Not erasable | Mass-produced consumer electronics |
| **PROM** (Programmable ROM) | User-once programmable | Not erasable | Prototyping, low-volume production |
| **EPROM** (Erasable PROM) | User-programmable | UV light (whole chip) | Development & debugging |
| **EEPROM** (Electrically EPROM) | User-programmable | Byte-level electrical | Storing small config data |
| **Flash Memory** | User-programmable | Block-level electrical | Firmware, solid-state drives, IoT |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Highlighted in KTU:**
> - **Endurance:** Typically **10,000 to 1,000,000 erase-write cycles** per sector.
> - **Retention:** Data retention of **10 to 20 years** at room temperature.
> - **Access Time:** Ranges from **25 ns to 200 ns** depending on type.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ROM Architecture — Operational Breakdown

A ROM cell is fundamentally a **transistor array** organized as a matrix of rows (word lines) and columns (bit lines). The stored bit is encoded by the **physical presence or absence of a connection** between the word line and bit line at each crosspoint.

**Logic Steps of ROM Operation:**

1. **Address Decoding:** The CPU presents an address on the address bus. The address decoder activates the corresponding word line.
2. **Word Line Activation:** The selected word line goes HIGH, turning ON the transistors in that row.
3. **Bit Line Sensing:** Where a transistor exists, the bit line is pulled LOW (logic 0). Where no transistor exists, the bit line remains HIGH through a pull-up resistor (logic 1).
4. **Output Buffering:** Sense amplifiers read the bit lines and place the data on the data bus.
5. **Chip Select (CS̄):** When CS̄ is HIGH, the output buffers are in high-impedance (Hi-Z) state, disabling the chip from the bus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Embedded Systems:**
> The bootloader code in microcontrollers (like STM32, LPC2148, ATmega328) is stored in Flash/ROM. The CPU fetches the reset vector from address 0x00000000 on power-up — this fetch relies on ROM's predictable, deterministic read access.

### 2.2 Flash Memory Deep Dive

Flash memory evolved to overcome EEPROM's two major limitations: **larger cell size** and **slower write speed**. Flash uses a **single floating-gate transistor** per cell.

#### 2.2.1 The Floating-Gate Transistor (FGFET)

A standard MOSFET is modified to have an **electrically isolated floating gate** between the control gate and the channel. By applying precise voltages, electrons can be **tunneled** through a thin oxide layer onto the floating gate using a phenomenon called **Fowler-Nordheim Tunneling**.

- **Programmed (Logic 0):** Electrons trapped on floating gate → raises threshold voltage → cell reads as "0".
- **Erased (Logic 1):** Electrons removed from floating gate → normal threshold voltage → cell reads as "1".

#### 2.2.2 NOR Flash vs NAND Flash

| Feature | NOR Flash | NAND Flash |
|---------|-----------|------------|
| **Cell Structure** | Parallel connection of cells to bit line | Cells connected in series (8/16/32 in a string) |
| **Random Access** | **Yes** (byte/word addressable) | **No** (page-based access) |
| **Read Speed** | **Fast** (25–70 ns) | Slower (serial page read) |
| **Write/Erase Speed** | Slow erase | **Fast** erase and write |
| **Density** | Lower (1–16 Mbit typical) | **High** (multi-Gbit) |
| **Endurance** | ~100,000 cycles/sector | ~10,000 cycles/page |
| **XIP (Execute-In-Place)** | **Supported** | Not supported |
| **KTU Typical Use** | Bootloader, firmware code in MCUs | Mass storage, SD cards, SSDs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> In microcontrollers like **LPC2148 (ARM7)**, the on-chip Flash is **NOR Flash** because the CPU needs to execute code directly from flash (XIP). In SD cards and SSDs, **NAND Flash** is preferred for its density advantage.

### 2.3 Read, Program (Write), and Erase Operations

- **READ Operation:** Word line raised to ~V_CC (e.g., 3.3 V). Cell current is sensed to determine the bit.
- **PROGRAM Operation:** High voltage (~10–12 V) applied between control gate and source/drain. Electrons tunnel onto floating gate.
- **ERASE Operation:** Reverse voltage applied. Electrons tunnel off the floating gate back to the channel. Erase happens in **blocks (sectors)**, not bytes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *block erase* with *page write*. In Flash, **erase is always block-level**, but **write/program is page-level** (e.g., 256 bytes to 4 KB pages). This asymmetry causes the classic *write-amplification* problem in SSDs.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| **Memory Capacity** | $N = 2^{n} \times m$ | $n$ = address lines, $m$ = data width (bits) |
| **Total Addressable Memory** | $A = 2^{A_{lines}}$ | in bytes/words |
| **Access Time** | $t_{acc} = t_{decode} + t_{sense} + t_{buffer}$ | nanoseconds (ns) |
| **Effective Write Time** | $t_{w} = t_{prog} + t_{verify}$ | $t_{prog}$ ~ µs, $t_{verify}$ ~ ms |
| **Block Erase Time** | $t_{erase}$ | typically **1 ms to 100 ms per block** |
| **Endurance (Cycles)** | $E_{max} = 10^{4} \text{ to } 10^{6}$ | per sector/page |
| **Data Retention** | $T_{ret} = 10 \text{ to } 20 \text{ years}$ | at $T = 25^\circ C$ |
| **NAND String Current** | $I_{string} = \frac{V_{th}}{R_{series}}$ | Determines cell threshold |
| **Programming Voltage** | $V_{pp} = 10 \text{ to } 12 \text{ V}$ | Internally generated by charge pump |
| **Power Consumption (Active Read)** | $P = V_{CC} \times I_{active}$ | mW range |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> These formulas are used by embedded firmware engineers to calculate *boot time*, *flash wear-leveling lifetimes*, and to determine *wait states* in microcontroller memory interfacing (e.g., adding wait states when $t_{acc} > t_{CPU\_cycle}$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Memory Capacity from Address Lines

**Problem:** A NOR Flash memory chip has **18 address lines** and a **16-bit data bus**. Calculate its total storage capacity in Mbytes.

**Given:**
- Number of address lines: $A_{lines} = 18$
- Data bus width: $m = 16 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ bytes}$

**Step 1:** Calculate the number of unique addressable locations.

$$
N_{locations} = 2^{A_{lines}} = 2^{18}
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the exponent.

$$
N_{locations} = 262144 \text{ locations}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by the data width to get total bits.

$$
\text{Total Bits} = 2^{18} \times 16 = 262144 \times 16
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the product.

$$
\text{Total Bits} = 4194304 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to Mbytes (1 Mbyte = $2^{20}$ bytes = 1048576 bytes).

$$
\text{Total Bytes} = \frac{4194304}{8} = 524288 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Capacity in Mbytes} = \frac{524288}{1048576} = 0.5 \text{ Mbytes} = 512 \text{ Kbytes}
$$

**Conclusion:** The chip is a **512 Kbyte NOR Flash** — a very common size in mid-range ARM7/ARM Cortex-M0 microcontrollers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Boot Time from Flash Read Access

**Problem:** An ARM Cortex-M3 MCU runs at **72 MHz** and boots from a Flash with $t_{acc} = 55$ ns. How many **wait states** are required for reliable zero-wait-state operation?

**Given:**
- CPU clock: $f_{CPU} = 72 \text{ MHz}$
- Flash access time: $t_{acc} = 55 \text{ ns}$

**Step 1:** Compute the CPU clock period.

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{1}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate.

$$
T_{CPU} \approx 13.89 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare $T_{CPU}$ with $t_{acc}$. For zero wait states, we need $T_{CPU} \geq t_{acc}$.

$$
13.89 \text{ ns} < 55 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4:** The CPU cycle is faster than Flash access. We need wait states.

$$
N_{wait} = \left\lceil \frac{t_{acc}}{T_{CPU}} \right\rceil - 1
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute values.

$$
N_{wait} = \left\lceil \frac{55}{13.89} \right\rceil - 1
$$

$$
N_{wait} = \lceil 3.96 \rceil - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3
$$

**Conclusion:** **3 wait states** must be inserted. The MCU's FLASH_ACR (Access Control Register) is programmed with **LATENCY = 3**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Flash Endurance Lifetime Estimation

**Problem:** A 16 Mbyte Flash is written in **64 KB sectors**. Each sector endures **100,000 erase cycles**. The firmware writes **1 MB of data per hour** uniformly. Estimate the lifetime in years.

**Given:**
- Total flash: $16 \text{ Mbytes}$
- Sector size: $S = 64 \text{ Kbytes} = 65536 \text{ bytes}$
- Endurance per sector: $E = 100{,}000$ cycles
- Write rate: $R = 1 \text{ MB/hour}$

**Step 1:** Number of sectors.

$$
N_{sectors} = \frac{16 \text{ Mbytes}}{64 \text{ Kbytes}} = \frac{16 \times 1024}{64} = 256 \text{ sectors}
$$

**Step 2:** Total erase cycles across all sectors.

$$
E_{total} = N_{sectors} \times E = 256 \times 100000 = 2.56 \times 10^{7} \text{ sector-erases}
$$

**Step 3:** Sectors erased per hour (1 MB written = 16 sectors erased per hour).

$$
N_{erase/hr} = \frac{1 \text{ MB}}{64 \text{ KB}} = \frac{1024}{64} = 16 \text{ sectors/hour}
$$

**Step 4:** Total operating hours.

$$
t_{hours} = \frac{E_{total}}{N_{erase/hr}} = \frac{2.56 \times 10^{7}}{16} = 1.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to years.

$$
t_{years} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{6}}{8760} \approx 182.6 \text{ years}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even with simple wear-leveling, the Flash can last **~180 years** — but only if wear is uniform. Without wear-leveling, hot sectors die in months.

---

### 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix: ROM Family Comparison

| Parameter | Mask ROM | PROM | EPROM | EEPROM | NOR Flash | NAND Flash |
|-----------|----------|------|-------|---------|-----------|------------|
| **Programming Method** | Mask | Fuse | Hot e- injection | Tunneling | Tunneling | Tunneling |
| **Erase Method** | None | None | UV (20 min) | Byte-electrical | Block-electrical | Block-electrical |
| **Cell Size** | Smallest | Small | Large | Large (~2T) | Small (1T) | Smallest (1T) |
| **Bit per Cell** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 (SLC) / 2 (MLC) / 3 (TLC) | 1/2/3 |
| **Speed** | Fast read | Fast read | Fast read | Slow write | Fast read | Fast burst |
| **Cost/bit** | **Lowest** | Low | Medium | High | Medium | **Lowest** |
| **Reprogrammable** | No | No | Yes (UV) | Yes (in-circuit) | Yes (in-circuit) | Yes (in-circuit) |
| **XIP Capable** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | **Yes** | No |
| **KTU Example** | Game cartridges | Fuse-link | Ceramic window chip | I2C 24Cxx | STM32 internal | SD card, eMMC |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ROM Memory Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpu["CPU / Microcontroller"] -->|Address Bus A0-An| addrdec["Address Decoder"]
    cpu -->|Chip Select CS| ctrl["Control Logic"]
    cpu -->|OE Read Enable| ctrl
    ctrl --> addrdec
    addrdec --> wl["Word Line Driver Matrix"]
    wl --> cell1["ROM Cell Row 0"]
    wl --> cell2["ROM Cell Row 1"]
    wl --> cellN["ROM Cell Row N"]
    cell1 --> sa["Sense Amplifiers"]
    cell2 --> sa
    cellN --> sa
    sa --> mux["Output Multiplexer"]
    mux -->|D0-Dm Data Bus| cpu
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The CPU presents an address and asserts **CS̄** (chip select, active low).
- The address decoder activates exactly one **word line**.
- The corresponding ROM cells drive the bit lines based on whether a transistor exists at that crosspoint.
- Sense amplifiers and the output buffer present the data to the data bus.

---

### 4.2 Floating-Gate Transistor Cell (FGFET)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cg["Control Gate\nVg = Vpp ~10V"] --> oxide1["Thin Oxide\nTunnel Layer"]
    oxide1 --> fg["Floating Gate\nIsolated"]
    fg --> oxide2["Gate Oxide"]
    oxide2 --> ch["P-Substrate Channel"]
    ch --> s["Source"]
    ch --> d["Drain"]
    fg -.->|"Trapped e-"| oxide1
```

**Functionality:**
- **Program:** Electrons tunnel from channel → floating gate via FN tunneling (raises Vth).
- **Erase:** Electrons tunnel from floating gate → source (lowers Vth).
- **Read:** Vg = VCC (3.3 V); cell current is sensed — high Vth = "0", low Vth = "1".

---

### 4.3 NOR vs NAND Flash Array Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nor["NOR Flash Array"]
        nor1["BL0 ---[T1]--- GND"]
        nor2["BL0 ---[T2]--- GND"]
        nor3["BL0 ---[Tn]--- GND"]
        norwl["WL0, WL1, ... WLn (parallel to all cells)"]
    end
    subgraph nand["NAND Flash String"]
        na1["BL ---[T1]---[T2]---...---[Tn]--- GND"]
        nawl["WL0, WL1, ... WLn (each controls one cell in series)"]
    end
```

**Key Difference:**
- **NOR:** Each cell's source is independently tied to GND → **fast random read** but **larger cell area**.
- **NAND:** Cells in series share a single ground path → **compact area** but **slower random access**.

---

### 4.4 Flash Read-Write-Erase State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Read: CS=0, OE=0
    Read --> Idle
    Idle --> Program: CS=0, WE=0, Vpp=10V
    Program --> Verify: t_program done
    Verify --> Program: Cell below Vth (retry)
    Verify --> Idle: Cell programmed
    Idle --> EraseBlock: Block erase command
    EraseBlock --> VerifyErase: t_erase done
    VerifyErase --> EraseBlock: Vth too high
    VerifyErase --> Idle: Erase complete
```

**Operational Notes:**
- All transitions are governed by the Flash memory controller's command state machine (e.g., CFI in NOR, ONFI/Toggle in NAND).
- The **verify steps** are essential to avoid bit errors and to bound programming/erase times.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Mask ROM and PROM. Mention one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Mask ROM | PROM |
|--------|----------|------|
| Programming | Done at the fabrication stage using a custom photo-mask | Done by the user using a PROM programmer (blowing fuses) |
| Cost (low-volume) | Very expensive (mask charges) | Cheap |
| Cost (high-volume) | Very cheap per unit | Costly per unit |
| Reprogrammable | No | No (one-time programmable) |
| Application | Mobile phone keypad firmware, calculator chips | Prototyping, low-volume industrial controllers, military-grade secure storage |

*Key Exam Point:* Mention the **bipolar fuse / antifuse** mechanism in PROM. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is Flash memory preferred over EEPROM for high-density applications? Give two reasons.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Smaller cell size:** Flash uses a **single transistor (1T) cell**, whereas EEPROM requires **two transistors (2T)** per cell (one for selection, one for storage). This gives Flash nearly **2x the density** of EEPROM at the same process node.
2. **Block erase capability:** Flash erases in **large blocks (sectors)**, dramatically reducing the per-bit erase overhead compared to EEPROM's byte-level erase. This makes Flash much faster for bulk data updates.
3. **Lower cost per bit:** Due to the smaller cell, Flash has a significantly lower manufacturing cost per Mbit, making it the technology of choice for SSDs, SD cards, and firmware storage in MCUs.

*Key Exam Point:* The term **1T vs 2T cell** is a high-value KTU phrase. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **internal structure and operation of a NOR Flash memory cell** with a neat diagram. Discuss the Fowler-Nordheim tunneling mechanism for program and erase operations.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare **NOR and NAND Flash** architectures in detail. Justify why NOR Flash is used for storing bootloader code in embedded microcontrollers.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The NOR Flash cell is based on a **split-gate or stacked-gate floating-gate MOSFET**.

1. **Cell Structure:**
   - A **control gate (CG)** connected to the word line.
   - A **floating gate (FG)** electrically isolated by a thin tunnel oxide (~7–10 nm) below and a thicker inter-poly oxide above.
   - **Source** and **drain** connected to bit line and source line.

2. **Read Operation:**
   - $V_{CG} = V_{CC}$ (~3.3 V) is applied to the word line.
   - If the floating gate is **charged** (electrons trapped), threshold voltage $V_{th} \gg V_{CC}$ → **no channel current** → bit = **"0"**.
   - If the floating gate is **discharged**, $V_{th} < V_{CC}$ → **channel conducts** → bit = **"1"**.

3. **Program Operation (FN Tunneling):**
   - A high voltage $V_{pp} = 10$–$12$ V is applied to the control gate; source is grounded.
   - The strong vertical electric field causes electrons to **quantum-mechanically tunnel** from the channel through the thin oxide onto the floating gate.
   - This is the **Fowler-Nordheim Tunneling** effect, governed by:
$$
J_{FN} = A \cdot E_{ox}^{2} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-B}{E_{ox}}\right)
$$
     where $E_{ox}$ is the oxide field and $A$, $B$ are constants.

4. **Erase Operation:**
   - Reverse polarity: source/drain raised to $V_{pp}$, control gate grounded.
   - Electrons tunnel **back** from the floating gate to the source, restoring $V_{th}$ to its original value.

5. **Block Erase:** All cells in a sector share common source diffusion, enabling simultaneous erase of kilobytes to megabytes in one operation.

**Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.2 (FGFET Diagram).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | NOR Flash | NAND Flash |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| **Cell connection** | Each cell connected in **parallel** to bit line | Cells connected in **series** in a string |
| **Random read** | Byte/word addressable, fast | Page-based, no byte access |
| **Read access time** | **25–70 ns** | 15–25 µs (page load) + serial shift |
| **Write speed** | Slow (µs) | Fast (page program in ~200 µs) |
| **Erase speed** | Slow (1 s/block) | Fast (2 ms/block) |
| **Endurance** | 100k cycles | 10k–100k cycles |
| **Density** | Lower | **Very high** (multi-Gbit) |
| **XIP support** | **Yes** | No |
| **Interface** | SRAM-like parallel | Multiplexed I/O, ONFI/Toggle |
| **Cost per bit** | Higher | **Lower** |
| **KTU example** | LPC2148 internal Flash, STM32 code region | SD cards, eMMC, SSDs |

**Justification for Bootloader Storage in MCUs:**

- The **reset vector** must be fetchable in **one CPU cycle** to begin execution. NOR Flash supports **XIP (Execute-In-Place)** because its random byte access matches the CPU's address bus protocol.
- Bootloaders are **small (typically < 64 KB)** but require **deterministic, low-latency reads**, which NOR Flash provides.
- NAND Flash cannot execute code in place because it is page-oriented and includes bad-block management overhead.
- Hence, **all major MCUs (ARM Cortex-M, AVR, PIC) use NOR Flash for on-chip code storage**, reserving NAND for external data storage.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a block diagram, explain the **organization of a Mask ROM**. How is data stored physically in a Mask ROM? Why is it called "Read-Only"?

**(b)** [7 Marks] A Flash memory chip has **20 address lines** and a **16-bit data bus**. Calculate:
   - (i) The total capacity in **Kbytes**.
   - (ii) The number of **wait states** required if the CPU runs at **100 MHz** and $t_{acc} = 50$ ns.
   - (iii) The **endurance lifetime in years** if each 128 KB block is erased **100,000 times**, and the firmware writes **2 MB/hour** uniformly across the entire chip.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Block Diagram of Mask ROM:**
   - Comprises: **Address Decoder**, **Memory Matrix** (rows × columns of transistors), **Output Buffers**, and **Chip Select logic**.

2. **Physical Data Storage:**
   - Data is encoded as the **presence or absence of a MOSFET at each crosspoint** of the word-line and bit-line matrix.
   - **Transistor present** at (row $i$, column $j$) → bit line pulled LOW → stored bit = **"0"**.
   - **No transistor** at the crosspoint → bit line remains HIGH through pull-up → stored bit = **"1"**.
   - The pattern of transistors is **defined by a custom photo-mask** used during IC fabrication, hence the name "Mask ROM".

3. **Why "Read-Only":**
   - Once fabricated, the transistor pattern is **physically fixed** in the silicon.
   - No electrical means exists to add or remove transistors post-fabrication.
   - Hence the user can only **read** the stored data — write/erase is impossible.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Capacity Calculation:**

$$
N_{locations} = 2^{20} = 1048576
$$

$$
\text{Total Bytes} = 1048576 \times 2 = 2097152 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Capacity} = \frac{2097152}{1024} = 2048 \text{ Kbytes} = 2 \text{ Mbytes}
$$

**(ii) Wait States Calculation:**

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 10 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
N_{wait} = \left\lceil \frac{50}{10} \right\rceil - 1 = 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ wait states}
$$

**(iii) Endurance Lifetime:**

$$
N_{blocks} = \frac{2 \text{ Mbytes}}{128 \text{ Kbytes}} = \frac{2 \times 1024}{128} = 16 \text{ blocks}
$$

$$
E_{total} = 16 \times 100000 = 1.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ block-erases}
$$

$$
N_{erase/hr} = \frac{2 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ KB}} = \frac{2048}{128} = 16 \text{ blocks/hour}
$$

$$
t_{hours} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{6}}{16} = 100000 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
t_{years} = \frac{100000}{8760} \approx 11.4 \text{ years}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing Flash erase with Flash write** — Erase is **block-level**; write is **page-level**. Many students state "byte-level erase for Flash", losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in wait-state problems** — Always state $T_{CPU}$ in ns before dividing $t_{acc}$.
> 3. **Not mentioning Fowler-Nordheim (FN) Tunneling by name** — KTU examiners specifically allocate 1–2 marks for the FN tunneling mechanism in NOR Flash cell questions.
> 4. **Confusing NOR and NAND cell topology** — NOR has each cell to GND individually; NAND has series-connected cells with select transistors at the ends. Drawing them as identical is a serious mark loser.
> 5. **Omitting the role of Vpp charge pump** — On-chip charge pumps generate the 10–12 V from 3.3 V. Mentioning this shows deeper understanding and earns a bonus mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ROM Hierarchy:** Mask ROM → PROM → EPROM → EEPROM → Flash. Each step adds reprogrammability at the cost of cell size.
- **Flash is the dominant non-volatile memory** in modern embedded systems — both on-chip (NOR) and off-chip (NAND).
- **NOR Flash = XIP-capable, random byte access, used for code.**
- **NAND Flash = high density, page-based, used for data storage.**
- **Fowler-Nordheim Tunneling** is the physical mechanism behind Flash program/erase.
- **Endurance (10⁴–10⁶ cycles)** and **retention (10–20 years)** are the two key reliability metrics.
- **Wait states** are required when Flash $t_{acc}$ > CPU $T_{CPU}$, configured via the Flash Access Control Register.
- **Block erase is mandatory** in Flash — you cannot erase a single byte electrically.
- **Wear-leveling algorithms** (used in SSDs and MCUs) extend Flash lifetime by distributing writes uniformly across sectors.
- **KTU must-know terms:** 1T cell, floating gate, Fowler-Nordheim tunneling, XIP, wait state, charge pump, CFI, ONFI, sector/block, page, wear-leveling, bad-block management.
- **Programming voltage Vpp = 10–12 V** is internally generated by a charge pump; do not apply it externally unless specified in the datasheet.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EEPROM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** EEPROM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**EEPROM (Electrically Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory)** is a type of non-volatile semiconductor memory device in which individual bytes of data can be electrically erased and reprogrammed by the application of controlled voltage pulses, without requiring removal of the chip from the circuit. EEPROM is a critical building block in embedded system design for storing small volumes of configuration data, calibration constants, device parameters, and system state information that must persist across power cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** EEPROM is classified as a *non-volatile, byte-level rewritable memory* that uses *Fowler–Nordheim quantum-mechanical tunneling* (or in older designs, hot-electron injection) through a thin oxide layer to alter the threshold voltage of a floating-gate MOSFET cell, thereby storing binary information.

The standardized nomenclature used in KTU board examinations and the IEEE memory taxonomy classifies EEPROM under the broader family of **Non-Volatile Memory (NVM)** technologies, alongside EPROM, Flash EEPROM, FRAM, MRAM, and PCM. However, EEPROM is uniquely distinguished from Flash by its **byte-level granularity** for both read and write operations, while Flash operates on block/sector granularity for erase.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Writable Chalkboard" Memory Model**

Imagine a classroom chalkboard (the memory cell) with two stable states:
- A **clean chalkboard** (representing binary `1`).
- A **chalkboard with a faint mark** (representing binary `0`).

In an **EPROM**, the chalkboard can only be *cleaned* by taking it outside, washing it with solvents, and exposing it to strong ultraviolet light in a special eraser box — a process that takes 20–30 minutes. In an **EEPROM**, however, the chalkboard has built-in invisible mini-erasers wired to a small electric current. Applying a precise electrical pulse (of the order of 12 V to 21 V) to each cell **erases only that single cell** in a few milliseconds — without disturbing the rest of the board. This "individual cell cleaning" capability is the essence of EEPROM.

A second analogy: think of EEPROM as a **filing cabinet with a tiny electric motor on each drawer**. The motor can open the drawer (read), place a new document (program), or shred the old one (erase) — all electrically, one drawer at a time, without a janitor having to come in and clean the entire cabinet (which is what bulk erase does in older EPROMs).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics for EEPROM (Bolded for KTU High-Yield Recall):**

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Programming Voltage (Vpp)** | **+12 V to +21 V** | Required to inject/tunnel charge across the tunnel oxide |
| **Read Voltage (Vcc)** | **+1.8 V to +5.5 V** | Standard logic supply for read operations |
| **Endurance (Write/Erase Cycles)** | **1 × 10⁶ cycles per cell** | Number of times a single byte can be rewritten |
| **Data Retention** | **10 to 100 years** | Time data persists without power |
| **Cell Size (2-transistor NMOS)** | **~ 50–100 F²** (F = feature size) | Larger than DRAM cell (~6 F²) |
| **Programming Time per Byte** | **1 ms to 10 ms** | Slower than SRAM, faster than EPROM |
| **Access Time (Read)** | **50 ns to 250 ns** | Comparable to EPROM, slower than SRAM |
| **Tunnel Oxide Thickness** | **~ 7 nm to 10 nm** | Thin enough for quantum tunneling |

## 1.4 Placement in the Memory Hierarchy

In the classical embedded memory hierarchy (relevant to KTU Module 2: Memory Subsystem), EEPROM sits between **SRAM** (fastest, volatile, expensive) and **Mask ROM / Flash** (slowest write, non-volatile, cheap). The trade-off is precisely captured below:

$$
\text{Cost/Bit (Low)} \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \text{SRAM > DRAM > EEPROM > Flash > Mask ROM} \;\; \leftarrow \;\; \text{Write Speed (High)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 emphasizes the *design trade-offs* of memory selection in embedded systems. EEPROM is the preferred choice when **byte-level non-volatile updates** are needed (e.g., storing last-known-good firmware configuration, RTC backup registers, secure key storage).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of an EEPROM Cell

The fundamental storage element of an EEPROM is a **two-transistor (2T) floating-gate MOSFET cell**, although early designs used a single-transistor (1T) cell with a select gate. The two transistors are:

1. **Storage Transistor (M1):** Contains the floating gate that traps charge.
2. **Select Transistor (M2):** A conventional MOSFET that acts as an access switch, isolating the cell during read and program operations.

### 2.1.1 Cross-Sectional Anatomy of the Floating-Gate Cell

From bottom to top, the cell is constructed as a layered silicon structure:

1. **P-type silicon substrate** (the bulk, body terminal).
2. **Source and Drain n+ diffusions** (separated by a channel region).
3. **Tunnel Oxide Layer** — an ultra-thin SiO₂ layer (**~ 7–10 nm**) grown thermally between the substrate and the floating gate. This is the critical quantum-tunneling barrier.
4. **Polycrystalline Silicon Floating Gate** — electrically isolated on all sides; it is the charge storage node.
5. **Inter-Poly Oxide (ONO – Oxide-Nitride-Oxide)** — dielectric between the floating gate and the control gate.
6. **Polycrystalline Silicon Control Gate** — the externally accessible gate terminal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **floating gate is electrically isolated** — it has no ohmic connection to any node in the circuit. Charge placed on it remains trapped for years (the basis of data retention). The only way to add or remove charge is by **quantum-mechanical tunneling** through the tunnel oxide.

## 2.2 Operational Phases: Programming, Erase, and Read

The cell operates in three distinct electrical regimes. KTU frequently asks students to explain these three phases in 7-mark questions.

### Phase A — Programming (Writing '0' onto a cell that was erased to '1')

- Apply a **high positive voltage** (typically **+18 V to +21 V**) to the **control gate** with respect to the **source/drain/substrate** (grounded).
- A strong electric field develops across the thin tunnel oxide.
- **Fowler–Nordheim tunneling** of electrons from the channel (inversion layer) into the floating gate occurs.
- Electrons accumulate on the floating gate → its potential becomes **negative relative to the control gate**.
- The effective threshold voltage of the transistor **increases** (e.g., from ~ +1 V to ~ +6 V).
- Under normal read bias (~ +3 V on the control gate), the cell **does not turn on** → this is read as a logical **`0`**.

### Phase B — Erasing (Restoring '1' to the cell)

- Apply a **high negative or high positive voltage** of opposite polarity to the **source/drain** (or substrate) with respect to the control gate, depending on the manufacturer's cell design.
- A reverse electric field across the tunnel oxide **pulls electrons back out of the floating gate** to the channel (or to the source junction in some designs).
- The floating gate becomes neutral (or slightly positive).
- The threshold voltage **decreases** to the **erased state** (e.g., ~ +1 V).
- Under normal read bias, the cell **turns on** → this is read as a logical **`1`**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from EPROM:** In EPROM, erase is done via UV light providing photon energy to liberate trapped electrons. In EEPROM, erase is **fully electrical**, hence "Electrically Erasable."

### Phase C — Read (Sense Amplifier Operation)

- Apply normal Vcc (e.g., +3.3 V) to the control gate and the select transistor.
- If the cell is **erased (Vth low)**, the storage transistor conducts current → sense amplifier reads `'1'`.
- If the cell is **programmed (Vth high)**, the storage transistor remains OFF → sense amplifier reads `'0'`.

## 2.3 Quantum Mechanical Foundation: Fowler–Nordheim Tunneling

The programming/erase mechanism is governed by the **Fowler–Nordheim (FN) tunneling equation**, which gives the current density $J_{FN}$ across a thin oxide under a high electric field:

$$
J_{FN} \;=\; A \cdot E_{ox}^{2} \cdot \exp\!\left(\,-\frac{B}{E_{ox}}\,\right)
$$

Where:
- $E_{ox}$ = electric field across the tunnel oxide (V/cm),
- $A$ = pre-exponential constant (depends on effective electron mass in SiO₂),
- $B$ = constant proportional to the barrier height $\Phi_B$ raised to the $3/2$ power.

For SiO₂, the barrier height $\Phi_B \approx 3.1$ eV and the electron effective mass $m^* \approx 0.5 m_0$ are standard textbook values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **exponential dependence on $1/E_{ox}$** is the engineering insight: by increasing Vpp to ~20 V across a 10 nm oxide, the field reaches ~ 10 MV/cm, and tunneling current becomes practically usable. Lowering the voltage dramatically reduces leakage, which is why EEPROMs retain data for **decades** at room temperature.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is your exam-day cheat sheet. Memorize the units and typical values for full marks on numerical/design questions.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value or Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fowler–Nordheim tunneling current density | $J_{FN}$ | $A \cdot E_{ox}^{2} \exp(-B/E_{ox})$ | A/cm² |
| 2 | Tunnel oxide field | $E_{ox}$ | $V_{pp} / t_{ox}$ | V/cm |
| 3 | Programming time per byte | $t_{prog}$ | **1 ms to 10 ms** | ms |
| 4 | Endurance (write/erase cycles) | $N_{WE}$ | **10⁶ cycles** | cycles |
| 5 | Data retention time | $t_{ret}$ | **10–100 years** | years |
| 6 | Read access time | $t_{acc}$ | 50–250 ns | ns |
| 7 | Threshold voltage (erased) | $V_{th,erase}$ | ~ +1 V | V |
| 8 | Threshold voltage (programmed) | $V_{th,prog}$ | ~ +6 V | V |
| 9 | Cell area (2T) | $A_{cell}$ | 50–100 F² | F² |
| 10 | Charge stored on floating gate | $Q_{fg}$ | $C_{fg} \cdot V_{shift}$ | C |

**CRITICAL markdown safeguard:** absolute values and conditionals inside the table are written as $\vert x \vert$ and $a \le b$ (no bare `|` characters that would break the table).

## 2.5 Why EEPROM, Not Flash, for Embedded Configuration?

This is a recurring KTU 14-mark question. The decision matrix:

| Requirement | Best Memory | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Bulk firmware storage (hundreds of KB to MB) | **Flash** | Higher density, cheaper per bit |
| Frequent updates of small configuration data (e.g., Wi-Fi credentials, calibration) | **EEPROM** | Byte-level erase, no need to erase a 4 KB sector to change 16 bytes |
| Over-the-air (OTA) firmware update buffer | **External SPI Flash** | Larger capacity |
| RTC backup, security keys, unique device ID | **Internal EEPROM or emulated EEPROM in Flash** | Non-volatile, infrequent writes, fast random access |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Many modern microcontrollers (e.g., Atmel ATmega328P used in Arduino Uno) integrate a small **on-chip EEPROM** (typically 1 KB) specifically to store user data. STM32 and PIC microcontrollers often use **emulated EEPROM** in a dedicated Flash sector with wear-leveling firmware to mimic EEPROM's byte-level write semantics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Threshold Voltage Shift Due to Floating-Gate Charge

This derivation is the **gold-standard 7-mark answer** for KTU ESE questions on EEPROM physics.

### Step 1: Define the Floating-Gate Capacitance Network

The floating gate is coupled to the **control gate**, the **channel (substrate)**, and the **source/drain diffusions** through a network of parasitic capacitances. The capacitive divider model is shown schematically below.

Let:
- $C_{cg}$ = capacitance between control gate and floating gate,
- $C_{ch}$ = capacitance between channel and floating gate,
- $C_{sd}$ = capacitance between source/drain and floating gate.

### Step 2: Express the Floating-Gate Potential

By applying **charge conservation on the isolated floating gate**, the floating-gate potential $V_{fg}$ is related to the control-gate voltage $V_{cg}$, the channel voltage $V_{ch}$, and the stored charge $Q_{fg}$ by:

$$
V_{fg} \;=\; \frac{C_{cg}}{C_{total}} \cdot V_{cg} \;+\; \frac{C_{ch}}{C_{total}} \cdot V_{ch} \;-\; \frac{Q_{fg}}{C_{total}}
$$

Where the total capacitance is:

$$
C_{total} \;=\; C_{cg} + C_{ch} + C_{sd}
$$

> **Logic:** The floating gate is a node in a capacitive network. With no DC path, the only way its potential can change is by capacitive coupling from the control gate and channel, and by the addition/removal of physical charge $Q_{fg}$ via tunneling.

### Step 3: Relate Floating-Gate Potential to Threshold Voltage

For a standard MOSFET, the threshold condition at the surface (channel) is $V_{fg} - V_{ch} = V_{th,\,bare}$, where $V_{th,\,bare}$ is the threshold voltage of a reference cell with no floating gate. Substituting:

$$
V_{th,\,effective} \;=\; V_{th,\,bare} \;-\; \frac{C_{cg}}{C_{total}} \cdot (V_{cg} - V_{ch}) \;-\; \frac{Q_{fg}}{C_{total}}
$$

Wait — we need to be careful with signs. The proper form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{th,\,effective} \;&=\; V_{th,\,bare} \;-\; \frac{Q_{fg}}{C_{total}} \\
&\quad \text{(the charge term dominates the shift)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Threshold Shift

The shift in threshold voltage $\Delta V_{th}$ between the erased state ($Q_{fg} = 0$) and the programmed state ($Q_{fg} = Q_{p}$) is:

$$
\Delta V_{th} \;=\; \frac{Q_{p}}{C_{total}}
$$

> **Logic:** Each electron added to the floating gate (during programming) makes the floating gate more negative, requiring a larger positive voltage on the control gate to invert the channel. This raises $V_{th}$. Conversely, electron removal (erasure) lowers $V_{th}$.

### Step 5: Express the Stored Charge in Terms of Tunneling Current

If a programming pulse of duration $t_{prog}$ drives a tunneling current density $J_{FN}$ through the tunnel oxide of area $A_{ox}$:

$$
Q_{p} \;=\; J_{FN} \cdot A_{ox} \cdot t_{prog}
$$

Substituting the FN formula:

$$
Q_{p} \;=\; A \cdot E_{ox}^{2} \cdot \exp\!\left(\,-\frac{B}{E_{ox}}\,\right) \cdot A_{ox} \cdot t_{prog}
$$

### Step 6: Final Working Equation for Threshold Shift

$$
\boxed{\;
\Delta V_{th} \;=\; \frac{A \cdot E_{ox}^{2} \cdot A_{ox} \cdot t_{prog}}{C_{total}} \cdot \exp\!\left(\,-\frac{B}{E_{ox}}\,\right)
\;}
$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The threshold shift is **exponentially sensitive** to the tunnel oxide field $E_{ox}$, which is why precise control of Vpp is critical during programming. A 1 V change in Vpp can shift $\Delta V_{th}$ by hundreds of millivolts.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (For Practice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style 3-Mark Numerical:** A serial EEPROM cell has the following parameters. Compute the threshold voltage shift.

- $C_{total} = 2 \times 10^{-15}$ F (2 fF)
- $Q_{p} = 1.6 \times 10^{-15}$ C (≈ 10,000 electrons)

Apply the formula $\Delta V_{th} = Q_{p} / C_{total}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V_{th} \;&=\; \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{C}}{2 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{F}} \\[4pt]
&=\; 0.8\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A stored charge equivalent to just **10,000 electrons** produces an 800 mV threshold shift — easily distinguishable by a sense amplifier. This illustrates the extreme sensitivity of the floating-gate cell.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: EEPROM vs. Other Memory Technologies

This comparison is the **single most important KTU 14-mark question pattern** in Module 2.

| Feature | SRAM | DRAM | Mask ROM | EPROM | **EEPROM** | Flash (NOR/NAND) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Volatile? | Yes | Yes | No | No | **No** | No |
| Cell type | 6T CMOS | 1T + 1C | 1T (hard-wired) | 1T floating gate | **2T floating gate** | 1T floating gate |
| Erase mechanism | Electrical (data decays) | Electrical (refresh) | None (mask-only) | UV light (~20 min) | **Electrical (FN tunneling, byte-level)** | Electrical (block-level) |
| Write granularity | Byte | Byte | Not writable | Byte program | **Byte** | Block / sector / page |
| Endurance | Unlimited | Unlimited | N/A | ~ 100 cycles | **~ 10⁶ cycles** | 10⁴–10⁵ cycles |
| Density (cell size) | Large (~120 F²) | Small (~6 F²) | Smallest (~4 F²) | Medium | **Large (~50–100 F²)** | Small (~10 F²) |
| Speed (read) | ~ 1 ns | ~ 50 ns | ~ 50 ns | ~ 100 ns | **~ 50–250 ns** | 25–100 ns |
| Speed (write) | ~ 1 ns | ~ 50 ns | N/A | ~ 1 ms | **~ 1–10 ms** | 1 µs–1 ms |
| Power (standby) | Leakage | Refresh current | Zero | Zero | **Near zero** | Near zero |
| Typical use | CPU cache, registers | Main memory | Firmware | Legacy firmware | **Config, keys, RTC backup** | Code storage, SSD |
| Reprogrammable in-circuit? | Yes | Yes | No | No | **Yes** | Yes (block erase first) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Pattern:** KTU typically awards 7 marks for the comparison table and 7 marks for explaining *why* EEPROM is preferred for embedded configuration storage over Flash. Always include both the table and the reasoning in your answer.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Comparison: Serial vs. Parallel EEPROM Interfaces

Embedded systems use two dominant EEPROM bus interfaces. KTU Module 2 includes peripheral interfacing; this is a frequently asked question.

| Feature | Parallel EEPROM (e.g., 28C256) | Serial EEPROM (e.g., 24C02, 25LCxxx) |
|---|---|---|
| Data bus width | 8 bits | 1 bit (I2C) or 1–4 bits (SPI) |
| Pin count | 28–32 pins | 4–8 pins |
| Access time | 70–200 ns | 1–10 µs |
| Capacity | 16 Kbit to 1 Mbit | 1 Kbit to 4 Mbit |
| Typical use | Legacy microcontrollers, DMA-driven designs | Modern MCUs, IoT devices |
| Example part | Atmel AT28C256 | Microchip 24LC256, 25LC1024 |
| Power consumption | Higher | **Much lower** (suits battery devices) |
| Board complexity | High (more traces, decoupling) | Low (2-wire or 4-wire) |

> **KTU Engineering Insight:** In the era of 32-bit ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers, **serial I²C and SPI EEPROMs dominate** because of pin constraints. The I²C bus supports up to 128 Kbit addressing space; the SPI bus supports higher speeds (> 20 MHz).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: EEPROM Cell Cross-Sectional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the layered cross-section of a 2T floating-gate EEPROM cell. Note: every node ID is purely alphanumeric and contains no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[CONTROL GATE Terminal]:::top
    A2[POLY CONTROL GATE Layer]:::metal
    A3[INTER POLY OXIDE ONO Layer]:::oxide
    A4[FLOATING GATE Poly Layer]:::fg
    A5[TUNNEL OXIDE Thin SiO2 Layer]:::tunnel
    A6[P TYPE Silicon Substrate Channel]:::channel
    A7[SOURCE N Plus Diffusion]:::diff
    A8[DRAIN N Plus Diffusion]:::diff
    A9[SELECT TRANSISTOR M2 Gate]:::select
    A10[BODY Substrate Contact]:::body

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7
    A6 --> A8
    A9 --> A6

    classDef top fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef metal fill:#cbd5e1,stroke:#1e293b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef oxide fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fg fill:#f9a8d4,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tunnel fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef channel fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef diff fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef select fill:#fcd34d,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef body fill:#d1d5db,stroke:#374151,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: EEPROM Program/Erase/Read Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    B1[Start Operation]:::start
    B2{Operation Mode Selected}:::decision
    B3[Apply Vpp 18 to 21 V on Control Gate]:::hv
    B4[Source Substrate Grounded]:::gnd
    B5[Fowler Nordheim Tunneling Electrons Into Floating Gate]:::physics
    B6[Threshold Voltage Rises to Vth prog]:::result
    B7[Cell Stores Logical 0]:::logic
    B8[Apply Reverse Bias to Source Drain]:::hv
    B9[Electrons Tunnel Out of Floating Gate]:::physics
    B10[Threshold Voltage Falls to Vth erase]:::result
    B11[Cell Stores Logical 1]:::logic
    B12[Apply Vcc 1.8 to 5.5 V on Control Gate]:::read
    B13[Sense Amplifier Detects Channel Current]:::sense
    B14{Is Vth low or high}:::decision
    B15[Output Logical 1 Erased Cell]:::logic
    B16[Output Logical 0 Programmed Cell]:::logic

    B1 --> B2
    B2 -->|Program| B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B7
    B2 -->|Erase| B8
    B8 --> B9
    B9 --> B10
    B10 --> B11
    B2 -->|Read| B12
    B12 --> B13
    B13 --> B14
    B14 -->|Vth low| B15
    B14 -->|Vth high| B16

    classDef start fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hv fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gnd fill:#1f2937,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef physics fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef read fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sense fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: System-Level Block Diagram of an Embedded System Using EEPROM

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1[Microcontroller MCU Core]:::cpu
    C2[CPU ALU and Registers]:::core
    C3[SRAM Volatile Working Memory]:::sram
    C4[Flash Non Volatile Code Memory]:::flash
    C5[EEPROM Non Volatile Config Data]:::eeprom
    C6[Serial Interface I2C Bus]:::bus
    C7[Serial Interface SPI Bus]:::bus
    C8[External EEPROM Chip 24LC256]:::ext
    C9[Real Time Clock RTC Module]:::rtc
    C10[Sensor ADC Interface]:::sensor
    C11[UART Debug Console]:::uart
    C12[Power Management Unit]:::pmu

    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C1 --> C6
    C1 --> C7
    C1 --> C11
    C1 --> C12
    C6 --> C8
    C6 --> C9
    C7 --> C10
    C1 -.Writes calibration constants.-> C5
    C5 -.Stores last good config.-> C1

    classDef cpu fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sram fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flash fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef eeprom fill:#f9a8d4,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rtc fill:#cbd5e1,stroke:#1e293b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef uart fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pmu fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram: EEPROM Write Cycle Polling Protocol

```mermaid
graph TD
    D1[MCU Issues WRITE Command]:::start
    D2[MCU Sends Slave Address and Word Address]:::tx
    D3[MCU Sends Data Byte]:::tx
    D4[EEPROM Enters Internal Write Cycle 1 to 10 ms]:::wait
    D5[EEPROM Acknowledges by Releasing ACK]:::ack
    D6[MCU Polls EEPROM with ACK Sequence]:::poll
    D7{EEPROM Responds with ACK}:::decision
    D8[EEPROM Ready for Next Operation]:::ready
    D9[MCU Waits and Retries Polling]:::retry
    D10[Write Cycle Complete]:::done

    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> D5
    D5 --> D6
    D6 --> D7
    D7 -->|Yes ACK| D8
    D7 -->|No NACK| D9
    D9 --> D6
    D8 --> D10

    classDef start fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tx fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wait fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ack fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef poll fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ready fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef retry fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef done fill:#cbd5e1,stroke:#1e293b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The **red-bordered node** in the architecture diagram (C5) marks the EEPROM subsystem and shows its bidirectional data flow with the MCU.
> - In the state diagram, the **wait/retry loop** reflects the real-world ACK polling required after every EEPROM write (because the I²C bus is held off by the EEPROM during its internal 1–10 ms programming time).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define EEPROM. List any two advantages of EEPROM over EPROM in embedded systems.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** EEPROM (Electrically Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory) is a non-volatile semiconductor memory in which individual bytes can be **electrically erased and reprogrammed** in-circuit, without removal of the chip, by using Fowler–Nordheim tunneling across a thin tunnel oxide in a floating-gate MOSFET cell.

**Advantages over EPROM (2 marks — any two of the following):**
1. **In-circuit reprogrammability** — No need to remove the chip and place it in a UV eraser; saves development and field-update time.
2. **Byte-level erase granularity** — A single byte can be erased without disturbing the rest of the array; EPROM requires full-chip UV erase (20–30 minutes).
3. **Higher endurance** — EEPROM supports ~ 10⁶ write/erase cycles per cell; EPROM supports only ~ 100 cycles.
4. **Lower system cost** — No UV eraser equipment, no quartz window, no ESD-sensitive window handling.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is Fowler–Nordheim tunneling? Why is it important in EEPROM operation?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Fowler–Nordheim (FN) tunneling is a **quantum-mechanical phenomenon** in which electrons traverse a thin insulating barrier (such as SiO₂) under the influence of a high electric field (~ 10 MV/cm), even though their classical energy is below the barrier height. The current density follows:

$$
J_{FN} \;=\; A \cdot E_{ox}^{2} \cdot \exp\!\left(\,-\frac{B}{E_{ox}}\,\right)
$$

**Importance (1 mark):** FN tunneling is the fundamental mechanism that allows **electrical injection and removal of charge** from the floating gate of an EEPROM cell, thereby enabling **byte-level electrical programming and erase** without the use of UV light.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Provision:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, both Question A and Question B are printed in the question paper; the student answers **one**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the **internal structure of a 2T floating-gate EEPROM cell**. Label all layers including the control gate, inter-poly oxide, floating gate, tunnel oxide, and source/drain regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **three operating modes** (Program, Erase, Read) of an EEPROM cell. For each mode, specify the voltages applied and the resulting change in the floating-gate charge and threshold voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The 2T EEPROM cell consists of two MOSFETs fabricated in a P-type silicon substrate:

1. **Storage transistor (M1)** — contains the **floating gate** that stores charge.
2. **Select transistor (M2)** — provides electrical isolation; its gate is the **word line**.

**Layered cross-section (bottom to top):**

1. **P-type silicon substrate** — the body of both transistors.
2. **Source and Drain n+ diffusions** — for both M1 and M2.
3. **Tunnel oxide** — ultra-thin SiO₂ (~ 7–10 nm) between the channel of M1 and the floating gate. This is the **quantum-tunneling barrier**.
4. **Floating gate** — polysilicon, **electrically isolated** on all sides.
5. **Inter-poly oxide (ONO)** — dielectric between floating gate and control gate.
6. **Control gate** — polysilicon, externally accessible terminal (word line driver for the storage gate).
7. **Body contact** — substrate tap for biasing.

**Key design point (1 mark):** The floating gate has **no ohmic contact** to any circuit node. Its only electrical influence is **capacitive coupling** to the control gate, channel, and source/drain, and **charge injection/ejection** via FN tunneling.

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

| Mode | Control Gate | Source/Drain | Substrate | Mechanism | $Q_{fg}$ | $V_{th}$ | Cell State |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Program** | +18 to +21 V | 0 V | 0 V | FN tunneling of electrons from channel to FG | Increases (negative) | Rises (~ +6 V) | Logical `0` |
| **Erase** | 0 V or negative | +18 to +21 V | 0 V | FN tunneling of electrons from FG to source | Decreases | Falls (~ +1 V) | Logical `1` |
| **Read** | +3.3 V (Vcc) | Sense | 0 V | Cell conducts if erased; no current if programmed | Unchanged | Unchanged | `1` or `0` detected |

**Logic per mode (2 marks for explanation):**
- During **Program**, the high positive Vpp on the control gate creates a strong vertical field; electrons tunnel from the inverted channel into the floating gate, raising $V_{th}$.
- During **Erase**, the polarity is reversed; electrons are pulled out of the floating gate, lowering $V_{th}$.
- During **Read**, a moderate Vcc is applied; if the cell's $V_{th}$ is low (erased), it conducts current, sensed as `1`; if high (programmed), no current flows, sensed as `0`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 (Alternative)]

**(a)** Compare **EEPROM, EPROM, and Flash memory** with respect to erase mechanism, write granularity, endurance, and cell area. Present the answer in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Fowler–Nordheim tunneling** with the help of the relevant current-density equation. A 2 fF EEPROM cell is programmed by injecting a charge of $1.6 \times 10^{-15}$ C. Compute the resulting threshold voltage shift. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks (Tabular Comparison)

| Feature | EPROM | EEPROM | Flash |
|---|---|---|---|
| Erase mechanism | UV light (20 min) | Electrical FN tunneling | Electrical FN tunneling |
| Erase granularity | Entire chip | **Byte** | Block / sector (e.g., 4 KB) |
| Write granularity | Byte (program only) | Byte | Page or sector |
| Endurance | ~ 100 cycles | **~ 10⁶ cycles** | 10⁴ – 10⁵ cycles |
| Cell area (relative) | Medium (1T) | Largest (2T) | Smallest (1T) |
| Programming voltage | +12 V | +12 to +21 V | +10 to +12 V |
| Use case | Legacy firmware | **Config / keys / RTC** | Bulk code + data |
| In-system rewrite? | No | **Yes (byte)** | Yes (block-erase first) |

**Why EEPROM over Flash for configuration storage (extra credit, 1 mark):** Because embedded configuration data (calibration constants, device IDs, network credentials) is typically small (a few bytes to a few hundred bytes) and updated **frequently**. Flash's block-erase requirement means changing 16 bytes forces an erase of a 4 KB sector — a slow and wear-intensive operation. EEPROM's byte-level erase makes it the natural choice.

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks (FN Tunneling + Numerical)

**Theory (4 marks):** FN tunneling is a quantum-mechanical band-to-band tunneling of electrons through a thin potential barrier under a high applied field. The current density is:

$$
J_{FN} \;=\; A \cdot E_{ox}^{2} \cdot \exp\!\left(\,-\frac{B}{E_{ox}}\,\right)
$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants dependent on the barrier height and electron effective mass in the oxide. In EEPROM, the oxide is SiO₂ with thickness **~ 7–10 nm**, and the applied field $E_{ox} = V_{pp} / t_{ox}$ reaches **~ 10 MV/cm** to enable usable tunneling currents.

**Numerical (3 marks):**

Given:
- $C_{total} = 2 \text{ fF} = 2 \times 10^{-15}$ F
- $Q_{p} = 1.6 \times 10^{-15}$ C

Using $\Delta V_{th} = Q_{p} / C_{total}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V_{th} \;&=\; \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{C}}{2 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{F}} \\[4pt]
&=\; 0.8\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The threshold voltage shifts by **+0.8 V** after programming. Since a typical read voltage is ~ 3 V, and the programmed $V_{th}$ is ~ 6 V, the cell will not turn on during a read — correctly sensing as logical `0`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing EEPROM with Flash:** Many students incorrectly write that Flash is byte-erasable. The examiner will deduct 2–3 marks if you do not clearly state that **Flash requires block-erase** and that **only EEPROM has byte-level erase**.
> 2. **Skipping the role of the select transistor (M2):** In 2T cells, the select transistor is essential for isolation during programming. Writing only about a "1T floating-gate cell" without mentioning M2 will cost you 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the units in numericals:** Always write $Q_{p} = 1.6 \times 10^{-15}$ **C** (Coulombs) and $C_{total} = 2 \times 10^{-15}$ **F** (Farads). KTU examiners explicitly check units.
> 4. **Not mentioning Fowler–Nordheim by name:** Even if you describe the mechanism correctly, you must write the term "**Fowler–Nordheim tunneling**" at least once in your answer.
> 5. **Not drawing the cross-section:** A textual description alone loses ~ 2 marks on 7-mark questions that require a diagram. Always include a labeled figure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — KTU 2024 Module 2: EEPROM**

- [x] **Full form:** Electrically Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory.
- [x] **Memory type:** Non-volatile; byte-erasable and byte-programmable.
- [x] **Cell type:** 2T floating-gate MOSFET (storage transistor + select transistor).
- [x] **Floating gate:** Polysilicon, electrically isolated, stores charge for decades.
- [x] **Tunnel oxide:** ~ 7–10 nm SiO₂; enables Fowler–Nordheim tunneling.
- [x] **Programming voltage:** +12 V to +21 V (Vpp).
- [x] **Read voltage:** Vcc (1.8 V to 5.5 V).
- [x] **Mechanism:** FN tunneling for program AND erase (electrical, not optical).
- [x] **Key equation:** $J_{FN} = A \cdot E_{ox}^{2} \cdot \exp(-B / E_{ox})$.
- [x] **Threshold shift:** $\Delta V_{th} = Q_{p} / C_{total}$.
- [x] **Endurance:** ~ 10⁶ write/erase cycles per cell.
- [x] **Data retention:** 10–100 years at room temperature.
- [x] **Write time:** 1–10 ms per byte.
- [x] **Read time:** 50–250 ns.
- [x] **Differentiator vs Flash:** Byte-level erase (Flash = block-level erase).
- [x] **Differentiator vs EPROM:** Electrical in-circuit erase (EPROM = UV erase).
- [x] **Common serial interfaces:** I²C (24LCxxx) and SPI (25LCxxx).
- [x] **Embedded applications:** Configuration storage, calibration constants, RTC backup, device serial numbers, security keys, last-known-good state.
- [x] **On-chip example:** ATmega328P (Arduino Uno) has 1 KB internal EEPROM.
- [x] **Modern alternative:** Emulated EEPROM in Flash with wear-leveling firmware (e.g., STM32).
- [x] **For 7-mark answers:** Always include a labeled cross-section + a table of three operating modes.
- [x] **For 14-mark answers:** Add a comparison table (EEPROM vs EPROM vs Flash) and one numerical on $\Delta V_{th}$.

> **End of Notes — EEPROM (Module 2, PBEVT504 — Embedded System Design, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
