<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: RAM - SRAM (Static Random Access Memory)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of RAM

**Random Access Memory (RAM)** is a class of volatile semiconductor memory in which any storage location (memory cell) can be accessed directly in a constant access time, independent of its physical or logical position, by specifying its address on the address bus. RAM is *volatile*, meaning it loses all stored data when supply power $V_{DD}$ is removed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *RAM is a read/write memory in which data can be written to or read from any address in essentially the same amount of time, regardless of the location of the data within the memory array.*

### 1.2 Classification of RAM

```
RAM (Volatile Memory)
├── SRAM (Static RAM)
│   ├── Bistable Latch Based (e.g., 6T, 4T, 8T, 10T cells)
│   └── Used for Cache (L1/L2/L3), Registers, CPU buffers
└── DRAM (Dynamic RAM)
    ├── 1T-1C Capacitor Based
    └── Used for Main Memory (DDR3/DDR4/DDR5)
```

### 1.3 Formal Definition of SRAM

**Static Random Access Memory (SRAM)** is a type of RAM that uses a **bistable flip-flop (latch) made of cross-coupled inverters** to store each bit. As long as power is supplied, the data remains stored **statically** — no periodic refresh is required (unlike DRAM). A standard SRAM cell is built using **6 MOSFETs (6T cell)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Properties of SRAM**
> - **Volatile**: Data lost without $V_{DD}$.
> - **Static**: No refresh cycles needed.
> - **Bistable**: Two stable states represent logic '0' and logic '1'.
> - **Fast access time**: typically **1 ns – 10 ns**.
> - **Low power at standby**: near-zero static current.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Switch Light Ladder"

Imagine a **ladder with two light bulbs** at the top and two rungs at the bottom:
- **Bulb A is ON** ⟺ **Bulb B is OFF** ⟺ *State represents logic '0'*.
- **Bulb A is OFF** ⟺ **Bulb B is ON** ⟺ *State represents logic '1'*.

The two bulbs are *cross-wired* — if A lights, it *physically forces* B off, and vice-versa. This is a **bistable** configuration. The bulbs stay lit as long as electricity is present, and *no one needs to come push a button every few milliseconds* to keep them lit. That is **SRAM**.

In DRAM's analogy, the bulbs would be **flashlights that fade out in 64 ms**, requiring a **janitor (refresh circuit)** to re-shine them constantly.

### 1.5 Key Physical Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlight)

| Metric | SRAM Typical Value | DRAM Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Cell Size | **~120 F²** (6T) | **~6 F²** (1T1C) |
| Access Time | **1 – 10 ns** | **40 – 70 ns** |
| Refresh | **Not required** | Required (~64 ms) |
| Static Power | **Very low** (leakage only) | Moderate (refresh current) |
| Density (per chip) | Low (4 – 8 Mb typical) | High (8 – 16 Gb typical) |
| Cost per bit | High | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **$F$** denotes the **minimum feature size** of the fabrication process (e.g., 7 nm, 14 nm, 28 nm). Area is measured in $F^2$ units.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 6T SRAM Cell — Operational Architecture

A single SRAM bit is stored in a **6-transistor (6T) memory cell**, which is the industry and KTU-syllabus standard.

### 2.1.1 Transistor Roles

| Transistor | Type | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **M1, M2** | NMOS (Pull-down) | Form the lower half of the two cross-coupled inverters |
| **M3, M4** | PMOS (Pull-up) | Form the upper half of the two cross-coupled inverters |
| **M5, M6** | NMOS (Access) | Connect the storage nodes to the bit lines during Read/Write |

### 2.1.2 Cross-Coupled Inverter Pair

The two inverters are connected in a positive-feedback loop:

- **Inverter 1**: M1 (NMOS) + M3 (PMOS) → output is node **Q**.
- **Inverter 2**: M2 (NMOS) + M4 (PMOS) → output is node **$\bar{Q}$**.
- The output of Inverter 1 drives the input of Inverter 2, and vice-versa.

This forms a **bistable latch** with two stable equilibrium points: $(Q=1, \bar{Q}=0)$ and $(Q=0, \bar{Q}=1)$.

### 2.2 SRAM Read Operation — Step-by-Step

1. **Precharge Phase**: Both bit lines **BL** and **$\overline{BL}$** are precharged to $V_{DD}/2$ (or to $V_{DD}$ in some designs).
2. **Word Line Activation**: The Word Line (WL) is driven HIGH, turning ON the access transistors M5 and M6.
3. **Cell Driving**: The stored value on Q (or $\bar{Q}$) pulls one bit line DOWN through its access transistor, while the other bit line stays high.
4. **Sense Amplifier**: A differential sense amplifier detects the small voltage difference $\Delta V$ between BL and $\overline{BL}$ and amplifies it to a full logic level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read Stability Constraint (Pull-Up Ratio)**
> The cell must NOT flip during a read. This requires:
> $$\text{Pull-up Ratio} = \frac{(W/L)_{\text{access}}}{(W/L)_{\text{pull-down}}} < 1$$
> The access transistor must be **weaker** than the pull-down NMOS so that the cell does not accidentally overwrite the stored value during reading.

### 2.3 SRAM Write Operation — Step-by-Step

1. **Bit Line Driving**: BL and $\overline{BL}$ are driven to opposite logic values representing the new data (e.g., BL = 1, $\overline{BL}$ = 0 to write '1').
2. **Word Line Activation**: WL is raised, connecting BL/$\overline{BL}$ to Q/$\bar{Q}$.
3. **Cell Flipping**: The strong bit-line drivers overpower the internal latch, flipping the cross-coupled inverters to the new state.
4. **Completion**: WL is pulled LOW, isolating the cell. The new value is now held statically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Write-Ability Constraint (Pull-Down Ratio)**
> $$\text{Pull-down Ratio} = \frac{(W/L)_{\text{access}}}{(W/L)_{\text{pull-up}}} < 1$$
> The access transistor must be **strong enough relative to the PMOS pull-up** to flip the cell.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Memory Capacity | $C = 2^N \times M$ | bits (N = address lines, M = data width) |
| 2 | Addressable Locations | $L = 2^N$ | words |
| 3 | Cell Area (6T) | $A_{\text{cell}} \approx 120\,F^2$ | $F^2$ |
| 4 | Read Access Time | $t_{\text{access}} = t_{\text{decode}} + t_{\text{wordline}} + t_{\text{bitline}} + t_{\text{sense}}$ | seconds |
| 5 | Static Power | $P_{\text{static}} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{leak}}$ | Watts |
| 6 | Dynamic Power | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | Watts |
| 7 | Total Power | $P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{static}} + P_{\text{dyn}}$ | Watts |
| 8 | Static Noise Margin | $\text{SNM}$ (from butterfly curve) | Volts |
| 9 | Sense Margin | $\Delta V_{\text{BL}} = \frac{C_B \cdot \Delta Q}{C_B + C_Q}$ | Volts |
| 10 | Refresh Power (DRAM ref) | $P_{\text{ref}} = I_{\text{ref}} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Watts (N/A for SRAM) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol Definitions**
> - $\alpha$ — activity factor (0 to 1)
> - $C$ — switched capacitance per write/read
> - $f$ — operating frequency
> - $N$ — number of address lines
> - $M$ — data bus width in bits
> - $F$ — minimum feature size of CMOS process

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **CPU L1/L2/L3 Caches**: All modern multi-core CPUs (Intel, AMD, ARM Cortex-A series) use SRAM for on-chip cache.
- **Registers & Register Files**: Inside the CPU, register files are essentially small, multi-ported SRAM arrays.
- **Networking ASICs / Routers**: Use SRAM for packet buffers (fast lookups).
- **FPGA Configuration Memory**: Some FPGAs embed SRAM to hold LUT (Look-Up Table) configuration.
- **Embedded Microcontrollers**: Devices like STM32 use SRAM for runtime data (e.g., 64 KB – 1 MB SRAM).
- **Battery-Backed IoT Devices**: SRAM retains state with sub-µA standby current.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Memory Capacity from Address Lines

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A 32-bit address bus SRAM chip has a 16-bit data word. Compute the total storage capacity in bits, bytes, kilobytes, and megabytes.

### 3.1.2 Given

- Number of address lines: $N = 32$
- Data width (word size): $M = 16$ bits
- Each addressable location holds $M$ bits

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Compute the number of unique addressable words.

$$L = 2^N = 2^{32}$$

This is because each of the $N$ address lines can be in one of two states (0 or 1), and the total number of unique binary combinations is $2^N$.

**Step 2** — Compute the number of addressable words in decimal.

$$L = 2^{32} = 4\,294\,967\,296 \text{ words}$$

This corresponds to 4G (Giga) unique locations, since $2^{30} = 1\,073\,741\,824 \approx 10^9$.

**Step 3** — Compute total bit capacity.

$$C_{\text{bits}} = L \times M = 2^{32} \times 16$$

$$C_{\text{bits}} = 2^{32} \times 2^4 = 2^{36} \text{ bits}$$

The 16-bit word was rewritten as $2^4$ to combine the exponents.

**Step 4** — Convert to bytes.

$$C_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{C_{\text{bits}}}{8} = \frac{2^{36}}{2^3} = 2^{33} \text{ bytes}$$

$$C_{\text{bytes}} = 8\,589\,934\,592 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 5** — Convert to kilobytes, megabytes, gigabytes.

$$C_{\text{KB}} = \frac{2^{33}}{2^{10}} = 2^{23} = 8\,192 \text{ KB}$$

$$C_{\text{MB}} = \frac{2^{33}}{2^{20}} = 2^{13} = 8\,192 \text{ MB}$$

$$C_{\text{GB}} = \frac{2^{33}}{2^{30}} = 2^{3} = 8 \text{ GB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result**: A 32-bit-addressed, 16-bit-wide SRAM has a capacity of **8 GB (gigabytes)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SRAM Total Power Dissipation

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A 1 MB SRAM chip operates at $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V, clock frequency $f = 200$ MHz, activity factor $\alpha = 0.2$, switched capacitance per cell $C = 50$ fF, and total cells $N_c = 8 \times 10^6$. Leakage current per cell is $I_{\text{leak}} = 5$ nA. Compute the total power.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Compute total switched capacitance.

$$C_{\text{total}} = N_c \cdot C = 8 \times 10^6 \times 50 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$C_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F} = 0.4 \;\mu\text{F}$$

**Step 2** — Compute dynamic (switching) power.

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_{\text{total}} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.2 \times 4 \times 10^{-7} \times (1.2)^2 \times 200 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.2 \times 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.44 \times 2 \times 10^8$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.2 \times 1.152 \times 10^{1}$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.2 \times 11.52$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 2.304 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3** — Compute total leakage current.

$$I_{\text{leak,total}} = N_c \cdot I_{\text{leak}} = 8 \times 10^6 \times 5 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$I_{\text{leak,total}} = 4 \times 10^{-2} \text{ A} = 40 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4** — Compute static (leakage) power.

$$P_{\text{static}} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{leak,total}} = 1.2 \times 0.04$$

$$P_{\text{static}} = 0.048 \text{ W} = 48 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 5** — Compute total power.

$$P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{static}} + P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.048 + 2.304$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 2.352 \text{ W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result**: Total power dissipation ≈ **2.35 W**, dominated by dynamic switching power (98%). This is why low-activity design (clock gating, sleep modes) is critical.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bit-Line Voltage Differential During Read

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

In a 6T SRAM cell, the bit-line capacitance is $C_B = 250$ fF and the storage-node capacitance is $C_Q = 50$ fF. If the cell stores '1' (so Q = $V_{DD}$), find the voltage drop on the bit line $\Delta V_{\text{BL}}$ during a read.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Identify the charge transfer.

When WL turns ON, charge $C_Q \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{DD}/2)$ flows from the storage node to the bit line.

$$\Delta Q = C_Q \cdot \left(V_{DD} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\right) = C_Q \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

**Step 2** — Apply charge sharing between $C_Q$ and $C_B$.

The total capacitance at the bit line node is:

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_B + C_Q$$

The new bit-line voltage after charge sharing:

$$V_{\text{BL,new}} = \frac{C_B \cdot V_{\text{BL,pre}} + C_Q \cdot V_Q}{C_B + C_Q}$$

**Step 3** — Substitute the values (assuming precharge at $V_{DD}/2 = 0.6$ V and $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V).

$$V_{\text{BL,new}} = \frac{250 \times 0.6 + 50 \times 1.2}{250 + 50}$$

$$V_{\text{BL,new}} = \frac{150 + 60}{300} = \frac{210}{300}$$

$$V_{\text{BL,new}} = 0.7 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4** — Compute differential voltage between BL and $\overline{BL}$ (the latter remains at 0.6 V).

$$\Delta V_{\text{BL}} = V_{\text{BL,new}} - V_{\overline{\text{BL}}} = 0.7 - 0.6 = 0.1 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result**: The sense amplifier must detect a **ΔV = 100 mV** differential. This tiny signal is amplified to full logic. The sense amplifier is therefore the **most critical analog block** in any SRAM.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SRAM vs. DRAM

| Parameter | SRAM (6T) | DRAM (1T-1C) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cell Elements | 6 Transistors | 1 Transistor + 1 Capacitor | SRAM has higher area |
| Cell Area | ~120 $F^2$ | ~6 $F^2$ | DRAM is 20× denser |
| Refresh | **Not required** | Required every 64 ms | SRAM is "static" |
| Access Time | 1 – 10 ns | 40 – 70 ns | SRAM is 5–10× faster |
| Power (Active) | Higher dynamic | Higher refresh power | Trade-off dependent |
| Power (Standby) | Low (leakage) | Higher (refresh) | SRAM better for idle |
| Volatile? | Yes | Yes | Both volatile |
| Process Complexity | Standard CMOS | Needs capacitor + CMOS | DRAM is more complex |
| Cost per bit | High | Low | DRAM ≈ 10× cheaper/bit |
| Typical Use | Cache, registers | Main memory | Hierarchical memory |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 6T SRAM Cell — Circuit-Level Schematic (Mermaid Flow)

The following diagram represents the **functional/architectural flow** of a 6T SRAM cell, mapping the connections and signal flow between the transistors, storage nodes, and bit lines.

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDD[VDD Supply Rail]:::supply
    GND[Ground GND]:::supply
    WL[Word Line WL]:::ctrl
    BL[Bit Line BL]:::bl
    BLB[Bit Line BL_bar]:::bl
    nodeQ[Storage Node Q]:::storage
    nodeQB[Storage Node Q_bar]:::storage

    M1[NMOS M1 Pull-Down]:::nmos
    M2[NMOS M2 Pull-Down]:::nmos
    M3[PMOS M3 Pull-Up]:::pmos
    M4[PMOS M4 Pull-Up]:::pmos
    M5[NMOS M5 Access]:::nmos
    M6[NMOS M6 Access]:::nmos

    VDD --- M3
    VDD --- M4
    M3 --- nodeQ
    M4 --- nodeQB
    M1 --- GND
    M2 --- GND
    M1 --- nodeQ
    M2 --- nodeQB

    nodeQ --- M2
    nodeQB --- M1

    WL --- M5
    WL --- M6
    M5 --- nodeQ
    M6 --- nodeQB
    M5 --- BL
    M6 --- BLB

    classDef supply fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bl fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nmos fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pmos fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.1.1 Reading the Diagram

- **VDD/GND** (cyan): Power supply rails.
- **WL** (yellow): Word Line control signal, gates M5 and M6.
- **BL / BL_bar** (pink): Complementary bit lines, connect to column circuitry.
- **Q / Q_bar** (green): Internal storage nodes forming the bistable latch.
- **M1, M2** (blue): NMOS pull-down transistors (drive Q/Q_bar to GND when ON).
- **M3, M4** (orange): PMOS pull-up transistors (drive Q/Q_bar to VDD when ON).
- **M5, M6** (blue): NMOS access transistors (gated by WL).

---

## 4.2 SRAM Memory Array Organization — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU[CPU or Master Device]:::master
    ADDR[Address Bus N bits]:::bus
    DATA[Data Bus M bits]:::bus
    CTRL[Control Signals RD WR CS OE]:::ctrl
    DEC[Address Decoder N to 2N]:::dec
    ROWDEC[Row Decoder]:::dec
    COLDEC[Column Decoder]:::dec
    ARRAY[SRAM Cell Array Mux 2N x M bits]:::array
    SA[Row of Sense Amplifiers]:::sense
    WDRV[Write Drivers]:::write
    MUXIO[Data I/O Mux]:::mux

    CPU --> ADDR
    CPU --> DATA
    CPU --> CTRL

    ADDR --> DEC
    DEC --> ROWDEC
    DEC --> COLDEC

    ROWDEC --> ARRAY
    COLDEC --> ARRAY

    ARRAY --> SA
    SA --> MUXIO
    MUXIO --> DATA

    DATA --> WDRV
    WDRV --> ARRAY

    classDef master fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef array fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sense fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef write fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mux fill:#d7ccc8,stroke:#3e2723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2.1 Read Operation Flow

```
CPU places address on bus -> Address decoder activates one row (WL = 1)
    -> Selected row cells drive bit lines -> Sense amplifier detects ΔV
    -> Data driven onto output bus -> CPU latches the result.
```

### 4.2.2 Write Operation Flow

```
CPU places address + data -> Decoder selects row -> Write drivers force BL/BL_bar
    -> WL activated -> Cell flips to new state -> WL de-asserted.
```

---

## 4.3 SRAM Read Stability — Butterfly Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Q voltage rising]:::rise
    B[VTC of Inverter 1 In1 Output vs Q]:::curve
    C[Intersection points]:::point
    D[Q_bar voltage falling]:::fall
    E[Static Noise Margin SNM Largest inscribed square]:::snr

    A --> B
    D --> B
    B --> C
    C --> E

    classDef rise fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef curve fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef point fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fall fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef snr fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Butterfly Curve** plots the VTC (Voltage Transfer Characteristic) of one inverter against the other. The two intersections represent the stable states. The **diagonal of the largest square that fits inside the two lobes** is the **Static Noise Margin (SNM)** — a key design metric for read stability.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM. List two applications of SRAM.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | SRAM | DRAM |
|---|---|---|
| Storage Element | 6T bistable latch | 1T + 1 capacitor |
| Refresh | Not required | Required (every ~64 ms) |
| Density | Low (~120 $F^2$/cell) | High (~6 $F^2$/cell) |
| Speed | Fast (1–10 ns) | Slower (40–70 ns) |
| Cost per bit | High | Low |
| Power (Standby) | Low leakage | High (refresh current) |

**Two applications of SRAM:**
1. **CPU Cache Memory** (L1/L2/L3) — fast, low-latency.
2. **Register Files** inside the processor — multi-ported, single-cycle access.

*(Other valid applications: networking buffers, FPGA LUTs, IoT low-power buffers.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]

**Q: Explain the role of the sense amplifier in an SRAM read operation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **sense amplifier** is an analog differential amplifier connected to each column's bit lines (BL and $\overline{BL}$). Its roles are:

1. **Detection of small differential**: After the word line is activated, the cell causes only a tiny voltage difference (ΔV ≈ 50–200 mV) on the bit lines. The sense amplifier detects this.
2. **Amplification**: It amplifies the small ΔV to a full-rail logic level (0 or $V_{DD}$), producing a valid digital output.
3. **Speed enhancement**: It dramatically reduces read access time, since the cell itself is too weak to drive a full bit-line swing quickly.
4. **Power reduction**: Bit lines are only partially discharged (ΔV) rather than fully switched, saving dynamic power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Tip**: A common mistake is to confuse "sense amplifier" with "sense margin". The amplifier is a circuit; the margin is the input voltage difference it can detect.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **structure and operation of a 6T SRAM cell**. Discuss the **read and write operations** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Structure:**
A 6T SRAM cell consists of:
- Two **cross-coupled CMOS inverters** (M1–M2: NMOS pull-down; M3–M4: PMOS pull-up), forming a bistable latch with two stable storage nodes Q and $\bar{Q}$.
- Two **NMOS access transistors** (M5, M6) controlled by the **Word Line (WL)** that connect Q and $\bar{Q}$ to the **bit lines BL and $\overline{BL}$**.

**Read Operation:**
1. BL and $\overline{BL}$ are precharged to $V_{DD}/2$.
2. WL is raised, turning ON M5 and M6.
3. The cell pulls ONE bit line down (e.g., if Q=1, then $\bar{Q}=0$, so $\overline{BL}$ stays at $V_{DD}/2$ while BL is pulled down through M5–M1 path).
4. Sense amplifier detects the small ΔV and amplifies it to full logic.

**Write Operation:**
1. BL and $\overline{BL}$ are driven to the desired complementary values.
2. WL is raised, M5 and M6 connect BL/$\overline{BL}$ to Q/$\bar{Q}$.
3. The strong write drivers overpower the cross-coupled latch, flipping it to the new state.
4. WL is lowered, isolating the cell. Data is held statically.

**Conditions for stability:**
- Read stability: pull-up ratio < 1 (access weaker than pull-down).
- Writability: pull-down ratio < 1 (access stronger than pull-up).

**(b)** A **1 MB SRAM** has an **8-bit data bus**. Calculate the number of address lines, the total number of memory cells, and the approximate die area if the process feature size is **F = 90 nm**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Capacity = 1 MB = $2^{20}$ bytes, M = 8 bits/word.

**Step 1** — Number of words:
$$L = \frac{1 \text{ MB}}{1 \text{ B/word}} = 2^{20} \text{ words}$$

**Step 2** — Number of address lines:
$$L = 2^N \Rightarrow 2^{20} = 2^N \Rightarrow N = 20 \text{ address lines}$$

**Step 3** — Total number of cells:
$$\text{Cells} = 1 \text{ MB} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} = 8 \times 10^6 = 8\,388\,608 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 4** — Approximate die area:
$$A_{\text{cell}} = 120\,F^2 = 120 \times (90 \times 10^{-9})^2$$

$$A_{\text{cell}} = 120 \times 8100 \times 10^{-18} = 9.72 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{\text{total}} = 8\,388\,608 \times 9.72 \times 10^{-13} \approx 8.15 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A_{\text{total}} \approx 8.15 \text{ mm}^2}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]

**(a)** Compare the **architecture and performance** of **SRAM and DRAM**. Why is SRAM preferred for cache memory? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | SRAM | DRAM |
|---|---|---|
| Cell | 6T (latch) | 1T + 1C (capacitor) |
| Refresh | No | Yes (every ~64 ms) |
| Access Time | 1 – 10 ns | 40 – 70 ns |
| Density | ~120 $F^2$ | ~6 $F^2$ |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Power (standby) | Low (leakage) | High (refresh) |
| Volatility | Yes | Yes |

**Why SRAM is preferred for cache:**
1. **Speed**: Cache operates at CPU clock speed (GHz); only SRAM's < 10 ns access time can keep up.
2. **No refresh overhead**: A cache miss already costs tens of cycles; DRAM's refresh cycles would further degrade performance.
3. **Predictable latency**: SRAM provides constant access time, critical for pipelined CPUs.
4. **Low standby power**: Caches are large but mostly idle; SRAM's near-zero static current is ideal.

**(b)** Compute the **total power dissipation** of a **4 MB SRAM** with parameters: $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $f = 250$ MHz, $\alpha = 0.15$, $C = 40$ fF/cell, $I_{\text{leak}} = 8$ nA/cell. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1** — Number of cells:
$$N_c = 4 \text{ MB} \times 8 = 32 \times 10^6 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 2** — Total capacitance:
$$C_{\text{total}} = N_c \cdot C = 32 \times 10^6 \times 40 \times 10^{-15} = 1.28 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$$

**Step 3** — Dynamic power:
$$P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_{\text{total}} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.15 \times 1.28 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.0 \times 250 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.15 \times 1.28 \times 250 = 48 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 4** — Total leakage current:
$$I_{\text{leak,total}} = 32 \times 10^6 \times 8 \times 10^{-9} = 0.256 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5** — Static power:
$$P_{\text{static}} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{leak,total}} = 1.0 \times 0.256 = 256 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 6** — Total power:
$$P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{static}} + P_{\text{dyn}} = 256 + 48 = 304 \text{ mW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Pitfall 1 (–2 marks)**: Confusing **MB (megabyte)** with **Mb (megabit)**. Always convert: 1 MB = 8 Mb.
> - **Pitfall 2 (–2 marks)**: Forgetting to include **static + dynamic** power; many students compute only $P_{\text{dyn}}$.
> - **Pitfall 3 (–2 marks)**: Confusing $C$ (per cell) with $C_{\text{total}}$ — always multiply by the number of cells.
> - **Pitfall 4 (–1 mark)**: Using $2^{20}$ for MB in one step and $10^6$ in another — be consistent: **1 MB = 2²⁰ bytes**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — RAM : SRAM**

- **RAM** = Random Access Memory; **volatile**; constant access time regardless of address.
- **SRAM** = Static RAM; uses **bistable latch** (cross-coupled inverters); **no refresh** needed.
- **6T Cell** = 2 PMOS pull-up + 2 NMOS pull-down + 2 NMOS access = 6 transistors per bit.
- **Storage nodes** = Q and $\bar{Q}$ (complementary).
- **Bit lines** = BL and $\overline{BL}$ (complementary).
- **Word Line (WL)** = control signal gating M5, M6.
- **Read**: precharge BL/$\overline{BL}$ to $V_{DD}/2$, raise WL, sense ΔV.
- **Write**: drive BL/$\overline{BL}$, raise WL, flip latch.
- **Pull-up ratio < 1** ensures **read stability** (cell does not flip during read).
- **Pull-down ratio < 1** ensures **writability** (cell can be flipped during write).
- **Sense amplifier** detects small ΔV (~100 mV) and amplifies to full logic.
- **Butterfly curve** → measures **Static Noise Margin (SNM)**.
- **Capacity formula**: $C = 2^N \times M$ bits.
- **Cell area**: ~120 $F^2$ for 6T SRAM; ~6 $F^2$ for 1T-1C DRAM.
- **Power formula**: $P = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f + V_{DD} I_{\text{leak}}$.
- **SRAM is faster, denser in speed; DRAM is denser in area and cheaper.**
- **Use cases**: SRAM → cache, registers, buffers. DRAM → main memory.
- **Modern trend**: eDRAM (embedded DRAM) and MRAM/RRAM in advanced nodes.
- **Word size matters**: 16-bit, 32-bit, 64-bit bus widths are common in microcontrollers.
- **Volatile** — both SRAM and DRAM lose data on power-off; use NVRAM/Flash for persistence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DRAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DRAM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM)** is a volatile semiconductor memory technology that stores each bit of data in a **single memory cell consisting of one access transistor and one storage capacitor (1T1C structure)**. Because the charge stored on the capacitor leaks away through sub-threshold leakage currents of the access transistor and parasitic paths, the data must be **periodically refreshed** (typically every **64 ms**) to retain its logical state. The word *Dynamic* thus refers to the time-varying nature of the stored charge, distinguishing it from Static RAM (SRAM), which uses a bistable latch (6T cell) to retain data indefinitely as long as power is supplied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** DRAM is examined under Module 2 — *Memory Architectures and Interfacing* — and is fundamental to understanding main memory design, memory-mapped I/O, and external memory interfacing in embedded processors (e.g., ARM, PIC, 8051 extensions).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **small bucket of water** sitting on a wobbly table.

- The **bucket** is the **storage capacitor** in the DRAM cell.
- The **water inside** represents the **electrical charge** (a logic '1' if full, a logic '0' if empty).
- The **small hole at the bottom of the bucket** represents the **leakage current** through the access transistor — water keeps dripping out.
- The **person walking past every few seconds with a measuring cup** is the **refresh controller** — they check the water level and top it up if needed.
- The **faucet on top of the bucket** is the **bit-line access transistor** — it can be opened to either fill (write) or measure (read) the water level.

If the person (refresh logic) forgets to refill the bucket, the water eventually drains completely, and the data is **lost forever** — even though power is still applied. This is why DRAM is called *dynamic*.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Standard DRAM parameters that must be memorized for KTU examinations:
>
> - **Cell density:** $1$ transistor $+ 1$ capacitor ($\approx 6F^2$ cell area where $F$ is the minimum feature size)
> - **Refresh interval:** typically **64 ms** (JEDEC standard) for the entire array
> - **Access time ($t_{AA}$):** $\mathbf{40\text{–}100\ ns}$ for asynchronous DRAM, lower for synchronous variants
> - **Capacitance ($C_S$):** on the order of $\mathbf{20\text{–}30\ fF}$ per cell
> - **Supply voltage ($V_{DD}$):** historically **5 V → 3.3 V → 1.8 V → 1.2 V → 1.1 V → 0.6 V** as technology scaled
> - **Standby power:** dominated by refresh current $I_{ref}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 1T1C DRAM Cell — Step-by-Step Operation

Each DRAM cell consists of three fundamental structures:

1. **Storage Capacitor ($C_S$):** A deep-trench or stacked capacitor holding charge $Q$.
2. **Access Transistor (nMOS):** Acts as a switch between $C_S$ and the bit-line.
3. **Bit-Line Capacitance ($C_{BL}$):** The parasitic capacitance of the shared bit-line, much larger than $C_S$.

**Write Operation:**
- Word-line (WL) is raised $\rightarrow$ access transistor turns ON.
- Bit-line (BL) is driven to $V_{DD}$ (logic '1') or **0 V** (logic '0').
- Charge flows into or out of $C_S$ until $V_{CS} = V_{BL}$.

**Read Operation (Destructive Read):**
- BL is precharged to $V_{BL} = V_{REF} = \tfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$.
- WL is raised $\rightarrow$ charge sharing between $C_S$ and $C_{BL}$.
- The resulting small voltage differential $\Delta V$ is amplified by the **sense amplifier**.

### 2.2 Charge Sharing Equation — The Heart of DRAM Read

When the word-line opens, the charge stored on $C_S$ redistributes across the parallel combination of $C_S$ and $C_{BL}$:

$$
Q_{\text{initial}} = C_S \cdot V_{CS}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{final}} = (C_S + C_{BL}) \cdot V_{BL,\text{final}}
$$

By conservation of charge:

$$
C_S \cdot V_{CS} = (C_S + C_{BL}) \cdot V_{BL,\text{final}}
$$

Solving for the final bit-line voltage:

$$
V_{BL,\text{final}} = \frac{C_S}{C_S + C_{BL}} \cdot V_{CS}
$$

The differential developed on the bit-line is therefore:

$$
\Delta V_{BL} = V_{BL,\text{final}} - V_{REF} = \frac{C_S}{C_S + C_{BL}} \cdot V_{CS} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Because $C_{BL} \gg C_S$ (typically a ratio of **10:1**), only a few hundred millivolts of signal swing is produced. This is why a **sense amplifier** is mandatory.

### 2.3 Refresh Mechanism

DRAM requires periodic refresh because the storage capacitor discharges through:

$$
i_{leak} = I_0 \, e^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{\eta V_T}} \left(1 - e^{\frac{-V_{DS}}{V_T}}\right)
$$

The voltage on the capacitor decays exponentially:

$$
V_{CS}(t) = V_{CS}(0) \cdot e^{-t / \tau_{leak}}
$$

where $\tau_{leak} = C_S / I_{leak}$.

For data retention across the full 64 ms interval, the leakage-induced voltage droop must remain above the sense amplifier's minimum detectable differential:

$$
V_{CS}(64\,\text{ms}) \geq V_{\min,\text{read}}
$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---------|------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Cell structure | 1T1C | — |
| 2 | Cell area | $6F^2$ | $F$ = feature size |
| 3 | Storage charge | $Q = C_S \cdot V_{CS}$ | Coulombs |
| 4 | Charge sharing output | $V_{BL} = \frac{C_S}{C_S + C_{BL}} \cdot V_{CS}$ | Volts |
| 5 | Bit-line voltage swing | $\Delta V_{BL} \approx \pm 200\,\text{mV}$ | mV |
| 6 | Refresh interval | $t_{REF}$ | **64 ms** (JEDEC) |
| 7 | Capacitor decay | $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ | Exponential |
| 8 | Leakage time constant | $\tau = C_S / I_{leak}$ | seconds |
| 9 | Access time | $t_{AA}$ | **40 – 100 ns** |
| 10 | Cycle time | $t_{RC}$ | **130 – 200 ns** |
| 11 | Page mode access | $t_{PAC}$ | **20 – 40 ns** |
| 12 | Bandwidth (DDR4-3200) | $BW = 2 \cdot f \cdot W$ | **25.6 GB/s** |
| 13 | Refresh commands | $8192$ rows / 64 ms | — |
| 14 | Self-refresh current | $I_{SREF}$ | **\textless 1 mA** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** DRAM forms the **main memory backbone** of virtually all embedded systems — from smartphones using **LPDDR5** to industrial controllers using **DDR3L**. Understanding refresh timing, page-mode hits/misses, and burst transfers is essential when configuring an **External Memory Controller (EMC)** in an ARM Cortex-M or Cortex-A SoC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bit-Line Voltage After Charge Sharing

**Step 1 — Initial Condition Setup.**

Before the read operation, the bit-line is precharged to exactly half the supply voltage:

$$
V_{BL,\text{pre}} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2} = V_{REF}
$$

The storage capacitor holds either $V_{CS} = V_{DD}$ (logic '1') or $V_{CS} = 0$ (logic '0').

**Step 2 — Charge Stored Initially.**

The total charge in the system is contributed by both the bit-line capacitance and the storage capacitor:

$$
Q_{\text{initial}} = C_{BL} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} + C_S \cdot V_{CS}
$$

**Step 3 — Word-Line Asserted, Transistor ON.**

When the access transistor turns ON, the bit-line and the storage capacitor become electrically parallel. The charge redistributes until a single common voltage $V_{BL,\text{final}}$ is reached.

**Step 4 — Conservation of Charge.**

Total charge is conserved (no path to ground):

$$
Q_{\text{initial}} = Q_{\text{final}}
$$

$$
C_{BL} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} + C_S \cdot V_{CS} = (C_{BL} + C_S) \cdot V_{BL,\text{final}}
$$

**Step 5 — Solving for the Final Bit-Line Voltage.**

$$
V_{BL,\text{final}} = \frac{C_{BL} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} + C_S \cdot V_{CS}}{C_{BL} + C_S}
$$

**Step 6 — Expressing the Differential Seen by the Sense Amplifier.**

Subtracting the precharge reference:

$$
\Delta V_{BL} = V_{BL,\text{final}} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}
$$

$$
\Delta V_{BL} = \frac{C_{BL} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} + C_S \cdot V_{CS} - (C_{BL} + C_S) \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}}{C_{BL} + C_S}
$$

**Step 7 — Simplification of the Numerator.**

The first and last terms in the numerator cancel exactly, leaving:

$$
\Delta V_{BL} = \frac{C_S \cdot \left(V_{CS} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\right)}{C_{BL} + C_S}
$$

**Step 8 — Final Closed-Form Result.**

$$
\Delta V_{BL} = \frac{C_S}{C_S + C_{BL}} \cdot \left(V_{CS} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\right)
$$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Given:**
- $C_S = 25\,\text{fF}$
- $C_{BL} = 250\,\text{fF}$
- $V_{DD} = 1.8\,\text{V}$
- Cell stores a logic '1' so $V_{CS} = 1.8\,\text{V}$

**Compute the final bit-line voltage:**

$$
V_{BL,\text{final}} = \frac{25 \cdot 1.8}{25 + 250} = \frac{45}{275} = 0.1636\,\text{V}
$$

**Compute the differential:**

$$
\Delta V_{BL} = 0.1636 - 0.9 = -0.7364\,\text{V} \quad \text{(negative polarity, sense amp flips it)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sense amplifier will rapidly pull the bit-line full-rail to either 0 V or $V_{DD}$ based on this differential. The magnitude of $\Delta V_{BL}$ directly determines **read access time** — a larger capacitance ratio $C_S / C_{BL}$ yields a faster, more reliable read.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DRAM vs. SRAM Comparison

| Parameter | DRAM | SRAM |
|-----------|------|------|
| Cell structure | **1T + 1C** | **6T** latch |
| Cell area | $6F^2$ | $\approx 120 F^2$ |
| Density | **High** | Low |
| Refresh needed | **Yes (every 64 ms)** | No |
| Read | **Destructive** | Non-destructive |
| Speed | Slower | **Faster** |
| Power (active) | Higher | Lower |
| Power (standby) | Refresh power | **Near zero** |
| Cost per bit | **Low** | High |
| Typical use | Main memory | Cache, registers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DRAM Memory Array Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CPU / Embedded Master] --> B[Memory Controller / EMC]
    B --> C1[Row Decoder]
    B --> C2[Column Decoder]
    B --> D[Sense Amplifiers per Bit-line]
    B --> E[Refresh Logic Controller]
    B --> F[Address Multiplexer]
    F --> C1
    F --> C2
    C1 --> G1[Word-lines WL0..WLn]
    C2 --> H1[Column MUX and Y-Select]
    G1 --> I1[Memory Array 1T1C Cells]
    H1 --> I1
    I1 --> D
    D --> J1[Read Latch / Write Driver]
    J1 --> B
    E --> K1[Refresh Counter and Row Sequencer]
    K1 --> C1
    E --> I1
```

### 4.2 Single 1T1C DRAM Cell Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BL[Bit-line BL] --- T1[Access nMOS Transistor]
    T1 --- C1[Storage Capacitor CS]
    C1 --- GND1[Ground VSS]
    T1 --- WL[Word-line WL as Gate]
    WL --> T1
```

**Node descriptions for the cell:**
- `BL` carries data to/from the sense amplifier.
- `WL` is driven by the row decoder to enable read/write.
- `C1` stores the binary value as charge.
- `GND1` provides the reference return path.

### 4.3 DRAM Read Cycle Timing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[State 1: BL Precharge to VDD/2] --> S2[State 2: Row Address Valid and RAS asserted]
    S2 --> S3[State 3: Word-line Rises and Cell shares charge with BL]
    S3 --> S4[State 4: Sense Amplifier Enabled and Detects Delta V]
    S4 --> S5{Logic 1 or Logic 0}
    S5 --> S6a[BL driven to VDD - Restore to cell]
    S5 --> S6b[BL driven to 0V - Restore to cell]
    S6a --> S7[State 5: Column Address Strobe CAS and Data Output]
    S6b --> S7
    S7 --> S8[State 6: Precharge for Next Cycle]
```

### 4.4 Synchronous DRAM (SDRAM) Command State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Active: ACT command with row address
    Active --> Read: READ command with column address
    Active --> Write: WRITE command with column address
    Read --> Precharge: PRE command ends burst
    Write --> Precharge: PRE command ends burst
    Read --> BurstTerm: Auto precharge after burst
    Write --> BurstTerm: Auto precharge after burst
    Precharge --> Idle
    BurstTerm --> Idle
    Idle --> SelfRefresh: SREF command
    SelfRefresh --> Idle: SREF exit
    Idle --> PowerDown: CKE low
    PowerDown --> Idle: CKE high
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is meant by a "destructive read" in DRAM? Why is it called destructive, and what is done to overcome this limitation?*

**Model Answer:**
A *destructive read* means that during a read operation, the charge stored in the cell capacitor $C_S$ is shared with the bit-line capacitance $C_{BL}$. By the principle of conservation of charge, the final voltage $V_{BL,\text{final}}$ settles to a value determined by the capacitance ratio, and the original charge distribution is permanently disturbed. The cell no longer holds its original logic level after the access transistor is opened.

It is called *destructive* because the data in the cell is corrupted by the act of reading it. To overcome this, every DRAM read is immediately followed by a **sense-amplifier-driven restore** operation. The sense amplifier detects the small differential $\Delta V_{BL}$ and drives the bit-line full-rail back to $V_{DD}$ or 0 V, simultaneously re-writing the original value into $C_S$. Thus, the read operation is automatically paired with a rewrite.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why does DRAM require periodic refresh, and what is the standard JEDEC refresh interval?*

**Model Answer:**
DRAM stores data as charge on a tiny capacitor $C_S$. Because the access transistor has non-zero sub-threshold leakage and there are parasitic leakage paths in the silicon, the stored charge gradually bleeds away. The voltage on the capacitor decays as $V_{CS}(t) = V_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$. Once $V_{CS}$ falls below the minimum detectable differential of the sense amplifier, the data cannot be reliably read.

Periodic refresh **rewrites** each row's contents, restoring the capacitor voltage back to full-rail. The **JEDEC standard refresh interval is 64 ms** for the entire array, which means all rows must be refreshed at least once within this window. For a memory with 8192 rows, this corresponds to one refresh every **7.8 µs**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**
*(a)* With a neat diagram, explain the construction and operation of a single **1T1C DRAM cell**. Derive the expression for the final bit-line voltage during a read operation. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A DRAM cell has $C_S = 30\,\text{fF}$ and $C_{BL} = 300\,\text{fF}$ with $V_{DD} = 1.5\,\text{V}$. The cell initially stores a logic '1'. Calculate (i) the final bit-line voltage, (ii) the differential voltage developed, and (iii) the time required to discharge the cell to 50% of $V_{DD}$ if the leakage current is $I_{leak} = 1\,\text{pA}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The 1T1C DRAM cell comprises a single nMOS access transistor and a storage capacitor. The bit-line BL connects to the drain of the transistor, the word-line WL connects to its gate, and the source connects to the top plate of the capacitor $C_S$. The bottom plate is grounded.

**Write '1':** WL is raised, BL is driven to $V_{DD}$ — charge flows into $C_S$ until $V_{CS} = V_{DD}$.

**Write '0':** WL is raised, BL is driven to 0 V — charge drains from $C_S$ until $V_{CS} = 0$.

**Read:** BL is first precharged to $V_{DD}/2$. The word-line is then asserted, and the access transistor turns ON. Charge is conserved across $C_S$ and $C_{BL}$ in parallel, yielding the final bit-line voltage:

$$
V_{BL,\text{final}} = \frac{C_{BL} \cdot \tfrac{V_{DD}}{2} + C_S \cdot V_{CS}}{C_S + C_{BL}}
$$

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

Given: $C_S = 30\,\text{fF}$, $C_{BL} = 300\,\text{fF}$, $V_{DD} = 1.5\,\text{V}$, $V_{CS} = 1.5\,\text{V}$ (logic '1').

**(i) Final bit-line voltage:**

$$
V_{BL,\text{final}} = \frac{300 \cdot 0.75 + 30 \cdot 1.5}{330} = \frac{225 + 45}{330} = \frac{270}{330} = 0.818\,\text{V}
$$

**(ii) Differential voltage developed:**

$$
\Delta V_{BL} = 0.818 - 0.75 = +0.068\,\text{V} = 68\,\text{mV}
$$

**(iii) Time to discharge to 50%:**

The discharge relation is $V(t) = V_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$ with $\tau = C_S / I_{leak}$:

$$
\tau = \frac{30 \times 10^{-15}}{1 \times 10^{-12}} = 30 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 30\,\text{ms}
$$

Setting $V(t) = 0.5 V_0$:

$$
0.5 = e^{-t/\tau} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \tau \ln 2 = 30\,\text{ms} \cdot 0.693 = 20.8\,\text{ms}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to use **half of $V_{DD}$** as the precharge reference and instead assume the BL starts at 0 V. This leads to incorrect answers in the differential calculation. Always write the precharge step explicitly.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
*(a)* Explain the **internal architecture of a DRAM chip**, including row decoder, column decoder, sense amplifiers, and refresh logic. Describe the role of $\overline{\text{RAS}}$ and $\overline{\text{CAS}}$ in asynchronous DRAM. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Compare and contrast **DRAM with SRAM** in terms of cell structure, density, speed, power, refresh requirement, and typical applications in an embedded system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

A DRAM chip is organized as a 2D matrix of 1T1C cells, divided into banks, rows, and columns.

- **Row Decoder:** Receives the row address bits and activates exactly one of $2^N$ word-lines.
- **Column Decoder:** Selects one of the bit-lines within the activated row to be connected to the I/O data path.
- **Sense Amplifiers:** Placed at the end of each bit-line pair; they detect the small charge-sharing differential (typically $\pm 100$ mV) and amplify it to full-rail CMOS logic levels.
- **Refresh Logic:** Contains a counter that cycles through all row addresses and triggers a refresh cycle, ensuring every row is rewritten within 64 ms.

In **asynchronous DRAM**, the row address is latched by the falling edge of $\overline{\text{RAS}}$ (Row Address Strobe) and the column address is latched by the falling edge of $\overline{\text{CAS}}$ (Column Address Strobe). This two-step multiplexing allows a smaller address bus to address a much larger memory array.

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

| Feature | DRAM | SRAM |
|---------|------|------|
| Cell structure | 1T + 1C | 6T latch |
| Density | **Higher** | Lower |
| Speed | Slower access | **Faster access** |
| Refresh | **Required** | Not required |
| Power (active) | Higher | Lower |
| Power (idle) | Refresh current | **Negligible** |
| Cost/bit | **Low** | High |
| Application | Main memory, frame buffer | Cache, registers, TCAM |

In a typical embedded SoC, **SRAM is used for on-chip L1/L2 caches and CPU registers**, while **DRAM (or LPDDR) is used for main system memory** where density and cost dominate over absolute speed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A very common mistake is stating that "DRAM is faster than SRAM." Always remember: **SRAM is faster**, but DRAM is denser and cheaper. Also, do not confuse **DRAM refresh** with **SRAM read** — SRAM reads are **non-destructive**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DRAM = 1T1C** cell storing charge on a capacitor; data is **dynamic** because the charge leaks.
- **Standard refresh interval is 64 ms** (JEDEC); a row must be refreshed before its capacitor droops below the sense-amp threshold.
- **Read is destructive** — the sense amplifier simultaneously **reads and restores** the cell.
- The **bit-line voltage after charge sharing** is:
$$
V_{BL,\text{final}} = \frac{C_{BL} \cdot \tfrac{V_{DD}}{2} + C_S \cdot V_{CS}}{C_S + C_{BL}}
$$
- **DRAM is dense and cheap**, **SRAM is fast and stable** — choose DRAM for main memory, SRAM for cache.
- In **asynchronous DRAM**, $\overline{\text{RAS}}$ latches the row and $\overline{\text{CAS}}$ latches the column — this is the basis of **address multiplexing**.
- **SDRAM** adds a clock; **DDR** transfers on both clock edges; **LPDDR** reduces power for mobile/embedded.
- **Refresh power** is a non-trivial fraction of total DRAM power; designers minimize it using **self-refresh** and **temperature-compensated refresh**.
- For KTU, always show the **charge-sharing derivation** and the **discharge exponential** explicitly — these are the two most frequently tested equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cache Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cache Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cache Memory** is a small, high-speed, volatile, Static Random Access Memory (SRAM) storage unit placed logically and physically between the Central Processing Unit (CPU) and the main memory (DRAM). It serves as a transparent staging area that exploits the **principle of locality** (temporal and spatial locality) to dramatically reduce the *effective memory access time* perceived by the processor.

In the KTU 2024 Embedded System Design framework, cache memory is treated as a critical architectural element that bridges the ever-widening **processor-memory performance gap** (the "Memory Wall"). Embedded processors like ARM Cortex-M/A series rely heavily on tightly coupled multi-level caches to meet real-time deterministic deadlines.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Librarian's Desk**
> Imagine a library (main memory) with **10 million books** stored deep in massive underground archives. A student (the CPU) needs to read pages from these books to write a thesis. The librarian (cache controller) observes the student and starts keeping the most recently referenced books and related reference books on a small desk right next to the student. This desk holds only a few books (cache, **typically 16 KB to 1 MB**). When the student wants a book, the librarian checks the desk first:
> - **Desk has the book** → Instant handover → **Cache Hit**
> - **Desk doesn't have it** → Long walk to the archive → **Cache Miss**

This dramatically cuts the average time to get a book, because the student usually re-reads the same chapters (temporal locality) and the surrounding chapters (spatial locality).

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Standard Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Hit** | — | Requested data is found in the cache. |
| **Miss** | — | Requested data is NOT found in the cache. |
| **Hit Rate** | $h$ | Fraction of accesses that result in a **hit**. |
| **Miss Rate** | $m$ | Fraction of accesses that result in a **miss**. $m = 1 - h$ |
| **Hit Time** | $t_{hit}$ | Time to access the cache on a hit (includes tag compare). |
| **Miss Penalty** | $t_{miss}$ | Extra time to fetch the block from main memory on a miss. |
| **Line / Block** | — | The fundamental transfer unit between cache and main memory (**typically 32 or 64 bytes**). |
| **Tag** | — | Portion of the address used to identify which block currently resides in a cache line. |
| **AMAT** | — | **Average Memory Access Time**, the canonical performance metric. |
| **Write Policy** | — | Strategy used to propagate writes (Write-Through vs Write-Back). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Sizes to Memorise (Bolded):**
> - **Typical Cache Block Size:** 4 to 128 bytes (most common: **64 bytes**)
> - **Typical L1 Cache Size:** **16 KB to 64 KB**
> - **Typical L2 Cache Size:** **256 KB to 1 MB**
> - **Typical L3 Cache Size:** **2 MB to 32 MB**
> - **Access Time of Cache:** ~**1–10 ns**
> - **Access Time of Main Memory (DRAM):** ~**50–100 ns**

## 1.4 Why Cache is Indispensable in Embedded Systems

> [!NOTE]
> Embedded SoCs (System-on-Chips) such as the NXP i.MX RT1060 or TI Sitara series integrate L1 and L2 caches directly on-die. The cache's role is to:
> 1. **Decouple CPU frequency from memory frequency** (CPUs run at GHz; DRAM runs at hundreds of MHz).
> 2. **Reduce power consumption** (SRAM access burns less energy than activating DRAM rows).
> 3. **Provide predictable acceleration** for control loops (cache miss → predictably slow).
> 4. **Free the memory bus** for DMA, peripherals, and other masters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Principle: Locality of Reference

Cache performance rests on two empirical behavioural patterns of programs:

- **Temporal Locality** — If a memory location is referenced *now*, it is highly likely to be referenced *again in the near future* (e.g., loop counters, stack variables, instruction PCs).
- **Spatial Locality** — If a memory location is referenced *now*, neighbouring memory locations are likely to be referenced *soon* (e.g., sequential instruction fetch, array traversal in order).

By transferring an entire **block (line)** of data on a miss, the cache inherently captures both forms of locality in hardware.

## 2.2 Architectural Anatomy of a Cache

A cache is structurally an **M-way associative array of sets**, where each set contains $M$ lines, and each line consists of:
1. **Valid Bit (V)** — Marks whether the line contains valid data (1 bit).
2. **Dirty Bit (D)** — Used only in write-back policy (1 bit).
3. **Tag** — Upper-order bits of the address used for matching.
4. **Data Block** — The actual payload (32, 64, or 128 bytes).

An incoming physical address is partitioned into three fields:

$$\text{Address} = \underbrace{\text{Tag}}_{\text{Highest bits}} \;\Big|\; \underbrace{\text{Index}}_{\text{Selects the Set}} \;\Big|\; \underbrace{\text{Block Offset}}_{\text{Selects byte in block}}$$

The number of **index bits** is determined by the number of sets:
$$\text{Index bits} = \log_2(\text{Number of Sets})$$

The number of **offset bits** is determined by the block size:
$$\text{Offset bits} = \log_2(\text{Block Size in bytes})$$

## 2.3 Three Fundamental Mapping Techniques

### 2.3.1 Direct-Mapped Cache
- Each main memory block maps to **exactly one** cache line, given by:
$$\text{Cache Line} = (\text{Memory Block Number}) \bmod (\text{Total Cache Lines})$$
- **Pros:** Simple, fast hardware, single tag comparator. **Cons:** High conflict miss rate (thrashing on alternating accesses).

### 2.3.2 Fully Associative Cache
- A memory block can be placed in **any** cache line.
- **Pros:** Minimal conflict misses, ideal hit rate. **Cons:** Requires a tag comparator for *every line* in parallel — expensive and power-hungry.

### 2.3.3 Set-Associative Cache (N-way)
- A hybrid: cache is divided into $S$ sets, each containing $N$ lines. A block maps to a specific set, but can occupy *any of the $N$ lines* within that set.
- **Pros:** Balances hit ratio against hardware cost. **Cons:** Requires $N$ parallel comparators per set.
$$\text{Set Number} = (\text{Memory Block Number}) \bmod (\text{Number of Sets})$$

## 2.4 Replacement Policies (for Set/Full Associative)

| Policy | Strategy | Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **LRU (Least Recently Used)** | Evict the line unused for the longest time | Best practical hit rate |
| **FIFO (First-In First-Out)** | Evict the oldest block | Cheap, ignores recent use |
| **Random** | Evict a randomly chosen line | Simplest, no tracking needed |
| **LFU (Least Frequently Used)** | Evict the line with fewest hits | Good for skewed access patterns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently expect LRU for textbook problems. Note that **true LRU is impractical for $N > 4$** due to hardware complexity — embedded SoCs often use **pseudo-LRU (PLRU)** bit trees.

## 2.5 Write Policies

| Policy | Mechanism | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Write-Through** | Every write updates both cache and main memory. | Simple, but bus traffic is high → hurts performance. |
| **Write-Back** | Writes update only the cache; main memory is updated *only when the line is evicted* (if Dirty bit = 1). | High performance, but requires a **write buffer** and dirty-bit tracking. |
| **Write-Around** | Write goes directly to main memory, bypassing cache. | Useful when the data is **not re-read** (e.g., streaming I/O). |
| **Write-Allocate** | On a write miss, fetch the block into cache, then write. | Usually paired with write-back. |
| **No-Write-Allocate** | On a write miss, write directly to memory, do NOT load into cache. | Usually paired with write-through. |

## 2.6 The Three C's of Cache Misses (Hill & Smith Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **very high-yield KTU topic.** When asked to "classify cache misses", always list the 3 C's:

1. **Compulsory Misses (Cold Misses)** — The *first* reference to a block always misses. Reduced by **larger block size** or **prefetching**.
2. **Capacity Misses** — The working set is larger than the cache. Reduced by a **larger cache**.
3. **Conflict Misses** — Two blocks map to the same line/set and evict each other. Reduced by **higher associativity**.

(A 4th C — **Coherence Misses** — is sometimes added in multiprocessor contexts, caused by cache coherence protocols like MESI.)

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation a KTU 2024 student must memorise for cache problems. Note: the vertical pipe `|` is replaced with `\vert` inside tables to avoid markdown breaking.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h + m = 1$ | Hit rate + Miss rate identity | dimensionless |
| 2 | $t_{avg} = h \cdot t_{hit} + m \cdot t_{miss}$ | Basic Average Access Time | seconds (or ns) |
| 3 | $\text{AMAT} = t_{hit} + m \cdot t_{miss}$ | AMAT (canonical form) | seconds |
| 4 | $\text{AMAT}_{L2} = t_{hit,L1} + m_{L1} \cdot \bigl(t_{hit,L2} + m_{L2} \cdot t_{miss,L2}\bigr)$ | 2-level hierarchical AMAT | seconds |
| 5 | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{t_{miss}}{t_{hit}} \quad \text{(when $m = 1$)}$ | Maximum theoretical benefit | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Total Access Time} = N_{hits} \cdot t_{hit} + N_{misses} \cdot t_{miss}$ | For a known access sequence | seconds |
| 7 | $\text{Number of Sets} = \dfrac{\text{Cache Size}}{\text{Associativity} \times \text{Block Size}}$ | Set count derivation | integer |
| 8 | $\text{Tag bits} = \text{Address bits} - \text{Index bits} - \text{Offset bits}$ | Tag field width | bits |
| 9 | $\text{Block number} = \lfloor \text{Address} / \text{Block Size} \rfloor$ | Mapping math | integer |
| 10 | $\text{Cache line} = (\text{Block number}) \bmod (\text{Total lines})$ | Direct-mapped placement | integer |
| 11 | $\text{Set number} = (\text{Block number}) \bmod (\text{Number of Sets})$ | Set-associative placement | integer |
| 12 | $\text{Memory Traffic Ratio} = 1 - h$ (write-through) | Fraction of accesses reaching main memory | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\text{CPI}_{eff} = \text{CPI}_{base} + m \cdot \text{Miss Penalty (cycles)}$ | Effective CPU cycles per instruction | cycles |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Optimisation:** Modern compilers emit `-mtune` flags that reorder data structures to maximise cache locality.
- **Embedded Real-Time Systems:** Worst-case execution time (WCET) analysis **must** account for cache behaviour; some safety-critical systems *lock* cache lines to ensure determinism.
- **DMA + Cache Coherency:** In ARM Cortex-A SoCs, hardware coherency engines (ACE-Lite) ensure DMA-transferred data is consistent across L1, L2, and main memory.
- **Power Optimisation:** Gating the clock to inactive cache ways (way-prediction) is a standard low-power technique in mobile SoCs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: AMAT for a Two-Level Cache Hierarchy

This is the **most frequently asked KTU derivation**. We will derive AMAT for an L1 + L2 system from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Starting Definitions

Let:
- $h_1, m_1$ be the L1 hit/miss rate, where $h_1 + m_1 = 1$.
- $h_2, m_2$ be the L2 hit/miss rate (conditional on an L1 miss), where $h_2 + m_2 = 1$.
- $t_{L1}$ = L1 hit time (includes tag compare, ~1 ns).
- $t_{L2}$ = L2 hit time (~5–10 ns).
- $t_{M}$ = Main memory access time on an L2 miss (~100 ns).

### 3.1.2 Logical Decomposition

The CPU always pays the L1 access cost first. Outcomes:

| Event | Probability | Time Cost |
|---|---|---|
| L1 Hit | $h_1$ | $t_{L1}$ |
| L1 Miss → L2 Hit | $m_1 \cdot h_2$ | $t_{L1} + t_{L2}$ |
| L1 Miss → L2 Miss → Main Memory | $m_1 \cdot m_2$ | $t_{L1} + t_{L2} + t_{M}$ |

### 3.1.3 Full Algebraic Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT}_{total} &= h_1 \cdot t_{L1} \;+\; m_1 h_2 \cdot (t_{L1} + t_{L2}) \;+\; m_1 m_2 \cdot (t_{L1} + t_{L2} + t_{M}) \\[6pt]
&= t_{L1} \cdot \bigl( h_1 + m_1 h_2 + m_1 m_2 \bigr) \;+\; t_{L2} \cdot \bigl( m_1 h_2 + m_1 m_2 \bigr) \;+\; t_{M} \cdot \bigl( m_1 m_2 \bigr) \\[6pt]
&\text{Since } h_1 + m_1 = 1 \text{ and } h_2 + m_2 = 1, \text{ the coefficient of } t_{L1} \text{ equals 1:} \\[6pt]
&= t_{L1} + t_{L2} \cdot m_1 (h_2 + m_2) + t_{M} \cdot m_1 m_2 \\[6pt]
&= t_{L1} + m_1 \cdot t_{L2} + m_1 m_2 \cdot t_{M} \\[6pt]
&\text{Factor } t_{L2} \text{ term to expose the recursive AMAT of L2:} \\[6pt]
&= t_{L1} + m_1 \cdot \bigl( t_{L2} + m_2 \cdot t_{M} \bigr) \\[6pt]
&\text{Recognise that } \text{AMAT}_{L2} = t_{L2} + m_2 \cdot t_{M}: \\[6pt]
\text{AMAT}_{total} &= t_{L1} + m_1 \cdot \text{AMAT}_{L2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical hierarchical AMAT formula** that KTU examiners expect you to state, derive, and apply.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Address Field Partitioning

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
A 32-bit physical address system has a 64 KB direct-mapped cache with a block size of 32 bytes. Determine the number of **tag bits**, **index bits**, and **offset bits**.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Offset Bits:** Block size = 32 bytes = $2^5$ bytes. Therefore, offset bits = 5.

**Step 2 — Number of Cache Lines:** Total cache size = 64 KB = $2^{16}$ bytes.
Number of lines = $2^{16} / 32 = 2^{11}$ lines. So **index bits = 11**.

**Step 3 — Tag Bits:** Address bits = 32.
Tag bits = 32 − 11 − 5 = **16 tag bits**.

**Step 4 — Total Bits per Cache Line (storage overhead):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Storage per line} &= \text{Tag} + \text{Valid} + \text{Dirty} + \text{Data} \\
&= 16 + 1 + 1 + 32 \times 8 \\
&= 274 \text{ bits per line}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Cache Memory (including overhead):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total bits} &= 2^{11} \times 274 = 561{,}152 \text{ bits} \approx 68.5 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the cache actually consumes ~68.5 KB of physical silicon to provide 64 KB of data.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3: Direct-Mapped Cache Mapping

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement
A system has 32 KB direct-mapped cache with 16-byte blocks. The CPU generates the address `0x1A3B4C`. Find the **tag**, **index**, and **offset**.

### 3.3.2 Solution

**Step 1 — Address in binary:** `0x1A3B4C` = `0001 1010 0011 1011 0100 1100` (24 bits shown; assume 32-bit address with leading zeros).

Full 32-bit address: `0000 0001 1010 0011 1011 0100 1100`

**Step 2 — Block Size:** 16 bytes → offset bits = $\log_2 16 = 4$.

**Step 3 — Number of Cache Lines:** $32\text{ KB} / 16 = 2048 = 2^{11}$ → **index bits = 11**.

**Step 4 — Tag Bits:** $32 - 11 - 4 = 17$.

**Step 5 — Extract fields:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address (binary)} &= \underbrace{0000\,0000\,0}_{\text{partial}} \underbrace{1\,1010\,0011\,1}_{\text{Tag (17 bits)}} \underbrace{011\,1011\,0100}_{\text{Index (11 bits)}} \underbrace{1100}_{\text{Offset (4 bits)}} \\
\text{Tag} &= \text{0x00D5E (approx)} \\
\text{Index} &= 0x6D4 \quad (\text{line number}) \\
\text{Offset} &= 0xC \quad (\text{byte 12 within block})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify placement rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block Number} &= \lfloor 0x1A3B4C / 16 \rfloor = 0x1A3B4 \\
\text{Cache Line} &= 0x1A3B4 \bmod 2048 = 0x1A3B4 - 170 \times 2048 = 0x6D4 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example #4: AMAT Numerical Problem (KTU Favourite)

### 3.4.1 Problem
A processor has an L1 cache with hit time = **1 ns**, miss rate = **5%**. Main memory access time = **100 ns**. Compute (a) AMAT, (b) speedup if we double the cache to halve the miss rate to 2.5%.

### 3.4.2 Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) AMAT} &= t_{hit} + m \cdot t_{miss} \\
&= 1 + 0.05 \times 100 \\
&= 1 + 5 = \mathbf{6 \text{ ns}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(b) AMAT}_{new} &= 1 + 0.025 \times 100 = 1 + 2.5 = 3.5 \text{ ns} \\
\text{Speedup} &= \frac{6}{3.5} \approx \mathbf{1.714 \times}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Halving the miss rate roughly doubled the speedup — illustrating that **miss rate reduction is the dominant lever** for cache performance, much more so than tweaking hit time.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Mapping Techniques

| Property | Direct-Mapped | Fully Associative | N-way Set-Associative |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardware Complexity | **Lowest** | **Highest** | Moderate |
| Tag Comparators | 1 | $N_{lines}$ | $N$ (per set) |
| Conflict Misses | Highest | Lowest | Intermediate |
| Compulsory Misses | Same across all | Same across all | Same across all |
| Access Speed | Fastest | Slowest | Slightly slower than direct |
| Power Consumption | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| Typical Use | L1 instruction caches | TLB, small fully-associative structures | L1/L2 data caches |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level CPU–Cache–Memory Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU Core"]
    L1I["L1 Instruction Cache"]
    L1D["L1 Data Cache"]
    L2["L2 Unified Cache"]
    L3["L3 Shared Cache"]
    MMU["MMU / TLB"]
    BUS["System Bus / NoC"]
    DRAM["Main Memory DRAM"]

    CPU -->|IFetch| L1I
    CPU -->|Load/Store| L1D
    L1I -->|Miss| L2
    L1D -->|Miss| L2
    L2 -->|Miss| L3
    L3 -->|Miss| BUS
    CPU -->|Addr Trans| MMU
    MMU -->|Phys Addr| L1I
    MMU -->|Phys Addr| L1D
    BUS --> DRAM
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical embedded SoC cache hierarchy** (think ARM Cortex-A53/A57). Note the asymmetric L1 split (Harvard) and unified lower levels (von Neumann).

## 4.2 Address Field Partitioning (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["32-bit Physical Address"] --> B["TAG (T bits)"]
    A --> C["INDEX (I bits)"]
    A --> D["OFFSET (O bits)"]
    C --> E["Cache Array: 2^I Sets"]
    B --> F["Tag Comparator & Valid Bit Check"]
    E --> F
    F --> G{"Hit or Miss?"}
    G -->|Hit| H["Output: Data word at Offset O"]
    G -->|Miss| I["Fetch Block from Next Level"]
    I --> J["Eviction / Allocation Logic"]
    J --> E
```

## 4.3 Mapping Techniques Compared

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Direct["Direct-Mapped Cache (1-way)"]
        D1["Mem Block 0"] -.-> DL0["Line 0"]
        D2["Mem Block 1"] -.-> DL1["Line 1"]
        D3["Mem Block 2"] -.-> DL2["Line 2"]
        D4["Mem Block 3"] -.-> DL3["Line 3"]
        D5["Mem Block 4"] -.-> DL0
        D6["Mem Block 5"] -.-> DL1
    end

    subgraph SetAssoc["2-way Set-Associative"]
        S1["Mem Block 0"] -.-> SL0A["Set 0 Way 0"]
        S1b["Mem Block 4"] -.-> SL0B["Set 0 Way 1"]
        S2["Mem Block 1"] -.-> SL1A["Set 1 Way 0"]
        S2b["Mem Block 5"] -.-> SL1B["Set 1 Way 1"]
    end

    subgraph FullAssoc["Fully Associative"]
        F1["Mem Block 0"] -.-> ANYA["Any Line"]
        F2["Mem Block 1"] -.-> ANYB["Any Line"]
        F3["Mem Block 2"] -.-> ANYC["Any Line"]
        F4["Mem Block 3"] -.-> ANYD["Any Line"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Read/Write Flow with Write-Back Policy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["CPU Issues Load/Store"]) --> Lookup{"Tag Match in Set? AND Valid=1?"}
    Lookup -->|Yes - Hit| RWCheck{"Read or Write?"}
    Lookup -->|No - Miss| Evict{"Empty Line in Set?"}
    Evict -->|Yes| Fetch["Fetch Block from L2/Memory"]
    Evict -->|No| LRU["Select Victim via LRU"]
    LRU --> DirtyCheck{"Dirty Bit = 1?"}
    DirtyCheck -->|Yes| WriteBack["Write Victim to L2/Memory"]
    DirtyCheck -->|No| Fetch
    WriteBack --> Fetch
    Fetch --> Allocate["Allocate Block in Selected Line"]
    Allocate --> UpdateV["Set Valid = 1, Clear Dirty"]
    UpdateV --> RWCheck
    RWCheck -->|Read| ReturnData["Return Data at Offset"]
    RWCheck -->|Write - WB| SetDirty["Update Data, Set Dirty = 1"]
    RWCheck -->|Write - WT| WriteMem["Write Through to L2/Memory"]
    ReturnData --> End(["Complete"])
    SetDirty --> End
    WriteMem --> End
```

## 4.5 AMAT Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["t_hit: Cache access cost"] --> C["AMAT = t_hit + m * t_miss"]
    B["t_miss: Miss penalty"] --> C
    D["m: Miss rate 0 to 1"] --> C
    C --> E["Output: Effective access time (ns)"]
    E --> F["CPU stalls if AMAT >> CPU cycle time"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms with one-line examples:**
(i) Cache hit, (ii) Cache miss, (iii) Hit rate, (iv) Miss penalty, (v) Tag, (vi) Block/Line.

#### Model Answer (Exam-Ready)

> (i) **Cache hit:** A memory access where the requested data is found in the cache.
> Example: Accessing a loop variable already in L1.
> (ii) **Cache miss:** A memory access where the requested data is NOT in the cache, forcing a fetch from main memory.
> Example: First access to a function in a cold cache.
> (iii) **Hit rate (h):** Fraction of total accesses served by the cache; $h = \text{Hits} / \text{Total accesses}$. Typical value: **0.90 – 0.99**.
> (iv) **Miss penalty:** The extra time incurred on a miss to fetch the block from main memory. Typical value: **50–100 ns**.
> (v) **Tag:** The high-order bits of an address stored alongside a cache line, used to identify *which* main-memory block currently occupies that line.
> (vi) **Block/Line:** The basic unit of data transfer between cache and memory; typical size **64 bytes**.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Distinguish between Write-Through and Write-Back cache policies. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

#### Model Answer

> **Write-Through (WT):** Every CPU write updates the cache **and** main memory **simultaneously**.
> *Advantage:* Main memory always has the latest data; simpler coherence.
> *Disadvantage:* Generates excessive bus traffic → poor performance.
>
> **Write-Back (WB):** CPU writes only update the cache; main memory is updated **only when the modified (dirty) line is evicted**.
> *Advantage:* Lower memory traffic → higher performance.
> *Disadvantage:* Needs a dirty bit, write buffer, and risks data loss on power failure.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question has internal choice: **either (a) OR (b)** style. We present here **Question A** and **Question B** as the two alternatives. Each contains two 7-mark sub-parts.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three cache mapping techniques — Direct, Associative, and Set-Associative — with diagrams. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.

#### Model Solution (Stepwise)

**Direct-Mapped Cache:**
- Each main memory block maps to exactly **one** cache line: $\text{line} = (\text{block no.}) \bmod N$.
- Hardware: 1 tag comparator per line array.
- *Advantage:* Simple, fast, low power.
- *Disadvantage:* High conflict misses (e.g., blocks 0, 128, 256 thrash on the same line in a 128-line cache).

**Fully Associative Cache:**
- A block may be placed in **any** cache line.
- Hardware: **$N$** parallel tag comparators.
- *Advantage:* Zero conflict misses; best hit rate.
- *Disadvantage:* Expensive, high power, impractical for large caches.

**N-way Set-Associative Cache:**
- Cache is split into $S$ sets, each with $N$ lines; a block maps to a specific set but can occupy any of the $N$ ways.
- Hardware: $N$ comparators per set.
- *Advantage:* Compromise — reduces conflict misses with moderate cost.
- *Disadvantage:* More complex replacement logic (LRU) and slightly higher hit time.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 32-bit system has a **Direct-Mapped Cache** of size **16 KB** with block size **32 bytes**. The CPU accesses the address `0x4000_2050`. Compute the **Tag**, **Index**, and **Offset** values. Also calculate the total cache memory (in bits) including valid and dirty bits.

#### Model Solution (Stepwise)

**Step 1 — Compute field widths:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset bits} &= \log_2(32) = 5 \\
\text{Number of lines} &= 16\text{ KB} / 32\text{ B} = 512 = 2^9 \\
\text{Index bits} &= 9 \\
\text{Tag bits} &= 32 - 9 - 5 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Extract fields from `0x4000_2050`:**

Address in binary (32 bits):
```
0100 0000 0000 0000 0010 0000 0101 0000
```

Split:
- **Tag** (top 18 bits) = `0100 0000 0000 0000 00` = `0x10000` (18 bits)
- **Index** (next 9 bits) = `1 0000 0010` = `0x102` (line 258)
- **Offset** (last 5 bits) = `1 0000` = `0x10` = byte 16

**Step 3 — Total cache bits:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Per line} &= \text{Tag} + \text{Valid} + \text{Dirty} + \text{Data} \\
&= 18 + 1 + 1 + 32 \times 8 \\
&= 276 \text{ bits} \\[4pt]
\text{Total cache bits} &= 512 \times 276 = 141{,}312 \text{ bits} \approx 17.25 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **AMAT (Average Memory Access Time)** for a **two-level cache hierarchy**. Derive the formula.

#### Model Solution (Stepwise)

**Step 1 — Define terms:**
- $t_{L1}$, $h_1$, $m_1$: L1 access time, hit rate, miss rate.
- $t_{L2}$, $h_2$, $m_2$: L2 access time, hit rate, miss rate (conditional on L1 miss).
- $t_M$: Main memory access time.

**Step 2 — Enumerate access outcomes (probability × cost):**

| Outcome | Probability | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| L1 hit | $h_1$ | $t_{L1}$ |
| L1 miss → L2 hit | $m_1 h_2$ | $t_{L1} + t_{L2}$ |
| L1 miss → L2 miss → Memory | $m_1 m_2$ | $t_{L1} + t_{L2} + t_M$ |

**Step 3 — Derive AMAT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT} &= h_1 t_{L1} + m_1 h_2 (t_{L1} + t_{L2}) + m_1 m_2 (t_{L1} + t_{L2} + t_M) \\
&= t_{L1}(h_1 + m_1 h_2 + m_1 m_2) + t_{L2}(m_1 h_2 + m_1 m_2) + t_M(m_1 m_2) \\
&= t_{L1} \cdot 1 + m_1 t_{L2} + m_1 m_2 t_M \\
\therefore \text{AMAT} &= t_{L1} + m_1 \cdot \text{AMAT}_{L2} \\
\text{where } \text{AMAT}_{L2} &= t_{L2} + m_2 \cdot t_M
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — (Bonus) Numerical sanity check:**
Given $t_{L1} = 1$ ns, $m_1 = 0.05$, $t_{L2} = 5$ ns, $m_2 = 0.20$, $t_M = 100$ ns:

$$
\text{AMAT} = 1 + 0.05 \times (5 + 0.20 \times 100) = 1 + 0.05 \times 25 = 1 + 1.25 = 2.25 \text{ ns}
$$

This is a **6.67× speedup** over no cache (100 ns).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Three C's of Cache Misses**. For each C, suggest a concrete hardware/architectural technique to reduce it.

#### Model Solution (Stepwise)

**C1 — Compulsory Misses (Cold Misses):**
- *Cause:* First reference to a block. Inevitable on a cold start.
- *Reduction:* **Larger block size** (captures spatial neighbours), **hardware prefetching** (stream buffers), or **software prefetch instructions** like ARM's `PLD`.

**C2 — Capacity Misses:**
- *Cause:* Working set (active blocks) exceeds cache size.
- *Reduction:* **Larger cache capacity**, **victim caches** (small fully-associative buffer for recently evicted lines), or **data compression** in memory (e.g., IBM's memory compression in mainframes).

**C3 — Conflict Misses:**
- *Cause:* Two highly active blocks map to the same set/line and evict each other.
- *Reduction:* **Higher associativity** (2-way → 4-way → 8-way), **better replacement policy** (LRU vs FIFO), or **hash-based re-mapping** (skewed associative caches — Seznec's skewing).

> (4th C — **Coherence Misses** in multi-core systems: caused by another core invalidating a line. Reduced by **MESI/MOESI coherence protocols**, **sharing tracking**, or **private/shared cache partitioning**.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas (read carefully):**
> 1. **Forgetting the L1 access cost in multi-level AMAT.** Many students write $m_1 \cdot \text{AMAT}_{L2}$ *without* the $t_{L1}$ term. The CPU **always** pays the L1 access time. **−2 marks** if missed.
> 2. **Confusing $h$ and $m$.** If a problem says "hit rate = 95%", then $m = 0.05$ — students often plug $0.95$ into the miss-penalty term. **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Off-by-one in offset bits.** For a 32-byte block, offset = 5 bits, not 6. Double-check: $2^5 = 32$. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Not mentioning the dirty bit in write-back storage calculation.** Including 1 dirty bit + 1 valid bit + tag + data. **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting the mod operation in cache mapping.** "Cache line = block number" is incomplete; it's `(block number) mod (lines)`. **−1 mark**.
> 6. **Mixing up "number of sets" with "number of lines"** in set-associative problems. Lines = sets × ways. **−1 mark**.
> 7. **Not showing units** (ns, µs, cycles) in numerical AMAT problems. KTU deducts **½ mark** for sloppy unit handling.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap: Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10 Things You Must NOT Forget on the Exam Day:**
> - [x] **Cache** is fast SRAM between CPU and DRAM; exploits *temporal* and *spatial* locality.
> - [x] **AMAT** formula: $t_{hit} + m \cdot t_{miss}$ (single level); $t_{L1} + m_1 \cdot (t_{L2} + m_2 \cdot t_M)$ (two levels).
> - [x] **Hit Rate + Miss Rate = 1** — identity always holds.
> - [x] **Three mapping techniques:** Direct (1 comparator, fast, thrashing), Fully Associative (N comparators, ideal hit rate, expensive), Set-Associative (compromise, used in practice).
> - [x] **Replacement policies:** LRU > FIFO > Random (in terms of hit rate); LRU is most asked.
> - [x] **Write policies:** Write-Through (always updates memory, simple), Write-Back (only on eviction, uses dirty bit, fast), Write-Around (bypasses cache on write).
> - [x] **Address fields:** Tag | Index | Offset. Offset = $\log_2$(block size), Index = $\log_2$(#sets), Tag = remaining bits.
> - [x] **Mapping math:** Cache line = Block number mod (number of lines).
> - [x] **The Three C's:** Compulsory (larger block helps), Capacity (larger cache helps), Conflict (higher associativity helps).
> - [x] **Storage overhead per line:** Tag + Valid bit + Dirty bit + (Block size × 8) data bits.
> - [x] **Multi-level hierarchy speedup:** Adding L2 reduces AMAT only if $m_1$ is non-zero and $t_{L2} < t_M$.
> - [x] **Embedded context:** ARM Cortex-M has no cache (deterministic); Cortex-A has L1/L2/L3 with hardware coherency.
> - [x] **Power tip:** Way-prediction and clock-gating unused ways save dynamic power in mobile SoCs.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Cache Memory Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PBEVT504 — Embedded System Design*
