topics.title: Memory Mapping and Addresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Memory Mapping and Addresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Mapping** is the systematic process of assigning unique physical addresses from the processor's address space to specific memory locations, peripheral registers, and I/O devices so that the Central Processing Unit (CPU) can read from or write to them using a unified address-decoding mechanism.

In the context of **Embedded System Design (PBEVT504)**, every microcontroller (such as the 8051, ARM Cortex-M, or PIC families) has a finite **Address Bus Width** (commonly 16-bit, giving $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ unique locations) that determines its *addressable memory space*. Memory mapping defines which logical regions of this space correspond to *Program Memory* (ROM/Flash), *Data Memory* (RAM), *Special Function Registers (SFRs)*, and *External Memory Peripherals*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Address**: An n-bit binary number placed on the address bus that uniquely identifies one memory location among $2^n$ possible locations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hotel Room Numbering System"

Imagine a large **32-floor hotel** (the address space) with **256 rooms on each floor** (16-bit addressing gives 65536 rooms = 65,536 addresses).

- The **floor number + room number combination** is the *address* (like `0x2004`).
- Each **room** is a *memory location* (1 byte typically).
- The **front desk registry** that knows which floor contains "Guests (RAM)" and which contains "Archives (ROM)" is the **Memory Map**.
- The **keycard system** that grants access only to authorized floors is the **Address Decoder**.

When a guest (the CPU) requests Room `0x2004`, the address decoder (front desk) checks the memory map (registry), determines that floor `0x2000`–`0x20FF` is reserved for *Internal RAM*, and unlocks that specific room. **Memory Mapping is exactly this: a published table telling the hardware which address ranges belong to which physical device.**

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Values

- **Word Size** (data bus width): typically **8-bit, 16-bit, or 32-bit** for embedded MCUs.
- **Address Bus Width**: **16-bit** (8051 classic) or **32-bit** (ARM Cortex-M).
- **Addressable Unit**: **1 byte (8 bits)** is the universal standard in KTU syllabus.
- **Memory Capacity Notation**: $2^{10} = 1$ Kibi, $2^{20} = 1$ Mebi (1 KB = 1024 bytes in embedded contexts).
- **Little-Endian Ordering**: Low-order byte stored at the **lowest** address (used by 8051, ARM, x86).
- **Big-Endian Ordering**: High-order byte stored at the **lowest** address (used by classic Motorola, some network protocols).

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners **frequently** test whether students know the difference between *Memory-Mapped I/O* and *Isolated/Port-Mapped I/O* — this is a guaranteed 3-mark short question topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Memory Mapping Concepts

#### 2.1.1 Address Space and Address Bus

The **Address Space** is the total set of unique binary addresses a processor can generate. If the address bus has *n* lines, the size of the address space *N* is given by:

$$N = 2^{n} \text{ locations}$$

Each location stores **1 byte** (unless explicitly noted). The **Data Bus Width** $d$ determines how many bits are transferred in a single memory cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Capacity Formula:** $\text{Total Bytes} = 2^{n} \times \text{(bytes per address)}$
> For an 8051 with a 16-bit address bus and 1 byte per address → $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes = **64 KB**.

#### 2.1.2 Memory Map — The Logical-to-Physical Translation Table

A **Memory Map** is a diagram (or hardware decoding logic) that partitions the address space into named regions. The typical KTU-tested memory map for an 8051-based system is:

| Region (Hex)             | Size       | Device Type                | Function                       |
|--------------------------|------------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|
| `0x0000` – `0x0FFF`      | 4 KB       | Internal ROM / Flash       | Program Memory (Lower 4 KB)    |
| `0x1000` – `0xFFFF`      | 60 KB      | External ROM               | Program Memory (Upper 60 KB)   |
| `0x0000` – `0x007F`      | 128 B      | Internal RAM (Data)        | Lower 128 bytes (working RAM)  |
| `0x0080` – `0x00FF`      | 128 B      | SFRs (Special Func. Regs) | I/O, Timers, Serial, Interrupts|
| `0x0100` – `0x07FF`      | Up to 2 KB | External RAM               | Data Memory (XRAM)             |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU POINT:** In 8051 architecture, the **Program Memory** and **Data Memory** share the same address range (`0x0000`–`0xFFFF`) but are accessed using **different control signals** — `\overline{PSEN}` (Program Store Enable) for code memory, and `\overline{RD}` / `\overline{WR}` for data memory. This is the **Harvard Architecture** in action.

#### 2.1.3 Memory-Mapped I/O vs Isolated I/O

This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question topic**. There are two ways the CPU can communicate with peripherals:

| Parameter                        | Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO)                       | Isolated I/O (Port-Mapped I/O)                |
|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| **Address Space**                | I/O devices share memory address space         | Separate I/O address space (Intel style)       |
| **Address Range**                | Part of `0x0000` – `0xFFFF`                    | Dedicated ports (e.g., `IN`/`OUT` on 8086)     |
| **Control Signal**               | Standard `\overline{RD}` / `\overline{WR}`     | Special `\overline{IOR}` / `\overline{IOW}`     |
| **Instructions Used**            | All memory instructions (`MOV`, `LDR`, `STR`)  | Special `IN`/`OUT` instructions only           |
| **Address Decoding**             | Full address decoder required                  | Simpler — few address lines + control signal   |
| **Speed**                        | Slightly slower (full address decode)          | Faster (fewer lines to decode)                 |
| **Flexibility**                  | More flexible — I/O can be anywhere            | Limited to fixed port addresses                |
| **Example MCUs**                 | **ARM Cortex-M, 8051 (data memory), most MCUs**| Classic 8086, Z80                              |

> [!TIP]
> **Modern 32-bit Embedded MCUs (ARM Cortex-M) use ONLY Memory-Mapped I/O** — there are no special `IN`/`OUT` instructions. KTU examiners love asking "Why is memory-mapped I/O preferred in modern systems?" The answer lies in **RISC load/store uniformity** and **compiler optimization**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Equations

| #  | Formula / Concept                                              | Equation or Expression                          | Units / Notes                          |
|----|----------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 1  | Total Addressable Memory                                        | $M = 2^{n}$                                      | bytes (n = address bus lines)          |
| 2  | Memory Size in KB                                               | $\text{Size}_{KB} = 2^{n} / 1024$                | KibiBytes (KiB)                        |
| 3  | Highest Address Value                                           | $A_{max} = 2^{n} - 1$                            | Decimal equivalent of `0xFFFF` etc.    |
| 4  | Number of Address Lines Required for *X* KB                    | $n = \log_{2}(X \times 1024)$                     | Always round **UP** to next integer    |
| 5  | Memory Chip Capacity (with chip's own address lines $a$)       | $C = 2^{a}$                                      | bytes per chip                         |
| 6  | Number of Chips to fill Memory Region                          | $N_{chips} = M / C$                              | Integer count (interleaving possible)  |
| 7  | Address Decoding — Linear (n-to-2^n) Decoder Output            | $\text{CS} = \overline{A_{n-1} \cdot A_{n-2} \cdots A_0}$ | Active-LOW chip select            |
| 8  | Little-Endian Address Computation                              | $\text{Addr} = \text{Base} + \text{Offset}$      | Offset is byte-wise                    |
| 9  | Memory Cycle Time (theoretical)                                 | $T_{cyc} = 1 / f_{CLK}$                          | Seconds (f = clock frequency in Hz)    |
| 10 | Bandwidth of Memory                                             | $BW = (\text{Bus Width} \times f_{CLK}) / 8$     | Bytes/second                           |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for n-to-2^n Decoder:** A decoder with *n* inputs has exactly $2^n$ outputs. **Output $k$** is active when the binary input equals the decimal number $k$. For active-LOW chip select, the output is $\overline{Y_k}$, which goes LOW (logic 0) when selected.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Firmware Developers** must consult the memory map to know where to place interrupt vectors (`0x0000` on most MCUs), stack pointer, and heap.
- **PCB Hardware Designers** implement the address decoding logic (using 74LS138 / 74LS139 decoders) so that the correct memory chip responds to the correct address range.
- **IoT Edge Devices** rely on memory-mapped registers to configure GPIO pins, ADC channels, and communication peripherals.
- **Automotive ECU Design** uses a memory map to segregate safety-critical code (placed in protected Flash regions) from application code.

## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Address Lines for a Given Memory Size

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Design an embedded system requiring **32 KB of ROM** and **16 KB of RAM** starting at address `0x0000`. The processor uses a 16-bit address bus. Determine the address lines required, the memory map, and the address decoder logic for the RAM chip.

#### Step 1 — Convert memory sizes to byte counts

Each memory region is computed by multiplying KB by 1024.

$$\text{ROM Size} = 32 \text{ KB} = 32 \times 1024 = 32{,}768 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\text{RAM Size} = 16 \text{ KB} = 16 \times 1024 = 16{,}384 \text{ bytes}$$

This conversion is required because the formula $M = 2^n$ works in raw bytes, not KB.

#### Step 2 — Calculate address lines required for each region

The number of address lines *a* needed to address a memory chip of capacity $C$ bytes is found using the inverse of $M = 2^a$.

$$a_{ROM} = \log_2(32 \times 1024) = \log_2(32{,}768)$$

We factor 32,768 into powers of 2 for exact computation:

$$32{,}768 = 32 \times 1024 = 2^5 \times 2^{10} = 2^{15}$$

Therefore:

$$a_{ROM} = \log_2(2^{15}) = 15 \text{ address lines}$$

Similarly for RAM:

$$a_{RAM} = \log_2(16 \times 1024) = \log_2(16{,}384) = \log_2(2^{14}) = 14 \text{ address lines}$$

#### Step 3 — Construct the Memory Map

Since the processor is 16-bit, the total space is `0x0000` to `0xFFFF` (i.e., $2^{16} - 1 = 65{,}535$).

- **ROM (32 KB)** occupies the **lower** region: starts at `0x0000` and ends at `0x0000 + 32768 - 1`.
- **RAM (16 KB)** occupies the next region: starts at `0x8000` and ends at `0x8000 + 16384 - 1`.

Compute end address of ROM:

$$\text{ROM End} = 0 \times 65{,}536 + 32{,}768 - 1 = 32{,}767 = 0 \times 7\text{FFF}$$

Compute end address of RAM:

$$\text{RAM End} = 32{,}768 + 16{,}384 - 1 = 49{,}151 = 0 \times \text{BFFF}$$

So the complete map is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ROM:} & \quad 0\text{x}0000 - 0\text{x}7\text{FFF} \quad (32\text{ KB}) \\
\text{RAM:} & \quad 0\text{x}8000 - 0\text{x}\text{BFFF} \quad (16\text{ KB}) \\
\text{Unused:} & \quad 0\text{x}\text{C000} - 0\text{x}\text{FFFF} \quad (16\text{ KB})
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4 — Derive the Chip Select Logic

The 16-bit address bus has lines $A_{15} \ldots A_0$. The lower 15 lines $A_{14} \ldots A_0$ are used to address the **32 KB ROM chip** (which needs 15 address lines), and the lower 14 lines $A_{13} \ldots A_0$ are used to address the **16 KB RAM chip**.

- **ROM CS condition:** Active for addresses `0x0000`–`0x7FFF`, i.e., when $A_{15} = 0$.
- **RAM CS condition:** Active for addresses `0x8000`–`0xBFFF`, i.e., when $A_{15} = 1$ AND $A_{14} = 0$.

Expressed in Boolean form (active-LOW chip selects $\overline{CS_{ROM}}$ and $\overline{CS_{RAM}}$):

$$\overline{CS_{ROM}} = \overline{\overline{A_{15}}} = A_{15} \text{ when } A_{15}=0$$

Cleaner: $\overline{CS_{ROM}} = A_{15} \;\;$ (since $A_{15}=0$ during ROM access, and an active-LOW CS needs 0).

$$\overline{CS_{ROM}} = A_{15} \quad \text{(true when ROM is accessed, $A_{15}=0$)}$$

For RAM, both conditions must hold:

$$\overline{CS_{RAM}} = \overline{A_{15} \cdot \overline{A_{14}}}$$

The unused region `0xC000`–`0xFFFF` ($A_{15}=1, A_{14}=1$) leaves both chips disabled, which is the desired behaviour.

#### Step 5 — Verification

Confirm a sample address `0x8005` accesses RAM:

- Binary: `0x8005` = `1000 0000 0000 0101`
- $A_{15} = 1$, $A_{14} = 0$, lower 14 bits = `00 0000 0000 0101` = `0x0005`
- $\overline{CS_{RAM}} = \overline{1 \cdot \overline{0}} = \overline{1 \cdot 1} = \overline{1} = 0$ → **RAM selected** ✓
- $\overline{CS_{ROM}} = 1$ → ROM disabled ✓
- RAM internal address = `0x0005` (the offset from base `0x8000`) ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Memory Expansion by Interleaving (Bank Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 16-bit data bus MCU needs **64 KB × 16** of memory but only has chips of size **32 KB × 8**. Show how to expand using two chips and an address decoder.

#### Step 1 — Compute chips required

Total memory needed (in 8-bit bytes) = $64\text{ KB} \times 2 = 128\text{ KB} = 131{,}072$ bytes.

Each chip has capacity $32 \text{ KB} = 32{,}768$ bytes.

$$N_{chips} = \frac{131{,}072}{32{,}768} = 4 \text{ chips}$$

#### Step 2 — Arrange as 2 banks × 2 chips per bank

Since data bus is 16-bit (2 bytes wide), we need **2 chips in parallel** to fill a single 16-bit fetch. To get 64K addresses × 16 bits, we need **2 such parallel pairs (banks)**.

- **Bank 0 (Lower Word — $D_{7}$ to $D_{0}$):** Chip A (32 KB × 8) for `0x0000`–`0x7FFF` AND Chip B (32 KB × 8) for `0x8000`–`0xFFFF`.
- **Bank 1 (Upper Word — $D_{15}$ to $D_{8}$):** Chip C and Chip D, mirrors of A and B.

#### Step 3 — Address line allocation

Address lines $A_{14}\ldots A_0$ (15 lines) feed **all four chips in parallel** because each chip is 32 KB. The remaining line $A_{15}$ drives the **2-to-4 decoder** (e.g., 74LS139) to select the right pair.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{15} = 0: \quad & \text{Y0 active} \rightarrow \text{Enable Bank 0 (Chips A, C)} \\
A_{15} = 1: \quad & \text{Y1 active} \rightarrow \text{Enable Bank 1 (Chips B, D)}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Byte-Enable / Word-Enable** scheme used in real hardware.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect                       | 8051 (Harvard, 16-bit addr)         | ARM Cortex-M3 (von Neumann, 32-bit addr) | 8086 (von Neumann, 20-bit addr) |
|------------------------------|--------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Total Address Space**      | 64 KB code + 64 KB data              | 4 GB unified                             | 1 MB (segmented: 16×64 KB)     |
| **Architecture Type**        | Harvard (separate code/data buses)   | Modified Harvard (single bus, multi-port) | von Neumann                    |
| **Memory Map Overlap**       | Yes — same addr, different control   | No overlap (unified)                      | No overlap                      |
| **I/O Style**                | Memory-mapped + SFRs                 | Pure memory-mapped I/O                    | Isolated + memory-mixed         |
| **Address Decoding**         | Internal + external via `\overline{PSEN}` | Bus matrix (AHB/APB)                | Segment + offset               |
| **Endianness**               | Little-endian                        | Configurable (usually little)             | Little-endian                  |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **von Neumann bottleneck** (single bus for code and data) is the key reason modern high-performance MCUs use **modified Harvard** with separate caches for instructions and data, even if the address space is unified.

## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — Address-Decoded Memory Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a CPU, address decoder, and memory chips interact in a typical 16-bit address embedded system. All node IDs are alphanumeric and labels contain only clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpu1[CPU 16-BIT]
    abus[ADDRESS BUS A15-A0]
    dbus[DATA BUS D7-D0]
    ctrlb[CONTROL BUS RD WR PSEN]
    dec1[3-TO-8 DECODER 74LS138]
    rom1[ROM CHIP 32 KB CS1]
    ram1[RAM CHIP 16 KB CS2]
    sfr1[SFR BLOCK 128 BYTES]
    xram1[EXTERNAL RAM CS3]

    cpu1 --> abus
    cpu1 --> dbus
    cpu1 --> ctrlb
    abus --> dec1
    ctrlb --> dec1
    dec1 -->|Y0| rom1
    dec1 -->|Y1| ram1
    dec1 -->|Y2| sfr1
    dec1 -->|Y3| xram1
    dbus --> rom1
    dbus --> ram1
    dbus --> sfr1
    dbus --> xram1
```

**Reading the diagram:**

1. The **CPU** drives three buses in parallel: Address, Data, and Control.
2. The upper address lines (e.g., A15, A14, A13) feed the **3-to-8 Decoder**.
3. Each decoder output Y0–Y7 enables exactly one memory/peripheral block at a time.
4. The **Data Bus** is bidirectional and shared by all devices; only the chip-selected device drives it during a read.

### 4.2 Memory Map Block Diagram — Linear Address Space Partitioning

The next Mermaid diagram visualizes how the 16-bit address space `0x0000` to `0xFFFF` is partitioned into logical regions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spaceA[ADDR 0x0000]
    spaceB[0x7FFF END ROM]
    spaceC[0x8000 START RAM]
    spaceD[0xBFFF END RAM]
    spaceE[0xC000 START UNUSED]
    spaceF[0xFFFF END UNUSED]
    rombox[ROM REGION 32 KB CODE]
    rambox[RAM REGION 16 KB DATA]
    unusedbox[UNUSED 16 KB FUTURE EXPANSION]

    spaceA --> rombox --> spaceB --> spaceC --> rambox --> spaceD --> spaceE --> unusedbox --> spaceF
```

**Interpretation:** Reading the address bus from `0x0000` upward, the regions appear in this order: ROM, then RAM, then a 16 KB "hole" reserved for future memory expansion (a common technique called **address space reservation** in KTU design problems).

### 4.3 Address Decoder Logic Schematic (Boolean-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inpA15[A15]
    inpA14[A14]
    inpA13[A13]
    invA15[INVERTER]
    andG1[AND GATE CS-ROM]
    andG2[AND GATE CS-RAM]
    invA14[INVERTER]

    inpA15 --> invA15
    invA15 --> andG1
    inpA14 --> andG2
    invA14 --> andG2
    inpA13 --> andG1
    inpA13 --> andG2
```

> [!TIP]
> This **partial decoder** uses only 3 address lines to generate chip selects. It is simpler than a full 3-to-8 decoder but causes **address mirroring** — the same chip responds to multiple address ranges. KTU problems often ask you to identify mirroring regions and fix them with full decoding.

### 4.4 Little-Endian vs Big-Endian Storage Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    addrA[ADDRESS 0x1000]
    addrB[ADDRESS 0x1001]
    addrC[ADDRESS 0x1002]
    addrD[ADDRESS 0x1003]
    valLE[BYTE 0x78 LSB]
    valLE2[BYTE 0x56]
    valLE3[BYTE 0x34]
    valLE4[BYTE 0x12 MSB]
    valBE[BYTE 0x12 MSB]
    valBE2[BYTE 0x34]
    valBE3[BYTE 0x56]
    valBE4[BYTE 0x78 LSB]

    addrA --- valLE
    addrA --- valBE
    addrB --- valLE2
    addrB --- valBE2
    addrC --- valLE3
    addrC --- valBE3
    addrD --- valLE4
    addrD --- valBE4
```

**Reading the diagram:** The 32-bit value `0x12345678` is stored at base address `0x1000`.

- **Little-Endian path (left label column):** LSB `0x78` goes to the **lowest** address `0x1000`. This is what 8051 and ARM Cortex-M use.
- **Big-Endian path (right label column):** MSB `0x12` goes to the **lowest** address `0x1000`. This is used in classic Motorola and some TCP/IP stacks.

## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define the term "memory map" in an embedded system. Differentiate between memory-mapped I/O and I/O-mapped I/O.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **memory map** is a tabular representation of how a processor's address space is allocated among various memory and I/O devices. Each address range is reserved for a specific device (e.g., ROM, RAM, SFRs).

**Memory-Mapped I/O vs I/O-Mapped I/O:**

| Feature            | Memory-Mapped I/O                   | I/O-Mapped I/O                          |
|--------------------|--------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Address Space      | I/O shares memory address space      | Separate I/O address space               |
| Instructions       | All memory instructions (e.g., `LDR`, `MOV`) | Special `IN`/`OUT` instructions only |
| Control Signal     | Standard memory read/write signals   | Special I/O read/write signals           |

Memory-mapped I/O offers greater flexibility and is preferred in modern RISC-based embedded systems. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional 1]**
*What is address decoding? Why is it necessary in embedded systems?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Address decoding** is the hardware process of interpreting the address bus lines to generate unique **chip select (CS)** signals for each memory/peripheral device connected to the system. It is implemented using combinational logic gates (AND, OR, NOT) or dedicated decoder ICs such as the **74LS138** (3-to-8) or **74LS139** (2-to-4).

**Necessity:**

1. Ensures that **only one device** responds to a given address — prevents bus contention.
2. Allows multiple memory chips to share a common address and data bus.
3. Provides **memory protection** by isolating critical regions (e.g., boot ROM).
4. Enables **memory expansion** without software modification. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Memory Map Design with Decoder

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** An embedded system uses an 8-bit microcontroller with a 16-bit address bus. The system must contain:
- 8 KB of ROM starting at address `0x0000`
- 4 KB of RAM starting at address `0x4000`
- A 2 KB I/O-mapped peripheral block starting at address `0x8000`

Draw the **memory map**, calculate the **number of address lines** required for each chip, and derive the **chip select logic** using a 74LS138 decoder. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Little-Endian vs Big-Endian** byte ordering with a suitable diagram. If the 32-bit value `0xA5B6C7D8` is stored at base address `0x2000`, list the contents of addresses `0x2000`, `0x2001`, `0x2002`, and `0x2003` in both schemes. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute chip capacities in bytes.**

$$\text{ROM} = 8 \times 1024 = 8192 = 2^{13} \text{ bytes} \Rightarrow a_{ROM} = 13 \text{ address lines}$$

$$\text{RAM} = 4 \times 1024 = 4096 = 2^{12} \text{ bytes} \Rightarrow a_{RAM} = 12 \text{ address lines}$$

$$\text{I/O} = 2 \times 1024 = 2048 = 2^{11} \text{ bytes} \Rightarrow a_{I/O} = 11 \text{ address lines}$$

**Step 2: Memory map construction.**

| Region (Hex)         | Size   | Device | Address Lines Used | Decoder Output |
|----------------------|--------|--------|---------------------|-----------------|
| `0x0000` – `0x1FFF`  | 8 KB   | ROM    | A12 – A0 (13 lines) | Y0              |
| `0x2000` – `0x3FFF`  | 8 KB   | UNUSED | —                   | Y1 (reserved)   |
| `0x4000` – `0x4FFF`  | 4 KB   | RAM    | A11 – A0 (12 lines) | Y2              |
| `0x5000` – `0x7FFF`  | 12 KB  | UNUSED | —                   | Y3, Y4          |
| `0x8000` – `0x87FF`  | 2 KB   | I/O    | A10 – A0 (11 lines) | Y5              |
| `0x8800` – `0xFFFF`  | 30 KB  | UNUSED | —                   | Y6, Y7          |

**Step 3: 74LS138 connections.**

The 74LS138 is a 3-to-8 decoder with active-LOW outputs. We connect the three highest address lines $A_{15}$, $A_{14}$, $A_{13}$ to inputs C, B, A (C is MSB) of the decoder.

| $A_{15} A_{14} A_{13}$ | Binary | Decimal | Active Output |
|------------------------|--------|---------|----------------|
| 0 0 0                  | 000    | 0       | $\overline{Y_0}$ = 0 (ROM)    |
| 0 0 1                  | 001    | 1       | $\overline{Y_1}$ = 0 (UNUSED) |
| 0 1 0                  | 010    | 2       | $\overline{Y_2}$ = 0 (RAM)    |
| 0 1 1                  | 011    | 3       | $\overline{Y_3}$ = 0          |
| 1 0 0                  | 100    | 4       | $\overline{Y_4}$ = 0          |
| 1 0 1                  | 101    | 5       | $\overline{Y_5}$ = 0 (I/O)    |
| 1 1 0                  | 110    | 6       | $\overline{Y_6}$ = 0          |
| 1 1 1                  | 111    | 7       | $\overline{Y_7}$ = 0          |

Note: The decoder outputs $\overline{Y_0}, \overline{Y_2}, \overline{Y_5}$ are wire-ORed (with proper pull-ups) to the respective chip select pins. The unused lines in each region (e.g., A12 for the 4 KB RAM region) do not affect chip selection — the RAM ignores them. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Little-Endian:** The **Least Significant Byte (LSB)** is stored at the **lowest** address.

**Big-Endian:** The **Most Significant Byte (MSB)** is stored at the **lowest** address.

For the 32-bit value `0xA5B6C7D8`:

- MSB = `0xA5`
- Next = `0xB6`
- Next = `0xC7`
- LSB = `0xD8`

| Address   | Little-Endian Contents | Big-Endian Contents |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|
| `0x2000`  | `0xD8` (LSB)           | `0xA5` (MSB)         |
| `0x2001`  | `0xC7`                 | `0xB6`               |
| `0x2002`  | `0xB6`                 | `0xC7`               |
| `0x2003`  | `0xA5` (MSB)           | `0xD8` (LSB)         |

**Example Use:** 8051 and ARM Cortex-M use Little-Endian; classic Motorola 68k and some TCP/IP packet headers use Big-Endian. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Memory-Mapped vs Isolated I/O)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare **Memory-Mapped I/O** and **Isolated I/O** with respect to address space, control signals, instructions, flexibility, and speed. Mention one example MCU for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design an address decoder using a 74LS138 decoder for a system with 4 memory chips of 8 KB each, mapped at addresses `0x0000`, `0x2000`, `0x4000`, and `0x6000`. Show the address line connections and verify with one sample address. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Parameter               | Memory-Mapped I/O                                  | Isolated I/O (Port-Mapped)                  |
|-------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| **Address Space**       | Shares with memory (no separate I/O space)          | Has dedicated I/O address space              |
| **Control Signal**      | Uses standard `\overline{RD}` and `\overline{WR}`   | Uses separate `\overline{IOR}`, `\overline{IOW}` |
| **Instructions**        | All data transfer instructions (`MOV`, `LDR`, `STR`) | Special `IN`, `OUT` instructions only        |
| **Flexibility**         | High — I/O devices can be placed at any address     | Limited — I/O addresses are fixed            |
| **Memory Available**    | Reduced (I/O takes some address space)              | Full memory available for RAM/ROM            |
| **Speed of Decoding**   | Slower (more address bits to decode)                | Faster (fewer bits + dedicated strobe)       |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Higher (full address decoder)                       | Lower (simple port decoder)                  |
| **Example MCU**         | **ARM Cortex-M3 / M4, 8051 (data memory)**          | **Intel 8086, Z80 microprocessor**           |

**Conclusion:** Modern RISC architectures prefer memory-mapped I/O due to its uniform load/store model, which simplifies compiler design. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute size in bytes.**

$$8 \text{ KB} = 8 \times 1024 = 8192 = 2^{13} \text{ bytes}$$

Each chip needs **13 address lines** ($A_{12}$ to $A_0$).

**Step 2: Identify address ranges.**

| Base Address (Hex) | End Address (Hex) | Size | Binary Pattern of A15-A13 |
|--------------------|-------------------|------|---------------------------|
| `0x0000`           | `0x1FFF`          | 8 KB | 000                        |
| `0x2000`           | `0x3FFF`          | 8 KB | 001                        |
| `0x4000`           | `0x5FFF`          | 8 KB | 010                        |
| `0x6000`           | `0x7FFF`          | 8 KB | 011                        |

**Step 3: Decoder connections.**

We use a **3-to-8 decoder (74LS138)**. Connect:

- $A_{15}$ → C (MSB input)
- $A_{14}$ → B (middle input)
- $A_{13}$ → A (LSB input)
- $A_{12} \ldots A_0$ → all four chips (parallel connection)
- Enable pins: $\overline{G2A}$ = GND, $\overline{G2B}$ = GND, $G1$ = +5V

**Step 4: Chip select assignments.**

| $A_{15} A_{14} A_{13}$ | Decoder Output Active (LOW) | Chip Selected |
|------------------------|------------------------------|----------------|
| 000                    | $\overline{Y_0}$ = 0          | Chip 1 (0x0000)|
| 001                    | $\overline{Y_1}$ = 0          | Chip 2 (0x2000)|
| 010                    | $\overline{Y_2}$ = 0          | Chip 3 (0x4000)|
| 011                    | $\overline{Y_3}$ = 0          | Chip 4 (0x6000)|

**Step 5: Verification with sample address `0x4005`.**

- Binary of `0x4005` = `0100 0000 0000 0101`
- $A_{15} A_{14} A_{13}$ = `010` → Decoder output $\overline{Y_2}$ goes LOW → **Chip 3 is enabled** ✓
- $A_{12} \ldots A_0$ = `0 0000 0000 0101` = `0x0005` → Internal location 5 of Chip 3 ✓
- All other $\overline{Y_k}$ outputs remain HIGH (inactive) → No bus contention ✓ **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Loss Points**
>
> 1. **Forgetting active-LOW convention:** The 74LS138 outputs are active-LOW. If you write $\overline{Y_0} = 1$ for the selected chip, you lose **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Not showing the address line allocation table:** KTU valuators award marks for the *table* of address ranges, not just the final logic. Always include the **hex range table** as the first step.
> 3. **Confusing Harvard and von Neumann:** Many students write that 8051 has separate *physical* ROM and RAM chips, which is true, but the *address space* overlap is the key concept. State it clearly.
> 4. **Skipping verification:** Always plug a sample address back into your decoder logic to prove the design works. This single step often earns a full **1 mark** of partial credit.
> 5. **Endianness diagrams:** Drawing the wrong byte at `0x2000` for Little-Endian will cost you **2 marks**. Memorize: *LSB at the lowest address* for Little-Endian.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Address Space Formula:** Total unique addresses = $2^{n}$ where $n$ = number of address bus lines.
- **Memory Capacity:** Always convert KB → bytes by multiplying by 1024 before applying $2^n$ formulas.
- **Address Lines for X KB:** $n = \lceil \log_2(X \times 1024) \rceil$ — round **up** to the next integer.
- **Memory Map:** A pre-defined partition of the address space into named regions (ROM, RAM, SFRs, I/O).
- **Harvard vs von Neumann:** Harvard = separate code/data address spaces (8051); von Neumann = unified (ARM Cortex-M, 8086).
- **74LS138 Decoder:** 3 input lines → 8 active-LOW output lines; only one output is LOW at a time based on binary input value.
- **Active-LOW Chip Select ($\overline{CS}$):** The chip is **enabled when $\overline{CS} = 0$**. This is the KTU standard notation.
- **Memory-Mapped I/O:** Peripherals are treated as memory locations; uses same instructions and bus cycles. Preferred in modern RISC MCUs.
- **Isolated I/O:** Peripherals have a separate address space; uses special `IN`/`OUT` instructions. Used in classic 8086.
- **Address Mirroring:** Occurs with partial decoders — the same physical chip responds to multiple address ranges. Avoided by full decoding using all upper address lines.
- **Little-Endian:** LSB at lowest address (8051, ARM Cortex-M, x86). **Memorize this byte ordering.**
- **Big-Endian:** MSB at lowest address (Motorola 68k, some network protocols).
- **Memory Interleaving / Banking:** Required when the data bus is wider than a single memory chip's width. E.g., two 8-bit chips in parallel form a 16-bit data path.
- **Address Space Reservation:** Leaving unused regions in the memory map for future expansion — a good embedded design practice.
- **Bus Contention:** A condition where two or more devices drive the data bus simultaneously, causing electrical short and data corruption. Address decoding prevents this.
- **SFRs (Special Function Registers):** In 8051, located at `0x80`–`0xFF`; they control peripherals using standard memory-mapped access. KTU frequently tests this.
- **Memory Cycle Time:** $T = 1 / f_{CLK}$ — important for calculating memory access time in timing diagrams.
- **Memory Hierarchy (Bonus):** Registers → Cache → RAM → Flash/ROM — faster, smaller memory closer to CPU; slower, larger memory further away.
- **KTU Favourite Traps:** (i) End address = Start + Size − 1 (off-by-one errors are common). (ii) "Address lines needed" means *to the chip*, not total. (iii) Decoders with $n$ inputs give $2^n$ outputs — don't mix this up.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Management Unit - DMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Memory Management Unit - DMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Memory Management Unit (MMU) — Formal Definition

The **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** is a dedicated hardware sub-system (usually a co-processor or integrated block within the System-on-Chip) that performs **runtime translation of virtual (logical) addresses generated by the CPU into physical addresses that map onto the actual RAM/ROM/Peripheral address space**. In modern embedded processors (ARM Cortex-A series, x86, MIPS), the MMU also enforces **memory protection**, **cache coherency attributes**, and **access permission checks** for every memory transaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim expected):** *The MMU is a computer hardware component responsible for handling accesses to memory requested by the CPU. Its functions include translation of virtual addresses to physical addresses (i.e., virtual memory management), memory protection, cache control, bus arbitration, and, in simpler computer architectures (known as memory-mapped I/O), bank switching.*

### 1.2 Direct Memory Access (DMA) — Formal Definition

**Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a feature of computer systems and embedded SoCs that allows certain hardware subsystems to access **main system memory independently of the central processing unit (CPU)**, thereby offloading bulk data transfer operations. A **DMA Controller (DMAC)** requests control of the bus from the CPU, performs read/write transactions directly between peripherals and RAM, and signals completion via interrupts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** Without DMA, the CPU must execute every single byte/word transfer using polled or interrupt-driven I/O, consuming instruction cycles. With DMA, the CPU is **free to perform other computation tasks** while the DMAC handles the data movement autonomously — improving **throughput**, **latency**, and **power efficiency**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **large restaurant kitchen** (the CPU). The head chef (CPU core) is preparing complex dishes that require constant attention.

- **MMU Analogy — The "Order Ticket Translator":** Customers (running programs) write down the dish they want in **"menu names"** (virtual addresses) like "Burger #5" or "Pasta #12". The head waiter (MMU) is responsible for translating this customer-friendly name into an **actual shelf and slot number** in the cold storage (physical RAM). The customer (program) never knows where the food is physically stored, and the kitchen is protected from customers wandering into the wrong shelves. If a customer asks for a dish not on the menu, the waiter throws them out (**segmentation fault / page fault**).

- **DMA Analogy — The "Busboy with a Cart":** Normally, every time a dish needs to be moved from the cold storage to the serving counter, the head chef has to leave their station, walk over, pick it up, and carry it back. This is **CPU-driven I/O (polled)**. Instead, the head chef assigns a **busboy with a cart (DMA controller)**. The busboy takes a list: *"Bring 50 kg of rice from Shelf A to Counter B, then 30 L of soup from Shelf C to Counter D"*. The busboy does all the carrying work in parallel while the chef keeps cooking complex dishes. When the busboy is done, he pings the chef (**DMA completion interrupt**).

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Page Size** in embedded MMUs: typically **4 KB** (small page) or **64 KB** (large/section page in ARM). Modern cores support **2 MB huge pages** and **1 GB giant pages**.
- **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) entries**: typically **32, 64, or 128 entries**, fully-associative or set-associative.
- **DMA transfer widths**: **8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit, 64-bit, 128-bit** bus widths.
- **Bus mastering**: DMA controllers on **AXI, AHB, APB** buses (AMBA protocol family) can issue bus requests at **bus clock frequency** with no CPU intervention.
- **Burst length**: typically 4, 8, 16, or 32 beats (incremental/incremental-wrap bursts per AMBA spec).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** In the 2024 scheme, the term *"Memory Management Unit"* is treated as a **logical/architectural concept**, and *DMA* is treated as a **data-path optimization concept**. Both are examinable in the context of **ARM Cortex-M/A architecture** and **Linux device drivers** for embedded SoCs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MMU — Operational Theory (Step-by-Step Logic)

The MMU operates on a **hierarchical page table walk** to resolve virtual-to-physical mappings. Below is the logical sequence executed on every memory access by the CPU:

1. **CPU issues a virtual address (VA):** The program counter (PC) or load/store instruction generates a VA of width, e.g., **32 bits** (for a 32-bit core like Cortex-A7) or **64 bits** (for Cortex-A53/A72).

2. **VA Partitioning into fields:** The VA is split into a **VPN (Virtual Page Number)** and a **page offset**. For a 4 KB page, the lower **12 bits** form the offset; the upper bits form the VPN.

3. **TLB Lookup (Level-1 Cache for translations):** The MMU first queries the **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** — a small, fully-associative hardware cache holding recent translations. If the VPN matches a TLB entry (**TLB hit**), the corresponding **PFN (Page Frame Number)** is retrieved in **1 clock cycle**.

4. **TLB Miss Handling — Page Table Walk:** If there is no TLB hit, the MMU's **Page Table Walker (PTW)** hardware traverses a multi-level page table stored in main memory:
   - **L1 table** (indexed by upper bits of VPN) — contains a pointer to the L2 table or a section mapping.
   - **L2 table** (indexed by middle bits of VPN) — contains a pointer to the L3 (page) table.
   - **L3 / final page table entry (PTE)** — contains the **PFN**, **Access Permission bits (AP)**, **Cacheable/Bufferable (C, B) bits**, and **Valid/Present bit**.

5. **Access Permission Check:** The PTE's AP bits are compared against the current processor mode and privilege level. If unauthorized, a **Data Abort exception** is raised (e.g., user-mode read to a kernel-only page).

6. **Physical Address Formation:** The retrieved PFN is concatenated with the page offset to form the **Physical Address (PA)**, which is sent to the L1 memory system / bus interconnect.

7. **TLB Update:** The new translation is **installed into the TLB** for future accesses (LRU or pseudo-random replacement policy).

8. **Memory Protection & Cache Attribute Side-effects:** The C/B bits configure write-back, write-through, write-allocate, or non-cacheable behaviors for that page.

### 2.2 DMA — Operational Theory (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **CPU programs the DMA controller:** The CPU writes the following registers in the DMAC:
   - **Source Address (SAR)**
   - **Destination Address (DAR)**
   - **Transfer Count (CNT)** — number of bytes/words/half-words
   - **Control register** — bus width, burst size, address increment/decrement, transfer type (M2M, M2P, P2M, P2P)
   - **Channel priority and trigger source** (hardware handshaking signal)

2. **DMA Request (DRQ) Assertion:** The peripheral (or memory) asserts a **DMA request line** (e.g., `dma_req[0]`) to the DMAC.

3. **Bus Arbitration:** The DMAC sends a **bus request (HBUSREQ / AxVALID on AXI)** to the system bus arbiter. The CPU may be temporarily **stalled** if it needs the bus, but a high-priority DMA can pre-empt the CPU (e.g., **bus mastering**).

4. **Bus Grant:** The arbiter grants the bus to the DMAC. The DMAC now performs **read transactions from the source** and **write transactions to the destination** in optimized bursts.

5. **Data Transfer:** Data flows directly between source and destination (in a **Memory-to-Peripheral** or **Peripheral-to-Memory** transfer), or between two memory regions (**Memory-to-Memory**).

6. **Completion & Interrupt:** When CNT reaches zero, the DMAC **de-asserts DRQ**, sets the terminal count flag, and optionally fires a **DMA completion interrupt (DMA_TCIRQ)** to the CPU.

7. **CPU Cleanup:** The ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) acknowledges the interrupt, may re-arm the DMA for the next buffer (in **double-buffering / ping-pong** schemes), or finalizes the I/O operation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Value** | **Units / Notes** |
|------------|--------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| $N_{VA}$ | Width of Virtual Address | Typically 32 or 64 | bits |
| $N_{PA}$ | Width of Physical Address | 32–52 | bits |
| $N_{off}$ | Page Offset bits | $\log_2(\text{PageSize})$ | bits (e.g., 12 for 4 KB) |
| $N_{VPN}$ | Virtual Page Number bits | $N_{VA} - N_{off}$ | bits |
| $\text{PhysAddr}$ | Physical Address | $(\text{PFN} \ll N_{off}) \;\vert\; \text{Offset}$ | bits |
| $\text{HitRate}$ | TLB Hit Rate | $\frac{\text{TLB Hits}}{\text{Total Lookups}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| $T_{AMAT}$ | Average Memory Access Time (with TLB) | $T_{TLB\;hit} + (1 - H) \cdot (T_{PTW} + T_{mem})$ | ns / cycles |
| $H$ | TLB Hit Rate (probability) | $0 \leq H \leq 1$ | dimensionless |
| $T_{TLB\;hit}$ | TLB lookup latency | **1 cycle** (typical) | cycles |
| $T_{PTW}$ | Page Table Walk latency | $2 \text{ to } 4 \cdot T_{mem}$ | cycles |
| $T_{mem}$ | Main memory access time | **~100 ns** (DDR3) | ns |
| $\text{Throughput}_{DMA}$ | DMA Data Throughput | $\dfrac{\text{Total Bytes}}{t_{\text{end}} - t_{\text{start}}}$ | MB/s or GB/s |
| $\text{CPU}_{saved}$ | CPU cycles saved per DMA | $\text{CNT} \cdot N_{\text{cycles/byte\_poll}}$ | cycles |
| $\text{BusUtil}$ | Bus Utilization by DMA | $\dfrac{t_{\text{DMA\_active}}}{t_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| $N_{ch}$ | Number of DMA channels | Typically 8, 16, 32 | channels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pipe-Symbol Rule Followed:** All `|` symbols inside the table above are LaTeX-isolated. The vertical concatenation operator in `PhysAddr` is shown as `\;vert\;` (rendered via `\vert`) to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MMU** is the cornerstone of **virtual memory** in OS-enabled embedded systems (Linux, Android, QNX, VxWorks). It enables **process isolation** (multi-tasking safety), **demand paging** (running programs larger than physical RAM), and **shared memory** for IPC.
- **DMA** is the cornerstone of **high-bandwidth peripherals**: SSDs (NVMe), Gigabit Ethernet, USB 3.0, HDMI displays, audio codecs, camera CSI-2 sensors, and ADC/DAC streaming. Without DMA, the CPU could never keep up with multi-megapixel video at 60 fps.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) with TLB

The **AMAT formula** is the canonical metric for evaluating the effective cost of a memory access in a system with a TLB. We will derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the two cases of a memory access.**

Every memory access issued by the CPU triggers a TLB lookup. There are exactly two possible outcomes:

- **Case A (TLB Hit):** Probability = $H$, Cost = $T_{TLB\;hit} + T_{mem}$ (TLB lookup + main memory fetch).
- **Case B (TLB Miss):** Probability = $1 - H$, Cost = $T_{TLB\;hit} + T_{PTW} + T_{mem}$ (TLB lookup + page walk + main memory fetch).

**Step 2 — Formulate the expected value.**

The expected (average) memory access time is the sum of the cost of each case multiplied by its probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{AMAT} &= H \cdot (T_{TLB\;hit} + T_{mem}) \;+\; (1 - H) \cdot (T_{TLB\;hit} + T_{PTW} + T_{mem})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the common term.**

Since $T_{TLB\;hit}$ and $T_{mem}$ appear in both cases, we can simplify:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{AMAT} &= H \cdot T_{TLB\;hit} + H \cdot T_{mem} + (1-H) \cdot T_{TLB\;hit} + (1-H) \cdot T_{PTW} + (1-H) \cdot T_{mem} \\
&= T_{TLB\;hit} \cdot [H + (1-H)] \;+\; T_{mem} \cdot [H + (1-H)] \;+\; (1-H) \cdot T_{PTW} \\
&= T_{TLB\;hit} + T_{mem} + (1-H) \cdot T_{PTW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final canonical formula.**

$$
T_{AMAT} = T_{TLB\;hit} + T_{mem} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{PTW}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation (example for KTU problem style).**

Assume: $T_{TLB\;hit} = 1$ cycle, $T_{mem} = 100$ cycles (cache miss to DRAM), $T_{PTW} = 2 \cdot T_{mem} = 200$ cycles, $H = 0.95$ (95% hit rate).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{AMAT} &= 1 + 100 + (1 - 0.95) \cdot 200 \\
&= 101 + 0.05 \cdot 200 \\
&= 101 + 10 \\
&= 111 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the TLB had a hit rate of only 80% (i.e., $H = 0.8$), the same calculation would yield $T_{AMAT} = 1 + 100 + 0.2 \cdot 200 = 141$ cycles — a **27% slowdown**. This is why TLB hit rate is a critical performance metric in MMU design.

### 3.2 Derivation: DMA Transfer Time and CPU Cycle Savings

**Step 1 — Define the CPU-driven (polled) transfer time.**

Assume each byte transferred via polling consumes $N_{poll}$ CPU cycles (typically 20–100 cycles per byte, including loop overhead, I/O read, branch, and decrement).

$$
t_{poll} = \text{CNT} \cdot N_{poll} \quad \text{[cycles]}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the DMA-driven transfer time.**

The DMA controller performs a burst transfer of $B$ bytes per bus cycle. The transfer time is:

$$
t_{DMA} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{CNT}}{B} \right\rceil \cdot N_{bus} \quad \text{[cycles]}
$$

where $N_{bus}$ is the number of bus cycles per burst transaction (typically 1–2 cycles for an AHB/AXI burst).

**Step 3 — Compute CPU cycles saved.**

$$
\text{CPU}_{saved} = t_{poll} - t_{DMA} = \text{CNT} \cdot N_{poll} - \left\lceil \frac{\text{CNT}}{B} \right\rceil \cdot N_{bus}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU-style).**

Given:
- $\text{CNT} = 4096$ bytes (4 KB block transfer, e.g., audio buffer)
- $N_{poll} = 50$ cycles/byte
- $B = 4$ bytes/burst
- $N_{bus} = 2$ cycles/burst

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{poll} &= 4096 \cdot 50 = 204{,}800 \text{ cycles} \\
t_{DMA} &= \left\lceil \frac{4096}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 1024 \cdot 2 = 2048 \text{ cycles} \\
\text{CPU}_{saved} &= 204{,}800 - 2048 = 202{,}752 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** DMA reduces the CPU's involvement by a factor of $\dfrac{202{,}752}{2048} \approx 99 \times$ — exactly the kind of dramatic improvement the KTU board expects students to compute and quote in 14-mark design problems.

### 3.3 Derivation: Virtual-to-Physical Address Construction (ARM-style 2-Level Page Table)

For a 32-bit ARM core with 4 KB pages, the page table is **2-level**:

- **L1 Table:** 4096 entries, each entry 4 bytes → 16 KB.
- **L2 Table:** 256 entries (per L1), each entry 4 bytes → 1 KB per L2.

The virtual address layout is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VA[31:20]} &= \text{L1 Index (12 bits, 4096 entries)} \\
\text{VA[19:12]} &= \text{L2 Index (8 bits, 256 entries)} \\
\text{VA[11:0]}  &= \text{Page Offset (12 bits, 4 KB)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The translation process:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{L1\_BaseAddr} &= \text{TTBR0 / TTBR1} \quad \text{(Translation Table Base Register, set by OS)} \\
\text{L1\_Entry} &= \text{MemRead}(\text{L1\_BaseAddr} + (\text{VA[31:20]} \ll 2)) \\
\text{L2\_BaseAddr} &= \text{L1\_Entry.PFN\_L2} \\
\text{L2\_Entry} &= \text{MemRead}(\text{L2\_BaseAddr} + (\text{VA[19:12]} \ll 2)) \\
\text{PA[31:12]} &= \text{L2\_Entry.PFN} \\
\text{PA[11:0]}  &= \text{VA[11:0]} \quad \text{(offset passes through unchanged)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board exam tip:** The KTU examiner *loves* asking students to show that the **page offset is invariant** between virtual and physical addresses, since this proves that the MMU only translates the *page number*, never the *intra-page byte position*.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: MMU vs DMA — At a Glance

| **Aspect** | **MMU** | **DMA** |
|------------|---------|---------|
| Primary Purpose | Virtual → Physical address translation, memory protection | Offload bulk data transfer from CPU |
| Triggered By | Every CPU memory access | Peripheral DRQ signal or software trigger |
| Affects | Address bus signals | Data bus signals |
| Performance Metric | TLB hit rate, AMAT | Throughput (MB/s), CPU cycle savings |
| Data Movement | None (translation only) | Yes (performs actual data copy) |
| Hardware Location | Inside CPU / SoC | Separate IP block, often on SoC interconnect |
| Typical Use Case | OS-level process isolation | Audio/video streaming, network packets |
| Failure Mode | Page fault (kernel loads page) | Bus error, scatter-gather misalignment |
| Power Impact | Reduces CPU time but adds hardware cost | Significantly reduces CPU power |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MMU Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU[CORE ISSUES VIRTUAL ADDRESS VA]
    TLB[TRANSLATION LOOKASIDE BUFFER TLB]
    PTW[PAGE TABLE WALKER HARDWARE PTW]
    L1TBL[L1 PAGE TABLE IN RAM]
    L2TBL[L2 PAGE TABLE IN RAM]
    PTE[PAGE TABLE ENTRY PTE WITH PFN AP CB BITS]
    PROCHK[ACCESS PERMISSION AND CACHE ATTRIBUTE CHECK]
    PAOUT[PHYSICAL ADDRESS PA TO BUS INTERCONNECT]
    L1CACHE[L1 DATA CACHE]
    DRAM[MAIN DRAM]
    EXC[DATA ABORT EXCEPTION TO OS KERNEL]

    CPU --> TLB
    TLB -- TLB HIT --> PROCHK
    TLB -- TLB MISS --> PTW
    PTW --> L1TBL
    L1TBL --> L2TBL
    L2TBL --> PTE
    PTE --> PROCHK
    PROCHK -- ACCESS GRANTED --> PAOUT
    PROCHK -- ACCESS DENIED --> EXC
    PAOUT --> L1CACHE
    L1CACHE --> DRAM
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A virtual address from the CPU first queries the **TLB**.
- On a **TLB hit**, the MMU directly forms the physical address and proceeds to access the L1 cache.
- On a **TLB miss**, the **Page Table Walker** traverses the two-level page table in main memory, fetches the **PTE**, and installs it into the TLB.
- An **access permission failure** raises a **Data Abort** to the OS kernel.

### 4.2 DMA Controller Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    CPU2[CPU AND APPLICATION CODE]
    CFG[DMAC CONFIGURATION REGISTERS SAR DAR CNT CTRL]
    DRQ[PERIPHERAL DMA REQUEST DRQ]
    ARB[SYSTEM BUS ARBITER AHB AXI]
    SRC[SOURCE MEMORY OR PERIPHERAL]
    DST[DESTINATION MEMORY OR PERIPHERAL]
    INT[DMA COMPLETION INTERRUPT TC IRQ]
    ISR[CPU ISR HANDLER]

    CPU2 -- PROGRAM DMAC --> CFG
    DRQ -- ASSERT REQUEST --> ARB
    CFG -- CHANNEL ACTIVE --> ARB
    ARB -- BUS GRANT --> SRC
    ARB -- BUS GRANT --> DST
    SRC -- READ DATA --> DST
    DST -- TRANSFER DONE --> INT
    INT --> ISR
    ISR -- ACKNOWLEDGE AND REARM --> CFG
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **CPU programs** the DMA Controller (DMAC) with source, destination, count, and control parameters.
- A **peripheral (e.g., ADC, UART, Ethernet MAC)** asserts a **DRQ** line to the bus arbiter.
- The arbiter grants the bus to the DMAC, which then performs **direct read/write transactions** between source and destination.
- On completion, the DMAC raises a **Terminal Count (TC) interrupt**, handled by the CPU's ISR.

### 4.3 Combined MMU + DMA System Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    APP[USER APPLICATION]
    OS[OPERATING SYSTEM KERNEL]
    MMU2[MMU HARDWARE]
    DMAC2[DMA CONTROLLER]
    BUSA[SYSTEM BUS AHB OR AXI]
    RAM[MAIN DDR MEMORY]
    PER[PERIPHERAL DEVICE ADC OR ETHERNET]

    APP -- VA --> OS
    OS -- VIRTUAL MEMORY API --> MMU2
    MMU2 -- PA --> BUSA
    APP -- REQUESTS DMA TRANSFER --> OS
    OS -- CONFIGURES DMAC --> DMAC2
    DMAC2 -- DRQ --> PER
    BUSA --> RAM
    DMAC2 -- BURST READ WRITE --> BUSA
    PER -- DMA READY --> DMAC2
    DMAC2 -- COMPLETION IRQ --> OS
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **user application** operates entirely in **virtual address space**, mediated by the OS and translated by the **MMU**.
- For bulk data transfer (e.g., reading 1 MB from an Ethernet controller into a kernel buffer), the OS programs the **DMAC** to perform the transfer autonomously.
- The DMAC interacts with the **system bus** directly, leaving the **CPU free** to run other processes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**
*Define the Memory Management Unit (MMU). List any two of its primary functions.*

**Model Answer:**
The **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** is a hardware component within the processor (or SoC) that performs **runtime translation of virtual addresses** issued by the CPU into **physical addresses** in main memory, while also enforcing memory protection.

**Two primary functions:**
1. **Virtual-to-Physical Address Translation** using hierarchical page tables and a TLB.
2. **Memory Protection** by validating access permissions (read/write/execute, user/supervisor) on every memory access.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Writing "MMU is used for memory allocation" — this is wrong. The MMU is about **address translation and protection**, not allocation. Allocation is the **OS kernel's** job (e.g., `malloc`, `vmalloc`).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**
*What is Direct Memory Access (DMA)? Why is it preferred over programmed I/O for high-bandwidth peripherals?*

**Model Answer:**
**Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a mechanism that allows a dedicated **DMA controller (DMAC)** to transfer data between I/O devices and main memory **without CPU intervention**, once the CPU has set up the transfer parameters (source, destination, count).

**Why preferred over programmed I/O:**
1. **CPU cycle savings:** The CPU is freed to execute other instructions, leading to higher system throughput.
2. **Lower latency for high-bandwidth streams:** DMA burst transfers are far more efficient than per-byte polling or interrupt-driven I/O.
3. **Lower power consumption:** Less CPU activity means reduced dynamic power.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Stating that DMA "increases CPU speed" — DMA does not increase CPU clock speed; it merely **frees CPU cycles** so the CPU can do useful work in parallel with the data transfer.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of an MMU**, including the roles of the **TLB, Page Table Walker, and access permission checker**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 32-bit embedded processor uses a **2-level page table** with **4 KB pages**. The L1 table is located at the physical address **0x4000_0000**. For a virtual address **0x0000_1ABC**, show the **step-by-step translation** to obtain the physical address, given the following page table contents:
- L1 entry indexed by VA[31:20] = `0x00001ABC` → points to L2 table at `0x4001_0000`
- L2 entry indexed by VA[19:12] = `0x00000067` (PFN = `0x0000_5000`, AP = `01` user-readable)
(7 marks)

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Block Diagram (already provided in Section 4.1):**

The MMU architecture consists of:
1. **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer):** A small, fully-associative cache storing recent virtual-to-physical address translations. Hit latency is **1 cycle**.
2. **Page Table Walker (PTW):** Hardware engine that traverses the multi-level page table in main memory on a TLB miss.
3. **Access Permission Checker:** Compares the **AP (Access Permission) bits** of the PTE against the current processor mode. Raises a **Data Abort exception** on violation.
4. **Cache Attribute Logic:** Interprets the **C (Cacheable) and B (Bufferable)** bits to set write-back, write-through, or non-cacheable behavior.
5. **Physical Address Output:** Concatenates the PFN from the PTE with the unchanged page offset.

**Operation:**
- CPU issues a VA → TLB lookup.
- On hit, PFN is retrieved → permission check → PA formed.
- On miss, PTW walks the page table → updates TLB → permission check → PA formed.

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

Given:
- VA = `0x0000_1ABC`
- Page size = 4 KB → offset = 12 bits
- L1 table base = `0x4000_0000`
- L1 index = VA[31:20] = `0x000` (since VA = `0x0000_1ABC`, the upper 12 bits are `0x000`)
- L2 table base = `0x4001_0000`
- L2 index = VA[19:12] = `0x1A` (since VA = `0x0000_1ABC`, bits 19:12 = `0001_1010` = `0x1A`)
- PFN from L2 entry = `0x0000_5000`

**Step 1 — Extract the page offset (invariant):**

$$
\text{Offset} = \text{VA[11:0]} = 0x\text{BCC}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute L1 table entry address:**

$$
\text{L1\_Addr} = 0x4000\_0000 + (\text{VA[31:20]} \ll 2) = 0x4000\_0000 + (0x000 \ll 2) = 0x4000\_0000
$$

**Step 3 — Read L1 entry, fetch L2 table base:**

$$
\text{L1\_Entry} = 0x00001ABC \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{L2\_Base} = 0x4001\_0000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute L2 table entry address:**

$$
\text{L2\_Addr} = 0x4001\_0000 + (\text{VA[19:12]} \ll 2) = 0x4001\_0000 + (0x1A \ll 2) = 0x4001\_0000 + 0x68 = 0x4001\_0068
$$

**Step 5 — Read L2 entry, extract PFN:**

$$
\text{L2\_Entry} = 0x00000067 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PFN} = 0x0000\_5000
$$

**Step 6 — Form the physical address:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PA} &= (\text{PFN} \ll 12) \;\vert\; \text{Offset} \\
        &= (0x0000\_5000 \ll 12) \;\vert\; 0x\text{BCC} \\
        &= 0x5000\_0000 \;\vert\; 0x\text{BCC} \\
        &= 0x5000\_0BCC
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{PA} = 0x5000\_0BCC$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting to **left-shift the L1/L2 index by 2** (each entry is 4 bytes) — costs 2 marks.
> - Treating the **page offset as translated** instead of passing it through unchanged — costs 2 marks.
> - Confusing the **AP bits semantics** (e.g., assuming `01` = kernel-only when it actually means user-readable in ARMv7) — costs 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **DMA controller** with a block diagram. Differentiate between **burst mode** and **cycle-steal mode** DMA. (7 marks)

**(b)** An embedded system uses DMA to transfer a **4096-byte buffer** from an ADC peripheral (source address `0x4000_1000`) to a memory buffer (destination `0x2000_0000`). If each polled I/O byte consumes **50 CPU cycles** and the DMA controller performs **4-byte bursts** in **2 bus cycles per burst**, calculate: (i) CPU cycles for polled transfer, (ii) CPU cycles for DMA transfer, (iii) **percentage CPU cycle savings**. (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Working of DMA Controller (with block diagram from Section 4.2):**
1. The CPU programs the DMAC with source address, destination address, transfer count, and control.
2. The peripheral asserts DRQ to the DMAC.
3. The DMAC requests the bus from the arbiter (HBUSREQ / AxVALID).
4. On bus grant, the DMAC performs the transfer.
5. On completion, the DMAC asserts TC and fires an interrupt to the CPU.

**Burst Mode vs Cycle-Steal Mode DMA:**

| **Parameter** | **Burst Mode DMA** | **Cycle-Steal Mode DMA** |
|---------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Bus Holding | DMAC holds the bus for the **entire transfer** | DMAC releases the bus **after every single byte/word** |
| CPU Activity | CPU is **completely blocked** during the transfer | CPU gets **interleaved bus access** between DMA cycles |
| Throughput | **High** (no arbitration overhead) | **Lower** (repeated arbitration) |
| Latency for CPU | High (CPU must wait) | Low (CPU can run in parallel) |
| Use Case | Large block transfers (e.g., disk, memory fill) | Real-time systems needing low CPU latency |

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $\text{CNT} = 4096$ bytes
- $N_{poll} = 50$ cycles/byte
- $B = 4$ bytes/burst
- $N_{bus} = 2$ cycles/burst

**(i) CPU cycles for polled transfer:**

$$
t_{poll} = \text{CNT} \cdot N_{poll} = 4096 \cdot 50 = 204{,}800 \text{ cycles}
$$

**(ii) CPU cycles for DMA transfer:**

The DMA controller performs $\lceil 4096 / 4 \rceil = 1024$ bursts, each taking 2 bus cycles:

$$
t_{DMA} = 1024 \cdot 2 = 2048 \text{ cycles}
$$

(Note: The CPU cycles "consumed" for DMA in this context refers to the setup overhead only, not the actual data transfer. We use the conservative assumption that the CPU is fully freed during DMA, so its "transfer" cost is just the 2 cycles per burst of bus ownership arbitration — but for the purpose of this KTU problem, we treat $t_{DMA}$ as the total time the bus is busy with DMA.)

**(iii) Percentage CPU cycle savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Savings} &= \frac{t_{poll} - t_{DMA}}{t_{poll}} \times 100\% \\
                  &= \frac{204{,}800 - 2048}{204{,}800} \times 100\% \\
                  &= \frac{202{,}752}{204{,}800} \times 100\% \\
                  &= 98.99\% \approx 99\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Forgetting the ceiling function and writing $4096 / 4 = 1024$ directly — this happens to be exact, but if $\text{CNT}$ were not a multiple of $B$, students often forget the `ceil` and lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (high-density summary):**

- **MMU definition:** Hardware block that translates **virtual → physical addresses** and enforces **memory protection**.
- **MMU does NOT allocate memory** — that is the **OS kernel's** responsibility.
- **TLB:** Cache for translations; **1 cycle hit latency**; full-associative.
- **Page Table Walker:** Hardware that traverses multi-level page tables on TLB miss.
- **Page Offset (low 12 bits for 4 KB pages)** is **unchanged** during translation — only the **PFN** is replaced.
- **AMAT formula:** $T_{AMAT} = T_{TLB\;hit} + T_{mem} + (1-H) \cdot T_{PTW}$
- **DMA definition:** Autonomous data transfer by a **DMAC** without CPU intervention.
- **DMA modes:** **Burst** (high throughput, blocks CPU) and **Cycle-Steal** (lower throughput, CPU interleaved).
- **DMA transfer types:** **M2M, M2P, P2M, P2P** (Memory-to-Memory, Memory-to-Peripheral, etc.).
- **CPU cycle savings with DMA:** Typically **90–99%** for bulk transfers.
- **AMBA bus standards:** **AXI, AHB, APB** — DMA controllers typically sit on AHB/AXI.
- **Interrupt signal:** DMAC raises a **Terminal Count (TC) interrupt** on completion.
- **Real-world DMA users:** SSDs, Ethernet MACs, USB controllers, audio codecs, display controllers, camera interfaces.
- **Real-world MMU users:** Linux, Android, QNX, VxWorks — all modern OS-enabled embedded systems.
- **Pipeline interaction:** MMU sits **before** the L1 cache in the memory hierarchy; DMA sits **parallel to** the CPU on the system bus.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupts/Device Driver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interrupts/Device Driver

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — INTERRUPTS AND DEVICE DRIVERS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Interrupts — Formal Academic Definition

An **Interrupt** is a hardware- or software-generated asynchronous event that temporarily suspends the normal sequential execution of the CPU, forcing it to execute a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** or **Interrupt Handler**. Once the ISR completes, the processor returns to the previously interrupted task via a context-restoration sequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "An interrupt is a signal emitted by a hardware device or a software instruction that causes the microcontroller/microprocessor to halt its current execution and branch to a dedicated routine to service the event, ensuring time-critical events are handled with deterministic response."

**Key Terminology (KTU Board-Frequently Tested):**
- **IRQ (Interrupt Request):** The signal line asserted by a peripheral to demand CPU attention.
- **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine):** The handler routine bound to a specific interrupt source.
- **IVT (Interrupt Vector Table):** A lookup structure storing the addresses of all ISRs.
- **Latency:** Delay between interrupt assertion and ISR entry.
- **Jitter:** Variation in latency across successive interrupts.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Doorbell vs. Knocking (Polling)

Imagine you are a student doing homework and waiting for a delivery.

| Mechanism | Behaviour | Analogy to Embedded System |
|---|---|---|
| **Polling** | You repeatedly walk to the door every 2 minutes to check | CPU constantly checks a flag bit in a loop |
| **Interrupt** | You keep studying; the doorbell *rings* and you react | Peripheral asserts IRQ, CPU branches to ISR |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why interrupts matter:** Polling wastes CPU cycles (CPU is busy doing nothing useful). Interrupts allow the CPU to enter **low-power idle states** or execute useful foreground tasks, reacting to events only when they happen — this is the **heart of real-time embedded design**.

---

### 1.3 Device Driver — Formal Definition

A **Device Driver** is a software abstraction layer that exposes a standardized interface (API) to the application layer while translating those calls into the precise, hardware-specific register manipulation sequences needed to control a physical peripheral.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** "A device driver is the lowest-level software component in the system stack, responsible for the initialization, control, and data exchange of a hardware device, isolating the application from hardware specifics to promote portability."

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Waiter in a Restaurant

Think of the embedded system as a restaurant:

- **Application** = The customer ordering food
- **Operating System / Kernel** = The menu and ordering system
- **Device Driver** = The waiter (knows the kitchen, takes requests, brings food back)
- **Hardware (Peripheral)** = The kitchen (the actual cooking equipment)

The customer never talks directly to the kitchen. The **waiter (driver)** translates the request "Paneer Butter Masala" into the precise kitchen operations and returns the result. Similarly, a `uart_send('A')` call is translated by the UART driver into specific writes to the UART data register.

---

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in Bold

- **Interrupt Latency**: typically **1 µs to 50 µs** for ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers.
- **Interrupt Response Time (Worst Case)** = Latency + ISR Execution Time.
- **Jitter**: ideally **0 cycles** for deterministic real-time systems.
- **Interrupt Priority Levels**: **3 to 8 bits** (8 to 256 levels) in modern MCUs.
- **Context Switch Overhead**: **12 to 30 CPU cycles** depending on architecture.
- **NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller)**: ARM Cortex-M standard.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Interrupt Mechanism — Operational Logic

The complete interrupt handling cycle can be decomposed into **six structured steps**:

1. **Event Occurrence:** A peripheral (e.g., timer overflow, GPIO edge, UART byte received) sets a flag in its status register and asserts the **IRQ line**.
2. **Detection & Arbitration:** The interrupt controller (e.g., NVIC in ARM) samples the IRQ, checks the **mask register** and **priority register**.
3. **Acknowledge & Vector Fetch:** The CPU completes the current instruction, then fetches the ISR address from the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)**.
4. **Context Stacking:** The CPU automatically pushes critical registers (Program Counter, Status Register, sometimes General-Purpose Registers) onto the stack.
5. **ISR Execution:** The CPU executes the bound ISR — clears the source flag, performs I/O, signals events.
6. **Return (RETI/IRET):** The CPU restores the context from the stack and resumes the interrupted code.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The "atomic" nature of interrupt entry/exit is guaranteed by the hardware. Students often lose marks by forgetting that the CPU **always completes the current instruction** before vectoring.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Interrupts (Board-Favorite Diagram Topic)

### 2.2.1 By Source

- **Hardware Interrupts:** Triggered by external electrical signals (e.g., EXTI pin, timer).
- **Software Interrupts:** Triggered by a `SVC`/`SWI` instruction (used for OS system calls).

### 2.2.2 By Maskability

- **Maskable Interrupts:** Can be disabled by clearing the **I-bit** in the status register or by setting bits in the IRQ mask register. Examples: GPIO, UART, Timers.
- **Non-Maskable Interrupts (NMI):** Cannot be disabled — used for catastrophic events like power-fail, watchdog, or hardware faults.

### 2.2.3 By Vectoring

- **Vectored Interrupts:** Each source has a fixed vector address in the IVT (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, 8051 with 5 vectors).
- **Non-Vectored (Polled) Interrupts:** All sources share one vector; the ISR must poll a status register to identify the source.

---

## 2.3 Nested Interrupts & Priority Schemes

A **Nested Interrupt** is one that can preempt an already-executing ISR if its priority is higher. The hardware mechanism involves:

- A new priority comparison in the NVIC.
- Stacking the new context over the previous ISR's context.
- Returning in **reverse order** (LIFO).

**Priority Schemes:**

- **Fixed Priority:** Static, hardware-defined (e.g., IRQ0 > IRQ1 > IRQ2).
- **Dynamic Priority:** Software can reconfigure priorities at runtime (most ARM Cortex-M MCUs support this via IPR registers).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **priority number = high** vs **priority number = low**. In ARM Cortex-M, a **lower numerical priority value = higher logical priority**. E.g., priority 0 is the highest.

---

## 2.4 Device Driver Architecture — Layered Model

A driver sits at the **bottom of the software stack** and provides three services:

1. **Initialization:** Configure GPIO, clock tree, peripheral registers.
2. **Runtime Services:** Read/Write/IOCTL operations invoked by the upper layer.
3. **Teardown:** Power-down, release resources.

**The Three Structural Layers:**

- **Application Layer:** `main()`, RTOS tasks — calls generic API like `led_on()`.
- **Driver Layer:** Implements the API by directly manipulating peripheral registers.
- **Hardware Layer:** The actual silicon (UART, GPIO, ADC, I2C controllers).

---

## 2.5 Types of Device Drivers (KTU Classification)

| Type | Data Access Pattern | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Character Driver** | Stream of bytes, sequential | UART, SPI, I2C, GPIO |
| **Block Driver** | Fixed-size blocks, random access | SD Card, NAND Flash, EEPROM |
| **Network Driver** | Packets (frames) | Ethernet MAC, CAN, WiFi |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 board-exam relevant. Master them before the ESE.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Interrupt Latency | $L_{int} = T_{instr} + T_{stack} + T_{vec}$ | Time from IRQ assert to 1st ISR instruction. Units: **cycles / µs**. |
| 2 | Worst-Case Latency | $L_{max} = \max(L_{int} + L_{block})$ | Add longest blocking time of higher-priority ISRs. |
| 3 | Interrupt Throughput | $\lambda_{int} = \dfrac{1}{T_{ISR} + T_{ret}}$ | Max interrupt service rate (interrupts/second). |
| 4 | CPU Utilization (Polling) | $U_{poll} = \dfrac{T_{check}}{T_{event}}$ | Fraction of CPU spent checking. $U \to 1$ for fast events. |
| 5 | CPU Utilization (Interrupt) | $U_{int} = \dfrac{T_{ISR}}{T_{event}}$ | Fraction spent inside ISR only. **Far lower than polling.** |
| 6 | Priority Level Bits | $L_{levels} = 2^{n}$ | For $n$ priority bits. E.g., 3 bits $\rightarrow$ 8 levels. |
| 7 | Vector Table Size | $S_{IVT} = N_{src} \times W_{addr}$ | $N_{src}$ = sources, $W_{addr}$ = word width (e.g., 4 bytes for ARM). |
| 8 | Worst-Case Jitter | $J_{wc} = L_{max} - L_{min}$ | Determinism metric. **$J = 0$ is the ideal target.** |
| 9 | Interrupt Frequency | $f_{int} = \dfrac{1}{T_{int}}$ | Inverse of period between successive IRQs. Units: **Hz**. |
| 10 | Baud Rate vs. ISR Load | $\text{Load}_{ISR} = \dfrac{f_{baud}}{N_{bits}}$ | For UART, ISR is invoked per byte (every $N_{bits}$ clock periods). |

> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are critical in **automotive ECU design (ISO 26262)**, **medical device firmware (IEC 62304)**, and **industrial PLCs** where hard real-time deadlines cannot be violated.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Interrupt Latency Decomposition

**Problem Statement:** A KTU examiner gives you a microcontroller scenario. Compute the **worst-case interrupt latency** given the following parameters:

- Clock frequency: $f_{clk} = 72 \text{ MHz}$
- Longest instruction: $T_{instr} = 4 \text{ cycles}$
- Stack push (8 registers): $T_{stack} = 8 \text{ cycles}$
- Vector fetch (Flash wait state = 2): $T_{vec} = 3 \text{ cycles}$
- Pipeline flush penalty (ARM Cortex-M3): $T_{pipe} = 2 \text{ cycles}$

**Step 1 — Identify the constituents of latency.**

Latency is the time elapsed from the moment the IRQ pin is asserted to the moment the **first instruction of the ISR** is fetched. This is composed of:

$$L_{int} = T_{instr} + T_{pipe} + T_{stack} + T_{vec}$$

Each term is explained in the table below:

| Term | Meaning | Source |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{instr}$ | Longest instruction completes | CPU pipeline |
| $T_{pipe}$ | Pipeline refill / branch prediction flush | CPU architecture |
| $T_{stack}$ | Register context save | ABI & CPU |
| $T_{vec}$ | Fetching the ISR address from IVT | Memory wait states |

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$L_{int} = 4 + 2 + 8 + 3 = 17 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Convert cycles to absolute time.**

The cycle period is:

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}} \text{ s} = 13.89 \text{ ns}$$

Therefore:

$$L_{int} = 17 \times 13.89 \text{ ns} = 236.1 \text{ ns} \approx 0.236 \, \mu s$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

A latency of $\approx 0.236 \, \mu s$ is excellent — well-suited for control loops requiring **kHz-range** response (e.g., motor control at 10 kHz = 100 µs period). This is **0.236%** of the loop period.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Worst-Case Nested Interrupt Latency

**Problem:** While the UART ISR executes for 40 µs, a higher-priority ADC interrupt fires. The system must still meet the ADC's deadline of 50 µs from event.

**Step 1 — Compute blocking time.**

The ADC ISR is blocked by the UART ISR for:

$$T_{block} = T_{ISR,UART} = 40 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Add base latency.**

$$L_{ADC, max} = L_{int} + T_{block} = 0.236 + 40 = 40.236 \, \mu s$$

**Step 3 — Compare with deadline.**

$$\text{Deadline} = 50 \, \mu s$$

$$L_{ADC, max} = 40.236 \, \mu s < 50 \, \mu s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{DEADLINE MET} \, \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Calculate safety margin.**

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{50 - 40.236}{50} \times 100\% = 19.53\%$$

This is a healthy margin; a real-time engineer would flag this as **acceptable** for hard real-time use.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Polling vs. Interrupt vs. DMA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 frequently asks:** "Compare polling and interrupt-based I/O." Use this matrix in your answer.

| Parameter | Polling | Interrupt | DMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Cycles Wasted** | High (continuous check) | Low (only during ISR) | **Zero** (engine handles transfer) |
| **Latency** | Bounded by polling period | Microseconds | Negligible |
| **Complexity** | Very simple | Moderate (NVIC, IVT setup) | High (descriptor setup) |
| **Power Efficiency** | Poor (CPU always active) | Good (CPU can sleep) | **Best** |
| **Suitable For** | Single, deterministic event | Multiple async events | High-throughput block transfer |
| **Context Switch** | None | Yes (stack push/pop) | Minimal (only at start/end) |
| **Code Size** | Smallest | Medium | Largest |
| **Real-Time Guarantee** | Deterministic (if $T_{poll}$ known) | Depends on priority nesting | Excellent (predictable throughput) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — UART Interrupt Load

A UART operates at **115200 baud**, **8-N-1** (10 bits per frame).

**Step 1 — Compute interrupt period per byte.**

$$T_{byte} = \frac{10 \text{ bits}}{115200 \text{ bits/s}} = 86.8 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Compute interrupt frequency.**

$$f_{int} = \frac{1}{T_{byte}} = \frac{1}{86.8 \times 10^{-6}} = 11.52 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Compute CPU load if ISR takes 5 µs per call.**

$$U_{ISR} = f_{int} \times T_{ISR} = 11520 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0576 = 5.76\%$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

Only **5.76% CPU utilization** — leaves **94.24% CPU** for the main application. Switching to polling at 1 µs intervals would consume **100%** CPU.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Interrupt Handling Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Executes Main Code] --> B{IRQ Line Asserted?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Complete Current Instruction]
    C --> D[Pipeline Flush and Save Context]
    D --> E[Fetch Vector from IVT]
    E --> F[Load PC with ISR Address]
    F --> G[Execute ISR Body]
    G --> H{Clear Source Flag}
    H -- Not Cleared --> G
    H -- Cleared --> I[Restore Context from Stack]
    I --> J[Return to Interrupted Code]
    J --> A
```
