## 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Stack Size for Nested ISRs

For an ARM Cortex-M system with hardware stacking of 32 bytes per ISR frame, and a maximum nesting depth of $M$ simultaneous ISRs (rare but possible during peak load):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{required} &= S_{main,min} + (M \times S_{ctx,hw}) + S_{safety} \\
&= S_{main,min} + (M \times 32\ \text{bytes}) + S_{safety}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a typical application with $S_{main,min} = 256$ bytes, $M = 3$, $S_{safety} = 128$ bytes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{required} &= 256 + (3 \times 32) + 128 \\
&= 256 + 96 + 128 \\
&= 480\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounding up to the next standard allocation granularity (e.g., 512 bytes) is the standard KTU-recommended practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack Overflow Risk:** If the calculated $S_{required}$ is not allocated, a deeply nested interrupt can corrupt memory and cause *silent system failure* — a common bug in KTU lab evaluations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level ISR Device Driver Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["APPLICATION TASK LAYER"]
    DD["DEVICE DRIVER LAYER"]
    ISR["INTERRUPT SERVICE ROUTINE"]
    HW["HARDWARE PERIPHERAL"]
    NVIC["NVIC / INTERRUPT CONTROLLER"]

    APP -->|API Calls open read write| DD
    DD -->|Register ISR Pointer| NVIC
    DD -->|Configure Registers| HW
    HW -->|IRQ Event Signal| NVIC
    NVIC -->|Vector Fetch and Context Save| ISR
    ISR -->|Read Data Register| HW
    ISR -->|Clear Pending Bit| HW
    ISR -->|Set Event Flag| DD
    DD -->|Wake Task| APP
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Application** interacts only with the **Device Driver API** (top-down calls).
- The **Device Driver** configures hardware and registers the ISR with the **NVIC** at boot.
- The **Hardware Peripheral** raises an IRQ line when an event occurs.
- The **NVIC** arbitrates, saves context, and invokes the **ISR**.
- The **ISR** services the hardware minimally and signals back to the driver layer.

---

## 4.2 ISR Execution Flow — Phase-by-Phase Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["PHASE 1: PERIPHERAL EVENT OCCURS"]
    P2["PHASE 2: IRQ LINE ASSERTED TO NVIC"]
    P3["PHASE 3: NVIC ARBITRATES PRIORITY"]
    P4["PHASE 4: CONTEXT STACK PUSH 8 REGISTERS"]
    P5["PHASE 5: VECTOR FETCH FROM IVT"]
    P6["PHASE 6: ISR BODY EXECUTES"]
    P7A["READ STATUS REGISTER"]
    P7B["CLEAR INTERRUPT PENDING BIT"]
    P7C["COPY DATA TO BUFFER"]
    P7D["SET SEMAPHORE OR FLAG"]
    P8["PHASE 7: EXECUTE EXC RETURN"]
    P9["PHASE 8: HARDWARE POPS CONTEXT"]
    P10["PHASE 9: RESUMED TASK CONTINUES"]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7A --> P7B --> P7C --> P7D --> P8 --> P9 --> P10
```

---

## 4.3 Top-Half and Bottom-Half Processing Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EVT["HARDWARE EVENT"]
    TOP["TOP HALF ISR CONTEXT URGENT MINIMAL"]
    FLAG["SHARED FLAG OR QUEUE"]
    BH["BOTTOM HALF TASK CONTEXT DEFERRED WORK"]

    EVT --> TOP
    TOP -->|Set Flag Copy Data Clear IRQ| FLAG
    FLAG -->|Task Notified| BH
    BH -->|Process Data| FLAG
```

**Design Intent:** The ISR handles only the **time-critical portion** (reading a UART byte before the next one overwrites it, clearing a watchdog, toggling a pin). The **bottom-half task** does the heavy processing (decoding, logging, sending to cloud), where it can sleep, block, or call OS functions.

---

## 4.4 Interrupt Vector Table Layout (ARM Cortex-M Convention)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V0["VECTOR 0 INITIAL SP"]
    V1["VECTOR 1 RESET HANDLER"]
    V2["VECTOR 2 NMI HANDLER"]
    V3["VECTOR 3 HARD FAULT"]
    V16["VECTOR 16 IRQ0 FIRST PERIPHERAL"]
    V17["VECTOR 17 IRQ1"]
    V18["VECTOR 18 IRQ2"]
    V47["VECTOR 47 LAST IRQ"]

    V0 --> V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V16 --> V17 --> V18 --> V47
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). List any FOUR characteristics of an ISR.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** is a hardware-triggered function that the processor executes in response to an asynchronous event from a peripheral or external source, with the goal of servicing the event in bounded, minimal time.

**Four Characteristics of an ISR:**

1. **Runs in Interrupt Context, not Task Context** — It is not scheduled by the OS; it preempts whatever code is currently running.
2. **Must be Short and Non-Blocking** — Cannot call blocking OS APIs (e.g., `vTaskDelay`, semaphore `take` that may block) to avoid unbounded latency.
3. **Hardware-Triggered and Asynchronous** — Execution timing is not deterministic from the application's perspective; it depends on real-world events.
4. **Requires Explicit Acknowledgment** — The ISR must clear the peripheral's interrupt pending bit, otherwise the controller will re-trigger it immediately on return, causing an infinite loop.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition; 0.5 marks per characteristic × 4 = 2 marks. Total 3.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is an Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)? Where is it located in ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers, and what happens during "vector fetch"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** is a static array of function pointers stored in non-volatile memory (typically flash), where each entry contains the starting address of a specific interrupt handler. The IVT enables the processor to locate the correct ISR for a given IRQ without software search.

**Location in ARM Cortex-M:** The IVT is located at the **base address of the boot memory space, which by default is `0x00000000`**. The first entry is the **initial stack pointer value**, and the second entry is the **reset handler address**. Subsequent entries correspond to NMI, HardFault, and peripheral IRQs (starting at vector 16 for IRQ0).

**Vector Fetch Process:** When the NVIC accepts a pending IRQ, the processor automatically reads the corresponding 4-byte entry from the IVT, loads it into the Program Counter (PC), and begins ISR execution. The entire fetch and context-save is performed in hardware in a deterministic number of cycles (typically 12 cycles on Cortex-M3/M4).

*(Award 1 mark for IVT definition; 1 mark for location; 1 mark for vector fetch mechanism.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 scheme, students must answer ONE question of 14 marks from the choice provided, with sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **complete execution flow of an Interrupt Service Routine** in an ARM Cortex-M based embedded system. Include a block diagram of the device driver interfacing with the NVIC. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **worst-case interrupt latency** for a system with three interrupt sources of priorities P1 (highest), P2, and P3 (lowest), given arbitration latencies $L_1 = 0.2\ \mu s$, $L_2 = 0.3\ \mu s$, $L_3 = 0.4\ \mu s$ and ISR execution times $T_1 = 2.5\ \mu s$, $T_2 = 1.5\ \mu s$, $T_3 = 1.0\ \mu s$. Verify if the system meets a real-time deadline of $8\ \mu s$. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The execution flow of an ISR in ARM Cortex-M comprises **six sequential phases**, supported by a tightly coupled device driver architecture.

**Phase 1 — Interrupt Request Generation:** A peripheral (e.g., UART, timer) detects an event and asserts its dedicated IRQ line to the **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)**.

**Phase 2 — Arbitration and Pending:** The NVIC checks priority. If the new IRQ has higher priority than the currently executing code (or current ISR), preemption occurs. The request is latched in the NVIC's pending register.

**Phase 3 — Hardware Context Save:** The Cortex-M core automatically pushes **8 registers** — R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR — onto the active stack (MSP or PSP). This consumes 32 bytes and 2 bus cycles.

**Phase 4 — Vector Fetch:** The NVIC provides the vector number; the core reads the corresponding 4-byte function pointer from the IVT at `0x00000000 + (vector_number × 4)` and loads it into PC.

**Phase 5 — ISR Body:** The ISR performs: (i) read status register to confirm the source, (ii) read/write data registers to service the device, (iii) clear the pending bit, (iv) signal a deferred task via semaphore/flag.

**Phase 6 — Return and Restore:** Writing `EXC_RETURN` to PC triggers automatic context restore (8 registers popped), and execution resumes at the preempted instruction.

**Block Diagram Description (already shown in Section 4.1):** The application calls driver API → driver configures peripheral and registers ISR pointer with NVIC → hardware raises IRQ → NVIC invokes ISR → ISR services device and notifies task.

*(Award 1 mark per phase × 6 = 6 marks; 1 mark for architectural explanation.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The worst-case latency for the **lowest priority task (P3)** must account for the *cumulative* delay caused by both higher-priority interrupts fully executing first, then P3's own ISR.

**Step 1 — Write the General Expression:**

$$
L_{worst,P3} = L_1 + T_{ISR,1} + L_2 + T_{ISR,2} + L_3 + T_{ISR,3}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Given Values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{worst,P3} &= 0.2\ \mu s + 2.5\ \mu s + 0.3\ \mu s + 1.5\ \mu s + 0.4\ \mu s + 1.0\ \mu s \\
&= (0.2 + 2.5 + 0.3 + 1.5 + 0.4 + 1.0)\ \mu s \\
&= 5.9\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Deadline:**

$$
L_{worst,P3} = 5.9\ \mu s \quad \text{vs.} \quad T_{deadline} = 8\ \mu s
$$

Since $5.9\ \mu s < 8\ \mu s$, the system **meets the real-time deadline** with a slack of $8 - 5.9 = 2.1\ \mu s$.

**Step 4 — Verdict:** The system is **schedulable**. The schedulability margin is $\dfrac{2.1}{8} \times 100\% = 26.25\%$, which is healthy for a hard-real-time application.

*(Award 2 marks for formula setup; 2 marks for substitution and arithmetic; 2 marks for comparison and conclusion; 1 mark for schedulability margin calculation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Top-Half and Bottom-Half processing model** in device drivers. Why is this model preferred over doing all processing inside the ISR? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A temperature monitoring embedded system uses a 1 Hz ADC sampling timer. The ADC conversion ISR takes $1.2\ \mu s$. The main loop's minimum iteration time is $250\ \mu s$. Calculate: (i) CPU utilization by the ISR, (ii) interrupt overhead percentage, and (iii) whether disabling interrupts for $0.5\ \mu s$ inside the ISR is acceptable. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **Top-Half / Bottom-Half model** splits interrupt processing into two temporally separated phases to balance *responsiveness* and *system throughput*.

**Top-Half (runs in ISR context):**
- Executes immediately upon IRQ entry.
- Performs only **time-critical, non-deferrable** work: reading a UART data register before the next byte overwrites it, clearing the watchdog, sampling an ADC result, clearing the interrupt pending bit.
- Duration: typically **< 5–10 µs**.
- Cannot call blocking OS APIs, cannot perform I/O to slow peripherals.

**Bottom-Half (runs in task/process context):**
- A deferred worker task, notified by the ISR via semaphore, event flag, or message queue.
- Performs **non-time-critical work**: data logging, protocol encoding, sending to a wireless module, complex calculations.
- Can call any OS API, can block, can take its time.
- Duration: unbounded, but lower priority than critical real-time tasks.

**Why This Model is Preferred:**

1. **Minimizes Interrupt Latency** — Short ISRs free the NVIC to service higher-priority IRQs faster.
2. **Enables Blocking Operations** — Bottom-half runs in task context, where `vTaskDelay()`, `xQueueSend()` (with block), and file I/O are all permitted.
3. **Improves Code Modularity** — Hardware-specific code is isolated in the ISR; business logic lives in the worker task.
4. **Reduces Stack Pressure** — Deep call chains and large local variables are pushed only in the task's stack, not the ISR's.

*(Award 2 marks for top-half explanation; 2 marks for bottom-half explanation; 3 marks for advantages and rationale.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- Sampling rate $f_{IRQ} = 1$ Hz
- ISR execution time $T_{ISR} = 1.2\ \mu s$
- Main loop minimum time $T_{main,min} = 250\ \mu s$
- Critical section time $T_{crit} = 0.5\ \mu s$

**(i) CPU Utilization by ISR:**

The total time period of one sampling cycle is $T_{period} = 1 / 1\text{ Hz} = 1\ \text{s} = 1{,}000{,}000\ \mu s$.

$$
U_{IRQ} = \frac{T_{ISR}}{T_{period}} \times 100\% = \frac{1.2\ \mu s}{1{,}000{,}000\ \mu s} \times 100\% = 0.00012\%
$$

**(ii) Interrupt Overhead Percentage (per main loop iteration):**

In one main loop iteration, the time *not available* to the main task due to ISR preemption:

$$
\text{Overhead per iteration} = T_{ISR} = 1.2\ \mu s
$$

Relative to main loop time:

$$
\text{Overhead \%} = \frac{1.2}{250} \times 100\% = 0.48\%
$$

**(iii) Acceptability of 0.5 µs Critical Section:**

The critical section disables interrupts globally. The total time the main task is held off is:

$$
T_{holdoff} = T_{crit} + T_{ISR} = 0.5\ \mu s + 1.2\ \mu s = 1.7\ \mu s
$$

This is **0.68% of one main loop iteration** — well within typical 1–5% overhead budgets. The critical section is **acceptable** because:
- It is shorter than the ISR itself, so it does not create new latency peaks.
- The main task has a 250 µs budget, so a 1.7 µs hold-off leaves ample slack.
- No higher-priority ISR exists in this scenario to be blocked unacceptably.

*(Award 2 marks for (i); 2 marks for (ii); 3 marks for (iii) with proper reasoning.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in ISR Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Context-Save Step** — Many students describe the ISR execution but omit the *automatic hardware stacking* on ARM Cortex-M. This is worth 1–2 marks. Always mention that **8 registers are pushed automatically**.
>
> 2. **Confusing "Interrupt Latency" with "Interrupt Response Time"** — Latency = IRQ to first ISR instruction; Response Time = IRQ to ISR completion. Examiners explicitly check for this distinction.
>
> 3. **Not Clearing the Interrupt Pending Bit** — In the "execution flow" question, students often list servicing the device but forget to mention *clearing the IRQ source*. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Using Blocking OS APIs Inside ISR** — When asked "what can't an ISR do?", students miss mentioning that `printf`, `malloc`, and blocking semaphore waits are forbidden. This loses 1–2 marks in design questions.
>
> 5. **Unit Conversion Errors in Numericals** — A 72 MHz clock gives a period of $1/72\text{ MHz} = 0.0139\ \mu s$ per cycle, not $1.39\ \mu s$. Always re-verify units before finalizing a numerical answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — ISR Device Drivers**
>
> - ISR = hardware-triggered callback, runs in **interrupt context**, must be **short and non-blocking**.
> - Device driver responsibilities: **register** ISR, **configure** peripheral, **service** events, **clear** pending bits.
> - Six-phase ISR flow: **IRQ assert → NVIC arbitrate → context save → vector fetch → ISR body → context restore**.
> - ARM Cortex-M automatically saves **8 registers (32 bytes)**; vector table at **`0x00000000`**.
> - **Interrupt Latency** $L_{int}$ = time from IRQ to first ISR instruction (12 cycles typical on Cortex-M3).
> - **Interrupt Response Time** $T_{resp} = L_{int} + T_{ISR}$.
> - **Worst-Case Latency (with N sources):** $L_{worst} = \sum (L_{arb,k} + T_{ISR,k})$.
> - **Top-half** = time-critical ISR work; **Bottom-half** = deferred task work.
> - ISRs **cannot**: block, call `printf`/`malloc`, use floating-point freely, or take semaphores that may block.
> - ISRs **must**: clear the pending bit, be reentrancy-safe, use `volatile` for shared globals.
> - **Critical section** inside an ISR should be shorter than the ISR itself and not exceed a few percent of the main loop time.
> - **Stack sizing** for nested ISRs: $S_{depth} = S_{main} + (M \times 32\ \text{bytes}) + S_{safety}$, where $M$ = max nesting depth.
> - Real-time schedulability: System passes if $L_{worst} < T_{deadline}$, with a healthy **20–30% margin** recommended.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupt Latency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interrupt Latency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Interrupt Latency

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> **Interrupt Latency** is the precise time interval measured from the instant an **interrupt request (IRQ)** is asserted at the processor pin (or internal peripheral flag) until the execution of the **first instruction of the corresponding Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. It quantifies the worst-case delay a system exhibits before responding to an asynchronous hardware or software event.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, interrupt latency is treated as a **deterministic timing parameter** of the real-time kernel, directly impacting the **schedulability bound** of any hard real-time embedded application (automotive ECUs, medical ventilators, flight controllers).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Emergency Room Triage"

Imagine you walk into a hospital emergency room with a heart-attack patient (the **hardware interrupt**).

1. You reach the reception desk at the **nurse station** — the interrupt is **recognized** but not yet serviced.
2. The on-duty doctor is currently performing a scheduled surgery (the **current instruction / critical section**). The surgery *cannot* be abandoned mid-stroke.
3. The doctor finishes the surgery, **washes hands, removes gloves, walks to the ER** — this is the **context save and vector fetch** overhead.
4. Only now does the doctor actually *begin* treating the heart-attack patient — the **first instruction of the ISR** is finally executed.

The total "waiting time" the patient experienced — from arrival at reception to the first touch of treatment — is the **Interrupt Latency**. The shorter and more predictable this time, the safer the hospital (embedded system) is for critical patients (time-sensitive events).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** Interrupt latency is universally measured in **microseconds (µs)** for modern Cortex-M MCUs and in **clock cycles** for silicon-level architectural analysis. The **worst-case interrupt latency (WCIL)** is the parameter of interest for hard real-time certification (e.g., ISO 26262, DO-178C).

---

## 2. Components of Interrupt Latency

Interrupt latency is *not* a single monolithic value. It is the algebraic sum of several hardware and software overheads:

| # | Component | Source | Typical Magnitude (Cortex-M4 @ 72 MHz) |
|---|-----------|--------|------------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Hardware Detection Delay** | Interrupt signal synchronization to system clock | 2 – 3 clock cycles |
| 2 | **Pipeline Flush / Completion** | Current instruction must retire | 0 – 12 clock cycles |
| 3 | **Interrupt Recognition Window** | Wait for next instruction boundary | 0 – 1 cycle |
| 4 | **Vector Fetch & Stack Push** | Hardware automatically pushes R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR | 12 clock cycles (fixed) |
| 5 | **ISR Prologue Software** | Compiler-generated register save (R4–R11) | Compiler-dependent |
| 6 | **Interrupt Disable / Priority Masking** | Kernel blocks lower-priority IRQs | 0 – N µs (kernel-dependent) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Insight:** When the question states *"Discuss interrupt latency"*, examiners expect you to enumerate **all six components** above and explain which one dominates in a **bare-metal** versus **RTOS-based** design.

---

## 3. Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Airbag ECU:** Must deploy within **10 µs** of crash sensor trigger → drives the choice of an MCU with WCIL ≤ 5 µs.
- **Industrial Motor Control (FOC):** Current sampling must occur at the PWM zero-crossing → interrupt latency jitter must be < 0.5 µs.
- **Hard Disk Drive Controllers:** Servo-loop interrupt must fire every **23.4 µs** (42 kHz) — any latency overrun causes a sector read failure.
- **Pacemaker / Defibrillator:** Therapeutic shock delivery latency bound is regulated by **IEC 60601-2-4** medical device safety standards.
- **Avionics Fly-By-Wire:** ARINC 653 partitioned scheduling demands a bounded WCIL per partition for **DO-178C Level A** certification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 4. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Anatomy of an Interrupt

### 4.1 Operational Sequence (The "Interrupt Journey")

When a peripheral asserts its IRQ line, the following deterministic sequence unfolds in a typical **ARM Cortex-M** pipeline:

1. **Event Occurrence (t = 0)** — A peripheral flag (e.g., `UART_TX_COMPLETE`, `ADC_EOC`, `GPIO_RISING_EDGE`) sets its interrupt request bit.
2. **Synchronization Stage** — The asynchronous external event is synchronized to the processor clock domain to prevent metastability (2 clock cycles).
3. **Priority Arbitration** — The Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC) compares the new IRQ's priority against the currently active **BASEPRI / PRIMASK** mask. If masked, the IRQ is **pended** until unmasked.
4. **Pipeline Drain** — The processor completes the currently executing instruction (cannot be interrupted mid-instruction on a RISC pipeline; instructions like `LDR`/`STR` with wait-states can extend this).
5. **Vector Fetch** — The CPU reads the ISR's entry address from the **Vector Table** in flash/SRAM.
6. **Automatic Hardware Stacking** — Cortex-M3/M4 hardware automatically pushes **8 registers** (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) onto the active stack pointer (`MSP` or `PSP`).
7. **First ISR Instruction Execution (t = T_latency)** — The latency window closes.

### 4.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why synchronize?** Asynchronous signals can violate setup/hold times, causing metastability. Synchronization flips reduce the probability of metastable propagation to near-zero (Mean Time Between Failures ≈ centuries).
- **Why drain the pipeline?** RISC architectures commit results in the write-back stage. Interrupting mid-instruction would corrupt architectural state and break ISA semantics.
- **Why vector fetch?** Avoids a software `JUMP` table dispatch — saves 4–8 cycles per interrupt.
- **Why automatic stacking?** Eliminates the need for the ISR prologue to manually save R0–R3, freeing the compiler to use them as scratch registers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Priority Masking vs. Latency:**
> In an **RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS)**, the kernel's critical sections (`taskENTER_CRITICAL()`) disable interrupts via `cpsid i` (ARM) or `sei/cli` (AVR). Any IRQ that arrives *during* this window **waits** — this waiting time is the largest contributor to **average-case interrupt latency** in RTOS designs.

### 4.3 Interrupt Latency vs. Interrupt Response Time

Students frequently confuse these two distinct KTU metrics:

| Parameter | Definition | Includes ISR Execution? |
|-----------|------------|------------------------|
| **Interrupt Latency** | Time from IRQ assertion → first ISR instruction | **No** |
| **Interrupt Response Time** | Time from IRQ assertion → completion of ISR | **Yes** |
| **Interrupt Recovery Time** | Time from ISR end → resumption of preempted task | **No** (separate metric) |

### 4.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol in the table to keep the markdown table valid.

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_{latency} = T_{detect} + T_{drain} + T_{vector} + T_{stack} + T_{mask}$ | Total interrupt latency decomposition | seconds |
| 2 | $WCIL = \max(T_{latency})$ over all reachable system states | Worst-Case Interrupt Latency (certification metric) | seconds |
| 3 | $T_{drain} \leq N_{pipeline\_stages} \times T_{clk}$ | Pipeline drain bound | seconds |
| 4 | $T_{stack} = 8 \times T_{mem\_write}$ | Cortex-M3/M4 automatic stacking (8 words) | seconds |
| 5 | $Jitter_{latency} = WCIL - BCIL$ | Latency jitter (Best-Case to Worst-Case spread) | seconds |
| 6 | $T_{response} = T_{latency} + T_{ISR}$ | Total response time including service routine | seconds |
| 7 | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CPU}}$ | System clock period | seconds |
| 8 | $WCIL_{RTOS} = T_{max\_critical\_section} + T_{latency\_bare}$ | RTOS upper bound on latency | seconds |

Where:
- $T_{detect}$ = Synchronization & arbitration time
- $T_{drain}$ = Pipeline / instruction completion time
- $T_{vector}$ = Vector table fetch time
- $T_{stack}$ = Hardware register stacking time
- $T_{mask}$ = Time spent with interrupts disabled
- $T_{mem\_write}$ = Single memory write cycle time

### 4.5 Bare-Metal vs. RTOS Interrupt Latency — KTU Favourite Comparison

| Aspect | Bare-Metal (Polling + ISR) | RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS) |
|--------|----------------------------|------------------------|
| Typical WCIL | **50 – 500 ns** | **1 – 50 µs** |
| Dominant Overhead | Pipeline drain | Critical section masking |
| Determinism | High (bounded) | Medium (kernel-version dependent) |
| Use Case | Hard real-time, signal processing | Multi-task consumer/industrial |
| KTU Question Likelihood | ★★★ (12 marks) | ★★★★★ (14 marks) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 5. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 5.1 Derivation 1 — Total Interrupt Latency Formula (Decomposed Form)

We start from the **first-principles timeline** of an interrupt event.

Let $t_0$ be the instant the peripheral asserts its interrupt request line.

**Step 1: Hardware Synchronization Delay**

The asynchronous IRQ must be sampled by the system clock. For a single-flop synchronizer, the worst-case delay equals one full clock period.

$$
T_{detect} = 1 \times T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{CPU}}
$$

*Logic:* The synchronizer flip-flop may have just missed the rising edge; it must wait one full cycle before sampling 'high' reliably.

---

**Step 2: NVIC Arbitration & Priority Check**

The NVIC performs a priority comparison against `BASEPRI`. If the new IRQ has strictly higher priority (lower numeric value for Cortex-M), it preempts. This takes one clock cycle.

$$
T_{arb} = 1 \times T_{clk}
$$

*Logic:* A single combinational priority encoder is used; propagation is sub-nanosecond at 72 MHz.

---

**Step 3: Pipeline Drain**

The processor must allow the currently executing multi-cycle instruction (e.g., `LDR` from slow flash, `MUL`, `SDIV`) to retire. For an N-stage pipeline:

$$
T_{drain} = N_{stages} \times T_{clk}
$$

*Logic:* Cannot interrupt mid-instruction without violating ARM Architecture Reference Manual commit semantics.

---

**Step 4: Vector Table Fetch**

The CPU reads the 32-bit ISR address from the vector table. Assuming zero-wait-state flash:

$$
T_{vector} = 1 \times T_{mem} = 1 \times T_{clk}
$$

*Logic:* Single 32-bit aligned load from the vector table offset (e.g., IRQ #6 → address `0x0000_0018`).

---

**Step 5: Automatic Register Stacking (Cortex-M3/M4 Specific)**

Eight 32-bit registers are pushed to the stack. Each write is a single cycle to zero-wait-state SRAM.

$$
T_{stack} = 8 \times T_{clk}
$$

*Logic:* Hardware pushes `xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0` in that exact order. Total = 8 × 4 bytes = 32 bytes written.

---

**Step 6: Critical Section Masking (RTOS only)**

If the system is inside an RTOS critical section when the IRQ fires, the IRQ must wait until interrupts are re-enabled.

$$
T_{mask} = T_{crit\_section} \quad \text{(0 for bare-metal)}
$$

*Logic:* RTOS primitives like `taskENTER_CRITICAL()` set `PRIMASK = 1`, blocking all maskable interrupts.

---

**Step 7: Summation — Total Interrupt Latency**

$$
T_{latency} = T_{detect} + T_{arb} + T_{drain} + T_{vector} + T_{stack} + T_{mask}
$$

Substituting the per-stage values:

$$
T_{latency} = (1 + 1 + N + 1 + 8) \times T_{clk} + T_{mask}
$$

$$
T_{latency} = (N + 11) \times T_{clk} + T_{mask}
$$

*Logic:* Final closed-form expression. For a 3-stage Cortex-M3 pipeline (N = 3) with $T_{clk} = 13.89$ ns (72 MHz) and no masking ($T_{mask} = 0$):

$$
T_{latency} = (3 + 11) \times 13.89 \, \text{ns} = 14 \times 13.89 \, \text{ns} = 194.46 \, \text{ns}
$$

This matches the **~200 ns** typical WCIL quoted in STM32 datasheets for bare-metal operation.

---

### 5.2 Derivation 2 — Worst-Case Interrupt Latency Bound

In an RTOS, the worst case occurs when an IRQ arrives **exactly one cycle before** the critical section entry, forcing it to wait the **entire critical section duration**.

$$
WCIL_{RTOS} = T_{latency\_bare} + \max(T_{crit\_section})
$$

For FreeRTOS, the worst-case critical section length is bounded by:

$$
\max(T_{crit\_section}) \approx T_{list\_insert} + T_{context\_switch}
$$

*Logic:* The longest uninterruptible code path inside the kernel (typically a list insertion) determines the masking window.

---

### 5.3 Derivation 3 — Latency Jitter for Hard Real-Time Certification

For a system to be certifiable under **DO-178C Level A**, the **latency jitter** must be quantified:

$$
Jitter = WCIL - BCIL
$$

Where BCIL (Best-Case Interrupt Latency) assumes zero pipeline drain, zero masking, and cache hits:

$$
BCIL = (1 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 8) \times T_{clk} = 11 \times T_{clk}
$$

Thus:

$$
Jitter = (N + 11) \times T_{clk} + T_{mask} - 11 \times T_{clk} = N \times T_{clk} + T_{mask}
$$

*Logic:* The jitter equals the *variable* components only — pipeline state + masking time. A **zero-jitter** system is achievable only with $N = 0$ (single-cycle processor) and $T_{mask} = 0$ (no RTOS critical sections).

---

### 5.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A Cortex-M4 MCU runs at 100 MHz (T_clk = 10 ns). The system uses an RTOS whose longest critical section takes 1.2 µs. Calculate:
> (a) Bare-metal interrupt latency
> (b) RTOS worst-case interrupt latency
> (c) Latency jitter

**Solution:**

**Given:** $f_{CPU} = 100$ MHz → $T_{clk} = 10$ ns, $N = 3$ (Cortex-M4 pipeline stages), $T_{mask} = 1200$ ns.

**(a)** Bare-metal latency:

$$
T_{latency\_bare} = (N + 11) \times T_{clk} = (3 + 11) \times 10 \, \text{ns} = 140 \, \text{ns}
$$

**(b)** RTOS worst-case latency:

$$
WCIL = 140 \, \text{ns} + 1200 \, \text{ns} = 1340 \, \text{ns} = 1.34 \, \mu s
$$

**(c)** Latency jitter:

$$
Jitter = N \times T_{clk} + T_{mask} = 30 \, \text{ns} + 1200 \, \text{ns} = 1230 \, \text{ns}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marking Tip:** Always show the *units* explicitly. Examiners award 1 mark for unit consistency in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 6. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 6.1 Interrupt Latency Timeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional flow of an interrupt request through the processor's response pipeline:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PERIPHERAL EVENT OCCURS] --> B[IRQ LINE ASSERTED AT t EQUALS 0]
    B --> C[STEP1 HARDWARE SYNCHRONIZATION]
    C --> D[STEP2 NVIC PRIORITY ARBITRATION]
    D --> E{IRQ MASKED BY CRITICAL SECTION?}
    E -->|YES| F[STEP3 WAIT IN PENDING STATE]
    F --> G[CRITICAL SECTION EXITS]
    G --> H[STEP4 PIPELINE DRAIN]
    E -->|NO| H
    H --> I[STEP5 VECTOR TABLE FETCH]
    I --> J[STEP6 AUTOMATIC STACK PUSH 8 REGISTERS]
    J --> K[FIRST ISR INSTRUCTION EXECUTED AT t EQUALS T LATENCY]
    K --> L[ISR BODY RUNS]
    L --> M[ISR EPILOGUE RESTORES CONTEXT]
    M --> N[PREEMPTED TASK RESUMES]
```

### 6.2 Latency Composition (Hierarchical Breakdown)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TOTAL INTERRUPT LATENCY] --> B[HARDWARE OVERHEAD]
    A --> C[SOFTWARE OVERHEAD]
    B --> D[SYNCHRONIZATION DELAY]
    B --> E[PIPELINE DRAIN]
    B --> F[VECTOR FETCH]
    B --> G[AUTOMATIC STACKING]
    C --> H[CRITICAL SECTION MASK]
    C --> I[ISR PROLOGUE REGISTER SAVE]
    C --> J[PRIORITY INHERITANCE DELAY]
```

### 6.3 Bare-Metal vs. RTOS Latency Comparison (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BARE METAL DESIGN] --> A1[HARDWARE DOMINANT LATENCY]
    A1 --> A2[WCIL APPROX 50 TO 500 NS]
    A1 --> A3[USED IN HARD REAL TIME]

    B[RTOS BASED DESIGN] --> B1[SOFTWARE DOMINANT LATENCY]
    B1 --> B2[WCIL APPROX 1 TO 50 US]
    B1 --> B3[USED IN MULTITASK SYSTEMS]

    C[LATENCY REDUCTION TECHNIQUES] --> C1[USE NVIC PRIORITY PREEMPTION]
    C --> C2[MINIMIZE CRITICAL SECTIONS]
    C --> C3[USE LOCK FREE DATA STRUCTURES]
    C --> C4[DEFER WORK TO DEFERRED CALLBACKS]
    C --> C5[PLACE ISR VECTORS IN ZERO WAIT STATE RAM]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Tip for the Diagram:** Trace the path `A → B → D → E → H` to see the **critical path** of latency. Any optimization in the Mermaid chart that shortens this critical path directly reduces WCIL.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 7. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define interrupt latency. List any four factors that affect it in an embedded system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Interrupt latency is the time delay between the assertion of an interrupt request signal at the processor and the start of execution of the first instruction of the corresponding Interrupt Service Routine (ISR).

**Factors (½ mark each, any four):**
1. Hardware synchronization delay of the asynchronous interrupt signal.
2. Time required for the currently executing instruction to complete (pipeline drain).
3. Time taken for the processor to fetch the ISR vector address from the vector table.
4. Duration for which interrupts remain masked inside RTOS critical sections.
5. Number of registers saved during the ISR prologue (compiler-dependent).
6. Priority masking and interrupt nesting configuration in the NVIC.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series 2]**
**Distinguish between interrupt latency and interrupt response time. Why is this distinction important in hard real-time systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Interrupt Latency | Interrupt Response Time |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Definition | IRQ → First ISR instruction | IRQ → End of ISR execution |
| Includes ISR body? | No | Yes |
| Determinism metric | Bounded by hardware | Bounded by ISR algorithm |

**Importance in hard real-time systems (1 mark):**
In hard real-time systems such as automotive braking or pacemakers, missing a deadline causes **catastrophic failure**. The system designer must guarantee that the **interrupt latency** (the *response* part) is strictly less than the deadline, regardless of what the ISR itself does. The interrupt response time, which includes the full ISR, is only useful for system-level throughput analysis, not for deadline certification. The WCIL (Worst-Case Interrupt Latency) is the certifiable bound that schedulability analysis (e.g., Rate Monotonic Analysis) relies upon.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert**
> - **Do NOT confuse "interrupt latency" with "interrupt response time"** — this is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks in the KTU board exam.
> - **Always quote the units** (ns, µs, ms) in numerical problems. A correct number without units gets **zero marks** per the strict 2024 Scheme rubric.
> - When asked to "discuss", you **must** include both **bare-metal and RTOS** perspectives — many students write only one and lose 3 marks.
> - Drawing a labelled timing diagram fetches **2 easy marks** — do not skip it.
> - Do **not** mention Python, MATLAB, or any high-level scripting in your answers for this topic — only C/assembly-level analysis is credited.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled timing diagram, explain the concept of **interrupt latency** in a microcontroller. Enumerate all the hardware and software components contributing to it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Timing Diagram (3 marks):**

```
    t0            t1    t2    t3   t4   t5
    |             |     |     |    |    |
    |---IRQ line--|     |     |    |    |
    |   asserted  |     |     |    |    |
    |             |     |     |    |    |
    |---Pipeline-|Sync-|NVIC-|Vec-|ISR-|
    |   running   |     |arb. |fetch|exec|
    |             |     |     |    |    |
    |---T_detect--|--T_drain-|-Tv-|-Tst-|
    |                                   |
    |<--------- T_latency ------------->|
```

- $t_0$: Peripheral event occurs, IRQ asserted
- $t_1$: Synchronization complete
- $t_2$: Priority arbitration done
- $t_3$: Vector address fetched
- $t_4$: First ISR instruction executed

**Components (2 marks each, totaling 4 marks):**

*Hardware components:*
1. **Synchronization delay** of the asynchronous IRQ (2 cycles).
2. **Pipeline drain** of the in-flight instruction.
3. **Vector table fetch** from flash/RAM.
4. **Automatic register stacking** (8 registers on Cortex-M3/M4).

*Software components:*
1. **Interrupt disable / critical section** masking in RTOS.
2. **ISR prologue** register saving (R4–R11, compiler-generated).
3. **Priority inheritance protocol** wait times in mutex-protected ISRs.

**Conclusion (½ mark):**
Interrupt latency is the cumulative sum of all these components. A well-designed embedded system minimizes each component to keep WCIL within the deadline bound.

---

**(b)** A **Cortex-M3** microcontroller runs at **50 MHz** and is used in a **bare-metal** motor control application. Compute the **interrupt latency** assuming a 3-stage pipeline and no RTOS. If the same system is ported to FreeRTOS with a critical section of **800 ns**, compute the new **WCIL** and the **latency jitter**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $f_{CPU} = 50$ MHz → $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{50 \times 10^6} = 20$ ns
Pipeline stages $N = 3$, $T_{mask} = 800$ ns.

**(i) Bare-metal interrupt latency (3 marks):**

$$
T_{latency\_bare} = (N + 11) \times T_{clk}
$$

$$
T_{latency\_bare} = (3 + 11) \times 20 \, \text{ns} = 14 \times 20 \, \text{ns}
$$

$$
T_{latency\_bare} = 280 \, \text{ns}
$$

**(ii) RTOS worst-case interrupt latency (2 marks):**

$$
WCIL = T_{latency\_bare} + T_{mask}
$$

$$
WCIL = 280 \, \text{ns} + 800 \, \text{ns} = 1080 \, \text{ns} = 1.08 \, \mu s
$$

**(iii) Latency jitter (2 marks):**

$$
Jitter = N \times T_{clk} + T_{mask} = 3 \times 20 \, \text{ns} + 800 \, \text{ns} = 60 + 800 = 860 \, \text{ns}
$$

**Result Summary:**
- Bare-metal latency: **280 ns**
- RTOS worst-case latency: **1.08 µs**
- Latency jitter: **860 ns**

> The introduction of FreeRTOS nearly **quadrupled** the worst-case latency due to the critical section overhead. For hard real-time sub-millisecond deadlines, this design choice may be acceptable; for sub-100 µs deadlines, a bare-metal approach is recommended.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected Pattern]*

**(a)** Discuss the architectural features of **ARM Cortex-M NVIC** that influence interrupt latency. Compare the latency of a **bare-metal** system versus an **RTOS-based** system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**ARM Cortex-M NVIC Architectural Features (4 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Tail-Chaining:** When one ISR finishes and another is already pending, the CPU *skips* the unstacking-restacking cycle between them, saving up to **30 clock cycles** per back-to-back interrupt. This dramatically reduces *effective* average latency in high-interrupt-load systems.

2. **Late Arrival:** If a higher-priority IRQ arrives while the CPU is stacking registers for a lower-priority ISR, the late-arriving IRQ preempts the stacking itself, *not* the ISR body. This saves up to **12 clock cycles** of latency.

3. **Hardware Stacking of 8 Registers:** The automatic push of `R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR` removes 8 software instructions from the ISR prologue, reducing the minimum latency floor.

4. **Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI) with Priority -2:** The NMI cannot be blocked by `PRIMASK` or `BASEPRI`, providing a deterministic **WCIL ≈ 12 cycles** for catastrophic events (watchdog timeout, power-loss warning).

**Bare-Metal vs. RTOS Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Bare-Metal | RTOS-Based |
|-----------|------------|-------------|
| **WCIL** | 50 – 500 ns (bounded by hardware) | 1 – 50 µs (bounded by critical sections) |
| **Dominant Overhead** | Pipeline drain | Critical section masking |
| **Jitter** | ~10 – 50 ns (low) | ~100 ns – 10 µs (higher) |
| **Predictability** | Excellent (deterministic) | Good (if kernel is certifiable) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (single control loop) | Excellent (multi-task) |
| **Use Case** | Hard real-time, signal processing | Complex multi-feature products |
| **Code Complexity** | Low | High (kernel configuration required) |

---

**(b)** A sensor generates interrupts at a **peak rate of 50 kHz**. The MCU runs at **72 MHz** with a 3-stage pipeline. The ISR takes **600 ns** to execute. Determine if the system can meet its deadlines under **(i)** bare-metal and **(ii)** RTOS (critical section = 1 µs) configurations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $f_{IRQ} = 50$ kHz → Inter-interrupt period $T_{period} = \dfrac{1}{50 \times 10^3} = 20 \, \mu s$
- $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{72 \times 10^6} = 13.89$ ns
- $N = 3$ (pipeline stages)
- $T_{ISR} = 600$ ns

**Deadline condition:** Each interrupt must be fully serviced before the next one arrives, i.e.:

$$
T_{response} = T_{latency} + T_{ISR} \leq T_{period} = 20 \, \mu s
$$

---

**(i) Bare-metal feasibility (3 marks):**

Latency calculation:

$$
T_{latency\_bare} = (N + 11) \times T_{clk} = (3 + 11) \times 13.89 \, \text{ns} = 194.46 \, \text{ns}
$$

Total response time:

$$
T_{response} = 194.46 \, \text{ns} + 600 \, \text{ns} = 794.46 \, \text{ns}
$$

Margin:

$$
\text{Slack} = 20 \, \mu s - 0.794 \, \mu s = 19.21 \, \mu s
$$

**Verdict:** ✅ **Feasible.** The CPU is idle ~96% of the time. Bare-metal is more than sufficient.

---

**(ii) RTOS feasibility (4 marks):**

Worst-case latency with critical section:

$$
WCIL = 194.46 \, \text{ns} + 1000 \, \text{ns} = 1194.46 \, \text{ns} = 1.194 \, \mu s
$$

Worst-case response time:

$$
T_{response} = 1.194 \, \mu s + 0.600 \, \mu s = 1.794 \, \mu s
$$

Slack:

$$
\text{Slack} = 20 \, \mu s - 1.794 \, \mu s = 18.21 \, \mu s
$$

**Verdict:** ✅ **Feasible with 91% idle margin.** Both designs comfortably meet the 20 µs deadline. The RTOS is the better choice if the sensor interrupt is one of *many* concurrent tasks; bare-metal is the better choice if this is the *only* real-time activity (less overhead, simpler certification).

**Final Comparison Table (½ mark):**

| Configuration | $T_{latency}$ | $T_{response}$ | Slack | Feasible? |
|---------------|---------------|----------------|-------|-----------|
| Bare-metal | 194.46 ns | 794.46 ns | 19.21 µs | ✅ Yes |
| RTOS | 1194.46 ns | 1794.46 ns | 18.21 µs | ✅ Yes |

---

## 8. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Interrupt Latency**

- [ ] **Definition:** Time from IRQ assertion → first instruction of the ISR. Not the same as response time.
- [ ] **Master Formula:** $T_{latency} = (N + 11) \times T_{clk} + T_{mask}$ for Cortex-M3/M4.
- [ ] **Five hardware components:** Synchronization → Pipeline drain → Vector fetch → Automatic stacking (8 regs) → Optional critical section wait.
- [ ] **Cortex-M hardware acceleration:** Tail-chaining, late arrival, and pop preemption reduce effective latency in chained ISRs.
- [ ] **WCIL** is the certifiable bound; **BCIL** is the theoretical minimum; **Jitter = WCIL − BCIL**.
- [ ] **RTOS overhead dominates** latency in software-heavy systems; **hardware overhead dominates** in bare-metal.
- [ ] **Critical sections** are the #1 cause of latency inflation in RTOS — keep them short, use lock-free structures when possible.
- [ ] **NMI** has the lowest WCIL (~12 cycles) and cannot be masked by `PRIMASK` / `BASEPRI`.
- [ ] **Interrupt latency ≠ Interrupt response time** — write both definitions explicitly in the exam.
- [ ] **Always include units** in numerical problems; **always draw a timing diagram** in descriptive answers (carries ~2 marks).
- [ ] **Interrupt nesting** allows higher-priority IRQs to preempt lower-priority ISRs, but increases worst-case stack depth.
- [ ] **Cache misses** in Cortex-M7 (Harvard + cache) can add 5–30 cycles of *unpredictable* latency — disable cache for ISR vector region.
- [ ] **JTAG/SWD debug mode** can artificially inflate measured latency by 10–100× — always measure on release-mode hardware.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 Scheme:** The module carries 14 marks in the End Semester Exam (ESE); expect either a pure descriptive (7+7) or descriptive + numerical split.
- [ ] **Real-time bound:** Use Rate Monotonic Analysis (RMA) to verify WCIL ≤ Deadline for hard real-time certification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Processor Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ARM Processor Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine)** is a family of **32-bit / 64-bit Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC)** processor architectures developed by **Arm Holdings (Cambridge, UK)**. It is the most widely deployed processor IP core in the world, dominating mobile, embedded, IoT, and increasingly server markets.

The **ARM architecture** is a **Load-Store architecture** with a fixed instruction length of **32 bits** (in the classic ARM state) or **16 bits** (in the Thumb state). It features a large uniform register file of **37 physical registers** (31 general-purpose + 6 status registers), of which up to **18 registers** are visible to the programmer at any instant depending on the operating mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Students must master the **ARM Programmer's Model**, the **banked registers** used in privileged modes, the **CPSR/SPSR** status registers, the **operational modes** (User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System), and the **three-stage pipeline** (Fetch → Decode → Execute) along with exception handling.

### Core Architectural Features

| Feature | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Architecture Class** | RISC (Load-Store) |
| **Instruction Width** | 32-bit (ARM) / 16-bit (Thumb) |
| **Data Width** | 32-bit (native), 64-bit in ARMv8-A |
| **Register File** | 37 × 32-bit registers |
| **Operating Modes** | 7 privileged + 1 user (8 total in classic ARM) |
| **Pipeline (Classic)** | 3-stage (Fetch, Decode, Execute) |
| **Endianness** | Bi-endian (configurable) |
| **MMU / MPU** | Optional (cortex variants include MPU) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Factory Floor Manager"

Imagine a **highly efficient factory floor** where the foreman (the **ARM processor core**) is responsible for directing workers (functional units) to perform tasks:

- **The General-Purpose Registers (R0–R12)** are like **15 workbenches** placed on the factory floor. The foreman can place raw materials, intermediate products, or tools on any of them. They are **universal** and not tied to one specific job.
- **R13 (Stack Pointer / SP)** is the **"Inbox Tray"** — a special workbench dedicated solely to the current pending paperwork (subroutine return addresses, local variables). Whenever a new task starts, the previous task's tray is filed away into a cabinet.
- **R14 (Link Register / LR)** is the **"Return Address Notepad"** — a sticky note the foreman uses to remember *where to go back* after finishing a delegated task (function call).
- **R15 (Program Counter / PC)** is the **"Pointer to the Current Instruction Manual"** — it always shows which line of the assembly instructions the foreman is currently reading.
- **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** is the **"Dashboard"** showing the factory's state: are the alarms on? (flags), which shift (N, Z, C, V), which foreman is in charge (mode bits), and whether interrupts are allowed (I, F bits).
- **Banked Registers (in FIQ/IRQ modes)** are **private backroom workbenches** — when the foreman steps out to handle an urgent alarm, he doesn't disturb the public floor; he uses a separate set of benches so the main work isn't lost.

> [!TIP]
> This analogy makes the **mode-switching behavior** of ARM intuitive: when an exception (alarm) occurs, the foreman swaps to private banked registers, handles the alarm (ISR), and then returns to the original workbench (original mode) using LR.

---

## 1.3 Why ARM Dominates Embedded Systems

- **Power Efficiency:** ARM cores consume roughly **one-tenth the power** of equivalent-performance x86 CISC cores — critical for battery-powered IoT devices.
- **Scalability:** From **Cortex-M0 (12 K gates)** for sensor nodes to **Cortex-A78 (billions of transistors)** for smartphones.
- **Licensing Model:** Arm **licenses its IP** to hundreds of silicon vendors (Qualcomm, Apple, Samsung, NXP, STMicro) — fostering ecosystem innovation.
- **Predictable Real-Time Behavior:** The simple pipeline and **deterministic interrupt latency** make it ideal for hard real-time control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ARM Programmer's Model (Detailed Breakdown)

The **ARM Programmer's Model** is the central concept of this module. It defines the registers and processor state that software can observe and modify.

### 2.1.1 General-Purpose Registers (R0 – R12)

- **R0 – R12** are **unbanked** registers — the **same physical registers** are visible across all non-FIQ modes.
- **R0 – R3** are conventionally used to pass **function arguments** and **return values** in the **ARM ABI (AAPCS)**.
- **R4 – R11** are **caller-saved** in the standard calling convention; callee functions must preserve them.
- **R12** is the **Intra-procedure-call scratch register (IP)**.

### 2.1.2 Special-Purpose Registers

| Register | Mnemonic | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **R13** | **SP** | Stack Pointer — points to top of active stack |
| **R14** | **LR** | Link Register — holds return address after `BL` |
| **R15** | **PC** | Program Counter — address of current instruction + 8 |
| **CPSR** | — | Current Program Status Register |
| **SPSR_** | — | Saved PSR (one per privileged mode, except User/System) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:** In the **ARM state**, **PC reads as current instruction address + 8 bytes** because the pipeline has already fetched two instructions ahead. This is the single most-asked question in Module 3 viva/papers.

### 2.1.3 The CPSR / SPSR Bit Layout

The CPSR is a 32-bit register with the following critical fields:

| Bits | Field | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **31** | **N (Negative)** | Set if ALU result is negative (MSB = 1) |
| **30** | **Z (Zero)** | Set if ALU result is zero |
| **29** | **C (Carry)** | Set if unsigned overflow / borrow / barrel shifter carry-out |
| **28** | **V (oVerflow)** | Set if signed two's-complement overflow |
| **27 – 25** | — | Reserved / IT block state (Thumb-2) |
| **24** | **J** | Jazelle state (Java bytecode execution) |
| **23 – 20** | — | Reserved |
| **19 – 16** | **GE[3:0]** | Greater-Than-or-Equal flags (SIMD) |
| **15 – 10** | **IT[1:0], IT[7:2]** | Thumb-2 If-Then state |
| **9** | **E** | Endianness (0 = big, 1 = little) |
| **8** | **A** | Imprecise data abort mask |
| **7** | **I** | IRQ disable (1 = masked) |
| **6** | **F** | FIQ disable (1 = masked) |
| **5** | **T** | Thumb state (0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb) |
| **4 – 0** | **M[4:0]** | Mode bits |

### 2.1.4 ARM Operating Modes (M[4:0] Encoding)

| Mode | Encoding | Privileged? | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **USR (User)** | `10000` | No | Normal application execution |
| **FIQ** | `10001` | Yes | Fast Interrupt handling — full banked R8–R14 |
| **IRQ** | `10010` | Yes | Standard Interrupt handling |
| **SVC (Supervisor)** | `10011` | Yes | OS Kernel / SWI (SVC) calls |
| **ABT (Abort)** | `10111` | Yes | Memory access / prefetch aborts |
| **UND (Undefined)** | `11011` | Yes | Undefined instructions / coprocessor emulation |
| **SYS (System)** | `11111` | Yes | Privileged OS tasks (uses User registers) |
| (Reserved) | others | — | Unused — trap to undefined exception |

> [!NOTE]
> The **FIQ mode** is unique because it **banks R8–R14 (total 7 extra registers)**, allowing the Fast Interrupt Service Routine to begin without saving user context — minimizing latency. This is the engineering reason for its existence.

---

## 2.2 Banked Register Mechanism (The Heart of the Module)

When the processor enters a **privileged mode** (e.g., on an IRQ), the **banked copies** of certain registers become visible. The unbanked registers (R0–R7, R15) remain the same physical storage. The banked registers are **physically separate** registers that share register numbers but reside in different hardware cells.

| Mode | Banked Registers |
|---|---|
| FIQ | R8_fiq – R12_fiq, R13_fiq (SP_fiq), R14_fiq (LR_fiq), SPSR_fiq |
| IRQ | R13_irq (SP_irq), R14_irq (LR_irq), SPSR_irq |
| SVC | R13_svc (SP_svc), R14_svc (LR_svc), SPSR_svc |
| ABT | R13_abt (SP_abt), R14_abt (LR_abt), SPSR_abt |
| UND | R13_und (SP_und), R14_und (LR_und), SPSR_und |

**Total physical registers** = (15 unbanked) + (6 status) + (2 × 5 for the 5 non-FIQ privileged modes) + (7 for FIQ) = **37 registers**.

---

## 2.3 The Three-Stage Pipeline (Classic ARM)

```
┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐   ┌────────────┐
│  FETCH  │ → │ DECODE  │ → │  EXECUTE   │
│  (F)    │   │  (D)    │   │    (E)     │
└─────────┘   └─────────┘   └────────────┘
   PC+0         PC+4          PC+8
```

- **Fetch:** Instruction is read from memory at the address in PC; PC increments by 4.
- **Decode:** Instruction is decoded; register operands are read from the register file; branch target is computed.
- **Execute:** ALU operates, memory is accessed (load/store), or branch PC is updated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline Conflict (KTU Pitfall):** Since the **PC reads as PC+8** in ARM state, the value placed in **LR by `BL`** is **PC+4** (the address of the *next* instruction after the branch), not the address of the branch itself. This is because the value read in Decode is already two stages ahead.

### Pipeline Hazards

1. **Data Hazard:** When a register is written in Execute but read in Decode of the *next* instruction (e.g., `ADD R0, R1, R2` immediately followed by `MOV R3, R0`). ARM historically resolved this via **register read-after-write forwarding** in Decode and the **read-during-write** mechanism (the write takes priority on conflict).
2. **Branch Hazard:** A branch is not resolved until Execute. Two pipeline slots (Fetch + Decode of the next two instructions) are wasted. **Branch folding** (in later cores) prefetches from the target address.

---

## 2.4 Exception Handling (Vector Table)

When an exception occurs, ARM:
1. Copies **CPSR** into the **SPSR_** of the new mode.
2. Sets appropriate **CPSR mode bits** (M[4:0]).
3. Sets **PC = vector address** (0x00000000 + offset, or high vectors 0xFFFF0000 + offset).
4. Sets **LR = return address** (PC+4 or PC+8 depending on exception type).
5. Sets **I bit (and F for FIQ)** in CPSR to mask further interrupts.

| Exception | Vector Address | Mode Entered | LR offset |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reset | 0x00000000 | SVC | — |
| Undefined Instruction | 0x00000004 | UND | PC+4 |
| Software Interrupt (SWI) | 0x00000008 | SVC | PC+4 |
| Prefetch Abort | 0x0000000C | ABT | PC+4 |
| Data Abort | 0x00000010 | ABT | PC+8 |
| IRQ | 0x00000018 | IRQ | PC+4 |
| FIQ | 0x0000001C | FIQ | PC+4 |

> [!CAUTION]
> The **Data Abort** uses `PC+8` (not PC+4) because by the time the abort is recognized, Execute has already consumed the instruction and the next instruction has been Fetched. The OS uses LR-8 to re-execute the faulting instruction.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Performance Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Even though ARM architecture is mostly conceptual, the KTU paper frequently tests **performance calculations** based on the architecture. Memorize these formulas.

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ | CPU execution time | seconds |
| 2 | $MIPS = \dfrac{IC}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | MIPS |
| 3 | $CPI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \times CPI_i \big/ IC_{total}$ | Weighted average CPI | cycles/instr |
| 4 | $Speedup = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$ | Amdahl's Speedup | dimensionless |
| 5 | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | Amdahl's Law (parallel fraction $f$, speedup $k$) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $ET_{pipeline} = (n + k - 1) \times T_{stage}$ | Pipeline execution time (n instr, k stages) | seconds |
| 7 | $T_{stage} = \max(T_{F}, T_{D}, T_{E})$ | Pipeline clock period | seconds |
| 8 | $Throughput = \dfrac{n}{ET_{pipeline}}$ | Pipeline throughput | instr/sec |
| 9 | $E_{pipeline} = \dfrac{n \times k}{n + k - 1}$ | Pipeline efficiency | fraction (0–1) |
| 10 | $S_{pipeline} = \dfrac{n \times k}{n + k - 1}$ | Speedup of pipeline over sequential | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> In the formula table above, I deliberately wrote vertical bar alternatives as `f` fractions to avoid pipe conflicts in markdown rendering. When you write a similar answer on paper, use real mathematical notation.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smartphone SoCs** (Apple M-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon, Samsung Exynos) all use ARM Cortex-A custom cores — the architectural features (banked registers, low-latency FIQ) directly enable fast context switching and power-efficient multitasking.
- **Automotive ECUs** (NXP i.MX, Renesas RH850-derived) use Cortex-R cores for hard real-time control; the deterministic pipeline and banked-register interrupt model are essential for airbag and brake-by-wire systems.
- **IoT Edge Nodes** (Cortex-M0/M4) exploit the **Thumb-2 instruction set** to achieve code density approaching 8-bit microcontrollers while retaining 32-bit performance.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ARM Performance from Architecture Parameters

### Problem Statement
A target ARM7TDMI processor runs at a clock frequency of **$f_{clock} = 60 \text{ MHz}$**. A given C program, when compiled for the ARM instruction set, results in an **instruction count $IC = 5 \times 10^{7}$** instructions. Empirically, the average **CPI is 1.2 cycles per instruction**. Compute:
(a) Total CPU execution time
(b) MIPS rating
(c) Speedup if we redesign the CPU to achieve CPI = 1.0 at the same clock

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the clock period from the frequency.**

The clock period is the reciprocal of the clock frequency:

$$T_{clock} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clock}}$$

Substituting $f_{clock} = 60 \text{ MHz} = 60 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$:

$$T_{clock} = \dfrac{1}{60 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{clock} = 1.6667 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} = 16.667 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total CPU execution time.**

Using the standard CPU time formula $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}$:

$$T_{CPU} = (5 \times 10^{7}) \times 1.2 \times (1.6667 \times 10^{-8})$$

$$T_{CPU} = 6.0 \times 10^{7} \times 1.6667 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$T_{CPU} = 1.0000 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the MIPS rating.**

The MIPS rating formula is $MIPS = IC \big/ (T_{CPU} \times 10^{6})$:

$$MIPS = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{7}}{1.0 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$MIPS = 50 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Step 4 — Recompute execution time with CPI = 1.0 (improved design).**

$$T_{CPU,new} = (5 \times 10^{7}) \times 1.0 \times 1.6667 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$T_{CPU,new} = 0.8333 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the speedup.**

$$Speedup = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \dfrac{1.0000}{0.8333} = 1.20$$

This is consistent with the ratio of CPIs: $Speedup = CPI_{old} / CPI_{new} = 1.2 / 1.0 = 1.20$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amdahl's Law Applied to an ARM Pipeline

### Problem Statement
The ARM7TDMI 3-stage pipeline is being upgraded. Currently, **$f = 70\%$ of instructions** benefit from the pipelined implementation (they are regular ALU/data-processing instructions). The remaining **$1 - f = 30\%$** are memory-access or branch instructions that suffer the full pipeline latency. The hardware team proposes a k = 4 stage deeper pipeline. Compute the overall speedup.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State Amdahl's Law.**

$$S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute f = 0.70, k = 4.**

$$S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.70) + \dfrac{0.70}{4}}$$

$$S = \dfrac{1}{0.30 + 0.175}$$

$$S = \dfrac{1}{0.475}$$

$$S = 2.105$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

The naive expectation of a 4× speedup is **unrealistic** because the 30% non-pipelined portion acts as a bottleneck. The practical ceiling is bounded as $k \to \infty$:

$$S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - f} = \dfrac{1}{0.30} = 3.33$$

So no matter how deep the pipeline, **speedup cannot exceed 3.33×** for this instruction mix.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pipeline Throughput and Efficiency

### Problem Statement
A 3-stage ARM pipeline (Fetch, Decode, Execute) processes **n = 1000 instructions**. Each stage has a delay of **$T_{stage} = 20 \text{ ns}$**. Compute:
(a) Total execution time
(b) Throughput
(c) Efficiency
(d) Speedup versus a non-pipelined version

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total execution time using $ET_{pipeline} = (n + k - 1) \times T_{stage}$.**

$$ET_{pipeline} = (1000 + 3 - 1) \times 20 \text{ ns}$$

$$ET_{pipeline} = 1002 \times 20 \text{ ns}$$

$$ET_{pipeline} = 20{,}040 \text{ ns} = 20.04 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 2 — Non-pipelined execution time (each instruction takes k × T_stage).**

$$ET_{non-pipe} = n \times k \times T_{stage} = 1000 \times 3 \times 20 \text{ ns} = 60{,}000 \text{ ns} = 60.00 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 3 — Throughput of the pipeline.**

$$Throughput = \dfrac{n}{ET_{pipeline}} = \dfrac{1000}{20{,}040 \text{ ns}} = 49.9 \times 10^{6} \text{ instr/sec} \approx 49.9 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Step 4 — Efficiency.**

$$E_{pipeline} = \dfrac{n \times k}{n + k - 1} = \dfrac{1000 \times 3}{1002} = 2.994 \text{ (as ratio of ideal)}$$

As a fraction of ideal (k = 3):

$$E_{pipeline} = \dfrac{S_{actual}}{k} = \dfrac{2.994}{3} = 0.998 = 99.8\%$$

**Step 5 — Speedup versus non-pipelined version.**

$$S_{pipeline} = \dfrac{ET_{non-pipe}}{ET_{pipeline}} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{20{,}040} = 2.994 \approx 3.0$$

This confirms the well-known result: for large n, **pipeline speedup approaches k**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ARM vs Typical CISC Processor

| Parameter | ARM7TDMI (RISC) | Intel 8086 (CISC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Load-Store | Register-Memory |
| **Instruction Length** | Fixed 32-bit | Variable 1–15 bytes |
| **Instruction Count** | $\approx 50$ | $\approx 200+$ |
| **Addressing Modes** | 9 (simple) | 24 (complex) |
| **Registers (visible)** | 16 + CPSR | 8 + flags |
| **CPI (typical)** | 1.0 – 1.2 | 4 – 10 |
| **Clock Rate (1995)** | 50 MHz | 100 MHz |
| **MIPS** | $\approx 40$ | $\approx 25$ |
| **Power Consumption** | $\approx 0.1$ W | $\approx 1.5$ W |
| **Code Size (bytes/instr)** | 4.0 | 3.5 – 5.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> Although the CISC chip runs at twice the clock, its CPI is roughly 5× higher, so the RISC ARM achieves a **higher effective MIPS at 1/15th the power** — the central engineering reason RISC dominates embedded systems.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Context Save / Restore on IRQ

A User-mode task is interrupted. Determine the exact register state and the actions ARM takes.

**Initial State (User mode):**
- CPSR = `0x40000010` (Thumb bit = 0, User mode, I = 0, F = 0)
- PC = `0x00008000` (current instruction)
- R13 (SP) = `0x00010000`
- R14 (LR) = `0x00007000`

**IRQ occurs during execution of an instruction at PC = `0x00008000`.**

**Step 1 — ARM completes the current instruction, then:** Copies CPSR → SPSR_irq. So `SPSR_irq = 0x40000010`.

**Step 2 — Update CPSR:**
- Mode bits → `10010` (IRQ). New CPSR = `0x40000092`.
- Sets I bit → 1 (IRQ masked). New CPSR = `0x40000092` (bit 7 = 1).
- Sets T bit unchanged (still 0).

**Step 3 — Set LR_irq = PC + 4 = `0x00008004`** (return address for `SUBS PC, LR_irq, #4` later).

**Step 4 — Set PC = `0x00000018`** (IRQ vector address). New CPSR final = `0x40000092`.

**Step 5 — Switch register banks:**
- User R13 (`0x00010000`) is preserved (banked copy of R13_irq = `?`).
- ISR uses `SP_irq` (separate register).
- LR_irq holds the return address `0x00008004`.

**Step 6 — ISR prologue (typical):**
```
SUB    LR, LR, #4         ; Adjust for pipeline (return to 0x00008000)
STMFD  SP_irq!, {R0-R12, LR}  ; Save user context
... (service the interrupt)
LDMFD  SP_irq!, {R0-R12, LR}  ; Restore
SUBS   PC, LR, #4         ; Atomically restore CPSR from SPSR_irq and return
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Explain the ARM register organization. Discuss the role of banked registers in privileged modes.**

**Model Answer:**

The ARM processor has a register file of **37 × 32-bit physical registers**. The programmer-visible portion depends on the current operating mode:

- **R0–R12**: General-purpose registers. R0–R3 are used for argument passing (AAPCS). R4–R11 are callee-saved.
- **R13 (SP)**: Stack Pointer — points to top of the current mode's stack.
- **R14 (LR)**: Link Register — holds the return address after a `BL` / `BLX` instruction.
- **R15 (PC)**: Program Counter — reads as **current instruction address + 8 bytes** in ARM state (due to the 3-stage pipeline).
- **CPSR**: Current Program Status Register — holds flags (N, Z, C, V), control bits (I, F, T), and mode bits (M[4:0]).
- **SPSR**: Saved PSR — one per privileged mode, holds CPSR at the moment of exception entry.

**Banked Registers** are physically separate register cells that share a register number with the User-mode register, but become visible only when the processor enters a specific privileged mode. **FIQ mode banks R8–R14 (7 registers)** so that the Fast Interrupt Service Routine can begin without pushing user context. **IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND** each bank R13, R14, and SPSR (3 registers each). Banked registers enable **fast, atomic context switching** — critical for real-time embedded systems.

*(3 marks — concept clarity, mention of 37 registers, banked purpose)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of the PC reading as PC+8 in ARM state? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

In ARM state, the three-stage pipeline (Fetch, Decode, Execute) means that when the processor is executing an instruction at address `A`, the instruction at `A+4` is being Decoded, and the instruction at `A+8` is being Fetched. Therefore, when software reads **R15 (PC)**, the value returned is `A+8`, not `A`.

**Significance:**
1. **Compiler correctness** — PC-relative loads (e.g., `LDR R0, [PC, #offset]`) must compensate for the +8 offset.
2. **Link Register value** — `BL subroutine` sets `LR = PC_of_branch + 4`. Since the branch is in Decode when read, the LR is the address of the *next* instruction. The ISR epilogue (`SUBS PC, LR, #4`) subtracts 4 to return to the original.

**Example:** If `BL SUB` is at address `0x00001000` and the next instruction is at `0x00001004`, the `BL` instruction reads PC as `0x00001008` and stores `LR = 0x00001008 - 4 = 0x00001004`. The instruction after `BL` is at `0x00001004`, so LR correctly points to the return address.

*(3 marks — pipeline +8 explanation, example with BL/LR)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### CHOICE A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **ARM Programmer's Model** and the **organization of banked registers** across the seven operating modes. Highlight how FIQ mode is special. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **ARM exception handling mechanism** in detail, including the **vector table**, the **actions taken on exception entry**, and the **return sequence** (including the role of the SPSR). Use a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part A (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

The ARM Programmer's Model comprises the registers that software can directly access. **In User mode**, the visible registers are **R0–R15 (16 registers) plus the CPSR**. The model is shown in the block diagram below:

**Key registers (bullet form, 3 marks):**
- **R0–R12**: General-purpose. R0–R3 are argument/return registers; R4–R11 are callee-saved.
- **R13 (SP)**: Stack pointer — one per mode (banked).
- **R14 (LR)**: Link register — holds return address; banked.
- **R15 (PC)**: Program counter; reads as `PC + 8` in ARM state.
- **CPSR**: Current Program Status Register; banked copy becomes SPSR on exception.

**Operating modes and banked registers (3 marks):**
- **User (USR)**: 10000 — unprivileged; R0–R15, CPSR.
- **FIQ**: 10001 — banks **R8_fiq, R9_fiq, R10_fiq, R11_fiq, R12_fiq, R13_fiq, R14_fiq, SPSR_fiq** (8 banked). This is **special** because it has 7 extra general-purpose registers banked (R8–R14), allowing the FIQ ISR to start without saving User context, achieving **fastest possible interrupt latency**.
- **IRQ**: 10010 — banks R13_irq, R14_irq, SPSR_irq.
- **SVC (Supervisor)**: 10011 — banks R13_svc, R14_svc, SPSR_svc. Entered on reset and SWI.
- **ABT (Abort)**: 10111 — banks R13_abt, R14_abt, SPSR_abt. Handles data and prefetch aborts.
- **UND (Undefined)**: 11011 — banks R13_und, R14_und, SPSR_und.
- **SYS (System)**: 11111 — privileged mode using User register set.

**Total physical registers** = 15 unbanked + 1 CPSR + 5×(2) for non-FIQ privileged + 7 for FIQ = 37 registers. (1 mark for total count)

---

#### Part A (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Vector table (2 marks):** Located at address `0x00000000` (or `0xFFFF0000` if high vectors configured). Contains branch instructions to the respective exception handlers. The seven vectors are: Reset (0x00), Undefined (0x04), SWI (0x08), Prefetch Abort (0x0C), Data Abort (0x10), Reserved (0x14), IRQ (0x18), FIQ (0x1C).

**Exception entry actions (3 marks):** When an exception occurs, ARM hardware atomically:
1. Copies `CPSR → SPSR_<mode>` (preserves the caller's flags and state).
2. Updates CPSR: sets `M[4:0]` to the new mode; sets the **I-bit = 1** (masks IRQ), and **F-bit = 1** for FIQ; clears the T-bit if applicable.
3. Sets `LR_<mode> = PC + 4` (or `PC + 8` for Data Abort — explains why in point 5).
4. Sets `PC = vector_address` — the processor branches to the handler.
5. **Why PC+8 for Data Abort:** The aborted instruction has already executed; PC has advanced by +8 by the time the abort is recognized. The OS re-runs it by using `LR - 8`.

**Return sequence (2 marks):** The exception handler restores User context with `LDM` then executes `SUBS PC, LR, #4`. The `S` (with `S` suffix) atomically:
- Loads PC from LR (return to caller).
- Copies `SPSR_<mode> → CPSR` (restores mode, flags, I/F state).

**Example:** An IRQ occurs while executing an instruction at `PC = 0x1000`. ARM sets `LR_irq = 0x1004`. The handler does its work and ends with `SUBS PC, LR_irq, #4` → PC becomes `0x1000`, CPSR is restored from `SPSR_irq`, and execution continues seamlessly.

---

### CHOICE B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]

**Question B (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Describe the **three-stage pipeline** of the classic ARM processor. Explain pipeline hazards with examples and discuss how ARM mitigates them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DSP application runs on an ARM7TDMI. The benchmark yields **$IC = 4.5 \times 10^{7}$** instructions, **CPI = 1.4**, and **clock frequency = 50 MHz**. Compute (i) execution time, (ii) MIPS rating, (iii) speedup achieved if a designer reduces CPI to 1.1 at the same clock, and (iv) verify the result using Amdahl's Law assuming the **parallelizable fraction $f = 0.6$** and **$k = 1.4$**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part B (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Three-stage pipeline (3 marks):**

The classic ARM7TDMI pipeline has three stages:
1. **Fetch (F):** Reads instruction from memory at address `PC`. PC is incremented by 4.
2. **Decode (D):** Instruction is decoded. Register operands are read from the register file. For data-processing instructions, the **barrel shifter** pre-processes one operand. Branch target is computed.
3. **Execute (E):** The ALU operates. For load/store, the memory address is computed and the bus cycle is initiated. For branches, the PC is updated with the target.

**Pipeline hazards (3 marks):**
- **Data hazard:** Occurs when an instruction produces a result in `E` that the *next* instruction needs in its `D`. Example:
  ```
  ADD R0, R1, R2   ; R0 written in E of cycle 1
  MOV R3, R0       ; R0 read in D of cycle 2
  ```
  ARM resolves this using the **register read-during-write** mechanism: the new value is forwarded to the read port in Decode, so no stall is needed.
- **Branch hazard:** The branch outcome is known only in `E`. The two speculatively fetched instructions in `F` and `D` must be discarded. **Branch folding** (in later cores) prefetches from the target during Decode.
- **Structural hazard:** ARM7's single memory bus means load/store in `E` cannot coincide with fetch in `F` of the next instruction — the fetched instruction is stalled by one cycle.

**Mitigations (1 mark):**
- Operand forwarding (bypass paths).
- Read-during-write priority.
- Branch prediction in later cores.
- Separate instruction and data memories (Harvard architecture) in Cortex-M to remove structural hazards.

---

#### Part B (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Execution time (2 marks):**

$$T_{clock} = \dfrac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock} = (4.5 \times 10^{7}) \times 1.4 \times 2 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$T_{CPU} = 6.3 \times 10^{7} \times 2 \times 10^{-8} = 1.26 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) MIPS rating (2 marks):**

$$MIPS = \dfrac{IC}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{7}}{1.26 \times 10^{6}} = 35.71 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Cross-check:** $MIPS = f_{clock} / (CPI \times 10^{6}) = (50 \times 10^{6}) / (1.4 \times 10^{6}) = 35.71$ MIPS ✓

**(iii) Speedup for CPI = 1.1 (2 marks):**

$$T_{CPU,new} = (4.5 \times 10^{7}) \times 1.1 \times 2 \times 10^{-8} = 0.99 \text{ s}$$

$$Speedup = \dfrac{1.26}{0.99} = 1.2727$$

This matches the ratio $CPI_{old}/CPI_{new} = 1.4/1.1 = 1.2727$. ✓

**(iv) Amdahl's Law verification (1 mark):**

$$S_{Amdahl} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.6) + \dfrac{0.6}{1.4}} = \dfrac{1}{0.4 + 0.4286} = \dfrac{1}{0.8286} = 1.207$$

The two speedup values are **close** (1.27 vs 1.21) but not identical because Amdahl's Law aggregates over a different parameter (parallel fraction) than the CPI reduction. Both confirm a **modest ~1.2× improvement**, illustrating diminishing returns from incremental optimization.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
>
> 1. **PC value pitfall (loses 2 marks):** Writing `PC = current address + 4` instead of `+ 8`. The `+8` arises only in **ARM state**; in **Thumb state** it is `+4`. Always state the state explicitly.
> 2. **LR offset pitfall (loses 2 marks):** Confusing the LR value set by `BL` with the value at exception entry. `BL` sets LR = `PC + 4` (no exception). An IRQ sets LR_irq = `PC + 4` of the *faulting* instruction. A Data Abort sets LR_abt = `PC + 8` (different exception type).
> 3. **CPSR field pitfall (loses 1 mark):** Forgetting that the **I and F bits** must be set on exception entry to **mask further interrupts** of the same or lower priority. Always mention them.
> 4. **Pipeline throughput pitfall (loses 1 mark):** Forgetting that pipeline throughput depends on **CPI, not clock period alone**. A deeper pipeline often *increases* clock frequency but may *increase* CPI due to hazards.
> 5. **MIPS miscalculation (loses 1 mark):** Confusing `IC` (instruction count) with `instruction mix`. Always convert clock period to seconds before multiplying.
