> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 (ARM Processor Architecture):**

- [x] ARM is a **32-bit RISC, Load-Store** architecture with **fixed 32-bit** instructions (Thumb = 16-bit).
- [x] **37 physical registers**: R0–R15 + CPSR + 5×(SPSR + banked R13 + banked R14) + 5 extra FIQ (R8–R12).
- [x] **8 operating modes**: USR, FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND, SYS, (Reserved).
- [x] **FIQ is special** — banks R8–R14 (7 extra registers) for fastest context save.
- [x] **PC reads as PC+8** in ARM state due to the 3-stage pipeline.
- [x] **CPSR** contains flags (N, Z, C, V), control (I, F, T, A, E, J), and mode (M[4:0]).
- [x] **Vector table** is at `0x00000000` (or `0xFFFF0000`); 8 entries × 4 bytes = 32 bytes.
- [x] **On exception:** CPSR → SPSR, mode changes, I/F bits set, LR set, PC = vector.
- [x] **Data Abort** sets LR = PC+8 (not PC+4) — kernel re-runs the faulting instruction.
- [x] **3-stage pipeline**: Fetch → Decode → Execute. Hazards mitigated by operand forwarding and read-during-write.
- [x] **Performance formulas**: $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}$, $MIPS = f/CPI$, $S_{Amdahl} = 1 / [(1-f) + f/k]$.
- [x] **End of program in pipeline**: $ET = (n + k - 1) \times T_{stage}$; throughput $\to$ $1/T_{stage}$ for large $n$.
- [x] **ARM is dominant in embedded** because of power efficiency (CISC's 8086 at 100 MHz ≈ 25 MIPS; ARM7 at 50 MHz ≈ 40 MIPS at 1/15th the power).
- [x] **Thumb-2** combines 16-bit and 32-bit instructions for code density + performance.
- [x] **AAPCS calling convention**: R0–R3 for args/returns; R4–R11 callee-saved; R12 IP scratch.
- [x] **Return from exception** uses `SUBS PC, LR, #4` — atomically restores PC and CPSR from SPSR.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Acorn RISC Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Acorn RISC Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Acorn RISC Machine (ARM)** is a family of **32-bit (and later 64-bit) Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC) processor architectures** originally designed by **Acorn Computers Ltd.** in the United Kingdom, with the first ARM1 prototype developed in **1985**. The acronym "ARM" originally stood for **"Acorn RISC Machine"**, and was later renamed to **"Advanced RISC Machine"** when ARM Holdings was spun off as a separate company in **1990**.

The ARM architecture is the most widely deployed processor core family in the world, dominating the **embedded systems**, **mobile computing**, **IoT**, and increasingly the **server and desktop** markets due to its exceptional **power efficiency** and **performance-per-watt** characteristics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping (PBEVT504 – Module 3):**
> The topic "The Acorn RISC Machine" establishes the foundational understanding of RISC processor philosophy that is later applied to study **ARM processor architecture**, **register banks**, **programmer's model**, **pipeline**, and **interrupt handling** in the broader context of embedded controller design.

## 1.2 Architectural Philosophy

The ARM processor was conceived around three core RISC principles:

1. **Large Register File:** A relatively large number of general-purpose registers (typically **16 visible registers** in user mode for ARM state, and **31 registers** in newer ARMv8/AArch64) to keep operands in fast on-chip storage.
2. **Load/Store Architecture:** Arithmetic and logical operations operate exclusively on **register operands**; memory access is permitted only via dedicated **load (LDR)** and **store (STR)** instructions.
3. **Fixed-Length, Decoded Instructions:** Most ARM instructions are **32 bits wide** (with Thumb-2 extensions providing 16-bit encodings), enabling single-cycle decode and simplified pipelining.

## 1.3 Historical Evolution

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| **1983** | Acorn begins RISC processor project | The "Acorn RISC Machine" project initiated |
| **1985** | ARM1 silicon fabricated | First ARM prototype (26-bit address space) |
| **1987** | ARM2 released | First commercial ARM, ~1 MIPS @ 8 MHz |
| **1990** | ARM6 / ARM Holdings founded | Acorn, Apple, VLSI form ARM Ltd. |
| **1992** | ARM7TDMI | Thumb + Debug + Multiplier + ICE; dominant in mobile phones |
| **1995** | ARM710 | Used in Apple Newton (early PDA) |
| **1998** | ARM9TDMI | 5-stage pipeline, Harvard architecture |
| **2001** | ARMv6 / ARM11 | SIMD media instructions |
| **2004** | ARMv7-A (Cortex-A8) | NEON SIMD, Jazelle RCT, dual-issue |
| **2011** | ARMv8-A (AArch64) | First 64-bit ARM (Apple A7 in iPhone 5s) |
| **2020+** | ARMv9 / Cortex-A710 | SVE2, enhanced security (PAC, MTE) |

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Specialized Workshop" vs. "General Hardware Store":**
>
> Imagine you are a carpenter who needs to build furniture every day.
> - A **CISC processor** is like a giant hardware store with thousands of specialized power tools — each complex tool can perform one giant operation, but most of the time you only need a hammer.
> - An **ARM (RISC) processor** is like a **specialized workshop** with only a small set of *simple, fast, highly optimized hand tools* (basic add, subtract, shift, load, store) and a **large workbench (register file)** where you keep your materials within arm's reach. Because each tool is simple and the workbench is huge, you rarely have to walk back to the (slow) storage room (memory). The result is **smaller chip area, lower power, and faster sustained throughput** — exactly what a battery-powered embedded device needs.

## 1.5 Why ARM Dominates Embedded Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** ARM's dominance in embedded systems is not accidental — it is the direct consequence of **three engineering realities**:
> 1. **Power Efficiency:** ARM cores typically consume **less than 1 W** even in multi-core configurations, allowing fanless, battery-powered designs.
> 2. **Licensable IP Model:** Unlike Intel/AMD, ARM does not manufacture chips; it *licenses its designs*, leading to thousands of variants optimized for specific applications.
> 3. **Mature Toolchain:** GCC, Keil MDK, IAR, LLVM/Clang, and ARM's own DS-5 / Arm Development Studio all provide first-class support.

By the year **2024**, more than **250 billion** ARM chips had been shipped, making ARM the **most produced processor architecture in human history**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The RISC Design Philosophy Underlying ARM

The ARM processor is the canonical industrial implementation of the **Berkeley RISC** and **Stanford MIPS** research philosophies of the early 1980s. The original design team — led by **Sophie Wilson** and **Steve Furber** at Acorn — distilled RISC down to five operational rules that still govern ARM cores today:

### The Five Pillars of ARM's RISC Design

1. **Single-cycle execution for most instructions** — Each simple instruction executes in one clock cycle.
2. **Load/Store memory model** — No instruction can directly access memory except LDR and STR (and their variants).
3. **Large, uniform register file** — 16 user-visible 32-bit registers (R0–R15) in classical ARM state.
4. **Fixed instruction format** — Every (non-Thumb) instruction is exactly 32 bits, simplifying the decoder.
5. **Hard-wired control logic** — Instead of microcode ROM, the control unit uses PLA/combinational logic, dramatically reducing die area and power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Question Anchor:** When asked "Why is ARM considered a RISC architecture despite its growing instruction set?", answer: *"ARM retains the RISC philosophy of simple, regular, single-cycle base instructions, even though modern ARMv8/v9 has added more specialized instructions. The core pipeline and register model remain fundamentally RISC."*

## 2.2 ARM Processor Family Classification

ARM groups its cores into three families based on **application profile**:

| Profile | Target Use | Example Cores | Key Features |
|---------|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **Cortex-A (Application)** | High-performance apps, Linux | Cortex-A53, A72, A78, X1 | MMU, NEON, multi-core, 64-bit (AArch64) |
| **Cortex-R (Real-time)** | Hard real-time, automotive | Cortex-R4, R5, R7, R8 | Locked caches, ECC, fast IRQ response |
| **Cortex-M (Microcontroller)** | MCU replacement, IoT | Cortex-M0, M3, M4, M7, M33 | Thumb-2 only, NVIC, low power |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** For the PBEVT504 course, the primary focus is on **Cortex-M** (used in STM32, LPC, Tiva-C, NRF52 boards studied in labs) and the **classical ARM7/ARM9 programmer's model** that establishes the foundation for all later ARM variants.

## 2.3 The ARM Programmer's Model (Classical ARM7/ARM9)

The classical ARM state has **37 registers**, of which only **16 are visible at any time** depending on the processor mode:

| Register | Alias | Role |
|----------|-------|------|
| **R0–R12** | — | General-purpose 32-bit registers |
| **R13** | **SP** | Stack Pointer (banked per mode) |
| **R14** | **LR** | Link Register (return address for subroutine calls) |
| **R15** | **PC** | Program Counter |
| **CPSR** | — | Current Program Status Register (flags, mode bits) |
| **SPSR** | — | Saved PSR (on exception entry) |

### Seven Processor Modes
- **User (USR):** Normal unprivileged mode
- **FIQ:** Fast Interrupt (banked R8–R14)
- **IRQ:** Normal Interrupt
- **Supervisor (SVC):** OS / SWI calls
- **Abort (ABT):** Memory access faults
- **Undefined (UND):** Undefined instructions
- **System (SYS):** Privileged user mode (ARMv4+)

## 2.4 The ARM Instruction Set (Classical ARM)

ARM instructions fall into **six broad classes**:

### (a) Data Processing Instructions
Operate on registers only. Examples: `ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, `ORR`, `EOR`, `MOV`, `CMP`, `TST`, `MUL`, `LSL`, `LSR`, `ASR`, `ROR`.

### (b) Data Transfer (Load/Store) Instructions
`LDR`, `STR`, `LDRB`, `STRB`, `LDM`, `STM`, `LDRH`, `STRH` — only mechanism to access memory.

### (c) Branch Instructions
`B`, `BL` (Branch and Link), `BX` (Branch and exchange to Thumb), `BLX`.

### (d) Multiply Instructions
`MUL`, `MLA`, `MULL`, `MLAL`, `SMULL`, `SMLAL`.

### (e) Status Register Access
`MRS` (Move from PSR), `MSR` (Move to PSR).

### (f) Exception/Software Interrupt
`SWI` (Software Interrupt — now called `SVC` in ARMv7), `BKPT`.

## 2.5 Addressing Modes (Seven Modes in Classical ARM)

| Mode | Format | Example |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Immediate** | `#imm` | `MOV R0, #0xFF` |
| **Register** | `Rn` | `ADD R0, R1` |
| **Register indirect** | `[Rn]` | `LDR R0, [R1]` |
| **Register with offset** | `[Rn, ±Rm]` | `LDR R0, [R1, R2]` |
| **Pre-indexed** | `[Rn, ±Rm]!` | `LDR R0, [R1, #4]!` |
| **Post-indexed** | `[Rn], ±Rm` | `LDR R0, [R1], #4` |
| **Scaled register** | `[Rn, ±Rm, shift]` | `LDR R0, [R1, R2, LSL #2]` |

## 2.6 The ARM Pipeline

The classical ARM7 uses a **3-stage pipeline**:

```
IF  →  ID  →  EX
 |       |       |
 v       v       v
Fetch  Decode  Execute
(Mem)   Reg    (Write-back later)
```

The ARM9 uses a **5-stage pipeline** (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB), enabling higher clock frequencies.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Performance Equations (Mandatory for KTU ESE Numerical Questions):**

### (1) CPU Execution Time (Basic)

$$T_{\text{CPU}} \;=\; N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{CPU}}$ = Total CPU execution time
- $N_{\text{inst}}$ = Total instruction count
- $\text{CPI}$ = Average cycles per instruction
- $T_{\text{clk}}$ = Clock period = $1/f_{\text{clk}}$

### (2) Clock Frequency–Period Relation

$$f_{\text{clk}} \;=\; \frac{1}{T_{\text{clk}}}$$

### (3) Speedup from Pipeline Depth

$$S_{\text{pipeline}} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{non-pipelined}}}{T_{\text{pipelined}}} \;\longrightarrow\; \lim_{n \to \infty} S_{\text{max}} \;=\; k$$

Where $k$ is the number of pipeline stages (ideal case, no hazards).

### (4) Amdahl's Law (Used When Adding Hardware Units)

$$S_{\text{overall}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\bigl(1 - f\bigr) \;+\; \dfrac{f}{k}}$$

Where:
- $f$ = fraction of execution time improved by the enhancement
- $k$ = speedup of the enhanced portion

### (5) Effective CPI with Pipeline Hazards

$$\text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} \;=\; \text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} \;+\; \sum_{i} \bigl( \text{stall}_i \times \text{freq}_i \bigr)$$

Where each $\text{stall}_i$ is the cycle penalty of hazard type $i$ and $\text{freq}_i$ is how often it occurs per instruction.

### (6) MIPS Rating

$$\text{MIPS} \;=\; \frac{f_{\text{clk}}\,(\text{in MHz})}{\text{CPI}}$$

### (7) Power Dissipation (Dynamic)

$$P_{\text{dyn}} \;=\; \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{\text{DD}}^2 \cdot f_{\text{clk}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-World Insight:** The cubic relation $P \propto V^2 f$ is *exactly* why ARM processors historically ran at **modest clock frequencies (~1 GHz for mobile)** while Intel x86 pushed **3–5 GHz**. ARM's high **IPC (Instructions Per Cycle)** from its deep pipeline + wide issue + NEON SIMD achieves comparable performance at **a fraction of the power**, which is the fundamental reason every smartphone on Earth uses ARM.

### ⚠ Markdown Table Syntax Note (per developer protocol)
The vertical pipe `|` has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in all formulas above to avoid breaking the table layout.

---

### Summary Cheat-Sheet Table

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| $T_{\text{CPU}}$ | CPU execution time | seconds |
| $N_{\text{inst}}$ | Instruction count | instructions |
| $\text{CPI}$ | Cycles per instruction | cycles/instruction |
| $T_{\text{clk}}$ | Clock period | seconds |
| $f_{\text{clk}}$ | Clock frequency | Hz |
| $k$ | Pipeline depth / speedup factor | dimensionless |
| $V_{\text{DD}}$ | Supply voltage | Volts |
| $\alpha$ | Switching activity factor | 0 to 1 |
| $C$ | Load capacitance | Farads |

## 2.8 Why This Matters in Engineering

The Acorn RISC Machine's design choices ripple through every modern engineering domain:

- **IoT & Wearables:** Battery life of 5–10 years is achievable because of ARM's $V^2 f$ scaling.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Cortex-R cores meet ASIL-D safety because of deterministic interrupt latency.
- **Edge ML:** Cortex-M55 + Ethos-U55 enables on-device TinyML inference.
- **Data Centers (AWS Graviton):** ARM Neoverse cores deliver **40% better perf/W** than x86 in cloud workloads.
- **Aerospace & Defence:** SPARC and RAD750 are being supplemented by ARM in newer satellites (NASA's next-gen spacecraft processors).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Pipeline Speedup with Stalls

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU Typical 14-Mark Part (a)):**
> An ARM7TDMI processor executes a benchmark of **N = 1,000,000 instructions** with an **ideal CPI = 1**. The 3-stage pipeline incurs a structural hazard (data memory access) causing **1 bubble cycle for every 4th instruction**. The clock frequency is **$f_{\text{clk}} = 50\,\text{MHz}$**.
> Calculate: **(i)** Effective CPI, **(ii)** Total CPU execution time, **(iii)** MIPS rating, **(iv)** Percentage slowdown vs. an ideal non-stalling pipeline.

### Given Data

$$
N_{\text{inst}} = 1{,}000{,}000,\quad \text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} = 1,\quad f_{\text{clk}} = 50\,\text{MHz}
$$

$$
\text{Stall}_{\text{data}} = 1\ \text{cycle per 4 instructions} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{stall frequency} = \tfrac{1}{4}
$$

### Step 1 — Calculate Effective CPI

The hazard adds a fractional number of stall cycles *per instruction*:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} + \sum_i (\text{stall}_i \times \text{freq}_i)
$$

Substitute the stall and frequency:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} = 1 + (1 \times \tfrac{1}{4})
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} = 1.25\ \text{cycles/instruction}}
$$

> **Logic:** Each instruction on average takes 1.25 clock ticks — the extra 0.25 comes from the bubble inserted once every 4 instructions.

### Step 2 — Calculate CPU Execution Time

Use the fundamental CPU time equation:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

Since $T_{\text{clk}} = 1 / f_{\text{clk}}$:

$$
T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz}} = 20\ \text{ns}
$$

Substitute:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 1.25 \times 20 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 1.25 \times 2 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{CPU}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{s} = 25\ \text{ms}}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate MIPS Rating

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}\ (\text{MHz})}{\text{CPI}_{\text{effective}}}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{50}{1.25}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MIPS} = 40\ \text{million instructions/sec}}
$$

### Step 4 — Percentage Slowdown vs. Ideal

Ideal CPU time (no stalls):

$$
T_{\text{ideal}} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 1.0 \times 20\ \text{ns} = 20\ \text{ms}
$$

Slowdown ratio:

$$
\text{Slowdown} = \frac{T_{\text{CPU}}}{T_{\text{ideal}}} = \frac{25\ \text{ms}}{20\ \text{ms}} = 1.25
$$

Percentage slowdown:

$$
\% \text{ Slowdown} = (1.25 - 1) \times 100\% = 25\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{The pipeline executes } 25\% \text{ slower than the ideal due to structural hazards.}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Amdahl's Law for ARM SIMD Acceleration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU Typical 14-Mark Part (b)):**
> An ARM Cortex-A8 processor spends **60% of its execution time** performing integer ALU operations that can be accelerated by a **NEON SIMD unit** with a speedup factor of **k = 8×**. Compute the overall speedup of the system.

### Given

$$
f = 0.60,\quad k = 8
$$

### Step 1 — Identify Enhanced and Unenhanced Portions

The unenhanced fraction (not accelerated):

$$
1 - f = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40
$$

The enhanced fraction time becomes:

$$
\frac{f}{k} = \frac{0.60}{8} = 0.075
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}
$$

Substitute:

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{0.40 + 0.075} = \frac{1}{0.475}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{overall}} \approx 2.105\times}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

> **Conclusion:** Even though NEON is 8× faster on the accelerated portion, the overall system speedup is **only ~2.1×** because **40% of the time is spent on code that NEON does not accelerate**. This is a classic illustration of Amdahl's Law: *"the reward of optimization is bounded by the unoptimized fraction."*

### Step 4 — Theoretical Maximum Speedup

If the accelerated fraction could approach 100% ($f \to 1$):

$$
S_{\text{max}} = \lim_{f \to 1} \frac{1}{(1 - f) + f / k} = \frac{1}{1/k} = k = 8
$$

But realistically with 40% non-accelerated baseline code:

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{max, realistic}} \approx 2.5\times \text{ (if even 100\% of optimizable code is identified)}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ARM Family Comparison

| Feature | ARM7TDMI | ARM9TDMI | Cortex-M3 | Cortex-M4 | Cortex-A53 | Cortex-A78 |
|---------|----------|----------|-----------|-----------|------------|------------|
| **Pipeline Stages** | 3 | 5 | 3 | 3 | 8 (in-order) | 11 (OoO) |
| **ISA Width** | 32-bit ARM | 32-bit ARM | Thumb-2 (16/32) | Thumb-2 + DSP | AArch32/AArch64 | AArch64 |
| **Architecture** | Von Neumann | Harvard | Harvard | Harvard | Modified Harvard | Modified Harvard |
| **MMU/MPU** | No / No | Yes / No | No / Yes (opt.) | No / Yes (opt.) | Yes | Yes |
| **Cache** | None | None | None | None | L1+I/D, L2 | L1+L2+L3 |
| **Typical Clock** | 50–80 MHz | 150–220 MHz | 50–100 MHz | 100–180 MHz | 1.0–1.5 GHz | 2.4–3.0 GHz |
| **MIPS @ Max** | ~60 MIPS | ~220 MIPS | ~90 MIPS | ~180 MIPS | ~2000 MIPS | ~8000 MIPS |
| **Interrupts** | IRQ + FIQ | IRQ + FIQ | NVIC (240 IRQs) | NVIC | GICv2/v3/v4 | GICv3 |
| **Typical Use** | Older MCU | Mobile (pre-smartphone) | STM32 F1/L1 | STM32 F4, DSP | Raspberry Pi 3, mid-range phones | Premium phones |
| **Power (active)** | ~0.05 mW/MHz | ~0.10 mW/MHz | ~0.03 mW/MHz | ~0.04 mW/MHz | ~0.5 mW/MHz | ~1.0 mW/MHz |

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 3 — Power Dissipation Across Voltage/Frequency Scaling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A Cortex-M3 chip operates at $V = 1.2\,\text{V}$, $f = 72\,\text{MHz}$. If we scale down to $V' = 0.9\,\text{V}$ and $f' = 48\,\text{MHz}$, by what factor does dynamic power decrease? (Assume $\alpha, C$ unchanged.)

### Solution

The dynamic power ratio is:

$$
\frac{P'}{P} = \frac{\alpha C (V')^2 f'}{\alpha C V^2 f} = \left(\frac{V'}{V}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{f'}{f}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\frac{P'}{P} = \left(\frac{0.9}{1.2}\right)^2 \times \left(\frac{48}{72}\right)
$$

Compute voltage ratio squared:

$$
\left(\frac{0.9}{1.2}\right)^2 = (0.75)^2 = 0.5625
$$

Compute frequency ratio:

$$
\frac{48}{72} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667
$$

Combine:

$$
\frac{P'}{P} = 0.5625 \times 0.6667 \approx 0.375
$$

$$
\boxed{P' \approx 0.375\,P \;\Longrightarrow\; 62.5\% \text{ power reduction}}
$$

> **Engineering Takeaway:** Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (**DVFS**) is exactly this principle, and is the *single most important* reason ARM processors dominate battery-powered IoT and mobile devices.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 ARM Historical Evolution Timeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
timeline
    title Evolution of the Acorn RISC Machine (1983 to Present)
    1983 : Acorn RISC Project Initiated
    1985 : ARM1 First Silicon Prototype
    1987 : ARM2 First Commercial Processor
    1992 : ARM7TDMI - Thumb and Debug Extensions
    1998 : ARM9TDMI - 5 Stage Pipeline Harvard
    2004 : ARM11 and Cortex Family Introduced
    2011 : ARMv8 - AArch64 64 bit ISA (Apple A7)
    2020 : ARMv9 - SVE2 and Enhanced Security
    2024 : Cortex X4 and Neoverse V3 in Production
```

## 4.2 Classical ARM7TDMI Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Instruction Memory<br/>ROM or Flash] -->|Fetch 32 bit| B[IF Stage<br/>Instruction Fetch]
    B --> C[ID Stage<br/>Instruction Decode and<br/>Operand Read from Register Bank]
    C --> D[EX Stage<br/>ALU Shift Multiply<br/>and Write Back]
    D --> E[Data Memory<br/>RAM]
    D --> F[Bank 2 Registers<br/>R8 to R14 FIQ Mode]
    D --> G[CPSR and SPSR<br/>Status Registers]
    H[Write Back Bus] --> D
    E -->|Load Data| D
    I[Exception Logic<br/>IRQ FIQ SWI] --> D
```

## 4.3 ARM Cortex-M3 NVIC and Interrupt Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[External Interrupt<br/>Source Pin] --> B[NVIC<br/>Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller]
    C[Peripheral Interrupt<br/>Timer UART ADC] --> B
    D[Software Generated<br/>Interrupt] --> B
    B --> E{Is Interrupt<br/>Enabled?}
    E -->|No| F[Ignore]
    E -->|Yes| G{Priority<br/>Greater than<br/>Current Base?}
    G -->|No| F
    G -->|Yes| H[Stack Push<br/>R0 R1 R2 R3 R12 LR PC PSR]
    H --> I[Vector Table<br/>Read Handler Address]
    I --> J[Jump to ISR<br/>Interrupt Service Routine]
    J --> K[Execute Interrupt<br/>Service Routine Body]
    K --> L[Restore Registers<br/>from Stack]
    L --> M[Return from<br/>Interrupt]
    M --> N[Resume Main<br/>Program Execution]
```

## 4.4 ARM Register Banking Across Modes

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph USR[User Mode]
        R0USR[R0]
        R1USR[R1]
        R12USR[R12]
        R13USR[R13 SP]
        R14USR[R14 LR]
    end

    subgraph FIQ[FIQ Mode Banked]
        R8FIQ[R8 FIQ]
        R9FIQ[R9 FIQ]
        R10FIQ[R10 FIQ]
        R11FIQ[R11 FIQ]
        R12FIQ[R12 FIQ]
        R13FIQ[R13 SP FIQ]
        R14FIQ[R14 LR FIQ]
    end

    subgraph IRQ[IRQ Mode Banked]
        R13IRQ[R13 SP IRQ]
        R14IRQ[R14 LR IRQ]
    end

    subgraph SVC[SVC Mode Banked]
        R13SVC[R13 SP SVC]
        R14SVC[R14 LR SVC]
    end

    R0USR -.Shared.-> R8FIQ
    R0USR -.Shared.-> R13IRQ
    R0USR -.Shared.-> R13SVC
```

## 4.5 RISC Pipeline Hazard Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Fetched IF] --> B{Hazard<br/>Detected?}
    B -->|No Hazard| C[Proceed to ID Decode]
    B -->|Data Hazard RAW| D[Insert 1 Bubble Cycle<br/>Forward from EX MEM WB]
    B -->|Control Hazard Branch| E[Predict Not Taken<br/>or Stall 1 Cycle]
    B -->|Structural Hazard| F[Stall Pipeline<br/>Wait for Resource]
    D --> G[Re execute with Forwarded Operand]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    C --> H[ID Decodes<br/>Reads Register Bank]
    G --> H
    H --> I[EX Executes<br/>ALU or Memory Address]
    I --> J[Memory Access or<br/>Write Back Stage]
```

## 4.6 ARM vs CISC Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ARM_RISC[RISC ARM Approach]
        A1[Simple<br/>Fixed Length<br/>Instructions] --> A2[Large Register File<br/>37 Registers]
        A2 --> A3[Load Store<br/>Architecture]
        A3 --> A4[Hardwired<br/>Control]
        A4 --> A5[Low Power<br/>Small Die Area]
    end

    subgraph CISC[Traditional CISC x86 Approach]
        B1[Complex<br/>Variable Length<br/>Instructions] --> B2[Smaller Register File<br/>8 to 16 GPRs]
        B2 --> B3[Memory Operand<br/>Allowed]
        B3 --> B4[Microcoded<br/>Control]
        B4 --> B5[Higher Power<br/>Larger Die Area]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
