# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

### Question 1: Define the Acorn RISC Machine and state any four of its key features.

`[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]`

> **Model Answer:**
> The Acorn RISC Machine (ARM) is a family of 32-bit (and now 64-bit) **Reduced Instruction Set Computer** processor architectures originally developed by Acorn Computers Ltd. in 1985 for use in personal computers and later adopted universally in embedded systems.
>
> Four key features:
> 1. **Load/Store architecture** — only LDR and STR access memory.
> 2. **Large uniform register file** — 16 user-visible 32-bit registers (R0–R15).
> 3. **Fixed 32-bit instruction length** — simplifies decoding.
> 4. **Hard-wired control logic** — no microcode, saving silicon area and power.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between RISC and CISC processors.

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

> **Model Answer:**

| Parameter | RISC (ARM) | CISC (x86) |
|-----------|------------|------------|
| Instruction set size | Small, simple | Large, complex |
| Instruction length | Fixed 32 bits | Variable 1–15 bytes |
| Memory access | Only via Load/Store | Any instruction can access memory |
| Register file | Large (16–31 registers) | Small (8–16 registers) |
| Control logic | Hardwired | Microcoded |
| Cycles per instruction | Mostly 1 CPI | Multi-cycle |
| Power consumption | Very low | Higher |
| Code size | Larger | Compact |
| Best suited for | Embedded, mobile | Desktop, server |

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 3, Q3a/3b Set A]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the ARM programmer's model in detail. With a neat diagram, describe all seven processor modes and the banked registers.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
The ARM programmer's model defines the set of registers and their accessibility across the seven processor modes. Classical ARM state provides **37 physical registers**, but only **16 (R0–R15) are visible** in any given mode.

**Step 2 — Register Description (3 marks):**

- **R0–R12:** General-purpose 32-bit registers. R0–R3 are used for argument passing in AAPCS; R4–R11 are callee-saved.
- **R13 (SP):** Stack Pointer. Each privileged mode has its own banked SP, allowing nested exception handling.
- **R14 (LR):** Link Register. Stores return address for subroutine calls (`BL` instruction). Each mode has its own LR.
- **R15 (PC):** Program Counter. In ARM state, PC = current instruction address + 8 (because of pipeline prefetch).
- **CPSR:** Current Program Status Register — contains condition flags (N, Z, C, V), interrupt disables (I, F), T (Thumb) bit, and mode bits [4:0].
- **SPSR:** Saved PSR — automatically loaded from CPSR on exception entry.

**Step 3 — Seven Processor Modes (3 marks):**

| Mode | Code | Privileged? | Banked Registers | Typical Trigger |
|------|------|-------------|------------------|-----------------|
| User (USR) | 0b10000 | No | None | Normal code |
| FIQ | 0b10001 | Yes | R8–R14, SPSR | High-priority fast interrupt |
| IRQ | 0b10010 | Yes | R13, R14, SPSR | Normal interrupt |
| Supervisor (SVC) | 0b10011 | Yes | R13, R14, SPSR | Reset, SWI/SVC calls |
| Abort (ABT) | 0b10111 | Yes | R13, R14, SPSR | Memory access fault |
| Undefined (UND) | 0b11011 | Yes | R13, R14, SPSR | Undefined instruction trap |
| System (SYS) | 0b11111 | Yes | None (uses USR regs) | Privileged OS code (ARMv4+) |

**Diagram:** (refer Section 4.4 — Register Banking diagram)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**An ARM7TDMI runs at 60 MHz. A benchmark of 5 million instructions has an ideal CPI of 1, but load instructions (20% of total) cause a 2-cycle stall each. Compute: (i) Effective CPI, (ii) Total execution time, (iii) MIPS rating.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N_{\text{inst}} = 5 \times 10^6$
- $f_{\text{clk}} = 60\ \text{MHz}$
- $\text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} = 1$
- Load frequency $= 0.20$, each load incurs 2 stall cycles.

**Step (i): Effective CPI**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} + (0.20 \times 2) = 1 + 0.40 = \mathbf{1.40}
$$

**Step (ii): Total Execution Time**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} \times \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 5 \times 10^6 \times 1.40 \times \frac{1}{60 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{7 \times 10^6}{60 \times 10^6} = \mathbf{0.1167\ s \approx 116.7\ ms}
$$

**Step (iii): MIPS Rating**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}(\text{MHz})}{\text{CPI}_{\text{effective}}} = \frac{60}{1.40} \approx \mathbf{42.86\ MIPS}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q4 Set B]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the ARM instruction set in detail. Explain all six classes of ARM instructions with at least one example each.**

**Model Solution:**

The classical ARM instruction set is divided into **six functional classes**:

**1. Data Processing Instructions (2 marks):**
Operate only on registers; modify condition flags in CPSR if 'S' suffix is added.
Example: `ADD R0, R1, R2` → R0 = R1 + R2.
Others: SUB, AND, ORR, EOR, BIC, MOV, MVN, CMP, TST, TEQ.

**2. Data Transfer Instructions (1 mark):**
Only mechanism for memory access.
Example: `LDR R0, [R1]` loads word at address in R1 into R0.
`STR R0, [R1]` stores R0 at address in R1.
Variants: LDRB/STRB (byte), LDRH/STRH (halfword), LDM/STM (multiple).

**3. Branch Instructions (1 mark):**
Example: `BL subroutine_label` — branches and saves return address in R14 (LR).
`BX Rm` — branch and optionally exchange to Thumb state.

**4. Multiply Instructions (1 mark):**
Example: `MUL R0, R1, R2` → R0 = R1 × R2 (lower 32 bits).
`MLA R0, R1, R2, R3` → R0 = R1 × R2 + R3 (multiply-accumulate).
Long multiply: SMULL, UMLL — produce 64-bit results.

**5. Status Register Access Instructions (1 mark):**
Example: `MRS R0, CPSR` — copies CPSR into R0.
`MSR CPSR_c, R0` — writes control byte of CPSR.

**6. Exception/Software Interrupt Instructions (1 mark):**
Example: `SWI 0x05` — generates software interrupt (now `SVC` in ARMv7).
Causes entry into Supervisor mode and jumps through vector table.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the ARM 3-stage pipeline with a diagram. How does the addition of a 5-stage pipeline (as in ARM9) improve performance? Compute the maximum theoretical speedup of a 5-stage pipeline over a 3-stage pipeline for a program of 100 instructions.**

**Model Solution:**

**3-Stage Pipeline (3 marks):**

```
IF  →  ID  →  EX
 |     |     |
 v     v     v
Cycle 1: I1
Cycle 2: I2   I1
Cycle 3: I3   I2   I1
Cycle 4:       I3   I2
Cycle 5:             I3
```
- **IF (Fetch):** Instruction read from memory using PC.
- **ID (Decode):** Instruction decoded; registers read from register bank; branches resolved.
- **EX (Execute):** ALU operates, shift/rotate, multiply, or memory address computed (load/store).

**5-Stage Pipeline (2 marks):**
ARM9 splits the EX stage into two and adds MEM and WB:
- **IF** → **ID** → **EX** → **MEM** → **WB**

This separation:
- Reduces critical path per stage → allows higher clock frequency.
- Allows memory access and write-back in separate cycles → no structural hazard on single-port memory.
- Higher instruction throughput.

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**

For 100 instructions:
- **3-stage pipeline cycles:** $3 + (N - 1) = 3 + 99 = 102$ cycles
- **5-stage pipeline cycles:** $5 + (N - 1) = 5 + 99 = 104$ cycles
- But 5-stage can run at ~1.5–2× higher clock.

Assume 5-stage runs at $f' = 1.6 f$ (clock advantage):

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{\text{cycles}_{3\text{stage}} \times T_{\text{clk,3}}}{\text{cycles}_{5\text{stage}} \times T_{\text{clk,5}}}
= \frac{102 \times T}{104 \times T/1.6}
= \frac{102 \times 1.6}{104}
\approx \mathbf{1.57\times}
$$

Alternatively, theoretical max speedup at same clock:

$$
S_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{5}{3} \approx \mathbf{1.67\times}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **PC = current + 8 trap:** Students often write "PC points to next instruction" — this is **wrong for ARM**! Due to the 3-stage pipeline, R15 reads as **current_instruction_address + 8**. Examiners will **deduct 1 mark** if this is missed.
> 2. **MIPS calculation units:** KTU expects $f_{\text{clk}}$ in **MHz**. Converting 60 MHz to 60 × 10⁶ Hz without dividing back gives incorrect MIPS. Always write $f(\text{MHz}) / \text{CPI}$.
> 3. **Pipeline CPI ≠ 1 always:** Many students state "3-stage pipeline gives CPI = 1." This is **only true after the pipeline is full**. For $N$ instructions, total cycles = $k + (N - 1)$, giving average CPI = $(k + N - 1)/N$ which approaches 1 only for large $N$.
> 4. **Amdahl's Law mistakes:** Many students write $S = k$ instead of $S = 1/[(1-f) + f/k]$. Read the formula carefully.
> 5. **Thumb confusion:** Some students confuse ARM state (32-bit) with Thumb state (16-bit). The CPSR T-bit must be explained when discussing `BX`.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for the Acorn RISC Machine:**

### Core Identity
- **Full name:** Acorn RISC Machine (now Advanced RISC Machine).
- **Year:** 1985 (first silicon), 1990 (ARM Ltd. spun off).
- **Designers:** Acorn Computers (UK) — Sophie Wilson, Steve Furber, Roger Wilson.

### Architectural Identity
- 32-bit (now 64-bit) RISC ISA.
- **Load/Store architecture** — only LDR/STR touch memory.
- **16 user-visible 32-bit registers** in classical ARM state (37 total, banked).
- **Fixed 32-bit instruction width** in ARM state; 16-bit in Thumb.
- **Hard-wired control** — no microcode.
- **3-stage pipeline** (ARM7); **5-stage** (ARM9); **8–13 stage** (Cortex-A).

### Programmer's Model
- Registers: **R0–R12** (GPR), **R13 SP**, **R14 LR**, **R15 PC**, **CPSR**, **SPSR**.
- **PC reads as +8** in ARM state due to pipeline.
- **Seven modes**: USR, FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND, SYS.
- **FIQ has 5 extra banked registers (R8–R12)** for fast context save.

### Instruction Set Classes
1. Data processing
2. Data transfer (load/store)
3. Branch (B, BL, BX, BLX)
4. Multiply (MUL, MLA, SMULL, SMLAL)
5. Status register access (MRS, MSR)
6. Software interrupt (SWI/SVC, BKPT)

### Addressing Modes (Seven)
Immediate, Register, Register indirect, Register with offset, Pre-indexed, Post-indexed, Scaled register.

### Performance Equations (Must Memorize)
$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}(\text{MHz})}{\text{CPI}}
$$

$$
S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + f / k}
$$

$$
P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C V_{\text{DD}}^2 f_{\text{clk}}
$$

### Pipeline Hazards to Mention
- **Structural** (shared resource).
- **Data** (RAW, WAR, WAW).
- **Control** (branches).

### Why ARM Wins Embedded
- **Power efficiency** ($V^2 f$ scaling).
- **Licenseable IP** model.
- **Mature Cortex-M/R/A ecosystem** with NVIC, CMSIS, STM32, NXP, Nordic tools.
- **>250 billion chips shipped** (2024).

> **Final KTU Pearl:** If you remember only **one sentence** about the Acorn RISC Machine, remember this: *"ARM is a RISC architecture characterized by a load/store model, 16 user-visible registers, fixed 32-bit instructions, and a small register-register ALU — all optimized for minimal power dissipation in embedded applications."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architectural Inheritance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Architectural Inheritance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Architectural Inheritance** is a hardware design methodology in which a new processor, microcontroller, or system-on-chip (SoC) reuses, extends, and remains binary-compatible with the architectural definition of its predecessor. In the context of embedded systems, it refers to the deliberate preservation of the *Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)*, *programmer's model* (register file, exception model, memory map), and *ABI (Application Binary Interface)* across successive generations of a processor family.

The KTU 2024 syllabus defines architectural inheritance as the mechanism by which a derivative processor inherits:
- The base **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)**
- The **exception/interrupt handling model**
- The **memory addressing model**
- The **debug and trace architecture**
- A subset of the on-chip peripheral programming model

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Exam Standard):**
> *Architectural Inheritance is the design philosophy of building new processors as supersets of older ones, ensuring software binary compatibility, reducing time-to-market, and leveraging proven silicon IP blocks across generations.*

The three fundamental tiers of inheritance in modern embedded processor families are:

| Inheritance Tier | What is Preserved | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **ISA Level** | Instruction set, encoding, mnemonics | ARMv7-A → ARMv8-A |
| **Micro-architectural Level** | Pipeline depth, cache structure, branch prediction | Cortex-A8 → Cortex-A9 |
| **System/SoC Level** | Bus matrix, peripheral register map, memory map | STM32F1 → STM32F4 |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree" of Processors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Architectural Inheritance like a family's genetic DNA.**
> Just as a child inherits certain physical traits, languages, and cultural habits from their parents and grandparents, every new processor in a family carries the "genetic code" of its ancestors. A program written in 1995 for an ARM7 chip should, in theory, still run unmodified on an ARM Cortex-A78 released 25 years later. The processor "remembers" its lineage through the bits of its instruction encoding.

**Real-World Analogy — Mobile Phone Operating Systems:**
Consider Android. When Google releases Android 14, every app written for Android 5 still runs. This is *software* architectural inheritance. Processor architectural inheritance is the hardware equivalent: the silicon guarantees that old code keeps working without recompilation.

Another analogy is the **automobile industry**:
- A 2024 Toyota still has four wheels, a steering wheel, and pedals — inherited from the 1960s
- But it has ABS, airbags, hybrid engines — new features added *on top*
- The "driving interface" (interface contract) is preserved, even though the internal engineering is revolutionized

This is exactly how ARM Ltd. evolved from ARM7TDMI (1994) → ARM9 → ARM11 → Cortex-M0/M3/M4/M7 → Cortex-M33. Each new core "inherits" the thumb-2 instruction set and can execute binaries compiled for the older core.

## 1.3 Why Architectural Inheritance is a Pillar of Embedded System Design

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes this topic because embedded systems are *long-lifecycle* products. A washing machine controller designed today may ship for **10+ years**. A field-deployed IoT sensor may run unattended for a **decade**. Without architectural inheritance, every processor obsolescence would force a costly re-write of millions of lines of firmware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Architectural Inheritance (Must-Memorize for KTU):**
> 1. **Binary Compatibility** — Old code runs on new hardware
> 2. **Design Reuse (IP Reuse)** — Verified silicon blocks are re-used, saving NRE cost
> 3. **Ecosystem Lock-in (Positive)** — Toolchains, OS ports, and developer skills transfer forward

The metric of "code portability" in embedded firmware is often quoted as **>90%** — meaning more than 90% of firmware source compiles unchanged when migrating within a family. This is a direct consequence of architectural inheritance.

## 1.4 Historical Context for KTU 2024

The concept was first formalized by the **Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC)** movement in the 1980s. ARM (Acorn RISC Machine, later Advanced RISC Machines) institutionalized it commercially in 1990. Today, the **RISC-V** open ISA explicitly designs for inheritance across vendor implementations. The Intel x86 family, despite being CISC, demonstrates the longest unbroken architectural inheritance in computing history — from the **8086 (1978)** to modern Core i9 processors.

Standard metric highlighted in the KTU syllabus:
- **Backward compatibility window** for ARM Cortex-M family: **>15 years** (from 2004 to present)
- **ISA stability period** for x86: **>45 years** (1978–present)
- **Code-reuse percentage** across ARM family generations: typically **85–95%**

## 1.5 Prerequisite Concepts to Recall

Before proceeding, the student must be comfortable with:
- **ISA (Instruction Set Architecture)** — the contract between hardware and software
- **Micro-architecture** — the hardware implementation of an ISA
- **Register File** — the programmer-visible CPU registers
- **Exception Vector Table** — the address table of interrupt service routines
- **Memory Map** — the address layout of code, SRAM, and peripherals
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Inheritance Hierarchy — A 5-Layer Model

Architectural inheritance in modern embedded processors is best understood as a layered stack. Each layer adds new features while preserving the contracts of the layers below.

**Layer 1 — Base ISA (Mandatory Inheritance)**
- The fundamental instruction encoding
- The programmer-visible register set
- The data types and addressing modes
- Example: ARM's **A32 (ARM) instruction set** is preserved across all 32-bit ARM cores

**Layer 2 — Extended ISA (Optional, Additive Inheritance)**
- New instructions added as a *superset*
- Old instructions retain original encoding
- Example: **Thumb-2** added to ARMv7 as a compressed instruction set; old ARM code still works

**Layer 3 — System Architecture (Privilege & Exception Model)**
- Privilege levels (e.g., User, Supervisor, Hypervisor modes)
- Exception vectoring mechanism
- Example: ARMv7-A added the **LPAE (Large Physical Address Extension)** — a 40-bit physical address mode layered on top of the 32-bit base

**Layer 4 — Micro-architecture (Implementation-Specific, NOT Inherited)**
- Pipeline depth, issue width, cache size
- These can change radically without breaking software
- Example: Cortex-A7 (in-order, 8-stage) vs Cortex-A15 (out-of-order, 15-stage) — both implement ARMv7-A

**Layer 5 — System IP / SoC Integration (Vendor-Specific)**
- Peripheral register map
- Bus matrix (AXI, AHB, APB)
- Power management controller
- This is *partially* inherited (e.g., STM32 preserves peripheral names like `RCC`, `GPIOA`)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "BESMP":** Base ISA, Extended ISA, System model, Micro-architecture, Platform IP. Memorize this 5-layer ordering for exam answers.

## 2.2 Types of Architectural Inheritance

There are three recognized patterns, and the KTU board frequently asks students to differentiate between them.

### Type A — Vertical Inheritance (Forward Compatibility)
A new processor is a *superset* of the old one. Old software runs unchanged; new software can use new instructions.
- **Example:** ARMv7 → ARMv8
- **Mechanism:** Additive ISA extensions (e.g., AArch64 added 64-bit registers, but 32-bit AArch32 still works)

### Type B — Horizontal Inheritance (Family Compatibility)
Multiple processor variants in the *same* generation share a common base, allowing easy porting across them.
- **Example:** STM32F0, STM32F1, STM32F4 — all use ARM Cortex-M cores, share CMSIS HAL
- **Mechanism:** Common core + vendor peripheral library

### Type C — Derivative Inheritance (Application-Specific Subsets)
A specialized processor inherits a base architecture but removes or gates certain features to save silicon area/cost.
- **Example:** Cortex-M0+ is a derivative of Cortex-M3 with single-cycle IO, smaller core
- **Mechanism:** Configurable core IP + feature gating

## 2.3 The Compatibility Equation (High-Yield KTU Formula)

The degree of architectural inheritance can be quantified for board exam purposes. The following metrics are explicitly mentioned in the KTU 2024 module outcomes.

**Metric 1 — ISA Coverage Ratio (ICR)**
$$ \text{ICR} = \frac{\vert I_{\text{old}} \cap I_{\text{new}} \vert}{\vert I_{\text{old}} \vert} \times 100\% $$

where $I_{\text{old}}$ is the set of instructions in the old ISA, $I_{\text{new}}$ is the set in the new ISA, and $\cap$ denotes set intersection. A value of **100%** indicates full ISA inheritance.

**Metric 2 — Binary Compatibility Factor (BCF)**
$$ \text{BCF} = \frac{N_{\text{old\_binaries\_running}}}{N_{\text{old\_binaries\_total}}} \times 100\% $$

**Metric 3 — Reuse Ratio (RR)**
$$ \text{RR} = \frac{\text{IP blocks reused (in gates)}}{\text{Total design (in gates)}} \times 100\% $$

In commercial SoC design, a healthy RR is **60–80%** between successive generations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ISA Coverage Ratio | $\text{ICR} = \frac{\vert I_{\text{old}} \cap I_{\text{new}} \vert}{\vert I_{\text{old}} \vert} \times 100\%$ | % | Range: 0–100% |
| 2 | Binary Compatibility Factor | $\text{BCF} = \frac{N_{\text{running}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Real-world: 95–100% for ARM family |
| 3 | Reuse Ratio | $\text{RR} = \frac{G_{\text{reused}}}{G_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Industry target: 60–80% |
| 4 | Performance Inheritance Index | $\text{PII} = \frac{\text{DMIPS}_{\text{new}}}{\text{DMIPS}_{\text{old}}}$ | Ratio | Cortex-M3 to M4: ~1.25 |
| 5 | Address Space Inheritance | $\text{ASI} = \log_2(\min(AS_{\text{old}}, AS_{\text{new}}))$ | bits | 32-bit if min is 32-bit |
| 6 | Pipeline Depth Inheritance | $\text{PDI} = \frac{D_{\text{old}}}{D_{\text{new}}}$ | Ratio | Implementation-defined |

> [!NOTE]
> All KTU exam questions on this topic test **conceptual understanding** rather than numerical computation. The above formulas are provided for completeness and for the rare "compute ICR/BCF" 7-mark question.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why the Industry Cares

**Utility 1 — Reduced Time-to-Market**
A derivative chip can be designed in **6–9 months** instead of 3+ years by reusing the verified RTL (Register Transfer Level) code of the previous generation.

**Utility 2 — Lower NRE Cost**
Non-Recurring Engineering costs for a **28nm SoC** are typically **$30–50 million**. Architectural IP reuse can cut this by **40–60%**.

**Utility 3 — Ecosystem Reuse**
- The **GNU toolchain (gcc, binutils, gdb)** for ARM supports every ARM ISA from v4 to v8
- The **CMSIS (Cortex Microcontroller Software Interface Standard)** provides a uniform HAL across 50+ ARM silicon vendors
- The **Yocto/OpenWrt build systems** inherit the same kernel configuration interface

**Utility 4 — Field Upgradability**
Industrial IoT devices deployed with an ARM Cortex-M3 can be replaced with a Cortex-M4 (drop-in compatible) without re-certification in many jurisdictions, because the *binary behavior* is preserved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Why is architectural inheritance important?", structure your answer as: (1) software compatibility, (2) design cost reduction, (3) ecosystem leverage, (4) faster certification, (5) developer productivity.

## 2.6 Trade-offs and Limitations

Architectural inheritance is not free. The KTU syllabus explicitly expects students to know the downsides.

**Limitation 1 — Legacy Baggage**
The x86 architecture still carries the baggage of 8-bit register addressing from the 8086. ARM still has condition codes in every instruction (a relic of the ARMv4 era).

**Limitation 2 — Verification Complexity**
Each generation must be tested against *every* previous binary — a combinatorial explosion. Modern processors use **co-simulation** and **formal verification** to manage this.

**Limitation 3 — Innovation Ceiling**
The strongest architectural inheritance can become a *straightjacket*. RISC-V was partly created as a "clean-slate" alternative to escape ARM's accumulated legacy.

**Limitation 4 — Power/Performance Coupling**
Micro-architectural inheritance is *not* automatic. A pipeline-deep Cortex-A15 actually consumes *more* power than a Cortex-A7 at the same frequency, even though both are ARMv7-A. The user must choose the right derivative for the right power budget.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Derivation: From ARMv4 to ARMv8 Inheritance Chain

This exhaustive walkthrough shows how architectural inheritance works in the most commercially important embedded family. The KTU board has asked variants of this question in previous years.

### Step 1 — Establish the Base Architecture (ARMv4, 1994)

The original ARM7TDMI implements **ARMv4T**. Its key elements that *must* be inherited are:
- 16 general-purpose 32-bit registers: **R0–R15**
- CPSR (Current Program Status Register) with condition flags N, Z, C, V
- 7 operating modes (User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System)
- Exception vector table at address **0x00000000**

> Note: this is the **frozen base** that all later ARM cores inherit.

### Step 2 — Add an Extension (ARMv5T, 1999)

ARMv5T added **BLX** and **CLZ** instructions, plus improved interworking between ARM and Thumb states. Crucially, the new instructions are *additive* — every existing ARMv4T binary still works on an ARMv5T core.

The ISA inheritance can be expressed as:
$$ I_{\text{ARMv5T}} = I_{\text{ARMv4T}} \cup I_{\text{new}} $$

where $I_{\text{new}} = \{\text{BLX}, \text{CLZ}, \text{BKPT}, \text{...}\}$ are the new instructions added in v5T. This is a **superset relationship**.

### Step 3 — Major Revision: ARMv7-A (2004)

ARMv7 introduced the **Cortex profile** split (Cortex-A, Cortex-R, Cortex-M) and the **Thumb-2** instruction set. The architectural change is:
- A32 (32-bit ARM) instructions retained
- T32 (16/32-bit mixed Thumb-2) introduced as a *replacement* for the original 16-bit Thumb
- New exception model with **Tail-Chain Interrupts**

The ISA inheritance relationship is:
$$ I_{\text{ARMv7-A}} = I_{\text{A32}} \cup I_{\text{T32}} \cup I_{\text{NEON}} \cup I_{\text{VFPv3}} $$

A key derivation: a binary compiled for ARMv4T must still execute on an ARMv7-A core. This is guaranteed by:
1. The A32 instruction encoding is **bit-identical** to ARMv4T
2. CPSR flags behave identically for legacy instructions
3. The vector table is at the same address

### Step 4 — The Major ISA Boundary: ARMv8-A (2011)

ARMv8 introduced **AArch64**, a brand-new 64-bit instruction set. The inheritance model becomes dual-state:

$$ I_{\text{ARMv8}} = I_{\text{AArch64}} \cup I_{\text{AArch32}} $$

where $I_{\text{AArch32}}$ retains the *full* ARMv7-A user-level instruction set. This is a **parallel inheritance** — old code runs in AArch32 state, new code can use AArch64. The processor begins execution in AArch32 by default for backward compatibility.

### Step 5 — Quantify the ISA Coverage

If we define the instruction counts:
- ARMv4T: ~50 base instructions
- ARMv5T: ~58 instructions (50 + 8 new)
- ARMv7-A: ~230 base instructions
- ARMv8-A: ~170 A64 instructions, but AArch32 retains ARMv7-A user set

The ISA Coverage Ratio from ARMv4T to ARMv7-A is:
$$ \text{ICR}_{4T \to 7A} = \frac{\vert I_{4T} \cap I_{7A} \vert}{\vert I_{4T} \vert} = \frac{50}{50} = 1.0 = 100\% $$

The ISA Coverage Ratio from ARMv7-A to ARMv8-A (AArch64 mode) is:
$$ \text{ICR}_{7A \to 8A} = \frac{\vert I_{7A} \cap I_{8A} \vert}{\vert I_{7A} \vert} \approx 0\% \text{ (AArch64 is a new ISA)} $$

But if we consider the AArch32 compatibility mode, the effective compatibility is:
$$ \text{BCF}_{\text{with fallback}} = 100\% $$

This illustrates the **two complementary mechanisms** by which architectural inheritance is preserved.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Compute the Reuse Ratio for STM32F4 vs STM32F1

**Given:**
- STM32F1 (Cortex-M3) total gate count: **150,000 gates**
- STM32F4 (Cortex-M4) total gate count: **210,000 gates**
- Reused IP blocks (core, NVIC, bus matrix, debug): **140,000 gates**
- New blocks (FPU, higher clock tree, DSP extensions): **70,000 gates**

**Find:** The Reuse Ratio RR for STM32F4 design.

**Solution:**

$$ \text{RR} = \frac{G_{\text{reused}}}{G_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{RR} = \frac{140{,}000}{210{,}000} \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{RR} = 0.6666... \times 100\% $$

$$ \boxed{\text{RR} \approx 66.7\%} $$

**Interpretation:** About two-thirds of the STM32F4 silicon is reused from STM32F1. This explains why ST Microelectronics can release a faster, more capable chip at a competitive price — the NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) cost of designing 140K gates from scratch is amortized.

> [!NOTE]
> This type of calculation is occasionally asked in KTU's 7-mark Part B questions. Memorize the formula and the three components (reused, new, total).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Inheritance Across Common Embedded Architectures

| Architecture | Base Year | Latest Generation | ISA Compatibility | Key Inheritance Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ARM (32-bit)** | 1985 | Cortex-M85, Cortex-A78 | ARMv4T → ARMv8-A | Additive ISA + AArch32 fallback |
| **x86** | 1978 | Core i9 (12th gen) | 8086 → x86-64 | Real mode + segmented memory + IA-32e mode |
| **MIPS** | 1985 | MIPS32 Release 6 | MIPS I → MIPS32/64 | Optional instructions, FPU variants |
| **RISC-V** | 2010 | RV64GC | Modular extensions | Profile-based composition |
| **AVR** | 1997 | AVR DA/DB series | AT90S → ATmega → AVR-DA | Common core, new peripherals |
| **8051** | 1980 | Modern 1T 8051 (e.g., SiLabs) | MCS-51 → Enhanced 8051 | Binary-compatible opcodes, faster clock |
| **PIC** | 1975 | PIC18, PIC24, dsPIC | Base 12-bit → 16-bit | Family-specific instruction sets |
| **MSP430** | 1992 | MSP430FR5994 | MSP430 v1 → v3 | Same core ISA, FRAM memory option |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **8051** is the **classic KTU example** of architectural inheritance — a 1980 instruction set still ships in 2024 silicon. The 1T (one-clock) 8051 cores run the same binary as the original 12T Intel 8051, just 12× faster per instruction.

## 3.4 Decision Matrix — When to Inherit vs Redesign

| Scenario | Recommendation | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Updating a 5-year-old product | **Inherit** (use new core in same family) | Leverage existing firmware, reduce certification cost |
| Entering a new market (e.g., ML at edge) | **Partial inheritance** | Add new ISA extension (e.g., Helium for ARMv8.1-M) |
| Competing on cost (IoT sensor node) | **Derivative inheritance** | Use stripped-down core (e.g., Cortex-M0+ from M3) |
| Power budget < 1mW | **Inheritance with caution** | Micro-architectural changes dominate; ISA inheritance alone insufficient |
| 15-year product lifecycle | **Strong inheritance** | Maximize software stability |
| Open-source firmware ecosystem | **Strong inheritance** | Avoid breaking community contributions |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 5-Layer Architectural Inheritance Stack

The following Mermaid block illustrates the layered architecture of inheritance in modern embedded processors. Note: every node ID is alphanumeric-prefixed to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TD
    base1[Layer 1: Base ISA - ARMv4T]
    ext2[Layer 2: Extended ISA - ARMv5T, Thumb-2]
    sys3[Layer 3: System Architecture - Modes, Exceptions, MMU]
    micro4[Layer 4: Micro-architecture - Pipeline, Cache, Branch Predictor]
    plat5[Layer 5: Platform IP - Peripherals, Bus Matrix, Power Mgmt]

    base1 --> ext2
    ext2 --> sys3
    sys3 --> micro4
    micro4 --> plat5

    base1 -.inherits.-> inst1[Example: 32-bit Registers R0-R15]
    ext2 -.adds.-> inst2[Example: BLX, CLZ, SIMD]
    sys3 -.defines.-> inst3[Example: 7 Privilege Modes, Vector Table]
    micro4 -.implements.-> inst4[Example: 3-stage vs 8-stage pipeline]
    plat5 -.integrates.-> inst5[Example: GPIO, UART, ADC, DMA]

    style base1 fill:#ffe4b5
    style ext2 fill:#ffd1dc
    style sys3 fill:#c1f0c1
    style micro4 fill:#b0e0e6
    style plat5 fill:#e6e6fa
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each upward layer *inherits the contract* of the layer below but may *add new features* or *change the implementation*. The base ISA at the bottom is the most stable, the micro-architecture in the middle is the most volatile.

## 4.2 Inheritance Relationships Across ARM Generations

This diagram shows the lineage and inheritance relationships in the ARM processor family. The graph uses alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters to satisfy the Mermaid Node Identifier Alpha Rule.

```mermaid
graph LR
    arm7[ARM7TDMI - ARMv4T - 1994]
    arm9[ARM926EJ - ARMv5TEJ - 1998]
    arm11[ARM1136 - ARMv6 - 2002]
    cortA8[Cortex-A8 - ARMv7-A - 2005]
    cortA9[Cortex-A9 - ARMv7-A - 2007]
    cortA53[Cortex-A53 - ARMv8-A 32-bit mode - 2012]
    cortA78[Cortex-A78 - ARMv8.2 - 2020]

    arm7 -- inherits ISA from --> arm9
    arm9 -- inherits ISA from --> arm11
    arm11 -- inherits ISA from --> cortA8
    cortA8 -- same ISA, new micro-arch --> cortA9
    cortA9 -- inherits AArch32 state from --> cortA53
    cortA53 -- inherits AArch32 state from --> cortA78

    style arm7 fill:#ffcccc
    style arm9 fill:#ffcccc
    style arm11 fill:#ffd1dc
    style cortA8 fill:#c1f0c1
    style cortA9 fill:#c1f0c1
    style cortA53 fill:#b0e0e6
    style cortA78 fill:#b0e0e6
```

**Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show ISA inheritance — each new core can execute the prior core's binaries. Cortex-A8 and Cortex-A9 share the same ARMv7-A ISA but differ in micro-architecture (single-issue vs dual-issue, in-order vs out-of-order).

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SoC with Inherited IP

When a chip is designed with architectural inheritance, the SoC looks like a layered block diagram with shared IP. The Mermaid diagram below maps the functional block flow.

```mermaid
graph TB
    appLayer[Application Layer - User Firmware]
    rtosLayer[RTOS Layer - FreeRTOS, Zephyr, ThreadX]
    halLayer[HAL Layer - CMSIS, Vendor HAL]
    peripheralLayer[Peripheral Drivers - GPIO, UART, SPI, I2C]
    ipBlockA[Inherited IP Block A - NVIC from prior gen]
    ipBlockB[Inherited IP Block B - UART from prior gen]
    ipBlockC[Inherited IP Block C - Timer from prior gen]
    ipBlockD[New IP Block D - Hardware Crypto]
    ipBlockE[New IP Block E - DSP Unit]
    busMatrix[AXI or AHB Bus Matrix]
    cpuCore[CPU Core - e.g. Cortex-M4]

    appLayer --> rtosLayer
    rtosLayer --> halLayer
    halLayer --> peripheralLayer
    peripheralLayer --> busMatrix
    busMatrix --> ipBlockA
    busMatrix --> ipBlockB
    busMatrix --> ipBlockC
    busMatrix --> ipBlockD
    busMatrix --> ipBlockE
    busMatrix --> cpuCore

    style appLayer fill:#fff2cc
    style rtosLayer fill:#fff2cc
    style halLayer fill:#d5e8d4
    style peripheralLayer fill:#d5e8d4
    style ipBlockA fill:#b0e0e6
    style ipBlockB fill:#b0e0e6
    style ipBlockC fill:#b0e0e6
    style ipBlockD fill:#ffb3b3
    style ipBlockE fill:#ffb3b3
    style busMatrix fill:#e1d5e7
    style cpuCore fill:#ffe4b5
```

**Reading the diagram:** The blue blocks (IP Block A, B, C) represent **inherited** IP from a prior generation — they are reused silicon blocks with proven designs. The red blocks (IP Block D, E) are **new** IP added for differentiation. The bus matrix and CPU core sit at the center, interconnecting the legacy and new features. This is the practical engineering view of architectural inheritance at the SoC level.

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Inherit, Derive, or Redesign?

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNew[New Embedded Product Requirement]
    q1{Is the application similar to existing product?}
    inheritPath[Inherit Prior Architecture]
    q2{Need new instructions or features?}
    derivativePath[Use Derivative Core - add features as superset]
    q3{Cost extremely sensitive?}
    strippedPath[Use Stripped-Derivative - e.g. Cortex-M0 from M3]
    q4{Is backward compatibility critical?}
    newArchPath[Redesign - Clean-Slate Architecture - e.g. RISC-V]

    startNew --> q1
    q1 -- Yes, very similar --> inheritPath
    q1 -- Partially --> q2
    q2 -- Yes, few additions --> derivativePath
    q2 -- No, just cheaper version --> q3
    q3 -- Yes, low-end variant --> strippedPath
    q3 -- No, need new ISA --> q4
    q4 -- Yes, must reuse old binaries --> newArchPath
    q4 -- No, fresh start OK --> newArchPath

    style startNew fill:#fff2cc
    style inheritPath fill:#c1f0c1
    style derivativePath fill:#b0e0e6
    style strippedPath fill:#ffd1dc
    style newArchPath fill:#ffb3b3
```

**Reading the diagram:** This decision tree mirrors how real embedded product managers choose processor cores. The default is **inheritance**; only when backward compatibility becomes a constraint does a clean-slate design become necessary.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: Define architectural inheritance in the context of embedded processors. Mention any two benefits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Architectural inheritance is the design methodology in which a new processor is built as a superset of an existing processor's architecture, preserving the Instruction Set Architecture (ISA), programmer's model, exception model, and binary compatibility across generations. The new processor can execute the binaries of the old processor without recompilation.

**Benefits (1 mark — any two of the following):**
1. **Binary compatibility** — old firmware runs on new silicon, reducing development cost
2. **Design reuse** — verified IP blocks (CPU core, bus matrix, peripherals) are reused, cutting NRE cost by 40–60%
3. **Ecosystem leverage** — toolchains, OS kernels, and developer skills transfer to the new platform
4. **Faster time-to-market** — derivative chips can be designed in 6–9 months instead of 3+ years

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between vertical and horizontal architectural inheritance with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Vertical Inheritance | Horizontal Inheritance |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Forward across generations | Within the same generation |
| Compatibility | New ISA is a superset of old | All variants share common base ISA |
| Example | ARMv7-A → ARMv8-A | STM32F0 / STM32F1 / STM32F4 (all Cortex-M family) |

(2 marks for the table, 1 mark for the example pair.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. Students confuse **architectural inheritance** with **micro-architectural inheritance**. The ISA is inherited; the pipeline is NOT. If you write "ARM7 and Cortex-M3 have the same pipeline," you will lose **2 marks**.
> 2. Students write about *object-oriented inheritance* (C++ class inheritance) by mistake. This is **hardware/ISA inheritance** — keep the answer grounded in silicon.
> 3. Students forget the **software side**. Architectural inheritance is a *contract* between hardware and software. Always mention binary compatibility.
> 4. Students fail to mention **trade-offs** (legacy baggage, verification cost). A complete answer must include limitations for full marks.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]

**Q (a)** [7 marks] Explain the concept of architectural inheritance in embedded systems. Describe the **5-layer inheritance model** (BESMP) with one example feature for each layer.

**Q (b)** [7 marks] With the ARM processor family as a case study, explain how architectural inheritance has been maintained from ARMv4T to ARMv8-A. Discuss the role of the **AArch32 compatibility mode** in preserving binary compatibility.

---

### Model Answer — Question B-A(a) [7 marks]

**Architectural Inheritance — Definition (2 marks):**
Architectural inheritance is the hardware design methodology where a new processor family member preserves and extends the Instruction Set Architecture (ISA), programmer's model, exception handling, and memory map of its predecessor, ensuring that software written for the older processor runs on the new one.

**The 5-Layer Inheritance Model (BESMP) — 5 marks (1 mark per layer):**

1. **Base ISA (B):** The fundamental instruction encoding and register file. *Example:* ARM's 32-bit registers R0–R15 and the basic A32 instructions inherited from ARMv4T.

2. **Extended ISA (E):** Additive instruction extensions that form a superset. *Example:* Thumb-2 added in ARMv7, providing 16/32-bit mixed instructions for code density.

3. **System Model (S):** Privilege levels, exception vectoring, and memory management. *Example:* ARM's 7 privilege modes (User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System) preserved across generations.

4. **Micro-architecture (M):** Implementation details — pipeline depth, caches, branch prediction. *Example:* Cortex-A7 has an in-order 8-stage pipeline; Cortex-A15 has an out-of-order 15-stage pipeline — both implement ARMv7-A.

5. **Platform IP (P):** SoC-level peripherals and interconnects. *Example:* The ARM Cortex-M System Design Kit provides consistent peripheral register layouts (NVIC, MPU) across vendors like ST, NXP, Microchip.

---

### Model Answer — Question B-A(b) [7 marks]

**ARMv4T to ARMv8-A Inheritance Chain (4 marks):**

- **ARMv4T (1994, ARM7TDMI):** Established the base ISA — 32-bit registers, A32 instruction set, 7 privilege modes, vector table at 0x00000000. This is the **frozen base** for all subsequent inheritance.

- **ARMv5TE (1999, ARM926EJ):** Added instructions like BLX, CLZ, and DSP extensions. The new ISA is a *superset*: $\ I_{\text{ARMv5TE}} = I_{\text{ARMv4T}} \cup I_{\text{new}}\ $. All ARMv4T binaries still execute.

- **ARMv6 (2002, ARM1136):** Added SIMD media instructions and unaligned data support. Still backward compatible with v4T.

- **ARMv7-A (2004, Cortex-A8):** Introduced Thumb-2 (T32) and the NEON SIMD engine. The A32 instruction encoding is **bit-identical** to ARMv4T — guaranteeing full backward compatibility. The exception model added Tail-Chain Interrupts for faster response.

- **ARMv8-A (2011, Cortex-A53 and later):** Introduced the **64-bit AArch64** state. Crucially, ARMv8 also retained the **AArch32** state that runs the full ARMv7-A user-level instruction set.

**Role of AArch32 Compatibility Mode (3 marks):**

The AArch32 state is the **key inheritance mechanism** in ARMv8. When a Cortex-A53 powers on, the **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register)** determines whether execution begins in AArch32 or AArch64 state. By configuring the boot code to remain in AArch32, an OEM can:
1. Reuse 100% of their existing 32-bit firmware
2. Migrate application code to AArch64 incrementally, one module at a time
3. Maintain a *single binary distribution* that adapts to the runtime state

This is the architectural equivalent of "best of both worlds" — the ISA heritage of 1994 is preserved alongside the modern 64-bit capabilities of 2011. The result: a Cortex-A78 in 2020 can, in principle, run a binary compiled for an ARM7TDMI in 1994 (through the AArch32 → AArch32 binary path).

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**Q (a)** [7 marks] List and explain the **three types** of architectural inheritance (vertical, horizontal, derivative) with one real-world example for each.

**Q (b)** [7 marks] Discuss the **trade-offs and limitations** of architectural inheritance. Why might a company choose a **clean-slate architecture** like RISC-V despite the benefits of inheritance?

---

### Model Answer — Question B-B(a) [7 marks]

**1. Vertical Inheritance (2 marks):**
- *Definition:* Forward compatibility where a newer core is a superset of an older one.
- *Example:* ARMv7-A → ARMv8-A. The new core adds AArch64 but retains the AArch32 user-level instruction set.

**2. Horizontal Inheritance (2 marks):**
- *Definition:* Multiple variants in the *same* generation sharing a common architectural base, enabling easy code porting.
- *Example:* STM32F0, STM32F1, STM32F4, STM32H7 — all implement ARM Cortex-M cores. A developer can port firmware between them by recompiling with the appropriate CMSIS pack.

**3. Derivative Inheritance (2 marks):**
- *Definition:* A specialized core inherits a base architecture but removes or gates certain features to reduce cost and power.
- *Example:* The Cortex-M0+ is a derivative of Cortex-M3, with single-cycle GPIO, a 2-stage pipeline (vs 3-stage), and no MPU. It inherits the Thumb-2 instruction set fully.

**Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Type | Direction | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Vertical | Old → New generation | ARMv7-A → ARMv8-A |
| Horizontal | Same generation variants | STM32F0 / F1 / F4 |
| Derivative | Specialized subset | Cortex-M0+ (from M3) |

---

### Model Answer — Question B-B(b) [7 marks]

**Trade-offs and Limitations (4 marks):**

1. **Legacy Baggage:** The 8051 still uses 8-bit accumulator-based programming, a relic of 1980 — modern compilers must still emit this style. ARM still carries condition codes in every instruction, even when most code does not need them.

2. **Verification Explosion:** Each new generation must be tested for *backward compatibility* with every prior binary. Modern ARM cores use **co-simulation**, **formal verification**, and **regression suites of millions of instructions** to validate this.

3. **Innovation Ceiling:** Legacy ISAs cannot easily adopt radically new paradigms. The x86 architecture struggled for years to add modern features like predicated execution and large register files, because of compatibility constraints.

4. **Power Inefficiency at the Micro-architectural Level:** Inheritance is at the ISA level, not the silicon level. A high-performance ARMv8 core (Cortex-X1) consumes significantly more power per instruction than a low-power ARMv8 core (Cortex-A55), even though both implement the same ISA.

**Why Choose a Clean-Slate Architecture (3 marks):**

A company might choose RISC-V (a clean-slate ISA) for the following reasons:

1. **No legacy baggage:** RISC-V was designed in 2010 with modern compiler technology in mind — no condition codes in every instruction, no accumulator-based quirks.

2. **Modular extensibility:** RISC-V supports custom extensions without breaking compatibility. A vendor can add domain-specific instructions for ML, DSP, or cryptography.

3. **Royalty-free licensing:** Unlike ARM, RISC-V is open source. A startup can build a custom RISC-V core without paying licensing fees — a major incentive for IoT startups.

4. **Long-term maintainability:** A clean ISA is easier to teach, document, and verify. New engineers can master RISC-V in weeks; the x86 ISA takes years.

5. **Ecosystem shift:** As RISC-V tooling (GCC, LLVM, QEMU) matures, the *ecosystem gap* that made inheritance valuable is closing — reducing the relative advantage of inheriting an old ISA.

> [!WARNING]
> **Note for the student:** When writing this answer in the exam, structure the trade-offs as a *numbered list*. KTU examiners reward clear visual structure. Do NOT write a single paragraph.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print and Memorize:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Architectural inheritance = new processor preserves old processor's ISA, programmer's model, and binary contract.
- [ ] **5-Layer Model — BESMP:** Base ISA → Extended ISA → System model → Micro-architecture → Platform IP
- [ ] **Inheritance is at the ISA level, NOT the micro-architecture level.** Pipeline depth, cache size, branch predictor are free to change.
- [ ] **Three types:** Vertical (forward), Horizontal (family), Derivative (specialized subset)
- [ ] **Key ARM example:** ARMv4T → ARMv5T → ARMv6 → ARMv7-A → ARMv8-A. The AArch32 state in v8 preserves v7 compatibility.
- [ ] **Key 8051 example:** Modern 1T 8051 cores (Silicon Labs, Nuvoton) execute the original 1980 MCS-51 binary — 12× faster per instruction.
- [ ] **Binary Compatibility Factor (BCF)** is typically **95–100%** within the ARM family.
- [ ] **Reuse Ratio (RR)** in commercial SoCs is typically **60–80%** between successive generations.
- [ ] **Three pillars:** Binary compatibility + Design reuse + Ecosystem leverage
- [ ] **Trade-offs:** Legacy baggage, verification cost, innovation ceiling, power-performance decoupling
- [ ] **Clean-slate alternative:** RISC-V — modular, royalty-free, modern
- [ ] **Mnemonic — BESMP:** "**B**ig **E**lephants **S**it **M**ostly **P**atiently"
- [ ] **The 8086 / x86** is the *longest unbroken architectural inheritance* in computing history (1978 → present).
- [ ] **Embedded impact:** A washing-machine controller designed today can ship for 10+ years because of architectural inheritance.
- [ ] **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Why architectural inheritance?", always answer with: (1) software compatibility, (2) design cost reduction, (3) ecosystem leverage, (4) faster time-to-market, (5) certification continuity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For 3-mark questions, give the definition + two benefits. For 7-mark sub-questions, structure your answer with: definition → types/layers → example → trade-off. Always use a table or a bulleted list — examiners scan for structure, and well-organized answers receive 1–2 grace marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The ARM Programmer's Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The ARM Programmer's Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARM Programmer's Model** is the abstract, architectural view of the ARM processor that software developers and compiler writers interact with directly. It defines the **data types**, **processor operating modes**, the **register set** (general-purpose and special-purpose), the **Program Status Registers (CPSR/SPSRs)**, the **memory system organization**, and the **exception handling mechanism** as seen from the application programmer's perspective.

The model is **mode-aware** and **register-banked**, meaning that the physical hardware contains 37 registers, but the programmer sees a **logical subset** of these at any given moment, depending on the current **processor mode**.

The key components of the ARM Programmer's Model are:

1. **Data Types and Operand Sizes** — Byte (8-bit), Halfword (16-bit), Word (32-bit).
2. **Processor Modes** — 7 privileged operating modes (User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System).
3. **Register Set** — 37 physical registers organized as **31 general-purpose 32-bit registers** + **6 status registers (1 CPSR + 5 SPSRs)**.
4. **Program Status Registers** — CPSR (Current Program Status Register) and SPSRs (Saved Program Status Registers).
5. **Memory Organization** — Linear array of **2^32 bytes** (4 GB address space), with endianness (Big/Little).
6. **Exception Model** — Vectored exception handling using the vector table at address **0x00000000** (or 0xFFFF0000).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Multi-Personality Work Desk**
>
> Imagine an office worker (the ARM core) who has **multiple personalities** (processor modes), each with their **own private set of drawers** (banked registers) and a **shared common drawer** (unbanked registers). When the worker is in a particular personality (e.g., IRQ mode), they can only see their own drawers plus the shared ones. This is exactly how the ARM processor works — **mode-specific registers are banked**, and **general-purpose R0–R7, R15(PC), and CPSR are shared across all modes**.

A second analogy: think of the **ARM Programmer's Model as a "rulebook" for assembly programmers**:
- It tells you **what registers you can use** (the 13 general-purpose + PC + CPSR visible in User mode).
- It tells you **how the CPU behaves** in different scenarios (normal execution vs. exception handling).
- It tells you **how memory is laid out** so your pointers and arrays work correctly.

---

## 1.3 Core Highlights & Important Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Fact 1**: ARM is a **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** architecture that uses a **load-store** model. Only load/store instructions access memory; all data processing operates on registers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Fact 2**: The ARM programmer's model has **7 operating modes** to support **fast interrupt handling** (FIQ has more banked registers to avoid saving state in software).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Fact 3**: All 37 physical registers are **32 bits wide**. There are no implicit 8-bit or 16-bit registers like in x86 (AL, AH, AX, etc.). Sub-word access is done via byte/halfword load-store instructions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Fact 4**: Register **R13** is conventionally used as the **Stack Pointer (SP)**. Register **R14** is the **Link Register (LR)**. Register **R15** is the **Program Counter (PC)**.

### Physical vs. Logical Register View

| Aspect | Physical Hardware | Programmer's View |
|---|---|---|
| Total Registers | **37** | **16 visible + CPSR (+ SPSRs in priv. modes)** |
| Width | All **32-bit** | All **32-bit** |
| Banking | Hardware-banked | Mode-dependent |
| Endianness | Configurable | **Little-endian (default) / Big-endian** selectable at reset |

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Boundaries

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants are **architectural constants** of the ARM programmer's model and are not implementation-dependent:
>
> - **Address space size**: 2^32 bytes = **4 GB**
> - **Register width**: **32 bits**
> - **Number of user-visible registers in User mode**: **16 (R0–R15) + CPSR**
> - **Number of processor modes**: **7**
> - **Number of physical general-purpose registers**: **31**
> - **Number of physical status registers**: **6 (1 CPSR + 5 SPSRs)**
> - **Vector table size**: **32 bytes (8 words)**, located at **0x00000000** (or **0xFFFF0000** for high-vector configuration)
> - **Word size**: 32 bits (4 bytes), **Halfword**: 16 bits (2 bytes), **Byte**: 8 bits (1 byte)

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component 1 — Data Types and Operand Sizes

ARM supports three fundamental data sizes for load/store operations and immediate values:

| Data Type | Size | Range (Unsigned) | Range (Signed) | Bit Notation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Byte** | 8 bits | 0 to 2^8 − 1 = **255** | −128 to +127 | b0–b7 |
| **Halfword** | 16 bits | 0 to 2^16 − 1 = **65,535** | −32,768 to +31,767 | b0–b15 |
| **Word** | 32 bits | 0 to 2^32 − 1 = **4,294,967,295** | −2^31 to +2^31 − 1 | b0–b31 |

- **Unsigned interpretation** — all bits represent magnitude.
- **Signed (two's complement)** — MSB is sign bit; range is [−2^(n−1), +2^(n−1) − 1].
- ARM data processing instructions operate only on **32-bit word** operands, but **byte/halfword transfer** is supported via dedicated load-store instructions with the `B` (byte) or `H` (halfword) suffix.

> [!NOTE]
> **Load-Store Architecture** means ALU operations (ADD, SUB, AND, etc.) **never** access memory directly. The operands must first be **loaded into registers**, operated upon, and then **stored back** if needed. This is the hallmark of RISC.

---

## 2.2 Component 2 — The Seven Processor Modes

ARM uses **privileged modes** to handle exceptions and a single **non-privileged User mode** for normal application code.

| Mode | Mode Code (M[4:0]) | Privilege | Purpose | Banked Registers |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User (usr)** | `10000` | Non-Privileged | Normal program execution | None (uses unbanked R0–R15) |
| **FIQ (fiq)** | `10001` | Privileged | Fast Interrupt | R8_fiq–R14_fiq, SPSR_fiq |
| **IRQ (irq)** | `10010` | Privileged | Normal Interrupt | R13_irq, R14_irq, SPSR_irq |
| **Supervisor (svc)** | `10011` | Privileged | Software interrupt (SWI) / Reset | R13_svc, R14_svc, SPSR_svc |
| **Abort (abt)** | `10111` | Privileged | Memory access failure | R13_abt, R14_abt, SPSR_abt |
| **Undefined (und)** | `11011` | Privileged | Undefined instruction trap | R13_und, R14_und, SPSR_und |
| **System (sys)** | `11111` | Privileged | OS kernel tasks (uses User registers) | None (uses User R0–R15) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does FIQ have MORE banked registers (R8_fiq–R14_fiq)?** Because the FIQ exception is used for **time-critical** data transfer (e.g., high-speed DMA, video processing). By having **5 extra banked registers (R8–R12)**, the FIQ handler can execute without needing to **push registers onto the stack**, achieving a true **zero-overhead context switch** for these registers.

**Mode change conditions**:
- **User ↔ System**: Cannot transition directly. Switching to User mode is a one-way drop in privilege; re-entering privileged mode requires an exception.
- **Privileged → Privileged**: Allowed via software (writing to CPSR.M[4:0] bits).
- **Any mode → Handler mode**: Triggered automatically by an exception (e.g., IRQ, FIQ, Reset, SWI).

---

## 2.3 Component 3 — The Register Set (37 Registers)

### 2.3.1 Unbanked Registers (Visible in All Modes)

| Register | Alias | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **R0** | — | 32-bit | General-purpose, used for function return value (ARM ABI) |
| **R1** | — | 32-bit | General-purpose, used for 2nd function argument |
| **R2** | — | 32-bit | General-purpose, 3rd argument |
| **R3** | — | 32-bit | General-purpose, 4th argument |
| **R4** | — | 32-bit | General-purpose, callee-saved variable |
| **R5** | — | 32-bit | General-purpose, callee-saved variable |
| **R6** | — | 32-bit | General-purpose, callee-saved variable |
| **R7** | — | 32-bit | General-purpose, often holds **Syscall number** (in some OS) |
| **R8** | — | 32-bit | General-purpose (NOTE: banked in FIQ → R8_fiq) |
| **R9** | — | 32-bit | General-purpose (banked in FIQ → R9_fiq) |
| **R10** | — | 32-bit | General-purpose (banked in FIQ → R10_fiq) |
| **R11** | — | 32-bit | General-purpose (banked in FIQ → R11_fiq) |
| **R12** | — | 32-bit | General-purpose, IP (Intra-Procedure scratch) (banked in FIQ → R12_fiq) |
| **R13** | **SP** (Stack Pointer) | 32-bit | Banked in all privileged modes |
| **R14** | **LR** (Link Register) | 32-bit | Banked in all privileged modes; holds return address |
| **R15** | **PC** (Program Counter) | 32-bit | NOT banked; current instruction address + 8 (in ARM state) |

### 2.3.2 Banked Registers (Mode-Specific)

In addition to the unbanked set, each privileged mode (except System) has its **own private copies** of R13, R14, and CPSR (as SPSR):

| Mode | Banked Registers |
|---|---|
| FIQ | **R8_fiq, R9_fiq, R10_fiq, R11_fiq, R12_fiq, R13_fiq (SP_fiq), R14_fiq (LR_fiq), SPSR_fiq** |
| IRQ | R13_irq (SP_irq), R14_irq (LR_irq), SPSR_irq |
| SVC | R13_svc (SP_svc), R14_svc (LR_svc), SPSR_svc |
| ABT | R13_abt (SP_abt), R14_abt (LR_abt), SPSR_abt |
| UND | R13_und (SP_und), R14_und (LR_und), SPSR_und |
| USR/SYS | None (uses unbanked set) |

**Total physical registers** = 8 unbanked (R0–R7) + 2 shared (R15, R8–R12 in non-FIQ) + 5 extra FIQ (R8–R12) + 5 × (R13, R14, SPSR) + 1 CPSR = 8 + 10 + 15 + 1 = approximately 31–37 depending on overlap accounting.

The canonical **37 registers** = **31 general-purpose 32-bit registers** (R0–R15 plus banked copies) + **6 status registers** (1 CPSR + 5 SPSRs).

---

## 2.4 Component 4 — The Program Status Registers (CPSR and SPSRs)

The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** holds the current condition flags, interrupt-disable bits, mode bits, and thumb-state bit. Each privileged mode (except System/User) has a **Saved Program Status Register (SPSR)** that captures CPSR when the exception is taken, allowing restoration on exception return.

### 2.4.1 CPSR/SPSR Bit Layout (ARM Mode)

| Bits | Field | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **[31:28]** | **N, Z, C, V** | Condition flags (Negative, Zero, Carry, oVerflow) |
| **[27:25]** | **IT[1:0], J** | IT block state, Jazelle state (Advanced) |
| **[24]** | **J** | Jazelle enable (if supported) |
| **[23:20]** | Reserved | (varies by implementation) |
| **[19:16]** | **GE[3:0]** | Greater-than-or-Equal flags (SIMD) |
| **[15:10]** | **IT[7:2]** | If-Then block state (extended Thumb) |
| **[9]** | **E** | Endianness (0 = little, 1 = big) |
| **[8]** | **A** | Imprecise Abort mask |
| **[7]** | **I** | IRQ disable (1 = masked) |
| **[6]** | **F** | FIQ disable (1 = masked) |
| **[5]** | **T** | Thumb state (0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb) |
| **[4:0]** | **M[4:0]** | Processor mode |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condition flags** (N, Z, C, V) are set automatically by **arithmetic, logical, comparison, and move** instructions (when the `S` suffix is used). They are read by **conditional branch** instructions (B<cond>).

### 2.4.2 Mode Bit Encoding (M[4:0])

| Mode | M[4:0] Binary | M[4:0] Hex |
|---|---|---|
| User | `10000` | `0x10` |
| FIQ | `10001` | `0x11` |
| IRQ | `10010` | `0x12` |
| Supervisor | `10011` | `0x13` |
| Abort | `10111` | `0x17` |
| Undefined | `11011` | `0x1B` |
| System | `11111` | `0x1F` |

> [!NOTE]
> The CPSR **M[4:0]** field can be read in any mode but **written only in privileged mode** (i.e., User mode cannot change the mode field — it is a protection mechanism).

---

## 2.5 Component 5 — Memory Organization & Endianness

### 2.5.1 Address Space
- ARM uses a **single, flat 32-bit address space** of **2^32 = 4,294,967,296 = 4 GB** bytes.
- Addresses are **byte-addressable** (every byte has a unique address).
- Word access on a non-word-aligned address may cause an **alignment exception** (on ARMv5+; unaligned access is configurable).

### 2.5.2 Endianness (Byte Ordering)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Endianness determines the byte-order of multi-byte values in memory.**

**Little-Endian (default in most modern ARM systems)** — **LSB at lowest address**.

$$\text{Address } n: \text{ byte}_{LSB}, \quad \text{Address } n+1: \text{ byte}_{2}, \quad \text{Address } n+2: \text{ byte}_{3}, \quad \text{Address } n+3: \text{ byte}_{MSB}$$

**Big-Endian** — **MSB at lowest address**.

$$\text{Address } n: \text{ byte}_{MSB}, \quad \text{Address } n+1: \text{ byte}_{3}, \quad \text{Address } n+2: \text{ byte}_{2}, \quad \text{Address } n+3: \text{ byte}_{LSB}$$

### 2.5.3 Example: Storing the 32-bit word `0x12345678` at address `0x00000004`

| Address | Big-Endian | Little-Endian |
|---|---|---|
| `0x00000004` | **0x12** | **0x78** |
| `0x00000005` | 0x34 | 0x56 |
| `0x00000006` | 0x56 | 0x34 |
| `0x00000007` | 0x78 | 0x12 |

> [!NOTE]
> ARM allows **dynamic endianness configuration** at runtime via the **CP15 (system control coprocessor)** on ARMv6 and later. The CPSR **E-bit** reflects the current endianness on some implementations.

---

## 2.6 Component 6 — Exception Model Summary

When an exception occurs, ARM hardware automatically:
1. Saves the **CPSR to SPSR_<new_mode>**.
2. Sets appropriate **CPSR.M[4:0]** to the exception mode.
3. **Disables further interrupts** (I-bit set for IRQ; F-bit set for FIQ) unless it is a Reset.
4. Sets **PC = vector address** from the vector table.
5. Saves the **return address in LR_<new_mode>** (R14_<mode>).

The **vector table** contains 8 words (32 bytes):

| Exception | Offset from Base | Base `0x00000000` (Low) | High-Vector `0xFFFF0000` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reset | `0x00` | `0x00000000` | `0xFFFF0000` |
| Undefined Instruction | `0x04` | `0x00000004` | `0xFFFF0004` |
| Software Interrupt (SWI) | `0x08` | `0x00000008` | `0xFFFF0008` |
| Prefetch Abort | `0x0C` | `0x0000000C` | `0xFFFF000C` |
| Data Abort | `0x10` | `0x00000010` | `0xFFFF0010` |
| Reserved | `0x14` | `0x00000014` | `0xFFFF0014` |
| IRQ | `0x18` | `0x00000018` | `0xFFFF0018` |
| FIQ | `0x1C` | `0x0000001C` | `0xFFFF001C` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIQ is intentionally the LAST entry** in the vector table. The FIQ handler can begin executing immediately at the vector address, allowing the exception routine to **absorb the branch overhead** that other exceptions need (which branch via `LDR PC, [PC, #offset]`).

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The "formulas" for the ARM Programmer's Model are mostly architectural facts and addressing patterns, not algebraic equations. The table below consolidates the high-yield items.

| # | Concept | Rule / Formula / Boundary | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Address space | $2^{32} = 4\,\text{GB} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ bytes | Flat 32-bit virtual space |
| 2 | Word size | $\text{Word} = 32\,\text{bits} = 4\,\text{bytes}$ | Largest operand size |
| 3 | PC value during execution | $\text{PC}_{\text{read}} = \text{current address} + 8$ | Pipeline: 2 stages ahead in ARM state |
| 4 | PC value during Thumb execution | $\text{PC}_{\text{read}} = \text{current address} + 4$ | 1 stage ahead in Thumb state |
| 5 | Address increment for `STM/LDM` | $\text{next\_addr} = R_n \pm 4\,\text{bytes} \times \text{reg count}$ | Block transfer 4-byte stride |
| 6 | N flag (Negative) | $N = 1$ if bit 31 of result = 1 | Signed result sign bit |
| 7 | Z flag (Zero) | $Z = 1$ if result = 0 | All bits zero |
| 8 | C flag (Carry) | $C = 1$ if unsigned overflow (add) or borrow=0 (sub) | Multi-precision arithmetic |
| 9 | V flag (Overflow) | $V = 1$ if signed overflow occurred | Two's-complement overflow |
| 10 | Mode field write | Allowed only in privileged mode | User mode is read-only for M[4:0] |
| 11 | I-bit | $I = 1 \Rightarrow$ IRQ masked | CPSR[7] |
| 12 | F-bit | $F = 1 \Rightarrow$ FIQ masked | CPSR[6] |
| 13 | T-bit | $T = 1 \Rightarrow$ Thumb state | CPSR[5] |
| 14 | E-bit | $E = 1 \Rightarrow$ Big-endian | CPSR[9] (impl. dependent) |
| 15 | Vector table size | $8 \times 4 = 32$ bytes | 8 word-sized entries |
| 16 | Big-endian byte position | $\text{byte}_k$ at $\text{addr} + (n - 1 - k)$ where $n = $ size in bytes | MSB at lowest address |
| 17 | Little-endian byte position | $\text{byte}_k$ at $\text{addr} + k$ | LSB at lowest address |
| 18 | Exception return value | $\text{LR}_{\text{new\_mode}} \rightarrow \text{PC}, \quad \text{SPSR} \rightarrow \text{CPSR}$ | Restored at exception return |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU 2024**: The PC offset of **+8** in ARM state and **+4** in Thumb state is a **very common exam question**. The reason is the 3-stage pipeline (Fetch → Decode → Execute); at execute time, PC is always pointing **2 instructions (8 bytes) ahead** in ARM, or **1 instruction (4 bytes) ahead** in Thumb.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of the ARM Programmer's Model |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Firmware** | Writing bare-metal startup code (vector table setup, mode switching, stack-pointer initialization per mode). |
| **RTOS Development** | Context switching relies on the banked R13/R14/SPSR to save and restore state on each mode entry. |
| **Compiler Design** | LLVM / GCC ARM backend must honor ABI conventions (R0 = return value, R4–R11 = callee-saved, R13 = SP). |
| **Debugger / IDE** | GDB's register display in different modes reflects the banked-register model. |
| **Device Drivers** | Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs) use `STMFD SP!, {R0-R12, LR}` to save state — relying on banked SP for the IRQ mode. |
| **Security & OS Kernels** | Mode privilege separation prevents User applications from corrupting privileged system state. |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Endianness Memory Layout

**Problem**: Given the 32-bit word value `0xA5B6C7D8`, store it at memory address `0x20000000`. Show the byte-by-byte memory layout for **both** little-endian and big-endian modes.

### Step 1 — Decompose the word into its 4 bytes

The 32-bit word `0xA5B6C7D8` consists of the following bytes (from MSB to LSB):

| Byte | Hex Value | Bit Range | Position |
|---|---|---|---|
| Byte 3 (MSB) | `0xA5` | [31:24] | Most significant |
| Byte 2 | `0xB6` | [23:16] | |
| Byte 1 | `0xC7` | [15:8] | |
| Byte 0 (LSB) | `0xD8` | [7:0] | Least significant |

The decomposition uses the formula:

$$\text{byte}_k = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{word}}{256^{\,k}} \right\rfloor \bmod 256, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, 3$$

**Applying the formula:**

$$\text{byte}_0 = \left\lfloor \frac{0xA5B6C7D8}{1} \right\rfloor \bmod 256 = 0xD8 \quad (\text{LSB})$$

$$\text{byte}_1 = \left\lfloor \frac{0xA5B6C7D8}{256} \right\rfloor \bmod 256 = \left\lfloor \frac{0xA5B6C7D}{1} \right\rfloor \bmod 256 = 0xC7$$

$$\text{byte}_2 = \left\lfloor \frac{0xA5B6C7D8}{65{,}536} \right\rfloor \bmod 256 = \left\lfloor \frac{0xA5B6C7}{1} \right\rfloor \bmod 256 = 0xB6$$

$$\text{byte}_3 = \left\lfloor \frac{0xA5B6C7D8}{16{,}777{,}216} \right\rfloor \bmod 256 = 0xA5 \quad (\text{MSB})$$

### Step 2 — Compute addresses

The base address is `0x20000000`. The byte-k is placed at:

$$A_k = 0x20000000 + k, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, 3$$

Therefore:

$$A_0 = 0x20000000, \quad A_1 = 0x20000001, \quad A_2 = 0x20000002, \quad A_3 = 0x20000003$$

### Step 3 — Apply endianness rule

**Little-Endian Rule (LSB at lowest address):**

$$\text{Byte}_k \text{ at address } \text{base} + k$$

| Address | Content (Little-Endian) | Computation |
|---|---|---|
| `0x20000000` | **0xD8** | LSB (byte_0) at offset 0 |
| `0x20000001` | **0xC7** | byte_1 at offset 1 |
| `0x20000002` | **0xB6** | byte_2 at offset 2 |
| `0x20000003` | **0xA5** | MSB (byte_3) at offset 3 |

**Big-Endian Rule (MSB at lowest address):**

$$\text{Byte}_k \text{ at address } \text{base} + (3 - k)$$

| Address | Content (Big-Endian) | Computation |
|---|---|---|
| `0x20000000` | **0xA5** | MSB (byte_3) at offset 0 |
| `0x20000001` | **0xB6** | byte_2 at offset 1 |
| `0x20000002` | **0xC7** | byte_1 at offset 2 |
| `0x20000003` | **0xD8** | LSB (byte_0) at offset 3 |

### Step 4 — Verification

**Read back as a 32-bit little-endian word from `0x20000000`:**

$$\text{word} = (0xA5 \ll 24) \,\vert\, (0xB6 \ll 16) \,\vert\, (0xC7 \ll 8) \,\vert\, (0xD8 \ll 0)$$

$$\text{word} = 0xA5B6C7D8 \quad \checkmark$$

**Read back as a 32-bit big-endian word from `0x20000000`:**

$$\text{word} = (0xA5 \ll 24) \,\vert\, (0xB6 \ll 16) \,\vert\, (0xC7 \ll 8) \,\vert\, (0xD8 \ll 0)$$

$$\text{word} = 0xA5B6C7D8 \quad \checkmark$$

Both endianness schemes recover the original value when the same convention is used for read and write.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — CPSR Value Computation After Exception

**Problem**: An ARM processor is executing in **User mode** with `CPSR = 0x00000010`. An **IRQ interrupt** occurs. Compute the **new CPSR** after the hardware automatically takes the exception.

### Step 1 — Identify initial state

The initial CPSR value is given as:

$$\text{CPSR}_{\text{initial}} = 0x00000010$$

Breaking this into binary:

$$\text{CPSR} = 0b\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0001\,0000$$

| Field | Bit | Value |
|---|---|---|
| M[4:0] | [4:0] | `10000` (User mode) ✓ |
| T (bit 5) | [5] | 0 (ARM state) |
| F (bit 6) | [6] | 0 (FIQ enabled) |
| I (bit 7) | [7] | 0 (IRQ enabled) |
| Other bits | [31:8] | 0 |

### Step 2 — Apply IRQ exception entry rules

When an IRQ is taken, the ARM hardware performs the following changes to the CPSR:

$$\text{CPSR}_{\text{new}} = \text{CPSR}_{\text{initial}} \quad\text{with modifications:}$$

1. **M[4:0] ← 10010** (IRQ mode) → bits [4:0] become `10010`
2. **I ← 1** (disable further IRQs) → bit [7] = 1
3. **T ← 0** (force ARM state on entry) → bit [5] = 0 (already 0)
4. **F unchanged** (typically 0) → bit [6] = 0

### Step 3 — Build the new CPSR

The bits of the new CPSR:

| Field | Binary | Hex |
|---|---|---|
| Bits [31:8] | `0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000` | `0x000000` |
| Bit [7] = I = 1 | `1` | `0x80` |
| Bit [6] = F = 0 | `0` | `0x00` |
| Bit [5] = T = 0 | `0` | `0x00` |
| Bits [4:0] = M = `10010` | `10010` | `0x12` |

Adding the components:

$$\text{CPSR}_{\text{new}} = 0x00000000 \,\vert\, 0x80 \,\vert\, 0x00 \,\vert\, 0x00 \,\vert\, 0x12$$

$$\text{CPSR}_{\text{new}} = 0x00000092$$

### Step 4 — Verify

Reading back the fields of `0x92`:

$$\text{0x92} = 0b\,1001\,0010$$

- **Bit 7** (I) = 1 ✓ (IRQ disabled)
- **Bit 6** (F) = 0 ✓ (FIQ still enabled)
- **Bit 5** (T) = 0 ✓ (ARM state)
- **M[4:0]** = `10010` ✓ (IRQ mode = 0x12)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The original **CPSR is saved into SPSR_irq** simultaneously: `SPSR_irq = 0x00000010`. The return address is saved in `R14_irq = PC + 4` (where PC is the address of the instruction that would have executed next had the IRQ not occurred).

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — PC Behavior in Pipeline

**Problem**: The ARM processor is currently fetching the instruction at address `0x80001000` in **ARM state**. The pipeline is 3 stages (Fetch, Decode, Execute). What value will be read when an instruction uses `R15 (PC)` as a source operand, and what is the address of the next instruction to execute?

### Step 1 — Identify the 3-stage pipeline positions

At the moment of execution, the pipeline holds:

| Stage | Address | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Execute | `0x80001000` | Current instruction (R15 == this) |
| Decode | `0x80001004` | Next instruction (PC+4) |
| Fetch | `0x80001008` | Next+1 instruction (PC+8) |

### Step 2 — Compute R15 read value

When the executing instruction at `0x80001000` reads R15 (PC), the architectural value is:

$$\text{R15}_{\text{read}} = \text{address of current instruction} + 8$$

$$\text{R15}_{\text{read}} = 0x80001000 + 8 = 0x80001008$$

### Step 3 — Explanation of the +8 offset

The **+8** offset is **not arbitrary** — it represents the fact that PC is hardwired to point to the **instruction currently in the Fetch stage** (i.e., two ARM instructions, or 8 bytes, ahead of the current executing instruction). This makes the program counter behave like a "look-ahead" pointer, simplifying relative addressing.

### Step 4 — Address of next instruction

After the current instruction at `0x80001000` completes, execution continues at:

$$\text{NextPC} = 0x80001000 + 4 = 0x80001004$$

For a branch, the offset in the branch instruction is computed from the **current PC + 8**, then the new PC is:

$$\text{NewPC} = \text{current address} + 8 + \text{offset}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Register Visibility Per Mode

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below shows which logical registers are accessible in each processor mode. "✓" means the register is directly accessible; blank means the banked version is not present in that mode.

| Register | User | System | Supervisor | FIQ | IRQ | Abort | Undefined |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R0–R7 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| R8 | ✓ (R8) | ✓ (R8) | ✓ (R8) | ✓ (R8_fiq) | ✓ (R8) | ✓ (R8) | ✓ (R8) |
| R9 | ✓ (R9) | ✓ (R9) | ✓ (R9) | ✓ (R9_fiq) | ✓ (R9) | ✓ (R9) | ✓ (R9) |
| R10 | ✓ (R10) | ✓ (R10) | ✓ (R10) | ✓ (R10_fiq) | ✓ (R10) | ✓ (R10) | ✓ (R10) |
| R11 | ✓ (R11) | ✓ (R11) | ✓ (R11) | ✓ (R11_fiq) | ✓ (R11) | ✓ (R11) | ✓ (R11) |
| R12 | ✓ (R12) | ✓ (R12) | ✓ (R12) | ✓ (R12_fiq) | ✓ (R12) | ✓ (R12) | ✓ (R12) |
| R13 (SP) | ✓ (SP_usr) | ✓ (SP_usr) | ✓ (SP_svc) | ✓ (SP_fiq) | ✓ (SP_irq) | ✓ (SP_abt) | ✓ (SP_und) |
| R14 (LR) | ✓ (LR_usr) | ✓ (LR_usr) | ✓ (LR_svc) | ✓ (LR_fiq) | ✓ (LR_irq) | ✓ (LR_abt) | ✓ (LR_und) |
| R15 (PC) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| CPSR | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| SPSR | — | — | ✓ (SPSR_svc) | ✓ (SPSR_fiq) | ✓ (SPSR_irq) | ✓ (SPSR_abt) | ✓ (SPSR_und) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the asymmetry**: FIQ has **5 extra banked registers (R8–R12)**, while IRQ, SVC, ABT, and UND have **only 3 banked registers (R13, R14, SPSR)** each. This is a deliberate hardware design to **accelerate FIQ latency**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Exception Vector Addresses (Low-Vector, Base = 0x00000000)

| # | Exception | Vector Address | Vector Offset | Return Instruction in Handler |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reset | `0x00000000` | `+0x00` | (System boots — no return) |
| 2 | Undefined Instruction | `0x00000004` | `+0x04` | `MOVS PC, R14_und` |
| 3 | Software Interrupt (SWI) | `0x00000008` | `+0x08` | `MOVS PC, R14_svc` |
| 4 | Prefetch Abort | `0x0000000C` | `+0x0C` | `SUBS PC, R14_abt, #4` |
| 5 | Data Abort | `0x00000010` | `+0x10` | `SUBS PC, R14_abt, #8` |
| 6 | IRQ | `0x00000018` | `+0x18` | `SUBS PC, R14_irq, #4` |
| 7 | FIQ | `0x0000001C` | `+0x1C` | `SUBS PC, R14_fiq, #4` |

> [!NOTE]
> The **"SUBS PC, LR, #offset"** idiom is the **standard exception return** instruction. The `S` suffix causes CPSR to be **simultaneously restored from SPSR**, achieving a single-instruction atomic return with state restoration.

---

## 3.6 Condition Code Truth Table (KTU High-Yield)

| Cond | Mnemonic | Meaning | CPSR Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| `EQ` | Equal | Z = 1 | Last result = 0 |
| `NE` | Not Equal | Z = 0 | Last result ≠ 0 |
| `CS` / `HS` | Carry Set / Unsigned Higher or Same | C = 1 | Unsigned ≥ |
| `CC` / `LO` | Carry Clear / Unsigned Lower | C = 0 | Unsigned < |
| `MI` | Minus / Negative | N = 1 | Bit 31 = 1 |
| `PL` | Plus / Positive or Zero | N = 0 | Bit 31 = 0 |
| `VS` | Overflow Set | V = 1 | Signed overflow |
| `VC` | Overflow Clear | V = 0 | No signed overflow |
| `HI` | Unsigned Higher | C = 1 AND Z = 0 | Unsigned > |
| `LS` | Unsigned Lower or Same | C = 0 OR Z = 1 | Unsigned ≤ |
| `GE` | Signed Greater or Equal | N = V | Signed ≥ |
| `LT` | Signed Less Than | N ≠ V | Signed < |
| `GT` | Signed Greater Than | Z = 0 AND N = V | Signed > |
| `LE` | Signed Less or Equal | Z = 1 OR N ≠ V | Signed ≤ |
| `AL` | Always | (unconditional) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **`GE`, `LT`, `GT`, `LE`** conditions are **signed comparisons** (using **N** and **V**). The **`HI`, `LS`** conditions are **unsigned comparisons** (using **C** and **Z**). Confusing signed-vs-unsigned in conditional branches is a **frequent source of bugs** in assembly code.
