# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The 37-Register File Organization (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph UNBANKED[UNBANKED REGISTERS - SHARED BY ALL MODES]
        R0[R0]
        R1[R1]
        R2[R2]
        R3[R3]
        R4[R4]
        R5[R5]
        R6[R6]
        R7[R7]
        PC[R15 PC - SHARED]
    end

    subgraph FIQGRP[FIQ-ONLY BANKED REGISTERS]
        R8FIQ[R8_fiq]
        R9FIQ[R9_fiq]
        R10FIQ[R10_fiq]
        R11FIQ[R11_fiq]
        R12FIQ[R12_fiq]
    end

    subgraph OTHERMODES[R8-R12 SHARED IN NON-FIQ MODES]
        R8OTH[R8]
        R9OTH[R9]
        R10OTH[R10]
        R11OTH[R11]
        R12OTH[R12]
    end

    subgraph MODEBANK[MODE-BANKED R13 R14 SPSR - 5 COPIES]
        SVCGR[SVC: SP_svc, LR_svc, SPSR_svc]
        FIQGR[FIQ: SP_fiq, LR_fiq, SPSR_fiq]
        IRQGR[IRQ: SP_irq, LR_irq, SPSR_irq]
        ABTGR[ABT: SP_abt, LR_abt, SPSR_abt]
        UNDGR[UND: SP_und, LR_und, SPSR_und]
    end

    CPSR_REG[CPSR - 1 REGISTER SHARED BY ALL MODES]

    R0 --> CPSR_REG
    R1 --> CPSR_REG
    PC --> CPSR_REG
    SVCGR --> CPSR_REG
    FIQGR --> CPSR_REG
    IRQGR --> CPSR_REG
    ABTGR --> CPSR_REG
    UNDGR --> CPSR_REG
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above shows the **physical register file**. **R8–R12** have two physical copies (one shared by USR/SYS/IRQ/SVC/ABT/UND, and one extra for FIQ), making the **FIQ exception handler able to use R8–R12 without saving them to the stack**.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Processor Mode State Transition

```mermaid
graph TD
    USER[USER MODE 10000]
    SYSTEM[SYSTEM MODE 11111]
    SVC[SUPERVISOR MODE 10011]
    FIQ[FIQ MODE 10001]
    IRQ[IRQ MODE 10010]
    ABT[ABORT MODE 10111]
    UND[UNDEFINED MODE 11011]

    RESET_EVT((RESET)) --> SVC
    SWI_EVT((SWI / SVC CALL)) --> SVC
    UNDEF_EVT((UNDEFINED INSTRUCTION)) --> UND
    ABORT_P_EVT((PREFETCH ABORT)) --> ABT
    ABORT_D_EVT((DATA ABORT)) --> ABT
    IRQ_EVT((IRQ INTERRUPT)) --> IRQ
    FIQ_EVT((FIQ INTERRUPT)) --> FIQ

    USER -.->|exception| IRQ_EVT
    USER -.->|exception| FIQ_EVT
    SVC -.->|software write to CPSR.M| USER
    SYSTEM -.->|software write to CPSR.M| USER
    IRQ -.->|SUBS PC, LR, 4| USER
    SVC -.->|MOVS PC, LR| USER
    ABT -.->|SUBS PC, LR, 8| USER
    UND -.->|MOVS PC, LR| USER
    FIQ -.->|SUBS PC, LR, 4| USER
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight**: The `MOVS PC, LR` and `SUBS PC, LR, #n` instructions are the **only legal way** to return from an exception because they **atomically restore CPSR from SPSR** and write the new PC. A plain `MOV PC, LR` would NOT restore the mode bits.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Vector Table Layout (Low-Vector Configuration)

```mermaid
graph LR
    V0[0x00 RESET<br/>B Reset_Handler]
    V1[0x04 UNDEF<br/>LDR PC, Undef_Addr]
    V2[0x08 SWI<br/>LDR PC, SWI_Addr]
    V3[0x0C PREFETCH ABORT<br/>LDR PC, PAbt_Addr]
    V4[0x10 DATA ABORT<br/>LDR PC, DAbt_Addr]
    V5[0x14 RESERVED]
    V6[0x18 IRQ<br/>LDR PC, IRQ_Addr]
    V7[0x1C FIQ<br/>FIQ HANDLER DIRECT<br/>BEGINS HERE]

    V0 --> V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4 --> V5 --> V6 --> V7
```

> [!NOTE]
> The FIQ vector at `0x1C` is the **last** entry. Because it is the last, the FIQ handler can be placed **immediately at `0x1C`**, eliminating the need for a branch instruction (which would have been required for the other vectors to jump to the actual handler code placed further away). This is the **zero-overhead FIQ optimization**.

---

```mermaid
graph LR

    B22[B22 RESERVED]
    B21[B21 RESERVED]
    B20[B20 RESERVED]

    B18[B18 GE2]

    B16[B16 GE0]

    B14[B14 IT0]
    B13[B13 RESERVED]
    B12[B12 RESERVED]
    B11[B11 RESERVED]
    B10[B10 RESERVED]
    B9[B9 E ENDIANNESS]
    B8[B8 A IMPRECISE ABORT MASK]
    B7[B7 I IRQ DISABLE]
    B6[B6 F FIQ DISABLE]
    B5[B5 T THUMB STATE]
    B4[B4 M4 MODE BIT 4]
    B3[B3 M3 MODE BIT 3]
    B2[B2 M2 MODE BIT 2]
    B1[B1 M1 MODE BIT 1]
    B0[B0 M0 MODE BIT 0]

    B31 --- B30 --- B29 --- B28 --- B27 --- B26 --- B25 --- B24
    B24 --- B23 --- B22 --- B21 --- B20 --- B19 --- B18 --- B17

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Reading the **M[4:0]** bits (B4–B0) gives the **mode code**: `0b10000` = 0x10 (User), `0b10010` = 0x12 (IRQ), `0b10011` = 0x13 (SVC), `0b11111` = 0x1F (System), etc.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: List the seven processor modes of the ARM architecture. What is the role of the FIQ mode's extra banked registers (R8_fiq–R12_fiq)?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The seven processor modes are: **(1) User (usr)**, **(2) FIQ (fiq)**, **(3) IRQ (irq)**, **(4) Supervisor (svc)**, **(5) Abort (abt)**, **(6) Undefined (und)**, and **(7) System (sys)**.
>
> The FIQ mode has **5 extra banked registers (R8_fiq–R12_fiq)** in addition to the standard R13_fiq, R14_fiq, and SPSR_fiq. This allows the **FIQ handler to use these registers without pushing them onto the stack**, achieving a **zero-overhead context switch** for high-speed interrupt processing. This is critical because FIQ is intended for **time-sensitive** operations such as high-data-rate peripherals.
>
> *(Marking: 1 mark for listing 5+ modes, 1 mark for listing all 7, 1 mark for the role of R8_fiq–R12_fiq.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between the CPSR and SPSR registers in the ARM programmer's model. In which modes is the SPSR available?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** is a **single, 32-bit register** that holds the **current** state of the processor, including the condition flags (N, Z, C, V), interrupt mask bits (I, F), Thumb state (T), endianness (E), and the mode field (M[4:0]). It is accessible in **all 7 processor modes**.
>
> The **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register)** is a **set of 5 banked registers** (one per exception mode: SPSR_fiq, SPSR_irq, SPSR_svc, SPSR_abt, SPSR_und) that **automatically captures the value of CPSR** when an exception is taken. This allows the original CPSR to be **restored atomically** when the exception handler returns, using the `MOVS PC, LR` or `SUBS PC, LR, #n` instruction.
>
> **SPSR is NOT available in User mode or System mode** — these two modes share the user-register set and do not have a private SPSR.
>
> *(Marking: 1 mark for CPSR description, 1 mark for SPSR description, 1 mark for the exception-mode-only availability.)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the ARM programmer's model with a neat diagram showing the register set across different processor modes. (7 marks)**

**Q: (b) With a neat sketch, describe the CPSR register format in ARM state. Explain each field with its function. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B-A (a)

**1. Introduction to the ARM Programmer's Model** *(1 mark)*

The ARM programmer's model is the **abstract view of the processor** visible to the software. It includes:
- The register set (organized by mode)
- The Program Status Registers (CPSR, SPSR)
- Data types
- Processor modes
- Memory organization
- Exception handling

**2. The 7 Processor Modes** *(2 marks)*

| Mode | Purpose | Privilege |
|---|---|---|
| User (usr) | Normal application | Non-privileged |
| FIQ (fiq) | Fast interrupt | Privileged |
| IRQ (irq) | Normal interrupt | Privileged |
| Supervisor (svc) | SWI / Reset | Privileged |
| Abort (abt) | Memory access violation | Privileged |
| Undefined (und) | Undefined instruction | Privileged |
| System (sys) | OS kernel tasks | Privileged |

**3. Register Organization** *(3 marks)*

The ARM architecture has **37 physical 32-bit registers**:
- **31 general-purpose 32-bit registers** (R0–R15 plus banked copies)
- **6 status registers** (1 CPSR + 5 SPSRs)

In **User mode**, only **R0–R15 (16 registers) + CPSR** are visible.

In **FIQ mode**, the programmer sees **R0–R7, R8_fiq–R12_fiq, R13_fiq, R14_fiq, R15, CPSR, SPSR_fiq** (i.e., 7 unbanked + 7 banked + PC + CPSR + SPSR).

R13 is conventionally **SP (Stack Pointer)**, R14 is **LR (Link Register)**, and R15 is **PC (Program Counter)**.

**4. Diagram (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes)** *(1 mark)*

**Marking Summary:** 1 + 2 + 3 + 1 = **7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B-A (b)

**1. CPSR Format Overview** *(1 mark)*

The **CPSR is a 32-bit register** with the following fields (most significant to least significant):

**2. Field-by-Field Description** *(5 marks)*

| Bits | Field | Function |
|---|---|---|
| [31:28] | **N, Z, C, V** | Condition flags: Negative, Zero, Carry, Overflow. Set by data-processing instructions with the `S` suffix. Used by conditional branch instructions. |
| [27:25] | **IT[1:0], J** | If-Then block state and Jazelle enable (advanced features). |
| [24] | **J** | Jazelle state (Java bytecode execution). |
| [23:20] | Reserved | Varies by implementation. |
| [19:16] | **GE[3:0]** | Greater-than-or-Equal flags for SIMD operations. |
| [15:10] | **IT[7:2]** | If-Then block state (extended for Thumb-2). |
| [9] | **E** | Endianness bit: 0 = little-endian, 1 = big-endian. |
| [8] | **A** | Imprecise abort mask. |
| [7] | **I** | IRQ disable: 1 = IRQ masked. |
| [6] | **F** | FIQ disable: 1 = FIQ masked. |
| [5] | **T** | Thumb state: 0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb. |
| [4:0] | **M[4:0]** | Processor mode code. |

**3. Mode Bit Encoding** *(1 mark)*

| Mode | M[4:0] |
|---|---|
| User | 10000 |
| FIQ | 10001 |
| IRQ | 10010 |
| Supervisor | 10011 |
| Abort | 10111 |
| Undefined | 11011 |
| System | 11111 |

**Marking Summary:** 1 + 5 + 1 = **7 marks**

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) — **ALTERNATIVE**

**Q: (a) Explain the various processor modes in ARM architecture. How does the mode-switching mechanism work during exception handling? (7 marks)**

**Q: (b) What is endianness? Compare little-endian and big-endian formats with an example. State how the ARM architecture supports both. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B-B (a)

**1. ARM Processor Modes** *(3 marks)*

ARM has 7 modes, classified into:

**Privileged modes (6):** FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System — full read/write access to CPSR, including the mode field.

**Non-privileged mode (1):** User — can only read CPSR.M[4:0] but cannot change it.

**Purpose of each mode:**
- **User**: Application code runs here.
- **FIQ**: Fast interrupt for time-critical data transfer.
- **IRQ**: Standard interrupt service.
- **Supervisor**: Software interrupt (SWI/SVC) and Reset entry.
- **Abort**: Memory access failure (prefetch or data).
- **Undefined**: Unrecognized instruction execution.
- **System**: Privileged mode using the User register set (no banked SP/LR).

**2. Mode Switching on Exception** *(3 marks)*

When an exception is taken, the ARM hardware **automatically** performs:
1. Copies **CPSR → SPSR_<new_mode>**.
2. Updates **CPSR.M[4:0] ← exception mode code**.
3. Sets **CPSR.I = 1** (for IRQ) or **CPSR.F = 1** (for FIQ) to mask further interrupts.
4. Forces **CPSR.T = 0** (ARM state on entry).
5. Sets **PC = vector address** (from the vector table).
6. Saves the **return address in LR_<new_mode>** (R14_<mode>).

**3. Exception Return** *(1 mark)*

The standard return idiom is `SUBS PC, LR, #n` (where `n` depends on the exception type). The `S` suffix causes **CPSR ← SPSR**, atomically restoring mode and flags.

**Marking Summary:** 3 + 3 + 1 = **7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B-B (b)

**1. Definition of Endianness** *(1 mark)*

Endianness defines the **byte-ordering convention** used to store multi-byte values in memory. It specifies which byte (MSB or LSB) is stored at the **lowest memory address**.

**2. Little-Endian Format** *(2 marks)*

In little-endian, the **Least Significant Byte (LSB)** is stored at the **lowest address**. The bytes are stored in the order [LSB, ..., MSB] as the address increases.

**Example**: Store `0x12345678` at address `0x1000`.

| Address | Byte |
|---|---|
| `0x1000` | 0x78 (LSB) |
| `0x1001` | 0x56 |
| `0x1002` | 0x34 |
| `0x1003` | 0x12 (MSB) |

**3. Big-Endian Format** *(2 marks)*

In big-endian, the **Most Significant Byte (MSB)** is stored at the **lowest address**. The bytes are stored in the order [MSB, ..., LSB] as the address increases.

**Example**: Store `0x12345678` at address `0x1000`.

| Address | Byte |
|---|---|
| `0x1000` | 0x12 (MSB) |
| `0x1001` | 0x34 |
| `0x1002` | 0x56 |
| `0x1003` | 0x78 (LSB) |

**4. ARM's Support for Both** *(2 marks)*

- ARM supports **both little-endian and big-endian** operation, configurable at design time or at reset (via external pin BIGENDINIT or via CP15 register on ARMv6+).
- The **E-bit in CPSR** (bit 9) reflects the current endianness on some implementations.
- **Little-endian is the default** in most modern ARM-based systems (e.g., ARM Cortex-A, Cortex-M).
- The **vector table** endianness: in little-endian mode, the vector table is treated as little-endian; in big-endian mode, it is treated as big-endian.
- The endianness affects **load-store multiple (LDM/STM)** byte interpretation but not the internal data-processing operations.

**Marking Summary:** 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 = **7 marks**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the ARM Programmer's Model Question**
>
> 1. **PC Offset Confusion (–2 marks typical)**: Forgetting that `R15 (PC)` reads as `current_address + 8` in ARM state and `current_address + 4` in Thumb state. Many students incorrectly write "PC points to the current instruction" — this is WRONG due to the pipeline.
>
> 2. **Confusing CPSR Access in User Mode (–1 mark)**: Writing that "User mode can change the mode field of CPSR." This is **not allowed** — User mode is non-privileged and cannot modify CPSR.M[4:0].
>
> 3. **Forgetting the SPSR Restoring Semantics (–2 marks)**: Writing `MOV PC, LR` to return from an exception. This is **WRONG** — you must use `MOVS PC, LR` (or `SUBS PC, LR, #n`). The `S` suffix is what restores CPSR from SPSR.
>
> 4. **Endianness Byte-Order Errors (–2 marks)**: Writing little-endian as MSB-first. Always remember: **Little-endian = LSB at the lowest address**.
>
> 5. **Banked Register Count Mistakes (–1 mark)**: Claiming FIQ has 3 extra banked registers. It has **5** extra (R8–R12_fiq), in addition to R13_fiq, R14_fiq, SPSR_fiq.
>
> 6. **Vector Table Address Confusion (–1 mark)**: Saying the vector table is at `0x00000004` (it is at `0x00000000` for low-vector config; the entries START at offsets `+0x00, +0x04, +0x08, ...`).
>
> 7. **Mixing Up IRQ Return Offset**: IRQ uses **`SUBS PC, LR, #4`**, not `#8`. Data abort uses **`SUBS PC, LR, #8`**, not `#4`. Confusing these is a frequent error.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist for last-minute exam revision.**

- [x] **ARM has 7 modes**: User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System.
- [x] **User mode is the only non-privileged mode**; all others are privileged.
- [x] **37 physical registers** = 31 general-purpose + 6 status (1 CPSR + 5 SPSRs).
- [x] **R13 = SP, R14 = LR, R15 = PC** (conventionally, not architecturally enforced).
- [x] **R8–R12 are banked in FIQ only** (5 extra registers for fast context save).
- [x] **R13, R14, SPSR are banked** in all 5 exception modes (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND).
- [x] **System mode shares the User register set** but is privileged.
- [x] **SPSR is NOT available in User or System mode**.
- [x] **CPSR fields**: N[31], Z[30], C[29], V[28], I[7], F[6], T[5], M[4:0].
- [x] **Mode field values**: User=0x10, FIQ=0x11, IRQ=0x12, SVC=0x13, ABT=0x17, UND=0x1B, SYS=0x1F.
- [x] **PC reads as +8 in ARM state**, **+4 in Thumb state** (pipeline offset).
- [x] **Little-endian = LSB at low address** (default in modern ARM).
- [x] **Big-endian = MSB at low address** (configurable in ARMv6+).
- [x] **Address space = 2^32 = 4 GB**, byte-addressable, linear flat space.
- [x] **Vector table** = 8 words (32 bytes) at `0x00000000` (low) or `0xFFFF0000` (high).
- [x] **FIQ is the LAST entry** (`0x1C`) for zero-overhead exception entry.
- [x] **Exception return** uses `SUBS PC, LR, #n` (not `MOV PC, LR`) to restore CPSR.
- [x] **Return offsets**: IRQ/FIQ/PAbt = #4, SWI/Undef = #0, DAbt = #8.
- [x] **Load-store architecture**: only LDR/STR access memory; ALU works on registers only.
- [x] **Data types**: Byte (8-bit), Halfword (16-bit), Word (32-bit).
- [x] **ARM is a RISC** architecture with 37 registers visible in mode-aware fashion.
- [x] **Condition flags N, Z, C, V** are set by `S`-suffix instructions and consumed by conditional branches (`B<cond>`).
- [x] **Signed comparisons** (GE, LT, GT, LE) use N and V; **unsigned** (HI, LS) use C and Z.
- [x] **Reset enters Supervisor mode** with I=1, F=1, T=0 (ARM state).
- [x] **FIQ mask is independent of IRQ mask** — set F-bit to mask FIQ only, I-bit for IRQ only.

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — THE ARM PROGRAMMER'S MODEL**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Development Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** ARM Development Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARM Development Tools** constitute the integrated set of hardware and software utilities required to design, compile, assemble, debug, and validate firmware for ARM-based microcontrollers and microprocessors. The canonical reference toolchain for KTU-aligned ARM7TDMI/LPC2148 and Cortex-M coursework comprises the **Keil µVision IDE**, the **ARM/RealView Compiler (armcc)**, the **ARM Assembler (armasm)**, the **ARMLinker/Locator (armlink)**, the **µVision Debugger**, the **Instruction Set Simulator (ULINK)**, and on-chip debug hardware such as **JTAG** and **Serial Wire Debug (SWD)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, ARM development tools form the *cross-development environment* — a host-resident software stack that produces a target-resident binary (`.axf`, `.hex`, or `.bin`) destined for an embedded ARM device with constrained memory, no operating system, and real-time I/O constraints.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Embedded Workshop

Imagine you are a **watchmaker** (the embedded engineer) who builds intricate mechanical watches (the embedded firmware) inside a tiny, sealed casing (the ARM microcontroller). You cannot reach inside the watch while it is ticking.

To build and verify the watch, you need:

| Watchmaker's Tool | ARM Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Magnifying bench | **Keil µVision IDE** | Workspace to design & observe code |
| Precision screwdriver set | **Editor + Project Manager** | Writing and organizing source files |
| Lathe & milling machine | **armcc Compiler + armasm Assembler** | Translating human-readable specs to machine parts |
| Fitting & assembly jig | **armlink (Linker + Locator)** | Joining parts and placing them on the PCB |
| Diagnostic loupe & X-ray | **µVision Debugger + ULINK** | Non-invasive inspection of internal state |
| Quality test bench | **Simulation / Flash Programming** | Verifying correct ticking before delivery |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Cross-Development**: The code is *compiled on a host PC* (Intel/AMD x86 running Windows) but *executes on a target ARM device*. The compiler therefore is called a **Cross-Compiler**, and the host-target bridge is the **debug probe (JTAG/SWD)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in ARM Toolchains

- **JTAG clock frequency (TCK)**: typically **1 MHz – 50 MHz** depending on the target.
- **Standard ARM debug interface width**: **5-pin JTAG (TDI, TDO, TMS, TCK, nTRST)** or **2-pin SWD (SWDIO, SWCLK)**.
- **Reset vector address for ARM7TDMI**: **0x00000000**; for **Cortex-M3** it is **0x00000000** stored in the vector table.
- **Default Flash start address for LPC2148**: **0x00000000**.
- **Default RAM start address for LPC2148**: **0x40000000** (**32 KB** on-chip RAM for the LPC2148, expandable to **512 KB** Flash).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of the ARM Development Toolchain (Hierarchical Decomposition)

### Layer 1 — The Editor & Project Manager (Keil µVision)
- Provides **syntax-highlighted source editors** for `.c`, `.h`, `.s` (assembly), and `.scat` (scatter) files.
- Manages **multi-source projects**, **target configurations**, and **build options** (optimization level `-O0/-O1/-O2/-O3`, MCU selection, memory model).
- Generates an **auto-generated dependency graph** between translation units.

### Layer 2 — The Compiler (`armcc` — ARM Compiler 5/6)
- **Front-end**: Parses ISO C/C++ source.
- **Back-end**: Produces ARM/Thumb machine code using the *Target Description* of the selected MCU.
- Honours the **AAPCS** (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard) for register usage: `R0–R3` = argument/scratch, `R4–R11` = variable-preserved, `R12` = IP, `R13` = SP, `R14` = LR, `R15` = PC.

### Layer 3 — The Assembler (`armasm`)
- Translates ARM assembly (`.s` files), directives (`AREA`, `ENTRY`, `EXPORT`, `IMPORT`, `DCD`, `EQU`).
- Outputs **ELF-format object files (.o)** consumable by the linker.

### Layer 4 — The Linker / Locator (`armlink`)
- Combines `.o` object files and libraries (`libc.lib`, `rt_sys.lib`).
- Resolves **external symbol references** (e.g., `main` calling `printf`).
- Generates the **scatter-loaded image** according to the Scatter-Loading Description File (`.sct`).

### Layer 5 — The Debugger + Probe (ULINK2/ULINKpro / J-Link)
- Communicates with the **CoreSight Debug Access Port (DAP)** on Cortex-M or the **EmbeddedICE** logic on ARM7TDMI.
- Provides **run/pause/step breakpoints**, **watchpoints**, **memory windows**, and **peripheral viewers**.

### Layer 6 — The Instruction Set Simulator (ISS)
- Pure-software execution of ARM instructions *without* real hardware — used for early validation and unit testing.

## 2.2 The Build Pipeline (Six Logical Stages)

1. **Preprocessing** — Macro expansion, header inclusion.
2. **Compilation** — `.c → .s` (ARM/Thumb assembly text).
3. **Assembly** — `.s → .o` (relocatable ELF object).
4. **Linking** — `.o + libraries → .axf` (ELF executable image).
5. **Locating / Scatter-loading** — Assigns absolute addresses to sections per the scatter file.
6. **Object conversion** — `fromelf` produces `.hex` (Intel HEX) and `.bin` (raw binary) for Flash programming.

## 2.3 Debug Architectures — JTAG vs SWD

| Parameter | JTAG (IEEE 1149.1) | SWD (ARM Specific) |
|---|---|---|
| Pin count | 4–5 | 2 |
| Speed | Up to **50 MHz TCK** | Up to **50 MHz SWCLK** |
| Topology | Daisy-chain (boundary scan) | Single-target point-to-point |
| Overhead | TAP controller state machine | ARM DAP register-based |
| Used in | ARM7, ARM9, ARM11, Cortex-A/R | Cortex-M (M0/M0+/M3/M4/M7) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact**: The LPC2148 (ARM7TDMI-S) uses the classic **5-pin JTAG** interface. The STM32 (Cortex-M3) uses the **2-pin SWD** interface. Examiners frequently test this mapping.

## 2.4 CMSIS — The Cortex Microcontroller Software Interface Standard

CMSIS is a vendor-independent **hardware abstraction layer** that provides:
- **CMSIS-Core**: Standardized access to NVIC, SysTick, SCB, and core registers.
- **CMSIS-Driver**: Unified peripheral driver APIs (UART, SPI, I2C).
- **CMSIS-DSP**: Optimized DSP library (FFT, FIR, matrix ops).
- **CMSIS-RTOS2**: Real-time operating system abstraction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility**: In industrial control (automotive ECUs, PLCs) and IoT edge devices, CMSIS allows firmware migration across vendors (NXP, ST, Microchip) with minimal code rewrite — slashing development time by **30–50%** in product cycles.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

$$
T_{\text{build}} = T_{\text{compile}} + T_{\text{assemble}} + T_{\text{link}} + T_{\text{locate}}
$$

$$
\text{Memory Model (Cortex-M3)}: \quad \text{Flash} \in [0x00000000,\, 0x1FFFFFFF] \;\;\cup\;\; \text{RAM} \in [0x20000000,\, 0x3FFFFFFF]
$$

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{initial}} = \text{Top of Stack} = \text{__initial\_sp} \quad \text{(from startup\_*.s vector table)}
$$

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{reset}} = \text{value at } 0x00000000 \quad ; \quad \text{LR}_{\text{on entry}} = 0xFFFFFFFF
$$

| Quantity | Symbol / Address | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reset Vector | $0x00000000$ | hex word | Initial PC on nRESET |
| Initial SP | $\text{\_\_initial\_sp}$ | byte | Loaded from vector table [0] |
| Vector Table Offset Reg | SCB $\rightarrow$ VTOR @ 0xE000ED08 | hex | Cortex-M3 only |
| AAPCS Scratch Regs | R0–R3, R12 | — | Caller-saved |
| AAPCS Preserved Regs | R4–R11 | — | Callee-saved |
| Link Register | R14 / LR | — | Holds return address |
| Stack Pointer | R13 / SP | — | Full-descending by default |
| APSR Flags | N, Z, C, V, Q | bit | In CPSR for ARM, xPSR for Cortex-M |

> **Engineering Utility**: Engineers use these formulas to compute **linker script region sizes**, **stack-depth requirements** (from call-graph analysis), and **interrupt latency bounds** in safety-critical ARM designs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Scatter-Loading Memory Map (LPC2148 Example)

We derive the linker allocation that Keil produces for an LPC2148 with **32 KB RAM** and **512 KB Flash**.

### Given
- Flash base = $0x00000000$, size = $512 \times 1024 = 524288$ bytes.
- RAM base = $0x40000000$, size = $32 \times 1024 = 32768$ bytes.
- Code section `.text` is read-only, placed in Flash.
- Initialized data `.data` is placed in RAM but its *load image* lives in Flash.
- Zero-initialized data `.bss` lives in RAM only.

### Step 1 — Define the load regions

$$
\text{Load Region LR\_ROM} : \text{Origin} = 0x00000000,\; \text{Length} = 0x00080000
$$

This is the **load region** — where the binary is initially programmed (Flash).

### Step 2 — Define the first execution region (read-only)

$$
\text{Exec Region ER\_ROM} : \text{Origin} = 0x00000000,\; \text{Length} = 0x00080000
$$

This contains `.text` (code) and `.rodata` (string literals, const arrays).

### Step 3 — Define the second execution region (read-write)

$$
\text{Exec Region RW\_RAM} : \text{Origin} = 0x40000000,\; \text{Length} = 0x00008000
$$

This contains `.data` (initialized variables) and `.bss` (zero-initialized variables).

### Step 4 — Show the scatter file textually

```
LR_ROM 0x00000000 0x00080000  {        ; load region, 512 KB
    ER_ROM 0x00000000 0x00080000  {     ; exec region for code
        *.o (RESET, +First)
        * (+RO)
    }
    RW_RAM 0x40000000 0x00008000  {     ; exec region for data
        * (+RW, +ZI)
    }
}
```

### Step 5 — Compute the address of `main`

The vector table (placed `+First`) consumes the first 32 bytes (8 vectors × 4 bytes) for ARM7. Therefore:

$$
\text{Addr}(main) = 0x00000000 + 32 + \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \text{sizeof}(F_i)
$$

where $F_i$ are the intermediate functions invoked between reset and `main` (typically the `__main` C runtime stub).

### Step 6 — Stack pointer initialization at reset

At reset, ARM7TDMI loads `SP` from vector table word 0 and `PC` from vector table word 1:

$$
SP \leftarrow M[0x00000000]
$$

$$
PC \leftarrow M[0x00000004] = \text{Reset Handler address}
$$

This is why every embedded C project begins with a **startup file** (`Startup.s` or `startup_LPC2148.s`) that defines the vector table at the very first address.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Build Time Budget

The total build time for an ARM project with $n$ source files is:

$$
T_{\text{build}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{\text{comp},i} + T_{\text{link}} + T_{\text{loc}}
$$

For incremental builds (where only $k$ files changed):

$$
T_{\text{build}}^{\text{inc}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_{\text{comp},i} + T_{\text{relink}} + T_{\text{loc}}
$$

**Real-world use**: Embedded CI/CD pipelines (e.g., GitHub Actions running ARM CI) use this relation to estimate compile-farm sizing for nightly builds across multiple product variants.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Debug vs Simulation Trade-off

| Criterion | Simulation (Keil ISS) | Emulation (ULINK + Target) | Production Probe (SWD/JTAG) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed | 10–100 MIPS | Real-time (target clock) | Real-time |
| Cost | Free (bundled) | Probe ~₹3,000–10,000 | Included in eval board |
| Peripheral accuracy | Behavioral only | Full hardware | Full hardware |
| Power analysis | Not possible | Possible with ULINKpro | Requires external tools |
| Use phase | Unit test, code bring-up | Lab validation | Field debug, OTA bootstrap |

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example** (KTU-style): An engineer is developing a UART driver on LPC2148. The first UART-baud-rate register (DLL/DLM) value is computed for **9600 baud** at a **PCLK = 15 MHz** (default after Keil startup). Compute the divisor.
>
> **Solution**:
> $$
> \text{Divisor} = \frac{P_CLK}{16 \times \text{Baud}} = \frac{15 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 9600} = 97.65625
> $$
> Rounding to nearest integer: $\text{DLL} = 98$, with fractional error:
> $$
> \epsilon = \frac{\vert 98 - 97.65625 \vert}{97.65625} \times 100\% = 0.3516\%
> $$
> This is well within the UART tolerance of $\pm 2\%$, hence **DLL = 98** is acceptable.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Reset Sequence for ARM7TDMI

When `nRESET` is de-asserted, the following micro-architectural sequence occurs:

1. The processor samples bank 0 (or bank 1 if remapped) at address `0x00000000`.
2. It loads the **top-of-stack** into `R13 (SP)` from `[0x00000000]`.
3. It loads the **reset handler address** into `R15 (PC)` from `[0x00000004]`.
4. It transitions to **Supervisor mode** with **IRQ/FIQ disabled**.
5. The reset handler typically:
   - Disables watchdog (`WDMOD = 0`).
   - Configures PLL (`PLLCON`, `PLLFEED`).
   - Sets the **VPB divider** for peripheral clock.
   - Branches to `__main` which copies `.data` from Flash to RAM, zeroes `.bss`, and calls `main()`.

$$
f_{\text{CCLK}} = M \times f_{\text{XTAL}} \quad ; \quad f_{\text{PCLK}} = \frac{f_{\text{CCLK}}}{\text{VPB\_DIV}}
$$

where for LPC2148, $M \in \{1, 2, 3, ..., 32\}$ and $\text{VPB\_DIV} \in \{1, 2, 4\}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARM Development Toolchain — End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Files .c .h] --> B[armcc Cross Compiler]
    A2[Assembly .s] --> C[armasm Assembler]
    B --> D[Object Files .o]
    C --> D
    D --> E[armlink Linker]
    F[Libraries libc.lib rt_sys.lib] --> E
    G[Scatter File .sct] --> E
    E --> H[ELF Image .axf]
    H --> I[fromelf Converter]
    I --> J[Intel HEX .hex]
    I --> K[Raw Binary .bin]
    H --> L[Keil Debugger]
    M[ULINK JTAG SWD Probe] --> L
    L --> N[Target ARM MCU]
    J --> O[Flash Programmer]
    K --> O
    O --> N
```

## 4.2 Cross-Development Architecture (Host vs Target)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOST[Host PC Windows Linux]
        direction TB
        IDE[Keil uVision IDE]
        CC[armcc Compiler]
        AS[armasm Assembler]
        LK[armlink Locator]
        DBG[Debugger Engine]
    end
    subgraph PROBE[Debug Probe ULINK or J-Link]
        PR1[JTAG TAP Controller]
        PR2[SWD Access Port]
    end
    subgraph TARGET[Target Board LPC2148 or STM32]
        MC[ARM7TDMI or Cortex-M Core]
        MEM[Flash and RAM]
        PRM[Peripherals UART GPIO Timer]
    end
    HOST --> PROBE
    PROBE --> TARGET
    TARGET --> PROBE
    PROBE --> HOST
    MC --> MEM
    MC --> PRM
```

## 4.3 Build Process — Detailed Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Edit source .c .h .s] --> S2[Project Manager groups files]
    S2 --> S3{Preprocessor runs}
    S3 --> S4[armcc compiles to .s]
    S4 --> S5[armasm assembles to .o]
    S5 --> S6[armlink combines objects and libraries]
    S6 --> S7[Scatter loader assigns addresses]
    S7 --> S8[fromelf generates .hex and .bin]
    S8 --> S9[Flash programmer writes target]
    S9 --> S10[Target resets and executes]
    S10 --> S11{Debug or release}
    S11 -->|Debug| S12[Attach ULINK step trace]
    S11 -->|Release| S13[Ship firmware]
    S12 --> S1
```

## 4.4 JTAG vs SWD TAP State Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph JTAG_DIAG[Classic JTAG 5 Pin]
        J1[TDI Test Data In]
        J2[TDO Test Data Out]
        J3[TMS Test Mode Select]
        J4[TCK Test Clock]
        J5[nTRST Test Reset]
    end
    subgraph SWD_DIAG[SWD 2 Pin Cortex-M]
        K1[SWDIO Bidirectional Data]
        K2[SWCLK Serial Wire Clock]
    end
    J1 --> CORE[ARM Core Debug Logic]
    J2 --> CORE
    J3 --> CORE
    J4 --> CORE
    J5 --> CORE
    K1 --> CORE
    K2 --> CORE
```

## 4.5 Keil µVision IDE Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UI[User Interface Layer]
        E1[Source Editor]
        E2[Project Workspace]
        E3[Build Output Window]
        E4[Memory Watch Window]
        E5[Peripheral Viewer]
    end
    subgraph ENGINE[Toolchain Engine Layer]
        EN1[armcc Cross Compiler]
        EN2[armasm Assembler]
        EN3[armlink Linker Locator]
        EN4[fromelf Image Converter]
        EN5[Debugger Core DLL]
    end
    subgraph HW[Hardware Interface Layer]
        H1[ULINK Driver]
        H2[JTAG Protocol]
        H3[SWD Protocol]
        H4[Simulator Engine]
    end
    UI --> ENGINE
    ENGINE --> HW
    H1 --> H2
    H1 --> H3
    H1 --> H4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Define a cross-compiler. Why is `armcc` classified as a cross-compiler in an LPC2148 development environment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **cross-compiler** is a compiler that runs on a host machine with one Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) but produces executable code for a target machine with a *different* ISA. **(1 mark)**
- `armcc` runs on an x86 host (Windows/Linux PC) but generates ARM/Thumb machine code for the LPC2148's **ARM7TDMI-S** core. **(1 mark)**
- This is necessitated because the target is resource-constrained and cannot host its own compiler toolchain — the firmware must be prepared on a powerful host and downloaded via JTAG/SWD or a Flash programmer. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
**List any three components of the ARM development toolchain and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **`armcc` (ARM Cross-Compiler)** — Translates C/C++ source into ARM/Thumb assembly. **(1 mark)**
2. **`armasm` (ARM Assembler)** — Assembles handwritten assembly `.s` files into relocatable `.o` object files. **(1 mark)**
3. **`armlink` (Linker & Locator)** — Resolves symbols, combines objects, and assigns absolute addresses per the scatter file to produce the final `.axf` image. **(1 mark)**

*(Alternative accepted items: `fromelf`, `µVision Debugger`, `ULINK Probe`, `Scatter File`, `CMSIS`.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — With Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Chosen Stream: Toolchain Pipeline*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the various stages of the ARM cross-development toolchain from source editing to target execution. List the file extensions produced at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of the **scatter-loading description file** in ARM linking. Construct a scatter file for an LPC2148 system with 512 KB Flash at `0x00000000` and 32 KB RAM at `0x40000000`, ensuring the vector table is placed first. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Toolchain Pipeline (7 marks)

| Stage | Tool | Input | Output | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Editing | µVision Editor | — | `.c`, `.h`, `.s` | Source code authoring |
| 2. Preprocessing | `armcc -E` | `.c` | `.i` | Macro expansion, header inclusion |
| 3. Compilation | `armcc -c` | `.c` | `.s` | C → ARM/Thumb assembly text |
| 4. Assembly | `armasm` | `.s` | `.o` | Assembly → relocatable ELF object |
| 5. Linking | `armlink` | `.o`, `.lib`, `.sct` | `.axf` | Symbol resolution + address allocation |
| 6. Conversion | `fromelf` | `.axf` | `.hex`, `.bin` | Image format conversion for Flash |
| 7. Programming | Flash Loader | `.hex` | Internal Flash | Permanent storage in target |
| 8. Execution | Target CPU | — | Run-time | Program executes on ARM core |

**Block Diagram** (text-rendered since the exam is on paper):

```
.c / .h / .s
   |
   v
[armcc Cross-Compiler] ---> .o (object)
   |
   v
[armasm Assembler]    ---> .o (object)
   |
   v
[armlink + Scatter]   ---> .axf (ELF image)
   |
   v
[fromelf]             ---> .hex  + .bin
   |
   v
[Flash Programmer]    ---> Target Flash
   |
   v
[Target Execution on ARM]
```

#### Model Solution (b) — Scatter File (7 marks)

**Role of scatter file** **(3 marks):**
- It instructs the linker **where** to place each section (`.text`, `.data`, `.bss`, `.rodata`) in the memory map.
- It allows **non-contiguous memory regions** (e.g., Flash at low address, RAM at high address).
- It enables the vector table to be placed at the **absolute address 0x00000000** for ARM7 reset semantics.

**Constructed Scatter File for LPC2148** **(4 marks):**

```
; Scatter-Loading Description for LPC2148
; 512 KB Flash @ 0x00000000, 32 KB RAM @ 0x40000000

LR_ROM 0x00000000 0x00080000   ; load region = Flash (512 KB)
{
    ER_ROM 0x00000000 0x00080000
    {
        startup_LPC2148.o (RESET, +First)   ; vector table at 0x00000000
        * (+RO)                              ; all read-only code & data
    }

    RW_RAM 0x40000000 0x00008000            ; RAM region (32 KB)
    {
        * (+RW, +ZI)                        ; initialized + zero-init data
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B - 7 marker)**:
> - Students often forget the **`+First`** attribute on the vector table object — losing **1 mark**.
> - Common error: placing the load region base at `0x40000000` (RAM) instead of `0x00000000` (Flash) — results in a non-bootable image, full **0 marks** for the scatter file.
> - Students frequently omit the `* (+RW, +ZI)` wildcard, leaving `.bss` uninitialized — **deduct 1 mark**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Stream: Debug Architectures*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **JTAG** and **SWD** debug interfaces. State which interface is used by the LPC2148 (ARM7TDMI) and the STM32 (Cortex-M3). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the function of the **µVision Debugger** with the **ULINK probe**. With a diagram, describe how breakpoints, watchpoints, and memory windows aid real-time firmware debugging. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — JTAG vs SWD (7 marks)

| Parameter | JTAG (IEEE 1149.1) | SWD (ARM) |
|---|---|---|
| Pin count | 4–5 (TDI, TDO, TMS, TCK, nTRST) | 2 (SWDIO, SWCLK) |
| Standard | Industry-wide boundary scan | ARM proprietary |
| Topology | Daisy-chainable | Point-to-point |
| Cortex-M support | Yes (backward compatible) | Native, preferred |
| Data rate | Up to **50 MHz TCK** | Up to **50 MHz SWCLK** |
| Overhead | TAP state machine (16 states) | DAP register-based |
| Power consumption | Higher (5 pins toggling) | Lower (2 pins) |
| Multi-drop debug | Supported via daisy chain | Not natively supported |

**Target Mapping** **(2 marks):**
- **LPC2148 (ARM7TDMI-S)** uses the classic **5-pin JTAG** interface via its EmbeddedICE macrocell. **(1 mark)**
- **STM32 (Cortex-M3)** uses the **2-pin SWD** interface via its CoreSight DAP; JTAG is also available as a fallback. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution (b) — µVision Debugger + ULINK (7 marks)

**Function of µVision Debugger (3 marks):**
- Provides a **graphical, source-level** view of program execution on the target.
- Communicates with the target via the **ULINK2/ULINKpro** probe, which translates USB packets into JTAG/SWD signals.
- Supports **run**, **halt**, **single-step**, **reset**, and **run-to-cursor** operations.

**Debug Features (4 marks):**

1. **Breakpoints** — When the PC reaches a specific address, the probe asserts DBGRQ, the core halts, and the debugger displays registers/source. Implementation: a **BP comparator** in the EmbeddedICE compares the address bus to a programmed value. **(1 mark)**
2. **Watchpoints** — Similar to breakpoints but trigger on **data accesses** (read/write to a memory address). Useful for catching illegal pointer dereferences. **(1 mark)**
3. **Memory Window** — Live read/write of arbitrary memory addresses; used to inspect peripheral registers (e.g., `U0RBR`, `T0TC`). **(1 mark)**
4. **Peripheral Viewer** — Decoded view of on-chip peripherals with bit-field descriptions; user-modifiable in real time. **(1 mark)**

**Diagram (text-rendered):**

```
[Keil µVision PC]
       |   USB
       v
[ULINK2 / J-Link Probe]
       |   JTAG or SWD
       v
[Target ARM Core + EmbeddedICE / DAP]
       |
       v
[Memory Mapped Peripherals and RAM]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B - 7 marker)**:
> - Students commonly confuse **breakpoint** (code) with **watchpoint** (data) — losing **1–2 marks**.
> - Do **not** claim that µVision can debug without a probe; pure simulation uses the **Instruction Set Simulator (ISS)** only.
> - Avoid mixing up **JTAG pin names** (TDI/TDO) with **SWD pin names** (SWDIO/SWCLK) — examiners will deduct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **ARM Development Tools** = a *cross-development* stack (host-resident) used to build target-resident firmware.
- [x] Canonical toolchain: **Keil µVision IDE** + **armcc** + **armasm** + **armlink** + **fromelf** + **ULINK/µVision Debugger**.
- [x] Build pipeline: **`.c → .o → .axf → .hex/.bin → Flash → Execution`**.
- [x] The **scatter file (.sct)** is mandatory for placing sections in non-contiguous memory maps (Flash at `0x00000000`, RAM at `0x40000000` on LPC2148).
- [x] The **vector table** MUST be placed `+First` at address `0x00000000` for ARM7 reset; SP loads from `[0x00]`, PC loads from `[0x04]`.
- [x] **JTAG** = 5-pin, used by **LPC2148 (ARM7TDMI-S)**. **SWD** = 2-pin, used by **Cortex-M3 (STM32)**.
- [x] AAPCS register convention: **R0–R3, R12** = scratch (caller-saved); **R4–R11** = preserved (callee-saved); **R13 = SP, R14 = LR, R15 = PC**.
- [x] **CMSIS** provides a vendor-independent HAL — critical for portable Cortex-M firmware.
- [x] Debugger features: **breakpoints** (instruction), **watchpoints** (data), **memory windows**, **peripheral viewers**.
- [x] **Cross-compiler** = compiles on host ISA (x86) for a different target ISA (ARM).
- [x] `fromelf` is the image-format converter (`.axf → .hex / .bin`).
- [x] `armlink` is the **linker + locator** — resolves symbols *and* assigns absolute addresses.
- [x] Reset sequence: load SP, load PC, transition to Supervisor mode, branch to `__main` (which copies `.data`, clears `.bss`, calls `main()`).
- [x] Baud-rate formula: $\text{Divisor} = \dfrac{f_{PCLK}}{16 \times \text{BaudRate}}$.
- [x] PLL relation: $f_{CCLK} = M \times f_{XTAL}$ for LPC2148, where $M \in [1, 32]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Assembly Language Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ARM Assembly Language Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARM Assembly Language** is a low-level symbolic representation of machine instructions for the **Advanced RISC Machine (ARM)** processor family, employing a **Load/Store RISC architecture** where arithmetic and logical operations are executed exclusively on operands held in registers, while memory is accessed solely through dedicated load (`LDR`) and store (`STR`) instructions.

In the context of KTU's PBEVT504 syllabus (Module 3), the topic encompasses:
- **ARM core register organization** (37 registers in physical banked architecture)
- **Instruction set formats** (Data-processing, Multiply, Branch, Load/Store, etc.)
- **Conditional execution** via the **Condition Field (`cond`)** in every instruction
- **Assembler directives** used to structure the source code (`.text`, `.data`, `.global`, `.end`)
- **Addressing modes** for efficient data manipulation

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Assistant"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition**: Imagine a librarian (the ARM CPU) who works at a fixed desk with exactly 16 numbered slots on the desk (the 16 registers: **R0–R15**). The librarian will *never* leave the desk to do math — all arithmetic is done using items already on the desk. To fetch a book from the library shelves (memory), the librarian must use a special "fetch" slip (the **`LDR`** instruction). To put a book back, the librarian uses a "return" slip (the **`STR`** instruction). This "desk-only math, fetch/return for memory" rule is the heart of the **RISC Load/Store philosophy**.

## 1.3 Key Architectural Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standard ARM7TDMI metrics must be memorized for KTU examinations:

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| **Programmable Registers** | **16 (R0–R15)** visible per mode |
| **Total Physical Registers** | **37** (banked across modes) |
| **Instruction Width** | **32-bit** (fixed for ARM state) |
| **Condition Field Width** | **4 bits** (top 28–31) |
| **Pipeline Stages (ARM7)** | **3 stages** (Fetch, Decode, Execute) |
| **CPSR Register Width** | **32 bits** (status flags in bits 28–31) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Registers in ARM (per KTU syllabus focus)**:
> - `R0–R12` : General purpose scratch registers
> - `R13` : **Stack Pointer (SP)** — physically distinct per mode
> - `R14` : **Link Register (LR)** — holds return addresses from subroutine calls
> - `R15` : **Program Counter (PC)** — current instruction address + **8** (in ARM state)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ARM Instruction Format — Structural Breakdown

Every ARM instruction (in **ARM state**, 32-bit) follows one of the formats below. The decoder uses bits **31–28** for the **condition field (`cond`)** — this is what makes conditional execution on *every* instruction possible.

**Data-Processing Instruction Format (Type 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bits: } & \underbrace{[31:28]}_{\text{cond}} \;
\underbrace{[27:25]}_{00\,1} \;
\underbrace{[24:21]}_{\text{opcode}} \;
\underbrace{[20]}_{\text{S}} \;
\underbrace{[19:16]}_{\text{Rn}} \;
\underbrace{[15:12]}_{\text{Rd}} \;
\underbrace{[11:0]}_{\text{Operand2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- **`cond`** : 4-bit condition code (EQ, NE, GT, LT, AL, NV …) — see cheat sheet
- **`opcode`** : Operation code (ADD, SUB, AND, ORR, EOR, MOV, CMP, TST …)
- **`S`** : Set condition codes if `1`
- **`Rn`** : First source register (Base register)
- **`Rd`** : Destination register
- **`Operand2`** : Second operand (immediate or register with optional shift)

## 2.2 Logical Flow — Why ARM is "Conditional Everywhere"

- **Step 1**: CPU fetches the 32-bit instruction from the address in `PC`.
- **Step 2**: The top 4 bits `[31:28]` are tested *before* execution.
- **Step 3**: If the `cond` field evaluates to **FALSE**, the instruction is **converted into a NOP** (single clock cycle) and never executes.
- **Step 4**: If **TRUE**, the instruction executes normally in the same pipeline stage.
- **Why it matters**: This eliminates branch penalties in small `if-else` blocks — a major KTU favorite question.

> [!NOTE]
> The default condition field is **`AL`** (Always, `1110`). If a programmer writes `ADD` with no suffix, the assembler implicitly appends `AL`.

## 2.3 Addressing Modes — The Second Operand (Operand2)

The `Operand2` field is one of the most heavily tested concepts. It can be:

1. **Immediate value** — an 8-bit value rotated right by an even number.
2. **Register** — `Rm` used directly.
3. **Register with logical shift** — `Rm, LSL #k`, `Rm, LSR #k`, `Rm, ASR #k`, `Rm, ROR #k`.
4. **Register with arithmetic shift** — `Rm, ASR #k` only.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU pitfall**: An immediate in ARM is **not** just an 8-bit number. It is generated as a 32-bit constant by rotating an 8-bit value right by `2 × rotate_imm`. So `0x3F0` is valid (rotate 0xFF by 28), but `0x101` is **not directly encodable** as an immediate.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Condition Codes

| Code (Suffix) | Condition | Tested Flag | Hex Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| `EQ` | Equal | **Z = 1** | `0x0` |
| `NE` | Not Equal | **Z = 0** | `0x1` |
| `GT` | Signed Greater Than | **Z = 0 AND N = V** | `0xC` |
| `LT` | Signed Less Than | **N ≠ V** | `0xB` |
| `GE` | Signed ≥ | **N = V** | `0xA` |
| `LE` | Signed ≤ | **Z = 1 OR N ≠ V** | `0xD` |
| `MI` | Minus (negative) | **N = 1** | `0x4` |
| `PL` | Plus/positive | **N = 0** | `0x5` |
| `CS/HS` | Carry Set / Unsigned ≥ | **C = 1** | `0x2` |
| `CC/LO` | Carry Clear / Unsigned < | **C = 0** | `0x3` |
| `AL` | Always (default) | — | `0xE` |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bare-metal firmware** for automotive ECUs, drones, and medical implants — every microsecond counts.
- **Bootloaders** (e.g., U-Boot) where the first 4 KB of code is hand-written ARM assembly.
- **Device drivers** requiring atomic register bit manipulation not expressible in C.
- **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs)** where saving CPSR into SPSR is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Decoding an ARM Data-Processing Instruction

**Problem**: Decode the 32-bit instruction `0xE3A01005` into its symbolic form.

**Step-by-step solution**:

**Step 1 — Convert hex to binary (group of 4 bits)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0xE3A01005 & = 1110 \; 0011 \; 1010 \; 0000 \; 0001 \; 0000 \; 0000 \; 0101
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Extract the field boundaries** (`cond | 00 1 | opcode | S | Rn | Rd | Operand2`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cond}     & = 1110 \;\; (\text{AL — Always}) \\
001             & = \text{Data-processing I-type} \\
\text{opcode}   & = 1101 \;\; (\text{MOV}) \\
S               & = 0 \;\; (\text{Do NOT set flags}) \\
\text{Rn}       & = 0000 \;\; (\text{R0 — unused for MOV}) \\
\text{Rd}       & = 0001 \;\; (\text{R1}) \\
\text{Operand2} & = 0000\,0000\,0000\,0101
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret Operand2**:
- `rotate_imm` = `0000` → rotate = `0` (no rotation)
- `imm8` = `00000101` = `5` decimal

**Step 4 — Final symbolic instruction**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\texttt{MOV R1, \#5}\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PC reads as `current + 8` in ARM state?** The ARM7 pipeline has 3 stages; when an instruction is being executed, two later instructions have already been fetched. Thus, `R15` (PC) appears 2 instructions (8 bytes) ahead of the address of the executing instruction.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Generating a 32-bit Immediate

**Problem**: Verify whether the constant `0xF0000003` can be encoded as an ARM immediate.

**Step-by-step solution**:

**Step 1 — Recognize the pattern**: The 8-bit value `0x03` rotated right by `2 × r` must yield `0xF0000003`.

**Step 2 — Compute bit-length of pattern**: `0xF0000003` in binary is `1111\,0000\,...\,0011`. The repeating pattern is `1111\,0011` — this is `0xF3`, an 8-bit value, but `0xF3 = 0b11110011` is **not 8-bit encodable** as a single byte because the trailing `0011` requires the value to be `0xF3` rotated by some amount.

**Step 3 — Test rotation**: The constant equals `0x03` rotated right by `0` for lower 8 bits; but upper 24 bits are all `1`s — this would require `0xFF` or similar. Because no single 8-bit value rotated by an even number can produce `0xF0000003`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\text{Result: 0xF0000003 is NOT encodable as a single ARM immediate.}\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU pitfall**: Students often write `MOV R0, #0xF0000003` and assume it works. It must be built using `MOV` + `ORR` (load literal from LDR pseudo-instruction is preferred).

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Trace of a Subroutine Call

Given the following assembly snippet:

```asm
BL   SUB1        ; LR = PC + 4, jump to SUB1
ADD  R0, R1, R2  ; resumes here after return
...
SUB1:
    MOV R3, #10
    SUB LR, LR, #4   ; adjust LR (Thumb vs ARM) — not needed in pure ARM
    MOV PC, LR       ; function return
```

**Step 4 — Register trace**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before BL:} \;\; & R15 = 0x00008000, \; R14 = \text{don't care} \\
\text{After BL:} \;\; & R15 = 0x00008004 \; (\text{SUB1}), \; R14 = 0x00008004 \; (\text{return addr}) \\
\text{After SUB1:} \;\; & R15 = 0x00008004, \; R14 \text{ unchanged}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Instruction Categories

| Category | Mnemonic Examples | Operand Restriction | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Processing** | `ADD, SUB, AND, ORR, EOR, MOV, MVN, CMP, TST, TEQ` | 3-operand, Rd = Rn OP Operand2 | **High (30%)** |
| **Multiply** | `MUL, MLA, MULL, UMULL, UMLAL, SMULL, SMLAL` | 64-bit result needs `MULL` variants | **Medium (15%)** |
| **Branch** | `B, BL, BX, BLX` | Offset is signed 24-bit | **High (20%)** |
| **Load/Store** | `LDR, STR, LDRB, STRB, LDM, STM` | Rn must point to memory | **High (25%)** |
| **Swap & SWP** | `SWP, SWPB` | Atomic read-modify-write | **Low (5%)** |
| **Software Interrupt** | `SWI` / `SVC` | Triggers exception mode | **Low (5%)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARM7TDMI Core Register Architecture (Banked View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph USER_MODE
        R0USR[Reg R0]
        R1USR[Reg R1]
        R2USR[Reg R2]
        R3USR[Reg R3]
        R4USR[Reg R4]
        R5USR[Reg R5]
        R6USR[Reg R6]
        R7USR[Reg R7]
        R8USR[Reg R8]
        R9USR[Reg R9]
        R10USR[Reg R10]
        R11USR[Reg R11]
        R12USR[Reg R12]
        R13USR[Reg R13 SP]
        R14USR[Reg R14 LR]
        R15USR[Reg R15 PC]
        CPSRUSR[Reg CPSR]
    end

    subgraph FIQ_MODE
        R8FIQ[Reg R8 FIQ]
        R9FIQ[Reg R9 FIQ]
        R10FIQ[Reg R10 FIQ]
        R11FIQ[Reg R11 FIQ]
        R12FIQ[Reg R12 FIQ]
        R13FIQ[Reg R13 SP FIQ]
        R14FIQ[Reg R14 LR FIQ]
        SPSRFIQ[Reg SPSR FIQ]
    end

    subgraph SUPERVISOR_MODE
        R13SVC[Reg R13 SP SVC]
        R14SVC[Reg R14 LR SVC]
        SPSRSVC[Reg SPSR SVC]
    end

    note1[Banked registers: R8-R14 and SPSR are physically separate per privileged mode]
    note1 ~~~ FIQ_MODE
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `note1` node and `~~~` operator create an annotation disconnected from the dataflow, satisfying the Mermaid Safeguards for reserved keywords.

## 4.2 Instruction Execution Flow — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Instruction Fetch from Memory at PC] --> B[Decode Top 4 Bits as cond Field]
    B --> C{Condition TRUE?}
    C -- No --> D[Instruction becomes NOP Pipeline Bubble]
    D --> E[Increment PC by 4]
    C -- Yes --> F[Decode opcode and Operand2]
    F --> G{Instruction Class}
    G -- Data Processing --> H[ALU executes in Execute Stage]
    G -- Load/Store --> I[Address Generator computes Effective Address]
    G -- Branch --> J[Update PC with target offset]
    H --> K[Writeback to Rd in Register Bank]
    I --> K
    J --> E
    K --> E
    E --> A
```

## 4.3 Load/Store Multiple — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MEMORY
        M0[Address Base+0]
        M1[Address Base+4]
        M2[Address Base+8]
        M3[Address Base+12]
    end
    subgraph REG_BANK
        REG0[Reg R0]
        REG1[Reg R1]
        REG2[Reg R2]
        REG3[Reg R3]
    end
    LDMIA[LDMIA Rb, {R0-R3}] -. Reads .-> M0
    LDMIA -. Reads .-> M1
    LDMIA -. Reads .-> M2
    LDMIA -. Reads .-> M3
    M0 --> REG0
    M1 --> REG1
    M2 --> REG2
    M3 --> REG3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **LDMIA = Load Multiple Increment After**. Other modes: `LDMIB` (Increment Before), `LDMDA` (Decrement After), `LDMDB` (Decrement Before). KTU frequently tests the **stack mode mapping**: `LDMFA/STMFA ↔ POP/PUSH`.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List any six general-purpose registers in ARM7 and write their specific names in ARM terminology.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
ARM7 has **16 general-purpose registers** named `R0` through `R15`. Of these:
- `R0`–`R12` : General-purpose scratch registers used for data processing and address storage.
- `R13` : **Stack Pointer (SP)** — points to the top of the current stack.
- `R14` : **Link Register (LR)** — stores the return address of a subroutine call.
- `R15` : **Program Counter (PC)** — holds the address of the current instruction + 8 in ARM state.

*(Award ½ mark per register correctly named and identified — 6 × ½ = 3 marks)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the `S` bit in an ARM data-processing instruction with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `S` bit (bit 20) determines whether the instruction **updates the condition flags (N, Z, C, V) in CPSR**.
- When `S = 0` : Flags are **not** updated.
- When `S = 1` : Flags **are** updated according to the ALU result.

**Example**: `ADDS R0, R1, R2` sets flags, whereas `ADD R0, R1, R2` does not. The `CMP` instruction is actually `SUBS` with `Rd = R0` and `S = 1` (result discarded, but flags reflect the comparison).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

### **Question A — Internal Choice Option 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **ARM data-processing instruction format** in detail. Show the bit field layout, list the fields, and explain the role of the **condition field**. Mention the significance of the `S` bit.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The 32-bit data-processing format is structured as:

| Bits | Field | Width | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 31–28 | `cond` | 4 | Condition code for conditional execution |
| 27–26 | `00` | 2 | Identifies data-processing |
| 25 | `I` | 1 | 0 = register operand, 1 = immediate operand |
| 24–21 | `opcode` | 4 | Operation (ADD, SUB, MOV …) |
| 20 | `S` | 1 | Update flags in CPSR if 1 |
| 19–16 | `Rn` | 4 | First source register |
| 15–12 | `Rd` | 4 | Destination register |
| 11–0 | `Operand2` | 12 | Second source (imm or reg+shift) |

**Condition field role**: Bits 31–28 are tested **before** execution. If the corresponding CPSR flag state is FALSE, the instruction is skipped (becomes a NOP). This eliminates branch overhead in short conditionals.

**`S` bit role**: When set, N, Z, C, V flags are updated. Instructions like `CMP` use `S=1` to perform comparison without storing the result.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Decode the instruction `0xE2811003` and explain its operation step-by-step. Verify if the immediate is valid.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Hex to binary**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0xE2811003 & = 1110 \; 0010 \; 1000 \; 0001 \; 0001 \; 0000 \; 0000 \; 0011
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Field extraction**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cond}   & = 1110 \; (\text{AL}) \\
\text{00 1}   & = \text{Data-processing with immediate} \\
\text{opcode} & = 0100 \; (\text{ADD}) \\
S             & = 0 \\
Rn            & = 0001 \; (\text{R1}) \\
Rd            & = 0001 \; (\text{R1}) \\
\text{Oper2}  & = 0000\,0000\,0011 \; (\text{rotate}=0,\;\text{imm8}=3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Symbolic form**:

$$
\boxed{\;\texttt{ADD R1, R1, \#3}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Immediate validity**: 8-bit immediate `0x03` with `rotate_imm = 0` (rotate by 0) is a valid ARM immediate.

---

### **Question B — Internal Choice Option 2** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **five addressing modes** available for `Operand2` in ARM data-processing instructions. Give one example of an ARM instruction using each.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Immediate addressing** — `Operand2` is an 8-bit value rotated right by an even number.
   - Example: `MOV R0, #5`
2. **Register direct** — A register `Rm` is used directly.
   - Example: `ADD R0, R1, R2`
3. **Register with logical shift left (LSL)** — `Rm` is left-shifted by an immediate or register.
   - Example: `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #2`
4. **Register with logical shift right (LSR)** — `Rm` is right-shifted logically.
   - Example: `MOV R0, R1, LSR #3`
5. **Register with arithmetic shift right (ASR)** — Sign-extending right shift.
   - Example: `MOV R0, R1, ASR #1`

*(1 mark per mode + example = 5 × ~1.4 = 7 marks)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an ARM assembly program to compute the **sum of first N natural numbers** using a loop. Assume N is stored in memory location `N_VAL` and the result should be stored in `SUM`. Show the complete program with directives.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

```asm
        .text                  @ Code section
        .global _start
        .equ    N_VAL, 0x40000000   @ Memory-mapped input N
        .equ    SUM,   0x40000004   @ Memory-mapped output SUM

_start:
        LDR     R1, =N_VAL          @ R1 = address of N
        LDR     R2, [R1]            @ R2 = N (loop count)
        MOV     R0, #0              @ R0 = accumulator (sum) = 0
        MOV     R3, #1              @ R3 = current counter i = 1

LOOP:
        CMP     R3, R2              @ Compare i with N
        BGT     DONE                @ If i > N, exit loop
        ADD     R0, R0, R3          @ sum = sum + i
        ADD     R3, R3, #1          @ i = i + 1
        B       LOOP                @ Repeat

DONE:
        LDR     R4, =SUM
        STR     R0, [R4]            @ Store final sum
        SWI     0x123456            @ Terminate (Angel SWI)

        .end
```

**Explanation of execution**:
- For `N = 5`: iterations 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 add to R0 → final `R0 = 15`.
- `BGT DONE` uses the GT condition on signed comparison (N flag and V flag).
- The `SWI` instruction is used to signal end-of-program to the debugger.

*(Breakdown: directives 2 marks, load/store 2 marks, loop body 2 marks, termination 1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — ARM Assembly Programs**
> 1. **Forgetting the `=SUM` syntax in `LDR`**: `LDR R4, =SUM` is a **pseudo-instruction** (assembler converts to `LDR R4, [PC, #offset]`). Writing `LDR R4, SUM` is a **compile error** worth 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing `R15` increment**: In ARM state, `PC` advances by **8**, not 4. If a student writes `LDR R0, =label` and the next instruction is exactly 4 bytes away, the linker may produce a wrong address. Always treat `PC` as `current + 8`.
> 3. **Using `MOV` for >8-bit immediates**: `MOV R0, #0x1234` is illegal; use `MOV R0, #0x34` followed by `ORR R0, R0, #0x1200` or use `LDR R0, =0x1234`.
> 4. **Mixing post-indexed and pre-indexed addressing**: `LDR R0, [R1], #4` (post) and `LDR R0, [R1, #4]` (pre-offset) behave very differently. Marks are often lost in pointer-traversal programs.
> 5. **Forgetting the `.end` directive**: Without `.end`, the assembler may not detect the end of source and may produce spurious bytes in the object file.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ARM Assembly Language Programming**
> - **Architecture** is **Load/Store RISC** — no memory operands in arithmetic ops.
> - **16 visible registers**: `R0–R12` (general), `R13` = SP, `R14` = LR, `R15` = PC.
> - `PC` reads as `current + 8` in **ARM state** and `current + 4` in **Thumb state**.
> - **Condition field** is the top 4 bits of every instruction — supports 16 conditions.
> - **Default condition** is `AL` (Always) — suffix omitted = always executes.
> - **Data-processing format**: `cond | 001 | opcode | S | Rn | Rd | Operand2` (32 bits).
> - **Immediate** = 8-bit value rotated right by `2 × rotate_imm`.
> - **`S` bit = 1** updates N, Z, C, V flags in CPSR; `CMP`, `TST`, `TEQ` always have `S=1`.
> - **Branch instructions**: `B` (simple), `BL` (with link, sets LR), `BX`/`BLX` (to register).
> - **Load/Store addressing** modes: pre-indexed, post-indexed, pre-indexed with writeback.
> - **Block transfers**: `LDM/STM` with suffixes `IA, IB, DA, DB` and stack variants `FD, FA, ED, EA`.
> - **Assembler directives**: `.text`, `.data`, `.global`, `.equ`, `.end`, `.align`.
> - **Pseudo-instructions**: `LDR Rd, =label` and `ADR Rd, label` are translated by the assembler.
> - **CPSR vs SPSR**: SPSR exists **only** in privileged modes; saves CPSR on exception entry.
> - **Banked registers**: R8–R14 and SPSR are physically distinct in FIQ; R13–R14 in SVC/IRQ/ABT/UND.
> - **Pipeline (ARM7)**: 3-stage (Fetch → Decode → Execute) — affects `PC` and `LR` semantics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Processing Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Processing Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Data Processing Instructions* in the ARM Cortex-M (Thumb-2) architecture are a class of instructions that **operate exclusively on values held in the Core Registers (R0–R12)** rather than fetching operands from memory. They are responsible for **data movement, arithmetic computation, bitwise logical manipulation, shifting, comparison, multiplication, saturation, sign/zero extension, and bit-field extraction** within the processor's register file. They do **not** access the data memory bus (no LDR/STR), making them the fastest class of instructions in the instruction set.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the CPU is a **chef in a kitchen**:
- The **registers (R0–R12)** are the **ingredients already in the chef's hand or on the cutting board** (instantly accessible).
- **Memory (RAM)** is the **pantry in the next room** — accessible, but requires the chef to walk over and fetch/return items (slower, takes bus cycles).
- **Data Processing Instructions** are the **knife skills and mixing actions** the chef performs on ingredients already in hand: *chopping (shifts), adding spices (ADD), tasting (CMP), squeezing juice (extract/extend), etc.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** Every data processing instruction in Cortex-M updates the **Application Program Status Register (APSR)** flags (N, Z, C, V) **only if the `S` (set-flag) suffix is appended**, e.g., `ADDS`, `SUBS`, `ANDS`. Without the `S` suffix, the flags remain untouched. This is a guaranteed KTU short-answer question.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **General-Purpose Registers: 13 (R0–R12)**
- **Special Registers: R13 (SP — Stack Pointer), R14 (LR — Link Register), R15 (PC — Program Counter)**
- **APSR Flag Bits: 4 (N, Z, C, V)**
- **Instruction Widths: 16-bit (Thumb) and 32-bit (Thumb-2)** within the same unified instruction stream
- **Barrel Shifter: 32 parallel processing lanes** — shifts are free (0 extra cycles)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification of Data Processing Instructions

The ARM Cortex-M data processing instruction set is partitioned into **ten major functional families**. The operational logic for each family is broken down below.

#### Family 1: Data Movement (No Computation)
- **MOV Rd, Operand2** → $Rd \leftarrow Operand2$
- **MVN Rd, Operand2** → $Rd \leftarrow \overline{Operand2}$ (bitwise NOT)
- **Why it matters:** Loads an immediate constant or a shifted register value without polluting the flags (unless `S` suffix is added).

#### Family 2: Arithmetic Operations
- **ADD Rd, Rn, Operand2** → $Rd \leftarrow Rn + Operand2$
- **SUB Rd, Rn, Operand2** → $Rd \leftarrow Rn - Operand2$
- **ADC / SBC** → include the Carry flag: $Rd \leftarrow Rn + Operand2 + C$
- **RSB / RSC** → reverse subtraction: $Rd \leftarrow Operand2 - Rn$
- **How:** All support the *Operand2* flex field — a register optionally shifted by an immediate or another register.

#### Family 3: Bitwise Logical Operations
- **AND Rd, Rn, Operand2** → $Rd \leftarrow Rn \ \& \ Operand2$
- **ORR Rd, Rn, Operand2** → $Rd \leftarrow Rn \ \vert\ Operand2$
- **EOR Rd, Rn, Operand2** → $Rd \leftarrow Rn \oplus Operand2$
- **BIC Rd, Rn, Operand2** → $Rd \leftarrow Rn \ \& \ \overline{Operand2}$ (Bit Clear)
- **ORN Rd, Rn, Operand2** → $Rd \leftarrow Rn \ \vert\ \overline{Operand2}$
- **Why:** These are the bedrock of *bit manipulation* — setting/clearing/toggling individual bits in peripheral configuration registers.

#### Family 4: Shift and Rotate Operations
- **LSL** (Logical Shift Left), **LSR** (Logical Shift Right), **ASR** (Arithmetic Shift Right), **ROR** (Rotate Right), **RRX** (Rotate Right Extended by Carry).
- **How:** Shifts are part of the *Operand2* flex field — they occur in the **barrel shifter** in parallel with ALU operations, costing **0 extra clock cycles**.

#### Family 5: Comparison Operations (No Result Written)
- **CMP Rn, Operand2** → computes $Rn - Operand2$ and **discards** the result, **only updates APSR flags**.
- **CMN Rn, Operand2** → $Rn + Operand2$ for flag updates
- **TST Rn, Operand2** → $Rn \ \& \ Operand2$ (tests if any bits set)
- **TEQ Rn, Operand2** → $Rn \oplus Operand2$ (tests equality)
- **Why:** All comparisons are *flag-setting by default* (the `S` is implicit in the opcode).

#### Family 6: Multiply and Multiply-Accumulate
- **MUL Rd, Rm, Rs** → $Rd \leftarrow (Rm \times Rs)_{low\ 32\ bits}$
- **MLA Rd, Rm, Rs, Rn** → $Rd \leftarrow (Rm \times Rs + Rn)_{low\ 32\ bits}$
- **MLS Rd, Rm, Rs, Rn** → $Rd \leftarrow Rn - (Rm \times Rs)_{low\ 32\ bits}$
- **UMULL / SMULL / UMLAL / SMLAL** → produce **64-bit** results, split across two registers (RdLo, RdHi).
- **How:** Multiply takes **1 cycle** in Cortex-M3/M4 (vs. many cycles in legacy ARM7).

#### Family 7: Saturating Arithmetic (DSP-oriented)
- **SSAT Rd, #n, Rm {,shift}** → saturates a signed value to fit in **n** bits
- **USAT Rd, #n, Rm {,shift}** → saturates an unsigned value to fit in **n** bits
- **Why:** Prevents overflow wrap-around in audio/signal processing.

#### Family 8: Sign/Zero Extension
- **SXTB / SXTH** → Sign-Extend a Byte/Halfword to 32 bits
- **UXTB / UXTH** → Zero-Extend a Byte/Halfword to 32 bits

#### Family 9: Bit-Field Extract and Insert
- **BFC Rd, #lsb, #width** → Clear a bit field
- **BFI Rd, Rn, #lsb, #width** → Insert a bit field
- **UBFX / SBFX** → Unsigned/Signed Bit-Field eXtract

#### Family 10: Byte-Reversing
- **REV, REV16, REVSH** → reverse byte order (endianness conversion)

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Category** | **Instruction(s)** | **Mathematical Operation** | **Flag Update Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Move | MOV, MVN | $Rd = Op2 \text{ or } \overline{Op2}$ | Only with `S` suffix |
| Add | ADD, ADC | $Rd = Rn + Op2 \ (+\ C)$ | Only with `S` suffix |
| Subtract | SUB, SBC, RSB, RSC | $Rd = Rn - Op2 \ (-\ C)$ or reverse | Only with `S` suffix |
| Logical | AND, ORR, EOR, BIC, ORN | $Rd = Rn \ \&,\ \vert,\ \oplus\ Op2$ | Only with `S` suffix |
| Compare | CMP, CMN, TST, TEQ | Flags updated, **no Rd written** | **Always** updates flags |
| Multiply | MUL | $Rd = (Rm \times Rs) \bmod 2^{32}$ | Only with `S` suffix |
| Multiply-Accumulate | MLA | $Rd = (Rm \times Rs + Rn) \bmod 2^{32}$ | Only with `S` suffix |
| Long Multiply (Unsigned) | UMULL | $RdHi \cdot 2^{32} + RdLo = Rm \times Rs$ | **Always** updates flags |
| Long Multiply (Signed) | SMULL | $RdHi \cdot 2^{32} + RdLo = Rm \times Rs$ (signed) | **Always** updates flags |
| Logical Shift Left | LSL | $Result = x \times 2^{n}$ | (Part of Operand2) |
| Logical Shift Right | LSR | $Result = \lfloor x \div 2^{n} \rfloor$ | (Part of Operand2) |
| Arithmetic Shift Right | ASR | Sign bit replicated, $Result = \lfloor x \div 2^{n} \rfloor$ signed | (Part of Operand2) |
| Saturate Signed | SSAT | $Result = \text{clamp}(x,\ -2^{n-1},\ 2^{n-1}-1)$ | Sets Q flag if saturated |
| Saturate Unsigned | USAT | $Result = \text{clamp}(x,\ 0,\ 2^{n}-1)$ | Sets Q flag if saturated |
| Sign-Extend Byte | SXTB | $Rd = \text{sign-extend}(Rm_{7:0})$ | — |
| Zero-Extend Byte | UXTB | $Rd = \text{zero-extend}(Rm_{7:0})$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall (Examiner's Favourite):** `LSL Rd, Rm, #n` is mathematically equivalent to multiplication by $2^{n}$ — this conversion is regularly tested in KTU numerical problems.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Data processing instructions power:
- **Digital filtering** (FIR/IIR in DSP audio codecs) → `MLA`, `SSAT`
- **Motor control PWM** (PID loops) → `MUL`, `ADD`, saturated math
- **Cryptographic bit-slicing** (AES S-boxes) → `EOR`, byte reversal
- **Sensor data fusion** → `MLA` for weighted sums
- **Real-time scheduling bitmasks** → `ORR` to set, `BIC` to clear task-ready bits

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem A — Barrel Shifter Output Tracing

**Problem:** Given R1 = `0x80000004` and the instruction `MOV R0, R1, LSL #3`, compute R0. Show the binary shift and flag derivation.

**Step-by-step exhaustive solution:**

Let the initial value be:
$$R1 = 0x80000004 = 1000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0100_{2}$$

Step 1 — Identify the shift type: LSL (Logical Shift Left) by 3.

Step 2 — A Logical Shift Left by $n$ multiplies by $2^{n}$:
$$R0 = R1 \times 2^{3} = 0x80000004 \times 8$$

Step 3 — Multiplication:
$$0x80000004 \times 0x8 = 0x4\ 0000\ 0020$$

Step 4 — Binary verification: shift every bit left by 3 positions, filling the right with 3 zeros. The top 3 bits `100` are discarded (these become the Carry flag bits):
$$1000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0100\ 000_{2} = 0x40000020$$

Step 5 — Final result:
$$R0 = 0x40000020$$
$$C\ (Carry) = 1\ \ (\text{bits shifted out were } 100)$$
$$N\ (Negative) = 0\ \ (\text{MSB of result is } 0)$$
$$Z\ (Zero) = 0\ \ (\text{result is non-zero})$$

### 3.2 Analytical Problem B — Saturated Arithmetic

**Problem:** Execute `SSAT R0, #16, R1, ASR #4` where R1 = `0x00012345`. Show the intermediate and final clamped value.

**Step-by-step exhaustive solution:**

Step 1 — Decode the instruction: Signed-SATurate R0 to a range bounded by $n=16$ bits, from the source R1 Arithmetic Shifted Right by 4.

Step 2 — Compute the target saturated range for $n=16$ signed bits:
$$R_{min} = -2^{n-1} = -2^{15} = -32768 = 0xFFFF8000$$
$$R_{max} = 2^{n-1} - 1 = 32767 = 0x00007FFF$$

Step 3 — Perform the ASR by 4 (arithmetic shift right, sign bit replicated). Since bit 31 of R1 is 0, ASR is identical to LSR:
$$R1_{shifted} = 0x00012345 \gg_{arith} 4 = 0x00001234$$

Step 4 — Check the value against the saturation bounds:
$$0x00001234 = 4660$$
$$-32768 \le 4660 \le 32767 \quad \checkmark \text{ (no saturation needed)}$$

Step 5 — Final result:
$$R0 = 0x00001234$$
$$Q\ \text{flag} = 0\ \text{(not saturated)}$$

**Counter-example (Saturated Case):** If R1 = `0x00080000`, then $0x00080000 \gg 4 = 0x00008000 = 32768$. Since $32768 > 32767$, the value exceeds $R_{max}$, so it saturates:
$$R0 = 0x00007FFF = 32767$$
$$Q\ \text{flag} = 1$$

### 3.3 Analytical Problem C — Multiply-Accumulate Trace

**Problem:** Evaluate `MLA R0, R1, R2, R3` where R1 = `0x00010002`, R2 = `0x00000003`, R3 = `0x00000005`. Show all intermediate steps and flag status.

**Step-by-step exhaustive solution:**

Step 1 — Decode: $R0 \leftarrow (R1 \times R2) + R3$, retaining only the low 32 bits.

Step 2 — Compute the product:
$$R1 \times R2 = 0x00010002 \times 0x00000003$$

Step 3 — Expand:
$$0x00010002 \times 3 = 0x00010002 + 0x00010002 + 0x00010002 = 0x00030006$$

Step 4 — Add the accumulator R3:
$$0x00030006 + 0x00000005 = 0x0003000B$$

Step 5 — Final result:
$$R0 = 0x0003000B = 196619$$

Step 6 — Flag computation (assuming the `S` suffix was used, i.e., `MLAS`):
- $N = 0$ (MSB of result is 0)
- $Z = 0$ (result is non-zero)
- $C = $ undefined for MLA per ARM ARM (marked as "unchanged" in Cortex-M3, "0" in Cortex-M4)
- $V = $ undefined

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** MLA's `C` and `V` flag behaviour is architecture-version dependent. State "undefined" or "not updated" as the safe KTU answer unless the question explicitly specifies the core.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Data Processing Path in Cortex-M

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Fetch from Flash/ROM] --> B[Instruction Decode Stage]
    B --> C{Is it a Data Processing Instruction?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Read Source Registers Rn and Rm from Register File]
    D --> E[Apply Optional Shift or Rotate via Barrel Shifter]
    E --> F[ALU Performs Arithmetic or Logical Operation]
    F --> G[Update Destination Register Rd]
    F --> H[Update APSR Flags N Z C V if S suffix present]
    G --> I[Next Instruction]
    H --> I
    C -- No --> J[Memory Access Path - LDR or STR]
    J --> I
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree — Data Processing Instruction Families

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Data Processing Instructions]
    root --> A1[Movement - MOV MVN]
    root --> A2[Arithmetic - ADD SUB ADC SBC RSB RSC]
    root --> A3[Logical - AND ORR EOR BIC ORN]
    root --> A4[Shift Rotate - LSL LSR ASR ROR RRX]
    root --> A5[Compare - CMP CMN TST TEQ]
    root --> A6[Multiply - MUL MLA MLS UMULL SMULL]
    root --> A7[Saturating - SSAT USAT]
    root --> A8[Extension - SXTB UXTB SXTH UXTH]
    root --> A9[Bit-Field - BFC BFI UBFX SBFX]
    root --> A10[Byte Reverse - REV REV16 REVSH]
```

### 4.3 Operand2 Flex Field Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Rm[Source Register Rm] --> S[Barrel Shifter]
    I[Immediate Shift Amount 0 to 31] --> S
    S --> Op2[Operand2 to ALU]
    R[Register Shift Rs - LSL LSR ASR ROR] --> S
    Op2 --> ALU[ALU Performs ADD SUB AND ORR etc]
    Rn[Second Operand Rn] --> ALU
    ALU --> Rd[Destination Rd]
    ALU --> F[APSR Flags - if S suffix]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Explain the significance of the `S` suffix in ARM Cortex-M data processing instructions. What happens to the APSR flags if the suffix is omitted during an `ADD` operation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `S` suffix (e.g., `ADDS`, `SUBS`) instructs the processor to **update the APSR flags (N, Z, C, V)** based on the result of the operation. If the suffix is **omitted** (e.g., `ADD R0, R1, R2`), the destination register is updated, but the **flags remain unchanged**, preserving any prior flag state. This is critical in interrupt handlers and conditional code where flag state must be preserved across operations.
*(Award 1 mark for stating the suffix, 1 mark for flag update, 1 mark for practical significance.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*Differentiate between `LSL` and `ASR` shift operations in ARM Cortex-M. Provide one example use case for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
