`LSL` (Logical Shift Left) shifts bits toward the MSB, filling the LSB end with zeros, and is mathematically equivalent to multiplication by $2^{n}$. `ASR` (Arithmetic Shift Right) shifts toward the LSB, **replicating the sign bit** into the vacated MSB positions, preserving the sign of a signed integer. `LSL` is used to **pack bit-fields** (e.g., constructing a peripheral configuration word). `ASR` is used in **signed fixed-point division** by powers of 2.
*(Award 1.5 marks for the difference, 1.5 marks for the use cases.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Operand2 flex field** mechanism in ARM Cortex-M data processing instructions. Explain how the barrel shifter executes the shift for **free** in parallel with the ALU. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the equivalent assembly sequence to perform the following C operation using **only** data processing instructions (no LDR/STR):
```c
int result = (a * 7) + (b >> 2) - c;
```
Assume variables `a`, `b`, `c`, and `result` are already in registers R0, R1, R2, R3 respectively. Show the register state after each instruction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks):**
The Operand2 mechanism is a **secondary input path** to the ALU, distinct from the standard Rn operand. Operand2 is a 12-bit field in the instruction encoding that can be one of:
1. **An immediate value** (8-bit constant, rotated right by an even number)
2. **A register Rm**, possibly **shifted** by a 5-bit immediate amount (0–31)
3. **A register Rm shifted by another register Rs**

The **barrel shifter** is a combinational logic block composed of 32 parallel multiplexers. When an instruction is decoded, the shift amount is presented to the barrel shifter, which produces the shifted Operand2 result in **a single clock cycle's combinational delay**, before the ALU latches it. Because the shifter is purely combinational, it does not consume an additional clock cycle. The ALU then performs ADD, SUB, AND, etc., on `Rn` and the shifted `Operand2` simultaneously. This is why an `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #4` takes the **same 1 cycle** as a plain `ADD R0, R1, R2` in the Cortex-M3 pipeline.
*(Award 2 marks for the diagram, 3 marks for the flex field description, 2 marks for the parallel-execution explanation.)*

**(b) Assembly Sequence (7 marks):**

```asm
        ; R0 = a, R1 = b, R2 = c, R3 = result
        ; Step 1: Compute a * 7 using MUL and ADD (a*7 = a*8 - a = a<<3 - a)
        LSL     R4, R0, #3          ; R4 = a * 8
        SUB     R4, R4, R0          ; R4 = a*8 - a = a*7    [1.5 marks]

        ; Step 2: Compute b >> 2 using LSR
        LSR     R5, R1, #2          ; R5 = b / 4            [1 mark]

        ; Step 3: Combine a*7 + (b>>2)
        ADD     R4, R4, R5          ; R4 = a*7 + b/4        [1 mark]

        ; Step 4: Subtract c
        SUB     R3, R4, R2          ; R3 = (a*7 + b/4) - c = result [1.5 marks]

        ; Final register state:
        ; R0 = a, R1 = b, R2 = c, R3 = result, R4 = scratch, R5 = scratch
```
**Alternative accepted:** `MUL R4, R0, #7` is not valid (MUL uses two registers), so `MLA R4, R0, R4_const, R0` is the canonical technique. Full marks awarded if the student demonstrates the LSL#3 + SUB technique or any other mathematically equivalent sequence.
*(Award 1 mark for correct mathematical decomposition of multiplication by 7, 1 mark each for correct shift/algebraic step, 0.5 mark for register tracking.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **saturating arithmetic instructions `SSAT` and `USAT`** in ARM Cortex-M. Describe their role in DSP applications and how the **Q (saturation) flag** in APSR is utilized. Provide the saturation range formula for `SSAT Rd, #n, Rm`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Trace the execution of the following code, and determine the final values of R0, R1, and the APSR flags (N, Z, C, V) after each instruction. Initial state: R0 = `0xF0000004`, R1 = `0x00000003`, APSR = `0x00000000`. **(7 marks)**
```asm
        ADDS    R2, R0, R1          ; (i)
        MOVS    R3, #0xFF
        BICS    R0, R0, R3, LSL #8  ; (ii)
        SUBS    R1, R0, R2          ; (iii)
```

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Saturating Arithmetic Explanation (7 marks):**
Saturation arithmetic prevents the **wrap-around** behaviour of modular 2's-complement arithmetic when a result exceeds the representable range. `SSAT Rd, #n, Rm` treats the value in Rm as a signed integer and clamps it to the range:
$$\text{clamp}(Rm) = \begin{cases} -2^{n-1} & \text{if } Rm < -2^{n-1} \\ Rm & \text{if } -2^{n-1} \le Rm \le 2^{n-1} - 1 \\ 2^{n-1} - 1 & \text{if } Rm > 2^{n-1} - 1 \end{cases}$$
The result is written to Rd. The Q flag in APSR is **set to 1** if any clamping occurred; otherwise, it remains unchanged. Once set, **Q is sticky** — it can only be cleared by writing directly to APSR (using `MSR`). `USAT` performs the same clamping for an **unsigned** range of $[0,\ 2^{n}-1]$. These instructions are essential in **DSP** because overflow in audio/video sample processing causes audible clicks and visual artifacts; saturation produces graceful, bounded distortion.
*(Award 2 marks for range formula, 2 marks for the Q flag mechanism, 2 marks for DSP significance, 1 mark for sticky nature of Q.)*

**(b) Trace Execution (7 marks):**

**Initial state:** R0 = `0xF0000004`, R1 = `0x00000003`, APSR = 0x00000000

**(i) `ADDS R2, R0, R1`**
$$R2 = 0xF0000004 + 0x00000003 = 0xF0000007$$
Flags: N=1, Z=0, C=0, V=0 *(result MSB is 1, no carry out, no signed overflow)*
`[1.5 marks]`

**(ii) `MOVS R3, #0xFF`** *(no dependency, but shown for completeness)*
$$R3 = 0x000000FF$$
`[0.5 marks]`

**(iii) `BICS R0, R0, R3, LSL #8`**
First, Operand2 = `R3 LSL #8` = `0x000000FF << 8` = `0x0000FF00`
Then, `BIC` clears the bits that are 1 in Operand2 from R0:
$$R0 = 0xF0000004 \ \& \ \overline{0x0000FF00} = 0xF0000004 \ \& \ 0xFFFF00FF = 0xF0000004$$
Since R0 already has zeros in bits [15:8], the BIC is a no-op for this value.
R0 remains `0xF0000004`. Flags unchanged (BICS does update flags; N=1, Z=0, C=0, V=0).
`[2 marks]`

**(iv) `SUBS R1, R0, R2`**
$$R1 = 0xF0000004 - 0xF0000007 = 0xFFFFFFFF$$
(With borrow: 0xF0000004 − 0xF0000007 = −3 in signed = `0xFFFFFFFD`. **Wait — correct calculation:**)
$$0xF0000004 - 0xF0000007 \rightarrow \text{two's complement subtraction}$$
$$= 0xF0000004 + \overline{0xF0000007} + 1 = 0xF0000004 + 0x0FFFFFF8 + 1 = 0xFFFFFFFD$$
Flags: N=1 (bit 31 = 1), Z=0, C=0 (borrow occurred, so carry flag is 0 in SUB semantics), V=0.
`[3 marks]`

**Final Register State:**
- R0 = `0xF0000004`
- R1 = `0xFFFFFFFD` (i.e., −3 signed)
- R2 = `0xF0000007`
- R3 = `0x000000FF`
- APSR: N=1, Z=0, C=0, V=0

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the C flag in SUB vs. ADD:** Many students set C=1 in `SUBS` when a borrow occurs. The correct KTU convention is: **C = 0 means a borrow happened; C = 1 means no borrow** (inverse of intuition). This single mistake costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the `S` suffix behaviour:** Writing `ADD R2, R0, R1` in sub-part (b) — the question explicitly mandates `ADDS`. Marks are deducted if flag analysis is missing.
> 3. **LSL#8 trace error:** Tracing `0xFF LSL #8` as `0xFF00` is correct, but tracing it as `0xFF000000` is a classic bit-shift error.
> 4. **Treating Q flag as ordinary:** Writing "Q flag is reset on the next instruction" — Q is **sticky-set** and must be explicitly cleared via `MSR APSR_nzcvq, Rn`.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **All data processing instructions operate ONLY on registers** (R0–R12) — never on memory directly. Memory access requires LDR/STR.
- The **`S` suffix is the gatekeeper for APSR flag updates**. Without `S`, flags are preserved.
- **Comparisons (CMP, CMN, TST, TEQ)** implicitly carry the `S` semantics — they **always** update flags and **never** write a result register.
- **Barrel shifter is free** — shifts in Operand2 add zero cycles to execution.
- **Multiplication takes 1 cycle** in Cortex-M3/M4, with `MUL` producing a 32-bit result and `UMULL/SMULL` producing a 64-bit result split across two registers.
- **LSL by n = multiplication by $2^{n}$**; **LSR by n = unsigned division by $2^{n}$**; **ASR by n = signed division by $2^{n}$**.
- The **Q flag is sticky** once set — explicit `MSR` is required to clear it.
- **`BIC` does NOT mean "branch if carry"** — it is the **Bit Clear** logical instruction.
- **Bounded shift values:** Immediate shifts are 0–31; register-controlled shifts use the **bottom byte** of Rs (mask `& 0xFF`).
- **`MLA` carries the accumulator as the 4th operand**, not as a separate add instruction.
- **Flag undefinedness** for `MUL`/`MLA` `C` and `V` flags is a common KTU trick question.
- **Operand2 supports only 8-bit immediates rotated by an even number** (the famous ARM "modified immediate" / "rotated byte" constant).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Transfer Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Transfer Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Data Transfer Instructions** are a class of machine-level instructions in an embedded processor's Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) that facilitate the movement of data between internal registers, between a register and a memory location, between an I/O port and a CPU register, or between the CPU stack and a register — **without modifying the contents of the source operand**.

These instructions form the **load-store backbone** of any Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC) or Complex Instruction Set Computer (CISC) architecture. In the context of 8051 / ARM Cortex-M / 8086-based embedded systems, data transfer operations are fundamental for input acquisition, output control, and intermediate variable handling.

## 1.2 Key Classification of Data Transfer Operations

| S.No | Transfer Type | Source → Destination | Example Instructions |
|:----:|:--------------|:---------------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | Register-to-Register | CPU Register → CPU Register | `MOV A, R0` (8051), `MOV R0, R1` (ARM) |
| 2 | Register-to-Memory | CPU Register → RAM | `MOV [R0], R1` |
| 3 | Memory-to-Register | RAM → CPU Register | `MOV R1, [R0]` |
| 4 | Immediate-to-Register | Constant → CPU Register | `MOV R0, #25H` |
| 5 | Register-to-Stack | CPU Register → Stack Memory | `PUSH R0` |
| 6 | Stack-to-Register | Stack Memory → CPU Register | `POP R0` |
| 7 | I/O Port Transfer | I/O Port → Register / Vice-versa | `IN A, P1`, `OUT P1, A` |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Warehouse Worker":**
> Imagine a warehouse where you (the **CPU**) are seated at a desk, and you have a personal notepad (your **registers**). You have a huge storage room in the back (**Memory**), and a loading dock (**I/O ports**) where trucks bring in raw materials and take finished goods out.
>
> - **MOV (Move):** A worker carries a box from one part of your desk to another — the box is *duplicated* into the destination, the original is *kept* (in 8051, MOV does copy; the source is preserved).
> - **PUSH:** You put a box onto a vertical stack of trays on your desk — to grab a specific tray, you must first take the one on top.
> - **POP:** You lift the topmost tray off the stack to read its contents.
> - **XCHG (Exchange):** Two workers swap boxes simultaneously — you give yours, you take theirs.
> - **LEA (Load Effective Address):** Instead of fetching a box, you note down *where the box is located* on a slip of paper.
> - **IN/OUT:** Trucks at the loading dock — you either receive a shipment (IN) or send one out (OUT).
>
> **The fundamental rule:** *No data is created or destroyed during transfer — only relocated or duplicated.* This is why the flags (except for very few specific cases) are **NOT** affected.

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **MOV execution time (8051, 12 MHz crystal):** **1 µs per machine cycle** (1 byte MOV) and **2 µs** (2 byte MOV involving direct addressing).
- **Stack growth direction (standard 8051 / 8086):** **Upward in memory** — stack pointer *increments* on PUSH and *decrements* on POP.
- **Default data width (8051):** **8 bits (1 byte)** per MOV.
- **ARM Cortex-M default transfer width:** **32 bits** (word), with support for 8-bit (byte) and 16-bit (halfword) transfers via suffix `B` and `H`.
- **Endianness (ARM Cortex-M):** **Little-endian** by default; `REV` / `REV16` / `REVSH` instructions used for byte-order reversal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Data Transfer Instruction

Every data transfer instruction, regardless of architecture, can be broken into **four logical components**:

1. **Opcode (Operation Code):** The binary pattern that tells the CPU *what* operation to perform (e.g., `MOV`, `PUSH`).
2. **Source Operand:** The location whose data is read (the *original*).
3. **Destination Operand:** The location that receives a *copy* of the data.
4. **Addressing Mode Encoding:** The mechanism used to specify *where* the operands physically reside (immediate, direct, indirect, indexed, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction — MOV vs. XCHG vs. LEA:**
> - `MOV` → Copies data, **source preserved**.
> - `XCHG` → Swaps data, **both operands change**.
> - `LEA` → Loads the **address** (pointer) into a register — does *not* fetch the actual data.

## 2.2 Detailed Classification with Addressing Modes

### A) MOV — The Workhorse Instruction

| Operand Format | 8051 Syntax | ARM Equivalent | Description |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Immediate to Register | `MOV A, #data` | `MOV R0, #0x25` | Loads a constant |
| Register to Register | `MOV A, R0` | `MOV R1, R0` | Copy between registers |
| Direct Memory to Acc | `MOV A, 30H` | `LDR R0, [R1]` | Read from RAM |
| Acc to Direct Memory | `MOV 30H, A` | `STR R0, [R1]` | Write to RAM |
| Indirect to Acc | `MOV A, @R0` | `LDR R0, [R1, #4]` | Pointer-based read |
| Acc to Indirect | `MOV @R0, A` | `STR R0, [R1, #4]` | Pointer-based write |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** MOV instructions are the **most frequently executed** instructions in any embedded firmware. Compiler-generated assembly code typically shows 30–40% of all opcodes as some form of MOV.

### B) PUSH and POP — Stack Operations

- **PUSH src:** `SP ← SP − 1 (or + 1 for descending stack); Memory[SP] ← src`
- **POP dst:** `dst ← Memory[SP]; SP ← SP + 1 (or − 1)`

**8051 (Ascending Stack):**

$$\text{PUSH: } SP \leftarrow SP + 1, \quad \text{RAM}[SP] \leftarrow \text{src}$$

$$\text{POP: } \quad \text{dst} \leftarrow \text{RAM}[SP], \quad SP \leftarrow SP - 1$$

**ARM Cortex-M (Full Descending Stack):**

$$\text{PUSH: } SP \leftarrow SP - 4, \quad \text{Memory}[SP] \leftarrow \text{Register}$$

$$\text{POP: } \quad \text{Register} \leftarrow \text{Memory}[SP], \quad SP \leftarrow SP + 4$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARM Cortex-M uses a "Full Descending" stack model**, which is the most common convention in modern RISC embedded systems. The stack pointer always points to the **last occupied** location, and the stack grows toward *lower* memory addresses.

### C) XCHG — Exchange Instruction

The `XCH` (Exchange) instruction swaps the contents of the accumulator with a specified register or memory location **in a single atomic operation**. This is useful in sorting algorithms and semaphore implementations in RTOS kernels.

**Operation:**
$$\text{XCH A, R0:} \quad A \leftrightarrow R0$$

After execution:
- New `A` = Old `R0`
- New `R0` = Old `A`

In ARM, there is **no native XCH instruction**, but it can be emulated using atomic read-modify-write sequences or the **exclusive access** instructions `LDREX`/`STREX`.

### D) LEA — Load Effective Address

`LEA` does **not access memory**; it computes an address and loads it into a register. It is used to set up pointer variables.

**8086 Example:**
```
LEA BX, [SI + 10H]    ; BX ← SI + 0x10, no memory read
MOV AX, [SI + 10H]    ; AX ← Memory[SI + 0x10], actual read
```

In ARM, this is typically a side-effect of `ADR` (pseudo-instruction) or `ADD Rd, Rn, Rm` since ARM uses **register-indirect** addressing.

### E) String/Block Transfer Instructions

Used for moving large blocks of memory efficiently.

| Architecture | Instruction | Function |
|:--|:--|:--|
| 8086 | `MOVSB` | Move 1 byte from `[DS:SI]` to `[ES:DI]`, auto-increment/decrement |
| 8086 | `MOVSW` | Move 1 word (2 bytes) |
| 8086 | `REP MOVSB` | Repeat `MOVSB` `CX` times — bulk transfer |
| ARM | `LDMIA` / `STMIA` | Load/Store Multiple, Increment After |
| ARM | `LDMFD` / `STMFD` | Full Descending stack operations (PUSH/POP aliases) |

### F) I/O Port Transfer (8051 Specific)

The 8051 has a **separate I/O address space** accessed via `MOVX`:

- `MOVX A, @DPTR` — Read external data memory / peripheral
- `MOVX @DPTR, A` — Write external data memory / peripheral
- `MOVX A, @R0` — Read using R0/R1 as pointer (8-bit address)
- `MOVX @R0, A` — Write using R0/R1

The dedicated 8051 SFRs for I/O are P0, P1, P2, P3 (Ports 0–3), accessible via:
- `MOV A, P1` (Read Port 1)
- `MOV P1, A` (Write to Port 1)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Stack Pointer after PUSH (8051) | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 1$ | Ascending stack |
| 2 | Stack Pointer after POP (8051) | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 1$ | Ascending stack |
| 3 | Stack Pointer after PUSH (ARM) | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 4$ | Full descending, 32-bit |
| 4 | Effective Address (Direct) | $EA = \text{Address field}$ | $EA$ in bytes |
| 5 | Effective Address (Immediate) | $EA = \text{PC} + \text{Offset}$ | PC-relative |
| 6 | Block transfer count | $N_{bytes} = CX$ (8086) or `$N_{words} = \text{register list count}$` (ARM) | In words/bytes |
| 7 | MOV execution time (8051) | $T_{MOV} = 12 \times T_{osc} = 1\,\mu s$ at 12 MHz | 1 machine cycle |
| 8 | MOVX execution time (8051) | $T_{MOVX} = 24 \times T_{osc} = 2\,\mu s$ at 12 MHz | 2 machine cycles |
| 9 | PUSH/POP execution (8051) | $T_{push} = T_{pop} = 2\,\mu s$ at 12 MHz | 2 machine cycles |
| 10 | Cycle count for ARM LDR/STR | $N_{cycles} = 1$ (single), $N_{cycles} = 2$ (literal pool) | 3-stage pipeline |
| 11 | Data width constants | 8-bit = 1 byte, 16-bit = 1 halfword, 32-bit = 1 word | Standard ISA widths |
| 12 | Address calculation (base+index) | $EA = \text{Base} + \text{Index} \times \text{Scale} + \text{Displacement}$ | x86 / ARM scaled |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility — Where Data Transfer Instructions Are Used:**
> - **Sensor Interfacing:** Reading ADC values into a register via `MOV`.
> - **Actuator Control:** Writing control bytes to GPIO ports via `MOVX` or `STR`.
> - **Context Switching (RTOS):** Bulk-register save/restore using `STMFD` / `LDMFD` (PUSH/POP multiple).
> - **DMA Setup:** Configuring source/destination addresses with `LEA` / `ADD`.
> - **Bootloader Firmware Update:** Block memory copy using `REP MOVSB` or `LDMIA/STMIA`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Stack Memory Behavior During PUSH/POP

Let us trace the exact memory transformations of a 8051 stack PUSH/POP sequence to derive the relationship between stack pointer and memory content.

**Initial Conditions (8051, Internal RAM, ascending stack):**
- Register `A = 0x3F`
- Register `R0 = 0x55`
- `SP = 0x07` (initial stack pointer, pointing to current top)

**Step 1 — Execute `PUSH ACC` (Push Accumulator)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1a: Increment SP first} \quad & SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 1 = 0x07 + 0x01 = 0x08 \\
\text{Step 1b: Write ACC to RAM[SP]} \quad & \text{RAM}[0x08] \leftarrow 0x3F \\
\text{Result: } & SP = 0x08,\ \text{RAM}[0x08] = 0x3F
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Execute `PUSH 00H` (Push R0, direct address 00H)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2a: Increment SP} \quad & SP_{new} = 0x08 + 0x01 = 0x09 \\
\text{Step 2b: Write R0 to RAM[SP]} \quad & \text{RAM}[0x09] \leftarrow 0x55 \\
\text{Result: } & SP = 0x09,\ \text{RAM}[0x09] = 0x55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Execute `POP 00H` (Pop into R0)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3a: Read RAM[SP] into R0} \quad & R0 \leftarrow \text{RAM}[0x09] = 0x55 \\
\text{Step 3b: Decrement SP} \quad & SP_{new} = 0x09 - 0x01 = 0x08 \\
\text{Result: } & R0 = 0x55,\ SP = 0x08
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Execute `POP ACC`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4a: Read RAM[SP] into ACC} \quad & A \leftarrow \text{RAM}[0x08] = 0x3F \\
\text{Step 4b: Decrement SP} \quad & SP_{new} = 0x08 - 0x01 = 0x07 \\
\text{Result: } & A = 0x3F,\ SP = 0x07
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** The SP has returned to its initial value of `0x07`, and the registers have been restored to their original values `A = 0x3F` and `R0 = 0x55`. This is the **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** property — the last value pushed (R0 = 0x55) was the first value popped.

## 3.2 Derivation: Address Calculation for LEA in 8086

The `LEA` instruction computes an address by combining segment base, offset components, and a displacement.

**Example:** `LEA BX, [SI + 10H]`

Given:
- `DS = 0x2000H` (Data Segment base)
- `SI = 0x0450H` (Source Index register)
- Displacement = `0x0010H`

**Step-by-step computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective Address (Offset)} \quad & EA = SI + \text{Displacement} \\
& EA = 0x0450H + 0x0010H \\
& EA = 0x0460H
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Address (20-bit)} \quad & PA = DS \times 16 + EA \\
& PA = (0x2000H \times 0x10) + 0x0460H \\
& PA = 0x20000H + 0x0460H \\
& PA = 0x20460H
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point:** `LEA BX, [SI + 10H]` loads `0x0460H` into BX — it does **not** read from physical address `0x20460H`. To access the *value* at that location, you would use `MOV BX, [SI + 10H]`.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Operand-Size & Effect on Flags

| Instruction | Architecture | Size (bits) | Source Preserved? | Flags Affected? | Cycle Count @ 12 MHz |
|:--|:--|:--:|:--:|:--|:--|
| `MOV A, R0` | 8051 | 8 | Yes | None | 1 µs |
| `MOV A, 30H` | 8051 | 8 | Yes | None | 2 µs |
| `MOV A, @R0` | 8051 | 8 | Yes | None | 2 µs |
| `MOV DPTR, #data16` | 8051 | 16 | Yes | None | 2 µs |
| `MOVX A, @DPTR` | 8051 | 8 | Yes | None | 2 µs |
| `PUSH direct` | 8051 | 8 | No (consumed) | None | 2 µs |
| `POP direct` | 8051 | 8 | Replaced | None | 2 µs |
| `XCH A, Rn` | 8051 | 8 | No (swapped) | None | 1 µs |
| `LDR R0, [R1]` | ARM | 32 | Yes | None | 1 cycle |
| `STR R0, [R1]` | ARM | 32 | Yes | None | 1 cycle |
| `PUSH {R4-R7, LR}` | ARM | 5 regs | Moved to stack | None | Variable |
| `POP {R4-R7, PC}` | ARM | 5 regs | Restored | None | Variable |
| `MOVSB` | 8086 | 8 | Yes | None (DF controls direction) | 18 cycles |
| `REP MOVSB` (CX = 100) | 8086 | 800 bits | Yes | None | 9 + 17×CX cycles |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Block Transfer Timing (8051)

**Problem:** A bootloader needs to copy **256 bytes** from external Flash (starting at `0x1000H`) to internal RAM (starting at `30H`) using 8051 assembly.

**Approach:** Use a loop with `MOVC` (code memory read) and `MOV` to RAM.

**Pseudocode:**
```
MOV DPTR, #1000H      ; 2 µs — point to source
MOV R0, #30H          ; 1 µs — destination pointer
MOV R1, #00H          ; 1 µs — counter (256)

LOOP:
  MOVC A, @A+DPTR     ; 2 µs — read byte from code ROM (Note: A=0 initial)
  MOV @R0, A           ; 1 µs — write to RAM
  INC DPTR             ; 2 µs — source advance
  INC R0               ; 1 µs — destination advance
  DJNZ R1, LOOP        ; 2 µs — loop until 256 done
```

**Total Time Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{setup} &= 2 + 1 + 1 = 4\,\mu s \\
T_{loop} &= (2 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 2)\,\mu s \times 256 = 8 \times 256 = 2048\,\mu s \\
T_{total} &= 4 + 2048 = 2052\,\mu s \approx 2.05\,ms
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimization Insight:** A real bootloader would use the **DPTR auto-increment** feature and unroll the loop, reducing overhead. For very large transfers, a DMA controller or a `REP MOVSB`-style architecture would be far more efficient.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Data Transfer Pathways in an Embedded CPU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CPU Core] --> B[Register File R0 to R7]
    A --> C[Accumulator ACC]
    A --> D[Data Pointer DPTR]
    A --> E[Stack Pointer SP]

    B <-->|MOV Rn, Rm| B
    C <-->|MOV A, Rn| B
    D <-->|MOV DPTR, data16| F[External Memory]
    E <-->|PUSH POP| G[Internal RAM Stack]

    C <-->|MOVX A, DPTR| F
    F -->|MOVC A, A+DPTR| H[Code ROM]

    I[I O Ports P0 P1 P2 P3] <-->|MOV Pn, A| C
    J[Peripherals ADC Timer UART] <-->|MOVX| F

    A -->|XCH A, Rn| B
    A -->|XCHD A, Rn| B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: PUSH/POP Stack Evolution (LIFO Behavior)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0[Step 0: SP = 0x07, Stack Empty]
    S1[Step 1: PUSH ACC, SP = 0x08, RAM 0x08 = 3F]
    S2[Step 2: PUSH R0, SP = 0x09, RAM 0x09 = 55]
    S3[Step 3: POP R0, R0 = 0x55, SP = 0x08]
    S4[Step 4: POP ACC, A = 0x3F, SP = 0x07]

    S0 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4

    style S0 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style S4 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: ARM Block Transfer Using LDM/STM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Register Bank: R0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7] -->|STMIA Rn, R0-R7| B[Memory Block A]
    B -->|LDMIA Rn, R0-R7| A
    C[Stack Memory: Full Descending] -->|STMFD PUSH R4-R7, LR| A
    A -->|LDMFD POP R4-R7, PC| C

    style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style B fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#003366
    style C fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart: Decision Logic for Choosing Data Transfer Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need to preserve source?}
    Q1 -- No, swap required --> X1[XCHG instruction]
    Q1 -- Yes, plain copy --> Q2{Data size?}

    Q2 -- 8-bit, single value --> M1[MOV immediate to register]
    Q2 -- 16/32-bit constant --> M2[MOV DPTR / LDR literal]
    Q2 -- Block of data --> M3[REP MOVSB / LDMIA STMIA]
    Q2 -- Save/Restore context --> M4[PUSH POP / STMFD LDMFD]

    Q1 -- Need address only, not data --> L1[LEA / ADR pseudo-instruction]

    style X1 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style L1 fill:#d9b3ff,stroke:#6600cc
    style M3 fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#0066cc
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Style]**
**Differentiate between the `MOV` and `XCH` instructions in the 8051 microcontroller. Which flags are affected by these instructions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `MOV` Instruction | `XCH` Instruction |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Operation | Copies data from source to destination | Swaps (exchanges) data between two operands |
| Source operand | Preserved | Modified (replaced with old destination value) |
| No. of operands | 2 (source, destination) | 2 (typically A and Rn or direct) |
| Example | `MOV A, R0` (A ← R0) | `XCH A, R0` (A ↔ R0) |
| Flag Effect | **No flags affected** | **No flags affected** |
| Use case | General data movement | Sorting, semaphore handshakes |

**Key Point:** Both `MOV` and `XCH` instructions do **not affect any program status word (PSW) flags** in the 8051.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**
**Explain the function of the `MOVX` instruction in 8051. Why is a separate instruction needed when `MOV` is already available?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `MOVX` (Move External) instruction is used to access the **external data memory space** of the 8051 (which shares address lines with external RAM and memory-mapped peripherals).

**Why a separate instruction is needed:**
1. The 8051 uses a **Harvard architecture** with separate address spaces for:
   - Internal RAM (128 / 256 bytes) — accessed via `MOV`
   - External Data Memory (up to 64 KB) — accessed via `MOVX`
   - Code ROM (up to 64 KB) — accessed via `MOVC`
2. `MOVX` uses a **different addressing mechanism** (16-bit `@DPTR` or 8-bit `@R0/@R1`) and generates the **RD/WR control signals** to external memory.
3. It typically takes **2 machine cycles (2 µs at 12 MHz)** vs. 1 cycle for internal `MOV`.

**Examples:**
- `MOVX A, @DPTR` — Read byte from external RAM pointed by DPTR.
- `MOVX @DPTR, A` — Write ACC to external RAM.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the various **addressing modes** supported by the 8051 `MOV` instruction with suitable examples. Discuss how each mode affects the instruction size and execution time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the operation of the **8051 stack** during a `PUSH` and `POP` sequence. Show the memory content and stack pointer value at each step, assuming the following initial state: `SP = 07H`, `A = 3FH`, `R0 = 55H`, `R1 = 7AH`. Execute: `PUSH ACC`, `PUSH 00H`, `PUSH 01H`, `POP 00H`, `POP ACC`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Addressing Modes of MOV (7 marks)

The 8051 `MOV` instruction supports **five primary addressing modes**:

**1. Immediate Addressing (1 byte constant embedded in instruction)**
- Syntax: `MOV A, #data`
- Example: `MOV A, #25H` → `A ← 0x25`
- Instruction size: **2 bytes** (opcode + immediate byte)
- Execution time: **1 machine cycle (1 µs)**

**2. Register Addressing (operands are CPU registers)**
- Syntax: `MOV A, Rn` (where n = 0–7)
- Example: `MOV A, R0` → `A ← R0`
- Instruction size: **1 byte**
- Execution time: **1 machine cycle (1 µs)**
- *Note:* In 8051, only the accumulator can be the destination for register-direct mode.

**3. Direct Addressing (operands are SFR or internal RAM addresses)**
- Syntax: `MOV A, direct`
- Example: `MOV A, 30H` → `A ← RAM[0x30]`
- Instruction size: **2 bytes**
- Execution time: **1 machine cycle (1 µs)**

**4. Register Indirect Addressing (pointer register holds the address)**
- Syntax: `MOV A, @Ri` (where i = 0 or 1)
- Example: `MOV A, @R0` → `A ← RAM[R0]`
- Instruction size: **1 byte**
- Execution time: **1 machine cycle (1 µs)**
- *Note:* The `@` symbol denotes indirection.

**5. Indexed Addressing (used only with MOVC for code ROM access)**
- Syntax: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` or `MOVC A, @A+PC`
- Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → `A ← CodeROM[A + DPTR]`
- Instruction size: **1 byte**
- Execution time: **2 machine cycles (2 µs)**

**Summary Table:**

| Mode | Example | Size | Cycles @ 12 MHz |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Immediate | `MOV A, #25H` | 2 B | 1 µs |
| Register | `MOV A, R0` | 1 B | 1 µs |
| Direct | `MOV A, 30H` | 2 B | 1 µs |
| Register Indirect | `MOV A, @R0` | 1 B | 1 µs |
| Indexed | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | 1 B | 2 µs |

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Stack Operation Trace (7 marks)

**Initial State:**
- `SP = 0x07`
- `A = 0x3F`
- `R0 = 0x55` (internal RAM address `0x00`)
- `R1 = 0x7A` (internal RAM address `0x01`)

**Step 1: `PUSH ACC` (Push Accumulator)**

$$
SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 1 = 0x07 + 0x01 = 0x08
$$

$$
\text{RAM}[0x08] \leftarrow A = 0x3F
$$

**State after Step 1:** `SP = 0x08`, `RAM[08] = 3FH`

**Step 2: `PUSH 00H` (Push R0, since SFR/IRAM address 00H = R0)**

$$
SP_{new} = 0x08 + 0x01 = 0x09
$$

$$
\text{RAM}[0x09] \leftarrow R0 = 0x55
$$

**State after Step 2:** `SP = 0x09`, `RAM[09] = 55H`

**Step 3: `PUSH 01H` (Push R1)**

$$
SP_{new} = 0x09 + 0x01 = 0x0A
$$

$$
\text{RAM}[0x0A] \leftarrow R1 = 0x7A
$$

**State after Step 3:** `SP = 0x0A`, `RAM[0A] = 7AH`

**Stack Diagram (Memory Map):**

```
Address : 0A  09  08  07  06  05 ...
Data    : 7A  55  3F  ??  ??  ?? ...
          ↑   ↑   ↑
        (R1) (R0) (A)  ← SP points here
```

**Step 4: `POP 00H` (Pop into R0)**

$$
R0 \leftarrow \text{RAM}[SP] = \text{RAM}[0x0A] = 0x7A
$$

$$
SP_{new} = 0x0A - 0x01 = 0x09
$$

**State after Step 4:** `SP = 0x09`, `R0 = 0x7A`

**Step 5: `POP ACC` (Pop into Accumulator)**

$$
A \leftarrow \text{RAM}[SP] = \text{RAM}[0x09] = 0x55
$$

$$
SP_{new} = 0x09 - 0x01 = 0x08
$$

**Final State after Step 5:** `SP = 0x08`, `A = 0x55`

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Because PUSH/POP is a LIFO operation, the **first value pushed (A = 0x3F) is the last to be popped**, while the **last value pushed (R1 = 0x7A) is the first to be popped**. The student must trace both the SP value and the memory content at every step to earn full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the different **types of data transfer instructions** available in an embedded processor (8051/ARM). Compare `MOV`, `PUSH/POP`, `MOVX`, and `XCH` in terms of operand size, addressing, and effect on flags. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to **transfer a block of 10 bytes** from internal RAM starting at location `30H` to external RAM starting at `1000H` using `MOVX`. Calculate the total execution time assuming a 12 MHz crystal. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Comparison of Data Transfer Instructions (7 marks)

**Types of Data Transfer Instructions in 8051:**

1. **`MOV`** — Internal data transfer (register ↔ register, register ↔ internal RAM, immediate to register).
2. **`MOVC`** — Transfer from code ROM (read-only program memory).
3. **`MOVX`** — Transfer with external data memory (uses MOVX for peripherals/external RAM).
4. **`PUSH` / `POP`** — Transfer between internal RAM and the hardware stack.
5. **`XCH` / `XCHD`** — Exchange instructions (full byte or lower nibble).
6. **`SWAP`** — Swap upper and lower nibbles of A.

**Comparative Table:**

| Feature | MOV | MOVC | MOVX | PUSH/POP | XCH |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Source | Reg / IRAM / #imm | Code ROM | External RAM | Reg/IRAM | Reg/IRAM |
| Destination | Reg / IRAM | A only | A only | Stack (RAM[SP]) | A (always) |
| Flag effect | None | None | None | None | None |
| Size (typical) | 1–3 bytes | 1 byte | 1–2 bytes | 2 bytes | 1 byte |
| Cycles @12 MHz | 1–2 µs | 2 µs | 2 µs | 2 µs | 1 µs |
| Use case | Data movement | Lookup tables | Peripheral I/O | Context save | Sorting/swap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Rule:** *No data transfer instruction in the 8051 affects the PSW flags* (Carry, Auxiliary Carry, Overflow, Parity). This is a KTU favourite concept and students must remember this clearly.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Block Transfer Program (7 marks)

**8051 Assembly Code:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV DPTR, #1000H    ; DPTR → destination in external RAM
        MOV R0, #30H        ; R0 → source in internal RAM
        MOV R2, #0AH        ; R2 = 10 (counter)

LOOP:   MOV A, @R0          ; Read byte from internal RAM (2 µs)
        MOVX @DPTR, A       ; Write to external RAM (2 µs)
        INC R0              ; Increment source pointer (1 µs)
        INC DPTR            ; Increment destination pointer (2 µs)
        DJNZ R2, LOOP       ; Decrement counter, loop if not zero (2 µs)
        END
```

**Execution Time Calculation:**

- **Setup phase (executed once):**
  - `MOV DPTR, #1000H` → **2 µs**
  - `MOV R0, #30H` → **1 µs**
  - `MOV R2, #0AH` → **1 µs**
  - **Subtotal:** $T_{setup} = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4\,\mu s$

- **Loop body (executed 10 times):**
  - `MOV A, @R0` → **2 µs**
  - `MOVX @DPTR, A` → **2 µs**
  - `INC R0` → **1 µs**
  - `INC DPTR` → **2 µs**
  - `DJNZ R2, LOOP` → **2 µs**
  - **Subtotal per iteration:** $T_{loop} = 2 + 2 + 1 + 2 + 2 = 9\,\mu s$

- **Total Time:**

$$
T_{total} = T_{setup} + (N \times T_{loop}) = 4 + (10 \times 9) = 4 + 90 = 94\,\mu s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** At 12 MHz crystal, one machine cycle = 1 µs. A 10-byte transfer thus takes only **94 µs** (~106 KB/s throughput), which is acceptable for slow peripheral data logging.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing MOV vs MOVX vs MOVC:** Many students incorrectly use `MOV` for external memory access. *Always remember:* internal RAM = `MOV`, external RAM = `MOVX`, code ROM = `MOVC`.
> 2. **Stack direction errors:** Writing `SP - 1` for PUSH on 8051 (which uses *ascending* stack) is a common mistake. ARM uses descending, 8051 uses ascending.
> 3. **Flag-affection assumption:** Some students wrongly state that `MOV` affects the Zero flag. **It does NOT.**
> 4. **Off-by-one in stack traces:** Forgetting to increment SP *before* writing during PUSH, or decrementing *after* reading during POP, leads to wrong memory mapping in exam answers.
> 5. **Cycle count errors:** Using 1 µs for MOVX (it is 2 µs at 12 MHz) or forgetting to multiply by iteration count.
> 6. **LEA confusion:** Stating that `LEA` reads memory — it loads only the *address*, not the *contents*.
> 7. **Skipping setup-phase time:** Not adding the initial `MOV DPTR` and counter-load instructions in the total time calculation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Transfer Instructions**

- ⭐ **MOV** = Copy only; **source is preserved**; **no flags affected**.
- ⭐ **MOVC** = Read from **code ROM**; uses `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC` indexed mode.
- ⭐ **MOVX** = Access **external data memory**; 2 machine cycles at 12 MHz.
- ⭐ **PUSH / POP** = Stack operations; 8051 uses **ascending** stack (`SP+1` on PUSH, `SP-1` on POP).
- ⭐ **ARM Cortex-M** uses **full descending** stack (`SP-4` on PUSH, `SP+4` on POP).
- ⭐ **XCH** = Atomic byte swap between A and another register/IRAM location.
- ⭐ **XCHD** = Swaps only the **lower nibble** of A with lower nibble of `@Ri`.
- ⭐ **SWAP** = Exchanges upper and lower 4 bits *within* A.
- ⭐ **LEA** loads the **address** (pointer) into a register — does **not** read memory.
- ⭐ **REP MOVSB (8086)** = Bulk byte transfer, count held in `CX` register.
- ⭐ **LDM/STM (ARM)** = Load/Store Multiple registers in one instruction (used for context save/restore).
- ⭐ **No data transfer instruction in 8051 modifies the PSW flags** — a KTU favourite question.
- ⭐ **MOV execution time (8051) = 1 µs (internal), 2 µs (external MOVX)** at 12 MHz crystal.
- ⭐ **Stack overflow** can corrupt user RAM and SFRs — always initialize SP before use.
- ⭐ **MOVC A, @A+DPTR** is heavily used for **lookup tables** (e.g., 7-segment codes, CRC tables).
- ⭐ **MOVX @DPTR, A** triggers the external **WR** signal; `MOVX A, @DPTR` triggers **RD**.
- ⭐ In **ARM Cortex-M**, the standard return sequence is `POP {..., PC}` — popping the saved LR value into PC to return from a function.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Flow Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Flow Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **ARM Cortex-M / ARM7TDMI architecture** (the canonical reference processor for the KTU PBEVT504 syllabus), **Control Flow Instructions** are the class of machine instructions that alter the sequential execution order of the instruction pipeline by modifying the value stored in the **Program Counter (PC / R15)**. Unlike data-processing instructions, which only manipulate general-purpose registers, control flow instructions explicitly redirect the processor's fetch stage to a non-sequential address, thereby enabling **decision-making, iteration, and modular program structuring** (subroutines, ISRs, branches).

Formally, the KTU 2024 Scheme defines three sub-classes:

1. **Branches (B / B<cond>)** – Conditional and unconditional PC-relative jumps.
2. **Branch-and-Link (BL) / Branch with eXchange (BX / BLX)** – Procedure calls supporting the **ARM-Thumb Procedure Call Standard (ATPCS)** / **AAPCS** subroutine linkage model.
3. **Special Data Operations with Flow Effects** – e.g., `CBNZ`, `CBZ` (Compare and Branch if Non-Zero/Zero) in Thumb-2, and `IT` (If-Then) conditional instruction blocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The focus of Module 3 is on **ARM7 / Cortex-M3 instruction set architecture**. Control flow instructions form the backbone of every embedded C/assembly program. Mastering the **condition codes (COND field in bits [31:28])** and the **branch range** is a guaranteed 7–14 mark item in ESE.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Indian Railway Train Switcher**
>
> Imagine a train (the CPU) running on a single straight track. The train normally goes forward to the next station (the next sequential instruction at PC+4). A **Control Flow Instruction** is like a **track switch (pointsman)** at a junction. The switch has two states:
>
> - **Unconditional Switch (B label):** The track always diverts to a different platform — like a "Diversion" board on Indian Railways. Every train *must* take it.
> - **Conditional Switch (B<cond> label):** The pointsman checks a **signal light** (the APSR condition flags: N, Z, C, V). If the signal is green, the train diverts; if red, it keeps going straight.
> - **Branch-and-Link (BL function):** This is like a "Call Train" — it diverts to another station, but **first records the original platform number in a logbook (LR = R14)** so the train can later return home. This is exactly how nested function calls work in C.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Word Size:** **32 bits (4 bytes)** — the fundamental unit of ARM instruction length in ARM state.
- **Thumb State Instruction Length:** **16 bits** (basic Thumb) or **32 bits** (Thumb-2 wide).
- **Pipeline Stages (Cortex-M3):** **3-stage** (Fetch → Decode → Execute). Branch latency is therefore **1 cycle** for taken branches with a standard branch predictor-free design.
- **Condition Code Field Width:** **4 bits** located in bits **[31:28]** of every ARM instruction.
- **Branch Range (ARM):** **±32 MB** from the current PC.
- **Branch Range (Thumb):** **±2 KB** for conditional, **±4 MB** for unconditional.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Control Flow Instructions

The KTU syllabus mandates understanding across the following hierarchy:

### 2.1.1 Branch Instructions (B and B<cond>)

- **Encoding Format (ARM state):**

  $$ \text{cond}\,[31:28] \;\; 1010 \;\; \text{offset}_{24} \;\; \text{offset}_{23:0} $$

- The lower **24 bits** form a **signed two's-complement word offset**.
- The target address is computed relative to the **aligned PC + 8** (because the ARM pipeline has already prefetched two instructions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "PC + 8" and not "PC + 4"?** When the branch instruction is being *executed*, the CPU has already fetched the next two instructions (PC+4 and PC+8). Branch offset is therefore relative to **PC + 8** to keep the target address calculation consistent with what is physically in the pipeline.

### 2.1.2 Condition Code Flags (APSR — Application Program Status Register)

The four standard condition flags are evaluated by the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** in ARM7 or the **Application Program Status Register (APSR)** in Cortex-M3:

| Flag | Bit Position | Meaning | Set When |
|------|--------------|---------|----------|
| **N** | 31 | Negative | Result bit 31 = 1 |
| **Z** | 30 | Zero | Result = 0 |
| **C** | 29 | Carry | Unsigned overflow / no borrow |
| **V** | 28 | oVerflow | Signed overflow |

### 2.1.3 Standard Condition Codes (KTU High-Yield)

| Mnemonic | Code [31:28] | Flag Test | Meaning |
|----------|--------------|-----------|---------|
| **EQ** | 0000 | Z = 1 | Equal |
| **NE** | 0001 | Z = 0 | Not equal |
| **CS / HS** | 0010 | C = 1 | Carry set / unsigned ≥ |
| **CC / LO** | 0011 | C = 0 | Carry clear / unsigned < |
| **MI** | 0100 | N = 1 | Minus |
| **PL** | 0101 | N = 0 | Plus |
| **VS** | 0110 | V = 1 | Overflow set |
| **VC** | 0111 | V = 0 | Overflow clear |
| **HI** | 1000 | C=1 AND Z=0 | Unsigned > |
| **LS** | 1001 | C=0 OR Z=1 | Unsigned ≤ |
| **GE** | 1010 | N=V | Signed ≥ |
| **LT** | 1011 | N≠V | Signed < |
| **GT** | 1100 | Z=0 AND N=V | Signed > |
| **LE** | 1101 | Z=1 OR N≠V | Signed ≤ |
| **AL** | 1110 | Always | Unconditional |

### 2.1.4 Branch with Link (BL) and Subroutine Linkage

- **BL** performs: `LR = PC - 4` (return address) **then** branches.
- **Return** is performed by re-loading the PC: e.g., `MOV PC, LR` (ARM7) or `BX LR` / `POP {PC}` (AAPCS for Cortex-M).
- **Leaf functions** (no nested calls) need not save LR; **non-leaf functions** must push LR onto the stack using `PUSH {LR}`.

### 2.1.5 If-Then (IT) Block — Cortex-M3 / Thumb-2 Specific

- The `IT` instruction provides **up to 4 conditional instructions** following a controlling condition.
- Format: `IT{x{y{z}}} <cond>`, where `x, y, z` are `T` (Then) or `E` (Else).
- Example: `ITTE NE` → next 3 instructions execute as: Then-NE, Then-NE, Else-NE.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — every KTU ESE from 2018 onwards has asked at least one of these formulas verbatim.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | ARM branch target | $\text{Target} = (\text{PC} + 8) + (\text{SignedOffset} \times 4)$ | bytes | Offset is 24-bit signed |
| 2 | ARM branch range | $-2^{25} \leq \text{Offset}_{bytes} \leq +2^{25}-4$ | bytes | i.e., ±32 MB |
| 3 | Thumb branch range (B) | $\text{Offset}_{bytes} \in [-2048, +2046]$ | bytes | 11-bit signed, ±2 KB |
| 4 | Thumb unconditional (B.W) | $\text{Offset}_{bytes} \in [\pm 16\text{ MB}]$ | bytes | 25-bit signed |
| 5 | Link register update (BL) | $\text{LR} = \text{PC} - 4$ | bytes | Return address in ARM7 |
| 6 | Stack decrement (PUSH) | $\text{SP} = \text{SP} - 4n$ | bytes | $n$ = number of registers |
| 7 | Condition code position | bits $\vert 31:28 \vert$ | bits | Top 4 bits of every ARM instruction |
| 8 | APSR flag test (HI) | $\text{HI} \iff C=1 \;\land\; Z=0$ | boolean | Unsigned greater than |
| 9 | APSR flag test (GE) | $\text{GE} \iff N=V$ | boolean | Signed ≥ |
| 10 | IT block max length | $1 + 4 = 5$ | instructions | 1 IT + up to 4 conditional |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Control flow instructions are the literal mechanism that allows an **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** in a Bosch ECU to skip the brake-release routine when wheel slip is below 5% — the entire decision tree of a Tesla Autopilot ECU is built on billions of `B.LT` style branches per second. In an IoT sensor node, the **Cortex-M0+** spends ~60% of its cycles in branch instructions within the main sensing loop. Mis-aligned or mis-computed branches are the **#1 cause of "hard fault" crashes** in embedded firmware.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Branch Target Address Calculation (ARM State)

**Problem:** An ARM branch instruction `B label` is located at address `0x00008000`. The 24-bit offset field in the instruction is `0x00FFFC2` (which represents $-60$ in two's complement). Compute the target address.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Let:
- $\text{PC}_{current} = 0x00008000$
- $\text{Offset}_{24bit} = 0x00FFFC2$ (hex)
- Word size = 4 bytes

**Step 1:** Convert the 24-bit field to a signed integer. The MSB (bit 23) is 0, so it is positive as raw bits, but we must check the sign extension to 32 bits first.

$$
\text{Offset}_{signed} = 0 \times 00\text{FFFC2}_{16} = 1{,}048{,}578_{10}
$$

Wait — this is positive. KTU examiners often use a negative offset. Let's re-express a typical *negative* example.

> **Re-formulated Problem:** Offset field = `0xFFFFA2` (hex, 24-bit). Find target.

**Step 1 — Sign extend the 24-bit offset to 32 bits:**

$$
\text{Offset}_{32bit} = 0 \times \text{FFFFA2} = -94_{10} \quad \text{(two's complement)}
$$

**Step 2 — Shift left by 2 to convert words → bytes (because branch offset is in words):**

$$
\text{Offset}_{bytes} = -94 \times 4 = -376_{10}
$$

**Step 3 — Add PC + 8 (ARM pipeline adjustment):**

$$
\text{PC} + 8 = 0x00008000 + 0x8 = 0x00008008
$$

**Step 4 — Final target address:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Target} &= (\text{PC} + 8) + \text{Offset}_{bytes} \\
&= 0x00008008 + (-376) \\
&= 0x00008008 - 0x178 \\
&= 0x00007\text{E90}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verification:** Target `0x00007E90` is 0x170 = 368 bytes before `0x00008000`, consistent with a backward branch of 376 bytes minus the pipeline offset of 8.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Forward Branch Range

The offset field is **24 bits** and is sign-extended. The signed range in *words* is:

$$
\text{Offset}_{words} \in [-2^{23},\; +2^{23} - 1]
$$

Converting to bytes (multiply by 4):

$$
\text{Offset}_{bytes} \in [-2^{25},\; +2^{25} - 4]
$$

$$
\text{Offset}_{bytes} \in [-33{,}554{,}432,\; +33{,}554{,}428]
$$

This gives the classic **±32 MB** range.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Condition Code GE (Signed ≥)

We need to prove that **`GE`** condition is true **iff** the result of a comparison `CMP a, b` yields $a \geq b$ for **signed** operands.

**Given:** After `SUBS a, b` (which is `a - b`):
- $N = $ bit 31 of the result
- $V = 1$ if signed overflow occurred

**Step 1:** Two signed numbers are "greater-or-equal" iff their difference is non-negative *and* the difference is correctly signed (no overflow). A non-negative signed result has $N = 0$, and absence of overflow means $V = 0$. So a *correctly-computed* non-negative result gives $N = V = 0$.

**Step 2:** A *negative* correctly-computed result (i.e., $a < b$) gives $N = 1$ and $V = 0$, so $N = V$ still.

**Step 3:** If overflow occurs AND result bit $N=1$, the *true* signed answer should have been positive (e.g., $-2^{31} - 1$ overflows). So in this pathological case $N=1, V=1$, still $N=V$.

**Step 4:** Overflow with $N=0$ means a negative result was overflowed to positive. So $N=0, V=1$, $N \neq V$, which is correctly "Less Than".

**Conclusion:**

$$
\text{GE} \iff N = V \quad \blacksquare
$$

This is why the KTU examiner loves the GE/LT pairing — the answer follows directly from this proof.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Branch

| Scenario | Best Instruction | Why |
|----------|------------------|-----|
| Simple `if (x == 0)` in tight loop | `CBZ R0, label` (Thumb-2) | No flag dependency, 1 cycle |
| `while (cnt != 0)` loop | `CBNZ R0, loop` | Saves CMP instruction |
| `if (a > b) { ... }` (signed) | `CMP a, b` + `BGT label` | Uses GE/LE flag pair |
| Subroutine call | `BL function` | Saves return in LR |
| Return from subroutine | `BX LR` (or `POP {PC}`) | AAPCS-compliant |
| Tiny delay loop (≥ 4 instructions) | `SUBS R0, R0, #1; BNE loop` | Classic ARM7 idiom |
| Multilevel condition cascade | `ITTT EQ` + 3 instructions | Avoid branch stalls |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARM Branch Instruction Encoding (32-bit)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bits 31-28: COND] --> B[Bit 27: 1]
    B --> C[Bit 26: 0]
    C --> D[Bit 25: 1]
    D --> E[Bit 24: 0]
    E --> F[Bits 23-0: Signed Word Offset]
    F --> G[Target = PC + 8 + Offset x 4]
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#166534,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:** The literal pattern `cond 1010` is the magic constant that uniquely identifies a *branch* instruction in ARM state. This is the **opcode signature** KTU examiners test in "identify the instruction" questions.

## 4.2 Subroutine Call and Return Flow (BL / BX LR)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Caller: main function] --> B[Execute BL subroutine]
    B --> C[LR = PC - 4 saved]
    C --> D[PC jumps to subroutine entry]
    D --> E[Subroutine: PUSH reglist, LR onto stack]
    E --> F[Subroutine body executes]
    F --> G[Subroutine: POP reglist, PC]
    G --> H[PC loaded from stack - original LR]
    H --> I[Caller resumes after BL line]
    style A fill:#1e40af,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#9a3412,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#166534,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#166534,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#1e40af,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PUSH LR onto the stack?** If the subroutine itself calls another function (`BL` inside the subroutine), LR gets *overwritten*. The traditional ARM7 idiom `STMFD SP!, {reglist, LR}` preserves the return address safely. In Cortex-M, this is largely automatic via the **Link Register (LR)** hardware handling and **Tail-chaining** in the NVIC, but the software model remains the same.

## 4.3 Conditional Execution Decision Tree (B<cond>)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Execute B cond Label] --> Eval[Evaluate APSR Flags]
    Eval --> CondMet{Condition TRUE?}
    CondMet -->|YES| Take[PC = PC + 8 + Offset x 4]
    CondMet -->|NO| Skip[PC = PC + 4 continue sequential]
    Take --> FetchNext[Fetch from new address]
    Skip --> FetchNext
    style Start fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style Eval fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style CondMet fill:#9a3412,color:#ffffff
    style Take fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
    style Skip fill:#374151,color:#ffffff
    style FetchNext fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 IT Block Construction (Thumb-2)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IT EQ] --> B[Instruction 1: execute if EQ]
    A --> C[Instruction 2: T or E]
    A --> D[Instruction 3: T or E]
    A --> E[Instruction 4: T or E]
    B --> F[APSR Z flag test]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    style A fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
```

**Example ITTE EQ:**
- Inst 1: Then-EQ (executes only if Z=1)
- Inst 2: Then-EQ (executes only if Z=1)
- Inst 3: Else (executes only if Z=0)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the format of an ARM branch instruction. How is the target address calculated?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An ARM branch instruction is a **32-bit** instruction with the format:
- **Bits [31:28]**: Condition code (COND)
- **Bits [27:24]**: Fixed pattern `1010` (identifies it as a branch opcode)
- **Bits [23:0]**: Signed 24-bit word offset

The **target address** is calculated as:

$$
\text{Target Address} = (\text{PC} + 8) + (\text{Offset} \times 4)
$$

The `+8` accounts for the **ARM pipeline prefetch** (two instructions ahead). The 24-bit signed offset is shifted left by 2 (i.e., multiplied by 4) to convert from word-count to byte-count, giving a branch range of **±32 MB**.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any six condition codes used in ARM branch instructions and state the flag condition tested by each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Mnemonic | Flag Test |
|---|----------|-----------|
| 1 | EQ | Z = 1 (Equal) |
| 2 | NE | Z = 0 (Not Equal) |
| 3 | GT | Z = 0 AND N = V (Signed >) |
| 4 | LE | Z = 1 OR N ≠ V (Signed ≤) |
| 5 | HI | C = 1 AND Z = 0 (Unsigned >) |
| 6 | LT | N ≠ V (Signed <) |

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting the "+8" PC pipeline correction** in branch target calculations.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply the word offset by 4** to convert to byte offset.
> 3. **Confusing signed vs unsigned** condition codes (e.g., using `HI` for signed comparison).
> 4. **Not showing the sign extension** of the 24-bit offset to 32 bits — examiners expect this step explicitly.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **execution flow of the Branch-and-Link (BL)** instruction. Discuss how a non-leaf subroutine must preserve the **Link Register (LR)** and the **role of the stack** in nested function calls. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ARM branch instruction at address `0x00009000` has a 24-bit offset field of `0xFFFA00` (hex). Calculate the **target address** of the branch. State the **maximum forward and backward range** of an ARM branch instruction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

The Branch-and-Link instruction performs two operations in one cycle:

1. **Save the return address** into the Link Register:
   $$\text{LR} = \text{PC} - 4$$
   (In ARM7; in Cortex-M with Thumb, LR = address of *next* instruction)

2. **Branch to the target** using the same formula as a regular B.

**Execution Flow Diagram:**

```
CALLER                  SUBROUTINE
  PC = 0x1000            (entry at 0x2000)
  BL SUB
   → LR = 0x1004
   → PC = 0x2000
  (waiting)              PUSH {R4-R11, LR}
                        ... (body) ...
                        POP {R4-R11, PC}
  PC resumes at 0x1004
```

**Why LR must be pushed onto the stack:**

If the subroutine itself calls another function (nested call), the inner `BL` will **overwrite LR** with its own return address. To prevent losing the outer return address, the standard ARM AAPCS prologue is:

```
SUB  SP, SP, #N         ; allocate stack frame
STMFD SP!, {reglist, LR} ; save LR (and callee-saved regs)
```

The corresponding epilogue uses:

```
LDMFD SP!, {reglist, PC} ; restore and return in one instruction
```

Loading `PC` (not `LR`) from the stack simultaneously returns control and discards the saved LR copy. This is the **standard ARM subroutine linkage** worth full marks.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Given:**
- $\text{PC} = 0x00009000$
- $\text{Offset}_{24} = 0xFFFA00_{16}$

**Step 1 — Sign-extend the 24-bit offset to 32 bits:**

The MSB (bit 23) of `0xFFFA00` is `1`, so the value is negative.

$$
\text{Offset}_{32bit} = 0xFFFFFA00 = -1536_{10}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert words to bytes (multiply by 4):**

$$
\text{Offset}_{bytes} = -1536 \times 4 = -6144_{10}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute PC + 8 (pipeline correction):**

$$
\text{PC} + 8 = 0x00009000 + 0x8 = 0x00009008
$$

**Step 4 — Final target address:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Target} &= 0x00009008 + (-6144) \\
&= 0x00009008 - 0x1800 \\
&= 0x00007808
\end{aligned}
$$

**Maximum branch range (2 marks):**

- **Forward:** $+2^{25} - 4 = +33{,}554{,}428$ bytes ≈ **+32 MB**
- **Backward:** $-2^{25} = -33{,}554{,}432$ bytes ≈ **−32 MB**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **APSR (Application Program Status Register) condition flags** N, Z, C, V. How are they used to implement the **signed greater-than (BGT)** and **signed less-than-or-equal (BLE)** conditional branches? Derive the flag-test condition for BGT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write short notes on: (i) **If-Then (IT) instruction** in Cortex-M3, (ii) **CBZ and CBNZ** instructions in Thumb-2. Compare the **range** of an ARM-state branch vs a Thumb-state conditional branch. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**APSR Condition Flags (3 marks):**

| Flag | Full Name | Set When |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **N** | Negative | MSB of result = 1 (i.e., result is negative in signed interpretation) |
| **Z** | Zero | Result equals 0 |
| **C** | Carry | Unsigned overflow occurred; or for subtraction, **no borrow** |
| **V** | oVerflow | Signed overflow occurred (result cannot be represented in N-bit two's complement) |

**Derivation of BGT condition (4 marks):**

The `BGT` instruction executes the branch iff $a > b$ after `CMP a, b` (which internally computes $a - b$).

For signed greater-than, the result must be **non-zero AND non-negative**:
- Non-zero: $Z = 0$
- Non-negative (with no overflow): $N = 0$ AND $V = 0$, but if overflow occurs *and* result is positive-signed-true, then $N = 1$ and $V = 1$, so still $N = V$.

Therefore:

$$
\text{BGT} \iff Z = 0 \;\land\; N = V
$$

**Symmetrically:**

$$
\text{BLE} \iff Z = 1 \;\lor\; N \neq V
$$

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**(i) If-Then (IT) instruction (2.5 marks):**

The IT instruction provides **conditional execution** of up to 4 following instructions, eliminating the need for short forward branches. Syntax: `IT{x{y{z}}} <cond>` where each `x, y, z ∈ {T, E}`.

Example: `ITTE NE` →
- Inst 1: Then-NE
- Inst 2: Then-NE
- Inst 3: Else (executes only if NOT EQual)

The IT block **must lie entirely within an 8-byte aligned region**; otherwise a fault is generated.

**(ii) CBZ and CBNZ (2.5 marks):**

- `CBZ Rn, label` — Compare and Branch if **Z = 1** (i.e., Rn is zero). Does **not** affect APSR flags.
- `CBNZ Rn, label` — Compare and Branch if **Z = 0** (i.e., Rn is non-zero).

Encoding: Thumb-2 only, 16-bit instruction, 5-bit operand register, **6-bit forward-only offset** in the range `[0, 126]` bytes.

**Range comparison table (2 marks):**

| State | Range (bytes) |
|-------|---------------|
| ARM (B) | ±32 MB |
| Thumb conditional (B<cond>) | ±2 KB |
| Thumb unconditional (B.W) | ±16 MB |
| CBZ/CBNZ | 0 to +126 (forward only) |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 3 Control Flow**

- [ ] **ARM branch target = (PC + 8) + (Offset × 4)** — the "magic +8" is the most-tested formula.
- [ ] **24-bit signed offset → ±32 MB range** in ARM state; **±2 KB** in Thumb conditional.
- [ ] **Opcode pattern `cond 1010`** identifies a branch; memorise this for "identify the instruction" questions.
- [ ] **Condition codes are bits [31:28]** — 4 bits, 16 combinations, with AL=1110 being unconditional.
- [ ] **BL** = B + save LR; **BX LR** returns; **PUSH/POP {…, LR/PC}** preserves LR across nested calls.
- [ ] **BGT** flag test: $Z = 0 \land N = V$; **BLE** flag test: $Z = 1 \lor N \neq V$.
- [ ] **HI/LS** are for **unsigned**; **GT/LT/GE/LE** are for **signed** comparisons.
- [ ] **IT block** in Cortex-M3 can enclose up to 4 instructions; must be in same 8-byte aligned region.
- [ ] **CBZ/CBNZ** do NOT modify APSR flags — useful for saving a CMP in tight loops.
- [ ] **Leaf functions** don't need to push LR; **non-leaf functions** must use AAPCS prologue/epilogue.
- [ ] **Pipeline flush** on taken branch = 1-cycle penalty (ARM7); 0-cycle for CBZ/CBNZ in Cortex-M3.
- [ ] **Sign extension** of the 24-bit offset to 32 bits is the most-skipped step — show it explicitly in derivations.
- [ ] **Thumb-2 IT blocks** can implement `if-else` without branches, saving pipeline cycles.
- [ ] **NVIC tail-chaining** (Cortex-M3) makes ISR entry/exit latency near zero — related to BL/BX mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Writing Simple Assembly Language Programs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Writing Simple Assembly Language Programs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBEVT504 – Embedded System Design, Module 3)**, *Writing Simple Assembly Language Programs* refers to the structured process of authoring low-level mnemonic instructions that are directly translated (assembled) into the native machine code executed by the target processor (typically an **ARM Cortex-M** or legacy **LPC2148/8051** core in the KTU laboratory syllabus). An Assembly Language Program (ALP) is composed of **mnemonics, labels, directives, operands, and comments**, arranged across multiple sections such as data, stack, and code regions defined by assembler directives like `AREA`, `ENTRY`, and `END`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students are expected to write, simulate, and debug simple ALPs covering arithmetic, logical, data-transfer, looping, branching, and sub-routine operations for an ARM-based target. Questions typically appear for **7 to 14 marks** in End Semester Examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card" of the Microcontroller

Imagine you are a chef in a high-precision kitchen where the head chef (the **CPU**) understands **only one language — binary (0s and 1s)**. You cannot whisper "add sugar" in English. Instead, you must hand the chef a strictly numbered **recipe card**, where every line says exactly:

> "Line 1: Pick up ingredient from bowl **R1**."
> "Line 2: Pick up ingredient from bowl **R2**."
> "Line 3: Use the *ADD* tool to combine them, store in bowl **R0**."

That recipe card is your **Assembly Language Program**. Every **mnemonic** is a single, atomic kitchen action. Every **operand** (R0, R1, R2) is a labeled bowl. The **assembler** is the translator who converts your readable recipe card into the chef's binary language.

The key insight: **One assembly instruction almost always maps to exactly one machine instruction** — there is no hidden interpretation, no compiler optimization magic. What you write is what the hardware executes. This is the reason embedded engineers use assembly for **time-critical ISR routines, bootloaders, and register-level hardware initialization**.

## 1.3 Why Assembly in an Embedded Course?

| Reason | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|
| **Deterministic Timing** | Every cycle is known — required for hard real-time systems. |
| **Direct Hardware Access** | Special Function Registers are accessed in a single instruction. |
| **Compact Code** | Critical for memory-constrained MCUs (e.g., 8051 with 4 KB ROM). |
| **Reverse Engineering / Security** | Firmware analysis, exploit research, bootloader inspection. |
| **Compiler-Generated Insights** | Helps engineers read the disassembly window during debugging. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Reference Architecture:** For the PBEVT504 laboratory and theory modules, KTU 2024 Scheme primarily assumes the **ARM7-TDMI (LPC2148)** core for assembly programming examples, with the **Keil µVision IDE** as the standard assembler/emulator toolchain. Instruction mnemonics used here follow the **ARM Unified Assembly Language (UAL)** standard.

## 1.4 The Three Building Blocks of Every ALP

An assembly program, no matter how complex, is constructed from only three logical categories of lines:

1. **Assembler Directives** — instructions *to the assembler* (not the CPU). Examples: `AREA`, `ENTRY`, `END`, `EQU`, `DCD`, `DCB`, `SPACE`, `EXPORT`, `IMPORT`.
2. **Executable Instructions (Mnemonics)** — instructions *to the CPU*. Examples: `MOV`, `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `LDR`, `STR`, `CMP`, `B`, `BL`, `BX`.
3. **Labels and Comments** — navigation aids for the programmer. Labels act as symbolic addresses; comments are ignored by the assembler.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Eye:** Most KTU students lose marks in the **"Identify the directive vs. instruction"** question. Memorize: *Directives never generate machine code; instructions always do.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an ARM Assembly Statement

A single ARM assembly line follows a strict grammar:

```
[label]   <whitespace>   <mnemonic/directive>   <whitespace>   <operands>   <;comment>
```

| Field | Mandatory? | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Label | Optional | Symbolic name for an address | `LOOP`, `START`, `NEXT` |
| Mnemonic / Directive | **Mandatory** | The operation to perform | `MOV`, `ADD`, `AREA` |
| Operands | Depends on mnemonic | Source/destination of data | `R0, #5`, `R1, R2` |
| Comment | Optional | Human-readable explanation (begins with `;`) | `; initialize counter` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Whitespace Rule:** A label, if present, **must start in column 1** of the line. The mnemonic must be separated from the label by at least one space or tab. This is a frequent source of assembler errors in KTU lab viva questions.

## 2.2 The ARM Unified Assembly Language (UAL) Mnemonic Set

ARM instructions fall into broad functional families. KTU Module-3 questions almost always draw from the following families:

### 2.2.1 Data Movement Instructions

| Mnemonic | Syntax | Operation | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV` | `MOV Rd, Op2` | `Rd ← Op2` | Initialize registers, load immediates. |
| `MVN` | `MVN Rd, Op2` | `Rd ← ~Op2` (bitwise NOT) | One's complement, useful in masking. |
| `LDR` | `LDR Rd, [Rn]` | `Rd ← Memory[Rn]` | Load a 32-bit word from memory. |
| `STR` | `STR Rd, [Rn]` | `Memory[Rn] ← Rd` | Store a 32-bit word to memory. |
| `LDRB` | `LDRB Rd, [Rn]` | Load a byte (zero-extended). | String / peripheral byte access. |
| `STRB` | `STRB Rd, [Rn]` | Store a byte. | GPIO byte access. |

### 2.2.2 Arithmetic Instructions

| Mnemonic | Syntax | Operation | Flags Affected |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ADD` | `ADD Rd, Rn, Op2` | `Rd ← Rn + Op2` | Updates N, Z, C, V |
| `SUB` | `SUB Rd, Rn, Op2` | `Rd ← Rn − Op2` | Updates N, Z, C, V |
| `RSB` | `RSB Rd, Rn, Op2` | `Rd ← Op2 − Rn` | Updates flags |
| `MUL` | `MUL Rd, Rm, Rs` | `Rd ← Rm × Rs` | Updates N, Z |
| `MLA` | `MLA Rd, Rm, Rs, Rn` | `Rd ← (Rm × Rs) + Rn` | Multiply-accumulate (DSP). |

### 2.2.3 Logical & Comparison Instructions

| Mnemonic | Syntax | Operation |
|---|---|---|
| `AND` | `AND Rd, Rn, Op2` | Bitwise AND — used for masking. |
| `ORR` | `ORR Rd, Rn, Op2` | Bitwise OR — used for setting bits. |
| `EOR` | `EOR Rd, Rn, Op2` | Bitwise XOR — toggle bits. |
| `BIC` | `BIC Rd, Rn, Op2` | Bit clear: `Rd ← Rn AND ~Op2`. |
| `CMP` | `CMP Rn, Op2` | `Rn − Op2`, sets flags, discards result. |
| `TST` | `TST Rn, Op2` | `Rn AND Op2`, sets flags, discards result. |

### 2.2.4 Branch & Branch-with-Link Instructions

| Mnemonic | Syntax | Operation |
|---|---|---|
| `B <label>` | Unconditional branch | `PC ← label` (jump). |
| `BL <label>` | Branch and link | `LR ← return address; PC ← label` (subroutine call). |
| `BX Rm` | Branch exchange | `PC ← Rm`, switches to Thumb if bit 0 = 1. |
| `BEQ / BNE / BGT / BLT / BGE / BLE` | Conditional branches | Jump only if the APSR flag matches. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Flag Registers in ARM:** The `CPSR` (Current Program Status Register) holds four condition flags — **N (Negative), Z (Zero), C (Carry), V (Overflow)**. These are the *only* mechanism by which the CPU "remembers" the result of a previous comparison. Every conditional branch in ARM tests one or more of these flags. KTU students often confuse the condition codes with the flags themselves.

## 2.3 Essential Assembler Directives

| Directive | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `AREA <name>, <attr>, <attr>` | Begins a code or data section. | `AREA MYCODE, CODE, READONLY` |
| `ENTRY` | Declares the program entry point. | `ENTRY` |
| `END` | Marks the end of source file. | `END` |
| `EQU <name> <value>` | Defines a symbolic constant. | `N EQU 10` |
| `DCD <value>` | Define Constant Data (32-bit word). | `DATA DCD 0x12345678` |
| `DCB <value>` | Define Constant Byte (8-bit). | `MSG DCB "Hello", 0` |
| `SPACE <bytes>` | Reserves a block of zero-initialized memory. | `BUF SPACE 100` |
| `EXPORT <label>` | Makes a label visible to the linker / other files. | `EXPORT __main` |
| `IMPORT <label>` | Declares an external symbol to be resolved at link time. | `IMPORT __use_two_region_memory` |
| `ALIGN` | Aligns the current location to a word (or specified) boundary. | `ALIGN` |
| `RN` | Names a register alias. | `TEMP RN R5` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** `DCD` reserves **4 bytes (one word)** by default, not 1 byte. To reserve a byte, use `DCB`. Forgetting this distinction results in an off-by-factor-of-4 memory layout error that silently corrupts the program and is hard to debug in the Keil simulator.

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The "formulas" of assembly are not algebraic — they are the **operational semantics** of each instruction. Below is the consolidated cheat sheet for KTU Module-3 derivations.

| Instruction Family | KTU Operational Equation | Operand Count | Flags Touched |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Move | $\text{Rd}_{new} = \text{Op2}$ | 2 | None (`MOV`/`MVN`) |
| Load / Store | $\text{Mem}[\text{Rn}] \leftrightarrow \text{Rd}$ | 2 | None |
| Add / Sub | $\text{Rd}_{new} = \text{Rn} \pm \text{Op2}$ | 3 | **N, Z, C, V** |
| Multiply | $\text{Rd}_{new} = \text{Rm} \times \text{Rs}$ | 3 | **N, Z** |
| Logical | $\text{Rd}_{new} = \text{Rn} \odot \text{Op2}$ | 3 | **N, Z, C** (for shifts) |
| Compare | $\text{Flags} = f(\text{Rn} - \text{Op2})$ | 2 | **N, Z, C, V** |
| Branch | $\text{PC} = \text{label}$ (if condition true) | 1 | None (tests flags) |
| Branch-Link | $\text{LR} = \text{PC}+4; \; \text{PC} = \text{label}$ | 1 | None |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The `BIC` (Bit Clear) instruction is heavily used in **LPC2148 GPIO programming** to clear specific pins of the IOPIN register without disturbing the others — a classic KTU lab viva question. The `MLA` instruction is the foundation of **DSP FIR filters** in audio codecs. Understanding the flag-update behaviour is essential for writing **condition-based control loops** in industrial embedded systems.

## 2.5 The Assembler Pipeline — How a `.s` File Becomes a `.hex` File

The conceptual flow is:

```
Source (.s)  →  Assembler  →  Object (.o)  →  Linker  →  ELF (.axf)  →  Object Copy  →  HEX (.hex)
```

- The **assembler** translates mnemonics to machine code and resolves local labels.
- The **linker** combines multiple object files and the **scatter file** (or startup `.s`) to assign absolute addresses.
- The **object copy** converts the ELF into Intel HEX for the flash programmer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Note:** In the Keil µVision IDE, the scatter loading is performed by the **LPC2148 Flash.uvproj** template. The startup file `Startup.s` is the first code that runs (it copies initialized data, zeroes the BSS section, then calls `__main` → your `ENTRY` label). Marks are often awarded for correctly placing the `ENTRY` directive and understanding the startup chain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **exhaustive step-by-step construction** of four canonical KTU Module-3 programs. Every register state, every flag transition, and every memory address is explicitly traced.

## 3.1 Program 1 — Addition of Two 32-bit Numbers

**Problem Statement (verbatim KTU style):**
*Write an ARM ALP to add two 32-bit numbers stored in memory and store the 64-bit result back in memory. Use the Keil assembler directives.*

### 3.1.1 Algorithm Derivation

Let the two operands be $A$ and $B$, with the result occupying two words $R_{hi}$ and $R_{lo}$.

$$
\text{Result}_{64} = A_{32} + B_{32}
$$

The lower 32 bits of the result are simply $A + B$. The upper 32 bits are the **carry-out** of that addition. In ARM, the carry flag (`C` in CPSR) records exactly this overflow. Therefore:

$$
R_{lo} = (A + B) \mod 2^{32}
$$

$$
R_{hi} = \left\lfloor \frac{A + B}{2^{32}} \right\rfloor = (C \text{ flag after } A+B) \;?\; 1 : 0
$$

In ARM UAL, the high word is obtained by the instruction `ADC` (Add with Carry), which automatically adds the previous carry:

$$
R_{lo} = A + B
$$

$$
R_{hi} = 0 + C_{prev}
$$

### 3.1.2 Memory Layout

| Address | Label | Content | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| `0x40000000` | `A` | `0x00000005` (5) | DCD (input) |
| `0x40000004` | `B` | `0x0000000A` (10) | DCD (input) |
| `0x40000008` | `RESLO` | `?` | DCD (result low) |
| `0x4000000C` | `RESHI` | `?` | DCD (result high) |

### 3.1.3 Source Program

```armasm
        AREA    ADD64, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main
__main
        LDR     R0, =A          ; R0 ← address of A
        LDR     R1, [R0]        ; R1 ← A (= 5)
        LDR     R2, [R0, #4]    ; R2 ← B (= 10)  ; offset +4 bytes
        ADDS    R3, R1, R2      ; R3 ← R1 + R2 ; sets C flag
        STR     R3, [R0, #8]    ; RESLO ← 15
        ADC     R4, R1, R1      ; R4 ← 0 + C (C=0 here as 5+10<2^32)
        STR     R4, [R0, #12]   ; RESHI ← 0
        B       __main          ; loop forever
        ALIGN
A       DCD     5
B       DCD     10
        AREA    DATA, DATA, READWRITE
RESLO   DCD     0
RESHI   DCD     0
        END
```

### 3.1.4 Register Trace (Step-by-Step)

| Step | Instruction | R0 | R1 | R2 | R3 | R4 | CPSR.C | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `LDR R0, =A` | `0x40000000` | — | — | — | — | — | Load literal-pool address. |
| 2 | `LDR R1, [R0]` | `0x40000000` | `5` | — | — | — | — | Read operand A. |
| 3 | `LDR R2, [R0,#4]` | `0x40000000` | `5` | `10` | — | — | — | Read operand B. |
| 4 | `ADDS R3, R1, R2` | `0x40000000` | `5` | `10` | `15` | — | **0** | `15 = 5 + 10`, no carry. |
| 5 | `STR R3,[R0,#8]` | `0x40000000` | `5` | `10` | `15` | — | 0 | Store low word. |
| 6 | `ADC R4, R1, R1` | `0x40000000` | `5` | `10` | `15` | `0` | 0 | `R4 = 0 + 0 = 0` (carry-in was 0). |
| 7 | `STR R4,[R0,#12]` | `0x40000000` | `5` | `10` | `15` | `0` | 0 | Store high word. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** Use `ADCS` (with the `S` suffix) only when you specifically need to update flags. The bare `ADC` does **not** modify CPSR — it only *reads* the carry flag. This subtle distinction is a common KTU viva trap.

### 3.1.5 Verifying the General 64-bit Case

Suppose $A = 0xFFFFFFFE$ and $B = 0x00000005$. Then:

$$
A + B = 0x1 \ 00000003
$$

The expected result is $R_{hi} = 1$ and $R_{lo} = 3$.

$$
\text{ADDS} \; R3, R1, R2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R3 = 3,\; C = 1
$$

$$
\text{ADC} \; R4, R1, R1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R4 = 0 + 1 = 1
$$

Confirmed: $R_{hi} = 1$, $R_{lo} = 3$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Program 2 — Sum of N Numbers (Array Reduction)

**Problem Statement:**
*Given an array of N 32-bit numbers stored in consecutive memory, write an ARM ALP to compute the sum of all elements and store the result.*

### 3.2.1 Algorithm Derivation

Let the array be $A[0], A[1], \dots, A[N-1]$. The desired sum is:

$$
S = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} A[i]
$$

This is a classic **iterative reduction**. The pseudocode is:

$$
\text{sum} \leftarrow 0; \quad \text{ptr} \leftarrow \&A[0]; \quad \text{count} \leftarrow N
$$

$$
\textbf{while} \; \text{count} > 0 \; \textbf{do}
$$

$$
\quad \text{sum} \leftarrow \text{sum} + \text{Mem}[\text{ptr}]
$$

$$
\quad \text{ptr} \leftarrow \text{ptr} + 4; \quad \text{count} \leftarrow \text{count} - 1
$$

$$
\textbf{end while}
$$

### 3.2.2 Source Program

```armasm
        AREA    ARRSUM, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  __main
__main
        LDR     R0, =ARRAY      ; R0 → base address of ARRAY
        LDR     R1, =N          ; R1 → address holding N
        LDR     R2, [R1]        ; R2 = N (loop counter)
        MOV     R3, #0          ; R3 = SUM (accumulator), init to 0
        MOV     R4, #0          ; R4 = OFFSET, init to 0
LOOP
```
