| 4 | — | **E (r0 = r1+r2 available)** | D | F | — |
| 5 | — | — | **E (uses r0)** | D | F |
| 6 | — | — | — | **E (data available at end)** | D |
| 7 | — | — | — | — | **E (branch resolved, flush 2)** |
| 8 | — | — | — | — | — |

**Hazard Analysis (3 marks):**

- **I1 → I2 (RAW on r0):** I1 writes r0 in cycle 3, I2 reads r0 in cycle 3 (Decode of I2 is cycle 3). This is the same cycle — handled by the **write-first read-after-write protocol** of the register file. **No stall needed** ✓.

- **I3 → I4 (No dependency):** I3 does not write to r0, and I4 uses r7. No hazard ✓.

- **I4 → I5 (BNE checks flags set by previous instruction):** I5 checks flags in Execute. If the previous instruction is not a data-processing instruction that set flags, no stall. Assume the previous flag-setting instruction finished in time. **No stall** ✓.

- **Branch I5:** The branch decision is made in Execute (cycle 7). Two instructions have already been fetched (I6, I7 — not shown). These are **flushed** from the pipeline. **2-cycle branch penalty** (2 marks).

**Total cycles = 7** (assuming no load-use hazard between I4 and I5). If a load-use hazard existed, +1 cycle bubble (1 mark for noting this).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Model Question Paper - KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Explain the **various pipeline hazards** that occur in a 3-stage pipelined ARM processor. Discuss how the ARM handles **data hazards** and **branch hazards** in this pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A pipeline hazard is any condition that prevents the next instruction from executing in the following cycle. Three types exist (1 mark):

1. **Structural Hazards:** Occur when hardware cannot support all combinations of instructions in the pipeline simultaneously. In the 3-stage ARM, the single memory port means that **LDR/STR** operations cannot fetch an instruction in the same cycle. This is resolved by making instruction memory and data memory **separate (Harvard architecture in ARM7)** (1 mark).

2. **Data Hazards:** Occur when an instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction that is not yet available. Three sub-types:
   - **RAW (Read After Write):** Most common. I2 tries to read a register before I1 has written it (1 mark).
   - **WAR (Write After Read):** Not possible in this 3-stage pipeline because reads occur in Decode (earlier) and writes in Execute (later) (1/2 mark).
   - **WAW (Write After Write):** Not possible in this 3-stage pipeline because writes occur in program order (1/2 mark).

   **ARM's solution for RAW hazards:**
   - For **register-to-register** transfers: The register file uses a **write-first protocol** — the value is written in the first half of the cycle and read in the second half. This provides **implicit forwarding** with no penalty (1 mark).
   - For **load-use** (LDR followed by an instruction using the loaded value): A **1-cycle bubble** is inserted because memory data is only available at the end of the Execute cycle (1 mark).

3. **Control Hazards:** Caused by branches and other control-flow changes.
   - In the 3-stage ARM, the branch target is computed in the **Execute** stage (1/2 mark).
   - The PC is only updated at the end of Execute.
   - Therefore, **2 instructions** fetched after the branch must be **flushed** → **2-cycle branch penalty** (1/2 mark).

**(b)** A 3-stage pipelined ARM processor is required to execute a program containing **500 instructions**. Each pipeline stage takes **20 ns**. Compute: **(7 marks)**
   (i) Total execution time of the pipelined processor
   (ii) Total execution time of the equivalent non-pipelined processor
   (iii) Speedup
   (iv) Efficiency
   (v) Throughput in MIPS

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 500$, $k = 3$, $T_{clk} = 20 \text{ ns} = 20 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}$

**(i) Pipelined total time:**

$$
T_{pipe} = (n + 2) \cdot T_{clk} = (500 + 2) \times 20 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{pipe} = 502 \times 20 \text{ ns} = 10040 \text{ ns} = 10.04 \text{ μs}
$$

(1 mark)

**(ii) Non-pipelined total time:**

$$
T_{non-pipe} = n \times k \times T_{clk} = 500 \times 3 \times 20 \text{ ns} = 30000 \text{ ns} = 30 \text{ μs}
$$

(1 mark)

**(iii) Speedup:**

$$
S = \dfrac{3n}{n + 2} = \dfrac{3 \times 500}{500 + 2} = \dfrac{1500}{502} \approx 2.988
$$

(2 marks)

**(iv) Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{2.988}{3} \approx 0.996 = 99.6\%
$$

(1 mark)

**(v) Throughput in MIPS:**

$$
Throughput = \dfrac{n}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{500}{10040 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}}
$$

$$
Throughput = \dfrac{500}{1.004 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 4.98 \times 10^{7} \text{ instr/s} \approx 49.8 \text{ MIPS}
$$

(2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+k-1" term:** Many students compute $T_{pipe}$ as $n \times T_{clk}$ — this is WRONG. Always include the pipeline fill time.
> 2. **Using k=5 instead of k=3:** The 3-stage pipeline is specific to ARM7-class cores. Do NOT use the 5-stage pipeline formula unless explicitly told (ARM9 uses 5 stages).
> 3. **Confusing CPI and total cycles:** CPI = 1 in steady state, but **average CPI** = $(n + k - 1)/n$ for a finite instruction sequence.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units in MIPS problems:** Throughput must be in instructions/second; convert ns/μs carefully.
> 5. **Ignoring hazards in timing diagrams:** If a question has a load-use hazard or a branch, students forget to insert the bubble/flush cycles.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist for last-minute revision before KTU exams.**

- [x] The **3-stage pipeline** consists of **Fetch, Decode, Execute** stages.
- [x] Each stage takes **1 clock cycle** in a balanced pipeline.
- [x] **Two pipeline registers** are used: **F/D** and **D/E**.
- [x] The **register file is read in Decode** (not Execute) — this is what enables implicit forwarding.
- [x] **No separate Write-Back stage** — results are written to the register file in the Execute stage itself.
- [x] **Implicit forwarding** is provided by the write-first, read-after-write protocol of the register file.
- [x] **Load-use hazards** require a **1-cycle bubble** (stall).
- [x] **Branch penalty** = **2 cycles** (because branch target is computed in Execute).
- [x] **Structural hazard** for memory is resolved by **Harvard architecture** (separate I-cache and D-cache).
- [x] Speedup formula: $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$. For 3-stage: $S = \dfrac{3n}{n + 2}$.
- [x] Maximum theoretical speedup = $k = 3$ (as $n \to \infty$).
- [x] Efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{n}{n + k - 1}$.
- [x] Total cycles = $n + k - 1 = n + 2$ (for 3-stage).
- [x] **First instruction latency** = 3 cycles. **Throughput** in steady state = 1 instruction/cycle.
- [x] This pipeline is implemented in **ARM7TDMI** and similar cores (ARM7EJ-S, ARM946E-S).
- [x] The 3-stage design is **low-power** and **predictable** — ideal for embedded and real-time systems.
- [x] Compiler-friendly: **1-cycle data dependency delay** can be statically scheduled.
- [x] In the Execute stage, **one of three** operations occurs: ALU computation, memory address + access, or branch target update.
- [x] PC increment by **4** (since ARM instructions are 32-bit / 4 bytes).
- [x] The **barrel shifter** is part of the Decode stage (it produces the second operand's shifted value).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Five Stage Pipeline ARM Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Five Stage Pipeline ARM Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Five Stage Pipeline ARM Organization** refers to the canonical pipelined micro-architecture implemented in classic ARM processors (notably the **ARM7TDMI** core), where every instruction is decomposed into **five sequential pipeline stages**. This organization allows the processor to overlap the execution of up to **five independent instructions**, achieving an instruction throughput of ideally one instruction per clock cycle (CPI ≈ 1) after the pipeline is filled.

The five canonical stages are:

1. **F — Fetch (IF)**: Instruction is fetched from memory using the Program Counter (PC).
2. **D — Decode (ID)**: Instruction is decoded, and operands are read from the register file.
3. **E — Execute (EX)**: Arithmetic/Logic operation is performed by the ALU; branch targets are computed.
4. **M — Memory (MEM)**: Data memory is accessed for load/store instructions.
5. **W — Writeback (WB)**: The result is written back to the destination register in the register file.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The 5-stage pipeline is a *core examinable topic*. Students must know the function of every stage, the exact register transfers between them, the nature of pipeline hazards, and the speedup formula. This is a high-frequency question in both Part A (3-mark definitions) and Part B (14-mark derivations).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Laundry Assembly Line

Imagine a **laundry shop** with 5 workers, each performing one task:

| Worker | Task | Time |
|--------|------|------|
| 1 | Wash | 30 min |
| 2 | Dry | 30 min |
| 3 | Fold | 30 min |
| 4 | Iron | 30 min |
| 5 | Pack & Deliver | 30 min |

- **Non-Pipelined (sequential)**: 1 shirt takes 5 × 30 = **150 minutes**. 5 shirts take 5 × 150 = **750 minutes**.
- **Pipelined**: Once the line is full, every 30 minutes a *new* shirt finishes. The first shirt still takes 150 minutes, but subsequent shirts emerge every 30 minutes. 5 shirts take only **150 + 4 × 30 = 270 minutes**.

**Mapping to ARM pipeline**:
- Each "shirt" = one ARM instruction (ADD, SUB, LDR, STR, B, etc.)
- Each "worker" = one pipeline stage (F, D, E, M, W)
- Each "30 min slot" = one **clock cycle**
- Throughput = **1 instruction per cycle** instead of 1 instruction per 5 cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway**: Pipelining does **not** make a single instruction faster — it increases **system throughput** by exploiting instruction-level parallelism. The **latency** of one instruction remains 5 cycles; the **throughput** becomes 1 instruction/cycle.

## 1.3 Why ARM Specifically Uses 5 Stages

The ARM7TDMI was designed in the early 1990s when:
- Clock frequencies were low (tens of MHz).
- A shorter pipeline (5 stages) meant **simpler hardware**, **lower power**, and **predictable interrupt latency** — all critical for embedded systems.
- Each stage could be completed in roughly **one clock cycle** because the combinational logic per stage was kept small (e.g., no deep integer multiplication in EX, no out-of-order execution).

This trade-off (more stages = higher clock but more hazards and power) is exactly why modern high-performance ARM cores (Cortex-A series) have moved to **15–20 stage out-of-order pipelines**, while embedded Cortex-M cores (M0/M3/M4) retain shorter pipelines (2–3 stages) for deterministic real-time behavior.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner tip**: When answering "Why 5 stages in ARM7?", always mention the **power-performance trade-off** and the suitability for **embedded/low-power** applications. The 5-stage design balances clock frequency boost with low silicon complexity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Functional Breakdown

### Stage 1 — **F (Fetch)**
- PC value is sent to **instruction memory**.
- PC is incremented by 4 (ARM is **32-bit**, fixed 4-byte aligned instructions) → PC + 4.
- The fetched 32-bit instruction is latched into the **IF/ID pipeline register**.

**Active Hardware:** Instruction Memory, PC + 4 adder.

---

### Stage 2 — **D (Decode / Register Read)**
- Instruction opcode is decoded.
- Source registers (Rn, Rm) and destination register (Rd) are read from the **register file** (which has 2 read ports + 1 write port).
- **Sign-extended immediate** values are generated.
- Branch target address is computed: $BT = PC + (\text{offset} \times 4)$.

**Active Hardware:** Decoder, Register File (dual-read), Sign-extension unit, Branch offset adder.

---

### Stage 3 — **E (Execute / ALU)**
- The **ALU** performs the arithmetic or logic operation.
- For data-processing instructions: `Rd = Rn OP Operand2` is computed.
- For memory instructions: `ALU_Result = Rn + offset` (effective address calculation).
- For branches: condition flags are evaluated, and target address is selected via a 2-to-1 mux.

**Active Hardware:** ALU, Barrel shifter (for Operand2), Condition code evaluator.

---

### Stage 4 — **M (Memory)**
- **Load (LDR)**: Data is read from data memory at the address computed in E.
- **Store (STR)**: Data is written to data memory.
- For non-memory instructions, this stage is a **pass-through (NOP)** — the ALU result simply flows through.

**Active Hardware:** Data Memory, Address and Data buses (read/write enables).

---

### Stage 5 — **W (Writeback)**
- Result (either from ALU or from memory) is written back to Rd in the register file.
- The destination register is decoded in D and forwarded; the value is written in W.

**Active Hardware:** Register File write port.

---

## 2.2 Pipeline Registers (Inter-Stage Buffers)

Between every pair of adjacent stages, a **pipeline register** latches the intermediate result and control signals. There are **4 sets** of pipeline registers:

| Register | Contents Held |
|----------|---------------|
| **IF/ID** | Fetched instruction, PC+4 |
| **ID/EXE** | Decoded control signals, Rn, Rm, Rd, Operand2, sign-extended immediate, PC+4 |
| **EXE/MEM** | ALU result, Rd, memory read/write control, branch flags |
| **MEM/WB** | Data read from memory (for LDR) or ALU result (for ALU ops), Rd |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These pipeline registers are **the heart of pipelining**. They isolate each stage so that stage N+1 can begin working on the next instruction while stage N is still working on the current one. **No two stages ever share a combinational path in the same cycle** — this is the formal definition of a synchronous pipeline.

## 2.3 Pipeline Hazards

When the next instruction cannot execute in the following clock cycle, a **hazard** occurs. Three classes:

### A. **Structural Hazards**
- Occur when **two stages need the same hardware resource** in the same cycle.
- Example: Single memory port cannot serve both Fetch and Memory access simultaneously.
- **ARM7 Solution**: Uses **separate instruction and data memories (Harvard architecture)** — Fetch uses I-memory, Memory stage uses D-memory. **No structural hazard** under normal conditions.

### B. **Data Hazards**
- Occur when an instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction still in the pipeline.
- Example:
  ```
  ADD  R1, R2, R3     ; R1 = R2 + R3   (result in W)
  SUB  R4, R1, R5     ; needs R1 in D, but R1 is being written in W
  ```
- **ARM7 Solution**: **Register Bypass / Forwarding** — the result computed in EX or MEM is forwarded directly to the input of the ALU in EX of the dependent instruction, eliminating the need to wait for the W stage.
- For the very common "producer-consumer" 1-instruction-apart case, **forwarding from EX/MEM register resolves it**.

### C. **Control Hazards (Branch Hazards)**
- Caused by branch instructions. The branch outcome is known only at the end of the **EX stage**, but by then **2 instructions have already been fetched** (and 1 decoded).
- The **branch penalty** is **2 cycles** for ARM7 (2 instructions must be discarded from the pipeline).
- **ARM7 mitigations**:
  - **Branch prediction (static)**: Default predict *not taken* (or in some implementations, *predict taken* with branch folding).
  - **Branch folding**: A conditional branch that is not taken is *removed* from the instruction stream by the decoder and replaced by a NOP — saving cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-cycle vs Two-cycle branch penalty**: In the classic textbook ARM7, the branch is resolved in EX, so the next two fetched instructions (one in D, one in F) must be flushed. Hence, **branch penalty = 2 cycles**. Some references state 3 cycles if a delayed branch is used; ARM7TDMI does *not* use delayed branches by default.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Ideal Speedup (k-stage pipeline) | $S_{ideal} = k$ | Theoretical maximum for k = 5 → 5× |
| Actual Speedup | $S = \dfrac{T_{non-pipe}}{T_{pipe}}$ | Compare wall-clock execution time |
| Non-pipelined time per instruction | $T_{np} = 5 \cdot t_{clk}$ | Each stage = 1 cycle |
| Pipelined throughput | $\theta = \dfrac{1}{t_{clk}}$ inst/cycle | After pipeline fill |
| Pipelined total time (n instructions) | $T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot t_{clk}$ | k = stages, n = instructions |
| Speedup formula | $S = \dfrac{5n}{5 + n - 1} = \dfrac{5n}{n + 4}$ | For 5-stage pipeline |
| CPI (ideal) | $CPI_{ideal} = 1$ | One instruction per cycle |
| Actual CPI | $CPI = 1 + \text{stall cycles per instruction}$ | Includes hazards |
| Branch Penalty (ARM7) | $BP = 2 \text{ cycles}$ | For taken branch |
| Forwarding paths | EX→EX, MEM→EX | Eliminates most data hazards |
| Pipeline Register Count | $k - 1 = 4$ | Between 5 stages |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe `|` avoidance**: Absolute value bars are written as $\vert x \vert$, never with raw `|` inside table cells, to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 5-stage pipeline concept is **not just academic** — it underpins:
- **CPU performance analysis** in benchmark tools (Dhrystone, CoreMark on ARM Cortex-M cores).
- **Worst-case execution time (WCET)** estimation in **real-time embedded systems** (AUTOSAR, RTOS scheduling).
- **Compiler design**: Compilers schedule instructions to *avoid hazards* (instruction scheduling / software pipelining).
- **Hardware verification**: Pipeline diagrams are used in formal verification and debug.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speedup of an n-Instruction Sequence on a 5-Stage Pipeline

We derive the closed-form speedup for executing **n instructions** on a 5-stage pipeline versus a non-pipelined datapath.

### Step 1: Non-pipelined execution time

In a non-pipelined (single-cycle or multi-cycle, sequential) datapath, each instruction must traverse **all 5 stages before the next instruction starts**. If each stage consumes 1 clock cycle:

$$T_{non-pipe} = n \times 5 \times t_{clk}$$

**Reasoning**: Every instruction takes 5 cycles; for n instructions, total cycles = 5n; multiplied by clock period $t_{clk}$ gives wall-clock time.

---

### Step 2: Pipelined execution time

In a pipelined datapath:
- The **first instruction** takes 5 cycles to complete (it must traverse all 5 stages).
- Every **subsequent instruction** emerges from the pipeline exactly **1 cycle later** (steady state, CPI = 1).
- Therefore, for n instructions:

$$T_{pipe} = \underbrace{5 \cdot t_{clk}}_{\text{first inst.}} + \underbrace{(n - 1) \cdot t_{clk}}_{\text{remaining } n-1 \text{ inst.}} = (n + 4) \cdot t_{clk}$$

**Reasoning**: Pipeline latency = k cycles; pipeline fill = (n-1) additional cycles; total = k + n - 1 cycles.

---

### Step 3: Speedup ratio

$$S = \frac{T_{non-pipe}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{5n \cdot t_{clk}}{(n + 4) \cdot t_{clk}}$$

Cancelling $t_{clk}$:

$$S = \frac{5n}{n + 4}$$

**Result**: The 5-stage pipeline gives a speedup of $\dfrac{5n}{n+4}$ over a non-pipelined datapath.

---

### Step 4: Asymptotic behavior

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{5n}{n + 4} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{5}{1 + 4/n} = 5$$

**Interpretation**: For a very large number of instructions, the pipeline approaches its **ideal 5× speedup**. For small n, the speedup is much less because of the "pipeline fill" overhead.

| n (instructions) | Speedup S = 5n/(n+4) | % of ideal (5×) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5/5 = 1.00 | 20% |
| 5 | 25/9 ≈ 2.78 | 55.6% |
| 10 | 50/14 ≈ 3.57 | 71.4% |
| 50 | 250/54 ≈ 4.63 | 92.6% |
| 100 | 500/104 ≈ 4.81 | 96.2% |
| 1000 | 5000/1004 ≈ 4.98 | 99.6% |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight**: For typical embedded program sizes (thousands of instructions), the 5-stage pipeline delivers > **99% of ideal speedup**. The pipeline fill overhead is amortized and negligible.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective CPI with Branch Stalls

Consider a program with:
- Fraction $f_b$ of instructions that are **branches** (e.g., 0.20 means 20% branches).
- Each taken branch incurs a **penalty of 2 cycles** (ARM7).
- The remaining instructions execute with **CPI = 1** (ideal, with forwarding eliminating data hazard stalls).

### Step 1: Baseline CPI without hazards

$$CPI_{ideal} = 1$$

### Step 2: Add branch penalty contribution

The average number of stall cycles per instruction is:

$$\text{Stall}_{avg} = f_b \times BP = f_b \times 2$$

### Step 3: Effective CPI

$$CPI_{eff} = CPI_{ideal} + f_b \times BP$$

$$CPI_{eff} = 1 + 2 f_b$$

**Numerical example**: If $f_b = 0.20$ (20% branches):

$$CPI_{eff} = 1 + 2(0.20) = 1 + 0.40 = 1.40$$

### Step 4: Performance degradation

Compared to an ideal CPI of 1.0:

$$\text{Slowdown} = \frac{CPI_{eff}}{CPI_{ideal}} = 1.40$$

The processor is **40% slower** than the ideal 5-stage pipeline due to branch hazards. This is why branch prediction is so important.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Forwarding Path Analysis (EX/MEM → EX)

We derive why **EX/MEM forwarding** resolves the most common data hazard (1-instruction-apart producer-consumer).

### Setup: Two consecutive instructions

```
I1: ADD  R1, R2, R3     ; I1: R1 ← R2 + R3
I2: SUB  R4, R1, R5     ; I2: needs R1
```

### Cycle-by-cycle tracking

| Cycle | I1 stage | I2 stage |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | F | — |
| 2 | D | F |
| 3 | **E** (computes R1) | D |
| 4 | M | **E** (needs R1!) |
| 5 | W | M |
| 6 | — | W |

### Problem
In **cycle 4**, I2 is in EX and needs R1, but I1 is still in M. The register file write hasn't happened yet (it happens in cycle 5).

### Solution: EX/MEM forwarding
At the end of I1's EX stage (cycle 3), the value of R1 is latched in the **EX/MEM pipeline register**. In cycle 4, this latched value is **forwarded directly to the ALU input** of I2 in EX — bypassing the register file entirely.

**Formal expression**:

$$R1_{forwarded} = \text{EX/MEM register} \rightarrow \text{ALU Operand1 input of I2}$$

This eliminates the **2-cycle bubble** that would otherwise be needed. With forwarding, the I2 EX stage proceeds without any stall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The MEM/WB forwarding path** is also needed for the case where the producer is a `LDR` (load) and the consumer is 2 instructions later — the data is available only after I1's MEM stage, so it must be forwarded from the MEM/WB register.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table

| Hazard Type | Cause | ARM7 Mitigation | Cycles Lost (per occurrence) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Structural | Resource conflict | Harvard (separate I/D memory) | 0 |
| Data (1-apart, ALU→ALU) | Producer in EX, consumer in EX next | EX/MEM forwarding | 0 |
| Data (1-apart, LDR→ALU) | Producer in MEM, consumer in EX next | MEM/WB forwarding (1-cycle bubble unavoidable) | 1 |
| Data (RAW on same inst.) | Software error | Compiler / programmer responsibility | N/A |
| Control (branch) | PC unknown until EX | Static prediction, branch folding | 2 (taken) / 0 (not taken + folding) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid

    ifm --> ifidreg["IF/ID REGISTER"]

    pc2["BRANCH TARGET"] --> muxpc["MUX"]

    muxpc --> pc1

    alu -.EX/MEM FORWARD.-> alu
    dm -.MEM/WB FORWARD.-> alu
```

**Reading the diagram**: At clock cycle 5, you can see all **5 instructions** are simultaneously active — I1 in W, I2 in M, I3 in E, I4 in D, I5 in F. This is the **filled pipeline** steady state.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: List the five stages of the ARM7 pipeline. State the function of the Execute (EX) stage in one sentence each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five stages of the ARM7 pipeline are:

| Stage | Full Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **F** | Fetch | Fetches the 32-bit instruction from instruction memory using the PC and computes PC + 4. |
| **D** | Decode | Decodes the instruction, reads source operands from the register file, and computes branch target. |
| **E** | Execute | Performs the ALU operation (arithmetic/logic or effective address for memory access) using the barrel-shifted Operand2. |
| **M** | Memory | Accesses data memory for load/store instructions; passes through for ALU instructions. |
| **W** | Writeback | Writes the result (ALU output or memory-read data) into the destination register Rd. |

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define pipeline hazard. Name the three classes of pipeline hazards and state one ARM7 mitigation for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pipeline hazard** is any condition in a pipelined processor that prevents the next instruction from executing in its designated clock cycle, causing a **stall** or **flush** in the pipeline.

| Hazard Class | Definition | ARM7 Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural** | Two stages contend for the same hardware resource. | **Harvard architecture** — separate I-memory (used in F) and D-memory (used in M) avoid the conflict. |
| **Data** | An instruction depends on the result of a previous in-flight instruction. | **Result forwarding (bypassing)** from EX/MEM and MEM/WB pipeline registers directly to ALU input. |
| **Control** | The next instruction to fetch is unknown due to a branch. | **Static branch prediction** (predict not-taken) and **branch folding** in the decoder reduce the penalty to 2 cycles. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 3, Q7]`

#### (a) Draw the block diagram of the ARM7 5-stage pipeline and explain the function of each stage. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The ARM7 5-stage pipeline consists of **five sequential stages** separated by four sets of pipeline registers: IF/ID, ID/EXE, EX/MEM, MEM/WB.

**Block Diagram:**

```
              PC
              │
       ┌──────▼──────┐
       │  F: FETCH   │  ← reads instr from I-memory at address PC
       │  (I-Mem)    │  ← computes PC + 4
       └──┬────────┬─┘
   IF/ID  │        │
   reg  ◄─┘        │
              ┌────▼─────┐
              │ D: DECODE │  ← decodes opcode
              │  (RegFile │  ← reads Rn, Rm from register file
              │   read)   │  ← computes branch target
              └──┬──────┬─┘
   ID/EXE reg ◄────┘      │
              ┌────▼─────┐
              │  E: EXEC │  ← ALU op (ADD, SUB, AND, OR, ...)
              │  (ALU +  │  ← effective address = Rn + offset
              │  Shifter)│  ← branch condition evaluated
              └──┬──────┬─┘
   EX/MEM reg ◄───┘      │
              ┌────▼─────┐
              │ M: MEM   │  ← LDR: reads from D-memory
              │  (D-Mem) │  ← STR: writes to D-memory
              │          │  ← ALU ops: pass-through
              └──┬──────┬─┘
   MEM/WB reg ◄───┘      │
              ┌────▼─────┐
              │ W: WRITE │  ← writes result to Rd
              │   BACK   │
              │(RegFile  │
              │  write)  │
              └──────────┘
```

**Function of each stage:**

1. **F (Fetch)**: Sends PC to instruction memory, retrieves the 32-bit instruction, and stores it in IF/ID register. PC is incremented to PC+4 for the next sequential fetch.
2. **D (Decode)**: Decodes the instruction opcode; reads source operands (Rn, Rm) and destination Rd from the register file; sign-extends immediates; computes the branch target address (PC + offset).
3. **E (Execute)**: The ALU performs the arithmetic/logic operation, or for memory instructions computes the effective address (Rn + offset). Branch conditions are evaluated; for a taken branch, the PC mux selects the branch target.
4. **M (Memory)**: For `LDR`, reads data from data memory. For `STR`, writes register data to data memory. For non-memory instructions, this stage is a transparent NOP.
5. **W (Writeback)**: Writes the result (either ALU output from E or memory data from M) into the destination register Rd in the register file.

**(7 marks: 2 for diagram + 5 for stage-wise explanation — 1 mark each)**

---

#### (b) Explain the three types of pipeline hazards in the ARM7 with suitable examples. How does ARM7 mitigate each of them? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Hazard 1 — Structural Hazard**

A structural hazard occurs when two pipeline stages need the **same hardware resource** at the same time.

*Example:* If a unified memory were used for both instruction fetch (F stage) and data access (M stage), an instruction performing a load in M would clash with the next instruction's fetch in F.

*ARM7 Mitigation:* ARM7 uses a **Harvard architecture** with **separate instruction memory** (accessed in F) and **data memory** (accessed in M). This eliminates structural hazards under normal conditions.

---

**Hazard 2 — Data Hazard**

A data hazard occurs when an instruction needs a value that a previous instruction has not yet written back to the register file.

*Example (Read-After-Write, RAW):*
```
ADD  R1, R2, R3     ; I1: R1 ← R2 + R3
SUB  R4, R1, R5     ; I2: R4 ← R1 - R5  (needs R1)
```
When I2 reaches the EX stage, I1 is still in M — R1 has not been written back yet. Without mitigation, a 2-cycle bubble would be needed.

*ARM7 Mitigation:* **Result forwarding (bypassing)**.
- The ALU result computed in I1's EX stage is latched in the **EX/MEM register**.
- This value is **forwarded directly to the ALU input** of I2 in EX.
- This eliminates the 1-cycle-apart stall entirely.
- For 2-cycle-apart dependencies (e.g., after a load), the **MEM/WB register** forwards to EX with only a **1-cycle bubble** (unavoidable because load data is available only after M).

---

**Hazard 3 — Control Hazard (Branch Hazard)**

A control hazard occurs because the PC for the next instruction depends on the outcome of a branch, which is known only at the end of the **EX stage** (in ARM7). Meanwhile, two more instructions may have already been fetched (and one decoded).

*Example:*
```
CMP  R1, #0
BEQ  TARGET        ; branch target unknown until EX
ADD  R2, R3, R4    ; already fetched, will be discarded
SUB  R5, R6, R7    ; already fetched, will be discarded
TARGET: ...
```
If the branch is taken, the two already-fetched instructions must be **flushed** from the pipeline. This costs **2 clock cycles** of bubble per taken branch.

*ARM7 Mitigations:*
1. **Static branch prediction (default: not-taken)**: Continue fetching the fall-through path; flush only if branch is taken.
2. **Branch folding**: The decoder detects short conditional branches and removes them from the instruction stream, replacing them with a NOP (or the target instruction). This saves 1 cycle per non-taken branch.
3. **Reducing branch penalty to 2 cycles**: The branch decision is made in EX (not M or WB), limiting the flush cost.

**(7 marks: 2 for hazard types + 2 for examples + 3 for ARM7 mitigations)**

---

### Question Choice B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 3, Q8]`

#### (a) Derive the speedup of a 5-stage pipeline over a non-pipelined datapath for executing n instructions. Plot a table of speedup values for n = 1, 5, 10, 50, 100. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption**: Each pipeline stage consumes 1 clock cycle of period $t_{clk}$.

---

**Step 1: Non-pipelined execution time**

In a non-pipelined datapath, every instruction must complete all 5 stages before the next instruction begins. Thus:

$$T_{non-pipe} = n \times 5 \times t_{clk} = 5n \, t_{clk}$$

---

**Step 2: Pipelined execution time**

In a pipelined datapath:
- The **first instruction** takes $k = 5$ cycles to traverse the entire pipeline (latency).
- Each **subsequent instruction** completes 1 cycle after the previous one (throughput).
- Total cycles for n instructions = $k + (n - 1) = 5 + n - 1 = n + 4$.

$$T_{pipe} = (n + 4) \, t_{clk}$$

---

**Step 3: Speedup**

$$S = \frac{T_{non-pipe}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{5n \cdot t_{clk}}{(n + 4) \cdot t_{clk}}$$

$$\boxed{S = \frac{5n}{n + 4}}$$

---

**Step 4: Tabulation**

| n | Speedup S = 5n/(n+4) | % of ideal (5×) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.00 | 20.0% |
| 5 | 2.78 | 55.6% |
| 10 | 3.57 | 71.4% |
| 50 | 4.63 | 92.6% |
| 100 | 4.81 | 96.2% |

---

**Step 5: Asymptotic analysis**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{5n}{n + 4} = 5$$

For large n, the pipeline approaches the ideal 5× speedup. The "pipeline fill" overhead of 4 cycles is amortized and becomes negligible.

**(7 marks: 2 for derivation + 2 for formula + 3 for tabulation and interpretation)**

---

#### (b) With a neat diagram, explain the result forwarding (bypassing) technique used in the ARM7 to handle data hazards. Show how an EX/MEM forwarding path resolves a 1-instruction-apart RAW hazard. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Concept**: Result forwarding (also called *bypassing*) is a hardware technique that routes the result of an arithmetic operation **directly from a pipeline register to the ALU input** of a dependent instruction, **without waiting** for the result to be written to the register file.

---

**Block Diagram (Forwarding Paths in ARM7):**

```
                 ┌──── ALU result ────► EX/MEM register
                 │                          │
   I1 EX stage  ─┘                          │ (Forwarding Path 1)
                                            ▼
                                  ┌──── MUX (at ALU Operand1 input) ────► ALU
                                            ▲
   MEM/WB register ──── (Forwarding Path 2) ┘
                                            │
   I2 EX stage needs operand ───────────────┘
```

The two forwarding paths are:
- **EX/MEM → EX ALU input**: Resolves 1-cycle-apart data dependencies where the producer's EX result is needed by the consumer's EX stage.
- **MEM/WB → EX ALU input**: Resolves 2-cycle-apart dependencies, e.g., after a `LDR` whose data is read from memory in M.

---

**Worked Example: 1-instruction-apart RAW hazard**

Instruction sequence:
```
I1: ADD  R1, R2, R3     ; R1 ← R2 + R3
I2: SUB  R4, R1, R5     ; R4 ← R1 - R5   (depends on I1)
```

**Cycle-by-cycle trace WITHOUT forwarding:**

| Cycle | I1 Stage | I2 Stage | R1 status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | F | — | — |
| 2 | D | F | — |
| 3 | E (R1 = R2+R3) | D | Stale value in reg |
| 4 | M | **EX (needs R1) → STALL** ❌ | R1 not yet in reg file |
| 5 | W (R1 ← ...) | EX (proceeds) | Correct R1 used |

Result: 1-cycle bubble required.

---

**Cycle-by-cycle trace WITH EX/MEM forwarding:**

| Cycle | I1 Stage | I2 Stage | Forwarding Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | F | — | — |
| 2 | D | F | — |
| 3 | E (R1 = R2+R3 computed, latched in EX/MEM reg) | D | — |
| 4 | M | EX (R1 received via **EX/MEM → ALU forwarding**) ✓ | Forwarded value used |
| 5 | W | M | — |

Result: **No bubble** — I2 EX proceeds without stalling. The value of R1 is forwarded directly from the EX/MEM register of I1 to the Operand1 input of the ALU in I2's EX stage.

---

**Control logic for the forwarding MUX**:
- The forwarding control unit checks: *Is the destination register Rd of the instruction in EX/MEM the same as the source register of the instruction in EX?*
- If **YES** → select forwarded value from EX/MEM register.
- Else if Rd of MEM/WB matches source of EX → select forwarded value from MEM/WB.
- Else → use the value from the register file (normal path).

**(7 marks: 2 for concept + 2 for diagram + 3 for worked example)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in the KTU exam:**
> 1. **Confusing stage count**: ARM7 has 5 stages, NOT 6 or 4. Writing "IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB" without naming them properly loses a mark.
> 2. **Wrong branch penalty**: Many students write 3-cycle branch penalty. The correct ARM7TDMI branch penalty is **2 cycles** (resolved in EX stage).
> 3. **Forwarding vs Stalling confusion**: Forwarding **eliminates** the need for stalling in the 1-cycle-apart case. Stalling is only required for **load-use** hazards (LDR followed by use of the loaded register).
> 4. **Speedup formula**: Writing $S = 5n/(n+4)$ is correct. Writing $S = 5$ (ideal only) without the formula derivation loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Pipeline register count**: There are **4 pipeline registers** (IF/ID, ID/EXE, EX/MEM, MEM/WB), not 5. The 5th "register" is the PC.
> 6. **Mixing up Harvard vs Von Neumann**: ARM7 is **modified Harvard** (separate caches, unified main memory). Saying "ARM7 has unified memory" is wrong and loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **ARM7 = 5-stage pipeline**: F (Fetch), D (Decode), E (Execute), M (Memory), W (Writeback).
- [x] **Pipeline registers**: 4 sets (IF/ID, ID/EXE, EX/MEM, MEM/WB) isolate combinational paths.
- [x] **Pipelining increases throughput, not latency**: Single instruction still takes 5 cycles; new instruction completes every 1 cycle.
- [x] **Speedup formula**: $S = \dfrac{5n}{n+4}$; ideal 5× for $n \to \infty$.
- [x] **CPI ideal = 1**; with branch stalls, $CPI_{eff} = 1 + 2 f_b$.
- [x] **Structural hazard → eliminated by Harvard architecture** (separate I/D memory).
- [x] **Data hazard → resolved by forwarding** (EX/MEM and MEM/WB bypass paths).
- [x] **Control hazard → branch penalty = 2 cycles**; mitigated by static prediction and branch folding.
- [x] **Load-use hazard → 1 bubble unavoidable** even with forwarding (load data available only after M).
- [x] **ARM7 was designed for low-power embedded**; deep pipelines (Cortex-A) trade power for clock speed.
- [x] **Pipeline fill = 4 cycles** for 5-stage pipeline (k − 1 cycles wasted before steady state).
- [x] **Branch decision is in EX stage**, not M or WB — this is why penalty is 2, not 3.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line summary for last-minute revision**:
> *"ARM7's 5-stage pipeline (F-D-E-M-W) overlaps instructions to give CPI ≈ 1, with structural hazards eliminated by Harvard, data hazards eliminated by forwarding, and control hazards costing 2 cycles per taken branch."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Instruction Execution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ARM Instruction Execution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARM Instruction Execution** is the structured, pipelined sequence of micro-operations performed by the ARM processor core to fetch, decode, execute, and write-back the result of a single machine-level instruction stored in program memory. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, instruction execution is studied as a deterministic state-machine transition where the Program Counter (PC), instruction registers, barrel shifter, ALU, and register bank are orchestrated by a finite-state machine (FSM) known as the **instruction cycle controller**.

The ARM7TDMI core (the canonical reference architecture for KTU Module 3) implements a **3-stage pipeline**:
- **Stage 1 — Fetch (F):** Reads the instruction from memory using the address in PC.
- **Stage 2 — Decode / Operand Read (D):** Decodes opcode, reads source operands from the register bank, and computes branch targets.
- **Stage 3 — Execute / Write-back (E / W):** Performs ALU operation, shift, multiply, or memory access, then writes the result back to the destination register.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Although the ARM7TDMI uses a 3-stage pipeline, modern ARM cores (Cortex-M3/M4) use 3-stage as well, but Cortex-A series use 7+ stages. For KTU Module 3, focus is on the **ARM7TDMI 3-stage pipeline model**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of ARM instruction execution as a **factory assembly line** for a single product (the instruction result):
- **Stage 1 (Fetch)** is the **raw materials warehouse clerk** who fetches the instruction "blueprint" from memory.
- **Stage 2 (Decode)** is the **foreman** who reads the blueprint, gathers the required tools (registers), and decides what the worker must do.
- **Stage 3 (Execute + Write-back)** is the **assembly worker** who actually builds the result and ships it to the destination register.

While the worker is finishing instruction *N*, the foreman is already preparing instruction *N+1*, and the clerk is fetching *N+2*. This **overlapped processing** is what gives pipelines their throughput advantage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Clock Cycle (T):** Time period of the processor clock, typically **1 ns** at 1 GHz.
> - **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction):** For ideal pipelined ARM, CPI approaches **1**.
> - **Pipeline Latency:** Number of cycles to complete one instruction = **3 cycles** for ARM7TDMI.
> - **Pipeline Throughput:** 1 instruction every clock cycle (after fill).

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The ARM Instruction Execution Cycle — Stepwise Operational Flow

ARM instruction execution is fundamentally a **Von Neumann / Modified Harvard** cycle. Every instruction, regardless of type (Data Processing, Load/Store, Branch, Multiply, Software Interrupt), passes through the canonical three-stage sequence with the FSM controller selecting appropriate datapath paths.

#### 2.1.1 Stage 1: FETCH

- The 32-bit **Program Counter (PC = R15)** holds the address of the instruction to be fetched. Internally, during fetch, the value presented to memory is **PC + 8** (because ARM is pipelined and PC is always 2 instructions ahead of the currently executing one in a 3-stage pipeline).
- The instruction at that address is read from memory (single 32-bit bus access for ARM, two 16-bit for Thumb).
- The fetched instruction is loaded into the **Instruction Register (IR)** at the end of the cycle.
- PC is incremented: $PC \leftarrow PC + 4$ (ARM state) or $PC \leftarrow PC + 2$ (Thumb state).

#### 2.1.2 Stage 2: DECODE / OPERAND READ

- The opcode is decoded by the instruction decoder.
- The **register bank** is read using up to two source register addresses from the instruction (Rn, Rm or Rd with shifts).
- If the instruction contains a **shifter operand**, the second operand is passed through the **barrel shifter** in parallel with register read — this is a key ARM feature (single-cycle shifts).
- Branch target address is computed by sign-extending the 24-bit offset and adding it to PC.
- **Condition codes** are evaluated here: if the instruction is conditionally executed and the condition is false, the instruction is killed (turned into a NOP).

#### 2.1.3 Stage 3: EXECUTE / WRITE-BACK

- The **ALU** performs the arithmetic/logic operation, or the **multiplier/array** performs multiplication, or the **memory unit** performs a load/store access.
- The result is written back to the destination register (Rd) in the register bank during the first half of the next cycle.
- For loads, the data from memory is captured and written back; for stores, the data is written to memory.

### 2.2 Datapath Components Activated During Execution

| Component | Role in Instruction Execution | Stage Active |
|---|---|---|
| Program Counter (PC / R15) | Holds address of next instruction to fetch | F |
| Instruction Register (IR) | Latches fetched opcode | F → D |
| Register Bank (37 × 32-bit) | Provides 2 read ports, 1 write port | D, E/W |
| Barrel Shifter | Performs shift/rotate in 1 cycle | D (operand prepare) |
| ALU | Performs +, −, AND, OR, XOR, compare | E |
| Address Register / Incrementer | PC + 4 logic | F |
| Multiplier Array | Performs 32×32→64 in 2–5 cycles | E (multi-cycle) |
| Memory Data Register | Holds load/store data | E |

### 2.3 Inter-Stage Hand-off and Latches

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The boundary registers between pipeline stages are called **pipeline latches** or **inter-stage holding registers**. They are NOT optional — without them, the next stage would consume unstable data mid-cycle. These are the ARM pipeline "valves" that synchronize data between stages.

The three pipeline latches in ARM7TDMI are:
1. **Fetch/Decode latch** — holds the instruction between F and D.
2. **Decode/Execute latch** — holds decoded control signals and operand values between D and E.
3. **Result/Write-back latch** — holds ALU output before final write to register file.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| Sequential Execution Time | $T_{seq} = N \times CPI_{seq} \times T_{clk}$ | Baseline for un-pipelined execution |
| Pipelined Execution Time | $T_{pipe} = (k + N - 1) \times T_{clk}$ | Ideal pipelined throughput model |
| Speedup Factor | $S = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{N \times CPI_{seq}}{k + N - 1}$ | Measures pipeline benefit |
| Pipeline Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{N}{k + N - 1} \times 100\%$ | Approaches 100% as $N \to \infty$ |
| ARM Throughput (ideal) | $CPI_{ideal} = 1$ | One instruction completed per cycle |
| PC offset (ARM7TDMI) | $PC_{fetch} = PC_{exec} + 8$ | Pipeline PC value presented to memory |
| Multi-cycle multiply (MUL) | $CPI_{mult} = 4$ to $5$ | Multiply is NOT single-cycle |
| Effective CPI (with stalls) | $CPI_{eff} = CPI_{ideal} + \text{Stall cycles per instruction}$ | Includes hazards/bubbles |

> Where:
> - $N$ = number of instructions in program
> - $k$ = number of pipeline stages (3 for ARM7TDMI)
> - $T_{clk}$ = clock period

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The instruction execution model directly determines:
- **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** analysis for **real-time embedded systems** (automotive ECUs, avionics, medical devices).
- **Interrupt latency** calculations — interrupts are only sampled at instruction boundaries in the pipeline.
- **Compiler scheduling decisions** — reordering instructions to hide pipeline stalls.
- **Power estimation** — dynamic power $\propto C \times V^2 \times f$, where $f$ is determined by how fast instructions complete.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pipeline Speedup Limit for ARM7TDMI

**Given:** $N$ instructions executed on a $k$-stage pipeline with ideal clock period $T_{clk}$.

**To Prove:** The maximum theoretical speedup of a $k$-stage pipeline is $k$.

#### Step 1: Sequential (Un-pipelined) Execution

In a non-pipelined ARM, each instruction completes fully before the next begins. If one instruction takes $k$ clock cycles:

$$
T_{seq} = N \times k \times T_{clk}
$$

This is because we process $N$ instructions serially, each consuming all $k$ stages of hardware time before moving on.

#### Step 2: Pipelined Execution

In the ideal pipelined case, after the pipeline is filled, one instruction completes per cycle. The pipeline must first be **filled** with $k$ instructions, then drained of $k$ instructions:

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + N - 1) \times T_{clk}
$$

The $-1$ is critical — it avoids double-counting the cycle where the first instruction finishes and the last instruction enters.

#### Step 3: Speedup Computation

$$
S = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{N \times k \times T_{clk}}{(k + N - 1) \times T_{clk}}
$$

Canceling $T_{clk}$:

$$
S = \frac{N \times k}{k + N - 1}
$$

$$
S = \frac{N \cdot k}{N + k - 1}
$$

#### Step 4: Limiting Behavior

As the number of instructions $N$ becomes very large (e.g., $N \to \infty$):

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N \cdot k}{N + k - 1} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{k \cdot N}{N(1 + \frac{k-1}{N})} = k
$$

Thus, **maximum speedup = number of pipeline stages = k = 3** for ARM7TDMI.

#### Step 5: Efficiency Bound

Dividing the speedup by $k$:

$$
\eta = \frac{S}{k} = \frac{N}{N + k - 1}
$$

For $N = 1$: $\eta = \frac{1}{3} = 33.3\%$ (very poor — one instruction cannot use the pipeline)
For $N = 100$: $\eta = \frac{100}{102} \approx 98\%$ (excellent — pipeline well utilized)

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An embedded system runs **10,000 instructions** on an ARM7TDMI at **50 MHz**. Compare sequential vs pipelined execution time.

#### Step 1: Compute clock period

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^6} = 20 \text{ ns}
$$

#### Step 2: Sequential execution

Assuming each instruction takes all 3 stages of hardware time without overlap (worst case, CPI = 3):

$$
T_{seq} = N \times k \times T_{clk} = 10000 \times 3 \times 20 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{seq} = 600{,}000 \text{ ns} = 600 \text{ μs}
$$

#### Step 3: Pipelined execution

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + N - 1) \times T_{clk} = (3 + 10000 - 1) \times 20 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{pipe} = 10002 \times 20 \text{ ns} = 200{,}040 \text{ ns} \approx 200.04 \text{ μs}
$$

#### Step 4: Speedup verification

$$
S = \frac{600}{200.04} \approx 2.999
$$

This confirms the theoretical limit of $k = 3$ is approached as $N$ becomes large.

### 3.3 Derivation: Effective CPI with Pipeline Stalls

In practice, ARM pipelines incur **hazards** — situations where the next instruction cannot proceed.

**Hazard types:**
1. **Data hazards** — instruction needs the result of a previous instruction not yet written back.
2. **Control hazards** — branch instruction changes PC, so instructions after the branch may be wrongly fetched.
3. **Resource conflicts** — two instructions need the same hardware unit (e.g., two memory accesses).

The effective CPI formula:

$$
CPI_{eff} = CPI_{ideal} + \sum_{i=1}^{H} (Stall_{i} \times Frequency_{i})
$$

Where each stall type $i$ contributes $Stall_{i}$ extra cycles occurring with frequency $Frequency_{i}$ per instruction.

#### Example: Data Hazard Stall

Suppose 20% of instructions are followed by an instruction that depends on their result. Each dependency costs 1 stall cycle (data forwarding may save this, but assume worst case):

$$
CPI_{eff} = 1 + (1 \times 0.20) = 1.20
$$

Performance penalty:

$$
\text{Penalty} = \frac{CPI_{eff} - CPI_{ideal}}{CPI_{ideal}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.20}{1.00} \times 100\% = 20\%
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: ARM Instruction Classes vs Pipeline Behavior

| Instruction Class | Example | Fetch Stages Used | Special Datapath | Cycles to Complete | Hazard Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data Processing | `ADD R0, R1, R2` | F → D → E | ALU + Barrel Shifter | 1 (post-pipeline) | Low |
| Data Transfer (Load) | `LDR R0, [R1]` | F → D → E + mem | Memory + Register | 1 (issued) + 2 (data) | High (load-use) |
| Data Transfer (Store) | `STR R0, [R1]` | F → D → E + mem | Memory + Register | 1 (issued) | Low |
| Branch (no link) | `B label` | F → D → E + flush | Branch adder | 1 + 2 flush | High (control) |
| Branch with link | `BL sub` | F → D → E + flush | Branch adder + LR write | 1 + 2 flush | High |
| Multiply | `MUL R0, R1, R2` | F → D → E (multi-cycle) | Multiplier array | 4–5 | Medium |
| Software Interrupt | `SWI #n` | F → D → E + exception | Exception controller | 1 + exception latency | Special |

### 4.3 Instruction Execution State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Reset
    Reset --> Fetch: Power On Reset
    Fetch --> Decode: Latch Instruction
    Decode --> Execute: Operand Ready
    Decode --> Kill: Condition False
    Kill --> Fetch: PC Next
    Execute --> WriteBack: ALU Ready
    Execute --> MemoryAccess: Load or Store
    MemoryAccess --> WriteBack: Data Captured
    WriteBack --> Fetch: PC Updated
    Fetch --> Exception: IRQ or FIQ
    Fetch --> Exception: Undefined or SWI
    Exception --> Fetch: Handler Return
```
