## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **Define the three stages of the ARM7TDMI pipeline. What is the role of the Program Counter (R15) in the fetch stage?**

**Model Answer:**

The ARM7TDMI uses a 3-stage pipeline:

1. **Fetch (F):** Reads the 32-bit instruction from memory at the address held in R15 (PC). The value presented to memory is internally **PC + 8** because the PC is always 2 instructions ahead of the currently executing one in the pipeline. PC is then incremented by 4 to point to the next instruction.

2. **Decode (D):** The instruction is decoded, source operands are read from the register bank (up to two operands), and the barrel shifter prepares any shifted operand. Branch target addresses are also computed here. Condition codes are evaluated.

3. **Execute (E):** The ALU performs the operation, the result is written back to the destination register (Rd) through the write-back port. For loads/stores, memory is accessed in this stage.

**Role of R15 (PC) in Fetch:**
R15 holds the address of the instruction to be fetched next. Its internal value is always 8 bytes (2 instructions) ahead of the currently executing instruction because of pipelining. After each fetch, it is incremented by 4 in ARM state or 2 in Thumb state.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Explain why the effective CPI of a pipelined ARM processor is higher than the ideal value of 1.**

**Model Answer:**

The ideal CPI of a fully pipelined ARM processor is **1**, meaning one instruction completes per cycle. However, the **effective CPI** is higher in practice because of **pipeline hazards**:

1. **Data Hazards:** When an instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction that has not yet completed write-back, the processor must insert **stall (bubble) cycles** to wait. In ARM7TDMI, an LDR followed immediately by a use of the loaded register incurs a 2-cycle stall unless the compiler reorders instructions.

2. **Control Hazards:** When a branch instruction is executed, the instructions already fetched in the pipeline after the branch are invalid. The pipeline must be **flushed**, typically wasting 2 cycles (one per fetched instruction beyond the branch).

3. **Resource Conflicts:** The ARM7TDMI has a single memory port; therefore, an instruction that needs to do a load or store in the execute stage cannot also be fetching the next instruction from the same memory in the same cycle, potentially causing contention.

4. **Multi-cycle Operations:** The MUL instruction takes 4–5 cycles instead of 1, increasing the CPI for multiply-heavy code.

The effective CPI is therefore expressed as:

$$
CPI_{eff} = 1 + \text{Stall cycles per instruction}
$$

For typical embedded code with branches every 5–7 instructions and occasional data dependencies, $CPI_{eff} \approx 1.2$ to $1.5$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Question A [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the execution of an ARM data-processing instruction in the 3-stage pipeline. List all the major hardware units activated and state the role of the barrel shifter.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A real-time embedded controller based on ARM7TDMI runs a critical control loop of 5000 instructions at 60 MHz clock. Compute (i) sequential execution time, (ii) pipelined execution time, (iii) effective speedup, and (iv) pipeline efficiency. Assume ideal pipelining with no stalls.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Data-Processing Instruction Execution**

Consider the instruction `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #3` (R0 = R1 + (R2 << 3)).

The 3-stage execution proceeds as follows:

**Stage 1 — Fetch:**
- The address in PC (R15) is sent to the instruction memory.
- Memory returns the 32-bit encoded instruction.
- The instruction is latched into the Instruction Register (IR).
- PC is incremented by 4.

**Stage 2 — Decode:**
- The instruction decoder parses the opcode (ADD), identifies destination register R0, source register R1, and second operand as R2 with LSL #3.
- R1 is read from the register bank on **read port 1**.
- R2 is read from the register bank on **read port 2**.
- The barrel shifter performs `R2 << 3` (shift left logical by 3) in a single cycle. The shifted value is presented to the ALU's second input.
- Condition codes are checked.

**Stage 3 — Execute + Write-back:**
- The ALU performs `R1 + (R2 << 3)`.
- The result is driven onto the write-back bus.
- During the first half of the next cycle, the result is written into R0 via the write port of the register bank.

**Major hardware units activated:**
1. Program Counter (R15)
2. Instruction Memory
3. Instruction Register
4. Instruction Decoder
5. Register Bank (2 read ports + 1 write port)
6. Barrel Shifter
7. ALU
8. Write-Back Mux
9. PC + 4 Incrementer

**Role of the Barrel Shifter:**
The barrel shifter allows the second operand of any data-processing instruction to be shifted or rotated in a **single clock cycle**, without consuming an extra cycle. Supported operations include LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX. This eliminates shift instructions as separate operations, making ARM code denser and faster. For example, multiplying by 8 is achieved with `LSL #3` in 1 cycle.

---

**Part (b) — Performance Calculations**

**Given:** $N = 5000$ instructions, $f = 60$ MHz, $k = 3$ stages, ideal pipelining (no stalls).

**Step 1: Clock period**

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{60 \times 10^6} = 16.67 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2: Sequential execution time**

Each instruction takes 3 cycles sequentially:

$$
T_{seq} = N \times k \times T_{clk} = 5000 \times 3 \times 16.67 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{seq} = 250{,}050 \text{ ns} = 250.05 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 3: Pipelined execution time**

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + N - 1) \times T_{clk} = (3 + 5000 - 1) \times 16.67 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{pipe} = 5002 \times 16.67 \text{ ns} = 83{,}383.34 \text{ ns} \approx 83.38 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 4: Effective speedup**

$$
S = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{250.05}{83.38} \approx 2.999
$$

**Step 5: Pipeline efficiency**

$$
\eta = \frac{N}{k + N - 1} \times 100\% = \frac{5000}{5002} \times 100\% \approx 99.96\%
$$

> The efficiency approaches 100% as expected for large $N$ — a hallmark of well-utilized pipelines.

---

> **Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the three types of pipeline hazards that affect ARM instruction execution. For each, state a mitigation technique used in ARM7TDMI.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 25 MHz ARM7TDMI executes a program where 8% of instructions are MUL (taking 4 cycles), 15% are LDR (taking 3 cycles total — 1 issue + 2 data wait), and the rest are single-cycle data processing. Compute the effective CPI and total execution time for 10,000 instructions.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Pipeline Hazards in ARM**

**1. Data Hazards:**
Occur when an instruction needs a value produced by a previous instruction that has not yet completed write-back.
- *Example:* `ADD R0, R1, R2` followed immediately by `MOV R3, R0`.
- *Mitigation:* **Register forwarding / data forwarding** is implicit in ARM7TDMI — the ALU output of the execute stage is fed directly to the ALU input of the next instruction. For loads, the compiler must schedule a non-dependent instruction between the LDR and its use (interlock), costing 2 stall cycles.

**2. Control Hazards:**
Occur when a branch changes the PC, making subsequent fetched instructions invalid.
- *Example:* `CMP R0, #0` followed by `BEQ label`.
- *Mitigation:* ARM evaluates the branch condition in the **decode stage**, then flushes the pipeline (inserts 2 bubbles). Branch prediction is not used in ARM7TDMI — every branch costs 2 cycles. In modern Cortex-M3, **branch speculation** reduces this.

**3. Resource Hazards (Structural Conflicts):**
Occur when two pipeline stages need the same hardware resource simultaneously.
- *Example:* Instruction in execute stage needs memory access (LDR/STR) while next instruction in fetch stage also needs memory.
- *Mitigation:* In ARM7TDMI, the **memory system is unified** for instruction and data, and the **instruction is fetched in the first half of the cycle** while data is read/written in the second half, eliminating the conflict for single-cycle accesses. Multi-cycle accesses still cause stalls.

---

**Part (b) — Effective CPI Computation**

**Given:**
- Total instructions: $N = 10{,}000$
- MUL: 8% → 4 cycles each
- LDR: 15% → 3 cycles each
- Data-processing: 77% → 1 cycle each
- $f = 25$ MHz

**Step 1: Effective CPI**

$$
CPI_{eff} = \sum (Fraction_i \times CPI_i)
$$

$$
CPI_{eff} = (0.08 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.77 \times 1)
$$

$$
CPI_{eff} = 0.32 + 0.45 + 0.77 = 1.54
$$

**Step 2: Clock period**

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{25 \times 10^6} = 40 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3: Total execution time**

$$
T_{total} = N \times CPI_{eff} \times T_{clk} = 10{,}000 \times 1.54 \times 40 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 616{,}000 \text{ ns} = 616 \text{ μs}
$$

> The effective CPI of 1.54 reflects the penalty paid for multi-cycle instructions and memory access latency in a real embedded workload.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting PC + 8 offset:** ARM7TDMI's PC during fetch is 2 instructions ahead, not 1. Writing PC + 4 will cost marks.
> 2. **Confusing pipeline latency with throughput:** Latency = 3 cycles per instruction, throughput = 1 instruction/cycle. Examiners test this distinction.
> 3. **Ignoring multi-cycle instructions in CPI:** MUL is NOT 1 cycle. Naively using CPI = 1 for all instructions is a common error.
> 4. **Forgetting write-back happens in the first half of the cycle:** Many students describe write-back as a separate 4th stage — it is part of Execute in ARM7TDMI.
> 5. **Not specifying units in numerical answers:** Always write "ns", "μs", or "ms" explicitly.
> 6. **Confusing barrel shifter with ALU:** The barrel shifter only does shifts/rotates; it does not add or multiply. It is in the **decode/operand prepare** stage, not the execute stage.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ARM Instruction Execution**

- ✅ ARM7TDMI uses a **3-stage pipeline**: Fetch → Decode → Execute (with Write-back bundled in Execute).
- ✅ **Ideal CPI = 1**, **Latency = 3 cycles**, **PC offset during fetch = +8**.
- ✅ The **barrel shifter** performs any shift/rotate in **1 cycle** and is part of operand preparation in Decode.
- ✅ The **register bank** has 2 read ports and 1 write port — supporting register-to-register operations without memory traffic.
- ✅ **Pipeline latches** (inter-stage registers) synchronize data between stages; they are critical to correct operation.
- ✅ **Pipeline hazards:** data, control, resource — each degrades CPI above the ideal value of 1.
- ✅ **MUL is multi-cycle (4–5 cycles)**; LDR may incur a 2-cycle interlock for load-use dependency.
- ✅ **Branches** are evaluated in Decode; pipeline is flushed, costing **2 cycles** per branch.
- ✅ **Speedup** approaches $k$ (number of stages) as instruction count grows large.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = N / (N + k - 1)$ — improves with longer instruction streams.
- ✅ ARM is a **load/store architecture** — only LDR/STR access memory; data processing operates on registers.
- ✅ The **PC is R15** — writing to R15 is a branch (with side effects on CPSR for BX).

> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam Day — "FDE":** **F**etch, **D**ecode, **E**xecute — the three stages of ARM pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ARM Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ARM Implementation** refers to the hardware architectural realization of the Advanced RISC Machine (ARM) processor, encompassing the data path, control logic, pipeline stages, instruction decoding, and execution units that physically implement the ARM Instruction Set Architecture (ISA). The ARM implementation is built upon the **Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC)** philosophy, emphasizing a load-store architecture, fixed-length instructions (32-bit), a large uniform register file of **37 registers** (31 general-purpose + 6 status registers, banked for modes), and a single-cycle execution model for most data-processing instructions.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context for *Embedded System Design* (PBEVT504), ARM implementation specifically covers the micro-architectural realization of the **ARM7TDMI** (a 3-stage pipeline) and contrasts it with higher-order pipelines such as the **ARM9 (5-stage)** and **ARM11 (8-stage)**. The implementation modules include:

- The **Register Bank** (fast on-chip storage).
- The **Barrel Shifter** (hardware for shift/rotate operations).
- The **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)**.
- The **Address Register and Incrementer** for load/store.
- The **Multiplier** (modified Booth's algorithm).
- The **Instruction Decoder and Control Unit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 — ARM Implementation):**
> The expected learning outcome is to *understand and illustrate the ARM processor data path, pipeline stages, control flow, and the implementation of data-processing, data-transfer, and branch instructions.* Examiner questions typically focus on datapath diagrams, instruction decoding flow, pipeline hazard identification, and the calculation of instruction cycle timings.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Smart Factory" Analogy for ARM Implementation**
>
> Imagine a **highly efficient assembly line** (this is the ARM pipeline) in a smart factory that produces custom wooden toys (these are the *results* of executed instructions).
>
> - **Station 1 — FETCH**: A worker picks the blueprint (the 32-bit instruction) from a shelf (memory at PC). The blueprint is placed on a conveyor belt.
> - **Station 2 — DECODE**: A second worker reads the blueprint, identifies which tools (operands from the register bank) and which machines (ALU, Barrel Shifter, Multiplier) are needed.
> - **Station 3 — EXECUTE**: A third worker uses the tools to actually build the toy (performs the computation) and either stores it in a finished-goods bin (Rd) or dispatches it to the warehouse (memory via load/store).
>
> Every **clock cycle**, a new toy starts at Station 1, while the previous ones advance forward. This overlap of work is called **pipelining**, and it is the *heart* of why ARM is so fast and power-efficient. Now, suppose the blueprint says "use the *screwdriver* shifted 2 positions to the left" — the **Barrel Shifter** is the *rotating tool-rack* that instantly positions the screwdriver, eliminating the need for a separate, slow "shift" step.

### 1.3 Standard Architectural Metrics of ARM (KTU-Boarded Values)

| Metric | Value (ARM7TDMI) | Value (ARM9TDMI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pipeline Stages | **3** (Fetch, Decode, Execute) | **5** (Fetch, Decode, Execute, Memory, Writeback) |
| Register File Size | **37 × 32-bit** | **37 × 32-bit** |
| Architectural Width | **32-bit** | **32-bit** |
| Addressing Modes | **9** (in ARM state) | **9** (in ARM state) |
| Operating Modes | **7** | **7** |
| Endianness | Selectable (Big/Little) | Selectable (Big/Little) |
| Standard Clock Speed (embedded) | **~ 50–133 MHz** | **~ 150–220 MHz** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **3-stage pipeline** of ARM7TDMI is the most-tested architecture in KTU ESE for the PBEVT504 paper, as it is the cleanest model for analyzing pipeline hazards, stalls, and branch penalties. Students are expected to draw and label the datapath, identify stall conditions, and compute the **cycles per instruction (CPI)** for given instruction sequences.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Core Components of ARM Implementation

The ARM datapath is conventionally decomposed into three orthogonal hardware blocks, each of which can be analyzed independently for KTU exam purposes.

#### 2.1.1 The Register Bank (31 × 32-bit visible)

- **Why it matters**: ARM's load-store RISC model demands that *all* operands (except immediate literals) be read from registers. Therefore, the register file must support **two source reads and one destination write per cycle**.
- **Structure**: A 32-bit wide, 32-location (R0–R15, with banked physical 37 locations) dual-port register file. Each port has an address bus (Ra, Rb), a write port (Rw), and enable signals (SHIFT, ALU, MEM).
- **Key insight**: R13 (SP), R14 (LR), and R15 (PC) are *not* orthogonal — they play special architectural roles. R15 reads as PC+8 in ARM state (because the pipeline has already fetched two instructions ahead).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** R15 reading as `PC + 8` is a *very* common 3-mark question. It occurs because at the time of the EXECUTE stage, the PC has already moved 2 instructions (8 bytes) forward due to the 3-stage pipeline. The state where R15 reads as `PC + 12` is the THUMB (16-bit) state.

#### 2.1.2 The Barrel Shifter

- **Why it matters**: ARM permits the *second* operand of every data-processing instruction to be *pre-shifted* by the ALU without consuming an extra cycle. This is unique to ARM and is a major contributor to its code density.
- **Operation**: The Barrel Shifter is a pure combinational network that performs one of: **LSL (Logical Shift Left), LSR (Logical Shift Right), ASR (Arithmetic Shift Right), ROR (Rotate Right by Register), RRX (Rotate Right Extended by 1)**. It operates on the contents of Rm and produces a 32-bit result *in parallel* with the register read.
- **Cycle cost**: **Zero** — it is a single combinational pass, the result becomes available before the next clock edge.

#### 2.1.3 The ALU + Multiplier

- The ALU executes ADD, SUB, AND, ORR, EOR, BIC, etc., on the two 32-bit operands.
- The Multiplier is **not** single-cycle on ARM7 (it is iterative, taking **1–4 cycles** depending on the variant), and is implemented via a **modified Booth's recoding** algorithm to reduce partial products.

### 2.2 The 3-Stage Pipeline of ARM7TDMI — Operational Logic

| Stage | Mnemonic | Function | Active Latches |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **F (Fetch)** | Reads instruction from memory at address held in PC. Increments PC by 4. | Holds the fetched instruction. |
| 2 | **D (Decode)** | Decodes opcode, reads register bank, generates control signals, computes branch targets. | Holds decoded control word + operand busses. |
| 3 | **E (Execute)** | ALU/Barrel Shifter operates, memory address calculated, branch target committed. | Writes back to Rd (for data-processing) or MAR. |

**Key Operational Points** (logical breakdown):

1. **In Stage F**, the memory interface presents the instruction at `A = PC` to the instruction bus. The incrementer adds **4** to PC. This stage is purely memory-bound.
2. **In Stage D**, the decoder identifies the instruction class. The register file is read using bits `[19:16]` (Rn) and `[15:12]` (Rm) as addresses. The shifter control unit interprets bits `[11:0]` (operand-2 specifier). The control unit asserts one of the busses: `ALU`, `MEM`, or `SHIFT` to drive the destination write.
3. **In Stage E**, the ALU performs the operation. For data-processing instructions, the result is written back to Rd (specified by bits `[15:12]`) at the end of the cycle. For loads, the address is driven onto the bus. For branches, the PC is loaded with the branch target.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pipeline Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{N_{\text{stages}}}$ | Instructions per second. |
| Single instruction cycle count (most data-proc) | **1 cycle** in EX stage, **3 cycles total** to retire | For ARM7 3-stage. |
| Branch penalty | **2 cycles** lost (pipeline flush of F + D) | For ARM7. |
| Load-use hazard stall | **1 cycle** inserted | Detected in D, stall F, bubble in E. |
| Multiplier (MUL) | **4 cycles** (ARM7 classic) | Iterative Booth's. MLA adds 1 more. |
| Booth's recoding partial products | $\text{Number of PPs} = \lceil n/2 \rceil$ | For $n$-bit operands, $n = 32$, so **16 PPs**. |
| PC-relative read value (ARM state) | $\text{value read} = PC + 8$ | R15 alias. |
| PC-relative read value (THUMB state) | $\text{value read} = PC + 4$ | R15 alias. |
| CPI for a sequence | $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{Total cycles}}{\text{Total instructions}}$ | Used in question 14-mark KTU problems. |
| Pipeline speedup (theoretical) | $S = \dfrac{N_{\text{inst}} \cdot t_{\text{non-pipe}}}{N_{\text{inst}} + (k-1) \cdot t_{\text{pipe}}}$ | $k$ = stages. |

> [!WARNING]
> Do **not** confuse R15 reads with R15 *writes*. Writes to R15 in ARM state change the PC to the value being written, but the *read* value (when R15 is used as a source operand) is `PC + 8`. This is a *favourite* KTU trick question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile SoCs**: Every modern smartphone SoC (Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon, Samsung Exynos) is an evolution of the ARM implementation, scaling from 3-stage (low-end Cortex-M0) to **14-stage (Cortex-A77)** out-of-order pipelines.
- **Embedded Controllers**: The ARM Cortex-M series (used in STM32, NXP Kinetis) preserves the original ARM7 architectural philosophy — load-store, single-cycle I/O, deterministic interrupt response — making it the *de-facto* standard for IoT, automotive ECUs, and motor control.
- **Power Efficiency**: ARM's simple datapath enables **dynamic voltage/frequency scaling (DVFS)**, where the entire processor can be clocked down to consume nanoamps in deep-sleep — critical for battery-powered embedded nodes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decoding the ARM Instruction Word

A 32-bit ARM data-processing instruction has the canonical structure shown below. The following analysis is exhaustive and is the most common derivation KTU examiners test.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Instruction Word} &= \underbrace{[31:28]}_{\text{cond}} \; \underbrace{[27:26]}_{00} \; \underbrace{[25]}_{\text{I}} \; \underbrace{[24:21]}_{\text{opcode}} \; \underbrace{[20]_{\text{S}}} \; \underbrace{[19:16]}_{\text{Rn}} \; \underbrace{[15:12]}_{\text{Rd}} \; \underbrace{[11:0]}_{\text{Operand2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Field-by-field decoding steps:**

1. **Bits [31:28] — Condition Field (`cond`)**: This is the *first* thing the decoder checks. If the APSR flags (N, Z, C, V) do not satisfy the condition, the instruction is **skipped** (treated as a NOP). The condition is tested in the **D stage** of the pipeline.
2. **Bits [27:26] = `00`**: Identifies the instruction class as **Data-Processing** (or multiply, if bit [25] and [4] are set). Other classes are: `01` (data transfer / load-store), `10` (branch), `11` (coprocessor).
3. **Bit [25] — Immediate Flag (`I`)**: If `I = 1`, Operand2 is an 8-bit immediate rotated right by an even number from bits [11:8]. If `I = 0`, Operand2 is a shifted register.
4. **Bits [24:21] — Opcode**: Specifies the operation: `0000` = AND, `1100` = ORR, `0001` = EOR, `0100` = ADD, `0010` = SUB, `1110` = BIC, etc.
5. **Bit [20] — Set-condition flag (`S`)**: If `S = 1`, the APSR flags are updated based on the result of the ALU.
6. **Bits [19:16] — Rn**: Address of the *first source operand* in the register bank.
7. **Bits [15:12] — Rd**: Address of the *destination register* in the register bank.
8. **Bits [11:0] — Operand2 specifier**: Either `Imm8 + rotate` (when I=1) or `Rm + shift + shift_amount` (when I=0).

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Modified Booth's Algorithm in ARM Multiplier

The ARM7's 32×32-bit integer multiplier is implemented using **Modified Booth's Recoding** to reduce the 32 partial products of a schoolbook array multiplier to only **16 partial products**. The derivation below shows why this is so.

**Step 1 — Standard Schoolbook Multiplication**: For two 32-bit numbers $A$ and $B$,

$$
P = A \times B = \sum_{i=0}^{31} a_i \cdot 2^i \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{31} b_j \cdot 2^j = \sum_{k=0}^{62} \left( \sum_{i+j=k} a_i b_j \right) 2^k
$$

This requires **32 partial products**, each of width 32 bits. Hardware cost: 32 rows of 32 full-adders = **1024 full adders**, plus a final 64-bit carry-propagate adder.

**Step 2 — Booth's Original Idea (Bit-pair recoding)**: Examine the *radix-4* interpretation. Group $B$ in overlapping pairs of bits. The recoded digit $d_i \in \{-2, -1, 0, +1, +2\}$ depends on the bits $(b_{2i+1}, b_{2i}, b_{2i-1})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_i = b_{2i-1} + b_{2i} - 2 b_{2i+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This reduces the number of partial products from 32 to 16.

**Step 3 — Modified Booth (used in ARM)**: The recoding is applied iteratively, processing **2 bits at a time** with a 1-bit overlap. For 32-bit operands, the iteration count is $n/2 = 16$ cycles (in the iterative implementation) or 16 parallel stages (in the combinational implementation).

**Step 4 — ARM7 Implementation Choice**: The ARM7TDMI chose an **iterative 4-cycle** design (each cycle computes 8 partial products via 2 levels of Booth recoding), giving a 32×32-bit multiply in **4 clock cycles** with significantly less hardware than a single-cycle 32-PP array.

**Step 5 — Cycle-by-cycle computation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycle 1: } &\text{Compute PP[0..7]  for bits 0..15 of } B, \text{ accumulate partial sum } S_1.\\
\text{Cycle 2: } &\text{Compute PP[8..15] for bits 16..31 of } B, \text{ combine with } S_1 \to S_2.\\
\text{Cycle 3: } &\text{Final carry-save add to compress } S_2.\\
\text{Cycle 4: } &\text{Vectored carry-propagate add to produce the 64-bit result.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total hardware cost is approximately 1/8 of the single-cycle array, at the price of 4× the latency.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Computing CPI for an Instruction Sequence

**Problem (KTU-style):** Consider the following ARM7 instruction sequence. Calculate the total cycles required and the CPI. Assume: data-processing = 1 EX cycle, load (LDR) = 2 EX cycles, branch = 3 EX cycles, and assume one load-use hazard stall is introduced after the LDR.

```
ADD R0, R1, R2     ; (1) data-processing
LDR R3, [R0, #16]  ; (2) load — uses R0 from (1)
SUB R4, R3, R5     ; (3) data-processing — needs value of R3 (1-cycle stall)
B   loop           ; (4) branch — 2-cycle flush penalty
```

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Instruction 1 (ADD):} &\quad 1 \text{ EX cycle} \\
\text{Instruction 2 (LDR):} &\quad 2 \text{ EX cycles} \\
\text{Instruction 3 (SUB):} &\quad 1 \text{ EX cycle} + 1 \text{ stall cycle} = 2 \\
\text{Instruction 4 (B):} &\quad 1 \text{ EX cycle} + 2 \text{ branch-penalty cycles} = 3 \\
\text{Total cycles} &= 1 + 2 + 2 + 3 = 8 \\
\text{Total instructions} &= 4 \\
\text{CPI} &= \frac{8}{4} = 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on pipeline flushing:** The 2-cycle branch penalty on ARM7 is *not* two empty cycles after the branch; rather, it is the two instructions that were fetched into F and D stages of the branch instruction that must be discarded and replaced with NOPs (or the target's instructions). On ARM9, branch prediction reduces this to **1 cycle** penalty.

### 3.4 Analytical Proof — Why R15 Reads as `PC + 8`

**Step 1 — Define the pipeline state**: Let $I_k$ be the instruction at address $4k$. The ARM7 3-stage pipeline is such that at any given clock edge:

- The F stage holds $I_{PC/4}$ (the instruction at the current PC).
- The D stage holds $I_{(PC/4) - 1}$ (the previously fetched instruction).
- The E stage holds $I_{(PC/4) - 2}$ (the instruction decoded two cycles ago).

**Step 2 — When R15 is read as Rn in a data-processing instruction in the E stage**: The current PC value has been incremented twice (once at the end of F-stage when the instruction was fetched, and once again in the next F-stage). The PC is therefore $4 \cdot (n+2) = \text{PC at fetch} + 8$.

**Step 3 — Conclusion**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{value\_read} &= \text{PC} + 8
\end{aligned}
$$

This 8-byte offset is the **architectural pipeline visibility** — an unavoidable consequence of the 3-stage design that compilers must respect (e.g., position-independent code uses `ADR` pseudoinstructions that account for this offset).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ARM7TDMI 3-Stage Pipeline Datapath — Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the canonical ARM7TDMI datapath, as expected in KTU 14-mark questions. It traces the flow of an instruction through Fetch → Decode → Execute, identifying all the major hardware blocks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["ADDRESS BUS A BUS B BUS"]:::mem
    B0["INSTRUCTION MEMORY"]:::mem
    C0["INSTRUCTION REGISTER IR"]:::reg
    D0["INSTRUCTION DECODER"]:::logic
    E0["CONTROL UNIT"]:::logic
    F0["REGISTER BANK R0 to R15 37x32 bit"]:::reg
    G0["BARREL SHIFTER LSL LSR ASR ROR RRX"]:::logic
    H0["ALU 32 bit ADD SUB AND ORR EOR BIC"]:::logic
    I0["MULTIPLIER 32x32 ITERATIVE 4 cycle"]:::logic
    J0["ADDRESS REGISTER AND INCREMENTER"]:::reg
    K0["DATA MEMORY LOAD STORE"]:::mem
    L0["WRITE BACK BUS TO Rd"]:::bus
    M0["PROGRAM COUNTER PC R15"]:::reg
    N0["APSR FLAGS N Z C V"]:::reg

    A0 --> B0
    B0 --> C0
    C0 --> D0
    D0 --> E0
    E0 --> F0
    E0 --> G0
    E0 --> H0
    E0 --> I0
    F0 -- A bus operand1 Rn --> H0
    G0 -- shifted operand2 Rm --> H0
    H0 -- result --> L0
    L0 --> F0
    M0 --> J0
    J0 --> A0
    E0 -- branch target --> M0
    H0 -- flags --> N0
    E0 -- address out --> K0
    K0 -- data in --> L0
    F0 -. uses .- M0
    E0 -- cond check --> N0

    classDef mem fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#6c0000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for KTU:** Trace the **A bus** (operand 1) from the register bank to the ALU; the **B bus** (operand 2) flows from the register bank → barrel shifter → ALU. The ALU result is fed onto the **writeback bus** to the register bank's `Rw` port. The PC is incremented by the address register + incrementer, and the **condition flags (APSR)** are written from the ALU output when the `S` bit is set.

### 4.2 Pipeline Timing Diagram — Instruction Sequence Through 3-Stage Pipeline

The following timing diagram shows how 4 instructions overlap across the 3 pipeline stages, and the position of a branch penalty.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Cycle as Clock Cycle
    participant F as Stage F Fetch
    participant D as Stage D Decode
    participant E as Stage E Execute
    Note over F,E: Cycle 1
    F->>F: I1 fetched PC 0x00
    Note over F,E: Cycle 2
    F->>F: I2 fetched PC 0x04
    D->>D: I1 decoded
    Note over F,E: Cycle 3
    F->>F: I3 fetched PC 0x08
    D->>D: I2 decoded
    E->>E: I1 executed Rd written
    Note over F,E: Cycle 4
    F->>F: I4 fetched PC 0x0C
    D->>D: I3 decoded
    E->>E: I2 executed Rd written
    Note over F,E: Cycle 5
    F->>F: I5 BRANCH target fetch
    D->>D: I4 decoded
    E->>E: I3 executed Rd written
    Note over F,E: Cycle 6
    F->>F: I6 flushed NOP
    D->>D: I5 BRANCH decoded target committed to PC
    E->>E: I4 executed Rd written
    Note over F,E: Cycle 7
    F->>F: I7 flushed NOP
    D->>D: I6 NOP
    E->>E: I5 BRANCH executed PC loaded
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Interpretation:** Notice how at Cycle 6 and 7, two instructions (the ones behind the branch in F and D) are **flushed** and the pipeline is filled with NOPs. This is the **2-cycle branch penalty** of the ARM7. A more advanced architecture (ARM9 + branch prediction) would speculatively fetch the branch target and only flush if the branch is *not* taken.

### 4.3 Modified Booth's Multiplier — Recoding Map

The following decision table summarizes the Modified Booth's recoding used in the ARM multiplier. The triplet $(b_{i+1}, b_i, b_{i-1})$ maps to a recoded digit $d \in \{-2, -1, 0, +1, +2\}$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0["Triplet b2i plus 1, b2i, b2i minus 1"]:::logic
    T1{"Case 0 0 0 -> d = 0"}:::logic
    T2{"Case 0 0 1 -> d = plus 1"}:::logic
    T3{"Case 0 1 0 -> d = plus 1"}:::logic
    T4{"Case 0 1 1 -> d = plus 2"}:::logic
    T5{"Case 1 0 0 -> d = minus 2"}:::logic
    T6{"Case 1 0 1 -> d = minus 1"}:::logic
    T7{"Case 1 1 0 -> d = minus 1"}:::logic
    T8{"Case 1 1 1 -> d = 0"}:::logic
    T9["Apply recoded digit as A shifted left 2i positions"]:::logic
    T10["Accumulate into 64 bit result"]:::logic

    T0 --> T1
    T0 --> T2
    T0 --> T3
    T0 --> T4
    T0 --> T5
    T0 --> T6
    T0 --> T7
    T0 --> T8
    T1 --> T9
    T2 --> T9
    T3 --> T9
    T4 --> T9
    T5 --> T9
    T6 --> T9
    T7 --> T9
    T8 --> T9
    T9 --> T10

    classDef logic fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4a235a,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *triplet* of three consecutive bits of the multiplier $B$ is examined; based on the case, a recoded digit is generated. Notice that cases `000` and `111` both yield `d = 0` — this is the *run-length compression* effect of Modified Booth's, and is the reason runs of 1's or 0's in $B$ are efficiently skipped. The 2× and +1/-1 partial products require only a *barrel-shifted version* of $A$ (no carry-propagation), which the ARM barrel shifter handles in a single combinational pass.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Explain the role of the **Barrel Shifter** in the ARM processor datapath. Why is it considered cycle-free, and how does it contribute to ARM's code density?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Barrel Shifter** is a combinational hardware unit in the ARM datapath that performs shift and rotate operations (LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX) on the second operand of every data-processing instruction. It is **cycle-free** because it is a pure combinational network — the shifted output is produced in the same clock cycle as the register read, without consuming any extra cycle. It contributes to ARM's code density because the shift amount can be *embedded* in the instruction word (bits [11:0]), allowing a single ARM instruction (e.g., `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #4`) to perform what would otherwise require an explicit `SHL` + `ADD` sequence in other ISAs. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for combinational nature, 1 for code-density benefit.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

When R15 (PC) is used as a source operand in an ARM data-processing instruction in **ARM state**, what value is read, and why does this differ from the address of the currently-executing instruction?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In ARM state, when R15 is read as a source operand, the value returned is **`PC + 8`**, where PC is the address of the currently-executing instruction. This is **8 bytes ahead** (i.e., 2 instructions ahead) of the executing instruction's address. The reason is the **3-stage pipeline** of ARM7TDMI: by the time the data-processing instruction is in the Execute (E) stage, the PC has already been incremented twice (once during its own Fetch, and once during the Fetch of the next instruction). This architectural pipeline visibility is a defining characteristic of ARM and must be respected by position-independent code. (3 marks: 1 for value, 1 for pipeline reason, 1 for impact on code.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an internal choice. The structure below provides **Question A** and **Question B** as the two alternatives, each split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **ARM7TDMI 3-stage pipeline datapath**. Identify the major functional blocks and describe the operations performed in each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A code segment on ARM7TDMI is given below. Assuming 1-cycle data-processing, 2-cycle load, and a 2-cycle branch penalty, compute the **total cycles** and the **CPI** for the sequence. Also identify any data hazards and pipeline stalls. **(7 marks)**

```asm
MOV   R0, #5          ; I1
LDR   R1, [R0, #0x20] ; I2
ADD   R2, R1, R0      ; I3
SUB   R3, R2, #1      ; I4
B     END_LOOP        ; I5
ADD   R4, R0, R0      ; I6 (flushed)
END_LOOP:
STR   R2, [R0, #0x40] ; I7
```

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

The ARM7TDMI 3-stage pipeline consists of the following three stages:

1. **Fetch (F) stage** — The address of the next instruction is held in the **PC (R15)**. The PC is presented to the **Address Register and Incrementer**, which drives the **A bus** to the **Instruction Memory**. The 32-bit instruction word is latched into the **Instruction Register (IR)**. The PC is incremented by **+4** (since instructions are 32-bit / 4-byte aligned). (2 marks)

2. **Decode (D) stage** — The instruction word is fed to the **Instruction Decoder** and **Control Unit**. The decoder identifies the instruction class from bits [27:25] and the condition from bits [31:28]. The **Register Bank** is read using bits [19:16] (Rn → A bus) and [15:12] (Rm → B bus). The Barrel Shifter is configured based on bits [11:0]. For branches, the target PC is computed in this stage using the offset and the condition flags. (2 marks)

3. **Execute (E) stage** — The ALU operates on the A-bus operand and the shifted B-bus operand. The result is driven onto the **Writeback Bus** to be latched into Rd (specified by bits [15:12]) at the next clock edge. For loads/stores, the ALU computes the effective address. For branches, the PC is loaded with the target computed in D. (2 marks)

A **block diagram** should be drawn showing: PC → A bus → Instruction Memory → IR → Decoder → Control Unit → Register Bank + Barrel Shifter → ALU → Writeback → Rd. (1 mark for diagram.)

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify instruction types and base cycle counts:**

- I1 (MOV): data-processing → 1 EX cycle.
- I2 (LDR): load → 2 EX cycles.
- I3 (ADD): data-processing → 1 EX cycle (but needs R1 from I2, so **1-cycle stall**).
- I4 (SUB): data-processing → 1 EX cycle.
- I5 (B): branch → 1 EX cycle + 2-cycle penalty (flush I6).
- I7 (STR): store → 2 EX cycles.

**Step 2 — Build the cycle-by-cycle table:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inst} &\quad \text{Stage F} \quad \text{Stage D} \quad \text{Stage E} \quad \text{Notes} \\
\text{I1 MOV}   & \quad 1 \quad 2 \quad 3 \quad - \\
\text{I2 LDR}   & \quad 2 \quad 3 \quad 4{-}5 \quad \text{2 EX cycles} \\
\text{I3 ADD}   & \quad 3 \quad 4 \quad \text{6 (stall in 5)} \quad \text{1-cycle load-use stall} \\
\text{I4 SUB}   & \quad 4 \quad 5 \quad 7 \quad - \\
\text{I5 B}     & \quad 5 \quad 6 \quad 8 \quad - \\
\text{I6 ADD}   & \quad 6 \quad 7 \quad \text{flushed} \quad \text{branch penalty} \\
\text{I7 STR}   & \quad 8 \quad 9 \quad 10{-}11 \quad \text{2 EX cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

(3 marks for the table.)

**Step 3 — Total cycles and CPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total cycles} &= 11 \\
\text{Total retired instructions} &= 6 \; (\text{I6 flushed}) \\
\text{CPI} &= \frac{11}{6} \approx 1.83
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks for calculation.)

**Step 4 — Hazards identified:** (2 marks for hazards.)

- **Data hazard (Read-After-Write)**: I3 (ADD R2, R1, ...) needs R1 produced by I2 (LDR). This is a **load-use hazard** that cannot be resolved by forwarding (LDR's data is not available until the *end* of I2's second EX cycle). A **1-cycle bubble** is inserted in the E stage of I3.
- **Control hazard**: I5 (B) causes a 2-cycle branch penalty; I6 is flushed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 3, Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **Modified Booth's Multiplication Algorithm** as used in the ARM processor. How many partial products are generated, and what is the latency of the iterative multiplier on ARM7? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **ARM7TDMI 3-stage pipeline** with the **ARM9TDMI 5-stage pipeline**. Discuss the CPI improvements, branch penalty differences, and clock frequency scaling enabled by the deeper pipeline. Use a numerical example showing the speedup. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

The **Modified Booth's Algorithm** is a radix-4 recoding scheme that processes the multiplier operand **2 bits at a time** with a 1-bit overlap. For 32-bit operands, this reduces the number of partial products from **32** (schoolbook) to **16** (one per overlapping bit pair). (2 marks)

The recoding examines each triplet $(b_{2i+1}, b_i, b_{2i-1})$ of consecutive bits in the multiplier $B$ and maps it to a recoded digit $d_i \in \{-2, -1, 0, +1, +2\}$. The mapping is:

| Triplet | $d_i$ | Operation |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 000 | 0 | Add nothing |
| 001 | +1 | Add $A \cdot 2^{2i}$ |
| 010 | +1 | Add $A \cdot 2^{2i}$ |
| 011 | +2 | Add $2A \cdot 2^{2i}$ |
| 100 | -2 | Subtract $2A \cdot 2^{2i}$ |
| 101 | -1 | Subtract $A \cdot 2^{2i}$ |
| 110 | -1 | Subtract $A \cdot 2^{2i}$ |
| 111 | 0 | Add nothing |

(2 marks for the table.)

The recoded digit determines the partial product, which is a left-shifted version of the multiplicand $A$ (the shift and sign-extension are handled by the **barrel shifter** in hardware). The 16 partial products are accumulated using a **carry-save adder tree** followed by a final **vectored carry-propagate adder**. (1 mark.)

The ARM7TDMI implements an **iterative 4-cycle** design, where each cycle processes 8 partial products, yielding a **4-clock-cycle latency** for a 32×32-bit MUL. (1 mark.)

The **MLA** (multiply-accumulate) instruction adds 1 extra cycle for the addition, totaling **5 cycles**. (1 mark.)

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Stage breakdown comparison:** (1 mark for table.)

| Pipeline | Stages | Stage Names |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| ARM7TDMI | 3 | Fetch, Decode, Execute |
| ARM9TDMI | 5 | Fetch, Decode, Execute, Memory, Writeback |

**Step 2 — Branch penalty differences:** (2 marks.)

- **ARM7**: Branch is resolved in the **D stage**; instructions in F and D behind the branch must be flushed → **2-cycle branch penalty**.
- **ARM9**: Memory and Writeback stages are split out, freeing the D stage to compute the branch earlier. Branch is resolved in the **E stage** with a static branch predictor predicting *not-taken*. Penalty is **1 cycle** (only the F-stage instruction needs to be flushed).

**Step 3 — CPI comparison (numerical example):** (2 marks.)

Consider a benchmark with 20% loads, 10% branches, 70% data-processing, on a 10-instruction sequence.

- **ARM7 (3-stage) cycles**:
  $$
  \begin{aligned}
  N_{\text{cycles}} &= 7 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot (1+1) + 1 \cdot (1+2) \\
  &= 7 + 4 + 3 = 14 \\
  \text{CPI}_{\text{ARM7}} &= 1.4
  \end{aligned}
  $$
- **ARM9 (5-stage) cycles**:
  $$
  \begin{aligned}
  N_{\text{cycles}} &= 7 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot (1+1) + 1 \cdot (1+1) \\
  &= 7 + 4 + 2 = 13 \\
  \text{CPI}_{\text{ARM9}} &= 1.3
  \end{aligned}
  $$

**Step 4 — Clock frequency scaling and speedup:** (2 marks.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } f_{\text{ARM9}} &= 1.5 \cdot f_{\text{ARM7}} \\
\text{Execution time ratio} &= \frac{\text{CPI}_{\text{ARM7}} \cdot T_{\text{ARM7}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{ARM9}} \cdot T_{\text{ARM9}}} = \frac{1.4 \cdot T}{1.3 \cdot T/1.5} = \frac{1.4 \cdot 1.5}{1.3} \approx 1.62 \\
\text{ARM9 speedup} &\approx 1.62\times
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake 1**: Forgetting that the **D stage computes the branch target** on ARM7 (not the E stage), leading to the 2-cycle penalty. Many students wrongly attribute 3 cycles to the branch.
> - **Common mistake 2**: Confusing **R15 read value** (PC + 8 in ARM state, PC + 4 in THUMB state) with the **R15 write value** (the value *being written* is loaded into PC).
> - **Common mistake 3**: In CPI questions, students often compute cycles per *stage* instead of cycles per *instruction*. The pipeline **overlaps** stages; the *lifetime* of an instruction in the pipeline is 3 cycles, but the *retirement rate* is 1 instruction per cycle (excluding stalls/penalties).
> - **Common mistake 4**: For the Modified Booth's algorithm, students sometimes claim it uses "fewer cycles but more hardware". This is correct only when the algorithm is *unrolled* into a combinational 16-stage tree. The ARM7's *iterative* implementation uses 4 cycles with *less* hardware.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for ARM Implementation (Module 3):**

- **ARM7TDMI is a 3-stage pipeline**: Fetch (F) → Decode (D) → Execute (E). The next two stages, Memory (M) and Writeback (W), are *not* separate — they are folded into E. This is the *root cause* of the 2-cycle branch penalty.
- **RISC fundamentals**: Load-store architecture, fixed 32-bit instructions, 37×32-bit register file (31 general + 6 status, banked for 7 modes), 32-bit ALU.
- **R15 (PC) alias**: Reads as `PC + 8` in ARM state, `PC + 4` in THUMB state. This is a *direct consequence* of pipelining — it is not a bug.
- **Barrel Shifter**: A combinational (zero-cycle) hardware that performs LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX on operand 2 of every data-processing instruction. Single biggest contributor to ARM's code density.
- **Multiplication**: Modified Booth's algorithm produces **16 partial products** from 32-bit operands. ARM7's iterative multiplier takes **4 cycles** (MUL) or **5 cycles** (MLA).
- **Branch penalty on ARM7**: **2 cycles** (flush F and D). On ARM9: **1 cycle** (only flush F).
- **Load-use hazard**: A load followed immediately by a use of the loaded register introduces a **1-cycle stall** (the data is not available until the *end* of the load's execute stage).
- **Endianness**: ARM is *bi-endian* — configurable as Big or Little. Most modern systems use **Little-Endian** by default.
- **Pipeline flushing**: Achieved by forcing NOPs into the F and D stages, or by the control unit asserting the `flush` signal to clear the pipeline latches.
- **ARM7 vs ARM9 vs ARM11**: 3-stage → 5-stage → 8-stage. Each generation allows higher clock frequency at the cost of more complex hazard management and branch prediction.
- **CPI formula for a sequence**: $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{Total cycles (including stalls and penalties)}}{\text{Number of retired instructions}}$.
- **Modified Booth's recoding table** (8 cases): $000 \to 0$, $001 \to +1$, $010 \to +1$, $011 \to +2$, $100 \to -2$, $101 \to -1$, $110 \to -1$, $111 \to 0$. **Memorize this table** for KTU ESE.
- **THUMB vs ARM state**: THUMB uses 16-bit compressed instructions, halves code size, and has reduced addressing modes. R15 reads as `PC + 4` in THUMB state.
- **APSR flags**: N (Negative), Z (Zero), C (Carry), V (oVerflow). Updated only when the `S` bit in the instruction is set.
- **Condition codes (`cond`)**: 16 values (EQ, NE, CS, CC, MI, PL, VS, VC, HI, LS, GE, LT, GT, LE, AL, NV). `NV` is reserved; `AL` (1110) is unconditional.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The ARM Coprocessor Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The ARM Coprocessor Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **ARM Coprocessor Interface** is a standardized, tightly-coupled hardware extension mechanism defined by ARM Ltd., that allows external dedicated processing units — called **coprocessors** — to share the ARM core's instruction stream, register context, and data bus through a set of well-defined instruction formats and handshaking signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBEVT504 – Module 3):**
> The ARM Coprocessor Interface is a bus protocol that allows a coprocessor (such as CP15 for system control, or a floating-point unit) to extend the ARM instruction set. The interface supports up to **16 logical coprocessors (CP0–CP15)**, seven dedicated coprocessor instruction classes, and a busy-waiting handshaking protocol for coprocessors that cannot complete operations in a single pipeline cycle.

The interface is **instruction-level coupled** rather than **bus-level coupled**: the coprocessor does not fetch its own instructions; instead, the ARM core fetches every instruction from memory, the coprocessor snoops the fetched instruction bus, and if the instruction is addressed to it, the coprocessor participates in the execution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Specialist Colleague

Imagine the **ARM core** as a **generalist doctor** in a busy hospital ward, and each **coprocessor** as a **specialist consultant** (a cardiologist, a radiologist, a neurologist). 

- The generalist (ARM) walks down the ward reading the case file (instruction stream).
- Every now and then, he reaches a line in the case file that says *"Take an X-ray"*, *"Compute a 64-bit floating-point divide"*, or *"Configure the memory protection unit"*.
- The generalist does not know how to do these things — he calls the **specialist (coprocessor)** over.
- The specialist reads the same case file line, performs the specialist work using his own private instruments (coprocessor register bank), and then hands the result back through a shared clipboard (MCR/MRC register transfer) or simply writes it into the patient's chart (CDP internal operation).
- If the specialist is **busy** (e.g., the FPU is still doing a long division), he raises a small **"Wait" flag (CPB – Coprocessor Busy)** so the generalist pauses (pipeline stalled) until the specialist is free.
- If the required specialist is **not present in the hospital**, the generalist raises an **"Undefined Instruction" exception**, and a software handler is called (perhaps to emulate the coprocessor in software).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The coprocessor does not increase the instruction bandwidth or modify the core's pipeline structure — it merely extends the *semantic* capability of the ISA. This is a clean, modular hardware design philosophy: keep the core simple, attach what you need.

### 1.3 Classification of Coprocessors

ARM supports up to **16 logical coprocessors**, numbered **CP0 to CP15**, each with a distinct role:

| Coprocessor ID | Typical Use | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **CP10 / CP11** | VFP (Vector Floating Point) | Single & double precision IEEE 754 floating-point |
| **CP14** | Debug / Trace | JTAG-style debug communication channel |
| **CP15** | System Control | MMU, caches, TCM, TLB, protection regions |
| **CP0–CP9, CP12, CP13** | Reserved / Custom | Available for OEM-specific accelerators (DSP, crypto, etc.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The interface is *logical* and *abstract*: not all 16 coprocessors need to physically exist in silicon. The ARM core will treat any coprocessor as "absent" if it does not assert its handshake signal `CPA` (Coprocessor Absent) appropriately, and trap to the Undefined Instruction handler.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Metrics (KTU Board Relevant)

- **Maximum logical coprocessors:** **16** (CP0 – CP15)
- **Coprocessor instruction classes:** **7** (CDP, LDC, STC, MCR, MRC, MCRR, MRRC)
- **ARM register operands per transfer:** **1** (MCR/MRC) or **2** (MCRR/MRRC)
- **Coprocessor internal register operand fields:** **3** (`CRn`, `CRd`, `CRm` with `op2`)
- **Pipeline stall handshake line:** **`CPB` (Coprocessor Busy)**
- **Coprocessor absence signal:** **`CPA` (Coprocessor Absent)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Coprocessor Instruction Classes

The ARM architecture reserves a contiguous set of **opcodes (bits [27:20])** in the ARM 32-bit instruction space purely for coprocessor operations. These seven classes are:

| Class | Mnemonic | Full Name | Direction of Data Flow |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1** | `CDP` | **C**o**p**rocessor **D**ata **P**rocessing | Coprocessor-internal (ARM regs untouched) |
| **2** | `MCR` | **M**ove to **C**oproc from **R**egister (ARM) | ARM register → Coprocessor register |
| **3** | `MRC` | **M**ove to ARM **R**egister from **C**oproc | Coprocessor register → ARM register |
| **4** | `LDC` | **L**oad to **C**oproc from memory | Memory → Coprocessor register |
| **5** | `STC` | **S**tore from **C**oproc to memory | Coprocessor register → Memory |
| **6** | `MCRR` | **M**ove to **C**oproc from **R**egisters (×2) | Two ARM regs → Two Coproc regs |
| **7** | `MRRC` | **M**ove to ARM **R**egisters from Coproc (×2) | Two Coproc regs → Two ARM regs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The mnemonic letter order encodes the data flow. `MCR` reads as "**M**ove **C**oproc, from **R**egister" → ARM-register-to-Coproc. `MRC` reads as "**M**ove **R**egister, from **C**oproc" → Coproc-to-ARM-register. A common student pitfall is to confuse `MCR` and `MRC`.

### 2.2 The Generic 32-bit Coprocessor Instruction Encoding

All seven coprocessor instructions share a common decode "skeleton" in bits [27:24]. The fields below describe the general layout for **CDP / MCR / MRC / LDC / STC**:

```
Bits:  [31:28] [27:24] [23:21] [20] [19:16] [15:12] [11:8] [7:5] [4] [3:0]
Field: cond    class    CP#    M    opcode   CRn      CRd     CRm   T   op2
```

#### Detailed field semantics:

- **`cond` [31:28] — Condition Code:** Standard ARM condition (e.g., `EQ`, `NE`, `AL` for unconditional). If the condition is false, the coprocessor is *not even signalled* — the instruction is silently skipped. This applies to **all** coprocessor instructions.
- **`class` [27:24] — Instruction Class:** One of the 7 values shown in §2.1.
- **`CP#` [23:21] — Coprocessor Number:** Selects which of the 16 coprocessors (CP0–CP15) executes the instruction.
- **`M` [20] — Mode Bit (LDC/STC only):** Long-transfer control.
  - `M = 0` → transfer 1 word (4 bytes)
  - `M = 1` → transfer multiple consecutive coprocessor registers
- **`opcode` [19:16] — Coprocessor Operation:** Operation semantics defined by the specific coprocessor.
- **`CRn` [15:12] — Coprocessor Register n:** Source or destination coprocessor register.
- **`CRd` [11:8] — Coprocessor Register d:** Source or destination coprocessor register.
- **`CRm` [7:5] — Coprocessor Register m:** Additional source or destination coprocessor register.
- **`T` [4] — Operand Type Flag:** Most coprocessors ignore this; some use it to indicate a 32-bit or 64-bit operand.
- **`op2` [3:0] — Coprocessor-specific Operand 2:** Extra qualifier passed to the coprocessor.

> [!NOTE]
> The fields `CRn`, `CRd`, `CRm`, and `op2` are not interpreted by the ARM core — they are **opaque to the core** and interpreted entirely by the coprocessor. This is what makes the interface *future-proof*: ARM Ltd. can add new coprocessors (e.g., a new CP9) without changing the core.

### 2.3 The Two-Operand Variants — MCRR and MRRC

These are *dual-register* transfer instructions introduced in ARMv5TE for 64-bit datapath operations. Their encoding occupies a different region of the instruction space (class bits [27:24] are different from the single-register variants):

```
Bits:  [31:28] [27:24] [23:21] [20:16] [15:12] [11:8] [7:4] [3:0]
Field: cond    1100/   CP#    opcode  Rd       Rn      CRm   op2
               1101
```

- `MCRR` (class = `1100`): Moves two ARM registers (`Rd`, `Rn`) into two coprocessor registers in `CRm` (a paired 64-bit destination).
- `MRRC` (class = `1101`): Moves two coprocessor registers (in `CRm`) into two ARM registers (`Rd`, `Rn`).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** MCRR/MRRC are *easier* than MCR/MRC because they do not have a `CRd` field — the dual pair is encoded into `CRm` directly. KTU frequently asks to "differentiate MCR/MRC from MCRR/MRRC" in 3-mark questions.

### 2.4 The Coprocessor Interface — Pipeline & Handshaking

The ARM coprocessor interface is **pipeline-coupled** and uses **four dedicated handshake signals** between the ARM core and the coprocessor:

| Signal | Direction | Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **`nCPI`** | ARM → Coprocessor | (negated) **Coprocessor Instruction.** Asserted LOW in the Execute stage when the instruction is for that coprocessor. |
| **`CPA`** | Coprocessor → ARM | **Coprocessor Absent.** If HIGH, the addressed coprocessor is not present → Undefined Instruction trap. |
| **`CPB`** | Coprocessor → ARM | **Coprocessor Busy.** If HIGH, the coprocessor needs the ARM core to stall the pipeline (e.g., waiting on a multi-cycle FPU divide). |
| **`CPRDATA` / `CPWDATA`** | Bidirectional | Coprocessor Read/Write data buses — used to ferry operands for MCR/MRC. |

#### The pipeline interlock sequence (for a multi-cycle coprocessor):

1. ARM fetches the coprocessor instruction and decodes it in the **Decode** stage.
2. In the **Execute** stage, ARM places the instruction on the coprocessor instruction bus and asserts **`nCPI` LOW**.
3. The coprocessor recognizes itself, but its data unit is busy. It asserts **`CPB` HIGH**.
4. The ARM core **freezes the pipeline** (Decode and Execute stages stall) — Fetch is *not* stalled because the next instruction must still be pre-fetched into the prefetch buffer.
5. When the coprocessor finishes, it de-asserts `CPB`. The ARM core completes the Execute stage and proceeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** When `CPB` is asserted, the **prefetch unit is *not* stalled** — only the **Decode and Execute stages** are stalled. The prefetch buffer continues to be filled because the next instruction may not be a coprocessor instruction and is safe to bring in.

### 2.5 Privileged Coprocessor Instructions

For ARM cores that have privileged modes (Supervisor, Abort, IRQ, FIQ, Undefined, System), coprocessor instructions can be classified as:

- **User-mode accessible** — Executable in any mode. The coprocessor instruction is *always* checked against the current CPSR mode bits.
- **Privileged-only** — If executed in User mode, the coprocessor raises an exception back to the ARM core, which jumps to the Undefined Instruction vector. This is how **CP15 (system control)** is protected.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **CP15 c1 (Control Register)** operations like enabling the MMU are privileged. A user-mode application writing to CP15 c1 will trap to the OS kernel, which decides whether to honor the request.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Item | Value / Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Max coprocessors | $2^4 = 16$ | Encoded in 4 bits (`CP#`) |
| 2 | Coprocessor classes | $7$ | CDP, MCR, MRC, LDC, STC, MCRR, MRRC |
| 3 | Condition code width | $4 \text{ bits}$ | `0xE` = always, `0xF` = never |
| 4 | `nCPI` polarity | **Active LOW** | Name prefix `n` indicates negation |
| 5 | Pipeline stall by `CPB` | Decode + Execute stalled | **Fetch continues** |
| 6 | `CPA = 1` consequence | Undefined Instruction exception | Software may emulate |
| 7 | `M` bit long transfer | `M=0`: 1 word, `M=1`: multi-word | LDC/STC only |
| 8 | MCRR/MRRC operand count | 2 ARM regs $\leftrightarrow$ 2 coproc regs | 64-bit datapath |
| 9 | Privileged protection | CPSR mode $\geq$ Supervisor | User-mode traps to Undef vector |
| 10 | `CRn` field width | $4 \text{ bits}$ → 16 coproc registers | Per coproc register file |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The coprocessor interface is the *backbone* of every system-on-chip (SoC) you have ever used:

- **ARM Cortex-A series** chips use **CP15** for L1/L2 cache control, MMU, and branch prediction configuration.
- **Apple M1/M2 series** uses **CP10/CP11 (VFP/NEON)** for floating-point and SIMD acceleration, offloading from the main pipeline.
- **DSP co-processors** in audio codecs and modems use coprocessor slots for fast convolution and FFT operations.
- **Crypto engines** in secure-element chips use reserved coprocessor numbers for AES/SHA acceleration.

> [!NOTE]
> The interface is *one of the most enduring* ARM design features — it survived from the original ARM2 in 1987 to the present-day Cortex-A78, even though the actual *bus* on which the coprocessor hangs has evolved (AMBA, AXI, CHI).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — How `CP#` maps to a 4-bit address space

**Goal:** Prove that the ARM coprocessor field width of 3 bits (bits [23:21]) and the MCRR/MRRC variant's opcode field combine to address **all 16** coprocessors.

> [!NOTE]
> Although some sources cite 4 bits for `CP#`, the actual encoding is **3 bits** (bits [23:21]) for the standard 5 classes (CDP, MCR, MRC, LDC, STC), and **4 bits** (bits [23:20]) for MCRR/MRRC. This is a common KTU question.

#### Step 1 — Standard 5 classes (CDP, MCR, MRC, LDC, STC)

For these classes, the `CP#` field occupies bits **[23:21]**, giving a width of:

$$
w_{CP\#} = 23 - 21 + 1 = 3 \text{ bits}
$$

The number of distinct coprocessors addressable is:

$$
N_{CP} = 2^{w_{CP\#}} = 2^3 = 8
$$

So, with the standard 5 classes, **CP0 through CP7** are directly addressable.

#### Step 2 — MCRR and MRRC dual-register variants

In MCRR/MRRC, the class bits [27:24] are `1100` or `1101`, and the `CP#` field expands to bits **[23:20]**, giving:

$$
w_{CP\#}^{64} = 23 - 20 + 1 = 4 \text{ bits}
$$

The number of distinct coprocessors addressable is:

$$
N_{CP}^{64} = 2^{w_{CP\#}^{64}} = 2^4 = 16
$$

So, **all 16 coprocessors (CP0–CP15)** are addressable via MCRR/MRRC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conclusion:** Although the interface supports 16 coprocessors in the *complete* ISA sense, the older ARMv4 and earlier architectures only had 8 directly addressable coprocessors. ARMv5TE (and later) extended this to 16 by reusing the freed `M` bit position for the coprocessor number in MCRR/MRRC.

#### Step 3 — Summary

$$
\boxed{
N_{CP} = \begin{cases}
2^3 = 8, & \text{for CDP / MCR / MRC / LDC / STC} \\
2^4 = 16, & \text{for MCRR / MRRC}
\end{cases}
}
$$

This explains why ARMv4 system-on-chips like the StrongARM-110 only had CP0–CP7 physically wired, while ARMv5TE and later chips (XScale, ARM926EJ-S, Cortex) have CP0–CP15.

### 3.2 Derivation — Instruction-Bit Budget

We can *derive* the total bit budget consumed by coprocessor instructions as a fraction of the entire ARM 32-bit instruction space.

#### Step 1 — Total possible 32-bit values

$$
N_{total} = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296
$$

#### Step 2 — Bit budget consumed by `class` field

The `class` field is 4 bits wide. For each class, the remaining 28 bits are partially consumed by fixed fields. But conservatively, the *full 32-bit space* is partitioned by the `class` field into:

$$
N_{classes} = 2^4 = 16
$$

So the coprocessor instruction space occupies approximately:

$$
\text{Fraction}_{\text{coproc}} = \frac{N_{\text{used classes}}}{N_{classes}} = \frac{7}{16}
$$

#### Step 3 — Effective instruction subspace

$$
\text{Coproc subspace} \approx \frac{7}{16} \times 2^{32} = 7 \times 2^{28} = 1{,}879{,}048{,}192 \text{ distinct 32-bit coprocessor instructions}
$$

This is more than enough to encode **hundreds of coprocessor operations per coprocessor** for the foreseeable future, which is why the coprocessor interface has never needed to be replaced despite ARM cores evolving from ARMv4 to ARMv9.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Instruction

| Goal | Best Instruction | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Compute something *entirely inside* the coprocessor (e.g., VFP `FADD`) | **`CDP`** | No need to disturb ARM registers; stays within coprocessor register file |
| Move a *single 32-bit value* from ARM `R0` to coproc register `CR0` | **`MCR`** | Minimum overhead; single ARM register involved |
| Read a *single 32-bit value* from coproc register `CR1` into ARM `R0` | **`MRC`** | Read-back, e.g., reading CP15 c0 (CPU ID) |
| Bulk-load coproc register file from a memory buffer (e.g., 8-word VFP vector) | **`LDC`** with `M = 1` | Multi-word transfer in one instruction |
| Bulk-store coproc register file to memory | **`STC`** with `M = 1` | Dump context during context switch |
| Transfer a *64-bit value* (e.g., double-precision float in two 32-bit regs) | **`MCRR`** / **`MRRC`** | Atomic 64-bit transfer; no torn reads |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Decoding an MCR Instruction

> **[Example Problem]:** Decode the following 32-bit ARM machine code word as a coprocessor instruction:
> `0xEE123F10`

#### Step 1 — Convert hex to binary (32 bits)

$$
0x\text{EE123F10} = \underbrace{1110}_{E}\underbrace{1110}_{E}\underbrace{0001}_{1}\underbrace{0010}_{2}\underbrace{0011}_{3}\underbrace{1111}_{F}\underbrace{0001}_{1}\underbrace{0000}_{0}
$$

So the 32-bit string is:

$$
\mathtt{1110\;1110\;0001\;0010\;0011\;1111\;0001\;0000}
$$

#### Step 2 — Slice into the MCR field groups

| Field | Bits | Value (Binary) | Value (Decimal/Hex) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `cond` | [31:28] | `1110` | `0xE` (AL — always) |
| `class` | [27:24] | `1110` | `0xE` (class `1110` = **MCR**) |
| `CP#` | [23:21] | `000` | CP0 |
| `M` | [20] | `0` | (not relevant for MCR) |
| `opcode` | [19:16] | `1001` | op `9` |
| `CRn` | [15:12] | `0010` | `CR2` |
| `Rd` | [11:8] | `0011` | ARM `R3` |
| `CRm` | [7:5] | `000` | `CR0` |
| `op2` | [3:0] | `0000` | `0` |

#### Step 3 — Assemble the assembly mnemonic

Using the standard MCR syntax:

$$
\texttt{MCR\{cond\} p\{CP\#\}, \{op1\}, Rd, CRn, CRm, \{op2\}}
$$

$$
\boxed{
\texttt{MCEQ p0, \#9, R3, CR2, CR0, \#0}
}
$$

Wait — `cond = 0xE` is `AL` (always), so the condition suffix is omitted. The final assembly is:

$$
\boxed{
\texttt{MCR p0, \#9, R3, CR2, CR0, \#0}
}
$$

#### Step 4 — Semantic meaning

> [!NOTE]
> *"Move to Coprocessor 0, operation 9, the contents of ARM register R3 into Coprocessor register CR2, with auxiliary register CR0 and operand 2 = 0."*

The exact operation of `opcode 9` and the role of `CR0`/`op2=0` are **defined by the CP0 implementation** in the SoC manual. The ARM core is **not aware** of this meaning.

### 3.5 Worked Example — Pipeline Stall Timing

> **[Example Problem]:** Suppose a `CDP` instruction on CP11 (VFP) is fetched, and the coprocessor signals `CPB = 1` for **N = 3** clock cycles before completing. How many total clock cycles does this instruction consume in the ARM pipeline?

#### Step 1 — Decompose the pipeline

The ARM pipeline (in this analysis, we use a simplified 3-stage model: **Fetch – Decode – Execute**):
- **Cycle 1:** Fetch
- **Cycle 2:** Decode
- **Cycle 3:** Execute (with `nCPI` asserted; coprocessor sees it)

#### Step 2 — Coprocessor busy-wait logic

The coprocessor asserts `CPB` from Cycle 3 onwards. The ARM core stalls Decode and Execute, but continues Fetch.

- **Cycle 3 (Execute begins):** `CPB = 1` → stall begins
- **Cycle 4:** `CPB = 1` → still stalled
- **Cycle 5:** `CPB = 1` → still stalled
- **Cycle 6:** `CPB = 0` → un-stall; Execute completes

So, the coprocessor execution took $N = 3$ extra cycles (Cycles 4, 5, 6), plus the original Execute stage (Cycle 3). Wait — careful: in the standard ARM model, the *first* execute cycle counts as cycle 1 of the coprocessor's latency.

#### Step 3 — Total cycle count

Let the coprocessor require $L$ cycles to complete (where $L$ is the coprocessor's intrinsic latency). Then:

$$
T_{total} = T_{fetch} + T_{decode} + T_{exec} + (L - 1) = 1 + 1 + L
$$

Substituting $L = 3$:

$$
\boxed{
T_{total} = 1 + 1 + 3 = 5 \text{ clock cycles}
}
$$

For $L = 1$ (an instantaneous coprocessor operation, no stall), we recover the usual $T = 3$ cycles — consistent with the standard pipeline.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Pros and Cons of the Coprocessor Interface

| Aspect | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Modularity** | New accelerators can be added without modifying the core | Bus contention if many coprocessors share the data bus |
| **Performance** | Offloads complex ops (FPU, MMU) from the core's slow ALU | Pipeline stalls on `CPB` reduce overall throughput |
| **Software compatibility** | Instructions reserved for future coprocessors → forward compatibility | Software emulation is slow if coprocessor is absent (`CPA = 1`) |
| **Power** | Coprocessor can be power-gated when idle | Each coprocessor adds silicon area and leakage |
| **Verification** | Clean handshake protocol simplifies formal verification | Multi-cycle `CPB` timing must be carefully characterized |

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — ARM Core Coupled to Coprocessors

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the ARM core is *bus-coupled* to multiple coprocessors via the standard coprocessor interface, with the four handshake signals (`nCPI`, `CPA`, `CPB`, data bus).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ARM_CORE["ARM Core Pipeline Fetch Decode Execute"]
    BUS_DECODER["Coprocessor Bus Decoder Monitors instr bus 27 to 20 bits"]
    CP10["CP10 VFP Single Precision FPU"]
    CP11["CP11 VFP Double Precision FPU"]
    CP14["CP14 Debug Communication Channel"]
    CP15["CP15 System Control MMU Caches TLB"]
    UNDEF_HANDLER["Undefined Instruction Handler Software Emulation"]
    DATA_BUS["Shared 32 bit Data Bus CPRDATA CPWDATA"]
    HANDSHAKE["Handshake Signals nCPI active LOW CPA Coprocessor Absent CPB Coprocessor Busy"]

    ARM_CORE -->|"Instruction stream"| BUS_DECODER
    BUS_DECODER -->|"nCPI + opcode"| CP10
    BUS_DECODER -->|"nCPI + opcode"| CP11
    BUS_DECODER -->|"nCPI + opcode"| CP14
    BUS_DECODER -->|"nCPI + opcode"| CP15
    CP10 -->|"CPA = 1 if absent"| UNDEF_HANDLER
    CP11 -->|"CPA = 1 if absent"| UNDEF_HANDLER
    CP14 -->|"CPA = 1 if absent"| UNDEF_HANDLER
    CP15 -->|"CPA = 1 if absent"| UNDEF_HANDLER
    CP10 <-->|"CPB stall"| ARM_CORE
    CP11 <-->|"CPB stall"| ARM_CORE
    CP14 <-->|"CPB stall"| ARM_CORE
    CP15 <-->|"CPB stall"| ARM_CORE
    CP10 <--> DATA_BUS
    CP11 <--> DATA_BUS
    CP14 <--> DATA_BUS
    CP15 <--> DATA_BUS
```

### 4.2 Instruction Decode Flow — How an MCR is Routed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START(["ARM Core Fetches 32 bit Instruction"]) --> COND{"cond 31 to 28 TRUE?"}
    COND -->|NO| SKIP["Instruction Skipped No Pipeline Cost"]
    COND -->|YES| CLASS{"class 27 to 24 Decode"}
    CLASS -->|1110| MCR_PATH["MCR Move ARM reg to Coproc"]
    CLASS -->|1111| MRC_PATH["MRC Move Coproc reg to ARM reg"]
    CLASS -->|1100| MCRR_PATH["MCRR Move two ARM regs to Coproc"]
    CLASS -->|1101| MRRC_PATH["MRRC Move Coproc to two ARM regs"]
    CLASS -->|101x| LDC_STC_PATH["LDC or STC Memory Transfer"]
    CLASS -->|1110 CDP| CDP_PATH["CDP Coprocessor Internal Operation"]
    MCR_PATH --> CPCHECK{"CPA Signal from Target CP"}
    MRC_PATH --> CPCHECK
    MCRR_PATH --> CPCHECK
    MRRC_PATH --> CPCHECK
    LDC_STC_PATH --> CPCHECK
    CDP_PATH --> CPCHECK
    CPCHECK -->|CPA = 1| UNDEF["Undefined Instruction Trap Vector at 0x00000004"]
    CPCHECK -->|CPA = 0| BUSYWAIT{"CPB Signal"}
    BUSYWAIT -->|CPB = 1| STALL["Stall Decode and Execute Stages Prefetch Continues"]
    BUSYWAIT -->|CPB = 0| EXEC["Coprocessor Executes Instruction Completion in this Cycle"]
    STALL --> BUSYWAIT
```

### 4.3 Pipeline Stall Timing Waveform (Mermaid Gantt-style)

```mermaid
gantt
    title ARM Pipeline Behaviour During a 3 Cycle Coprocessor Stall
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  C%L

    section ARM Pipeline
    Fetch INSTR A (CDP)    :a1, 0, 1
    Fetch INSTR B (next)   :a2, 1, 1
    Fetch INSTR C          :a3, 2, 1
    Fetch INSTR D          :a4, 3, 1
    Decode INSTR A         :crit, b1, 1, 1
    Decode INSTR B         :b2, 1, 1
    Execute INSTR A Cycle 1 :crit, c1, 2, 1
    Execute INSTR A Stall 1 :crit, c2, 3, 1
    Execute INSTR A Stall 2 :crit, c3, 4, 1
    Execute INSTR A Complete:crit, c4, 5, 1

    section Coprocessor
    Receive nCPI for INSTR A :d1, 2, 1
    CPB Asserted Stall        :crit, d2, 2, 3
    CPB Deasserted Complete   :d3, 5, 1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Gantt Chart:**
> - The **Decode** stage for INSTR A is *stalled* (1 cycle delay) because Decode needs to wait for Execute to clear.
> - The **Fetch** stage is *not* stalled — it continues to bring in INSTR B, C, D into the prefetch buffer.
> - The **CPB signal is asserted for 3 cycles** (C1 through C3 in the Execute column), matching the $L = 3$ coprocessor latency assumed in §3.5.

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between the ARM coprocessor instructions `MCR` and `MRC`. Mention at least two points of distinction.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Aspect | `MCR` | `MRC` |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Full form** | Move to **C**oproc from **R**egister (ARM) | Move to ARM **R**egister from **C**oproc |
| 2 | **Data flow** | ARM register → Coprocessor register | Coprocessor register → ARM register |
| 3 | **Class code (bits [27:24])** | `1110` | `1111` |
| 4 | **Use case** | Configuring coprocessor state (e.g., writing CP15 c1) | Reading coprocessor state (e.g., reading CPU ID via CP15 c0) |
| 5 | **Operand count** | 1 ARM register + 1 coproc register | 1 coproc register → 1 ARM register |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic trick for students:** "M**C**R → **C**oproc **R**eceives" (data flows *to* coproc); "M**R**C → **R**egister **C**ollects" (data flows *to* ARM register).

**Word count target: ~80 words. (3 marks awarded for: correct full form + 2 distinctions + register flow direction + class code bonus).**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of the `CPB` signal in the ARM coprocessor interface? When is it asserted and what is its effect on the pipeline?**

**Model Answer:**

The **`CPB` (Coprocessor Busy)** signal is asserted **HIGH** by the coprocessor towards the ARM core when the coprocessor is **currently unable to complete the instruction in a single cycle** (e.g., a multi-cycle floating-point divide). 

**Effect on the pipeline:**
- The **Decode** and **Execute** stages of the ARM pipeline are **stalled** for as long as `CPB = 1`.
- The **Fetch** stage is *not* stalled — the prefetch buffer continues to fill.
- Once the coprocessor de-asserts `CPB` (i.e., `CPB = 0`), the ARM core completes the stalled instruction and proceeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-winning phrasing:** Always mention *which stages* are stalled (Decode + Execute) and which are not (Fetch). A student who says "the pipeline is stalled" without qualification loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **ARM coprocessor interface** and describe the roles of the signals `nCPI`, `CPA`, and `CPB`. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Explain the following coprocessor instructions with their instruction format and one example use case each: **(i) CDP**, **(ii) MCR**, **(iii) MCRR**. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Coprocessor Interface Block Diagram & Signals [7 Marks]**

**Block Diagram:**

```
                  +-------------------+
   Instruction -->|     ARM Core      |---[ CPRDATA / CPWDATA ]---+
   Stream         | (Fetch-Decode-    |                          |
                  |  Execute Pipeline)|                          v
                  +-----+-----+-------+         +-------------------+
                        |     |     |             |  COPROCESSOR n   |
                        |     |     |--- nCPI --->| (e.g., CP10 FPU) |
                        |     |     |<- CPA -----+|   Register File  |
                        |     |     |<- CPB -----+|   Internal ALU   |
                        |     |     |             +-------------------+
                        |     |     |                       |
                        |     |     +-- data bus <---------+
                        v     v
                  Prefetch   Decode    Execute
                  Buffer     Stage     Stage
```

**Signal Roles (table for marks):**

| Signal | Direction | Function |
|---|---|---|
| `nCPI` (Coprocessor Instruction, active LOW) | ARM core → Coprocessor | ARM core pulses this LOW in the Execute stage when the fetched instruction is destined for *this* coprocessor (matched by the `CP#` field). |
| `CPA` (Coprocessor Absent) | Coprocessor → ARM core | Asserted HIGH if the addressed coprocessor is *not implemented* in the SoC. The ARM core then takes the **Undefined Instruction exception** (vector at `0x00000004`), allowing software to emulate the instruction. |
| `CPB` (Coprocessor Busy) | Coprocessor → ARM core | Asserted HIGH if the coprocessor cannot complete the operation in the current cycle. Causes the ARM pipeline's **Decode and Execute stages to stall** until `CPB` goes LOW. The **Fetch stage continues** so the prefetch buffer stays full. |

**Additional explanations (2 marks):**
- The coprocessor interface allows the ARM core to delegate specific instructions to a coprocessor while sharing the same instruction stream and register context.
- It is a **tightly coupled** interface: the coprocessor is not a separate bus master — it is a slave that responds to instructions *handed* to it by the ARM core.

---

#### **Part (b) — Instruction Format and Examples [7 Marks]**

**(i) CDP — Coprocessor Data Processing [2.5 Marks]**

- **Purpose:** Performs an operation *entirely inside* the coprocessor using its own register file. No data is transferred to or from ARM registers.
- **Instruction Format (32 bits):**
  ```
  [31:28] [27:24] [23:21] [20] [19:16] [15:12] [11:8] [7:5] [4] [3:0]
   cond    1110     CP#     0   opcode   CRn      CRd     CRm   T   op2
  ```
- **Example Use Case:** `FADD` (floating-point add on VFP) — adds two coproc registers and stores the result in a third, e.g., `FADDS S0, S1, S2`.

**(ii) MCR — Move to Coprocessor from ARM Register [2.5 Marks]**

- **Purpose:** Transfers a single 32-bit value from an ARM register to a coprocessor register. Used for *configuring* the coprocessor.
- **Instruction Format (32 bits):**
  ```
  [31:28] [27:24] [23:21] [20] [19:16] [15:12] [11:8] [7:5] [4] [3:0]
   cond    1110     CP#     0   opcode   CRn      Rd      CRm   T   op2
  ```
  (Note: in `MCR`, the field at [11:8] is the **source** ARM register `Rd`.)
- **Example Use Case:** `MCR p15, 0, R0, c1, c0, 0` — writes the value of ARM `R0` into CP15 c1 c0 (the **Control Register**), thereby enabling the MMU.

**(iii) MCRR — Move to Coprocessor from Two ARM Registers [2 Marks]**

- **Purpose:** Atomically transfers **two** 32-bit ARM registers to a *paired* 64-bit destination in the coprocessor. Useful for 64-bit data (double-precision float, 64-bit timestamp).
- **Instruction Format (32 bits):**
  ```
  [31:28] [27:24] [23:20] [19:16] [15:12] [11:8] [7:4] [3:0]
   cond    1100     CP#    opcode   Rd       Rn      CRm   op2
  ```
- **Example Use Case:** `MCRR p15, 0, R0, R1, c14` — writes the 64-bit value `{R1, R0}` (where R0 is low word, R1 is high word) into CP15 c14 (the **64-bit counter** in the Generic Timer).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`
