## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet — Data Types (Memorize for ESE)**

| **Symbol / Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Unit** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unsigned range ($N$ bits) | $0 \le x \le 2^{N}-1$ | count | Includes both endpoints |
| Signed range ($N$ bits) | $-2^{N-1} \le x \le 2^{N-1}-1$ | count | Asymmetric by 1 |
| Max value of `uintN_t` | $U_{\max} = 2^{N}-1$ | count | e.g., `uint8_t` → 255 |
| Min value of `intN_t` | $I_{\min} = -2^{N-1}$ | count | e.g., `int8_t` → -128 |
| Storage size | $S = N/8$ | bytes | Round up for non-multiples of 8 |
| IEEE 754 bias | $B = 2^{e-1} - 1 = 127$ | — | For single precision |
| IEEE 754 precision | $\epsilon \approx 2^{-23} \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-7}$ | relative | ~7 decimal digits |
| IEEE 754 exponent range | $E \in [-126, +127]$ (normal) | power of 2 | $E=0$ subnormal, $E=255$ special |
| Endian byte index | $B_i = (V \gg 8i) \;\&\; 0xFF$ | byte | LE stores $i=0$ at low address |
| Two's complement negate | $-x = (\sim x) + 1$ | bitwise | Modulo $2^N$ arithmetic |
| Sign extension | $X_{N \to M} = \text{replicate MSB}$ for $M-N$ bits | bits | On right-shift of signed |
| Zero extension | $X_{N \to M} = \text{pad with 0s}$ for $M-N$ bits | bits | For unsigned promotion |
| Structure padding | $\text{aligned to} \max(\text{field sizes})$ | bytes | Compiler inserts gaps |
| `sizeof(T)` on N-bit MCU | $\lceil N/8 \rceil$ minimum, alignment may expand | bytes | E.g., `int32_t` on ARM = 4 |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Size and Range of `int8_t` and `int16_t`

**Given:** $N$-bit signed two's complement integer, $N \in \{8, 16\}$.

**Step 1 — Establish the range formula.**
For an $N$-bit signed two's complement integer, the *positive* half uses bit patterns $00\ldots0$ to $01\ldots1$ (i.e., $0$ to $2^{N-1}-1$), and the *negative* half uses $10\ldots0$ to $11\ldots1$ (i.e., $-2^{N-1}$ to $-1$).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimum} &= -2^{N-1} \\
\text{Maximum} &= +2^{N-1} - 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $N = 8$ for `int8_t`.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Range}_{int8_t} &= [-2^{7}, \; 2^{7}-1] \\
&= [-128, \; +127] \\
\text{Total distinct values} &= 2^8 = 256
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $N = 16$ for `int16_t`.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Range}_{int16_t} &= [-2^{15}, \; 2^{15}-1] \\
&= [-32\,768, \; +32\,767] \\
\text{Total distinct values} &= 2^{16} = 65\,536
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verification via the bit-pattern count.**
For *any* $N$-bit two's complement type, the cardinality is exactly $2^N$ because each of the $N$ bits may independently be 0 or 1. The mapping is one-to-one onto the value set $[-2^{N-1}, +2^{N-1}-1]$; no value is skipped, none is duplicated.

## 3.2 Derivation: IEEE 754 Single-Precision Decoding

**Given:** A 32-bit pattern `0x40490FDB` (this is the encoding of $\pi$).

**Step 1 — Convert the hex pattern to binary (32 bits).**
$$0x40490FDB = 0100\;0000\;0100\;1001\;0000\;1111\;1101\;1011$$

**Step 2 — Partition into sign, exponent, mantissa.**
$$\begin{aligned}
s &= 0 \quad (\text{bit 31}) \\
E_{\text{raw}} &= 10000000_2 = 128 \quad (\text{bits 30..23}) \\
M_{\text{fraction}} &= 100\;1001\;0000\;1111\;1101\;1011 \quad (\text{bits 22..0})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Unbias the exponent.**
The bias for single precision is $127$. So:
$$E = E_{\text{raw}} - 127 = 128 - 127 = 1$$

**Step 4 — Reconstruct the mantissa with the implicit leading 1.**
$$1.M = 1.100\;1001\;0000\;1111\;1101\;1011_2$$

**Step 5 — Compute the value.**
$$V = (-1)^{0} \times 1.M \times 2^{1} = 2 \times 1.M$$

**Step 6 — Convert $1.M$ to decimal.**
$$\begin{aligned}
1.M &= 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{16} + \frac{1}{512} + \frac{1}{32768} + \ldots \\
    &\approx 1.5707963\ldots
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Final value.**
$$V \approx 2 \times 1.5707963 = 3.1415926\ldots \approx \pi \;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The 32-bit pattern `0x40490FDB` is indeed the IEEE 754 single-precision encoding of $\pi$. *This is a classic viva question — "Show that `0x40490FDB` represents $\pi$."*

## 3.3 Worked Example: Endianness Byte Reassembly

**Problem:** A 32-bit sensor value `V = 0xDEADBEEF` is transmitted byte-by-byte over UART (low byte first by protocol). The receiver stores them at addresses `0x2000 0000`, `0x2000 0001`, `0x2000 0002`, `0x2000 0003`. Reconstruct `V` on (a) little-endian and (b) big-endian MCUs. *Show the memory layout in each case.*

**Step 1 — Decompose `V` into 4 bytes.**
$$\begin{aligned}
B_0 &= V \;\&\; 0xFF = 0xEF \\
B_1 &= (V \gg 8) \;\&\; 0xFF = 0xBE \\
B_2 &= (V \gg 16) \;\&\; 0xFF = 0xAD \\
B_3 &= (V \gg 24) \;\&\; 0xFF = 0xDE
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Little-endian reconstruction (ARM Cortex-M default).**
The first byte received (`0xEF`) goes to the *lowest* address. To reassemble, the MCU performs a 32-bit aligned load and the CPU hardware reverses the byte lanes.

| **Address** | **Byte Stored** | **Weight** |
|---|---|---|
| 0x20000000 | 0xEF | $2^{0}$ |
| 0x20000001 | 0xBE | $2^{8}$ |
| 0x20000002 | 0xAD | $2^{16}$ |
| 0x20000003 | 0xDE | $2^{24}$ |

**Step 3 — Reconstruct on little-endian CPU.**
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{LE} &= 0xEF + (0xBE \times 256) + (0xAD \times 65536) + (0xDE \times 16777216) \\
       &= 0xEF + 0xBE00 + 0xAD0000 + 0xDE000000 \\
       &= 0xDEADBEEF \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Big-endian reconstruction.**
If the same bytes were stored with the *first received* byte at the *lowest* address on a big-endian CPU, the value would be misread as `0xEFBEADDE` — a byte-reversal bug that has crashed real spacecraft (ESA Schiaparelli EDM lander, 2016, was attributed in part to endian-conversion errors in IMU telemetry).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic in C (portable):** When you must read a multi-byte value in a known endianness regardless of host, use byte-shift assembly: `V = (B0) | (B1<<8) | (B2<<16) | (B3<<24);` works on *both* LE and BE for "low-byte-first" protocols.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Structure Padding Waste

**Problem:** Define `struct { uint8_t a; uint32_t b; uint8_t c; }` for an ARM Cortex-M target. (a) Compute `sizeof(struct)`. (b) Compute the memory waste due to padding.

**Step 1 — Alignment rules on ARM.**
- `uint8_t` has alignment 1 byte.
- `uint32_t` has alignment 4 bytes.

**Step 2 — Lay out the fields sequentially.**
- Offset 0: `a` (1 byte).
- Offsets 1–3: **3 bytes of padding** (so that `b` starts on a 4-byte boundary).
- Offsets 4–7: `b` (4 bytes).
- Offset 8: `c` (1 byte).
- Offsets 9–11: **3 bytes of trailing padding** to make the struct's total size a multiple of its strictest member's alignment (4 bytes).

**Step 3 — Compute `sizeof`.**
$$\text{sizeof} = 12 \;\text{bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the waste.**
$$\text{Useful data} = 1 + 4 + 1 = 6 \;\text{bytes}$$
$$\text{Padding} = 12 - 6 = 6 \;\text{bytes}$$
$$\text{Waste fraction} = \frac{6}{12} = 50\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Field ordering optimization:** Reordering to `{ uint32_t b; uint8_t a; uint8_t c; }` yields `sizeof = 8` (4 + 1 + 1 + 2 trailing pad) — saving 33% of RAM. *KTU examiners often test this on Part A.*

## 3.5 Worked Example: `volatile` in a Polling Loop

**Problem:** A 16-bit timer register at address `0x4000 1000` is read in a busy-wait loop. Show how the *absence* of `volatile` breaks the firmware.

**Without `volatile`:**
```c
uint16_t * const TIMER = (uint16_t *)0x40001000;
while (*TIMER < 1000) { /* spin */ }
```
The compiler reasons: *"The pointer is `const`, no other code in this translation unit writes to it, the loop body has no side effects, therefore `*TIMER` is invariant → infinite loop with the value of the first read hoisted out."* The generated assembly is a single load followed by a comparison against a constant — the hardware update is *never observed*.

**With `volatile`:**
```c
volatile uint16_t * const TIMER = (volatile uint16_t *)0x40001000;
while (*TIMER < 1000) { /* spin */ }
```
The compiler is now *required* to re-issue the load on every iteration. The loop terminates correctly when the hardware timer crosses 1000.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Data Type Taxonomy for Embedded C

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Data Types in Embedded C] --> B[Object Types]
    A --> C[Function Types]
    A --> D[Incomplete Types]

    B --> E[Scalar Types]
    B --> F[Aggregate Types]
    B --> G[Derived Types]

    E --> E1[Integer]
    E --> E2[Floating Point]
    E --> E3[Pointer]
    E --> E4[_Bool and enum]

    E1 --> E1a[signed int8 int16 int32 int64]
    E1 --> E1b[unsigned uint8 uint16 uint32 uint64]

    E2 --> E2a[float 32 bit IEEE 754]
    E2 --> E2b[double 64 bit soft float on 8 bit MCU]

    F --> F1[struct with bit fields]
    F --> F2[union overlapping storage]
    F --> F3[array fixed and variable]

    G --> G1[Pointer to T]
    G --> G2[Array of T]
    G --> G3[Function returning T]

    E1a -.volatile.- V1[Memory Mapped IO]
    E1b -.volatile.- V1
    F1 -.volatile.- V1
    E1a -.const.- V2[Lookup Tables in Flash]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Memory Layout of a Mixed Bit-field Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Word0 [Word at offset 0: 32 bits]
        D0[MODE bits 0 to 1 width 2] --> D1[EN bit 2 width 1]
        D1 --> D2[DIR bit 3 width 1]
        D2 --> D3[RESERVED bits 4 to 7 width 4]
        D3 --> D4[PRESC bits 8 to 11 width 4]
        D4 --> D5[IE bit 12 width 1]
    end
    Word0 --> Word1[Trailing padding 19 bits to align to 32]

    D0 -.read modify write.- GPIO[GPIO CTRL register at 0x40020000]
```

## 4.3 Flow Diagram: How a Compiler Handles `volatile` vs Non-volatile Reads

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Source: read x] --> CheckQ{Is x volatile?}
    CheckQ -- No --> OptPass[Optimizer pass]
    OptPass --> CacheCheck{Is x already in a register?}
    CacheCheck -- Yes --> Reuse[Reuse cached register value]
    CacheCheck -- No --> Load1[Issue single load instruction]
    Reuse --> EndOp[Continue]
    Load1 --> EndOp

    CheckQ -- Yes --> ForceLoad[Force memory load on every syntactic use]
    ForceLoad --> EndOp
    EndOp[Emit assembly with the chosen number of loads]
```

## 4.4 Architecture Diagram: Endianness Visualization of a 32-bit Word

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LE [Little Endian layout address low to high]
        LE0[Byte at addr 0x00 equals 0x78 LSB]
        LE1[Byte at addr 0x01 equals 0x56]
        LE2[Byte at addr 0x02 equals 0x34]
        LE3[Byte at addr 0x03 equals 0x12 MSB]
        LE0 --> LE1 --> LE2 --> LE3
    end

    subgraph BE [Big Endian layout address low to high]
        BE0[Byte at addr 0x00 equals 0x12 MSB]
        BE1[Byte at addr 0x01 equals 0x34]
        BE2[Byte at addr 0x02 equals 0x56]
        BE3[Byte at addr 0x03 equals 0x78 LSB]
        BE0 --> BE1 --> BE2 --> BE3
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Differentiate between `volatile` and `const` qualifiers in C as applied to embedded systems. Why is `volatile` mandatory when accessing memory-mapped peripheral registers?

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **`const`** | **`volatile`** |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | "Read-only at this scope" | "Subject to change outside program flow" |
| Compiler action | Disallows writes (warning/error) | Disallows optimization/caching of reads |
| Embedded use | Lookup tables in Flash, calibration constants | Memory-mapped registers, ISR-shared variables |
| Example | `const uint16_t sine_table[256]` | `volatile uint32_t * const UART_DR` |

`volatile` is **mandatory** for memory-mapped peripheral registers because:
1. The value of the register can change asynchronously — driven by hardware events (timer overflow, DMA completion, external interrupt) that the compiler cannot see.
2. Without `volatile`, the optimizer treats consecutive reads as redundant, hoists the first read out of loops, and the firmware never observes hardware updates.
3. The qualifier guarantees that every access compiles to a real load/store instruction, preserving the side-effect contract required for correct hardware handshake.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (–1 mark):** Students often write *"volatile makes the variable change faster"* or *"volatile is for global variables"*. Both are **wrong**. `volatile` is a contract about *access semantics*, not timing or linkage.

---

### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
A 12-bit ADC on an embedded board produces samples in the range 0–4095. Which fixed-width C data type from `<stdint.h>` is the most memory-efficient signed type to hold this value without overflow, and why?

**Model Answer:**

- 12-bit ADC range = $[0, 4095]$, which requires representing values up to $2^{12} - 1 = 4095$.
- The nearest *standard* fixed-width signed type that can hold 4095 *without overflow* and *with positive range* is **`int16_t`** (range $-2^{15}$ to $+2^{15}-1 = [-32768, +32767]$).
- Although 4095 fits in 12 bits, C does not have a native 12-bit type. The choices are:
  - `int8_t`: range $-128$ to $+127$ — **insufficient** (4095 > 127).
  - `int16_t`: range $-32768$ to $+32767$ — **fits**, smallest signed option.
  - `uint16_t`: also fits, but if the application needs negative offsets or signed math (e.g., delta from a setpoint), `int16_t` is preferred.
- **Most memory-efficient signed choice = `int16_t`**, occupying **2 bytes** on any MCU.

If the value can never be negative, `uint16_t` is the canonical "KTU-correct" answer for an unsigned 0–4095 ADC reading.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** A single Part B question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Two alternative full questions (Question A and Question B) are provided below. Only one of them is set on the actual exam paper; the student answers the one chosen.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `int8_t` / `uint8_t` Deep-Dive**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model]**
For an 8-bit signed integer in two's complement representation, derive the algebraic range, compute the storage requirement, and explain what happens — bit by bit and at the C language level — when you execute `int8_t x = -1; x = x + 1;`. What is the final value and why?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Derive the range.**
For an $N$-bit signed two's complement integer, the range is $[-2^{N-1}, \; +2^{N-1}-1]$. With $N = 8$:
$$\text{Range}_{int8_t} = [-2^{7}, \; 2^{7}-1] = [-128, \; +127]$$
This is **256 distinct values**, requiring $\log_2(256) = 8$ bits of storage, i.e., **1 byte**.

**Step 2 — Encode $-1$ in 8-bit two's complement.**
The positive equivalent of $-1$ is $1 = 0b00000001$. Two's complement negation is $-x = \sim x + 1$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sim(0b00000001) &= 0b11111110 \\
0b11111110 + 1   &= 0b11111111 = 0xFF
\end{aligned}$$
So `x = -1` results in the bit pattern `0xFF` stored in memory.

**Step 3 — Execute `x + 1`.**
$$0b11111111 + 0b00000001 = 0b00000000 \;\text{(with carry out discarded)}$$
The 8-bit result is `0b00000000` = **0**.

**Step 4 — Interpret in the `int8_t` domain.**
Since `x` is declared `int8_t`, the value `0b00000000` is interpreted as $0$ (which is in range $[-128, +127]$). The C standard guarantees that two's complement signed overflow is *well-defined* in C23; in C99/C11 it is *implementation-defined* for `-128 + -1`, but for `0xFF + 1 = 0` the result is **always 0** on any two's complement system.

**Final answer:** `x = 0`. This is the *correct* behavior of two's complement wrap-around, not a "bug" — it is exactly why $-1$ is encoded as all-ones and why the ring $\mathbb{Z}/2^8\mathbb{Z}$ is the natural algebra of 8-bit arithmetic.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model]**
A firmware engineer declares: `volatile uint8_t * const UART_TX = (volatile uint8_t *)0x40011004UL;` and then writes `*UART_TX = 'A';`. Explain, with reference to the C standard, the meaning of every keyword and qualifier in this declaration, and describe the exact sequence of hardware events on an ARM Cortex-M3 when the assignment executes.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Decode the declaration from the identifier outward.**

| **Token** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|
| `0x40011004UL` | A 32-bit unsigned long hex literal — the absolute physical address of the UART data register in the device memory map. |
| `(volatile uint8_t *)` | Cast: the address is to be treated as a pointer to an 8-bit unsigned volatile object. |
| `const` (after `*`) | The pointer itself is constant; the *value of the pointer* (the address it holds) cannot be reassigned after initialization. The *pointee* (the hardware register) is still modifiable. |
| `volatile` | Every access to the pointee must be a real load/store — the compiler must not elide, merge, or reorder the access with respect to other `volatile` accesses. |
| `uint8_t` | The pointee is 1 byte wide, accessed as a single-byte load/store (vs. `uint32_t` which would require word-aligned access). |

**Step 2 — Interpret the assignment `*UART_TX = 'A';`.**
1. The character `'A'` is promoted: `'A' = 0x41` (per ASCII). It is converted to `uint8_t` (already fits).
2. The compiler emits a single `STRB` (Store Register Byte) instruction targeting address `0x4001 1004`.
3. The ARM Cortex-M3 System Bus performs a byte-write to that address. The Bus Matrix routes it to the APB bridge connected to the UART peripheral.
4. The UART peripheral's transmit data register (TXDR) latches the byte, the transmit shift register begins clocking it out on the TX line at the configured baud rate, and a TX-complete flag will be set in the status register when done.

**Step 3 — Why `volatile` and `const` both matter here.**
- `volatile` ensures the store is *actually emitted* and not dead-code-eliminated by the linker (the optimizer cannot prove the write has no effect because it cannot see the hardware side effect).
- `const` on the pointer is a defensive programming idiom — it prevents accidental "pointer arithmetic" like `UART_TX++`, which would walk off the register map into the next peripheral's address space, a common source of mysterious hardware faults.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Endianness, IEEE 754, and Bit-fields**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
A 32-bit floating-point variable stored at address `0x2000 4000` has the byte sequence (in increasing address order) `0xC1`, `0xA0`, `0x00`, `0x00` on a little-endian ARM Cortex-M4. Determine the value stored, showing the IEEE 754 decoding steps, and explain why the resulting value is exactly **-5.0**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reassemble the 32-bit word on a little-endian CPU.**
The least significant byte is at the lowest address. So:
$$\begin{aligned}
B_0 &= 0x00 \quad (\text{at } 0x20004000) \\
B_1 &= 0x00 \quad (\text{at } 0x20004001) \\
B_2 &= 0xA0 \quad (\text{at } 0x20004002) \\
B_3 &= 0xC1 \quad (\text{at } 0x20004003) \\
W   &= 0xC1A00000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to binary.**
$$0xC1A00000 = 1100\;0001\;1010\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000$$

**Step 3 — Partition into IEEE 754 fields.**
$$\begin{aligned}
s &= 1 \quad (\text{bit 31}) \\
E_{\text{raw}} &= 10000011_2 = 131 \quad (\text{bits 30..23}) \\
M_{\text{fraction}} &= 010\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000 \quad (\text{bits 22..0})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Unbias the exponent.**
$$E = 131 - 127 = 2$$

**Step 5 — Reconstruct the mantissa with the implicit leading 1.**
$$1.M = 1.010\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000_2 = 1 + 0.25 = 1.25$$

**Step 6 — Compute the value.**
$$V = (-1)^{1} \times 1.25 \times 2^{2} = -1 \times 1.25 \times 4 = -5.0 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Cross-check by a quick sanity rule.**
- $5.0 = 1.25 \times 4 = 1.25 \times 2^2$, so unbiased exponent is 2, biased exponent is 129 = `0x81`. Sign bit is 1, so high byte is `0xC1`. The mantissa for 0.25 is `0.01` in binary → bits at positions 22, 21... only position 22 contributes 0.25 → `0x200000`. So the full word is `0xC1A00000`. ✓

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Define a C `struct` with bit-fields to model the layout of a hypothetical 16-bit Timer Control Register at address `0x4000 001C` with the following bit-map: **bits 0–3** = Prescaler (4 bits, R/W), **bit 4** = Counter Enable (1 bit, R/W), **bit 5** = Interrupt Enable (1 bit, R/W), **bits 6–7** = Mode select (2 bits, R/W), **bits 8–15** = Reserved (read as 0). Show how to set Counter Enable *without disturbing any other bit* using the standard read-modify-write idiom. Explain why the keyword `volatile` is essential on the structure pointer.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the bit-field structure definition.**
```c
typedef struct {
    uint16_t PRESC  : 4;   /* bits 0..3  */
    uint16_t CEN    : 1;   /* bit  4     */
    uint16_t UIE    : 1;   /* bit  5     */
    uint16_t MODE   : 2;   /* bits 6..7  */
    uint16_t RES    : 8;   /* bits 8..15 */
} TIM_CR_t;

#define TIM_CR (*(volatile TIM_CR_t *)0x4000001CUL)
```

**Step 2 — Set only the CEN bit using a portable read-modify-write.**
```c
uint16_t snapshot = TIM_CR;          /* volatile read   */
snapshot |= (1U << 4);                /* set bit 4       */
TIM_CR = snapshot;                   /* volatile write  */
```

**Step 3 — Why this idiom is mandatory.**
A naive `TIM_CR.CEN = 1;` *should* generate a read-modify-write, but:
- Without `volatile` on the pointer, the compiler may cache the read, miss a hardware update to PRESC/UIE/MODE, and write back a *stale* value — corrupting the other bits.
- With `volatile`, the compiler is contractually bound to issue a fresh `LDRH` before the write and a `STRH` after, so the other bits are preserved.
- Alternatively, the bit-band alias region (on Cortex-M3/M4) can be used: write a single `STR` to the alias address and the hardware performs the atomic RMW for you, eliminating the software window.

**Step 4 — Endianness footnote.**
On little-endian ARM, bit 0 of the structure is the *LSB of the low byte* — which is also bit 0 of the 16-bit word at the target address. So our bit-map aligns perfectly with hardware convention. On a big-endian MCU, the bit-field *implementation-defined ordering* could invert the bit numbering; always cross-check with the datasheet.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Points (–2 each):**
> 1. Forgetting the outer parentheses in `#define TIM_CR (*(volatile TIM_CR_t *)0x4000001CUL)` — the dereference precedence silently breaks every access.
> 2. Writing `volatile TIM_CR_t *TIM_CR` (pointer is volatile, pointee is not) — the *exact opposite* of what we need.
> 3. Using `int` (signed) for bit-fields where the field can be negative — causes undefined bit-pattern shifts; use `unsigned int` or `uint16_t`/`uint32_t`.
> 4. Relying on bit-field order across compilers — the C standard explicitly says bit-field *allocation order within a storage unit* is implementation-defined. Always verify with a `sizeof` and a memory dump.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Types for Embedded System Design**

- **Fixed-width types win:** Always prefer `<stdint.h>` types (`uint8_t`, `int32_t`, etc.) over native `int`/`long` in embedded C; native sizes are architecture-dependent.
- **Two's complement is universal** on all KTU-recommended MCUs (ARM, AVR, PIC18+, MSP430); range formula is $[-2^{N-1}, +2^{N-1}-1]$.
- **Unsigned overflow wraps silently** (defined behavior); **signed overflow is undefined** in C99/C11 and well-defined as wrap in C23 — never rely on it in portable firmware.
- **`volatile` is non-optional** for: (i) memory-mapped peripheral registers, (ii) variables shared between an ISR and main code, (iii) variables touched by a DMA engine, (iv) the loop-control variable in a busy-wait on hardware.
- **`const` on a pointer** means the *pointer* cannot be reseated; `const` on the *pointee* means the data is read-only. They are independent qualifiers.
- **Endianness:** ARM Cortex-M is little-endian by default. When communicating with a big-endian peer (some sensors, network byte order), always perform explicit byte-reversal via shifts and masks — never assume a cast will do it.
- **Bit-fields** are the cleanest way to model hardware registers in C, but their *allocation order within a byte* is implementation-defined — use them for readability, not for ABI-stable wire formats.
- **Structure padding** can waste 30–50% of RAM if fields are not aligned by descending size. Reorder fields from largest to smallest to minimize padding.
- **IEEE 754 single precision** is 32 bits: 1 sign + 8 biased-exponent (bias 127) + 23 mantissa with implicit leading 1. Decoding the constant $\pi$ (`0x40490FDB`) is the canonical viva.
- **Storage class specifiers** (`auto`, `static`, `extern`, `register`) interact with data types to control *lifetime* and *linkage* — `static` inside a function gives a persistent local (key for debounced button state), `extern` declares a global defined elsewhere.
- **Pointer size equals the address bus width** — 4 bytes on 32-bit ARM, 2 bytes on 8051, 2 bytes on AVR (Harvard, flash pointer is `uint16_t`).
- **`_Bool` occupies 1 byte minimum**; you cannot bit-address it. Use `bool` from `<stdbool.h>` for clarity.
- **Type promotion:** `char` and `short` are *automatically promoted* to `int` in expressions — beware in ISR routines that read 8-bit hardware flags and then compare them with 16-bit masks.

> **Final KTU Tip:** When a question says "write C code to access a register", the *first* thing the examiner scans for is the word `volatile`. If it is missing, expect to lose at least **2 marks instantly**, even if the rest of the logic is flawless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floating-Point Data Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Floating-Point Data Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Embedded System Design (PBEVT504)**, a **Floating-Point Data Type** is a numerical representation defined by the **IEEE 754 Standard for Floating-Point Arithmetic** (revised 2008 / 2019) that encodes a real number using three logical fields — a **Sign bit ($s$)**, a **Biased Exponent ($e$)**, and a **Significand/Mantissa ($f$)** — packed into a fixed-width register (typically **16-bit, 32-bit, or 64-bit**). Unlike integer or fixed-point types, the radix point is *not* fixed; it "floats" depending on the exponent value, enabling representation of both very large and very small magnitudes within the same data width.

For KTU 2024 Scheme, the floating-point data types relevant to embedded targets (typically ARM Cortex-M4/M33 with single-precision FPU, or Cortex-M7 with double-precision FPU) are:

- **`float`** — IEEE 754 **binary32** (single precision, 32-bit)
- **`double`** — IEEE 754 **binary64** (double precision, 64-bit)
- **`long double`** — IEEE 754 **binary64** or extended precision (compiler-dependent, e.g., ARM AAPCS)
- **`half`** / **`__fp16`** — IEEE 754 **binary16** (half precision, 16-bit, used in ML/AI accelerators and graphics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The single-precision (`float`, 32-bit) format is the **primary** data type tested. Students **must** memorize the exact bit partition (1 + 8 + 23), the bias value (**127**), and the range/precision formulas. Half precision is a high-yield supplementary topic under "Floating-Point Data Types."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Scientific Notation" Idea

Imagine you are a librarian with only a **single 8-digit calculator** to record the mass of every celestial object in the universe. You cannot write the Sun's mass ($1.989 \times 10^{30}$ kg) and an electron's mass ($9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg) using the same fixed decimal format. The trick is **scientific notation**: write the number as a *mantissa* (the significant digits, e.g., $1.989$) and a *power of ten* (the exponent, e.g., $+30$).

A **floating-point number does exactly the same thing in binary**:

$$
\text{Value} = (-1)^{s} \times 1.f \times 2^{(e - \text{bias})}
$$

The **mantissa** controls *precision* (how many significant digits you remember), the **exponent** controls *range* (how big or small the number can be), and the **sign bit** controls *polarity*. The "floating" in floating-point refers to the binary point drifting left or right based on the exponent — just as a decimal point "floats" in $1.23 \times 10^{5}$ vs $1.23 \times 10^{-5}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use binary, not decimal?** Embedded processors (ARM, RISC-V, AVR) have native binary ALUs. Storing numbers in base-2 means floating-point addition, multiplication, and rounding can be implemented using fast shifters and adders — critical for real-time DSP, motor control, and sensor fusion loops.

### 1.3 The Three Logical Fields (At a Glance)

| Field | Role | Bit Position in 32-bit | Bit Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sign ($s$)** | Positive / Negative | `[31]` (MSB) | **1 bit** |
| **Exponent ($e$)** | Magnitude scaling | `[30:23]` | **8 bits** |
| **Mantissa ($f$)** | Significant digits (fraction) | `[22:0]` (LSB) | **23 bits** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implicit Leading 1 Rule:** For *normalized* numbers, the mantissa is implicitly prefixed with a leading $1.$ (i.e., the effective significand is $1.f$ in binary). This gives an extra bit of precision "for free" without storing it.

### 1.4 Physical / Engineering Constants Frequently Used

The following standard metrics must be memorized for KTU 2024 ESE problems:

- **Exponent bias (single):** $127$ ($= 2^{8-1} - 1$)
- **Exponent bias (double):** $1023$ ($= 2^{11-1} - 1$)
- **Exponent bias (half):** $15$ ($= 2^{5-1} - 1$)
- **Machine epsilon (single):** $\varepsilon = 2^{-23} \approx 1.192 \times 10^{-7}$
- **Machine epsilon (double):** $\varepsilon = 2^{-52} \approx 2.220 \times 10^{-16}$
- **Maximum finite value (single):** $(2 - 2^{-23}) \times 2^{127} \approx 3.4028 \times 10^{38}$
- **Smallest positive normal (single):** $2^{-126} \approx 1.175 \times 10^{-38}$

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generalized IEEE 754 Binary Format

For a floating-point format with $p$ bits of significand precision and $q$ bits of exponent (i.e., total width $= 1 + q + p$), any real number $x$ is represented in one of four classes determined by the biased exponent $e$ and mantissa $f$:

$$
x = \begin{cases}
(-1)^{s} \times 2^{(1-\text{bias})} \times 0.f, & \text{if } e = 0 \text{ and } f \neq 0 \quad (\text{Subnormal / Denormal}) \\
(-1)^{s} \times 2^{(e - \text{bias})} \times 1.f, & \text{if } 0 < e < 2^{q} - 1 \quad (\text{Normalized}) \\
(-1)^{s} \times \infty, & \text{if } e = 2^{q} - 1 \text{ and } f = 0 \quad (\text{Infinity}) \\
\text{NaN}, & \text{if } e = 2^{q} - 1 \text{ and } f \neq 0 \quad (\text{Not a Number})
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Encoding Scheme

1. **Sign bit ($s$)**: Captures the sign of the number. $s = 0$ means positive, $s = 1$ means negative. Why? It allows the magnitude circuit to be reused for $|x|$, simplifying hardware.
2. **Biased exponent ($e$)**: Stores the exponent as an *unsigned* integer, then subtracts a fixed **bias** during interpretation. Why bias? It converts all exponents to non-negative values, eliminating the need for a separate sign-of-exponent bit and making integer comparators usable for floating-point ordering.
3. **Mantissa ($f$)**: Stores only the **fractional** bits after the leading $1.$ (the leading $1$ is implicit for normal numbers). Why implicit? It doubles the effective precision without using an extra bit.
4. **Subnormal handling**: When $e = 0$ but $f \neq 0$, the leading implicit $1$ is replaced by $0$, allowing a graceful underflow down to $\pm 2^{-149}$ (for single) without a sudden "gap" at zero. This is called **gradual underflow**.
5. **Special encodings**: $e = \text{all-ones}$ reserves two encodings — Infinity (when $f = 0$) and NaN (when $f \neq 0$) — to represent results of undefined operations like $0/0$ or $\sqrt{-1}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall — Implicit Bit Rule:** Students often forget that normalized numbers have an *implicit* leading $1.$ The mantissa bits stored are **only the fractional part**. If stored mantissa is $0110\ldots0$, the true significand is $1.0110\ldots0_2$, **not** $0.0110\ldots0_2$.

### 2.3 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula (Single Precision, $q=8$, $p=23$) | Formula (General $q, p$) | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bias | $127$ | $2^{q-1} - 1$ | Unitless |
| 2 | Max biased exponent | $254$ | $2^{q} - 2$ | Reserved 255 for Inf/NaN |
| 3 | Min biased exponent (normal) | $1$ | $1$ | Unitless |
| 4 | True exponent range (normal) | $-126$ to $+127$ | $-(2^{q-1}-2)$ to $+(2^{q-1}-1)$ | Unitless integer |
| 5 | Largest finite value | $(2 - 2^{-23}) \times 2^{+127}$ | $(2 - 2^{-(p-1)}) \times 2^{2^{q-1}-1}$ | Real number $\approx 3.4028 \times 10^{38}$ |
| 6 | Smallest positive normal | $1.0 \times 2^{-126}$ | $1.0 \times 2^{-(2^{q-1}-2)}$ | Real number $\approx 1.175 \times 10^{-38}$ |
| 7 | Smallest positive subnormal | $2^{-149}$ | $2^{-(2^{q-1}-2)-(p-1)}$ | Real number $\approx 1.401 \times 10^{-45}$ |
| 8 | Machine epsilon (ULP at 1.0) | $2^{-23} \approx 1.192 \times 10^{-7}$ | $2^{-(p-1)}$ | Relative spacing |
| 9 | Decimal digits of precision | $\approx 7.22$ | $p \cdot \log_{10}(2) \approx 0.301\,p$ | Significant digits |
| 10 | Total representable values | $2^{32}$ | $2^{(1+q+p)}$ | Discrete count |
| 11 | Generic value (normal) | $(-1)^{s} \cdot 1.f \cdot 2^{e-127}$ | $(-1)^{s} \cdot 1.f \cdot 2^{e-\text{bias}}$ | Real number |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule:** In the formula table, any notation like $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) has been intentionally avoided. KTU 2024 evaluation scripts and Markdown parsers will break tables containing unescaped `|` characters. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` (LaTeX) when writing absolute values outside tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ARM Cortex-M4 FPU (Single Precision)**: Used in STM32F4, NXP Kinetis, and TI Tiva-C for motor control (FOC algorithms), audio DSP, and IMU sensor fusion. Hardware single-cycle multiply-accumulate (MAC) operates on `float32`.
- **ARM Cortex-M7 (Double Precision)**: Used in high-end MCUs (STM32H7) for radar signal processing, image processing, and machine learning inference at the edge.
- **Half Precision (`__fp16`)**: Dominant in TinyML frameworks (TensorFlow Lite Micro, CMSIS-NN) for quantized neural network weights, since ML tolerates lower dynamic range.
- **Cortex-M33 with Helium (M-Profile Vector Extension)**: Supports SIMD-style operations on half-precision vectors, doubling throughput for ML workloads.

### 2.5 Floating-Point vs Fixed-Point — The Embedded Trade-off

| Property | Floating-Point (`float`) | Fixed-Point (`Q15`, `Q31`) |
|---|---|---|
| **Range** | Very large ($\sim 10^{\pm 38}$) | Limited, format-dependent |
| **Precision** | Variable (drops near overflow) | Uniform across range |
| **Hardware cost** | Requires FPU (extra silicon / power) | Pure integer ALU |
| **Determinism** | May vary across cores / compilers | Fully deterministic |
| **Code complexity** | Natural, human-readable | Manual scaling needed |
| **Typical use** | DSP, control, ML | Low-power MCUs (Cortex-M0), codecs |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Range of Single-Precision Floating-Point Numbers

**Goal:** Derive the maximum finite value and the smallest positive normal value representable in IEEE 754 binary32.

**Step 1 — Identify the bit fields.** For binary32, $q = 8$ (exponent bits), $p = 23$ (mantissa bits), bias $= 2^{q-1} - 1 = 2^{7} - 1 = 127$.

**Step 2 — Find the maximum allowed biased exponent.** The value $e = 2^{q} - 1 = 255$ is **reserved** for $\pm\infty$ and NaN. Therefore the largest usable biased exponent is:

$$
e_{\max} = 2^{q} - 2 = 255 - 1 = 254
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the largest true (unbiased) exponent.** Subtracting the bias:

$$
E_{\max} = e_{\max} - \text{bias} = 254 - 127 = 127
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the largest mantissa value.** The mantissa bits $f$ can be at most all-ones, i.e., $f_{\max} = 1 - 2^{-p} = 1 - 2^{-23}$. The full significand (with implicit leading $1.$) is:

$$
1.f_{\max} = 1 + (1 - 2^{-23}) = 2 - 2^{-23}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the maximum finite value.** The sign bit is $0$ (positive) for the magnitude:

$$
V_{\max} = (2 - 2^{-23}) \times 2^{127}
$$

Evaluating numerically:

$$
V_{\max} = (2 - 2^{-23}) \times 2^{127} \approx 1.9999998808 \times 1.7014118 \times 10^{38} \approx 3.4028235 \times 10^{38}
$$

> **Step 6 — Note for KTU:** Many textbooks simply write $V_{\max} \approx (2 - \varepsilon) \cdot 2^{127}$ where $\varepsilon = 2^{-23}$. Both forms are accepted; the decimal form above is often required in numerical answer keys.

**Step 7 — Derive the smallest positive *normal* value.** The smallest usable biased exponent is $e_{\min} = 1$ (since $e = 0$ is reserved for subnormals and zero). The true exponent is:

$$
E_{\min} = 1 - 127 = -126
$$

With mantissa $= 0$ (significand $= 1.0$):

$$
V_{\min,\text{normal}} = 1.0 \times 2^{-126} \approx 1.1754944 \times 10^{-38}
$$

**Step 8 — Derive the smallest positive *subnormal* value.** For subnormals, the leading $1.$ is replaced by $0.$, and the true exponent is fixed at $E_{\min} = -126$. The mantissa has only the LSB set:

$$
V_{\min,\text{subnormal}} = 0.\underbrace{0\ldots0}_{22}\,1 \times 2^{-126} = 2^{-23} \times 2^{-126} = 2^{-149} \approx 1.4012985 \times 10^{-45}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Decimal Precision of Single Precision

**Step 1 — Start from the mantissa bit count.** With $p = 23$ stored fraction bits plus $1$ implicit bit, the total significand precision is:

$$
p_{\text{total}} = p + 1 = 24 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert bits to decimal digits.** Each binary digit corresponds to $\log_{10}(2)$ decimal digits. The number of reliable decimal digits is:

$$
D = p_{\text{total}} \times \log_{10}(2) = 24 \times 0.30103 \approx 7.2247
$$

**Step 3 — Round down to the conservative floor.** Because of rounding, the number of *guaranteed correct* decimal digits is $\lfloor D \rfloor = 7$. KTU 2024 answer: **single precision gives approximately 7 decimal digits of precision**.

**Step 4 — Apply the same to double precision.** With $p = 52$:

$$
D_{\text{double}} = 53 \times 0.30103 \approx 15.95 \implies \text{about 15-16 decimal digits}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply to half precision.** With $p = 10$:

$$
D_{\text{half}} = 11 \times 0.30103 \approx 3.31 \implies \text{about 3-4 decimal digits}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Answer Rule:** For any IEEE 754 format with $p$ mantissa bits, the decimal precision is $\lfloor (p+1) \cdot \log_{10}(2) \rfloor = \lfloor 0.301 \cdot (p+1) \rfloor$. Memorize the *exact* results: half → 3, single → 7, double → 15.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Encoding $x = -12.625$ in IEEE 754 Single Precision

**Step 1 — Convert the absolute value to binary.** $12.625 = 12 + 0.625 = 1100_2 + 0.101_2 = 1100.101_2$.

**Step 2 — Normalize to $1.\text{fraction} \times 2^{\text{exp}}$.** Shift the binary point three places left:

$$
1100.101_2 = 1.100101_2 \times 2^{3}
$$

So the *unbiased* exponent is $E = 3$ and the fractional part is $f = 100101\ldots0_2$ (padded with zeros to 23 bits).

**Step 3 — Compute the biased exponent.** $e = E + 127 = 3 + 127 = 130$. In 8-bit binary: $e = 10000010_2$.

**Step 4 — Set the sign bit.** $x$ is negative, so $s = 1$.

**Step 5 — Concatenate the three fields (MSB → LSB).**

$$
\underbrace{1}_{s}\;\underbrace{10000010}_{e}\;\underbrace{10010100000000000000000}_{f}
$$

**Step 6 — Express the result as a 32-bit hex word.** Group into nibbles: `1100 0001 0100 1010 0000 0000 0000 0000` = `0xC14A0000`.

> **Verification (optional):** 
> $$x = (-1)^{1} \times 1.100101_2 \times 2^{130 - 127} = -1.100101_2 \times 2^{3} = -1100.101_2 = -12.625 \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Format Comparison Table (Critical for KTU)

| Parameter | Half (binary16) | Single (binary32) | Double (binary64) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total width | **16 bits** | **32 bits** | **64 bits** |
| Sign bits | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| Exponent bits ($q$) | 5 | 8 | 11 |
| Mantissa bits ($p$) | 10 | 23 | 52 |
| Implicit leading bit | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Bias | $15$ | $127$ | $1023$ |
| True exponent range | $-14$ to $+15$ | $-126$ to $+127$ | $-1022$ to $+1023$ |
| Max finite value | $65504$ | $\approx 3.4028 \times 10^{38}$ | $\approx 1.7977 \times 10^{308}$ |
| Smallest positive normal | $\approx 6.10 \times 10^{-5}$ | $\approx 1.175 \times 10^{-38}$ | $\approx 2.225 \times 10^{-308}$ |
| Smallest positive subnormal | $\approx 5.96 \times 10^{-8}$ | $\approx 1.401 \times 10^{-45}$ | $\approx 4.94 \times 10^{-324}$ |
| Machine epsilon | $2^{-10} \approx 9.77 \times 10^{-4}$ | $2^{-23} \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-7}$ | $2^{-52} \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-16}$ |
| Decimal digits of precision | $\approx 3$ | $\approx 7$ | $\approx 15$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Memorization Block:** The three rows the examiner will most likely test are **(a) bias values 15 / 127 / 1023**, **(b) mantissa bits 10 / 23 / 52**, and **(c) max exponents +15 / +127 / +1023**. Master these and you secure a guaranteed 3-4 marks in any related question.

---

### 3.5 Derivation — Subnormal (Denormal) Number Behavior

**Step 1 — Identify the trigger.** Subnormal numbers are activated when the biased exponent $e = 0$ *and* the mantissa $f \neq 0$.

**Step 2 — Modify the implicit bit rule.** For subnormals, the leading $1.$ is replaced by $0.$, and the true exponent is *fixed* at $E_{\min} = 1 - \text{bias}$ (not $0 - \text{bias}$). This ensures that the smallest subnormal equals $2^{-(p-1)} \times 2^{E_{\min}}$ with a *smooth* transition to zero.

**Step 3 — Example for single precision.**

$$
V_{\min,\text{subnormal}} = 0.\underbrace{0\ldots0}_{22}1_2 \times 2^{-126} = 2^{-23} \times 2^{-126} = 2^{-149}
$$

**Step 4 — Why this matters in embedded DSP.** Subnormals are *very slow* on most FPUs (they require microcode traps, e.g., on ARM Cortex-M4). Production code in KTU-style problems often disables them via the FPU configuration register (`FPDSCR.AOFF = 1`) to gain determinism — at the cost of flushing subnormals to zero.

### 4.2 Floating-Point Decode Pipeline — Functional Block Architecture

When an embedded CPU fetches a `float` from memory, the FPU follows a deterministic decode-evaluate pipeline. The diagram below shows the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the interactions during IEEE 754 evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef ioCls fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef procCls fill:#e2ecfd,stroke:#36c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDecCls fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#aa8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classResCls fill:#d6f7d6,stroke:#3a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    fetch["STEP A FETCH 32 BITS FROM MEMORY"]:::ioCls
    split["STEP B BIT SLICE INTO FIELDS s e f"]:::procCls
    subNormCheck{"STEP C e equals 0"}:::procCls
    infNaNCheck{"STEP D e equals 2 to q minus 1"}:::procCls
    unbias["STEP E COMPUTE TRUE EXPONENT E equals e minus BIAS"]:::procCls
    implicitAdd["STEP F PREPEND IMPLICIT 1 DOT FRACTION"]:::procCls
    implicitZero["STEP F PRIME PREPEND IMPLICIT 0 DOT FRACTION"]:::procCls
    specialInf["STEP G OUTPUT INFINITY OR NAN"]:::resCls
    assemble["STEP H ASSEMBLE FINAL VALUE V equals NEG ONE TO S times SIGNIFICAND times TWO TO E"]:::resCls
    out["STEP I DELIVER REAL NUMBER V TO ALU"]:::ioCls

    fetch --> split
    split --> subNormCheck
    subNormCheck -- "yes and f equals 0" --> infNaNCheck
    subNormCheck -- "yes and f not 0" --> implicitZero
    subNormCheck -- "no" --> infNaNCheck
    infNaNCheck -- "yes" --> specialInf
    infNaNCheck -- "no" --> unbias
    unbias --> implicitAdd
    implicitAdd --> assemble
    implicitZero --> unbias
    specialInf --> out
    assemble --> out
```

### 4.4 Round-off Error Accumulation — Embedded DSP Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef opCls fill:#e2ecfd,stroke:#36c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef storeCls fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#aa8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    multiply["COMPUTE PRODUCT y equals a times x OF k"]:::opCls
    round1["ROUND TO NEAREST EPSILON equals 2 POWER MINUS 23"]:::storeCls
    accumulate["COMPUTE SUM s equals s plus y"]:::opCls
    round2["ROUND AGAIN SECOND ROUND EVENT"]:::storeCls
    err1["TOTAL ERROR AFTER N ITERATIONS UP TO N TIMES EPSILON"]:::errCls
    output["OUTPUT FINAL VALUE s AS FLOAT RESULT"]:::resCls

    start --> sample
    sample --> multiply
    multiply --> round1
    round1 --> accumulate
    accumulate --> round2
    round2 --> err1
    err1 --> output
    output -- "next k" --> sample
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The `ROUND1` and `ROUND2` blocks indicate the two rounding events per multiply-accumulate (MAC) cycle. This is the classic structure used to illustrate why long DSP loops in `float32` can drift by up to $N \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{machine}}$ — a common 14-mark question theme.
