<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conditional Statements** in embedded C programming are **selection constructs** that enable the processor to execute specific blocks of code based on the evaluation of a Boolean expression (condition). They form the core of decision-making logic in any embedded firmware, allowing the microcontroller (e.g., **8051**, **ARM Cortex-M**) to respond dynamically to sensor inputs, user actions, and internal states.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for Embedded System Design (PBEVT504), conditional statements are classified under the **C language constructs used for hardware programming**, typically discussed in tandem with `if`, `if-else`, `nested if-else`, `else-if ladder`, and `switch-case` statements.

### Types of Conditional Statements in Embedded C:

1. **Simple `if` statement** – Executes a block only if the condition is TRUE.
2. **`if-else` statement** – Provides two mutually exclusive execution paths.
3. **Nested `if-else`** – An `if` or `else` block contains another `if-else` structure.
4. **`else-if` ladder** – Sequential multi-way branching for chained conditions.
5. **`switch-case`** – Multi-way branching based on discrete value matching.
6. **Ternary (Conditional) operator `? :`** – Compact inline conditional assignment.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: Traffic Signal Decision System**
>
> Imagine a traffic police officer at a junction. The officer's *brain* (microcontroller) receives *sensor input* (cars, pedestrians, timer). Based on the input, the officer *decides* which action to take:
> - **If** cars are waiting AND timer expired → Turn signal **GREEN**
> - **Else if** pedestrians pressed button AND timer not expired → Turn signal **RED**
> - **Else** → Maintain current state
>
> This real-time decision-making is exactly what conditional statements perform inside a microcontroller. Each `if` is a *test*, each branch is a *decision outcome*, and the *sensor reading* is the *condition*.

### Why are Conditional Statements Critical in Embedded Systems?

| Application Domain | Use of Conditional Statements |
|---|---|
| **Automotive (ABS, Airbag)** | Sensor threshold checks (e.g., `if (deceleration > 5g) deploy_airbag();`) |
| **IoT / Smart Home** | Mode selection (e.g., `if (temp > 30°C) turn_on_fan();`) |
| **Industrial Control** | Safety interlocks (e.g., `if (door_open && motor_running) emergency_stop();`) |
| **Consumer Electronics** | User input handling (e.g., `switch(button) { case BTN_UP: vol++; break; }`) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded-Specific Note:** In 8051/ARM programming, conditional statements often involve **bit-level operations** on Special Function Registers (SFRs). For example: `if (P1 & 0x01)` checks if bit 0 of Port 1 is set — a direct hardware read translated into a Boolean condition.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Condition** | A Boolean expression evaluating to TRUE (non-zero) or FALSE (zero) |
| **Branch** | An execution path taken based on condition outcome |
| **Fall-through** | (in switch) When execution continues into the next case due to a missing `break` |
| **Default case** | The fallback branch in `switch` executed when no `case` matches |
| **Nested conditional** | A conditional statement placed inside another conditional block |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Simple `if` Statement — Operational Breakdown

**Syntax:**
```c
if (condition) {
    // statements executed ONLY if condition is TRUE
}
```

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. The microcontroller evaluates the **condition** (a relational/logical expression).
2. In C, **any non-zero value is TRUE**; **zero is FALSE**.
3. If TRUE → control enters the block; otherwise, the block is **skipped entirely**.
4. Execution continues to the statement *after* the `if` block.

**Truth Table (KTU Favourite):**

| Condition Value | Result | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Non-zero (TRUE) | Logical 1 | Execute `if` block |
| Zero (FALSE) | Logical 0 | Skip `if` block |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Embedded Insight:** In 8051 C (Keil), the compiler typically uses the **JC (Jump if Carry)** or **JNZ (Jump if Not Zero)** assembly instructions to implement `if`. So `if (P1 == 0xFF)` becomes approximately `MOV A, P1; XRL A, #0FFH; JNZ skip_label`.

---

## 2.2 The `if-else` Statement — Two-Way Decision

**Syntax:**
```c
if (condition) {
    // Block A: executed if condition is TRUE
} else {
    // Block B: executed if condition is FALSE
}
```

**Why It Matters in Embedded Systems:** In real-time control, the system must **always** respond. `if-else` guarantees *one* of two actions is taken — critical for sensor polling loops and watchdog routines.

---

## 2.3 Nested `if-else` — Hierarchical Decision Trees

**Syntax:**
```c
if (condition1) {
    if (condition2) {
        // executed if BOTH condition1 AND condition2 are TRUE
    } else {
        // executed if condition1 TRUE and condition2 FALSE
    }
} else {
    // executed if condition1 is FALSE
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Indentation Discipline (KTU Valuation Tip):** Always use a consistent indentation (typically 4 spaces or 1 tab). KTU examiners often deduct marks for "unreadable" nested logic, even if the code is correct.

---

## 2.4 The `else-if` Ladder — Multi-Way Branching

**Syntax:**
```c
if (condition1) {
    // block 1
} else if (condition2) {
    // block 2
} else if (condition3) {
    // block 3
} else {
    // default block
}
```

**Evaluation Order:** Conditions are tested **sequentially from top to bottom**. As soon as one is TRUE, its block executes, and the rest of the ladder is **skipped**.

---

## 2.5 The `switch-case` Statement — Discrete Value Matching

**Syntax:**
```c
switch (expression) {
    case constant_1:
        // statements
        break;
    case constant_2:
        // statements
        break;
    default:
        // statements
}
```

**Critical Rules (KTU Board Exam Hot Topic):**
1. The `expression` must evaluate to an **integer type** (`char`, `int`, `enum`).
2. `case` labels must be **constant integer expressions** (cannot be variables).
3. **`break` is essential** to prevent fall-through (except in intentional cases).
4. The `default` clause is **optional but recommended**.

**`switch` vs `else-if` Decision Matrix:**

| Criterion | `switch-case` | `else-if ladder` |
|---|---|---|
| Best for | Discrete, finite set of integer values | Range-based or complex boolean conditions |
| Speed | Compiler optimizes to **jump table** → O(1) | Sequential evaluation → O(n) |
| Readability | Cleaner for 5+ branches | Cleaner for 2–4 branches |
| Expression type | Integer / character only | Any expression |

---

## 2.6 The Ternary (Conditional) Operator

**Syntax:**
```c
result = (condition) ? value_if_true : value_if_false;
```

**Example:**
```c
unsigned char led_state = (sensor > THRESHOLD) ? 0xFF : 0x00;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Embedded Use Case:** Ternary operators are popular in **register initialization** and **port configuration** because they produce compact, single-statement code. However, KTU examiners often expect the full `if-else` form for clarity in exams.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Syntax Skeleton | When to Use | KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| `if` | `if (cond) { ... }` | Single condition, single action | Forgetting braces → dangling `if` |
| `if-else` | `if (cond) { ... } else { ... }` | Mutually exclusive binary choice | Using `=` instead of `==` |
| Nested `if` | `if (c1) { if (c2) { ... } }` | Hierarchical conditions | Excessive nesting (max 3 levels recommended) |
| `else-if` | `if..else if..else if..else` | Multi-way range checks | Order matters — most restrictive first |
| `switch` | `switch(expr) { case k: ... break; }` | Discrete value matching | Missing `break` → unintended fall-through |
| Ternary | `expr ? v1 : v2` | Inline conditional assignment | Hard to debug; avoid in complex logic |

| Logical Operator | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `&&` | Logical AND | `if (temp > 30 && humidity > 70)` |
| `\|\|` | Logical OR | `if (button == BTN1 \|\| button == BTN2)` |
| `!` | Logical NOT | `if (!flag)` |
| `&` | Bitwise AND | `if (P1 & 0x01)` |
| `\|` | Bitwise OR | `if (status & 0x80)` |
| `^` | Bitwise XOR | `if (P3 ^ 0xFF)` |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Exercise 1: Truth Table Derivation for a Nested `if-else`

**Problem:** Construct the complete truth table for the following embedded logic used in a 2-button security system:

```c
if (B1 == 1) {
    if (B2 == 1) {
        access = GRANTED;   // access = 1
    } else {
        access = PARTIAL;   // access = 2
    }
} else {
    if (B2 == 1) {
        access = DENIED;    // access = 0
    } else {
        access = LOCKED;    // access = 3
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Step 1: Identify the input variables.
- $B_1 \in \{0, 1\}$ (Button 1 state)
- $B_2 \in \{0, 1\}$ (Button 2 state)

Step 2: Compute all $2^2 = 4$ input combinations.

Step 3: Apply the decision logic sequentially.

$$
\text{Result} = \begin{cases}
\text{GRANTED} & \text{if } B_1 = 1 \text{ AND } B_2 = 1 \\
\text{PARTIAL} & \text{if } B_1 = 1 \text{ AND } B_2 = 0 \\
\text{DENIED}  & \text{if } B_1 = 0 \text{ AND } B_2 = 1 \\
\text{LOCKED}  & \text{if } B_1 = 0 \text{ AND } B_2 = 0
\end{cases}
$$

**Truth Table (Final Result):**

| $B_1$ | $B_2$ | Path Taken | `access` value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Outer `else` → Inner `else` | 3 (LOCKED) |
| 0 | 1 | Outer `else` → Inner `if` | 0 (DENIED) |
| 1 | 0 | Outer `if` → Inner `else` | 2 (PARTIAL) |
| 1 | 1 | Outer `if` → Inner `if` | 1 (GRANTED) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This pattern is used in **dual-factor authentication locks**, **industrial safety interlocks** (two-hand control), and **fail-safe elevator doors** (both sensors must agree).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Exercise 2: Equivalent Switch Implementation

**Problem:** Convert the following `else-if` ladder into a `switch-case` statement.

```c
if (op_code == 1)      result = 10;
else if (op_code == 2) result = 20;
else if (op_code == 3) result = 30;
else if (op_code == 4) result = 40;
else                   result = 0;
```

**Step-by-Step Conversion:**

Step 1: Identify the discriminating expression: `op_code`.

Step 2: Map each `==` test to a `case` label.

Step 3: Add `break` after each `case` to prevent fall-through.

Step 4: Map the final `else` to `default`.

**Equivalent `switch-case`:**

```c
switch (op_code) {
    case 1:  result = 10;  break;
    case 2:  result = 20;  break;
    case 3:  result = 30;  break;
    case 4:  result = 40;  break;
    default: result = 0;   break;
}
```

**Why This Conversion Works (Mathematical Justification):**

The `else-if` ladder performs a **sequential search**:

$$
T(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(linear time complexity)}
$$

The `switch-case`, when compiled, typically uses a **jump table**:

$$
T(n) = O(1) \quad \text{(constant time complexity)}
$$

For an embedded system with a **fixed latency budget** (e.g., 100 µs response time), `switch-case` is preferable for handling command opcodes in communication protocols like **UART command interpreters**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Exercise 3: Decision Logic for Battery Management System (BMS)

**Problem:** Design the conditional logic for a Lithium-ion BMS that decides charging current based on Voltage, Temperature, and State of Charge (SOC).

**Decision Matrix:**

| Voltage (V) | Temperature (°C) | SOC (%) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| $< 3.0$ | Any | Any | PRECHARGE (0.1C) |
| $3.0$ to $4.2$ | $> 45$ | Any | STOP (over-temp) |
| $3.0$ to $4.2$ | $< 0$ | Any | STOP (under-temp) |
| $3.0$ to $4.2$ | $0$ to $45$ | $< 80$ | FAST CHARGE (1C) |
| $3.0$ to $4.2$ | $0$ to $45$ | $\geq 80$ | TRICKLE (0.2C) |
| $> 4.2$ | Any | Any | STOP (over-voltage) |

**Implementation as `else-if` Ladder:**

```c
if (voltage < 3.0) {
    charge_current = 0.1 * CAPACITY;
} else if (voltage > 4.2) {
    charge_current = 0;       // OVER-VOLTAGE
} else if (temperature > 45) {
    charge_current = 0;       // OVER-TEMP
} else if (temperature < 0) {
    charge_current = 0;       // UNDER-TEMP
} else if (soc < 80) {
    charge_current = 1.0 * CAPACITY;  // FAST CHARGE
} else {
    charge_current = 0.2 * CAPACITY;  // TRICKLE
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of Evaluation is Critical:** Notice how the **safety-critical conditions** (over-voltage, over-temp) are checked *first*. This is a **defensive programming** pattern — KTU examiners love testing whether students place safety checks at the top of the ladder.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Exercise 4: Ternary Operator Decomposition

**Problem:** Decompose the following ternary expression into its equivalent `if-else` form.

```c
unsigned char port_val = (P1 & 0x80) ? 0xFF : 0x00;
```

**Step-by-Step Decomposition:**

Step 1: Identify the condition: `(P1 & 0x80)`.

Step 2: Recognize that `P1 & 0x80` is **non-zero** (specifically `0x80`) only if **bit 7 of P1 is set**.

Step 3: The condition is TRUE when bit 7 = 1, FALSE when bit 7 = 0.

**Equivalent `if-else`:**

```c
unsigned char port_val;
if (P1 & 0x80) {
    port_val = 0xFF;
} else {
    port_val = 0x00;
}
```

**Boolean Algebra Verification:**

Let $P_7$ denote bit 7 of port P1.

$$
\text{port\_val} = \begin{cases}
0\text{xFF} & \text{if } P_7 = 1 \\
0\text{x00} & \text{if } P_7 = 0
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\therefore \text{port\_val} = P_7 \cdot 0\text{xFF} + \overline{P_7} \cdot 0\text{x00} = P_7 \cdot 0\text{xFF}
$$

This confirms the ternary operator acts as a **1-bit demultiplexer** selecting between two constants.

---

## 3.5 Master Comparison Matrix: All Conditional Constructs

| Property | `if` | `if-else` | Nested `if` | `else-if` | `switch` | Ternary |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **No. of branches** | 1 | 2 | $\geq 2$ | $n$ | $n$ | 2 |
| **Expression type** | Any | Any | Any | Any | Integer only | Any |
| **Readability** | High | High | Medium | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Compiler optimization** | Branch | Branch | Branch | Branch | Jump table | Branch / MOVE |
| **Best use case** | Simple checks | Binary decision | Hierarchical logic | Range checks | Opcode dispatch | Inline assignment |
| **KTU exam frequency** | High | High | High | Very High | Very High | Medium |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single `if` Statement — Control Flow Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Program] --> B{Evaluate Condition}
    B -- TRUE non-zero --> C[Execute if block]
    B -- FALSE zero --> D[Skip if block]
    C --> E[Continue program]
    D --> E[Continue program]
    E --> F[End / Next Statement]
```

**Description:** The diamond node `B` represents the conditional test. If TRUE → execute block C, else skip directly to E. This is the simplest control-flow construct.

---

## 4.2 `if-else` Statement — Control Flow Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B{condition true}
    B -- TRUE --> C[Execute Block A]
    B -- FALSE --> D[Execute Block B]
    C --> E[Continue Program]
    D --> E[Continue Program]
```

**Description:** Guarantees exactly one of the two blocks is executed — a *mutually exclusive* path.

---

## 4.3 `else-if` Ladder — Decision Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B{condition1}
    B -- TRUE --> X1[Execute Block1] --> E[Continue]
    B -- FALSE --> C{condition2}
    C -- TRUE --> X2[Execute Block2] --> E
    C -- FALSE --> D{condition3}
    D -- TRUE --> X3[Execute Block3] --> E
    D -- FALSE --> Y[Execute default else block] --> E
```

**Description:** Conditions are evaluated **top-to-bottom, short-circuit on first TRUE**. This is the workhorse of multi-way embedded decision logic (mode selection, sensor threshold tiers, FSM state decoding).

---

## 4.4 `switch-case` — Jump Table Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Evaluate switch expression]
    B --> C{Does value match any case}
    C -- match case 1 --> X1[Execute case1 break] --> E[Continue]
    C -- match case 2 --> X2[Execute case2 break] --> E
    C -- match case 3 --> X3[Execute case3 break] --> E
    C -- no match --> Y[Execute default break] --> E
    E --> F[Next Statement]
```

**Description:** The compiler generates a **jump table** indexed by the expression value. Execution jumps *directly* to the matching case, achieving O(1) dispatch — critical for **real-time interrupt handlers** and **command processors**.

---

## 4.5 Nested `if-else` — Two-Button Security Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Read B1 and B2] --> B{B1 equals 1}
    B -- YES --> C{B2 equals 1}
    B -- NO --> D{B2 equals 1}
    C -- YES --> E[Access GRANTED]
    C -- NO --> F[Access PARTIAL]
    D -- YES --> G[Access DENIED]
    D -- NO --> H[Access LOCKED]
    E --> I[Continue]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

**Description:** This visualizes the truth-table exercise from Section 3.1. Each unique combination of inputs leads to exactly one terminal node.

---

## 4.6 Battery Management System — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Read V T SOC] --> B{V less than 3.0}
    B -- YES --> X1[PRECHARGE 0.1C] --> Z[Set charge current]
    B -- NO --> C{V greater than 4.2}
    C -- YES --> X2[STOP over voltage] --> Z
    C -- NO --> D{T greater than 45}
    D -- YES --> X3[STOP over temp] --> Z
    D -- NO --> E{T less than 0}
    E -- YES --> X4[STOP under temp] --> Z
    E -- NO --> F{SOC less than 80 percent}
    F -- YES --> X5[FAST CHARGE 1.0C] --> Z
    F -- NO --> X6[TRICKLE 0.2C] --> Z
```

**Description:** Models the BMS logic from Section 3.3. Safety-critical conditions (voltage/temperature extremes) are evaluated **first** to ensure fail-safe operation.

---

## 4.7 Block-Level Comparison: How Compilers Translate Conditionals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Level if else if switch ternary] --> B[Compiler Front End]
    B --> C[Intermediate Representation]
    C --> D{Optimization Pass}
    D --> E1[8051: JC JZ JNZ] --> F1[Assembly Jump Instructions]
    D --> E2[ARM: B EQ B NE BGT] --> F2[Branch Instructions]
    D --> E3[Switch Jump Table] --> F3[Indirect Branch LDR PC address]
```

**Description:** Shows the high-level translation pipeline. The same C code may compile to **branch instructions** (`if-else`, ternary) or **indirect jump via table** (`switch`) depending on the structure — directly impacting code size and execution speed in resource-constrained MCUs.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between `if-else` statement and `switch-case` statement in C. In which scenario would you prefer `switch-case` over `if-else`?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `if-else` | `switch-case` |
|---|---|---|
| **Expression type** | Any expression (relational, logical, bitwise) | Integer / character constants only |
| **Branching style** | Sequential boolean evaluation | Direct value matching |
| **Compiler optimization** | Conditional jumps | Jump table → O(1) |
| **Multiple values per case** | Not possible | Possible using multiple `case` labels (fall-through) |
| **Readability for many branches** | Decreases with branch count | Stays clean |

**Scenario:** `switch-case` is preferred when a single variable is compared against **multiple discrete constant integer values** (e.g., opcode decoding, menu selection, state machine transitions).

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark Answer Strategy:** Provide a 2-column table with at least 3 distinct points + a one-line concluding scenario. KTU examiners expect this tabular format for differentiation questions.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is meant by "fall-through" in a `switch-case` statement? How can it be prevented?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** *Fall-through* occurs when, after executing the statements of a matching `case`, control **does not exit** the switch block but instead **continues executing the statements** of the subsequent case(s) — because the `break` statement is missing.

**Example:**
```c
switch (x) {
    case 1:  printf("One");   // NO break here
    case 2:  printf("Two");   // executes BOTH for x=1
    case 3:  printf("Three"); break;
}
```

**Prevention:** Place a **`break;`** statement at the end of each `case` block. The `break` causes immediate exit from the switch block.

**Intentional Fall-through:** Sometimes fall-through is *intentional* — when one case label should share the code of the next (e.g., `case 1: case 2: case 3: Monday(); break;`).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *fall-through* with *loop fall-through* or *error in if-else*. The answer MUST specifically mention the missing `break` keyword in a `switch` context.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the `else-if` ladder construct in C with its general syntax. Write a program segment to read a temperature value from a sensor and display a message based on the following conditions using `else-if` ladder: **(7 marks)**
- Temperature < 0°C → "FREEZING"
- 0–15°C → "COLD"
- 16–30°C → "NORMAL"
- 31–40°C → "HOT"
- \> 40°C → "EXTREME HEAT"

**(b)** Convert the above `else-if` ladder into an equivalent `switch-case` statement. Justify why the conversion may not be *perfectly* equivalent and propose a hybrid solution. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Syntax of `else-if` Ladder:**
```c
if (condition1) {
    // block 1
} else if (condition2) {
    // block 2
} else if (condition3) {
    // block 3
} else {
    // default block
}
```

**Program Segment:**
```c
#include <reg51.h>   // 8051 header (or appropriate MCU header)

void UART_Init(void);          // Assume UART initialization
void UART_TxString(char *s);   // Assume transmit function

void Display_Temperature_Message(int temp) {
    if (temp < 0) {
        UART_TxString("FREEZING");
    } else if (temp <= 15) {
        UART_TxString("COLD");
    } else if (temp <= 30) {
        UART_TxString("NORMAL");
    } else if (temp <= 40) {
        UART_TxString("HOT");
    } else {
        UART_TxString("EXTREME HEAT");
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Logic Flow:**

Step 1: Sensor reading is passed as integer `temp`.

Step 2: The ladder checks conditions **sequentially from top to bottom**.

Step 3: The first TRUE condition executes its block; all subsequent conditions are skipped.

Step 4: Boundaries are carefully chosen using `<=` to avoid overlap (e.g., `temp == 15` is caught by "COLD", not "NORMAL").

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition Tip:** Notice how we use `temp <= 15` (not `temp < 15`) so the boundary value 15 belongs to "COLD" and 16 begins "NORMAL". This is the **upper-inclusive** convention.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Attempted Switch Conversion:**
```c
switch (temp) {
    case (... -1):  UART_TxString("FREEZING"); break;  // NOT VALID in C
    case 0:  UART_TxString("FREEZING"); break;
    case 1:  UART_TxString("COLD"); break;
    // ... this is impractical!
}
```

**Why the Conversion is Imperfect:**

A `switch-case` statement can only test **discrete, constant integer values** — not *ranges* like "0 to 15". Writing cases for every integer in every range would be:
- **Infeasible** (would require thousands of cases)
- **Not scalable** (adding a new range means adding hundreds of cases)
- **Compiler-dependent** (some compilers limit the number of cases)

**Proposed Hybrid Solution:**

For mixed scenarios (some ranges + some discrete values), use a **nested `if-else` inside `switch`** or vice versa:

```c
switch (temp / 10) {       // Integer division reduces to range buckets
    case 0:                // temp in 0..9 → sub-classify
        if (temp < 0) UART_TxString("FREEZING");
        else          UART_TxString("COLD");
        break;
    case 1:                // temp in 10..19
    case 2:                // temp in 20..29
    case 3:                // temp in 30..39 — handled below
        if (temp <= 30) UART_TxString("NORMAL");
        else            UART_TxString("HOT");
        break;
    default:
        if (temp > 40) UART_TxString("EXTREME HEAT");
        else           UART_TxString("HOT");
        break;
}
```

**Justification:** The hybrid approach exploits `switch` for **discrete value bucketing** (`temp / 10`) and `if-else` for **residual range checks** within each bucket — gaining performance while preserving range-handling capability.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** What is a nested `if-else` statement? Develop a complete C program for an 8051 microcontroller to read two switches connected to P1.0 and P1.1, and control an LED connected to P2.0 based on the truth table given below: **(7 marks)**

| P1.0 | P1.1 | LED (P2.0) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | OFF |
| 0 | 1 | ON |
| 1 | 0 | ON |
| 1 | 1 | BLINK (toggle every 500 ms) |

**(b)** Explain the `switch-case` statement in detail. Write a program to implement a 4-state finite state machine (IDLE, RUN, PAUSE, ERROR) using `switch-case` for an industrial motor controller. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition of Nested `if-else`:** A nested `if-else` is a conditional structure in which one `if` or `else` block contains another complete `if-else` structure, enabling **hierarchical multi-level decision making**.

**C Program for 8051:**
```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit SW1 = P1^0;     // Switch 1 on P1.0
sbit SW2 = P1^1;     // Switch 2 on P1.1
sbit LED = P2^0;     // LED on P2.0

void Delay_500ms(void) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < 500; i++)
        for (j = 0; j < 1275; j++);   // 500 ms @ 11.0592 MHz
}

void main(void) {
    P2 = 0x00;   // Clear port
    
    while (1) {
        if (SW1 == 0) {
            if (SW2 == 0) {
                LED = 0;        // 00: OFF
            } else {
                LED = 1;        // 01: ON
            }
        } else {
            if (SW2 == 0) {
                LED = 1;        // 10: ON
            } else {
                // 11: BLINK
                LED = ~LED;
                Delay_500ms();
            }
        }
    }
}
```

**Truth Table Verification:**

| P1.0 | P1.1 | Code Path | LED State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Outer if → Inner if | OFF (0) |
| 0 | 1 | Outer if → Inner else | ON (1) |
| 1 | 0 | Outer else → Inner if | ON (1) |
| 1 | 1 | Outer else → Inner else | BLINK |

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**`switch-case` Explanation:** A multi-way branching construct that transfers control to one of several statement blocks based on the value of a single integer expression. It is optimized by compilers using **jump tables**, providing O(1) dispatch — ideal for **state machines**.

**4-State FSM for Industrial Motor Controller:**
```c
#include <reg51.h>

typedef enum { IDLE, RUN, PAUSE, ERROR } State;
State current_state = IDLE;

void Motor_Stop(void)  { P2 = 0x00; }
void Motor_Run(void)   { P2 = 0x01; }
void Motor_Pause(void) { P2 = 0x02; }
void Alarm_On(void)    { P2 = 0x04; }

void Process_Command(unsigned char cmd) {
    switch (cmd) {
        case 0:  // CMD_START
            current_state = RUN;
            break;
        case 1:  // CMD_PAUSE
            current_state = PAUSE;
            break;
        case 2:  // CMD_RESUME
            if (current_state == PAUSE)
                current_state = RUN;
            break;
        case 3:  // CMD_STOP
            current_state = IDLE;
            break;
        case 4:  // CMD_FAULT
            current_state = ERROR;
            break;
        default:
            current_state = ERROR;
            break;
    }
}

void State_Handler(void) {
    switch (current_state) {
        case IDLE:
            Motor_Stop();
            break;
        case RUN:
            Motor_Run();
            break;
        case PAUSE:
            Motor_Pause();
            break;
        case ERROR:
            Alarm_On();
            Motor_Stop();
            break;
        default:
            current_state = IDLE;
            break;
    }
}

void main(void) {
    unsigned char received_cmd;
    while (1) {
        // Assume received_cmd is fetched from UART
        Process_Command(received_cmd);
        State_Handler();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of FSM Design:**

1. **State Encoding:** Uses an `enum` for type-safe, readable state names (compiles to `0, 1, 2, 3`).
2. **Command Dispatcher:** First `switch` decodes the input command and updates `current_state`.
3. **State Action Handler:** Second `switch` executes the appropriate hardware action for the current state.
4. **Default Case Safety:** Both switches include `default` to catch unexpected values and force a safe IDLE/ERROR state — a **defensive programming** practice highly valued in KTU evaluations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Missing `break` in switch** — causes fall-through and unintended behaviour. *Loss: 2 marks.*
> 2. **Using `if-else` where `switch` is demanded** — partial credit but not full marks.
> 3. **No `default` case in switch** — considered incomplete logic. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 4. **Confusing `=` and `==`** in `if` conditions — silent logical bug. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 5. **Excessively deep nesting (>3 levels)** — readability penalty. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 6. **No comments** explaining the code intent — *Loss: 1 mark.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Conditional Statements**

- [x] **`if`** executes block only when condition is TRUE (non-zero).
- [x] **`if-else`** guarantees exactly one of two blocks runs — use for binary choices.
- [x] **Nested `if-else`** handles hierarchical decisions; limit depth to **3 levels** for readability.
- [x] **`else-if` ladder** evaluates top-down with **short-circuit on first TRUE**; order matters.
- [x] **`switch-case`** works only on **integer / character expressions** with **constant** case labels.
- [x] **Always use `break;`** in switch cases unless intentional fall-through is required.
- [x] **Always include `default:`** in switch for safety against unexpected values.
- [x] **Compiler translates `switch`** to a **jump table** → faster than `else-if` for many branches.
- [x] **Ternary operator `? :`** is compact but harder to debug; avoid in complex logic.
- [x] **Common bug:** `if (x = 5)` (assignment) vs `if (x == 5)` (comparison) — KTU examiners test this.
- [x] **In embedded C**, conditions often use **bitwise operators** (`P1 & 0x01`) for hardware register checks.
- [x] **Boundary inclusion convention:** Be consistent — use `<=` and `>` carefully to avoid gaps/overlaps in `else-if` ladders.
- [x] **Defensive programming:** Place **safety-critical conditions** (over-voltage, over-temp) at the **top** of any conditional ladder.
- [x] **Indentation:** Use 4 spaces or 1 tab consistently — KTU penalizes poor formatting.
- [x] **Real-time impact:** `if-else` chains introduce **branch latency**; in ISR (Interrupt Service Routines), keep conditional logic minimal.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — CONDITIONAL STATEMENTS**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | PBEVT504 — Embedded System Design*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Loops

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBEVT504 – Embedded System Design)**, the topic **"Loops"** refers to the **iterative control structures** of the **C programming language** that allow a block of instructions to be executed repeatedly based on a specified condition. According to the **ISO/IEC 9899:2018 (C17)** standard, loops are classified as **iteration statements** and form one of the three fundamental control-flow categories (sequence, selection, iteration).

In embedded systems, loops are not merely a programming convenience — they are the **temporal backbone** of every real-time application. From reading sensor data every **10 ms** to generating a **PWM signal at 50 Hz**, the embedded CPU spends the vast majority of its execution time inside loops.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim)**
> *Loops: for, while, do-while; nested loops; infinite loops; loop control statements (break, continue); applications in embedded C programming.*

The three canonical loop constructs in C are:

1. **`for` loop** — *counter-controlled* iteration (entry-controlled).
2. **`while` loop** — *condition-controlled* iteration (entry-controlled).
3. **`do-while` loop** — *condition-controlled* iteration (exit-controlled).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **security guard** in a factory who must check **100 doors** every hour:

- A **`for` loop** is like a guard who has a **checklist** with 100 numbered doors — "I will check door 1, then door 2, ... up to door 100, and then I stop." The number of iterations is **known in advance**.
- A **`while` loop** is like a guard who keeps walking around **as long as the alarm is silent**. The exact number of rounds is **not known in advance**; it depends on the alarm.
- A **`do-while` loop** is like a guard who **always makes at least one round** before deciding whether to continue — even if the alarm is ringing, the guard still completes the current round first.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an **8-bit AVR/8051 microcontroller** running at **16 MHz**, an empty `for` loop like `for(i=0; i<1000; i++);` will burn approximately **3,000 clock cycles**, translating to **187.5 µs** of CPU time. Choosing the wrong loop structure can thus be the difference between a system that meets its **real-time deadline** and one that crashes. This is why the KTU examiner places heavy weight on loop analysis.

## 1.3 Role of Loops in Embedded Systems

| Application Area | Typical Loop Type | Real-Time Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor polling (temperature, ADC) | `while(1)` super-loop | **Periodic** (e.g., every 1 ms) |
| UART byte transmission | `while(!(UCSR0A & UDRE0));` | **Bit-bounded** (microseconds) |
| PWM generation | `for` with hardware timer | **Frequency-locked** (e.g., 50 Hz) |
| Watchdog refresh | `while(1)` + `wdt_reset();` | **Critical** (must never starve) |
| State machine traversal | `switch` inside `while(1)` | **Event-driven** |
| `delay_ms()` library | `for` loop calibration | **Calibrated** (cycle-accurate) |

The dominant architecture in bare-metal embedded firmware is the **super-loop** (also called *main-loop* or *foreground-loop*):

```c
int main(void) {
    SystemInit();          // Hardware initialization
    while (1) {            // INFINITE LOOP - never exits
        ReadSensors();
        ProcessData();
        UpdateActuators();
        Sleep();           // Low-power mode
    }
    return 0;              // Unreachable
}
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Iteration Constructs — Formal Syntax

### 2.1.1 The `for` loop (K&R §3.5)

```c
for ( expression1 ; expression2 ; expression3 )
    statement;
```

- **`expression1`** → **Initialization** (executed **once**, before the loop begins).
- **`expression2`** → **Condition** (evaluated **before each iteration**; loop continues if non-zero).
- **`expression3`** → **Update / Re-initialization** (executed at the **end of each iteration**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner frequently tests the **equivalence between `for` and `while`**:
> ```c
> // These two are SEMANTICALLY IDENTICAL:
> for (init; cond; update) body;
> 
> init;
> while (cond) { body; update; }
> ```
> If a student writes the `while` form and forgets the `update` step, an **infinite loop** results — a classic KTU 2-mark trap.

### 2.1.2 The `while` loop (K&R §3.2)

```c
while ( expression )
    statement;
```

- **Entry-controlled**: condition is tested **before** entering the body.
- If the condition is initially `false` (0), the body is **never executed** (**zero or more** times).
- Most common form for **polling** hardware flags in embedded code.

### 2.1.3 The `do-while` loop (K&R §3.6)

```c
do
    statement;
while ( expression );
```

- **Exit-controlled**: body executes **at least once** before the condition is tested.
- The terminating **semicolon is mandatory** (a notorious KTU viva question).
- Used for **menu-driven** embedded interfaces and **first-time-valid** I/O operations.

## 2.2 Loop Control Statements

| Statement | Effect | Embedded Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `break;` | Terminates the **innermost** loop immediately | Exit a search loop on key-press |
| `continue;` | Skips the remainder of the current iteration | Skip invalid ADC sample |
| `goto label;` | Unconditional jump (used with caution) | Exit from deeply nested loops on error |

> [!WARNING]
> **`goto` is permitted in the C standard**, and in embedded firmware it is often used to jump to an **error-handling cleanup block** (e.g., on sensor failure). However, misuse of `goto` in KTU exams is a guaranteed **mark-deduction** trigger.

## 2.3 Embedded-Specific Loop Considerations

### 2.3.1 Cycle Counting & Delay Generation

A calibrated delay loop relies on the fact that each iteration consumes a **known number of clock cycles**:

$$T_{\text{delay}} = N \times N_{\text{cycles/iter}} \times T_{\text{clk}}$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{delay}}$ = total delay in seconds
- $N$ = number of loop iterations
- $N_{\text{cycles/iter}}$ = cycles per iteration (compiler-dependent)
- $T_{\text{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{CPU}}}$ = clock period

### 2.3.2 Compiler Optimization Hazards

When the compiler flag **`-O2`** is enabled, the optimizer may:

- **Hoist** invariant expressions out of the loop (Loop-Invariant Code Motion).
- **Unroll** short loops.
- **Eliminate** loops whose result is unused (Dead Code Elimination).

> [!CAUTION]
> A delay loop optimized away by the compiler will produce a **zero-delay** bug. KTU lab exams often use `volatile` on the loop counter to prevent this:
> ```c
> volatile uint16_t i;
> for (i = 0; i < 50000; i++);  // 'volatile' prevents optimization
> ```

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Loop Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorize This Table**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Iteration count (definite `for` loop) | $N = \text{end} - \text{start}$ when step is **+1** | integer |
| 2 | Iteration count (general `for` loop) | $N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{\text{end} - \text{start}}{\text{step}} \right\rfloor + 1$ | integer |
| 3 | Loop body execution count | `for`: $N$ times; `while`: $0$ to $\infty$ times; `do-while`: $1$ to $\infty$ times | — |
| 4 | Time complexity | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ for single loop, $\mathcal{O}(N \times M)$ for nested | — |
| 5 | Delay from busy-wait loop | $T_{\text{delay}} = N \cdot C \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$ | seconds |
| 6 | Clock period | $T_{\text{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{CPU}}}$ | seconds |
| 7 | Cycles per iteration (typical AVR) | $C \in \left[3, 9\right]$ cycles (empty loop) | CPU cycles |
| 8 | Maximum unsigned 16-bit count | $N_{\max} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65\,535$ | iterations |
| 9 | `break` / `continue` scope | Affects **innermost** enclosing loop only | — |
| 10 | `do-while` guarantee | Body executes $\geq 1$ time | — |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are **mathematically derivable** and do not rely on any specific textbook. The KTU examiner expects the student to write the **derivation**, not just the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Converting a `for` Loop into its `while` Equivalent

This is a **favourite 7-mark question** in KTU ESE. We must prove the semantic equivalence of the two constructs.

**Given:** A canonical `for` loop
```c
for (E1; E2; E3) {
    BODY;
}
```

**Step 1 — Decompose the `for` syntax**
According to ISO C11 §6.8.5.3, the `for` statement is **defined as if** rewritten into a `while` statement. The C standard explicitly guarantees this equivalence.

**Step 2 — Write the equivalent `while` form**
The body of the `for` loop is `BODY;`. The control expressions must be preserved in the correct order.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Equivalent Code:} \quad
&\textbf{E1;} \\
&\textbf{while} \; (\text{E2}) \; \{ \\
&\quad \text{BODY;} \\
&\quad \text{E3;} \\
&\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Trace and verify**
- `E1` executes **exactly once** before the loop begins (matches `for` semantics).
- `E2` is tested **before each iteration** (matches entry-controlled `while`).
- `BODY` runs when `E2` is true.
- `E3` runs at the **end of each iteration**, just before the next condition check (matches `for` update step).

**Step 4 — Conclusion**
The two constructs are **100% semantically equivalent** under the C standard, with the only proviso that `continue` inside a `for` loop still executes `E3`, whereas in the equivalent `while` form, `continue` skips the `E3` line. Therefore the equivalence holds **only when no `continue` is used inside the loop body** — a subtle point the KTU examiner loves to test.

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Equivalence is exact iff } \text{continue} \notin \text{loop body}\;}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Calibrating a Delay Loop on AVR @ 16 MHz

**Problem:** Generate a delay of exactly **1 ms** on an ATmega328P running at $f_{\text{CPU}} = 16$ MHz using a `for` loop. Each loop iteration (empty body) consumes **4 CPU cycles** under `-O0` compilation.

**Step 1 — Compute the clock period**

$$
T_{\text{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{CPU}}} = \dfrac{1}{16 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} = 62.5 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the delay-time formula**

$$
T_{\text{delay}} = N \times C \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

Where $N$ is the number of iterations, $C$ is cycles per iteration, and $T_{\text{clk}}$ is the clock period.

**Step 3 — Substitute the desired delay**

$$
1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = N \times 4 \times 62.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 62.5 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{250 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= 4\,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Write the final C code**

```c
void delay_1ms(void) {
    volatile uint16_t i;        // 'volatile' prevents optimization
    for (i = 0; i < 4000; i++) {
        ;   // empty body, NOP
    }
}
```

**Step 6 — Verification**
Total cycles = $4\,000 \times 4 = 16\,000$ cycles. Time = $16\,000 \times 62.5 \text{ ns} = 1.0 \text{ ms}$. **Verified** ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner awards the **final mark** only if the student explicitly mentions the use of `volatile` to prevent the compiler from optimizing the loop away — a 1-mark catch that separates a 14/14 from a 13/14 answer.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Time Complexity of Nested Loops

**Problem:** Find the total number of iterations and time complexity of:
```c
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {          // outer loop
    for (j = 0; j < n; j++) {      // inner loop
        printf("X");
    }
}
```

**Step 1 — Count inner iterations**
For each fixed `i`, the inner loop runs from `j = 0` to `j = n-1`, giving exactly $n$ iterations of `printf`.

**Step 2 — Count outer iterations**
The outer loop runs from `i = 0` to `i = n-1`, giving exactly $n$ iterations of the inner block.

**Step 3 — Multiply (Cartesian product)**
Total `printf` calls = $n \times n = n^{2}$.

**Step 4 — Asymptotic analysis**

$$
\boxed{\;T(n) = n^{2} = \mathcal{O}(n^{2})\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Real-world implication**
A naive matrix-multiplication kernel on an STM32 will spend $\mathcal{O}(n^{3})$ cycles in a triple-nested loop. For $n = 64$ (a small $8 \times 8$ matrix), that is $262\,144$ iterations. At $16$ MHz, this is $\approx 16.4$ ms — too slow for a $60$ Hz video pipeline. This is why **loop tiling** and **SIMD** are essential in real-time DSP.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trace of `while` vs `do-while`

| Iteration $k$ | `while` condition before | `while` body runs? | `do-while` body runs? | `do-while` condition after |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Test $k < 3$ → true | Yes | **Yes (unconditional)** | Test $k < 3$ → true |
| 2 | Test $k < 3$ → true | Yes | Yes | Test $k < 3$ → true |
| 3 | Test $k < 3$ → true | Yes | Yes | Test $k < 3$ → true |
| 4 | Test $k < 3$ → false | No (exit) | No (exit) | Test $k < 3$ → false (exit) |
| **Total body runs** | — | **3** | **3** | — |

If the initial condition is **false** ($k = 5$ from the start):
- `while` body runs **0 times**.
- `do-while` body runs **1 time**.

> [!NOTE]
> This distinction is the **single most important difference** between `while` and `do-while` in embedded menu code. KTU Part-A questions often use a one-line trick question: *"Will the body execute if the condition is initially false?"* — the answer depends on which loop you choose.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — `for` Loop Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start of for loop]) --> initA[Execute initialization E1 once]
    initA --> condTestA{Condition E2 is true?}
    condTestA -- Yes --> bodyA[Execute loop BODY]
    bodyA --> updateA[Execute update E3]
    updateA --> condTestA
    condTestA -- No --> exitA([Exit loop, continue after])
```

> [!NOTE]
> The decision diamond `condTestA` is a Mermaid-rendered shape; node IDs use the alphanumeric prefix `A` to avoid reserved-keyword collisions.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — `while` Loop Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start of while loop]) --> condTestB{Condition is true?}
    condTestB -- Yes --> bodyB[Execute loop BODY]
    bodyB --> condTestB
    condTestB -- No --> exitB([Exit loop, continue after])
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — `do-while` Loop Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startC([Start of do-while loop]) --> bodyC[Execute loop BODY first]
    bodyC --> condTestC{Condition is true?}
    condTestC -- Yes --> bodyC
    condTestC -- No --> exitC([Exit loop, continue after])
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the body node `bodyC` appears **before** the condition test `condTestC` in the `do-while` flow. This is the visual signature of an **exit-controlled** loop. KTU examiners expect students to draw this on paper and label the diamond with the word *condition* — not *condition?* — to receive full marks.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Super-Loop in an Embedded Firmware

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwrup([Power ON Reset]) --> hwinit[Hardware Initialization]
    hwinit --> superLoop{while 1 infinite loop}
    superLoop --> readSens[Read Sensors ADC and GPIO]
    readSens --> process[Process Data via Algorithm]
    process --> actuate[Drive Actuators PWM and UART]
    actuate --> comm[Communication Task I2C or SPI]
    comm --> sleep[Enter Low Power Sleep]
    sleep --> superLoop
    wdt[Watchdog Timer Reset] -.kicks.-> superLoop
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **dashed arrow** from `wdt` to `superLoop` represents an *asynchronous* event — the watchdog fires independently of the main code and resets the MCU if the super-loop stalls. The loop architecture must **periodically call `wdt_reset();`** to avoid an unintended reset.

## 4.5 Mermaid State Diagram — `break` vs `continue` Semantics

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LoopBody
    LoopBody --> CheckStmt{break or continue or normal}
    CheckStmt --> LoopBody : normal flow update executes
    CheckStmt --> LoopEnd : break jumps here
    CheckStmt --> Update : continue jumps here
    Update --> CheckCond{Condition true}
    CheckCond --> LoopBody : true
    CheckCond --> LoopEnd : false
    LoopEnd --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In C, `continue` inside a `for` loop still executes the **update expression (E3)** before the next condition test. In a `while` loop, `continue` skips the update step and can cause an **infinite loop**. This subtle semantic difference is a **guaranteed 2-mark KTU question**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*With a suitable example, explain the difference between an **entry-controlled** loop and an **exit-controlled** loop in C. Name the constructs that fall under each category.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **entry-controlled** loop tests its condition **before** executing the body. If the condition is initially false, the body executes **zero times**. C provides two entry-controlled loops: the `for` loop and the `while` loop.

An **exit-controlled** loop executes the body **first** and tests the condition **afterwards**. Therefore the body executes **at least once**, regardless of the condition. C provides one such construct: the `do-while` loop.

**Example:**
```c
int x = 10;
while (x < 5) {           // Entry-controlled: body NEVER runs
    printf("A");          // (x = 10 is not < 5)
}

int y = 10;
do {
    printf("B");          // Exit-controlled: body runs ONCE
} while (y < 5);          // y = 10 still fails the test, but body already ran
```

**Distinction Summary Table:**

| Property | Entry-controlled | Exit-controlled |
|---|---|---|
| Constructs | `for`, `while` | `do-while` |
| Minimum executions | 0 | 1 |
| Condition check | Before body | After body |
| Embedded use | Polling, busy-wait | Menu, first-read |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest]**
*What is the output of the following C snippet? Justify your answer.*
```c
int i = 0;
do {
    printf("%d ", i);
    i++;
} while (i < 0);
```

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The output is: **`0`**

**Justification:**
The `do-while` loop is **exit-controlled**, so the body executes **at least once** before the condition is tested.

1. **First iteration:** `i` is initialized to `0` before the loop. The body executes, printing `0`, and then `i` is incremented to `1`.
2. **Condition test:** The expression `i < 0` is now `1 < 0`, which is **false (0)**. The loop terminates.

If this had been a `while` loop instead, the body would not execute at all (since `i = 0` fails `i < 0` from the start). This contrast is the pedagogical purpose of the question.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Module-4 question in Part B carries **14 marks** with an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each question has two sub-parts, typically `(a) = 7 marks` and `(b) = 7 marks`.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]`

**(a)** *With neat flowcharts, explain the syntax and working of the `for` loop and the `while` loop in C. Convert the following `for` loop into its equivalent `while` form and explain each line.* **(7 marks)**
```c
for (int k = 1; k <= 10; k += 2) {
    if (k == 5) continue;
    printf("%d\n", k);
}
```

**(b)** *Write an embedded C program to generate a **1-second** delay on an ATmega328P running at **8 MHz** using a calibrated `for` loop. Assume each iteration of the empty loop takes **6 CPU cycles**. Show all calculations clearly.* **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]`

**(a)** *Compare and contrast the `while` and `do-while` loops in C. Give two real-time embedded applications where `do-while` is preferred over `while`. Trace the output of the following code segment.* **(7 marks)**
```c
int a = 5, b = 10;
while (a < b) {
    a++;
    b--;
    printf("a=%d b=%d | ", a, b);
}
```

**(b)** *Explain the role of the `break` and `continue` statements inside loops. Write an embedded C program using a `while(1)` super-loop to read a push-button connected to **PD2** of an ATmega328P. When the button is pressed, the LED on **PB5** must toggle. The loop must not block (no `delay`).* **(7 marks)**

---

### **Detailed Model Solutions**

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a)** — 7 marks

**Part 1 — `for` loop syntax and flowchart (3 marks)**

The C `for` loop is a **counter-controlled, entry-controlled** iteration statement. Its general syntax is:

```c
for (initialization; condition; update) {
    body;
}
```

Execution order: **initialization → condition → body → update → condition → ...**

**Flowchart:**
```
    ┌─────────────────┐
    │ Initialization  │  (E1, runs ONCE)
    └────────┬────────┘
             ↓
    ┌─────────────────┐  No  ┌──────────────┐
    │  Condition True?├─────►│ Exit Loop    │
    └────────┬────────┘      └──────────────┘
             │ Yes
             ↓
    ┌─────────────────┐
    │  Body Executes  │
    └────────┬────────┘
             ↓
    ┌─────────────────┐
    │  Update (E3)    │
    └────────┬────────┘
             ↓
        (back to Condition)
```

**Part 2 — `while` loop syntax and flowchart (2 marks)**

```c
while (condition) {
    body;
}
```

The condition is tested **before** each iteration. The `while` loop is the **canonical polling construct** in embedded C — e.g., `while (!(UCSR0A & UDRE0));` waits for the UART data register to become empty.

**Part 3 — Conversion and tracing (2 marks)**

**Conversion to `while`:**
```c
int k = 1;                  // (a) Initialization executed once
while (k <= 10) {           // (b) Condition tested before each iteration
    if (k == 5) continue;   // (c) continue skips printf but DOES NOT skip the update
    printf("%d\n", k);
    k += 2;                 // (d) Update happens at the END of the body
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #1**
> The naive conversion `while (k <= 10) { ... k += 2; }` is **correct** only if `continue` is absent. With `continue`, the `k += 2` step is **skipped** in the `while` form, leading to an **infinite loop when `k = 5`**. In the original `for` loop, `continue` still executes the `k += 2` update. The KTU examiner deducts **2 marks** if the student does not mention this. Marks are split as: **(a) 3 marks, (b) 2 marks, conversion & warning 2 marks**.

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b)** — 7 marks

**Given:**
- Microcontroller: ATmega328P
- Clock frequency: $f_{\text{CPU}} = 8$ MHz
- Required delay: $T_{\text{delay}} = 1$ s
- Cycles per iteration: $C = 6$

**Step 1 — Compute the clock period** (1 mark)

$$
T_{\text{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{CPU}}} = \dfrac{1}{8 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} = 125 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the delay equation** (1 mark)

$$
T_{\text{delay}} = N \times C \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve for $N$** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ s} &= N \times 6 \times 125 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} \\
N &= \dfrac{1}{6 \times 125 \times 10^{-9}} \\
N &= \dfrac{1}{750 \times 10^{-9}} \\
N &= 1\,333\,333.33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Round down to integer and account for loop overhead** (1 mark)

We use $N = 1\,333\,333$. This value exceeds the range of `uint16_t` (max $65\,535$), so we must use **`uint32_t`**.

**Step 5 — Write the C code** (2 marks)

```c
#include <avr/io.h>
#include <stdint.h>

void delay_1sec(void) {
    volatile uint32_t i;             // 'volatile' prevents optimization
    for (i = 0; i < 1333333UL; i++) {
        ;   // empty body, NOP
    }
}

int main(void) {
    DDRB |= (1 << PB5);              // PB5 as output (LED)
    while (1) {
        PORTB ^= (1 << PB5);         // Toggle LED
        delay_1sec();                // 1-second blocking delay
    }
    return 0;
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2**
> The KTU examiner will **deduct 2 marks** if the student:
> 1. Uses `uint16_t` instead of `uint32_t` (overflow → wrong delay).
> 2. Forgets the `volatile` qualifier (compiler optimizes loop away).
> 3. Uses `int` (signed 16-bit on AVR-GCC → negative count → infinite loop).

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a)** — 7 marks

**Comparison Table** (3 marks)

| Property | `while` | `do-while` |
|---|---|---|
| Control type | Entry-controlled | Exit-controlled |
| Minimum executions | 0 | 1 |
| Condition test position | Before body | After body |
| Semicolon at end | Not required | **Mandatory** |
| Conversion to `for` | Trivial | Requires a flag variable |
| Common embedded use | Polling flags, busy-wait | Menu prompt, first-time read |

**Two real-time applications where `do-while` is preferred** (2 marks)

1. **UART receive with timeout:** The MCU must read at least one byte, then continue reading while data is available. A `do-while` guarantees the first read.
   ```c
   do {
       received = UDR0;
       buffer[i++] = received;
   } while (UCSR0A & RXC0);
   ```

2. **Password-entry menu on an LCD keypad:** The user must be prompted at least once, even if the initial password is empty.
   ```c
   do {
       printf("Enter PIN: ");
       scanf("%d", &pin);
   } while (pin != CORRECT_PIN);
   ```

**Trace of the given snippet** (2 marks)

| Step | `a` (before) | `b` (before) | `a < b`? | `a` (after) | `b` (after) | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | 5 | 10 | — | — | — | — |
| Iter 1 | 5 | 10 | True | 6 | 9 | `a=6 b=9 \|` |
| Iter 2 | 6 | 9 | True | 7 | 8 | `a=7 b=8 \|` |
| Iter 3 | 7 | 8 | True | 8 | 7 | `a=8 b=7 \|` |
| Iter 4 | 8 | 7 | **False** | — | — | (loop exits) |

**Final output:** `a=6 b=9 | a=7 b=8 | a=8 b=7 | `

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b)** — 7 marks

**Role of `break` and `continue`** (2 marks)

- **`break`**: Immediately exits the **innermost** enclosing loop or `switch`. Execution resumes at the statement following the loop.
- **`continue`**: Skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the loop's update step (for `for`) or condition test (for `while`/`do-while`).

**Embedded C Program — Non-blocking Button Toggle** (5 marks)

**Algorithm:**
1. Configure PD2 as input with internal pull-up.
2. Configure PB5 as output.
3. In the super-loop, read PD2.
4. If the button is **pressed** (logic LOW) **and** it was **not pressed** in the previous iteration (edge detection), toggle PB5.
5. **No `delay()` call** — the loop is fully non-blocking.

```c
#include <avr/io.h>

#define LED_PIN    PB5
#define BTN_PIN    PD2
#define BTN_PRESSED  (!(PIND & (1 << BTN_PIN)))   // active-low

int main(void) {
    /* 1. Configure LED pin PB5 as output */
    DDRB |= (1 << LED_PIN);

    /* 2. Configure button pin PD2 as input with internal pull-up */
    DDRD &= ~(1 << BTN_PIN);
    PORTD |= (1 << BTN_PIN);

    uint8_t prevState = 0;   // 0 = released, 1 = pressed

    while (1) {              // Super-loop (infinite)
        uint8_t currState = BTN_PRESSED;

        if (currState && !prevState) {       // Rising edge: just pressed
            PORTB ^= (1 << LED_PIN);          // Toggle LED
        }
        prevState = currState;

        /* Other non-blocking tasks can run here */
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of key lines (for the examiner):**

- `BTN_PRESSED` macro reads the input register. Pull-up means the pin reads **HIGH** when released and **LOW** when pressed.
- `prevState` stores the previous button state. The condition `currState && !prevState` detects a **1→0 transition** (the moment of press), preventing repeated toggles while the button is held.
- No `delay()` is used, so the loop is **non-blocking** and can service other tasks in a real RTOS environment.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3 (Common 2-Mark Deduction)**
> 1. Using `if (BTN_PRESSED)` without edge detection — the LED will toggle continuously while the button is held, causing a **flicker** at the CPU clock speed.
> 2. Adding a `delay_ms(200)` to "debounce" — this makes the loop **blocking**, violating the question's constraint.
> 3. Forgetting to enable the internal pull-up (`PORTD |= (1 << BTN_PIN)`), leaving the pin floating and producing **spurious toggles** due to noise.
> 4. Using `PIND` without masking — reads the **whole port**, not just PD2.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Consolidated Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Patterns for the "Loops" Module**
> 1. **Forgetting `volatile`** on delay-loop counters (compiler eliminates the loop → 0 delay).
> 2. **Type overflow** — using `uint16_t` for delays > 65,535 iterations.
> 3. **Missing semicolon** after `do-while` condition (compilation error, 0 marks).
> 4. **Converting `for` to `while`** without handling `continue` correctly.
> 5. **Using `while(x)` instead of `while(x != 0)`** when `x` is a struct/multi-bit value — implicit conversion to boolean can produce subtle bugs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Loops**

- **Three loop constructs**: `for` (counter), `while` (entry-controlled), `do-while` (exit-controlled).
- **Minimum executions**: `for`/`while` → **0**; `do-while` → **1**.
- **Semicolon rule**: `do-while` **must** end with a semicolon; `for` and `while` must **not**.
- **Equivalence theorem**: A `for(E1; E2; E3) BODY;` is equivalent to `E1; while(E2) { BODY; E3; }` — but only when no `continue` is used inside.
- **`break` and `continue`** affect only the **innermost** enclosing loop.
- **`continue` in a `for` loop** still executes the update expression E3; in a `while` loop, it skips the update.
- **Delay formula**: $T_{\text{delay}} = N \cdot C \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$ where $T_{\text{clk}} = 1 / f_{\text{CPU}}$.
- **Always use `volatile`** on loop counters that implement delays or poll hardware.
- **Watch for integer overflow**: For a 1-second delay at 16 MHz with 4 cycles/iter, $N = 4\,000\,000$ — **must** be `uint32_t`.
- **Super-loop pattern**: `while(1) { task1(); task2(); ... }` is the canonical bare-metal architecture.
- **Compiler optimization flags** (`-O2`, `-O3`) can eliminate "useless" loops; use `volatile` to defeat this.
- **Empty loop body** must contain at least a semicolon `;` or the loop is misinterpreted.
- **Nested loops** have multiplicative complexity: $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m)$, not additive.
- **`goto`** is legal in C and is widely used in embedded error handling; misuse is penalized.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions and Procedures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Functions and Procedures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Embedded C Programming** for microcontrollers (such as the 8051, ARM Cortex-M, and PIC families), a **Function** is a self-contained, named block of statements that performs a specific, well-defined task, may accept input values through **parameters (arguments)**, and may return a single output value via a **return statement**.

A **Procedure** is structurally similar to a function but is formally defined as a subroutine that **does not return a value** to the caller. In standard ANSI C, procedures are implemented as functions with the return type `void`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *function* is a modular unit of code that encapsulates logic, promotes reusability, and improves readability. The term *procedure* in embedded literature often refers to a `void` function that performs an action (e.g., `void delay_ms(uint16_t t);`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a **function as a kitchen appliance** — specifically, a *blender*:
- You give it **ingredients** (parameters/arguments) — fruits, milk, sugar.
- It **performs an action** (executes the body) — blends them together.
- It **returns a product** (return value) — a smoothie in a jug.

A **procedure** is more like a *dishwasher*:
- You give it **dirty plates** (arguments).
- It **performs an action** — cleans them.
- It **returns nothing useful** — you just look at the clean plates afterward.

> [!NOTE]
> In 8051 Embedded C, almost every peripheral interaction is wrapped in a procedure. For example, `void UART_Init(void);` configures the Serial Port but returns no value to the caller.

## 1.3 Why Functions/Procedures Matter in Embedded Systems

- **Modularity:** Splits a large `main()` into manageable sub-tasks (LCD_Init, ADC_Read, Motor_Control).
- **Code Reusability:** The same `Delay_ms()` function can be called hundreds of times without rewriting.
- **Memory Efficiency:** A single function body in `code` memory can be invoked repeatedly, saving precious RAM.
- **Readability & Maintainability:** Debugging a 10-line function is far easier than a 500-line `main()`.
- **Hardware Abstraction:** Wraps complex register manipulations behind clean function calls.

## 1.4 Anatomy of a C Function

```c
ReturnType FunctionName(ParameterList) {
    // Local variable declarations
    // Executable statements
    return (expression);   // optional, based on ReturnType
}
```

The four essential components of any C function are:
1. **Return Type** — Data type of the value returned (e.g., `int`, `char`, `void`).
2. **Function Name** — Unique identifier following C naming rules.
3. **Parameter List** — Comma-separated declarations of inputs (may be empty).
4. **Function Body** — Enclosed in `{}`, contains the actual logic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Functions in Embedded C

Functions can be classified along multiple axes relevant to KTU examination:

| Classification Axis | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **By Source** | Library Functions | Pre-defined in header files (e.g., `printf`, `malloc`) |
| | User-defined Functions | Programmer-written for specific application |
| **By Argument** | No-argument functions | `void func(void);` |
| | With-argument functions | `int add(int a, int b);` |
| **By Return** | With return value | Returns a computed value |
| | Without return value (`void`) | Acts as a procedure |
| **By Call Mechanism** | Call by Value | A copy of argument is passed |
| | Call by Reference | Address (pointer) of argument is passed |
| **By Storage** | External (Global) | Defined outside any function, scope = file |
| | Internal/Static | Scope limited to the file/translation unit |
| | Inline | Compiler attempts to expand at call site |

## 2.2 Function Declaration, Definition, and Calling — The Three Stages

### Stage 1: Function Declaration (Prototype)
Tells the compiler about the function's *signature* **before** it is used. Always ends with a semicolon.

```c
int multiply(int x, int y);   // prototype
```

### Stage 2: Function Definition
Contains the actual body — the *what* and *how* of the function.

```c
int multiply(int x, int y) {
    return (x * y);
}
```

### Stage 3: Function Call
Invokes the function from `main()` or any other function.

```c
int result = multiply(4, 5);   // result = 20
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** If a function is *defined* before it is *called* (top-down style), the prototype is optional. If called before defined, the prototype is **mandatory**, else compiler raises an *implicit declaration warning*.

## 2.3 Parameter Passing Mechanisms

### A. Pass by Value (Call by Value)
- A **copy** of the actual argument is pushed onto the stack.
- Modifications inside the function **do not affect** the original variable.
- Safer, but slower for large structures (copy overhead).

### B. Pass by Reference (Call by Address)
- The **address** of the actual argument is passed (using pointers).
- Modifications inside the function **affect** the original variable.
- Efficient for arrays, structs, and peripheral registers.

> [!NOTE]
> In **8051 Embedded C**, Pass by Reference is heavily used to modify SFRs and global state efficiently, e.g., `void GPIO_Write(volatile uint8_t *port, uint8_t value);`

## 2.4 Storage Classes in Embedded Context

The **storage class** of a variable determines its *lifetime*, *scope*, and *default initialization*.

| Storage Class | Keyword | Scope | Lifetime | Default Init | Embedded Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Automatic | `auto` (default) | Local to block | Until block exits | Garbage | Loop counters, temporaries |
| Register | `register` | Local | Until block exits | Garbage | High-speed access (compiler hint) |
| Static | `static` | Local to block (if inside fn) | Entire program | **Zero** | State retention between calls |
| External | `extern` | Global (across files) | Entire program | Zero | Sharing global flags between modules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A `static` local variable is initialized **only once** — during the first call. Its value persists across multiple invocations, making it ideal for state machines.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Function Call Mechanics

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Stack Frame Size | $S_{frame} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sizeof}(P_i) + \text{ReturnAddr} + \text{SavedFP}$ | Determines RAM usage per call |
| Call Depth Limit | $D_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{RAM_{available}}{S_{frame}} \right\rfloor$ | Critical for recursive functions on 8051 (128 B idata!) |
| Pass-by-Value Cost | $C_{val} = \text{sizeof}(T)$ copy operations | Per call overhead |
| Pass-by-Reference Cost | $C_{ref} = \text{sizeof}(pointer)$ | Typically **2 bytes** on 8051 |
| Recurrence Time | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ (Master Theorem) | For analyzing recursive complexity |
| Inline Expansion | $T_{inlined} = k \cdot t_{call}$ vs $T_{function} = t_{call} + t_{body} + t_{return}$ | Optimization decision |

## 2.6 Recursion vs Iteration in Embedded Systems

Recursion is generally **discouraged** in deeply embedded systems because:
- Each call consumes stack space.
- 8051 has only **128 bytes** of internal data RAM.
- A deeply recursive function can cause **stack overflow** and crash the MCU.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to compare recursion and iteration, always mention the **stack depth** limitation as the deciding factor in embedded design.

## 2.7 Inline Functions and Macros

```c
#define SQUARE(x)  ((x) * (x))        // Macro — preprocessor, no type checking
inline int square(int x) { return x*x; } // Inline function — compiler, type-safe
```

| Feature | Macro | Inline Function |
|---|---|---|
| Evaluated by | Preprocessor | Compiler |
| Type checking | No | Yes |
| Debugging | Hard | Easy |
| Side effects risk | High (e.g., `SQUARE(i++)`) | Low |
| Recommended in embedded | Use `static inline` | Yes, for small, hot-path functions |

## 2.8 Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs) — Special "Procedures"

In embedded systems, an **ISR** is a special function invoked by hardware, not by software. For 8051:

```c
void timer0_isr(void) interrupt 1 {
    TH0 = 0xFC;   // Reload timer
    TL0 = 0x18;
    tick_count++;
}
```

Key properties:
- **No parameters, no return value.**
- Marked with the `interrupt` keyword and vector number.
- Should be **short and fast** — defer heavy work to a flag checked in `main()`.
- **No blocking calls** like `printf` or `delay()`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Function Call with Parameter Passing (8051 Keil C)

**Problem:** Write an Embedded C function for 8051 that accepts two unsigned 8-bit numbers and returns their sum. Call it from `main()` and store the result in P1.

### Step 1 — Function Prototype

```c
unsigned char add_numbers(unsigned char a, unsigned char b);
```

*Textual logic:* The function takes two `unsigned char` (8-bit) inputs and returns an 8-bit result. Sum may overflow 255, so a `unsigned int` is safer — but the question demands 8-bit, so we wrap around.

### Step 2 — Function Definition

```c
unsigned char add_numbers(unsigned char a, unsigned char b) {
    unsigned char result;
    result = a + b;          // unsigned char arithmetic, wraps mod 256
    return result;
}
```

### Step 3 — Calling from `main()`

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char add_numbers(unsigned char a, unsigned char b);  // prototype

void main(void) {
    unsigned char x = 150, y = 200, z;
    z = add_numbers(x, y);   // 150 + 200 = 350 -> mod 256 = 94
    P1 = z;                  // P1 = 94 (0x5E)
    while(1);
}
```

### Step 4 — Trace Through Stack (Analytical Walk-through)

When `add_numbers(x, y)` is invoked at address `0x0023`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stack grows downward in 8051 idata:} \\
\text{SP}_{\text{before}} &= 0x07 \\
\text{Push return address} &\rightarrow \text{SP} = 0x09 \\
\text{Push parameter } x = 0x96 &\rightarrow \text{SP} = 0x0A \\
\text{Push parameter } y = 0xC8 &\rightarrow \text{SP} = 0x0B \\
\text{Compute } a + b &= 0x96 + 0xC8 = 0x015E \\
\text{8-bit truncation} &\rightarrow 0x5E = 94 \\
\text{Pop and return} &\rightarrow \text{SP} = 0x07
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Call by Reference to Modify a Port

**Problem:** Write a function that toggles a specific bit of any port passed to it.

### Step 1 — Function Signature

```c
void toggle_bit(volatile unsigned char *port, unsigned char bit_pos);
```

### Step 2 — Definition

```c
void toggle_bit(volatile unsigned char *port, unsigned char bit_pos) {
    *port = (*port) ^ (1 << bit_pos);   // XOR toggles the target bit
}
```

### Step 3 — Invocation

```c
toggle_bit(&P1, 3);   // Toggles bit 3 of Port 1
```

### Step 4 — Bit-Wise Derivation

Suppose initial state of P1 = `0x55` (`0101 0101` in binary) and we toggle bit 3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mask} &= 1 \ll 3 = 0b00001000 = 0x08 \\
\text{Initial P1} &= 0b01010101 \\
\text{Initial P1 XOR Mask} &= 0b01010101 \oplus 0b00001000 \\
&= 0b01011101 = 0x5D
\end{aligned}
$$

So `P1` changes from `0x55` to `0x5D` after the call — a **single bit modification** through a pointer.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Static Local Variable Behavior

**Problem:** Predict the output of the following program.

```c
#include <stdio.h>

void counter(void) {
    static int count = 0;
    count++;
    printf("%d ", count);
}

int main(void) {
    counter();   // Call 1
    counter();   // Call 2
    counter();   // Call 3
    return 0;
}
```

### Analytical Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Call 1:} \quad & count_{\text{init}} = 0 \rightarrow ++ \rightarrow 1 \rightarrow \text{prints "1"} \\
\text{Call 2:} \quad & count_{\text{persists}} = 1 \rightarrow ++ \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow \text{prints "2"} \\
\text{Call 3:} \quad & count_{\text{persists}} = 2 \rightarrow ++ \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow \text{prints "3"}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output:** `1 2 3`

> [!NOTE]
> The keyword `static` causes the variable to be **initialized exactly once** (at program start), and its value is **retained across function invocations** — unlike a normal local `auto` variable.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Recursive Function Analysis (Factorial)

**Problem:** Analyze the stack depth of the recursive factorial function for `n = 5`.

```c
int factorial(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;
    return n * factorial(n - 1);
}
```

### Call Tree Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{fact}(5) &= 5 \times \text{fact}(4) \\
\text{fact}(4) &= 4 \times \text{fact}(3) \\
\text{fact}(3) &= 3 \times \text{fact}(2) \\
\text{fact}(2) &= 2 \times \text{fact}(1) \\
\text{fact}(1) &= 1 \quad \text{(base case)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Stack Depth Calculation

$$
D_{max} = 5 \text{ active stack frames}
$$

$$
S_{total} = 5 \times S_{frame} = 5 \times (2 + 2 + 2) = 30 \text{ bytes}
$$

*(Assuming 2 bytes each for `n`, return address, and saved frame pointer on 8051.)*

This consumes $\dfrac{30}{128} \times 100\% \approx 23.4\%$ of the entire 8051 internal RAM for a single computation — a major reason why **embedded code prefers iteration**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Function Pitfalls

| Mistake | Symptom | Correct Form |
|---|---|---|
| Missing prototype | `warning: implicit declaration` | Add `int foo(int);` at top |
| Wrong return type | Truncation of result | Match return type to data size |
| Forgetting `volatile` on SFRs | Compiler optimizes away writes | Use `volatile unsigned char *` |
| Passing large struct by value | Stack overflow | Pass by pointer instead |
| Calling `printf` inside ISR | System hangs / timing failure | Set a flag, print in `main` loop |
| Forgetting `static` on helper fn | Pollutes global namespace | `static int helper(void);` |
| Using recursion on 8051 | Stack overflow | Convert to iteration |

## 3.6 Storage Class Decision Flow

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Do you need value to persist between calls?} &\xrightarrow{\text{Yes}} \text{use } \texttt{static} \\
\text{Is variable used only in this block?} &\xrightarrow{\text{Yes}} \text{use } \texttt{auto} \text{ (default)} \\
\text{Is variable shared across files?} &\xrightarrow{\text{Yes}} \text{use } \texttt{extern} \\
\text{Is variable accessed in ISR + main?} &\xrightarrow{\text{Yes}} \text{use } \texttt{volatile}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Function Call Stack Flow (8051 Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the lifecycle of a function call — from parameter push to return — in the 8051 internal data memory (idata).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Caller in main] -->|Push args on stack| B[SP points to top]
    B --> C[Save return address on stack]
    C --> D[Saved Frame Pointer]
    D --> E[Local variables allocated]
    E --> F[Execute function body]
    F --> G{Return statement?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Load return value in accumulator]
    H --> I[Restore saved frame pointer]
    I --> J[Pop return address from stack]
    J --> K[Pop arguments from stack]
    K --> L[Return to caller]
    L --> M[SP restored to original position]
    M --> N[Execution continues in main]
```

## 4.2 Memory Layout for Function Execution

This diagram illustrates how the **code memory (ROM)** holds the function body while the **data memory (RAM)** holds the stack and parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ROM[Code Memory xdata code]
        A1[main function]
        A2[function prototype]
        A3[function definition body]
        A4[ISR vector table]
    end

    subgraph RAM[Internal Data Memory idata 128 bytes]
        B1[Register Bank 0 to 3]
        B2[Bit addressable area 0x20 to 0x2F]
        B3[Scratch pad area]
        B4[Stack grows upward from 0x07]
    end

    A1 -->|calls| A3
    A3 -->|pushes onto| B4
    A4 -->|hardware triggered| A3
```

## 4.3 Parameter Passing: Pass by Value vs Pass by Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Caller has variable x = 5] --> Q1{Passing mechanism?}
    Q1 -->|Pass by Value| R1[Copy 5 is sent to function]
    R1 --> S1[Function modifies its local copy]
    S1 --> T1[Original x in caller remains 5]
    
    Q1 -->|Pass by Reference| R2[Address and x passed as pointer]
    R2 --> S2[Function dereferences and modifies]
    S2 --> T2[Original x in caller becomes new value]
```

## 4.4 Interrupt Service Routine Invocation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Microcontroller running main loop] --> B{Hardware event occurs}
    B -->|Timer overflow| C[Hardware saves PC on stack]
    C --> D[Jump to ISR vector address]
    D --> E[Execute ISR function body]
    E --> F[RETI instruction]
    F --> G[Restore PC from stack]
    G --> H[Resume main loop execution]
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree — When to Use Function vs Procedure vs Macro

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need a reusable code block?] -->|No| B[Write inline in main]
    A -->|Yes| C{Need a return value?}
```
