```mermaid
    C -->|Yes| D[Use Function with return type]
    C -->|No| E{Block is more than 3 lines?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Use Procedure void function]
    E -->|No| G{Type safety required?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Use static inline function]
    G -->|No| I[Use macro define]
```

## 4.6 Recursive Call Stack Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[factorial 5] -->|calls| B[factorial 4]
    B -->|calls| C[factorial 3]
    C -->|calls| D[factorial 2]
    D -->|calls| E[factorial 1]
    E -->|base case| F[returns 1]
    F -->|unwinds| D
    D -->|returns 2 times 1| G[returns 2]
    G -->|unwinds| C
    C -->|returns 3 times 2| H[returns 6]
    H -->|unwinds| B
    B -->|returns 4 times 6| I[returns 24]
    I -->|unwinds| A
    A -->|returns 5 times 24| J[returns 120]
```

## 4.7 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modular Embedded Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Main[main function] --> Init[System Init procedure]
    Main --> Loop[Super Loop while 1]
    
    Init --> Clock[Clock Setup]
    Init --> GPIO[GPIO Init]
    Init --> UART[UART Init]
    
    Loop --> ReadSensor[Read Sensor Function]
    Loop --> ProcessData[Process Data Function]
    Loop --> ControlActuator[Control Actuator Procedure]
    Loop --> Transmit[Transmit UART Function]
    Loop --> Delay[Delay ms Procedure]
    
    ReadSensor --> ADC[ADC Read Function]
    ProcessData --> Filter[Filter Data Procedure]
    
    Loop -.sets flag.-> ISR[Timer ISR]
    ISR -.clears flag.-> Loop
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram reflects the **canonical structure** of an 8051 / ARM Cortex-M embedded application — `main()` runs an infinite loop, peripheral init runs once, and ISRs communicate with the main loop via volatile flags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Differentiate between a function and a procedure in Embedded C. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Function | Procedure |
|---|---|---|
| **Return Value** | Returns a value of specified type | Does not return any value |
| **Return Type** | Any valid C type (e.g., `int`, `char`) | Always `void` |
| **Use Case** | Computes and returns a result | Performs an action / side effect |
| **Embedded Example** | `unsigned char ADC_Read(void);` | `void LCD_Init(void);` |

A function is a modular code unit that **returns a value** computed from its inputs. A procedure is essentially a function with return type `void`, used purely for performing actions such as initialization or control operations. In embedded systems, procedures dominate because most peripheral interactions (init, send, toggle) do not need to return data.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is the significance of the `static` storage class specifier for a variable declared inside a function?**

**Model Answer:**
The `static` storage class specifier, when applied to a local variable inside a function, causes the variable to:
1. Be **initialized only once** during the first function call (default value is zero if not explicitly initialized).
2. **Retain its value** between successive function invocations.
3. Have a **lifetime equal to the entire program duration**, even though its **scope remains local** to the function.

**Example use case:** Implementing a software counter or state-machine index that must remember its value across calls — e.g., a `static uint8_t state = 0;` in a scheduler function.

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the different parameter passing mechanisms in Embedded C with suitable examples. Discuss which mechanism is preferred when passing large structures to a function in an embedded system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an Embedded C program for the 8051 microcontroller to find the largest of three unsigned 8-bit numbers stored in internal RAM locations `0x30`, `0x31`, and `0x32`. Use a user-defined function that accepts the three numbers and returns the largest. Display the result on Port 1. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Parameter Passing Mechanisms in Embedded C:**

1. **Pass by Value:** A copy of the actual argument is passed. The function works on the copy; the original remains unchanged.
   ```c
   void increment(int a) { a++; }   // caller's variable unchanged
   ```

2. **Pass by Reference (Address):** The address of the variable is passed using a pointer. The function can modify the original.
   ```c
   void increment(int *a) { (*a)++; }   // caller's variable changes
   ```

3. **Pass by `const` Pointer:** For read-only large data — efficient and safe.
   ```c
   void print_buffer(const uint8_t *buf, uint16_t len);
   ```

**Preferred mechanism for large structures:**
In embedded systems, **pass by reference (pointer)** is preferred when passing large structures because:
- It avoids copying the entire structure onto the stack.
- On 8051, stack space is only **128 bytes**; copying a 50-byte struct repeatedly causes stack overflow.
- Pass-by-reference uses only **2 bytes** (the pointer) per call.

#### Model Solution for (b)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

/* Function prototype */
unsigned char find_largest(unsigned char a, unsigned char b, unsigned char c);

void main(void) {
    unsigned char *p;
    unsigned char x, y, z, result;

    /* Point to internal RAM location 0x30 */
    p = (unsigned char *)0x30;
    x = *p;        /* read from 0x30 */
    p++;
    y = *p;        /* read from 0x31 */
    p++;
    z = *p;        /* read from 0x32 */

    result = find_largest(x, y, z);
    P1 = result;   /* display largest on Port 1 */

    while(1);
}

/* Function definition */
unsigned char find_largest(unsigned char a, unsigned char b, unsigned char c) {
    unsigned char largest = a;
    if (b > largest) largest = b;
    if (c > largest) largest = c;
    return largest;
}
```

**Explanation:**
- The function `find_largest` uses a *linear comparison* approach: assume the first is largest, then compare with the other two.
- The result is written to `P1`, an SFR of 8051, which can be observed on LEDs or a logic analyzer.
- All variables are `unsigned char` to match 8051's 8-bit native data size.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: (a)** What is recursion? Explain with a code example. Why is recursion generally avoided in 8051-based embedded systems? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an Embedded C function `void swap_bytes(unsigned char *p, unsigned char *q)` that swaps the contents of two memory locations using pass-by-reference. Demonstrate its use to exchange the values stored at internal RAM locations `0x40` and `0x41`, and verify the swap by writing the values to Port 0 and Port 2. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Recursion:**
Recursion is a programming technique in which a function calls itself, either directly or indirectly, to solve a problem. Each recursive call reduces the problem size until a *base case* is reached.

**Example: Computing factorial of n**
```c
int factorial(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;        /* base case */
    return n * factorial(n - 1); /* recursive call */
}
```

**Why recursion is avoided in 8051 embedded systems:**

1. **Limited stack memory:** The 8051 has only **128 bytes** of internal RAM (idata). Each recursive call uses 4–6 bytes for return address, parameters, and local variables.
2. **Stack overflow risk:** A recursion depth of even 20–30 calls can exhaust the stack, causing the MCU to **crash or reset unpredictably**.
3. **Performance overhead:** Each call involves pushing/popping and jumps, making recursion slower than iteration.
4. **Debugging difficulty:** Recursive crashes on MCUs are hard to trace without a debugger.
5. **Predictability:** Real-time systems demand bounded execution time; recursion's depth is data-dependent.

**Preferred alternative:** Convert recursive logic to iterative loops wherever feasible.

#### Model Solution for (b)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

/* Function prototype */
void swap_bytes(unsigned char *p, unsigned char *q);

void main(void) {
    unsigned char *ptr1, *ptr2;
    unsigned char temp;

    ptr1 = (unsigned char *)0x40;
    ptr2 = (unsigned char *)0x41;

    /* Initialize the two RAM locations with distinct values */
    *ptr1 = 0xAA;
    *ptr2 = 0x55;

    /* Display initial values on ports */
    P0 = *ptr1;   /* P0 = 0xAA */
    P2 = *ptr2;   /* P2 = 0x55 */

    /* Swap the contents */
    swap_bytes(ptr1, ptr2);

    /* Display swapped values on ports */
    P0 = *ptr1;   /* P0 should now be 0x55 */
    P2 = *ptr2;   /* P2 should now be 0xAA */

    while(1);
}

/* Function definition — Pass by reference */
void swap_bytes(unsigned char *p, unsigned char *q) {
    unsigned char temp;
    temp = *p;
    *p   = *q;
    *q   = temp;
}
```

**Explanation:**
- Pointers `ptr1` and `ptr2` hold the addresses of internal RAM locations 0x40 and 0x41.
- The function receives these addresses and swaps the **values stored at those addresses** by using a temporary variable.
- After the call, `P0` displays the new value of location 0x40 (`0x55`), and `P2` displays the new value of location 0x41 (`0xAA`).
- This is a classic demonstration of **call by reference**, where modifications inside the function are visible to the caller.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse the **pointer** with the **value pointed to**. Remember: `*p` is the value, `p` is the address.
> - In pass-by-reference questions, marks are awarded only if the function **actually modifies the original variable**, not just a local copy.
> - For 8051 SFR access, always use the `volatile` keyword to prevent compiler optimization from removing legitimate writes.
> - When using `*(unsigned char *)0x30`, ensure you understand this is **memory-mapped access** — wrong addresses can corrupt SFRs and crash the MCU.
> - Forgetting the `while(1);` at the end of `main()` may cause undefined behavior on 8051 (program counter falls off into unknown memory).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Functions and Procedures in Embedded Systems**

- A **function** returns a value; a **procedure** (`void` return) does not.
- Three stages of a C function: **Declaration (prototype) → Definition → Call**.
- A **prototype** is mandatory if a function is called before being defined.
- **Pass by value** = copy of data; original is safe but slow for large data.
- **Pass by reference** = address via pointer; original can be modified; efficient.
- `static` local variables are initialized **once** and retain value across calls.
- `volatile` qualifier is essential for variables shared between ISR and `main`.
- **Recursion is risky** on 8051 due to **128-byte idata limit** — prefer iteration.
- **ISRs** are special `void` functions invoked by hardware, with no params/return.
- **Inline functions** offer type safety; **macros** offer speed but no type checking.
- In embedded code, prefer `static inline` for small, frequently-called utilities.
- Storage class choices:
  - `auto` (default) → block-local temporaries.
  - `register` → compiler hint for fast access.
  - `static` → value retention between calls.
  - `extern` → cross-file sharing.
- **8051 function call overhead** = push return address + push params + pop on return.
- Modular design principles: `Init()` once, `while(1) { Read → Process → Control }`.
- Avoid `printf`, `malloc`, and floating-point inside ISRs — they introduce unbounded latency.
- For memory-mapped peripheral access, use **pointers with `volatile`** qualifier.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly and C Language Programming Applications of Embedded Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assembly and C Language Programming Applications of Embedded Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Assembly Language Programming** in embedded systems refers to the low-level symbolic programming of a target microcontroller/processor using mnemonics that map one-to-one (or near one-to-one) with the machine's native instruction set. Each mnemonic represents a binary opcode understood by the CPU's Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) and control unit.

**Embedded C** is a superset of the standard ANSI C language, extended with features such as **hardware register access through pointers**, **bit-level manipulation**, **interrupt service routine (ISR) declarations**, and the use of type qualifiers (`volatile`, `const`, `register`) to interact deterministically with memory-mapped Special Function Registers (SFRs) and on-chip peripherals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: An *Embedded Application Program* is the firmware (typically written in Assembly, C, or a mixture of both) that orchestrates the MCU's SFRs, memory, and peripherals to perform a dedicated, real-time control or monitoring function inside an embedded product.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the **captain of a ship** (the microcontroller):

- **Assembly Language** is like giving orders **directly to the engine room in technical mechanical jargon**: "Open valve 3, increase steam pressure by 5 PSI, engage gear box at tick 4." You have full, fine-grained control, but you must specify every tiny detail. One wrong word and the engine misfires.

- **Embedded C** is like giving orders to a **chief engineer who translates your high-level intent** ("Move the ship forward at half speed") into those fine-grained mechanical commands. The chief engineer (the compiler) handles bookkeeping, variable storage, and sub-tasks. You get faster development, but you must trust the translation is correct.

- **Mixing C and Assembly** is like being the captain who gives strategic orders in English (C) but personally walks down to the engine room (inline assembly) for the most time-critical operations such as disabling interrupts or executing an exact delay loop.

> [!NOTE]
> Both languages target the **same physical hardware**, but they differ in **abstraction level**, **code size**, **execution speed**, and **developer productivity**.

---

## 1.3 Why Two Languages? The Core Trade-off

| Aspect | Assembly Language | Embedded C |
|---|---|---|
| Abstraction Level | **Lowest (1:1 with opcodes)** | Medium (hardware-aware) |
| Execution Speed | Fastest (hand-optimized) | Fast (compiler-optimized) |
| Code Size | Smallest possible | Larger than Assembly |
| Portability | **CPU-specific** | Highly portable |
| Developer Productivity | Slow | Fast |
| Use Case | Boot code, ISRs, ISRs, DSP loops | Application logic, protocol stacks |

---

## 1.4 Key Hardware Concepts Used During Programming

- **Program Counter (PC)**: Holds the address of the next instruction to fetch (typically **16-bit** on 8051, giving a **64 KB** code memory space).
- **Special Function Registers (SFRs)**: A mapped memory area in the **upper internal RAM (0x80–0xFF on 8051)** used to control peripherals (P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, TMOD, SCON, etc.).
- **Stack Pointer (SP)**: An 8-bit register on 8051 pointing to the top of the stack in internal RAM (resets to **0x07**, conventionally re-initialized to **0x30** or **0x60** by the startup code).
- **Accumulator (A)**: The primary operand register for arithmetic and logic operations.
- **B Register**: Used as a second operand in multiplication and division.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categories of Assembly Instructions (8051 Architecture — Reference)

The 8051 instruction set is grouped into **five functional families**, each mapping to specific opcodes.

### 2.1.1 Data Transfer Instructions
Used to move data between registers, internal RAM, and external memory.

- **MOV** — Move data between registers / immediate-to-register / memory-to-accumulator.
- **MOVX** — Move data to/from **external** data memory (uses `@DPTR` or `@R0/@R1`).
- **MOVC** — Move data from **code memory** (program ROM), accessed via `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC`. Used for lookup tables.
- **PUSH / POP** — Stack operations. **PUSH** increments SP then writes; **POP** reads then decrements SP.

### 2.1.2 Arithmetic Instructions
- **ADD / ADDC** — Addition / addition with carry.
- **SUBB** — Subtract with borrow (note: there is no SUB without borrow on 8051).
- **INC / DEC** — Increment / decrement.
- **MUL AB** — Multiplies A by B, leaves the 16-bit result in **B (high byte) : A (low byte)**.
- **DIV AB** — Divides A by B, quotient in **A**, remainder in **B**.
- **DA A** — Decimal Adjust Accumulator (for BCD arithmetic after ADD/ADDC).

### 2.1.3 Logical Instructions
- **ANL / ORL / XRL** — Bitwise AND / OR / XOR.
- **CLR / CPL** — Clear (to 0) / Complement (bitwise NOT) on A or single bits.
- **RL / RLC / RR / RRC** — Rotate A left/right (with or without carry).
- **SWAP A** — Swap the upper and lower nibbles of A.

### 2.1.4 Boolean (Bit) Instructions
8051 has a **bit-addressable** area of **16 bytes (0x20–0x2F)**, yielding **128 individually addressable bits (00H–7FH)** plus the **16 bit-addressable SFRs** (acc bits, port pins, etc.).

- **SETB bit** — Set bit to 1.
- **CLR bit** — Clear bit to 0.
- **JB bit, rel** — Jump if bit set.
- **JNB bit, rel** — Jump if bit not set.
- **JBC bit, rel** — Jump if bit set, then clear bit.
- **ANL C, bit / ORL C, bit** — AND/OR carry with a bit.

### 2.1.5 Program Branching Instructions
- **ACALL / LCALL** — Absolute call (2 KB page) / Long call (full 64 KB).
- **RET / RETI** — Return from subroutine / Return from interrupt.
- **AJMP / LJMP / SJMP** — Absolute jump / Long jump / Short jump (relative).
- **JC / JNC / JZ / JNZ** — Conditional jumps on carry / zero.
- **CJNE A, direct, rel / DJNZ Rn, rel** — Compare-and-jump / Decrement-and-jump (used for loops).
- **NOP** — No operation (1 machine cycle).

---

## 2.2 Embedded C — Special Constructs

### 2.2.1 Memory-Mapped I/O via Pointers
SFRs are accessed by casting their address to a pointer:

```c
#define PORT1   (*(volatile unsigned char *)0x90)

PORT1 = 0x55;   // Write 0x55 to Port 1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `volatile` qualifier tells the compiler: *"Do not optimize this variable into a register — its value may change unexpectedly because hardware, not software, modifies it."*

### 2.2.2 Bit Manipulation
Two standard idioms are used to set, clear, toggle, and check individual bits:

```c
#define SET_BIT(reg, bit)    ((reg) |=  (1U << (bit)))
#define CLR_BIT(reg, bit)    ((reg) &= ~(1U << (bit)))
#define TOG_BIT(reg, bit)    ((reg) ^=  (1U << (bit)))
#define CHK_BIT(reg, bit)    ((reg) &   (1U << (bit)))
```

### 2.2.3 Interrupt Service Routines
In **Keil C51**, an ISR is declared with the `interrupt` keyword and a vector number:

```c
void Timer0_ISR(void) interrupt 1
{
    TH0 = 0xFC;   // Reload high byte
    TL0 = 0x18;   // Reload low byte
    PORT1 ^= 0x01; // Toggle P1.0
}
```

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Symbol | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Duration | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | 8051 divides crystal by **12** |
| 2 | Instruction Cycle Count | $T_{inst} = N \cdot T_{mc}$ | $N$ depends on opcode |
| 3 | Timer Tick Frequency | $f_{tick} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | One timer increment per $T_{mc}$ |
| 4 | Timer Reload Value | $Reload = 2^{n} - \dfrac{T_{desired}}{T_{tick}}$ | $n = 16$ for Mode 1 |
| 5 | Serial Baud Rate (Mode 1) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \cdot \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$ | **SMOD** in PCON.7 |
| 6 | Program Memory Size | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes = **64 KB** | 16-bit PC |
| 7 | Internal RAM Size | $128$ bytes (00H–7FH) | SFRs map to 80H–FFH |
| 8 | External Address Range | $0x0000 \rightarrow 0xFFFF$ | 16-bit data and code buses |
| 9 | Stack Growth | Grows **upward** in internal RAM | PUSH increments SP |
| 10 | MOVX Cycle Count | **2 machine cycles** | Reading/writing external memory |

> [!NOTE]
> Always substitute $\vert x \vert$ with $\lvert x \rvert$ when writing inline math to avoid table-pipe conflicts. The above formulas are exam-ready.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Embedded Programming Use |
|---|---|
| Automotive | ECU firmware, ABS, airbag controllers (C + critical paths in ASM) |
| Consumer Electronics | Washing machine controllers, microwave ovens (C dominant) |
| Industrial Automation | PLCs, motor drivers (C with ASM PID loops) |
| IoT Devices | Sensor nodes (C + RTOS) |
| Medical Devices | Pacemakers, infusion pumps (ASM-verified C for safety) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Delay Loop Timing Derivation

> **Problem (KTU-style):** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Calculate the time delay produced by the following assembly subroutine.

```asm
DELAY: MOV R0, #0FFH        ; (1 MC)
LOOP:  DJNZ R0, LOOP        ; (2 MC per iteration × 255 iterations)
       RET                  ; (2 MC)
```

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the Machine Cycle Period:**

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{mc} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Total Machine Cycles:**

The `MOV R0, #0FFH` instruction executes **once** and consumes **1 machine cycle**.
The `DJNZ R0, LOOP` instruction executes **255 times** (decrements from FFH down to 00H, then falls through on the 256th decrement), consuming **2 machine cycles per iteration**.
The `RET` instruction consumes **2 machine cycles**.

$$N_{total} = (1 \times 1) + (255 \times 2) + (1 \times 2)$$

$$N_{total} = 1 + 510 + 2 = 513 \text{ machine cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Total Delay:**

$$T_{delay} = N_{total} \times T_{mc} = 513 \times 1.085\,\mu s$$

$$T_{delay} = 556.6\,\mu s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap**: Students often forget the `RET` cost (2 MC) or miscount the DJNZ iterations (it executes the branch 255 times, NOT 256). Always state both assumptions explicitly.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Timer Reload Derivation (1 ms Tick)

> **Problem:** Generate a **1 ms** tick using Timer 0 in **Mode 1** with $f_{osc} = 12\,\text{MHz}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Machine Cycle:**

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12\,\text{MHz}} = 1\,\mu s$$

**Step 2 — Timer Increments Needed for 1 ms:**

$$N_{ticks} = \frac{T_{desired}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{1\,\text{ms}}{1\,\mu s} = 1000$$

**Step 3 — Reload Value for 16-bit Timer:**

$$Reload = 65536 - 1000 = 64536$$

$$64536_{10} = FC18_{H}$$

**Step 4 — Split into High and Low Bytes:**

$$\text{TH0} = \text{FC}_{H}, \quad \text{TL0} = \text{18}_{H}$$

**Step 5 — Embedded C Implementation:**

```c
void Timer0_Init(void)
{
    TMOD = (TMOD & 0xF0) | 0x01;   // Timer 0, Mode 1, 16-bit
    TH0  = 0xFC;
    TL0  = 0x18;
    TR0  = 1;                       // Start timer
    ET0  = 1;                       // Enable Timer 0 interrupt
    EA   = 1;                       // Enable global interrupt
}

void Timer0_ISR(void) interrupt 1
{
    TH0 = 0xFC;                     // Manual reload (no auto-reload in Mode 1)
    TL0 = 0x18;
    /* user action every 1 ms */
}
```

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Serial Baud Rate Derivation

> **Problem:** Calculate the TH1 reload value to achieve **9600 baud** with $f_{osc} = 11.0592\,\text{MHz}$, **SMOD = 0**, in Mode 1.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Start from the baud-rate formula:**

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \cdot \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute SMOD = 0:**

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{1}{32} \cdot \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for $(256 - TH1)$:**

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \cdot 32 \cdot \text{Baud}}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numbers:**

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times 32 \times 9600}$$

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{3.6864 \times 10^{6}} = 3$$

**Step 5 — Solve for TH1:**

$$TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FD}_{H}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz?** Because it is exactly divisible by 12, 32, and all standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 19200, ...), giving a **0 %** error — crucial for clean serial communication.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Bit-Mask Logical Equivalence

> **Problem:** Show that `SET_BIT(P1, 3)` produces the same effect as `P1 = P1 | 0x08;`.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Expand the macro:**

$$\text{SET\_BIT}(P1, 3) \equiv P1 \;\vert\; (1 \ll 3)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the shift:**

$$1 \ll 3 = 0000\;1000_{b} = 0x08$$

**Step 3 — Apply OR mask (preserves all other bits):**

$$P1_{\text{new}} = P1_{\text{old}} \;\vert\; 0x08$$

For example, if $P1_{old} = 0x55 = 0101\;0101$:

$$P1_{new} = 0101\;0101 \;\vert\; 0000\;1000 = 0101\;1101 = 0x5D$$

Bit 3 is now forced to 1; all other bits unchanged.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Programming Tasks

| Application | Peripherals Used | Key SFRs | Algorithm Summary |
|---|---|---|---|
| LED Blinking | GPIO | `P1` | Toggle bit with delay |
| Switch Debouncing | GPIO | `P3`, Timer | Filter bounces via time check |
| 7-Segment Multiplex | GPIO + Timer | `P0`, `P2` | Time-multiplexed display driving |
| LCD Interface | GPIO | `P2`, `P0` | 8-bit/4-bit parallel protocol |
| ADC Reading | ADC | `ADC_CONTR`, `ADC_RES` | Polling or interrupt-driven read |
| PWM Generation | Timer | `CCON`, `CMOD`, `PCA` | Use PCA module or Timer + SW |
| UART Tx/Rx | UART | `SCON`, `SBUF`, `TCON` | Configure Mode 1, baud rate |
| I²C EEPROM | I/O Bit-banging | `P1.0` (SCL), `P1.1` (SDA) | Start, address, ACK, data, Stop |
| Stepper Motor | GPIO + Timer | `P1` (coils) | Phase sequence with hold times |
| Keypad Scan | GPIO | `P3` (rows + cols) | Row-scan matrix algorithm |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Q: Explain the role of the `volatile` qualifier in Embedded C programming. Why is it essential when accessing hardware registers?**

**Model Answer:**
The `volatile` qualifier is a type qualifier in Embedded C that instructs the compiler **not to optimize** reads or writes to a variable, because its value may change **outside the compiler's knowledge** (e.g., modified by hardware or an ISR).

- When accessing **SFRs**, hardware can modify a register between two reads (e.g., a flag bit being cleared by a peripheral).
- Without `volatile`, the compiler may cache the value in a CPU register, leading the program to **read a stale value**.
- With `volatile`, every access is forced to be an **actual load/store from the SFR address**, ensuring correctness.

**Example:**
```c
#define FLAG_REG (*(volatile unsigned char *)0x88)
while (FLAG_REG & 0x01) { /* wait until bit 0 is set */ }
```

Without `volatile`, the compiler might optimize the loop into an infinite one because it thinks `FLAG_REG` never changes.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: Differentiate between `MOV`, `MOVX`, and `MOVC` instructions in the 8051 instruction set.**

**Model Answer:**

| Instruction | Data Source / Destination | Address Space | Address Width | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV` | Internal RAM, SFRs, immediate values | **Internal data memory** | 8-bit | General register/memory copy |
| `MOVX` | External RAM | **External data memory** | 16-bit (via DPTR or R0/R1) | Accessing off-chip RAM/IO |
| `MOVC` | Program ROM (code memory) | **Code memory space** | 16-bit (`A+DPTR` or `A+PC`) | Lookup tables, constant data |

**Key Point:**
- `MOVX` uses **RD/WR strobes** to access off-chip memory.
- `MOVC` uses **PSEN strobe** and reads code memory (read-only).
- Plain `MOV` operates only on the **internal 128 bytes** of RAM and the SFR region.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts typically distributed as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either Question A or Question B** in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**Q(a)** Write an **8051 assembly program** to read the state of **Port P1.0** (a switch) and toggle **Port P2.0** (an LED) **only when the switch is pressed** (logic 0 — active-low). Assume the switch is already hardware-debounced. Use a simple polling loop with a delay of **~100 ms** between reads. Crystal frequency = **11.0592 MHz**.

**Q(b)** Convert the same program to **Embedded C** using `volatile` pointers and explain each line.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Assembly (7 Marks)

```asm
ORG 0000H
LJMP MAIN

ORG 0030H
MAIN:    MOV SP, #60H       ; (1 MC) Move stack to safe area

LOOP:    JB P1.0, LOOP      ; (2 MC) Wait for switch press (P1.0 = 0)
         CPL P2.0           ; (1 MC) Toggle LED

         ; 100 ms delay subroutine call follows
         ACALL DELAY_100MS
         SJMP LOOP          ; (2 MC) Repeat
;--------------------------------------------------------------
DELAY_100MS:
         ; Two nested loops give approx 100 ms
         ; MC count tuned for 11.0592 MHz -> 1.085 us per MC
         MOV R0, #200       ; (1 MC) outer counter
OUTER:   MOV R1, #247       ; (1 MC) inner counter
INNER:   DJNZ R1, INNER     ; (2 MC) 247 × 2 = 494 MC
         DJNZ R0, OUTER     ; (2 MC) 200 × (1 + 494 + 2) = 200 × 497 = 99400 MC
         RET                ; (2 MC)
END
```

**Timing Verification:**

$$T_{delay} = 99400 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx 107.8\,\text{ms}$$

Close to 100 ms within acceptable tolerance.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Embedded C (7 Marks)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit SW   = P1^0;     /* Switch on P1.0, active-low */
sbit LED  = P2^0;     /* LED on P2.0 */

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms)
{
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < ms; ++i)
        for (j = 0; j < 123; ++j)   /* approx 1 ms @ 11.0592 MHz */
            ;
}

void main(void)
{
    while (1)
    {
        if (SW == 0)             /* Active-low switch */
        {
            LED = !LED;          /* Toggle LED */
            delay_ms(100);       /* 100 ms debounce/wait */
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines:**

| Line | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `sbit SW = P1^0;` | Declares `SW` as a **bit-addressable alias** for pin P1.0 — readable and writable as 0/1. |
| `if (SW == 0)` | Checks if switch is pressed (active-low). |
| `LED = !LED;` | Toggles the LED using logical NOT assignment. |
| `delay_ms(100)` | Calls a software delay calibrated to the crystal frequency. |
| `while (1)` | Super-loop — embedded systems run **forever** unless reset. |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]

**Q(a)** Design a **timer-based 1-second blink** on **P1.0** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1** with $f_{osc} = 12\,\text{MHz}$. Show the **reload value calculation** and write the complete **assembly ISR-based** program.

**Q(b)** Explain how **bit manipulation macros** (`SET_BIT`, `CLR_BIT`, `TOG_BIT`) are used in Embedded C, and rewrite the same 1-second blink program in **C using Timer 0 ISR**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Timer-based Assembly (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Required delay for 50 ms (so that HIGH + LOW = 1 second):**

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12\,\text{MHz}} = 1\,\mu s$$

$$N_{ticks} = \frac{50\,\text{ms}}{1\,\mu s} = 50{,}000$$

But Mode 1 is 16-bit (max 65,536), so reload:

$$Reload = 65536 - 50000 = 15536 = 3CB0_{H}$$

$$\text{TH0} = 3C_{H}, \quad \text{TL0} = \text{B0}_{H}$$

**Step 2 — Program:**

```asm
ORG 0000H
LJMP MAIN
ORG 000BH             ; Timer 0 Interrupt Vector
LJMP ISR_T0

ORG 0030H
MAIN:    MOV SP, #60H
         MOV TMOD, #01H   ; Timer 0, Mode 1
         MOV TH0, #3CH
         MOV TL0, #B0H
         SETB ET0         ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
         SETB EA          ; Enable global interrupts
         SETB TR0         ; Start Timer 0
HERE:    SJMP HERE         ; Idle super-loop

ISR_T0:  MOV TH0, #3CH     ; Reload
         MOV TL0, #B0H
         CPL P1.0         ; Toggle LED
         RETI
END
```

**Step 3 — Note:** The interrupt fires every 50 ms; toggling inside the ISR gives a full **100 ms period**, and toggling again at the 10th interrupt gives a **1-second** blink if you use a software counter. The simpler version toggles every 50 ms, producing a 25 Hz blink.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Bit Macros & C ISR (7 Marks)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

#define SET_BIT(reg, bit)    ((reg) |=  (1U << (bit)))
#define CLR_BIT(reg, bit)    ((reg) &= ~(1U << (bit)))
#define TOG_BIT(reg, bit)    ((reg) ^=  (1U << (bit)))

unsigned int tickCount = 0;

void Timer0_Init(void)
{
    TMOD = (TMOD & 0xF0) | 0x01;   /* Timer 0, Mode 1 */
    TH0  = 0x3C;
    TL0  = 0xB0;
    SET_BIT(TR0, 0);               /* Start Timer 0 */
    SET_BIT(ET0, 1);               /* Enable Timer 0 interrupt */
    SET_BIT(EA,  7);               /* Enable global interrupt */
}

void Timer0_ISR(void) interrupt 1
{
    TH0 = 0x3C;
    TL0 = 0xB0;
    tickCount++;
    if (tickCount >= 10)           /* 10 × 50 ms = 500 ms half-period */
    {
        tickCount = 0;
        TOG_BIT(P1, 0);            /* Toggle P1.0 */
    }
}

void main(void)
{
    Timer0_Init();
    while (1) { /* idle — ISR does the work */ }
}
```

**Why bit macros?**
- **Portability:** Works for any SFR, not just port pins.
- **Readability:** `TOG_BIT(P1, 0)` is clearer than `P1 ^= 0x01`.
- **Safety:** Less chance of mis-typed bit masks (e.g., `0x02` vs `0x04`).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always reload TH0/TL0 inside the ISR** — Mode 1 has **no auto-reload**. Forgetting this causes a one-shot 65 ms delay followed by silence.
> 2. **Re-enable interrupts** by setting `EA = 1` globally — a common mistake.
> 3. **Match `TMOD` write carefully** — Timer 0 occupies low nibble, Timer 1 occupies high nibble. Always AND with `0xF0` before OR-ing to avoid clobbering Timer 1.
> 4. **`DJNZ` count**: It executes the branch **N times** for an initial value of `N`, then exits on the (N+1)th decrement.
> 5. **`MOVC` vs `MOVX`**: Mixing these up on the exam loses 2-3 marks instantly. Recall: **C**ode memory for `MOVC`, e**X**ternal RAM for `MOVX`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**

### Assembly Programming Essentials
- [x] **MOV** — internal RAM/SFR transfer.
- [x] **MOVX** — external RAM (uses DPTR or R0/R1; 2 MC).
- [x] **MOVC** — code ROM lookup (uses A+DPTR or A+PC).
- [x] **ACALL** — 2 KB page call; **LCALL** — full 64 KB call.
- [x] **DJNZ** — primary loop counter (2 MC, decrements first).
- [x] **CJNE** — compare and jump if not equal (used for decision logic).
- [x] **SETB / CLR / JB / JNB** — bit-level operations.
- [x] **Machine cycle** = **12 oscillator periods** on 8051.

### Embedded C Essentials
- [x] `volatile` is **mandatory** for SFR access.
- [x] SFR address range: **0x80–0xFF** of internal data memory.
- [x] Bit manipulation via `<<`, `&`, `|`, `^`.
- [x] ISR declared with `interrupt <vector>` keyword (Keil C51).
- [x] Use `sbit` for single-bit SFR aliasing (e.g., `sbit LED = P1^0;`).
- [x] `code` qualifier places constants in ROM (8051-specific).

### Calculation Quick-Refs
- [x] Machine cycle: $T_{mc} = 12 / f_{osc}$.
- [x] Timer reload: $Reload = 2^{16} - (T_{desired} / T_{mc})$.
- [x] Baud rate: $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \cdot \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12(256 - TH1)}$.
- [x] 11.0592 MHz crystal → **0% error** at standard baud rates.

### Exam Strategy
- [x] **Always show the timer/baud-rate derivation** step by step — 3-5 marks guaranteed.
- [x] **Explain why `volatile` is used** — not just "define" it.
- [x] **Draw the SFR memory map** if the question touches SFRs.
- [x] Use **both assembly and C** in comparative questions to score full marks.
- [x] Mention **interrupt latency** and **reentrancy** when discussing ISRs.

---

topics.title: Architectural Support for System Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Architectural Support for System Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Architectural Support for System Development** in the context of Embedded System Design refers to the underlying hardware and software infrastructure — encompassing processor architecture, memory hierarchy, I/O subsystems, system-on-chip (SoC) integration, intellectual property (IP) cores, operating system support units (timers, interrupt controllers, MMUs), and development toolchains — that collectively enable the efficient design, debugging, deployment, and maintenance of an embedded product.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus (Module 4), this topic specifically covers the selection criteria, design trade-offs, and integration strategies that an embedded systems engineer must adopt to translate a product specification into a working hardware–software co-designed system.

The core elements of architectural support can be enumerated as:

1. **Processor Architecture Support** — RISC vs CISC, Harvard vs Von Neumann, single-core vs multi-core.
2. **Memory Architecture Support** — RAM, ROM, Flash, Cache, MMU/MPU support.
3. **I/O Architecture Support** — Polling, Interrupt-driven, DMA-based transfers.
4. **SoC & IP-Core Support** — Reusable hardware blocks, soft/firm/hard cores.
5. **OS Architectural Support** — Timers, Watchdogs, Interrupt Controllers, Memory Protection Units.
6. **Development Environment Support** — Cross-compilers, In-Circuit Emulators (ICE), JTAG debuggers, simulators.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy — Building a Customised House**  
> Imagine that designing an embedded system is like **building a customised house on a tight budget**:
>
> - The **processor** is the *foundation + main structure* — it determines how strong, fast, and flexible the house can be.
> - The **memory architecture** is the *storage area* (cupboards, garage, warehouse) — you must choose between cheap, slow, spacious storage versus fast, expensive, small storage.
> - The **I/O subsystem** is the *doors and windows* — they let the house communicate with the outside world.
> - **SoC and IP cores** are *pre-fabricated rooms* (kitchen, bathroom) that you can drop in rather than building from scratch.
> - **OS support (timers, interrupts, MMU)** is the *electrical wiring and plumbing* — invisible infrastructure that makes everything else work smoothly.
> - **Development tools (JTAG, ICE, cross-compilers)** are the *blueprint, measuring tape, and architect's oversight* — they let you design, test, and fix the house *before* and *after* construction.

This analogy is what the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned pedagogy promotes — connecting low-level hardware concepts to relatable, high-level reasoning.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following **bold**-highlighted metrics form the syllabus vocabulary that every KTU student must internalise:

- **MIPS** (Million Instructions Per Second) — processor throughput metric.
- **MFLOPS** (Million Floating Point Operations Per Second) — DSP/CPU performance for numeric workloads.
- **DMIPS** — Dhrystone MIPS, an integer benchmark metric.
- **Clock Frequency (f_clk)** — measured in **MHz** or **GHz**.
- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** — average clock cycles consumed per machine instruction.
- **Memory Latency** — time delay between request and data delivery, in **nanoseconds (ns)**.
- **Bus Bandwidth** — maximum data transfer rate, in **MB/s** or **GB/s**.
- **Power Dissipation (P)** — in **milliwatts (mW)** or **watts (W)**.
- **MIPS/Watt** — energy efficiency metric, crucial for battery-powered embedded nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 4 specifically tests the student's ability to **justify a processor-memory-I/O selection** for a given embedded application based on constraints such as cost, power, performance, and real-time determinism.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Processor Architecture Support

A processor is the *heart* of an embedded system. Architectural support begins here.

#### 2.1.1 Von Neumann vs Harvard Architecture

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| **Bus Structure** | Single bus for data and instructions | Separate buses for data and instructions |
| **Fetch & Execute** | Sequential (one at a time) | Simultaneous (overlap possible) | 
| **Clock Cycles/Inst.** | Higher (CPI ≥ 2 typical) | Lower (CPI ≈ 1 achievable) |
| **Power Consumption** | Lower | Higher (more bus toggling) |
| **Determinism** | Less deterministic | Highly deterministic |
| **Typical Use** | General-purpose PCs | DSP, Microcontrollers (e.g., 8051, ARM Cortex-M) |
| **Example CPU** | Intel x86 family | ARM Cortex-M, PIC, AVR |

> [!NOTE]  
> Most modern embedded processors use a **Modified Harvard Architecture** — physically separate caches for instructions and data, but unified external memory bus. ARM Cortex-M3/M4 is a textbook KTU example.

#### 2.1.2 RISC vs CISC

- **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer):** Fixed-length instructions, load/store architecture, large register file. Examples: ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, PowerPC.
- **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer):** Variable-length instructions, memory-to-memory operations. Examples: x86, 68k.

**Why RISC dominates embedded systems:**
1. Simpler hardware → lower power, lower die area.
2. Easier pipelining → higher clock frequencies.
3. Compiler-friendly → predictable performance for real-time tasks.

#### 2.1.3 Processor Selection Criteria

An embedded designer must balance these factors:

- **Performance:** MIPS, MFLOPS, DMIPS ratings.
- **Power:** Active (mW/MHz) and sleep (µW) dissipation.
- **Cost:** One-time licensing (for IP cores) + per-unit silicon cost.
- **Package & I/O:** Pin count, peripheral mix (UART, I²C, SPI, USB, CAN, ADC).
- **Toolchain maturity:** GCC support, IDE availability, RTOS port availability.
- **Scalability:** From low-end 8-bit (8051) to high-end 64-bit (Cortex-A53).

---

### 2.2 Memory Architecture Support

Memory in embedded systems is hierarchical. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises the **memory pyramid**:

| Level | Type | Typical Size | Latency | Volatility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L1 Cache (on-chip) | SRAM | 4 KB – 64 KB | 1–4 cycles | Volatile |
| L2 Cache (on-chip) | SRAM | 64 KB – 1 MB | 10–20 cycles | Volatile |
| Main Memory (off-chip) | SDRAM/DDR | 1 MB – 1 GB | 50–100 ns | Volatile |
| Storage | NOR/NAND Flash, EEPROM | 1 MB – 1 TB | 50–100 µs | Non-volatile |

#### 2.2.1 Memory Selection Rules

- **Code storage:** NOR Flash (XIP — Execute In Place) or Mask ROM.
- **Data storage:** NAND Flash (high density) for bulk; EEPROM for small config.
- **Runtime heap/stack:** SRAM or PSRAM.
- **Boot loader:** Often Mask ROM or internal Flash at a fixed address.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **MMU vs MPU:**  
> - **MMU (Memory Management Unit):** Full virtual-to-physical address translation with paging. Used in Cortex-A series running Linux.  
> - **MPU (Memory Protection Unit):** Only enforces *access permissions* on pre-defined regions. Used in Cortex-M series running RTOS.  
> MPU is lighter, faster, and deterministic — ideal for hard real-time embedded systems.

---

### 2.3 I/O Architecture Support

There are three fundamental mechanisms for I/O data transfer:

1. **Programmed I/O (Polling):** CPU actively waits in a loop. Simple, deterministic, but CPU-wasting.
2. **Interrupt-driven I/O:** Device raises an interrupt when ready. CPU is free to do other tasks. Preferred for low-bandwidth, asynchronous events.
3. **DMA (Direct Memory Access):** Dedicated controller offloads bulk data transfer between peripheral and memory. CPU only sets up the descriptor. Best for high-bandwidth, streaming data (audio, video, network packets).

> [!NOTE]  
> Modern SoCs like NXP i.MX8, STM32H7, and TI Sitara integrate **dedicated DMA engines** (e.g., ARM's PL080, Microchip's DMAC) as core architectural support.

---

### 2.4 System-on-Chip (SoC) and IP Core Support

#### 2.4.1 SoC Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> A **System-on-Chip (SoC)** is an integrated circuit that consolidates **all major functional blocks** of a complete electronic system onto a single silicon die: CPU cores, memory controllers, peripheral interfaces, timers, ADC/DAC, and often wireless radios.

#### 2.4.2 IP Core Classification

| IP Type | Description | Flexibility | Performance | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Soft IP** | Synthesizable HDL (RTL) | Highest | Lowest (place-and-route dependent) | UART, I²C controllers |
| **Firm IP** | Netlist-level, partly placed | Medium | Medium | DSP blocks, crypto engines |
| **Hard IP** | GDSII layout, fully placed | Lowest | Highest (timing-closed) | SRAM compilers, PLL, ADC |

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly asks students to compare these three IP delivery forms in exam answers.

#### 2.4.3 SoC Design Flow

The architectural support for SoC development includes:
- **Specification → RTL Design → Synthesis → Place & Route → Verification → Fabrication → Test.**
- Supported by **EDA tools**: Cadence, Synopsys, Mentor Graphics (now Siemens EDA), and open-source flows like OpenROAD and Yosys.

---

### 2.5 Architectural Support for Operating Systems

A real-time OS (RTOS) requires specific hardware support:

| OS Support Unit | Function | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **System Timer (SysTick)** | Generates periodic tick for scheduler | Time slicing, delay |
| **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** | Resets system if software hangs | Safety-critical systems |
| **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** | Prioritises and vectors IRQs | ARM Cortex-M |
| **Generic Interrupt Controller (GIC)** | Multi-core interrupt routing | ARM Cortex-A |
| **MMU/MPU** | Memory protection & virtual addressing | Process isolation |
| **FPU (Floating Point Unit)** | Hardware floating-point math | DSP, control loops |
| **Cache Controller** | Manages L1/L2 caches | High-performance cores |
| **Bus Matrix / NoC** | Arbitrates multi-master bus access | Multi-core SoC |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why SysTick matters:** In FreeRTOS, the SysTick interrupt triggers the scheduler's `vTaskSwitchContext()`. Without it, no preemptive multitasking is possible — a fact often tested in KTU Part A questions.

---

### 2.6 Development Environment Support

Hardware-software co-design demands robust development tools. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats the following as *architectural support*:

1. **Cross-Compilation Toolchain:** GCC + binutils + newlib, producing target binaries on a host PC.
2. **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE):** Replaces the target CPU with a header-connected emulator pod for real-time debugging.
3. **JTAG / SWD Interface:** Standardised debug port; uses TAP (Test Access Port) with TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO signals.
4. **On-Chip Debug (OCD):** Embedded debug logic (e.g., ARM CoreSight) allowing breakpoints, watchpoints, trace.
5. **Logic Analyser / Oscilloscope:** For verifying bus timing, peripheral waveforms.
6. **RTOS-aware Debugging:** IDE plugins that show task states, stack usage (e.g., Percepio Tracealyzer, SEGGER SystemView).
7. **Simulators:** QEMU, OVP, Renode — allow software development before hardware is available.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following equations are the *high-yield* analytical tools that the KTU 2024 module expects a student to apply. All symbols are universally accepted in embedded systems literature.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPU Time} &= N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}} \\
&= \frac{N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI}}{f_{\text{clk}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MIPS} &= \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Amdahl's Law:} \quad S_{\text{overall}} &= \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{k}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory Bandwidth} &= \frac{\text{Bus Width (bytes)} \times f_{\text{bus}}}{10^{6}} \;\; \text{MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dynamic Power} \; P_{\text{dyn}} &= \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy per Instruction (EPI)} &= \frac{P \cdot \text{CPU Time}}{N_{\text{inst}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Interrupt Latency} &= T_{\text{recognition}} + T_{\text{save state}} + T_{\text{ISR entry}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DMA Transfer Time} &= T_{\text{setup}} + \frac{\text{Data Size (bytes)}}{\text{DMA Rate (bytes/s)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Symbol Legend (for formula sheet):**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $N_{\text{inst}}$ | Number of executed instructions | dimensionless |
| CPI | Average cycles per instruction | cycles/instruction |
| $T_{\text{clk}}$ | Clock period | seconds |
| $f_{\text{clk}}$ | Clock frequency | Hz |
| $f$ | Parallelisable fraction (Amdahl) | dimensionless, $0 \le f \le 1$ |
| $k$ | Speedup of parallel portion | dimensionless |
| $\alpha$ | Switching activity factor | dimensionless, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ |
| $C$ | Load capacitance | Farads |
| $V$ | Supply voltage | Volts |
| $f$ | Clock frequency (in $P_{\text{dyn}}$) | Hz |

> [!WARNING]  
> In the dynamic power equation, **the symbol $f$ is reused** for two different meanings (parallel fraction vs. clock frequency). In KTU answer scripts, *always* use a *clearly different symbol* — e.g., $f_p$ for parallel fraction and $f_{\text{clk}}$ for clock — to avoid examiner confusion and the resulting mark deduction.

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The architectural support concept underpins every commercial embedded product:

- **Automotive ECUs** (Bosch, Continental) use **multi-core lockstep Cortex-R52 cores with MPU and dedicated interrupt controllers** for ASIL-D functional safety.
- **Smartphones** (Apple A17, Snapdragon 8 Gen 3) are SoCs that integrate a **12-core CPU, GPU, NPU, ISP, 5G modem, and on-die LPDDR5X controller** — all architectural support.
- **IoT nodes** (Espressif ESP32, Nordic nRF52) rely on **ultra-low-power architectural support**: deep-sleep currents in single-digit µA, with RTC + WDT and DMA.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: CPU Execution Time and Performance

**Problem Statement (Worked Example 1):**  
A KTU sample question asks: *A processor runs at 120 MHz, executes 50 million instructions, and has an average CPI of 1.5. Calculate the CPU execution time, the MIPS rating, and the total energy consumed if the average power dissipation is 75 mW.*

**Step 1 — Identify the governing formula.**

The fundamental relationship between execution time, instruction count, CPI, and clock frequency is:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

The clock period is the inverse of the clock frequency:

$$
T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

We are given:
- $N_{\text{inst}} = 50 \times 10^{6}$ instructions
- $\text{CPI} = 1.5$ cycles/instruction
- $f_{\text{clk}} = 120 \times 10^{6}$ Hz

Substituting:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 50 \times 10^{6} \times 1.5 \times \frac{1}{120 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify numerator and denominator separately.**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{75 \times 10^{6}}{120 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{75}{120} = 0.625 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute MIPS rating.**

Using the standard MIPS formula:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{120 \times 10^{6}}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{120}{1.5} = 80 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute total energy consumed.**

Energy is power multiplied by time:

$$
E = P \times T_{\text{CPU}}
$$

Given $P = 75 \text{ mW} = 75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W}$:

$$
E = 75 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.625 = 46.875 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 46.875 \text{ mJ}
$$

**Final Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| CPU Time ($T_{\text{CPU}}$) | **0.625 s** |
| MIPS Rating | **80 MIPS** |
| Energy Consumed | **46.875 mJ** |

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Embedded Speedup

**Problem Statement (Worked Example 2):**  
A KTU Part B question may ask: *A particular image-processing algorithm executed on an embedded DSP takes 800 ms. 75% of the algorithm is parallelisable, and we add a co-processor that accelerates the parallel portion by 8×. Calculate the overall speedup, the new execution time, and the maximum theoretical speedup with infinite co-processor speed.*

**Step 1 — State Amdahl's Law.**

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{k}}
$$

Where:
- $f = 0.75$ (parallelisable fraction)
- $k = 8$ (parallel portion speedup)

**Step 2 — Substitute into the denominator.**

$$
(1 - f) = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25
$$

$$
\frac{f}{k} = \frac{0.75}{8} = 0.09375
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the denominator terms and invert.**

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.09375} = \frac{1}{0.34375}
$$

$$
S_{\text{overall}} \approx 2.909
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the new execution time.**

$$
T_{\text{new}} = \frac{T_{\text{old}}}{S_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{800 \text{ ms}}{2.909} \approx 275.0 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the maximum theoretical speedup (as $k \to \infty$).**

When the co-processor is infinitely fast, the parallel portion takes zero time, so the speedup is limited only by the serial portion:

$$
S_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4
$$

**Final Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Overall Speedup ($S_{\text{overall}}$) | **≈ 2.91×** |
| New Execution Time | **≈ 275 ms** |
| Maximum Theoretical Speedup | **4×** |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Insight:** Even with an infinitely fast co-processor, we can never beat **4× speedup** because the remaining 25% of the code is inherently serial. This is why KTU emphasises Amdahl's Law — to teach students that *adding hardware does not linearly add performance*.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: DMA Transfer vs CPU-Driven Transfer

**Problem Statement (Worked Example 3):**  
*Transfer a 1 MB buffer from a UART to memory. CPU-driven transfer takes 1000 cycles per byte; DMA transfer takes 50 cycles of CPU setup plus 1 cycle per byte for descriptor walking. Clock = 100 MHz. Calculate and compare the total time for both methods.*

**Step 1 — CPU-driven time.**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = N_{\text{bytes}} \times C_{\text{per byte}} \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

With $N_{\text{bytes}} = 1 \text{ MB} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes}$, $C_{\text{per byte}} = 1000$, $T_{\text{clk}} = 10 \text{ ns}$:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 2^{20} \times 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} = 10.48576 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — DMA-driven time.**

$$
T_{\text{DMA}} = \left( N_{\text{bytes}} + N_{\text{setup}} \right) \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{DMA}} = (2^{20} + 50) \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \approx 10.4858 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the speedup.**

$$
S_{\text{DMA}} = \frac{T_{\text{CPU}}}{T_{\text{DMA}}} = \frac{10.48576 \text{ s}}{10.4858 \text{ ms}} \approx 1000
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> This is a **1000× speedup** because the CPU is *not* doing per-byte work; the DMA engine handles it autonomously while the CPU is free for other tasks. This is the single most compelling reason KTU includes DMA in the *architectural support* syllabus.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Architecture Support Choices by Application Domain

| Application Domain | Processor Type | Memory | I/O Mechanism | OS Support Required |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Washing Machine** | 8-bit MCU (8051) | Internal Flash + SRAM | Polling + WDT | None (bare-metal) |
| **Digital Camera** | 32-bit SoC (Cortex-M7) | SDRAM + NAND Flash | DMA for image sensor | Lightweight RTOS |
| **Automotive ECU** | 32-bit lockstep (Cortex-R52) | ECC SRAM + Flash | CAN, FlexRay + DMA | AUTOSAR OS |
| **Smartphone** | 64-bit SoC (Cortex-A78) | LPDDR5X + UFS 4.0 | USB, PCIe, DisplayPort | Android (Linux kernel) |
| **IoT Sensor Node** | 32-bit low-power (Cortex-M0+) | Internal Flash | I²C/SPI + sleep | Optional TinyOS / Contiki |
| **Industrial PLC** | 32-bit Cortex-M4 + FPGA | Battery-backed SRAM | EtherCAT, Profinet | FreeRTOS or VxWorks |
| **Avionics** | Multi-core (Cortex-A53 + GPU) | Rad-hard Flash + SRAM | MIL-STD-1553, ARINC 664 | RTOS + hypervisor |

This matrix is **gold-dust** for KTU 2024 Part B (14-mark) questions that ask: *"Select a suitable architecture for X application and justify."*

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Generic Embedded SoC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpuCore[CPU Core - RISC Pipeline] --> busMatrix[AMBA AHB APB Bus Matrix]
    busMatrix --> sramCtl[SRAM Controller]
    busMatrix --> flashCtl[Flash Controller]
    busMatrix --> dmaEng[DMA Engine]
    busMatrix --> intCtl[NVIC Interrupt Controller]
    busMatrix --> timer1[SysTick Timer]
    busMatrix --> wdt1[Watchdog Timer]
    busMatrix --> uart1[UART]
    busMatrix --> i2c1[I2C Bus]
    busMatrix --> spi1[SPI Bus]
    busMatrix --> adc1[ADC Channel]
    busMatrix --> pwm1[PWM Output]
    sramCtl --> sramMem[On-chip SRAM]
    flashCtl --> flashMem[External Flash]
    dmaEng --> extMem[External SDRAM]
    intCtl --> extIrq[External IRQ Lines]
    timer1 --> rtosTick[RTOS Scheduler Tick]
    wdt1 --> sysReset[System Reset Generator]
    uart1 --> uartIO[UART I/O Pins]
    i2c1 --> i2cIO[I2C I/O Pins]
    spi1 --> spiIO[SPI I/O Pins]
    adc1 --> adcIO[ADC Input Pins]
    pwm1 --> pwmIO[PWM Output Pins]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> The above **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mirrors the canonical ARM Cortex-M SoC layout — the same topology a student would find in STM32, NXP LPC, or Nordic nRF datasheets. In KTU 2024 answer sheets, drawing *even a simplified version* of this diagram with all bus connections fetches 2–3 bonus marks.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Embedded System Development Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spec[Product Specification] --> archSel[Architecture Selection]
    archSel --> procSel[Processor Selection]
    archSel --> memSel[Memory Selection]
    archSel --> ioSel[I/O and Peripheral Selection]
    procSel --> rtl[RTL or Firmware Design]
    memSel --> rtl
    ioSel --> rtl
    rtl --> sim[Simulation and Verification]
    sim --> synth[Synthesis or Cross-Compilation]
    synth --> proto[Prototype Bring-up]
    proto --> debug[JTAG SWD Debug]
    debug --> test[Validation and Certification]
    test --> prod[Mass Production]
    prod --> maint[Field Maintenance and Updates]
    maint -.OTA Firmware Update.-> debug
```

> [!NOTE]  
> Notice the **feedback loop from maintenance back to debug** — this represents the **OTA (Over-The-Air) firmware update** cycle, an increasingly common architectural requirement in modern IoT devices. KTU 2024 emphasises this closed-loop support as a measure of a *complete* development environment.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Soft IP vs Hard IP Integration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph softIPPool[Soft IP Library - Synthesizable HDL]
        softUart[UART Soft Core]
        softI2c[I2C Soft Core]
        softSpi[SPI Soft Core]
    end
    subgraph firmIPPool[Firm IP Library - Netlist]
        firmDsp[DSP Block Netlist]
        firmCrypto[AES Crypto Engine]
    end
    subgraph hardIPPool[Hard IP Library - GDSII]
        hardSram[SRAM Compiler]
        hardPll[PLL Block]
        hardAdc[ADC Hard Macro]
    end
    softIPPool --> socBus[SoC Bus Matrix]
    firmIPPool --> socBus
    hardIPPool --> socBus
    socBus --> socTapeout[SoC Tapeout]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> In a real SoC design, **soft IPs** are compiled onto FPGA or ASIC fabric, **firm IPs** are already placed but not routed, and **hard IPs** are guaranteed-timing physical blocks. Combining all three optimises for *flexibility* and *performance* simultaneously — a hallmark of professional SoC architectural support.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Interrupt-Driven I/O

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Application Code
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    participant NVIC as NVIC Controller
    participant Dev as Peripheral Device
    App->>CPU: Enable IRQ in NVIC
    CPU->>NVIC: Set priority
    App->>App: Execute main loop
    Dev->>NVIC: Raise IRQ line
    NVIC->>CPU: Interrupt vector fetch
    CPU->>CPU: Push context, jump to ISR
    CPU->>Dev: Read data register
    Dev-->>CPU: Returns data
    CPU->>CPU: Service interrupt
    CPU->>App: Return from ISR, restore context
    App->>App: Resume main loop
```

> [!NOTE]  
> The **NVIC** in ARM Cortex-M is a textbook KTU example of architectural support. It supports *tail-chaining* (back-to-back interrupts with only 6 cycles overhead) and *late arrival* — features that reduce worst-case interrupt latency to a deterministic value, essential for hard real-time systems.

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram: Memory Hierarchy with MMU/MPU

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpuCore2[CPU Core] --> l1iCache[L1 Instruction Cache]
    cpuCore2 --> l1dCache[L1 Data Cache]
    l1iCache --> l2Cache[Unified L2 Cache]
    l1dCache --> l2Cache
    l2Cache --> mmuOrMpu{MMU or MPU}
    mmuOrMpu --> mainMem[Main External SDRAM]
    mmuOrMpu --> flashArea[Flash Code Region]
    mmuOrMpu --> ioRegion[Memory Mapped I/O Region]
    mmuOrMpu --> periphRegion[Peripheral Register Region]
    mainMem --> extStorage[Bulk NAND/eMMC Storage]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> The MMU/MPU block is the *gatekeeper* — it enforces access permissions, translates virtual to physical addresses, and triggers a *fault exception* on illegal access. In RTOS environments, this enables **process isolation** (one task cannot corrupt another's memory), a critical architectural support feature for safety-critical embedded applications.
