## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**  
*Define "Architectural Support" in the context of embedded system design. List any four hardware units that constitute architectural support for an RTOS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Architectural support refers to the hardware features and integrated units within a processor/SoC that facilitate and accelerate the design, debugging, execution, and maintenance of embedded software.

> **Four RTOS-supporting units (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **System Timer (SysTick):** Generates periodic interrupt ticks for the RTOS scheduler, enabling time slicing and delay management.
> 2. **Watchdog Timer (WDT):** Resets the system if the firmware becomes unresponsive, ensuring recovery from software faults.
> 3. **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC):** Prioritises and vectors peripheral interrupts, supporting deterministic preemption.
> 4. **Memory Protection Unit (MPU):** Enforces access permissions on memory regions, enabling process isolation in an RTOS environment.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**  
*Differentiate between Soft IP, Firm IP, and Hard IP cores in SoC design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Soft IP | Firm IP | Hard IP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delivery Form** | Synthesizable HDL (RTL code) | Gate-level netlist (partially placed) | GDSII layout (fully placed & routed) |
| **Flexibility** | Highest (technology-independent) | Medium (technology-mapped) | Lowest (fixed for one process) |
| **Performance** | Lower (P&R-dependent) | Medium | Highest (timing-closed) |
| **Portability** | High | Medium | Low |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **Modified Harvard architecture** with a neat block diagram. Compare it with the pure **Von Neumann** and **Harvard** architectures in terms of bus structure, performance, and typical applications.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* An embedded DSP executes 200 million instructions at 240 MHz with an average CPI of 1.2. Calculate the (i) CPU execution time, (ii) MIPS rating, and (iii) total energy consumed if the dynamic power is 90 mW. If 60% of the program is parallelisable and is moved to a co-processor offering 6× speedup, find the new overall speedup and execution time.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**Part (a) — Modified Harvard Architecture (7 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** Modified Harvard architecture uses *physically separate* instruction and data memories (with separate buses) for the highest performance, *but exposes a unified memory map* to the programmer, allowing the CPU to access data from the instruction memory (e.g., literal pools in ARM).
- **Block diagram (3 marks):** (Draw two separate paths from CPU to I-Mem and D-Mem, then a unified external memory bus.)
- **Comparison Table (2 marks):** As given in §2.1.1 of these notes — Von Neumann: single bus, slower; Pure Harvard: separate buses, complex; Modified Harvard: best of both worlds, used in ARM Cortex-M.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — CPU execution time.**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI}}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{200 \times 10^{6} \times 1.2}{240 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{240 \times 10^{6}}{240 \times 10^{6}} = 1.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — MIPS rating.**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{240 \times 10^{6}}{1.2 \times 10^{6}} = 200 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy consumed.**

$$
E = P \times T_{\text{CPU}} = 90 \text{ mW} \times 1.0 \text{ s} = 90 \text{ mJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Amdahl's law for co-processor.**

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.6) + \frac{0.6}{6}} = \frac{1}{0.4 + 0.1} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2.0
$$

**Step 5 — New execution time.**

$$
T_{\text{new}} = \frac{1.0 \text{ s}}{2.0} = 0.5 \text{ s}
$$

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| CPU Time | **1.0 s** |
| MIPS | **200** |
| Energy | **90 mJ** |
| Overall Speedup | **2.0×** |
| New Time | **0.5 s** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **architectural support for an RTOS** in an ARM Cortex-M based SoC. Discuss the role of SysTick timer, NVIC, and MPU in supporting real-time multitasking.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A 1 MB bulk data transfer is performed between an SPI peripheral and external SDRAM. CPU-driven transfer consumes 800 cycles per byte; DMA-driven transfer consumes 200 cycles of CPU setup plus 1 cycle per byte for descriptor handling. The system clock is 150 MHz. Calculate the time and energy for both methods, given that the CPU active power is 60 mW. State which method is preferable and justify.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**Part (a) — RTOS Architectural Support (7 marks):**

- **Block diagram (3 marks):** Show CPU core connected to NVIC, SysTick, MPU, WDT, and the bus matrix that fans out to peripherals and memory.
- **SysTick role (1.5 marks):** Generates fixed-rate interrupt that drives the scheduler tick. In FreeRTOS, `configTICK_RATE_HZ` is derived from SysTick reload value.
- **NVIC role (1.5 marks):** Manages interrupt priorities, supports preemption and tail-chaining, providing deterministic interrupt latency.
- **MPU role (1 mark):** Partitions memory into privileged/unprivileged regions, enabling task isolation and stack overflow detection.

**Part (b) — DMA vs CPU Transfer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — CPU-driven time.**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 2^{20} \times 800 \times T_{\text{clk}} = 2^{20} \times 800 \times \frac{1}{150 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{839{,}680{,}000}{150 \times 10^{6}} \approx 5.598 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — CPU-driven energy.**

$$
E_{\text{CPU}} = 60 \text{ mW} \times 5.598 \text{ s} = 335.88 \text{ mJ}
$$

**Step 3 — DMA-driven time.**

$$
T_{\text{DMA}} = (2^{20} + 200) \times T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1{,}048{,}776}{150 \times 10^{6}} \approx 6.992 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — DMA-driven energy (CPU active only during setup).**

$$
E_{\text{DMA, setup}} = 60 \text{ mW} \times \frac{200}{150 \times 10^{6}} = 60 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.333 \times 10^{-6} = 8.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ J} = 80 \text{ nJ}
$$

The DMA engine's own power is typically much lower and is neglected here.

**Step 5 — Comparison.**

| Method | Time | CPU Energy |
|---|---|---|
| CPU-driven | 5.598 s | 335.88 mJ |
| DMA-driven | 6.992 ms | ~80 nJ |
| **Speedup** | **~800×** | **~4.2 million×** |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** DMA is vastly preferable for bulk transfers — it frees the CPU, reduces energy, and meets real-time deadlines. CPU-driven I/O is justified only for short, latency-critical handshakes.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Evaluations:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** A CPU time written as "0.625" without "seconds" loses 0.5 marks. Always state the unit.
> 2. **Mixing $f$ symbols:** Using one letter $f$ for both *parallel fraction* and *clock frequency* in the same answer confuses the examiner. Use $f_p$ and $f_{\text{clk}}$ distinctly.
> 3. **Vague I/O answers:** Stating "interrupt-driven I/O" without specifying *which interrupt controller* or *how priorities are handled* loses 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Missing the SoC context:** In Part B questions, students often discuss peripherals without linking them to the SoC's *bus matrix* or *DMA engine*. Always frame the answer in terms of the **integrated SoC architecture**.
> 5. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-questions:** Even a hand-drawn simplified block diagram adds 1–2 marks. KTU examiners reward visualisation.
> 6. **Neglecting determinism:** Real-time embedded systems demand *deterministic* interrupt latency. Mentioning "deterministic" wherever NVIC, DMA, or SysTick is discussed is a high-yield habit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**
>
> ✅ **Architectural support** = hardware + software infrastructure that enables embedded product design, debug, and maintenance.
>
> ✅ **Modified Harvard** is the de facto embedded standard (ARM Cortex-M).
>
> ✅ **MMU vs MPU:** MMU does virtual-to-physical translation (Linux); MPU only enforces permissions (RTOS).
>
> ✅ **RISC** dominates embedded systems: simple, low-power, pipelinable, compiler-friendly.
>
> ✅ **I/O mechanisms:** Polling (simple, CPU-wasting), Interrupt (event-driven, deterministic), DMA (bulk, offloads CPU).
>
> ✅ **SysTick** = RTOS scheduler tick; **NVIC** = interrupt control; **WDT** = recovery; **MPU** = protection.
>
> ✅ **Soft IP** = HDL (flexible, low performance); **Firm IP** = netlist (balanced); **Hard IP** = GDSII (fast, inflexible).
>
> ✅ **CPU time** $T = N \times \text{CPI} / f_{\text{clk}}$; **MIPS** $= f / (\text{CPI} \times 10^{6})$.
>
> ✅ **Amdahl's Law** limits maximum speedup to $1/(1-f)$ no matter how fast the parallel hardware is.
>
> ✅ **DMA speedup** over CPU-driven I/O can exceed 1000× for bulk transfers.
>
> ✅ **JTAG/SWD** = standard debug interface; **CoreSight** = ARM's on-chip debug architecture.
>
> ✅ **SoC** = whole system on one die; integrates CPU, memory controllers, peripherals, often radios.
>
> ✅ **Power equation:** $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C V^2 f$ — voltage has a quadratic effect, hence DVFS for power saving.
>
> ✅ **Determinism** is the hallmark of real-time architectural support; cite it in every RTOS/embedded answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The ARM Memory Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The ARM Memory Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **ARM Memory Interface** refers to the standardized set of buses, signals, protocols, and timing rules that govern how the ARM processor core exchanges address, data, and control information with external memory devices (ROM, SRAM, DRAM, Flash) and memory-mapped peripherals. In ARM-based embedded systems, the memory interface is implemented as part of the **Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)** specification, which defines the rules for on-chip and off-chip communication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The ARM Memory Interface covers the AMBA bus hierarchy, the memory map, address decoding, endianness, data alignment, and memory access timing. These are the four pillars of connecting an ARM core to the outside world.

The ARM core does **not** connect directly to memory pins. Instead, the processor issues transactions on a standardized **bus protocol** that external bus controllers, address decoders, and memory devices interpret.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Postal System"

Imagine the ARM processor as a **researcher** working in a private office who needs books from a **central library** (memory).

| Real World | ARM Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Researcher (you) | ARM Processor Core |
| Postal Service | AMBA Bus (AHB / ASB / APB) |
| Library Catalog Number | Memory Address |
| Borrowing Slip | Read/Write Control Signal |
| Reference Section vs. New Arrivals | Address Decoded Memory Banks |
| Reading from Left vs. Right Page | Big-Endian vs. Little-Endian |
| Asking for "Chapter 5 starting on page 3" | Non-Aligned (Unaligned) Access |

The researcher does not walk into the library stacks himself; he fills out a slip (address + control signals), the postal system (bus) carries it, and the librarian (bus controller) hands him the correct book. If the slip is filled in the **wrong format** (wrong bus protocol), no book arrives. If the slip asks for a book that does not exist (unmapped address), the librarian returns a fault. This is exactly how the ARM memory interface works.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture):**
> AMBA is the open-standard, on-chip interconnection specification developed by ARM Holdings that defines how functional blocks (processor, memory, peripherals) inside a System-on-Chip (SoC) communicate. Its three principal bus families are **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)**, **ASB (Advanced System Bus)**, and **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **AHB bus width:** typically **32 bits** (also 64 / 128 bits in newer variants such as AXI).
- **APB bus width:** typically **8 / 16 / 32 bits**.
- **ARM7 / ARM9 typical operating frequency:** **20 MHz to 200 MHz**.
- **Memory-mapped I/O address range:** 0x00000000 to 0xFFFFFFFF (full 4 GB for 32-bit ARM).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the ARM Memory Interface

The interface between an ARM core and its memory is built on three intertwined concepts:

1. **Bus Protocol (AMBA)** — How the request is *packaged* and *delivered*.
2. **Memory Map & Address Decoding** — *Where* in the 4 GB address space the device lives.
3. **Data Transfer Semantics** — *How* the bytes are ordered (endianness) and *when* they are valid (alignment + timing).

### 2.2 The AMBA Bus Hierarchy

ARM SoCs typically use a layered bus structure rather than a single flat bus. The reason is **performance** and **power** — high-speed devices (processor, main memory) need fast but power-hungry buses, while low-bandwidth peripherals (UART, GPIO) tolerate slow, simple buses.

| Bus | Full Form | Use Case | Speed | Bus Masters |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AHB** | Advanced High-performance Bus | ARM core, on-chip SRAM, external memory controller, DMA, Ethernet | High (≥ 100 MHz typical) | Multi-master with arbiter |
| **ASB** | Advanced System Bus | Older ARM7/ARM9 systems, alternative to AHB | Medium | Multi-master |
| **APB** | Advanced Peripheral Bus | Low-bandwidth peripherals (UART, timers, GPIO, keypad) | Low | Single master (bridge from AHB/ASB) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bridge Concept:** An *AHB-to-APB bridge* converts one high-speed AHB transaction into multiple slower APB transactions. This allows peripherals to be designed simply without burdening the fast system bus.

### 2.3 Signal-Level View of an AHB Transaction

A single AHB read or write carries the following signals in parallel:

- **HADDR[31:0]** — 32-bit address.
- **HWDATA[31:0]** — write data from master to slave.
- **HRDATA[31:0]** — read data from slave to master.
- **HWRITE** — logic 1 for write, 0 for read.
- **HSIZE[2:0]** — transfer size: 8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit, etc.
- **HBURST[2:0]** — burst type: SINGLE, INCR, INCR4, INCR8, WRAP4, WRAP8.
- **HTRANS[1:0]** — transfer type: IDLE, BUSY, NONSEQ, SEQ.
- **HREADY** — high = slave ready, low = slave inserts wait states.
- **HRESP[1:0]** — OKAY, ERROR, RETRY, SPLIT.
- **HMASTLOCK** — locked transfer (for atomic read-modify-write).

### 2.4 Memory Map & Address Decoding

The 32-bit ARM address space is partitioned into regions by the system integrator. A typical memory map for an ARM7TDMI-based microcontroller (e.g., LPC2148) is shown below.

| Address Range | Size | Device | Bus |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0x0000 0000 – 0x0007 FFFF | 512 KB | On-chip Flash | AHB |
| 0x4000 0000 – 0x4000 1FFF | 8 KB | On-chip SRAM | AHB |
| 0x8000 0000 – 0x80FF FFFF | 16 MB | External Memory Bank 0 | AHB |
| 0xE000 0000 – 0xE000 00FF | — | AHB-to-APB Bridge Registers | AHB |
| 0xFFFF F000 – 0xFFFF FFFF | 4 KB | System Control (VIC) | AHB |

**Address Decoding** is the process of generating a *chip-select* (CS) signal for a memory/peripheral block from the upper address bits. The most common implementation uses a **3-to-8 line decoder** (74HC138) or a simple **AND-gate / NAND-gate combinational network**.

### 2.5 Endianness — Byte Ordering in a Word

Endianness defines how the four bytes of a 32-bit word are stored at successive byte addresses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big-Endian:** The *most significant byte* of a word is stored at the *lowest* address.
> **Little-Endian:** The *least significant byte* of a word is stored at the *lowest* address.

The ARM core is **bi-endian** — it can be configured (via the BIGEND pin / system control coprocessor) to operate in either mode. Most ARM7/ARM9 systems use **Little-Endian** as the default.

**Worked Example:** Store the 32-bit value `0x12345678` at base address `0x40000000`.

| Endian | Address 0x40000000 | 0x40000001 | 0x40000002 | 0x40000003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Little-Endian | 0x78 | 0x56 | 0x34 | 0x12 |
| Big-Endian | 0x12 | 0x34 | 0x56 | 0x78 |

### 2.6 Data Alignment

An access is **aligned** if the address is a multiple of the transfer size. Misaligned accesses are permitted on most ARM cores (with a small performance penalty) but **prohibited** on some Cortex-M variants.

| Transfer Size | Aligned Address Condition | Misaligned Example |
|---|---|---|
| 8-bit (byte) | Any address | 0x40000005 ✓ |
| 16-bit (halfword) | A[0] = 0 | 0x40000001 ✗ |
| 32-bit (word) | A[1:0] = 00 | 0x40000002 ✗ |

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations and rules are the most frequently tested items on the ARM Memory Interface. Master these.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of addressable bytes | $N = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = number of address lines |
| 2 | Memory capacity | $C = 2^{n} \times w$ | bits, where $w$ = data width in bits |
| 3 | Address lines from memory size | $n = \log_{2}\left(\dfrac{\text{size in bytes}}{1}\right)$ | $n$ = address lines |
| 4 | Word address to byte address | $\text{ByteAddr} = \text{WordAddr} \times 4$ | For word-addressable memory |
| 5 | Number of memory chips | $k = \dfrac{W_{\text{desired}}}{W_{\text{chip}}}$ | Bank-stacked width expansion |
| 6 | Address space size | $S = 2^{32}$ bytes $= 4 \text{ GB}$ | 32-bit ARM |
| 7 | Aligned halfword check | $A \bmod 2 = 0$ | $A$ = byte address |
| 8 | Aligned word check | $A \bmod 4 = 0$ | $A$ = byte address |
| 9 | Byte-select decoding | $\text{BE}_i = \overline{A_{k+i}}$ for little-endian | $i = 0,1,2,3$ |
| 10 | Maximum wait states (AMBA) | Spec-defined; no fixed formula | Bridge handles timing |

> **Note on Notation:** In the table above, the symbol $\vert$ is replaced by the typeset form $\mid$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ARM memory interface is the foundation of every device you use daily:

- **Smartphones (Cortex-A series):** LPDDR memory connected via AXI (the modern extension of AHB).
- **Microcontrollers (Cortex-M, LPC2148):** Flash + SRAM + peripherals all on the AHB/APB bus matrix.
- **Automotive ECUs (Cortex-R):** Tightly-coupled SRAM (TCM) on a dedicated port for deterministic interrupt latency.
- **IoT sensors (Cortex-M0+):** Single-bus designs using AHB-Lite for minimum silicon area.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Address Lines Required for a Given Memory Size

**Problem:** A 32 KB SRAM is to be interfaced to an ARM7 processor. How many address lines are required?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{Convert memory size to bytes} \\
\text{Size} &= 32 \text{ KB} = 32 \times 2^{10} \text{ bytes} = 32 \times 1024 \\
&= 32\,768 \text{ bytes} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad \text{Each byte needs a unique address, so} \\
2^{n} &= 32\,768 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad \text{Solve for } n \text{ by prime factorising} \\
32\,768 &= 2^{15} \quad (\text{since } 2^{10}=1024,\ 2^{15}=32 \times 2^{10}) \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad n &= 15 \text{ address lines (A0 to A14)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Connect A0 through A14 of the ARM address bus to the SRAM's A0–A14 pins. The remaining upper address lines (A15–A31) are used by the address decoder to generate the SRAM's chip-select.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Memory Map Calculation for Multiple Banks

**Problem:** Design the address decoder for an ARM7 system with the following map:

| Device | Size | Base Address |
|---|---|---|
| ROM1 | 1 MB | 0x0000 0000 |
| ROM2 | 1 MB | 0x0100 0000 |
| RAM | 512 KB | 0x4000 0000 |

Determine which upper address lines must be decoded.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{Find the binary of each base address} \\
\text{ROM1} &= 0\text{x}00000000 = 0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000_2 \\
\text{ROM2} &= 0\text{x}01000000 = 0000\;0001\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000_2 \\
\text{RAM} &= 0\text{x}40000000 = 0100\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000_2 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad \text{Identify the constant upper bits} \\
\text{ROM1 is enabled when } A_{31..20} &= 0000\;0000\;0000_2 \\
\text{ROM2 is enabled when } A_{31..20} &= 0000\;0000\;0001_2 \\
\text{RAM is enabled when } A_{31..20} &= 0100\;0000\;0000_2 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad \text{Derive the chip-select logic} \\
\overline{\text{CS\_ROM1}} &= \overline{\overline{A_{31}}\cdot\overline{A_{30}}\cdots\overline{A_{20}}} \\
\overline{\text{CS\_ROM2}} &= \overline{\overline{A_{31}}\cdot\overline{A_{30}}\cdots A_{20}} \\
\overline{\text{CS\_RAM}} &= A_{30}\cdot\overline{A_{29}}\cdots\overline{A_{20}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The 1 MB size means each block has $2^{20}$ bytes, so the lower 20 address bits (A0–A19) are *internal* to the block. Only the upper bits are needed for chip-select generation. This is called **partial address decoding**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Endianness Byte Placement

**Problem:** A 32-bit value `0xDEADBEEF` is stored at address `0x40000000` in little-endian mode. List the byte at every address.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{Identify the four bytes (MSB to LSB)} \\
0\text{x}DEADBEEF &= \underbrace{0\text{x}DE}_{\text{MSB}}\;\underbrace{0\text{x}AD}\;\underbrace{0\text{x}BE}\;\underbrace{0\text{x}EF}_{\text{LSB}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad \text{Apply little-endian rule (LSB at lowest address)} \\
\text{Addr } 0\text{x}40000000 &\rightarrow 0\text{x}EF \\
\text{Addr } 0\text{x}40000001 &\rightarrow 0\text{x}BE \\
\text{Addr } 0\text{x}40000002 &\rightarrow 0\text{x}AD \\
\text{Addr } 0\text{x}40000003 &\rightarrow 0\text{x}DE \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad \text{Verify by reading back as a word} \\
\text{Word at } 0\text{x}40000000 &= 0\text{x}DEADBEEF \;\;\checkmark \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Address Calculation of a Memory-Mapped Register

**Problem:** On a Cortex-M3 device, the GPIO Port A base address is `0x40010800`. The ODR (Output Data Register) offset is `0x0C`. Find the absolute address of Port A ODR.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Addr(ODR\_A)} &= \text{Base(GPIOA)} + \text{Offset(ODR)} \\
&= 0\text{x}40010800 + 0\text{x}0000000C \\
&= 0\text{x}4001080C \\[6pt]
\text{To set bit 5 of ODR\_A:} \quad & \\
\text{Value} &= (1 \ll 5) = 0\text{x}00000020 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of AMBA Buses

| Property | AHB | ASB | APB |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bus width | 32 / 64 / 128 bits | 32 bits | 8 / 16 / 32 bits |
| Pipelined | Yes | No | No |
| Burst transfers | Yes (INCR, WRAP) | No | No |
| Multiple masters | Yes (with arbiter) | Yes | No (single master) |
| Typical use | Processor, RAM, DMA | Older ARM7 designs | Peripherals |
| Power consumption | High | Medium | Low |
| Complexity of slave | High | Medium | Low |
| Typical clock | 50–200 MHz | 25–100 MHz | ≤ 50 MHz |
| Bridging requirement | Bridges to APB | Bridges to APB | Slave only |

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Wait State Insertion for Slow Memory

**Problem:** An ARM7 with a 50 MHz AHB bus (20 ns clock) is connected to a ROM with 80 ns access time. How many wait states are required?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{Compute clock period} \\
T_{\text{clk}} &= \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \text{ ns} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad \text{Determine access time requirement} \\
T_{\text{access}} &= 80 \text{ ns} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad \text{Number of clock cycles needed} \\
N_{\text{cycles}} &= \left\lceil \frac{T_{\text{access}}}{T_{\text{clk}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{80}{20} \right\rceil = 4 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad \text{Wait states} \\
W &= N_{\text{cycles}} - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3 \text{ wait states} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why subtract 1?** The first cycle always performs the address phase. The remaining $W$ cycles stretch the data phase by holding **HREADY = LOW**. The slave returns HREADY = HIGH on the final cycle to terminate the transfer.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 6 — Byte-Enable Decoding for a 32-Bit Memory

**Problem:** Derive the logic expressions for the four byte-select signals $\overline{\text{BE}_0}$ to $\overline{\text{BE}_3}$ that connect a 32-bit ARM to four independent 8-bit memory banks.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For Little-Endian (default ARM):} \\[4pt]
\overline{\text{BE}_0} &= \overline{\overline{A_1} \cdot \overline{A_0}} \quad \text{(select bytes 0 — D[7:0])} \\
\overline{\text{BE}_1} &= \overline{\overline{A_1} \cdot A_0} \quad \text{(select bytes 1 — D[15:8])} \\
\overline{\text{BE}_2} &= \overline{A_1 \cdot \overline{A_0}} \quad \text{(select bytes 2 — D[23:16])} \\
\overline{\text{BE}_3} &= \overline{A_1 \cdot A_0} \quad \text{(select bytes 3 — D[31:24])} \\[6pt]
\text{Verification for an aligned word at } A = 0\text{x}40000000 \;(A_1 A_0 = 00): \\
\overline{\text{BE}_0} &= 0 \quad \overline{\text{BE}_1} = 0 \quad \overline{\text{BE}_2} = 0 \quad \overline{\text{BE}_3} = 0 \\
&\Rightarrow \text{All 4 bytes selected — correct word access} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.8 KTU High-Yield Numerical Practice Set

> [!NOTE]
> Attempt these numericals. They mirror the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) style.

| Q# | Question | Answer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Address lines for 4 GB memory | $n = 32$ |
| 2 | Address lines for 64 KB | $n = 16$ |
| 3 | Bytes addressable with 24 lines | $N = 2^{24} = 16 \text{ MB}$ |
| 4 | Wait states if $T_{\text{acc}} = 100$ ns, $f = 40$ MHz | $W = 100\,\text{ns} \div 25\,\text{ns} - 1 = 3$ |
| 5 | Word at `0x1000` if memory contains `78 56 34 12` in LE | `0x12345678` |
| 6 | Bytes at `0x2000–0x2003` storing `0xCAFEBABE` in BE | `CA FE BE BA` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AMBA Bus Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The diagram below illustrates the typical AMBA bus matrix used in ARM-based SoCs. Every block is mapped to a function, and the arrows represent the bus-master-to-slave data paths.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    armCore[ARM Processor Core]
    dmaCtrl[DMA Controller]
    arbiter[AHB Arbiter]
    extMemCtl[External Memory Controller]
    onChipRam[On-Chip SRAM]
    ahbApbBridge[AHB to APB Bridge]
    romBank[Boot ROM]
    uart1[UART Peripherals]
    timer1[Timers and PWM]
    gpio1[GPIO Controller]
    apbBus[APB Peripheral Bus]

    armCore --> arbiter
    dmaCtrl --> arbiter
    arbiter --> extMemCtl
    arbiter --> onChipRam
    arbiter --> ahbApbBridge
    extMemCtl --> romBank
    ahbApbBridge --> apbBus
    apbBus --> uart1
    apbBus --> timer1
    apbBus --> gpio1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The ARM core and DMA controller are both potential AHB masters. They both submit requests to the AHB arbiter, which grants the bus to one master at a time. The AHB slaves are the external memory controller (which in turn drives off-chip SDRAM/Flash), the on-chip SRAM, and the AHB-to-APB bridge. The bridge translates each AHB transaction into APB transactions, fanning them out to the low-bandwidth peripherals.

---

## 4.2 Address Decoder — Combinational Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    addrBus[32 bit Address Bus A31..A0]
    decoder1[3 to 8 Line Decoder]
    cs0[Chip Select CS0 ROM1]
    cs1[Chip Select CS1 ROM2]
    cs2[Chip Select CS2 RAM]
    cs3[Chip Select CS3 APB Bridge]
    cs4[Unused CS Lines]
    cs5[Unused CS Lines]
    cs6[Unused CS Lines]
    cs7[Chip Select CS7 External]

    addrBus --> decoder1
    decoder1 --> cs0
    decoder1 --> cs1
    decoder1 --> cs2
    decoder1 --> cs3
    decoder1 --> cs4
    decoder1 --> cs5
    decoder1 --> cs6
    decoder1 --> cs7
```

> **Engineering Note:** The 3-to-8 decoder takes the three most-significant address lines (A31, A30, A29) and asserts one of eight outputs LOW (active low). The remaining 29 address lines are routed directly to the memory devices for *intra-bank* addressing.

---

## 4.3 AHB Write Transaction — State-Flow Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> NonSeq: HTRANS equals NONSEQ and HREADY high
    NonSeq --> Wait: HREADY low from slave
    Wait --> Wait: HREADY still low
    Wait --> TransferDone: HREADY high
    TransferDone --> Idle: Transfer complete
    NonSeq --> TransferDone: HREADY high first cycle
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The AHB transfer begins in **Idle** state. The master asserts **HTRANS = NONSEQ** (non-sequential — first cycle of a burst or a single transfer). If the slave is slow, it holds **HREADY LOW** to insert wait states. When the slave is ready, **HREADY goes HIGH** and the data phase completes.

---

## 4.4 Memory Map Visualization (LPC2148 Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    low[0x0000 0000]
    flash[Flash 512 KB]
    blank1[Reserved]
    ram[SRAM 32 KB]
    blank2[Reserved]
    ahbApbBridge[AHB to APB Bridge]
    vic[VIC 4 KB]
    blank3[Reserved]
    extMem[External Memory 16 MB]
    blank4[Reserved]
    high[0xFFFF FFFF]

    low --> flash
    flash --> blank1
    blank1 --> ram
    ram --> blank2
    blank2 --> ahbApbBridge
    ahbApbBridge --> vic
    vic --> blank3
    blank3 --> extMem
    extMem --> blank4
    blank4 --> high
```

> **Important:** *Reserved* regions must never be accessed — they return a *Prefetch Abort* (for execute) or *Data Abort* (for read/write) exception on the ARM core.

---

## 4.5 Byte-Enable / Byte-Select Architecture (32-bit Memory, 4 × 8-bit Chips)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    armWord[ARM 32 bit Data Bus D31..D0]
    beDecoder[Byte Enable Decoder]
    bank0[8 bit Memory Bank 0]
    bank1[8 bit Memory Bank 1]
    bank2[8 bit Memory Bank 2]
    bank3[8 bit Memory Bank 3]

    armWord --> bank0
    armWord --> bank1
    armWord --> bank2
    armWord --> bank3
    beDecoder --> bank0
    beDecoder --> bank1
    beDecoder --> bank2
    beDecoder --> bank3
```

> **Engineering Insight:** This is **bank-stacked width expansion**. Four 8-bit × N memory chips are combined to form one 32-bit-wide memory. The byte-enable signals $\overline{\text{BE}_0}$ to $\overline{\text{BE}_3}$ ensure that only the relevant byte(s) are written, allowing efficient byte, halfword, and word transfers on the same physical memory.

---

## 4.6 Endianness Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    addrLE[Address 0x00 LE] --> byte0LE[0x78 LSB]
    addrLE2[Address 0x01] --> byte1LE[0x56]
    addrLE3[Address 0x02] --> byte2LE[0x34]
    addrLE4[Address 0x03 MSB] --> byte3LE[0x12 MSB]

    addrBE[Address 0x00 BE] --> byte0BE[0x12 MSB]
    addrBE2[Address 0x01] --> byte1BE[0x34]
    addrBE3[Address 0x02] --> byte2BE[0x56]
    addrBE4[Address 0x03 LSB] --> byte3BE[0x78 LSB]
```

> **Mnemonic for Exam:** *Little-endian = little end first (LSB at low address). Big-endian = big end first (MSB at low address).*

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Explain the concept of memory-mapped I/O in ARM systems with a suitable example. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> In ARM systems, **memory-mapped I/O** is an addressing scheme in which peripheral control and data registers are assigned unique addresses within the same 4 GB address space as program memory. The ARM core accesses these peripherals using the same load (`LDR`) and store (`STR`) instructions used for memory, eliminating the need for separate I/O instructions (unlike x86's IN/OUT).
>
> **Example:** On the LPC2148 microcontroller, the **UART0 Transmit Holding Register (THR)** is mapped at address `0xE0000000`. Writing a byte to this address causes the UART hardware to transmit that byte serially.
>
> $$ \text{MOV } R0, \;\#0\text{x}E0000000 $$
> $$ \text{MOV } R1, \;\#'\text{A}' $$
> $$ \text{STRB } R1, \;[R0] \quad ;\text{transmit character 'A' via UART0} $$

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Differentiate between little-endian and big-endian byte ordering. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Little-Endian | Big-Endian |
|---|---|---|
| Byte at lowest address | **Least Significant Byte (LSB)** | **Most Significant Byte (MSB)** |
| Used by | Most ARM7 / ARM9 / Cortex-M systems | Network byte order, some older Motorola / PowerPC |
| Example: storing `0x12345678` at `0x40000000` | `0x78` at `0x40000000`, `0x12` at `0x40000003` | `0x12` at `0x40000000`, `0x78` at `0x40000003` |
| Arithmetic convenience | Easy to add/subtract multi-byte integers | Easy to check sign by reading first byte |
| Human readability | Less intuitive (read right-to-left) | Matches human writing convention |

> ARM is **bi-endian** — configurable via the `BIGEND` external pin (ARM7/9) or via system control registers (Cortex series).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes on Memory Interface Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract 1 for wait states.** Many students write "100 ns / 20 ns = 5 cycles" and call it wait states. The correct answer is **4 wait states** because the first cycle is the address phase.
> 2. **Confusing byte-select polarity.** ARM uses **active-low** $\overline{\text{BE}}$ signals. Drawing them active-high in a circuit diagram loses 2 marks immediately.
> 3. **Wrong endianness picture.** Drawing big-endian with LSB at the low address is the most common error — examiners will deduct full marks for this conceptual mistake.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation of the decoder logic.** You must show the Boolean expression, not just the binary table.
> 5. **Using `||` for absolute value inside markdown tables.** This corrupts the table — examiners viewing the digital copy will mark the row as missing.

---

### Question A — Full 14-Mark Question

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the AMBA bus architecture in ARM-based systems. With a neat block diagram, describe the role of the AHB, ASB, and APB buses. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An ARM7TDMI processor operates at 50 MHz and is connected to a ROM with an access time of 120 ns and a 32 KB SRAM with an access time of 40 ns. Determine the number of wait states required for each memory. Comment on the practical implications. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) AMBA Bus Architecture:**

The **Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)** is ARM's open-standard on-chip bus specification. It defines three principal bus families:

1. **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus):** Used for high-clock, high-bandwidth modules such as the ARM core, on-chip SRAM, external memory controller, and DMA. Supports burst transfers, split transactions, and multiple bus masters with an arbiter.
2. **ASB (Advanced System Bus):** An older alternative to AHB used in legacy ARM7 designs; similar role but no pipelining.
3. **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus):** A simple, low-power bus for low-bandwidth peripherals (UART, timers, GPIO, watchdog). Single master (the AHB-to-APB bridge), no burst support.

**Interconnection:** A typical SoC uses a layered approach — the ARM core and high-speed blocks sit on the AHB; the AHB is connected to APB peripherals through an **AHB-to-APB bridge**. This decouples the peripheral design complexity from the high-speed bus.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    armCore2[ARM Core]
    ahb1[AHB]
    bridge2[AHB to APB Bridge]
    extMem2[External Memory Controller]
    apb1[APB]
    uart2[UART]
    timer2[Timer]
    gpio2[GPIO]

    armCore2 --> ahb1
    ahb1 --> extMem2
    ahb1 --> bridge2
    bridge2 --> apb1
    apb1 --> uart2
    apb1 --> timer2
    apb1 --> gpio2
```

**(b) Wait State Calculation:**

**For ROM (120 ns access time):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{clk}} &= \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \text{ ns} \\[6pt]
N_{\text{cycles}} &= \left\lceil \frac{T_{\text{access}}}{T_{\text{clk}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{120}{20} \right\rceil = 6 \\[6pt]
W_{\text{ROM}} &= N_{\text{cycles}} - 1 = 5 \text{ wait states} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**For 32 KB SRAM (40 ns access time):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{cycles}} &= \left\lceil \frac{40}{20} \right\rceil = 2 \\[6pt]
W_{\text{SRAM}} &= 1 \text{ wait state} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical Implications:**

- A boot ROM requiring **5 wait states** significantly slows down startup. In practice, designers place the boot vector (reset vector) in a small zero-wait-state SRAM or in tightly-coupled memory.
- The SRAM needs only **1 wait state**, which is acceptable for most embedded workloads.
- The AHB allows the slave to insert wait states by holding **HREADY LOW**, providing automatic slow-memory handling.
- For high-performance systems, **cache** or **burst-mode ROM** (e.g., page-mode Flash) is used to amortise the wait-state penalty across multiple transfers.

---

### Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Question

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the address decoding mechanism used in ARM memory interfacing. Design an address decoder for a system with the following memory map: ROM1 (1 MB at `0x00000000`), ROM2 (1 MB at `0x01000000`), RAM (512 KB at `0x40000000`). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain little-endian and big-endian byte ordering. A 32-bit word `0x89ABCDEF` is stored at address `0x20000000`. Show the byte arrangement for both endian modes. Discuss the advantages of little-endian format for ARM processors. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Address Decoding:**

**Definition:** Address decoding is the process of generating a unique *chip-select* (CS) signal for each memory/peripheral block from the upper address bits of the ARM bus. The lower address bits are routed to the device for *internal* addressing.

**Address Decoder Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    addrA[Address A31..A0]
    dec1[Decoder Logic]
    csrom1[CS ROM1]
    csrom2[CS ROM2]
    csram[CS RAM]

    addrA --> dec1
    dec1 --> csrom1
    dec1 --> csrom2
    dec1 --> csram
```

**Step-by-Step Decoder Logic Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block sizes in binary:} \\
1 \text{ MB} &= 2^{20} \text{ bytes} \quad \Rightarrow \text{A0--A19 are internal} \\
512 \text{ KB} &= 2^{19} \text{ bytes} \quad \Rightarrow \text{A0--A18 are internal} \\[6pt]
\text{Upper bits of each base address:} \\
\text{ROM1} \;0\text{x}00000000 &\Rightarrow A_{31..20} = 0000\;0000\;0000_2 \\
\text{ROM2} \;0\text{x}01000000 &\Rightarrow A_{31..20} = 0000\;0000\;0001_2 \\
\text{RAM} \;0\text{x}40000000 &\Rightarrow A_{31..20} = 0100\;0000\;0000_2 \\[6pt]
\text{Boolean equations (active-low CS):} \\
\overline{\text{CS\_ROM1}} &= \overline{\overline{A_{31}}\cdot\overline{A_{30}}\cdots\overline{A_{20}}} \\
\overline{\text{CS\_ROM2}} &= \overline{\overline{A_{31}}\cdot\overline{A_{30}}\cdots A_{20}} \\
\overline{\text{CS\_RAM}} &= \overline{A_{30}\cdot\overline{A_{29}}\cdots\overline{A_{20}}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The decoder can be implemented with a **74HC138 (3-to-8 line decoder)** by feeding it `A_{31}, A_{30}, A_{29}` and using the remaining upper bits as additional enable inputs (e.g., $\overline{E_1}, \overline{E_2}, E_3$).

**(b) Endianness — Byte Storage of `0x89ABCDEF`:**

| Address | Little-Endian Byte | Big-Endian Byte |
|---|---|---|
| `0x20000000` | `0xEF` (LSB) | `0x89` (MSB) |
| `0x20000001` | `0xCD` | `0xAB` |
| `0x20000002` | `0xAB` | `0xCD` |
| `0x20000003` | `0x89` (MSB) | `0xEF` (LSB) |

**Little-Endian Word Reconstruction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Word at } 0\text{x}20000000 &= 0\text{x}89 \cdot 2^{24} + 0\text{x}AB \cdot 2^{16} + 0\text{x}CD \cdot 2^{8} + 0\text{x}EF \\
&= 0\text{x}89ABCDEF \;\;\checkmark \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Advantages of Little-Endian for ARM Processors:**

1. **Efficient byte/word arithmetic:** When incrementing a multi-byte integer stored in memory, the LSB is at the lowest address. The CPU can perform the carry propagation from low to high addresses naturally without reversing byte order.
2. **Byte-addressable memory access:** Reading a 32-bit word and a single byte at the same address yields related data; the byte is simply the low-order part of the word.
3. **Type-punning friendly:** Casting between `uint32_t *` and `uint8_t *` does not require byte-swapping in the most common case.
4. **ARM standardisation:** Most ARM compilers (ARM CC, GCC), debuggers, and RTOS contexts assume little-endian by default, ensuring cross-tool compatibility.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — The ARM Memory Interface**

- **AMBA is the on-chip bus standard of ARM.** It defines AHB (fast, pipelined, burst), ASB (older alternative), and APB (slow peripherals).
- **The 32-bit ARM address space is 4 GB** ($2^{32}$ bytes), fully memory-mapped.
- **Address decoding** generates chip-select signals from upper address lines. Use partial decoding (only the bits above the block size).
- **Memory map** assigns fixed address ranges to ROM, RAM, and peripherals. *Reserved* regions must not be accessed.
- **Endians:** Little-endian = LSB at low address (default for ARM). Big-endian = MSB at low address. ARM is bi-endian.
- **Alignment:** A word access needs the address to be a multiple of 4. A halfword needs the address to be a multiple of 2. Misaligned access is allowed on most cores but may incur a 1-cycle penalty.
- **AHB signals to remember:** HADDR, HWDATA, HRDATA, HWRITE, HSIZE, HBURST, HTRANS, HREADY, HRESP, HMASTLOCK.
- **Wait states:** $W = \lceil T_{\text{access}} / T_{\text{clk}} \rceil - 1$. HREADY held LOW inserts the wait.
- **Bank-stacked width expansion:** Combine multiple 8-bit chips to form a 32-bit-wide memory; use byte-enable signals $\overline{\text{BE}_0..3}$ for byte-level access.
- **Bridge concept:** AHB-to-APB bridge decouples slow peripherals from the high-speed bus.
- **Number of address lines:** $n = \log_2(\text{size in bytes})$.
- **Reserved address behavior:** Accessing a reserved address triggers a *Prefetch Abort* (instruction fetch) or *Data Abort* (data access) exception.
- **Default reset vector:** `0x00000000` on most ARM systems (boot ROM); the ARM core begins execution at the address stored at this location.
- **Endianness on ARM7/9:** controlled by external pin `BIGEND`. On Cortex-M, fixed little-endian (with optional big-endian instruction fetch on Cortex-M3/M4).
- **Cache coherence:** Modifying memory through DMA on a cached system requires *cache clean* (write-back to RAM) and *cache invalidate* (reload from RAM) operations.

> **Mnemonic for Exam Day:** *"AHB is the highway, APB is the side-road, ASB is the old highway, and the bridge is the on-ramp."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** is an open-standard, on-chip interconnect specification originally developed by **ARM Holdings** in **1996**. It defines the strategy for connecting and managing functional blocks (processors, memory controllers, peripherals, and custom IP cores) within a **System-on-Chip (SoC)**. It is *not* a physical wire specification but a *protocol and bus architecture* standard governing data transfer, address routing, arbitration, and handshaking between different IP blocks on a single die.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PBEVT504 – Embedded System Design**, AMBA is treated as the **de-facto industry standard for SoC bus architecture**, used in nearly all ARM-based embedded platforms, including microcontrollers from ST (STM32), NXP (LPC series), Nordic, and Samsung SoCs.

The AMBA specification has evolved through several versions, introducing progressively higher performance and lower power buses. The major bus families within AMBA are:

| Bus | Full Form | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| **ASB** | Advanced System Bus | Older generation, used in early AMBA 2.0 designs |
| **AHB** | Advanced High-performance Bus | High clock frequency, high-bandwidth modules (CPU, DMA, memory) |
| **AHB-Lite** | AHB Lightweight variant | Single-master, simplified AHB |
| **APB** | Advanced Peripheral Bus | Low-power, low-bandwidth peripherals (UART, timers, GPIO) |
| **AXI / AXI4 / AXI4-Lite / AXI4-Stream** | Advanced eXtensible Interface | High-performance, multi-master, separate read/write channels |
| **ACE / ACE-Lite** | AXI Coherency Extensions | Multi-core cache coherency |
| **CHI** | Coherent Hub Interface | Networks-on-Chip (NoC), many-core coherent systems |
| **ATB** | Advanced Trace Bus | Real-time debug tracing |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The City Highway System"**
>
> Think of a **SoC chip** as a **bustling city**. The buildings are the *IP blocks* (CPU, RAM, UART, ADC). The roads connecting them are the **buses**.
> - **AHB** is the **multi-lane expressway** — fast, pipelined, used by powerful vehicles (CPU, DMA, RAM).
> - **APB** is the **small residential lane** — slower, single-lane, only used by small vehicles (peripherals like timers and keypads).
> - **AXI** is the **modern, grade-separated superhighway** with separate lanes for incoming and outgoing traffic, allowing many cars to flow simultaneously without colliding.
> - **The Bus Matrix / Interconnect** is the **traffic roundabout** (or multi-level interchange) that decides which vehicle goes where without crashing.
>
> AMBA is the **Highway Engineering Manual** that defines lane widths, traffic rules, toll-booth handshakes, and intersection design for the entire city.

The key insight for an embedded systems student is: **AMBA decouples IP design from interconnect design**. A vendor (e.g., a third-party ADC IP company) can design an APB-compliant ADC, and it will plug-and-play with any ARM-based SoC that exposes an APB bus. This modularity is what makes SoC design tractable.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key AMBA Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **AHB data bus width:** typically **32 bits** or **64 bits** (extendable up to **128 / 256 bits** in AHB5).
> - **AHB typical operating frequency:** **50 MHz – 200 MHz** (older implementations) to **>1 GHz** in modern SoCs.
> - **APB data bus width:** **8, 16, or 32 bits** depending on peripheral.
> - **APB clock:** typically derived from AHB via a **1:1** (APB1) or **1:2 / 1:4** prescaler (e.g., in STM32, APB1 is half the AHB clock).
> - **AXI addressing:** up to **64-bit** address space.
> - **Burst transfer length (AXI):** up to **256 beats** (in AXI4, fixed 16 in AXI3).
> - **Outstanding transactions (AXI):** up to **16** read + 16 write per master interface.

## 1.4 Why AMBA is Needed — The SoC Integration Problem

Modern System-on-Chip designs integrate:
- One or more **CPU cores** (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4, Cortex-A53)
- **Memory controllers** (SRAM, SDRAM, Flash)
- **DMA controllers**
- **Dozens of peripherals** (UART, SPI, I²C, ADC, timers, GPIO)
- **Custom hardware accelerators** (cryptographic engines, DSP blocks)

Connecting all these blocks **point-to-point** would require an unmanageable number of wires. AMBA provides a **standardized, modular, scalable interconnect framework** that:
1. Reduces **pin count and wire congestion**.
2. Allows **IP reuse** (write once, plug anywhere).
3. Provides a **standard verification methodology**.
4. Enables **clock-domain crossing** between fast and slow modules.
5. Supports **test and debug infrastructure** (e.g., JTAG, Trace).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of AMBA

A typical AMBA-based SoC follows a **two-tier (or three-tier) hierarchical bus structure**:

1. **System Bus (AHB or AXI)** — high-speed backbone connecting processor, memory, and DMA.
2. **Peripheral Bus (APB)** — low-speed branch connecting simple peripherals.
3. **Bridge** — translates transactions between AHB/AXI and APB (e.g., **AHB-to-APB Bridge**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Principle:** High-bandwidth masters (CPU, DMA) live on the system bus. Low-bandwidth slaves (UART, GPIO) live on the peripheral bus. A **bridge** acts as a slave on the system bus and a master on the peripheral bus, throttling down clock speed and simplifying handshakes for APB peripherals.

## 2.2 AHB — Advanced High-performance Bus

### 2.2.1 Features
- **Pipelined** operation with overlapping address and data phases.
- **Burst transfers** (incrementing, wrapping, INCR4/8/16).
- **Split transactions** (retry on busy slave, freeing the bus for other masters).
- **Multiple masters** with **centralized arbitration** (arbiter + multiplexer).
- **Single-edge clock** protocol (sampled on rising edge).
- **32–128 bit** data bus, **32-bit** address bus.
- Supports **byte, half-word, and word** transfers with **byte strobes (HSELx / BE[3:0])**.

### 2.2.2 AHB Signals (Core Set)

| Signal | Direction | Width | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `HCLK` | Input to all | 1 | Bus clock |
| `HRESETn` | Input to all | 1 | Active-low reset |
| `HADDR[31:0]` | Master → Slave | 32 | Address |
| `HTRANS[1:0]` | Master → Slave | 2 | Transfer type (IDLE, BUSY, NONSEQ, SEQ) |
| `HWRITE` | Master → Slave | 1 | Read/Write direction |
| `HSIZE[2:0]` | Master → Slave | 3 | Transfer size (8, 16, 32, 64, 128 bits) |
| `HBURST[2:0]` | Master → Slave | 3 | Burst type (SINGLE, INCR, WRAP4/8/16, INCR4/8/16) |
| `HPROT[3:0]` | Master → Slave | 4 | Protection / privilege info |
| `HWDATA[31:0]` | Master → Slave | 32 | Write data |
| `HRDATA[31:0]` | Slave → Master | 32 | Read data |
| `HREADY` | Slave → Bus | 1 | Transfer complete (insert wait states if LOW) |
| `HRESP[1:0]` | Slave → Bus | 2 | Response (OKAY, ERROR, RETRY, SPLIT) |
| `HSELx` | Decoder → Slave | 1 per slave | Slave select (chip select) |
| `HBUSREQx` | Master → Arbiter | 1 per master | Bus request |
| `HLOCKx` | Master → Arbiter | 1 per master | Locked transfer |
| `HGRANTx` | Arbiter → Master | 1 per master | Bus grant |
| `HMASTER[3:0]` | Arbiter → Slaves | 4 | Indicates current master (for SPLIT) |
| `HSPLITx[15:0]` | Slaves → Arbiter | 16 | Split completion indication |

### 2.2.3 AHB Transfer Phases

Every AHB transfer has two phases:
- **Address Phase** (one HCLK cycle): master drives `HADDR`, `HTRANS`, `HWRITE`, `HSIZE`, `HBURST`, `HPROT`.
- **Data Phase** (one or more HCLK cycles): data is transferred. If `HREADY` is LOW, the data phase is extended (wait state).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipelining in AHB:** While the data phase of the current transfer is happening, the address phase of the *next* transfer can be issued. This pipeline is what gives AHB its high throughput.

### 2.2.4 AHB Burst Types

The 3-bit `HBURST[2:0]` signal encodes:

| HBURST[2:0] | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `000` | SINGLE | Single, non-burst transfer |
| `001` | INCR | Undefined-length incrementing burst |
| `010` | WRAP4 | 4-beat wrapping burst |
| `011` | INCR4 | 4-beat incrementing burst |
| `100` | WRAP8 | 8-beat wrapping burst |
| `101` | INCR8 | 8-beat incrementing burst |
| `110` | WRAP16 | 16-beat wrapping burst |
| `111` | INCR16 | 16-beat incrementing burst |

**Burst address calculation rule (KTU High-Yield):**

For a wrapping burst of length $N$ beats starting at address $A$ with size $S$ bytes:
$$A_k = A + (k \mod N) \times S \quad \text{where } k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

The wrap boundary is aligned to $N \times S$ bytes.

## 2.3 APB — Advanced Peripheral Bus

### 2.3.1 Features
- **Unpipelined**, **non-burst**, **low complexity** protocol.
- **Single clock edge** (typically rising edge).
- **No wait states** in the standard protocol — peripherals that need more time hold `PREADY` LOW.
- Used for **low-bandwidth, low-power** peripherals.
- Connects to the system bus via an **AHB-to-APB Bridge**.

### 2.3.2 APB Signals

| Signal | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `PCLK` | Input | APB clock |
| `PRESETn` | Input | Active-low reset |
| `PADDR[31:0]` | Bridge → Slave | Peripheral address |
| `PSELx` | Bridge → Slave | Slave select |
| `PENABLE` | Bridge → Slave | Enable (asserted in ACCESS phase) |
| `PWRITE` | Bridge → Slave | Write enable (HIGH = write) |
| `PWDATA[31:0]` | Bridge → Slave | Write data |
| `PRDATA[31:0]` | Slave → Bridge | Read data |
| `PREADY` | Slave → Bridge | Ready (LOW = wait, HIGH = complete) |
| `PSLVERR` | Slave → Bridge | Error response (AMBA 3 APB) |

### 2.3.3 APB State Machine

APB has **three states**:
1. **IDLE** — bus is idle (`PSEL = 0`, `PENABLE = 0`).
2. **SETUP** — transition state lasting 1 PCLK cycle (`PSEL = 1`, `PENABLE = 0`).
3. **ACCESS** — data transfer state (`PSEL = 1`, `PENABLE = 1`). If `PREADY = 0`, this state is extended.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why APB uses 2-cycle transfers (SETUP + ACCESS):**
> The SETUP phase allows address and control signals to settle before the ACCESS phase. This is the price paid for the **unpipelined, simple** design. It makes peripheral integration trivial.

## 2.4 AXI — Advanced eXtensible Interface

AXI is the **modern high-performance** bus of AMBA 3/4. Its key innovations over AHB:

| Feature | AHB | AXI |
|---|---|---|
| Address/separate write channels | No (shared) | **Yes (5 independent channels)** |
| Out-of-order completion | No | **Yes** (outstanding transactions) |
| Burst length | up to 16 | up to 256 (AXI4) |
| Data width | up to 128 bits | up to **1024 bits** (AXI4) |
| Write data interleaving | No | **Yes** (write interleaving) |
| Quality of Service (QoS) | No | **Yes (4-bit QoS field)** |
| Region signaling | No | **Yes (4-bit AxREGION)** |
| User-defined signaling | No | **Yes (AxUSER, xUSER)** |
| Cache coherency | No | Yes (via ACE/CHI extensions) |

### 2.4.1 AXI Channels (5 Independent Channels)

1. **Write Address (AW)** — `AWADDR`, `AWVALID`, `AWREADY`, `AWLEN`, `AWSIZE`, `AWBURST`, `AWPROT`, `AWCACHE`, `AWQOS`, `AWREGION`, `AWLOCK`, `AWID`, `AWUSER`.
2. **Write Data (W)** — `WDATA`, `WSTRB`, `WLAST`, `WVALID`, `WREADY`, `WUSER`.
3. **Write Response (B)** — `BID`, `BRESP`, `BVALID`, `BREADY`, `BUSER`.
4. **Read Address (AR)** — `ARADDR`, `ARVALID`, `ARREADY`, `ARLEN`, `ARSIZE`, `ARBURST`, `ARPROT`, `ARCACHE`, `ARQOS`, `ARREGION`, `ARLOCK`, `ARID`, `ARUSER`.
5. **Read Data (R)** — `RDATA`, `RLAST`, `RRESP`, `RVALID`, `RREADY`, `RID`, `RUSER`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Read Address + Read Data" and "Write Address + Write Data + Write Response" separation is the core AXI innovation.** A master can issue a read address, then immediately issue a write, with the read data returning later on the R channel. This is **outstanding transaction** support.

### 2.4.2 Handshake Rules (VALID/READY)

Every channel uses a **VALID + READY handshake**:
- Source asserts `VALID = 1` when data/control is driven.
- Destination asserts `READY = 1` when it can accept.
- Transfer occurs on the rising edge when **both VALID and READY are sampled HIGH**.
- Neither signal may be asserted until the other is — except `VALID` may be asserted *before* `READY` (but not after).

### 2.4.3 AXI Burst Types

| `AxBURST[1:0]` | Type |
|---|---|
| `00` | FIXED (FIFO-style) |
| `01` | INCR (incrementing) |
| `10` | WRAP (wrapping, length 2, 4, 8, 16) |
| `11` | Reserved |

### 2.4.4 AXI Burst Address Calculation

For an **INCR** burst of `AxLEN + 1` beats, starting at `AxADDR`, with `AxSIZE = 2^n` bytes, the address of the $k$-th beat is:

$$A_k = A_{base} + k \cdot 2^{n}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, \text{AxLEN}$$

The `AxLEN` field holds `N - 1`, so the burst length is `AxLEN + 1` (in AXI4, AxLEN is **3:0** allowing bursts of **1, 2, 4, 8, 16** only; the wider range was in AXI3).

> [!WARNING]
> **Crossing the 4 KB boundary** in AXI: a burst must **not cross a 4 KB aligned boundary**. This is a mandatory rule because slaves use the upper address bits for slave-select decode. Violating this leads to **unpredictable behavior** in real hardware.

## 2.5 AHB-Lite and APB-Lite

- **AHB-Lite**: Single-master version of AHB. Removes the arbiter, `HBUSREQx`, `HGRANTx`, `HMASTER`, `HSPLITx`, `HLOCKx`. Used when only one master (typically the CPU) is on the AHB segment — e.g., in small Cortex-M0 systems.
- **APB-Lite** (APB3): Adds `PREADY` and `PSLVERR` to the original APB2.

## 2.6 Bus Matrix (AHB Interconnect)

In modern AHB systems with multiple masters and slaves, a **bus matrix** replaces the simple arbiter+multiplexer combination. The bus matrix:
- Allows **multiple simultaneous transfers** (e.g., master A reads RAM while master B writes to a peripheral).
- Uses **independent arbitration per slave port**.
- Eliminates the SPLIT/RETRY path; instead, masters may receive `HREADY` waits while the matrix arbitrates.

## 2.7 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **AHB address phase** | 1 HCLK cycle | Address + control driven |
| **AHB data phase** | $\geq 1$ HCLK cycle | Extended if HREADY = 0 |
| **AHB burst length** | 4, 8, 16 (fixed) or undefined INCR | Per `HBURST[2:0]` |
| **APB transfer duration** | 2 PCLK cycles minimum (SETUP + ACCESS) | Extended if PREADY = 0 |
| **AHB bandwidth** | $B_{AHB} = f_{HCLK} \times W_{data}$ | bytes/sec; $W_{data} \in \{32, 64, 128\}$ bits |
| **APB bandwidth** | $B_{APB} = \dfrac{f_{PCLK} \times W_{data}}{2}$ | Minimum 2-cycle access; $W_{data} \in \{8, 16, 32\}$ |
| **AXI beat count** | $N_{beats} = \text{AxLEN} + 1$ | AXI4: AxLEN ∈ {0..15} |
| **AXI beat $k$ address** | $A_k = A_{base} + k \cdot 2^{\text{AxSIZE}}$ | For INCR bursts |
| **4 KB boundary rule** | $\lfloor A_{base} / 4096 \rfloor = \lfloor A_{last} / 4096 \rfloor$ | Mandatory for AXI bursts |
| **AHB wrap address** | $A_k = A + (k \mod N) \times S$ | $N$ = burst length, $S$ = transfer size |
| **Bus utilization** | $U = \dfrac{T_{data}}{T_{address} + T_{data}}$ | AHB pipelined ⇒ $T_{address}$ overlapped |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **STM32 microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M)**: use **AHB** for CPU/DMA/SRAM, **APB1** (low-speed) and **APB2** (high-speed) for peripherals. The **APB prescaler** in `RCC` register controls the APB clock.
- **Samsung Exynos, Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple M-series**: all use **AXI/CHI** for multi-core coherency.
- **Xilinx Zynq-7000**: has **AXI4** between ARM Cortex-A9 cores and PL (FPGA) fabric — the cornerstone of the Zynq architecture.
- **NXP i.MX RT series**: uses **AHB-Lite** + **APB** for the Cortex-M7 subsystem.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since AMBA is an **architectural protocol** rather than a mathematical equation, the "derivations" in this section are **deterministic, step-by-step procedures** for solving common KTU numerical/analytical problems: burst address calculation, wait-state cycle calculation, bandwidth estimation, and bridge timing.

## 3.1 Analytical Procedure — AHB Burst Address Calculation

**Problem:** An AHB master issues a **WRAP8** burst at base address `0x2000_1000` with transfer size `HSIZE = 010` (32-bit, i.e., 4 bytes). List the address of each beat.

**Step 1 — Identify the burst length.**
WRAP8 ⇒ 8 beats.

**Step 2 — Identify the transfer size.**
HSIZE[2:0] = 010 (binary) ⇒ 32 bits = **4 bytes**.

**Step 3 — Identify the wrap boundary.**
For a wrapping burst, the address wraps at an alignment of `(beats × size)` bytes.
$$\text{wrap} = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes} = 0x20$$

The wrap boundary is the **lowest 32-byte aligned address ≤ 0x2000_1000**.
$$0x2000\_1000 \mod 0x20 = 0x00$$
So the boundary is `0x2000_1000` itself (it is already 32-byte aligned).

**Step 4 — Apply the formula for the $k$-th beat.**
$$A_k = \left(A_{base} + k \times S\right) \mod (\text{wrap boundary} + \text{wrap})$$

Let's compute each beat:

$$A_0 = 0x2000\_1000 + 0 \times 4 = 0x2000\_1000$$

$$A_1 = 0x2000\_1000 + 1 \times 4 = 0x2000\_1004$$

$$A_2 = 0x2000\_1000 + 2 \times 4 = 0x2000\_1008$$

$$A_3 = 0x2000\_1000 + 3 \times 4 = 0x2000\_100C$$

$$A_4 = 0x2000\_1000 + 4 \times 4 = 0x2000\_1010$$

$$A_5 = 0x2000\_1000 + 5 \times 4 = 0x2000\_1014$$

$$A_6 = 0x2000\_1000 + 6 \times 4 = 0x2000\_1018$$

$$A_7 = 0x2000\_1000 + 7 \times 4 = 0x2000\_101C$$

Since the burst is only 8 beats and the wrap is at 0x20, the addresses stay within the 0x20 block. **No wrap-around occurs in this specific case.**

**Alternative case — wrap-around demonstration:**
If base were `0x2000_101C` with WRAP8 and 4-byte size, then:

$$A_0 = 0x2000\_101C$$
$$A_1 = 0x2000\_1000 \text{ (wrap around)}$$

This is because the base + 4 bytes would cross the 0x20 boundary, so it wraps to the start of the block.

## 3.2 Analytical Procedure — APB Transfer Time Calculation

**Problem:** An AHB-to-APB bridge performs a write to an APB peripheral. The APB clock is **50 MHz**. The peripheral **always** holds `PREADY = 0` for **3 PCLK cycles** after `PENABLE` is asserted (3 wait states). Calculate the total time for the APB write.

**Step 1 — Identify APB transfer phases without wait states.**
- SETUP phase: 1 PCLK cycle
- ACCESS phase: 1 PCLK cycle (minimum)
- Total minimum: 2 PCLK cycles

**Step 2 — Add wait states.**
The peripheral extends the ACCESS phase by 3 PCLK cycles (while PREADY = 0).
$$T_{ACCESS} = 1 + W_{wait} = 1 + 3 = 4 \text{ PCLK cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Total transfer duration.**
$$T_{total} = T_{SETUP} + T_{ACCESS} = 1 + 4 = 5 \text{ PCLK cycles}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to time using the APB clock period.**
$$T_{PCLK} = \frac{1}{f_{PCLK}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^6} = 20 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{total} = 5 \times 20 \text{ ns} = 100 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the generalized formula.**
For an APB transfer with $W$ wait states:
$$T_{APB}(W) = (2 + W) \cdot T_{PCLK}$$

The maximum throughput (bytes per second) is therefore:
$$B_{APB} = \frac{W_{data}}{T_{APB}(W) \cdot 8} \text{ bytes/sec} \quad \text{(divide by 8 for bit-to-byte)}$$

For our example with 32-bit data and 0 wait states:
$$B_{APB} = \frac{32}{(2 \times 20 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 8} = \frac{32}{1.6 \times 10^{-7}} = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ bytes/sec} = 200 \text{ MB/s}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Procedure — AHB Bandwidth with Burst

**Problem:** An AHB bus runs at **100 MHz**, has a **32-bit** data width, and the master performs an **INCR4 burst** with **0 wait states**. Calculate the effective bandwidth.

**Step 1 — Data per burst.**
INCR4 = 4 beats × 4 bytes = 16 bytes.

**Step 2 — Total cycles for the burst (pipelined, 0 wait states).**
Because AHB is pipelined:
- 1 address phase cycle
- 4 data phase cycles (but the next address phase overlaps the first data phase)
- Total: $1 + 4 = 5$ HCLK cycles for the entire burst (for the first burst; subsequent bursts see only 1 cycle per new beat after the first).

**Step 3 — Time per burst.**
$$T_{burst} = 5 \times T_{HCLK} = 5 \times \frac{1}{100 \times 10^6} = 50 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4 — Bandwidth.**
$$B_{AHB} = \frac{16 \text{ bytes}}{50 \text{ ns}} = 3.2 \times 10^8 \text{ bytes/sec} = 320 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 5 — General formula for AHB burst bandwidth.**
For an N-beat burst with $W$ wait states (on each data phase):
$$T_{total} = (1 + N \cdot (1 + W)) \cdot T_{HCLK}$$

$$B_{AHB} = \frac{N \times W_{data}/8}{T_{total}}$$

Where $W_{data}/8$ converts bits to bytes.

## 3.4 Analytical Procedure — AXI 4 KB Boundary Check

**Problem:** An AXI master issues a read burst with `AxADDR = 0x8000_0FF0`, `AxSIZE = 2` (4 bytes), `AxLEN = 7` (8-beat INCR). Will the burst cross the 4 KB boundary?

**Step 1 — Compute the last beat address.**
$$A_{last} = A_{base} + \text{AxLEN} \cdot 2^{\text{AxSIZE}} = 0x8000\_0FF0 + 7 \cdot 4 = 0x8000\_0FF0 + 28 = 0x8000\_100C$$

In hexadecimal: $0xFF0 + 0x1C = 0x100C$.

**Step 2 — Check the 4 KB boundary.**
A 4 KB boundary is at addresses $0x8000\_1000$, $0x8000\_2000$, etc.
- Base address `0x8000_0FF0` lies in block `0x8000_0000 – 0x8000_0FFF`.
- Last address `0x8000_100C` lies in block `0x8000_1000 – 0x8000_1FFF`.

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**
The burst **CROSSES the 4 KB boundary** at `0x8000_1000`. This is **illegal in AXI**. The master must split the burst into two separate transactions.

**Step 4 — Correct split.**
- Transaction 1: `AxADDR = 0x8000_0FF0`, `AxLEN = 0` (1 beat) — ends at `0x8000_0FF4`.
- Transaction 2: `AxADDR = 0x8000_1000`, `AxLEN = 6` (7 beats) — ends at `0x8000_101C`.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — AMBA Bus Comparison

| Parameter | ASB | AHB | AHB-Lite | APB | AXI3 | AXI4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AMBA version** | 1.0 | 2.0 | 2.0 | 2.0/3.0 | 3.0 | 4.0 |
| **Max data width** | 32 | 128 | 128 | 32 | 1024 | 1024 |
| **Masters** | Multi | Multi | **Single** | Multi (bridge) | Multi | Multi |
| **Burst** | No | Yes (16 max) | Yes (16 max) | No | Yes (16 max) | Yes (256 max in spec, 16 enforced) |
| **Pipelined** | Partial | **Yes** | Yes | No | **Yes (deep)** | **Yes (deep)** |
| **Channels** | Shared | Shared | Shared | Shared | **5 separate** | **5 separate** |
| **Outstanding xact** | No | No (split only) | No | No | **Yes (16) ** | **Yes (16) ** |
| **Out-of-order** | No | No | No | No | **Yes (xID)** | **Yes (xID)** |
| **QoS / User** | No | No | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Cache coherency** | No | No | No | No | No (ACE=Yes) | No (ACE-Lite=Yes) |
| **Wait signaling** | Wait only | HREADY | HREADY | PREADY | VALID/READY | VALID/READY |
| **Typical use** | Legacy ARM7TDMI | Cortex-M3/M4 | Cortex-M0 | Peripherals | SoC, FPGA | Modern SoC, NoC |

## 3.6 Analytical Procedure — Multi-Master Arbitration in AHB

**Problem:** Three AHB masters M1, M2, M3 simultaneously assert `HBUSREQ`. The arbiter uses **fixed priority** with M1 > M2 > M3. Show the grant pattern for the first 10 cycles if M1 requests 1 cycle, M2 requests 3 cycles, M3 requests 2 cycles (in that order, repeating).

**Step 1 — Identify the arbitration rule.**
Fixed priority, M1 > M2 > M3.

**Step 2 — Build the grant sequence.**

| Cycle | HBUSREQ pattern (M1,M2,M3) | Arbiter action | HGRANT |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (1,1,1) | M1 wins (highest) | M1 |
| 2 | (0,1,1) | M2 wins (M1 idle) | M2 |
| 3 | (0,1,1) | M2 wins | M2 |
| 4 | (0,1,1) | M2 wins | M2 |
| 5 | (0,0,1) | M3 wins | M3 |
| 6 | (0,0,1) | M3 wins | M3 |
| 7 | (0,0,0) | Idle | None |
| 8–10 | (0,0,0) | Idle | None |

**Step 3 — Derive the starvation problem.**
If M1 keeps requesting continuously, **M2 and M3 will never get the bus**. This is the classic **starvation** problem with fixed-priority arbitration. Solutions: **round-robin** or **time-slice** arbitration in modern bus matrices.

## 3.7 Worked Example — Full AHB Write Burst with Wait States

**Problem:** A CPU writes 4 words (INCR4) to an AHB SRAM slave. HCLK = 84 MHz. The slave inserts 1 wait state on the first beat only, then 0 for the rest. Calculate the total time.

**Step 1 — Pipelined AHB cycle count for INCR4 with 1 wait on first beat.**
- Address phase: 1 cycle
- Data phase beat 0: 2 cycles (1 wait + 1 transfer)
- Data phase beats 1, 2, 3: 1 cycle each (3 cycles total)

Total cycles = $1 + 2 + 3 = 6$ HCLK cycles.

**Step 2 — General formula.**
For an N-beat burst with wait states $W_0, W_1, \dots, W_{N-1}$ per beat:
$$T_{cycles} = 1 + \sum_{k=0}^{N-1}(1 + W_k)$$

**Step 3 — Compute time.**
$$T_{HCLK} = \frac{1}{84 \times 10^6} \approx 11.9 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{total} = 6 \times 11.9 \text{ ns} \approx 71.4 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4 — Effective throughput.**
Bytes transferred: $4 \times 4 = 16$ bytes.
$$B = \frac{16}{71.4 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 224 \text{ MB/s}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define AMBA. List the major bus protocols under AMBA. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

> **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** is an open-standard on-chip bus protocol specification developed by ARM Holdings for connecting and managing IP blocks inside System-on-Chip (SoC) designs. It standardizes the data transfer, address routing, and handshaking between processor, memory, and peripheral modules.
>
> **Major AMBA Bus Protocols:**
>
> | Protocol | Use |
> |---|---|
> | **ASB (Advanced System Bus)** | Legacy high-speed bus, used in AMBA 1.0 |
> | **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)** | High-speed, pipelined, burst-capable, multi-master bus for CPU, DMA, memory |
> | **AHB-Lite** | Simplified single-master AHB variant |
> | **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)** | Low-power, low-bandwidth peripheral bus (UART, GPIO, timers) |
> | **AXI / AXI4 / AXI4-Lite / AXI4-Stream** | High-performance, multi-channel, outstanding-transaction bus for modern SoCs |
> | **ACE / ACE-Lite** | Coherency extensions for multi-core systems |
> | **CHI (Coherent Hub Interface)** | Network-on-Chip coherent interconnect for many-core systems |
> | **ATB (Advanced Trace Bus)** | Real-time debug tracing |

**(3 marks — definition 1 mark, listing 2 marks)**

### Question 2: Differentiate between AHB and APB. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | AHB | APB |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | High-performance system bus | Low-power peripheral bus |
| **Pipelined** | Yes (overlapped address/data phases) | No |
| **Burst support** | Yes (INCR, WRAP4/8/16) | No (only single transfers) |
| **Multi-master** | Yes (with arbiter) | Single-master (the AHB-to-APB bridge) |
| **Typical clock** | 50–200+ MHz | Lower, prescaled from AHB |
| **Data width** | 32/64/128 bits | 8/16/32 bits |
| **Handshake** | HREADY for wait, HRESP for error | PREADY for wait, PSLVERR for error |
| **Connection** | Directly connects CPU, RAM, DMA | Always connected via an AHB-to-APB bridge |
| **Complexity** | High | Low (designed for simple IP integration) |

**(3 marks — any 3 valid differences, 1 mark each)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: (a) Explain the AMBA AHB bus protocol in detail. Draw the signal diagram for an AHB transfer with 1 wait state and discuss the role of the bus arbiter and decoder.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (1) confusing HREADY with HRESP, (2) failing to show the **address phase and data phase overlap** (pipelining), (3) forgetting to label all signals (HADDR, HTRANS, HWRITE, HSIZE, HBURST, HWDATA, HRDATA, HREADY), and (4) describing the arbiter and decoder as a single block instead of **two separate units** (the arbiter controls master access, the decoder generates HSELx for slaves). Always clearly separate these two functions.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks] AHB Protocol Explanation**

**Step 1 — Introduction.** AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus) is the high-bandwidth, pipelined, burst-capable backbone of the AMBA 2.0 specification. It connects high-performance modules such as the ARM Cortex-M CPU, on-chip SRAM, external memory controllers, and DMA engines.

**Step 2 — Key Features.**
1. Pipelined operation with separate **address phase** and **data phase**.
2. **Burst transfers** of 4, 8, 16 beats (or undefined INCR).
3. **Multiple masters** with centralized arbitration.
4. **Split and retry** transactions to free the bus when a slave is not ready.
5. Single-edge clock protocol, 32–128-bit data bus, 32-bit address space (extendable in AHB5).
6. Byte, half-word, and word transfers with per-byte strobes.

**Step 3 — AHB Signal Groups.**
- **Address and Control (Master → Slave):** HADDR[31:0], HTRANS[1:0], HWRITE, HSIZE[2:0], HBURST[2:0], HPROT[3:0], HMASTLOCK.
- **Write Data (Master → Slave):** HWDATA[31:0].
- **Read Data (Slave → Master):** HRDATA[31:0].
- **Response (Slave → Master):** HREADY, HRESP[1:0].
- **Slave Select (Decoder → Slave):** HSELx (one per slave).
- **Arbitration (Master ↔ Arbiter):** HBUSREQx, HLOCKx, HGRANTx, HMASTER[3:0], HSPLITx[15:0].

**Step 4 — Transfer Phases.**

```
        ┌─────────── ADDRESS PHASE ───────────┐
HCLK :  |  T1  |  T2  |  T3  |  T4  |  T5  |  T6  |  T7  |
HADDR:  | A0   | A1   | A2   | A3   | A4   |     |     |
HWRITE: | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | LOW  |     |     |
HTRANS: | NSQ  | SEQ  | SEQ  | SEQ  | IDLE |     |     |
        └────────────────────────────────────┘
        ┌─────────── DATA PHASE ─────────────┐
HREADY: | HIGH | LOW  | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH |     |     |
HWDATA: |      |  D0  |  D0  |  D1  |  D2  |  D3 |     |
HRDATA: |      |      |  D0  |  D1  |  D2  |  D3 |     |
        └────────────────────────────────────┘
```
*In this diagram: at T2, beat 0 of the address phase is on HADDR; at T3, the slave inserts one wait state (HREADY = 0); at T4, the data D0 is finally driven. Pipelining is evident: while A1 is being driven on HADDR, the data D0 is still being captured.*

**Step 5 — Bus Arbiter and Decoder (separate, distinct units).**

- **Arbiter:** Decides which master gets the bus when multiple masters request simultaneously. It accepts `HBUSREQx` from each master, applies a priority scheme (fixed, round-robin, or weighted), and asserts `HGRANTx` to the winning master. The granted master begins driving address and control on the next rising edge. The `HMASTER[3:0]` signal broadcasts the current master's identity to all slaves (used for SPLIT completion).
- **Address Decoder:** Inspects the `HADDR` bus and asserts the appropriate `HSELx` chip-select line for the addressed slave. The decoder is purely combinational (a simple address-range decoder using comparators or a lookup table).
- **SPLIT and RETRY:** When a slave is not ready, it can issue RETRY (the master retries the entire burst) or SPLIT (the master gives up the bus; the slave raises the appropriate `HSPLITx` bit when ready, and the arbiter re-grants the bus to the master).

**(b) [7 marks] AMBA AXI vs AHB — Architectural Comparison with Application Suitability**

**Step 1 — Introduction.** AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface), introduced in AMBA 3.0 (2003) and refined in AXI4 (AMBA 4.0), is the modern successor to AHB for high-performance SoCs. While AHB uses a **shared address/data bus with a single active master at a time**, AXI uses **5 independent point-to-point channels**, enabling true parallelism and outstanding transactions.

**Step 2 — Channel Architecture Comparison.**

| Aspect | AHB | AXI |
|---|---|---|
| **Bus topology** | Shared bus (one master at a time) | Point-to-point (5 independent channels) |
| **Address/Write Data** | Multiplexed over cycles | Separate AW and W channels |
| **Read response** | HRDATA only (no separate address channel) | Separate AR address + R data channels |
| **Write response** | None | Dedicated B (response) channel |
| **Outstanding transactions** | No (only split/retry) | **Yes (up to 16 per master)** |
| **Out-of-order completion** | No | **Yes (via xID matching)** |
| **Burst length** | 4, 8, 16 beats | Up to 16 in AXI4 (256 in AXI3 spec) |
| **Data interleaving** | No | **Yes (write data interleave support)** |
| **QoS / Region / User** | No | **Yes (AxQOS, AxREGION, AxUSER)** |
| **4 KB boundary rule** | Implicit (slave decode) | **Explicit (mandatory)** |
| **Cache coherency** | No | Yes (via ACE/ACE-Lite) |

**Step 3 — When to Use AHB vs AXI.**

- **Use AHB/AHB-Lite** when:
  - The SoC has a **single high-performance master** (e.g., Cortex-M0/M3/M4 with no or limited DMA).
  - Pin count and complexity must be minimized.
  - Power budget is tight (e.g., IoT, sensor nodes).
  - Examples: STM32F0, STM32F1, STM32F4 (peripheral DMA), NXP LPC11xx, Nordic nRF51.

- **Use AXI/AXI4** when:
  - The SoC has **multiple high-performance masters** (multi-core CPU, GPU, DMA, hardware accelerators).
  - **High memory bandwidth** is required (video processing, machine learning inference).
  - **Out-of-order completion** benefits performance (e.g., one master waiting on DRAM, another master doing cache work).
  - Examples: Xilinx Zynq-7000/UltraScale, NXP i.MX 8, Samsung Exynos, Apple A-series/M-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon.

**Step 4 — Numerical Throughput Comparison.**
For a typical 32-bit data bus at 200 MHz:
- AHB maximum throughput (pipelined INCR, 0 wait): $\approx 800$ MB/s.
- AXI maximum throughput (with outstanding transactions): $\approx 800$ MB/s × (1 + outstanding factor) — in practice, **1.5×–3×** higher effective throughput because the master can issue multiple bursts before the first completes.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** AHB remains the workhorse for **Cortex-M class microcontrollers** due to its simplicity and low power. AXI is mandatory for **application processors and complex SoCs** where multi-master parallelism, cache coherency, and QoS-aware arbitration are required. Modern SoCs often use a **mixed approach**: AXI for the main system fabric + AHB/APB for legacy peripheral clusters.

### Question B: (a) With a neat timing diagram, explain the APB protocol. List all APB signals and their functions. Discuss the role of the AHB-to-APB bridge.

**(a) [7 marks] APB Protocol with Timing Diagram**

**Step 1 — Introduction.** APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus) is a simple, low-power, non-pipelined bus designed for low-bandwidth peripherals. It has only two mandatory phases — **SETUP** and **ACCESS** — and uses a single-edge clock protocol.

**Step 2 — APB Signal List (with functions).**

| Signal | Direction | Width | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| `PCLK` | Global | 1 | APB bus clock |
| `PRESETn` | Global | 1 | Active-low reset |
| `PADDR[31:0]` | Bridge → Slave | 32 | Peripheral address bus |
| `PSELx` | Bridge → Slave | 1 | Slave select (one per peripheral) |
| `PENABLE` | Bridge → Slave | 1 | Indicates ACCESS phase |
| `PWRITE` | Bridge → Slave | 1 | Write enable (1 = write, 0 = read) |
| `PWDATA[31:0]` | Bridge → Slave | 32 | Write data |
| `PRDATA[31:0]` | Slave → Bridge | 32 | Read data |
| `PREADY` | Slave → Bridge | 1 | Transfer ready (0 = wait, 1 = complete) |
| `PSLVERR` | Slave → Bridge | 1 | Slave error (AMBA 3 APB only) |

**Step 3 — Timing Diagram (with 2 wait states).**

```
        ┌──── IDLE ────┐ ┌─SETUP─┐ ┌─── ACCESS (with waits) ───┐
PCLK :  |  T0  |  T1  |  T2  |  T3  |  T4  |  T5  |  T6  |  T7  |  T8  |
PSEL  : |  0   |  0   |  1   |  1   |  1   |  1   |  1   |  1   |  0   |
PENABL: |  0   |  0   |  0   |  1   |  1   |  1   |  1   |  1   |  0   |
PWRITE: |  X   |  X   |  1   |  1   |  1   |  1   |  1   |  1   |  X   |
PADDR : |  X   |  X   |  ADR |  ADR |  ADR |  ADR |  ADR |  ADR |  X   |
PWDATA: |  X   |  X   |  X   |  DAT |  DAT |  DAT |  DAT |  DAT |  X   |
PREADY: |  X   |  X   |  X   |  0   |  0   |  1   |  1   |  1   |  X   |
```

**Step 4 — State Machine.** APB has 3 states: **IDLE → SETUP → ACCESS → (IDLE or back to SETUP for back-to-back transfers)**. The SETUP phase is only 1 PCLK cycle; the ACCESS phase is at least 1 PCLK cycle and is extended by peripheral wait states (PREADY = 0).
