Saves and restores are *two* memory operations per register. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cycles per CST} &= 2 \times N_{regs} = 2 \times 16 = 32 \text{ cycles} \end{aligned} $$ **Reasoning step:** We multiply by 2 because the dispatcher both **saves** the outgoing task AND **restores** the incoming task. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{cst} &= \frac{\text{Cycles per CST}}{f_{cpu}} \\ &= \frac{32}{f_{cpu}} \quad \text{(seconds)} \end{aligned} $$ **Reasoning step:** Convert cycles to seconds using the CPU clock frequency. **Numerical example** for an STM32F407 at $f_{cpu} = 168$ MHz: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{cst} &= \frac{32}{168 \times 10^6} \\ &= 1.905 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} \\ &= 190.5 \text{ ns} \\ &\approx 0.19 \; \mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Reasoning step:** Divide 32 cycles by 168 million cycles/sec to obtain ~190 nanoseconds. This is the *theoretical lower bound* — real-world CST is **2–3× higher** due to scheduler logic, queue manipulation, and pipeline flush. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Liu & Layland Utilisation Bound Given $N$ independent periodic tasks scheduled under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**, the system is *guaranteed* schedulable if: $$ \begin{aligned} U &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le N \cdot (2^{1/N} - 1) \end{aligned} $$ ### Derivation of the bound itself The worst-case scheduling scenario is when all tasks become ready simultaneously (the **critical instant**, proved by Liu & Layland in 1973). Under this condition, the response time of the lowest-priority task is maximised. **Step 1 — Define utilisation for one task:** $$ \begin{aligned} U_i = \frac{C_i}{T_i} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Total utilisation is the sum:** $$ \begin{aligned} U = \sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the worst-case lemma** (intuition: a higher-priority task of period $T_j$ preempts task $i$ exactly $\lceil T_i / T_j \rceil$ times in one period of $i$): $$ \begin{aligned} R_i^{worst} &= C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{worst}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Take the asymptotic limit** as $T_i / T_j \to \infty$ for all $j$: $$ \begin{aligned} \lim_{N \to \infty} \; N \cdot (2^{1/N} - 1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \end{aligned} $$ **Resulting bound (the famous "69.3 % rule"):** $$ \begin{aligned} U &\le 0.693 \quad \text{(large } N \text{)} \\ U &\le N \cdot (2^{1/N} - 1) \quad \text{(exact, finite } N\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The bound is **sufficient, not necessary.** A system with $U = 0.85$ may still be schedulable; the bound only guarantees schedulability below it. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Interrupt Latency Decomposition The total latency from an external event to the start of user code can be decomposed: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{lat,total} &= T_{prop,hw} + T_{IRQ,lat} + T_{dispatch,lat} + T_{kernel,lock} + T_{ISR,entry} \end{aligned} $$ **Reasoning for each term:** | Term | Physical Meaning | Typical Value (Cortex-M4) | |---|---|---| | $T_{prop,hw}$ | Signal propagation through PCB traces / GPIO debounce | **5–50 ns** | | $T_{IRQ,lat}$ | Time for NVIC to assert interrupt to core | **12 cycles ≈ 71 ns @168 MHz** | | $T_{dispatch,lat}$ | Vector fetch + stacking of 8 registers (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) | **12 cycles ≈ 71 ns** | | $T_{kernel,lock}$ | Waiting for kernel critical section (spinlock) | **0 – 50 µs (worst case)** | | $T_{ISR,entry}$ | First instruction of ISR body | **2–3 cycles** | **Numerical example for a deterministic kernel with lock-free fast-path:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{lat,total} &= 50 \text{ ns} + 71 \text{ ns} + 71 \text{ ns} + 0 + 15 \text{ ns} \\ &= 207 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **"zero-latency interrupt"** mode used in **FreeRTOS configKERNEL_INTERRUPT_PRIORITY = highest**. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Kernel Architecture | Decision Criterion | Monolithic | Microkernel | Hybrid (Recommended for KTU Exam) | |---|---|---|---| | Resource Constrained MCU (< 64 KB Flash) | ✅ Good | ❌ Too heavy | ✅ Best | | Safety-Critical (ISO 26262, DO-178) | ❌ Hard to certify | ✅ Best | ✅ Acceptable | | Multi-core / SMP | ⚠️ Possible | ✅ Native | ✅ Native | | Hard Real-Time (µs deadlines) | ✅ Best | ⚠️ IPC cost | ✅ Best | | Hot-swappable modules | ❌ | ✅ | ⚠️ Possible | --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Kernel Architecture — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid block diagram maps the internal architecture of a generic embedded RTOS kernel and its interactions with hardware and application tasks. It is the **canonical figure** for a Module 4 KTU question asking "Draw and explain the kernel architecture." ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph APP["Application Layer (User Space)"] taskA["Task A
Priority 5
Sensor Read"] taskB["Task B
Priority 3
Motor PWM"] taskC["Task C
Priority 7
UI Update"] isrUser["User ISR
GPIO Handler"] end subgraph KRN["Kernel Core (Privileged Space)"] sch["Scheduler
Priority Queue"] disp["Dispatcher
Context Switch Engine"] tcb["TCB Pool
Task Control Blocks"] ipc["IPC Manager
Sem Mutex Queue"] mem["Memory Manager
Heap / Static Pool"] tick["SysTick Handler
Tick Generator"] end subgraph HAL["Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL / BSP)"] drv1["UART Driver"] drv2["ADC Driver"] drv3["GPIO Driver"] nvic["NVIC
Nested Vector Ctrl"] end subgraph HW["Target Hardware (MCU / SoC)"] cpu["CPU Core
ARM Cortex-M4"] memHW["SRAM / Flash"] peri["Peripherals
Timers ADC UART"] end taskA --> sch taskB --> sch taskC --> sch isrUser --> nvic nvic --> sch sch --> disp disp --> tcb disp --> cpu tick --> sch taskA -.signal.-> ipc taskB -.wait.-> ipc taskC -.post.-> ipc ipc --> sch drv1 --> mem drv2 --> mem drv3 --> mem mem --> memHW drv1 --> peri drv2 --> peri drv3 --> peri nvic --> cpu cpu --> memHW ``` **Reading guide (for the KTU valuation key):** - The **Application Layer** sits *above* the kernel and has no direct access to hardware. - All arrows between Application and HAL **must pass through the Kernel** — this is the central dogma of kernel design. - The **Scheduler** is the brain; the **Dispatcher** is the muscle; the **TCB** is the memory. - The **SysTick** is the heartbeat that drives preemptive scheduling. ## 4.2 Context Switch State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Created: xTaskCreate Created --> Ready: Scheduler adds to RQ Ready --> Running: Dispatcher selects Running --> Ready: Time slice expired Running --> Blocked: Wait on semaphore/queue Running --> Suspended: vTaskSuspend Blocked --> Ready: Event occurred Suspended --> Ready: vTaskResume Running --> [*]: Task deleted state Running { [*] --> SaveContext SaveContext --> PickHighestPrio PickHighestPrio --> RestoreContext RestoreContext --> [*] } ``` **Legend for examiner:** - **Ready → Running** = Dispatcher preempts or time slice expires. - **Running → Blocked** = Task calls `xQueueReceive()` or `xSemaphoreTake()` with no resource. - **Blocked → Ready** = ISR or another task gives the resource. ## 4.3 Priority Inversion Problem & Inheritance — A Must-Draw Diagram > [!IMPORTANT] > The "Priority Inversion" diagram is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question.** Memorize the timeline. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant H as Task H (High Prio) participant M as Task M (Med Prio) participant L as Task L (Low Prio) participant MTX as Mutex X L->>MTX: Lock (t=0) Note over L: L starts critical section H->>MTX: Try Lock (t=2) Note over H: H BLOCKED (waiting) M->>M: Starts running (t=3) Note over L: L preempted by M Note over H: H still waiting! M->>M: Runs to completion (t=10) L->>MTX: Unlock (t=11) Note over L: Without inheritance:
Worst-case unbounded delay H->>MTX: Lock granted (t=11) H->>MTX: Unlock (t=12) Note over H: Total H-latency = 10 units ``` **With Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** - At t=3, the kernel **temporarily boosts** L's priority to match H. - M can no longer preempt L. - L finishes its critical section at t=4 → unlocks → H runs immediately. - **Bounded delay** restored. --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper, Q1]** **Q1. Define an Embedded Kernel. Differentiate between a monolithic kernel and a microkernel.** **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** A kernel is the privileged core of an embedded OS that manages CPU, memory, I/O and provides scheduling, IPC and interrupt services to application tasks. - **Monolithic (1 mark):** All services (drivers, FS, network) run in a single privileged address space — *fast but unsafe*. - **Microkernel (1 mark):** Only scheduler, IPC, and basic memory management are privileged; drivers and services run as user-mode servers — *safe but IPC overhead is high*. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q2]** **Q2. What is a Task Control Block (TCB)? List any four fields stored in it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** A TCB is a kernel data structure that stores all runtime information about a task — its "passport." - **Four fields (2 marks — ½ mark each):** 1. Stack Pointer (SP) — current top of stack. 2. Program Counter (PC) — resumption address. 3. Task Priority (static and dynamic). 4. Task State (Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended). - *(Optional extras: Task ID, time slice counter, wait-queue pointer.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Question (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Q11(a)(b), Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the architecture of a typical embedded RTOS kernel with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the Scheduler, Dispatcher, and TCB in detail. **(b) (7 Marks)** For an ARM Cortex-M4 MCU running at 72 MHz, the kernel must save 16 registers per context switch. Calculate the theoretical and practical (3× overhead) context switch time. Comment on its impact on system throughput for a 1 kHz tick rate. --- #### Model Solution — Q.A (a) **Introduction (1 mark):** A kernel is structured into distinct functional blocks. Each block is loosely coupled to enable modular certification. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(See Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram.)* Should show Application Layer → Kernel Core (Scheduler, Dispatcher, TCB, IPC, Memory) → HAL → Hardware. **Scheduler (2 marks):** - Maintains **priority-ordered ready queues** (often a bitmap + linked list for O(1) selection). - Implements algorithm: **Fixed Priority Preemptive** (most common), **Round-Robin** for equal-priority tasks, or **EDF** for dynamic priority. - Decides *which* task runs next; **does not perform** the switch. **Dispatcher (1 mark):** - The "execution arm" of the kernel. Saves outgoing task's registers onto its stack, restores incoming task's registers, and triggers a **PendSV exception** (Cortex-M) to perform the atomic switch. - The actual register shuffle is done in the **PendSV handler** for atomicity. **TCB (1 mark):** - Holds: SP, PC, xPSR, priority, state, stack base, stack limit, wait-queue pointer, time slice remaining, optional floating-point lazy save flag. - Allocated from a static pool (no `malloc` in the kernel hot path). --- #### Model Solution — Q.A (b) **Step 1 — Identify the parameters (1 mark):** $N_{regs} = 16$, $f_{cpu} = 72$ MHz. **Step 2 — Theoretical CST (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{cst,theory} &= \frac{2 \times N_{regs}}{f_{cpu}} \\ &= \frac{2 \times 16}{72 \times 10^6} \\ &= 4.44 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} \\ &= 0.444 \; \mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Practical CST (2 marks):** Apply 3× overhead factor for scheduler bookkeeping: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{cst,prac} &= 3 \times 0.444 \; \mu s \\ &= 1.333 \; \mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Impact on throughput (2 marks):** - Tick period $T_{tick} = 1$ ms. - Overhead per tick = $\dfrac{1.333 \; \mu s}{1 \; ms} \times 100\% = 0.133\%$. - CPU stolen by context switching is **negligible (< 0.2 %)** → safe for 1 kHz tick. - If tick were 100 µs, overhead would be 1.33 % — still acceptable. - If tick were 10 µs, overhead becomes 13.3 % — **unacceptable**; need to lower tick rate or optimize ISR. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "the context switch time is 0.444 µs" and forget the 2× factor for **save + restore**. *Both* directions cost cycles. Many students also forget to apply the practical overhead multiplier. **You will lose 2–3 marks** for these omissions. --- ### ✅ Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — 14 Marks > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Q12(a)(b), Adapted]** **(a) (7 Marks)** With a suitable timing diagram, explain the **Priority Inversion problem** in RTOS kernels. How does **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** resolve it? What are its limitations? **(b) (7 Marks)** A system has 3 periodic tasks scheduled by Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS). Their worst-case execution times (C) and periods (T) are: | Task | C (ms) | T (ms) | |---|---|---| | T1 | 1 | 4 | | T2 | 2 | 6 | | T3 | 3 | 12 | Using Liu & Layland's bound, test the schedulability. Then perform **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** to confirm. --- #### Model Solution — Q.B (a) **Priority Inversion — Definition (1 mark):** A high-priority task is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority task, causing its response time to become *unbounded*. **Timing Diagram (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid sequence diagram.)* Must clearly show: - t=0: L locks mutex. - t=2: H tries to lock → blocks. - t=3: M preempts L (unrelated, mid-priority). - t=11: M finishes, L unlocks, H finally runs. - **H's wait time = 9 ms** (not bounded by its own period). **Priority Inheritance Protocol (2 marks):** - The kernel **temporarily raises** the priority of L to equal H's priority as long as L holds the mutex H is waiting for. - M can no longer preempt L; L runs to completion quickly, releases the mutex, H proceeds. - Wait time is now *bounded* by the length of L's critical section. **Limitations (1 mark):** - Does **not** prevent **deadlocks** (only inversion). - **Chained blocking** is not fully solved — multiple nested mutexes can still cause problems. - Adds runtime overhead (priority changes require scheduler re-evaluation). --- #### Model Solution — Q.B (b) **Step 1 — Compute total utilisation U (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} U &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} \\ &= \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{12} \\ &= 0.25 + 0.3333 + 0.25 \\ &= 0.8333 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Liu & Layland bound for N=3 (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} U_{bound} &= N \cdot (2^{1/N} - 1) \\ &= 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \\ &= 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) \\ &= 3 \cdot 0.2599 \\ &= 0.7798 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Decision (1 mark):** $U = 0.8333 > U_{bound} = 0.7798$ → **Not guaranteed by Liu-Layland bound.** *Sufficient condition fails.* We must use RTA. **Step 4 — RTA for T1 (highest priority, no interference) (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} R_1 &= C_1 = 1 \text{ ms} \\ R_1 &\le T_1 = 4 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — RTA for T2 (interference from T1) (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} R_2^{(0)} &= C_2 = 2 \\ R_2^{(1)} &= C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 \\ &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 0 = 2 \\ R_2 &= 2 \text{ ms} \le T_2 = 6 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — RTA for T3 (interference from T1 and T2) (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} R_3^{(0)} &= C_3 = 3 \\ R_3^{(1)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 3 + 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 6 \\ R_3^{(2)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \\ R_3^{(3)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \quad \text{(fixed point)} \\ R_3 &= 7 \text{ ms} \le T_3 = 12 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** All three tasks meet their deadlines ⇒ **System is schedulable** even though it failed the Liu-Layland bound. The bound is *sufficient but not necessary.* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — RTA:** > 1. Forgetting the ceiling brackets $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ — yields fractional interference (loss of 1 mark). > 2. **Not iterating** until the fixed point is reached — you may falsely conclude non-schedulability (loss of 1–2 marks). > 3. Mixing up periods and deadlines — for periodic tasks they are equal, but in **sporadic tasks** they differ, and you must compare $R_i$ against $D_i$ (deadline), not $T_i$ (loss of 1 mark). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision** - 🔹 **Kernel =** privileged core that manages CPU, memory, I/O, interrupts, IPC. - 🔹 **Monolithic** = fast, large surface area, hard to certify. - 🔹 **Microkernel** = safe, small TCB, but IPC overhead dominates. - 🔹 **Hybrid** = the industry default for embedded (FreeRTOS, Zephyr, ThreadX). - 🔹 **TCB** = stack pointer, PC, priority, state, time slice, wait queue. - 🔹 **Context Switch Time** = $\dfrac{2 \cdot N_{regs}}{f_{cpu}}$ (theoretical) → add **2–3×** for practical. - 🔹 **Interrupt Latency** = hardware propagation + NVIC + dispatch + kernel lock + ISR entry. - 🔹 **Liu & Layland bound** = $N \cdot (2^{1/N} - 1)$; sufficient but not necessary. - 🔹 **RTA** = $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$, iterate to fixed point. - 🔹 **Priority Inversion** = high waits for low while medium preempts low → unbounded delay. - 🔹 **PIP** temporarily boosts low's priority to match high; does NOT prevent deadlock. - 🔹 **Tick rate** = 1 ms typical; smaller tick → finer preemption but more CST overhead. - 🔹 **SysTick + PendSV** are the two key ARM Cortex-M exceptions the kernel uses. - 🔹 **FreeRTOS tasks** have **4 states** (Running, Ready, Blocked, Suspended) — *not* 5. - 🔹 **Determinism** > Throughput for hard real-time; **Worst-Case** matters, not average. - 🔹 **Mutex vs Binary Semaphore** — mutex has **ownership**; semaphore does not. Use mutex for resource protection, semaphore for signalling. - 🔹 **Tickless idle** = kernel feature that stops SysTick during idle to save power. - 🔹 **Memory footprint** of a typical embedded kernel = **1–32 KB ROM, 256 B–4 KB RAM/task**. ---

topics.title: Types of Operating Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types of Operating Systems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Embedded System Design (PBEVT504)**, an **Operating System (OS)** is a system software layer that manages the hardware resources of an embedded target (CPU, memory, I/O peripherals) and provides application software with a set of well-defined services such as task scheduling, inter-process communication, memory management, and interrupt handling. Based on **design objectives, timing guarantees, and architectural complexity**, operating systems used in embedded systems are broadly classified into the following types: 1. **General Purpose Operating Systems (GPOS)** — e.g., Windows, Linux (Desktop flavor), macOS. 2. **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)** — e.g., VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS, μC/OS-II, RTLinux. 3. **Bare-Metal (No OS) / Super-loop** systems. 4. **Hybrid / Multi-domain OS** (e.g., Embedded Linux with PREEMPT-RT patches, Android). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The classification is primarily based on **determinism**, **latency**, **footprint**, and **kernel architecture (monolithic vs microkernel vs exokernel)**. Examiners frequently ask students to *distinguish between GPOS and RTOS* and *justify selection of an OS for a given embedded application*. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition | OS Type | Real-World Analogy | | :--- | :--- | | **Bare-Metal / Super-Loop** | A **single cook** in a small tea stall — he takes one order, finishes it completely, then moves to the next. Simple, but no preemption. | | **GPOS (e.g., Desktop Linux)** | A **restaurant manager** focused on serving the maximum number of customers per hour (high *throughput*). If a VIP arrives, the manager may not serve them instantly. | | **Hard Real-Time OS (RTOS)** | An **air traffic controller at a busy airport** — every instruction must be issued within a strict, non-negotiable deadline (e.g., radar sweep every 50 ms). Missing a deadline is catastrophic. | | **Soft Real-Time OS** | A **video streaming server** — frames must arrive on time for smooth playback, but occasional late frames only cause a brief glitch, not a system crash. | > [!NOTE] > **Determinism** is the single most important metric separating a GPOS from an RTOS. A deterministic system is one whose **worst-case response time is bounded and predictable**, regardless of system load. --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Standard Vocabulary) - **Kernel:** The core of the OS that runs in privileged mode and controls scheduling, IPC, and hardware abstraction. - **Determinism:** Guaranteed upper bound on response time to an event. - **Jitter:** Variation in latency between successive operations (lower is better). - **Latency:** Time delay between event occurrence and system response. - **Throughput:** Number of tasks completed per unit time. - **Footprint:** Combined size of code, data, and stack in memory (typically measured in **KB** for embedded). - **Task / Thread:** Independent schedulable unit of execution. - **Preemption:** The act of forcibly taking the CPU from a running task to give it to a higher-priority task. - **Context Switch:** The act of saving the state of one task and loading the state of another. > [!TIP] > **For KTU Boards:** Always quote the *primary design goal* of each OS type. GPOS → maximize average throughput. RTOS → minimize worst-case latency. Bare-metal → minimize memory and complexity. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Operating Systems for Embedded Systems ### 2.1.1 Bare-Metal (Super-Loop) Systems - **Architecture:** A single `while(1)` infinite loop that polls peripherals and calls processing functions sequentially. - **No OS kernel** is present. The application is statically linked with a small Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL). - **Examples:** Arduino `loop()`, STM32 HAL projects, BLDC motor control firmware. - **Advantages:** Zero overhead, smallest footprint (**few KB**), fully deterministic (if written carefully). - **Disadvantages:** No task isolation, no preemption, difficult to scale, any blocking call halts the entire system. ### 2.1.2 General Purpose Operating Systems (GPOS) - **Architecture:** Monolithic or modular kernel designed for desktops, servers, and high-end embedded platforms. - **Examples:** Windows 10/11 IoT, Desktop Linux distributions. - **Design Goal:** Maximize **average throughput** and provide rich user experience. - **Limitations in Embedded Use:** Non-deterministic scheduling, large footprint (hundreds of MB to GB), long interrupt latencies. - **Use Case:** HMI (Human-Machine Interface) panels, gateways, edge AI boxes. ### 2.1.3 Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) An RTOS is an operating system designed to process data and respond to events **within strictly defined time constraints**. The correctness of the system depends not only on the logical result but also on **the time at which the result is produced**. **Sub-classification of RTOS:** **(a) Hard Real-Time OS (HRTOS)** - Missing a deadline is a **total system failure** (catastrophic). - Requires **bounded worst-case execution time (WCET)** for all tasks and ISRs. - Examples: **VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS, LynxOS-178**. **(b) Soft Real-Time OS (SRTOS)** - Missing a deadline **degrades performance** but does not cause failure. - Used in multimedia, networking equipment, consumer electronics. - Examples: **Embedded Linux (with PREEMPT_RT), FreeRTOS, Windows CE.** **(c) Firm Real-Time OS** - Late results are **useless** but a few missed deadlines are tolerable and not catastrophic. ### 2.1.4 Kernel Architecture Variants | Architecture | Description | Pros | Cons | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Monolithic Kernel** | All services (scheduler, FS, drivers, network) run in kernel space. | High performance, simple IPC. | Large footprint, lower fault isolation. | | **Microkernel** | Only essential services (scheduling, IPC) in kernel; rest in user space. | High reliability, modularity. | Performance overhead from context switches. | | **Exokernel** | Minimal kernel exposes hardware; OS libs in user space. | Maximum flexibility, performance. | Complex, rarely used commercially. | | **Hybrid Kernel** | Combines monolithic and microkernel. | Balance of performance and modularity. | Design complexity. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Hot Question:** *Compare microkernel vs monolithic kernel with an example each used in embedded systems.* (Microkernel → QNX Neutrino, Monolithic → Embedded Linux, VxWorks) --- ## 2.2 Why and How: The Determinism Argument **Why** is determinism so important in embedded systems? Consider an **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** in a car. The wheel speed sensor must be sampled every **5 ms** exactly. If the OS takes 50 ms to respond because a background antivirus scan is running, the vehicle crashes. Therefore, the OS must **guarantee** that the response always occurs within the deadline. **How** does an RTOS provide this guarantee? 1. **Priority-Based Preemptive Scheduling** — the highest-priority ready task always runs. 2. **Bounded Interrupt Latency** — ISRs have a deterministic entry time. 3. **Bounded Scheduling Latency** — time from "highest-priority task ready" to "it starts executing" is bounded. 4. **No Unbounded Blocking** — no priority inheritance violations, no deadlock-prone calls. 5. **O(1) or O(log n) system calls** — execution time of kernel services is provably bounded. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > **Note:** Use `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol in all table cells to preserve markdown rendering. | # | Parameter / Concept | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit | KTU Context | | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | | 1 | **Task Period** | $T$ | Time between successive activations of a periodic task. | **ms** | Basic parameter of any RTOS task. | | 2 | **Relative Deadline** | $D$ | Maximum allowed time from release to completion. | **ms** | $D \le T$ in implicit-deadline systems. | | 3 | **Absolute Deadline** | $d_n$ | $d_n = r_n + D$, where $r_n$ is the release time of the $n$-th job. | **ms** | Used in feasibility tests. | | 4 | **Worst-Case Execution Time** | $C$ | Maximum CPU time a task ever consumes in a single job. | **ms** | Must be measured or statically analyzed. | | 5 | **CPU Utilization (Single Task)** | $U_i$ | $U_i = \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | unitless (0–1) | Building block of schedulability tests. | | 6 | **Total CPU Utilization** | $U_{total}$ | $U_{total} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | unitless (0–1) | Liu & Layland criterion input. | | 7 | **Liu & Layland Bound (RMS)** | $U_{bound}$ | $U_{bound} = n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | unitless | Sufficient (not necessary) feasibility test. | | 8 | **Interrupt Latency** | $t_{IL}$ | Time from interrupt assertion to first ISR instruction. | **µs** | Determinism benchmark. | | 9 | **Scheduling Latency** | $t_{SL}$ | Time from "event ready" to "task starts running". | **µs** | Affected by kernel preemption model. | | 10 | **Context Switch Time** | $t_{CS}$ | Time to save + restore task state. | **µs** | Lower = better responsiveness. | | 11 | **Response Time** | $R$ | $R = $ completion time $-$ release time. | **ms** | Must satisfy $R \le D$ for feasibility. | | 12 | **Jitter** | $J$ | $J = \max(R) - \min(R)$ | **ms** | Lower jitter = more deterministic. | | 13 | **Memory Footprint** | $M$ | $M = M_{code} + M_{data} + M_{bss} + M_{stack}$ | **KB** | Critical for MCU selection. | | 14 | **Priority Inversion Duration** | $t_{PI}$ | $t_{PI} \propto C_{low}$ without PCV. | **ms** | Bounded by Priority Inheritance / Ceiling. | | 15 | **Kernel Tick Rate** | $f_{tick}$ | Configurable system tick frequency. | **Hz** | Typical: 1 kHz to 10 kHz. | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Embedded Application | Chosen OS Type | Why? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Washing machine controller | **Bare-Metal** | Simple, no IP, low cost. | | Drone flight controller | **Hard RTOS (FreeRTOS/NuttX)** | 1 kHz control loop, strict deadlines. | | Smart TV UI | **Soft RTOS / Embedded Linux** | Multimedia smoothness, rich graphics. | | Automotive Infotainment | **GPOS (Android Auto / Linux)** | High throughput, graphical UI. | | Industrial PLC | **Hard RTOS (VxWorks / RTEMS)** | Determinism + IEC 61508 SIL compliance. | | Mars Rover | **Hard RTOS (VxWorks)** | Radiation tolerance + hard real-time guarantees. | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section develops the **analytical matrix** used by KTU board questions on OS selection and feasibility. The derivations follow a bottom-up approach: from a single task's timing parameters to a complete schedulability argument for a task set. --- ## 3.1 Derivation: Liu & Layland's Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) Upper Bound ### Step 1 — Define a periodic task A periodic task $\tau_i$ is characterized by the triple: $$ \tau_i \;=\; (C_i, \; T_i, \; D_i) $$ where $C_i$ is the WCET, $T_i$ is the period, and $D_i$ is the relative deadline. ### Step 2 — Define the processor utilization of task $\tau_i$ The fraction of the processor consumed by $\tau_i$ in steady state is: $$ U_i \;=\; \frac{C_i}{T_i} $$ The total utilization of $n$ tasks is therefore: $$ U_{total} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} $$ ### Step 3 — State the worst-case phasing assumption The **critical instant** for a set of $n$ independent, preemptable, periodic tasks scheduled by RMS is when **all tasks are released simultaneously** at $t=0$. ### Step 4 — Worst-case response time occurs at the critical instant Under the critical instant, the response time of the highest-priority task $\tau_n$ (longest period) is maximized. The processor demand over $[0, t]$ must be less than $t$ for the system to be schedulable. ### Step 5 — Derive the sufficient bound for $n$ tasks For $n$ independent, preemptable, periodic tasks with $D_i = T_i$, a sufficient condition for RMS schedulability is: $$ U_{total} \;\le\; n \cdot \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) $$ The proof (Liu & Layland, 1973) uses the fact that the bound is tightest at $n \to \infty$: $$ \lim_{n \to \infty} n \cdot \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) \;=\; \ln(2) \;\approx\; 0.693 $$ ### Step 6 — Bound table for quick reference | $n$ | $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Approx. % | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | $1.000$ | 100.0 % | | 2 | $0.828$ | 82.8 % | | 3 | $0.780$ | 78.0 % | | 4 | $0.757$ | 75.7 % | | 5 | $0.743$ | 74.3 % | | 10 | $0.718$ | 71.8 % | | $\infty$ | $\ln 2 = 0.693$ | 69.3 % | > [!NOTE] > **Important:** The Liu & Layland bound is **sufficient but not necessary**. A task set may still be feasible even if $U_{total} > U_{bound}$. The exact test is the **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** iterative equation. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Iterative Response Time Analysis (RTA) For a task $\tau_i$ under fixed-priority preemptive scheduling, the worst-case response time $R_i$ satisfies: $$ R_i \;=\; C_i \;+\; \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j $$ where $hp(i)$ is the set of tasks with higher priority than $\tau_i$. ### Step 1 — Initial guess Set $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$. ### Step 2 — Iterate Update using: $$ R_i^{(k+1)} \;=\; C_i \;+\; \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j $$ ### Step 3 — Termination condition - If $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$: **converged**, use this value. - If $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$: **task infeasible**, stop. - If $R_i^{(k+1)} < R_i^{(k-1)}$: **diverged with large initial guess** — task infeasible. ### Step 4 — Feasibility verdict The system is feasible if and only if $\forall i, \; R_i \le D_i$. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: GPOS vs RTOS vs Bare-Metal The following matrix is the **single most important comparison** for KTU Module 4 questions. Memorize the italicized differentiators. | Parameter | **Bare-Metal** | **GPOS** | **Hard RTOS** | **Soft RTOS** | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **Scheduling Algorithm** | None (super-loop) | CFS, O(1), multi-level feedback | *Rate Monotonic / EDF* | Priority-based, best-effort | | **Determinism** | *Full (if no blocking calls)* | *None guaranteed* | *Bounded WCET* | Bounded average case | | **Worst-Case Latency** | Depends on longest blocking call | *Tens of ms to seconds* | *µs to low ms* | ms range | | **Footprint (typical)** | < 32 KB | > 100 MB | *4 KB – 512 KB* | 1 MB – 100 MB | | **Context Switch Time** | N/A | 10 – 100 µs | *< 10 µs (often 1–5 µs)* | 5 – 50 µs | | **Interrupt Latency** | < 1 µs (vectored) | *Unbounded* | *< 5 µs (PREEMPT_RT) / < 1 µs* | < 20 µs | | **Preemption** | No (cooperative only) | Yes | *Yes, priority-based* | Yes | | **Kernel Architecture** | N/A | Monolithic (Linux) | Microkernel (QNX) / Monolithic | Hybrid | | **Kernel Space Size** | 0 | Hundreds of MB | *< 20 KB core* | Hundreds of KB | | **Examples** | Arduino sketch, STM32 bare | Windows, Desktop Linux | *VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS* | FreeRTOS, Embedded Linux | | **Cost** | Free (toolchain) | Free – High | *High (VxWorks) – Free (RTEMS)* | Free | | **Certification Support** | None | None | *DO-178C, IEC 61508, ISO 26262* | Limited | | **Failure Mode on Deadline Miss** | System hang | User-perceived lag | *Catastrophic (safety-critical)* | Quality degradation | > [!TIP] > **Examiner Tip:** When asked *why VxWorks is used in aerospace*, the expected answer must mention: **(i) hard real-time guarantees, (ii) DO-178C certification pedigree, (iii) microkernel architecture with memory protection, (iv) deterministic interrupt response < 5 µs.** --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Effect of Priority Inversion Without Inheritance Consider three tasks: - $\tau_H$ — high priority, $C_H = 1$ ms, $T_H = 5$ ms. - $\tau_M$ — medium priority, $C_M = 5$ ms, $T_M = 20$ ms. - $\tau_L$ — low priority, $C_L = 10$ ms, $T_L = 50$ ms. **Scenario without Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** 1. $\tau_L$ starts at $t=0$ and acquires a shared lock. 2. $\tau_H$ is released at $t=1$ ms, tries to acquire the lock, blocks. 3. $\tau_M$ is released at $t=1$ ms, preempts $\tau_L$. 4. $\tau_M$ runs to completion from $t=1$ ms to $t=6$ ms. 5. $\tau_L$ resumes at $t=6$ ms, finishes at $t=15$ ms, releases lock. 6. $\tau_H$ finally runs from $t=15$ ms to $t=16$ ms. **Response time of $\tau_H$:** $$ R_H \;=\; 1 + (5 - 1) + (10 - 5) \;=\; 1 + 4 + 5 \;=\; 15 \text{ ms} $$ But the *direct* blocking should have been only $C_L = 10$ ms minus the $1$ ms already executed. The priority inversion lasted $14$ ms. **With Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP):** $\tau_L$ inherits the priority of $\tau_H$ while holding the lock, preventing $\tau_M$ from preempting. Worst-case inversion is bounded by the duration of one critical section of any lower-priority task. $$ R_H^{PCP} \;\le\; C_L - 1 \;=\; 9 \text{ ms} $$ --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Operating Systems for Embedded Use ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Embedded System Software Stack] --> B{Is an OS Kernel Present?} B -- No --> C[Bare-Metal Super-Loop] B -- Yes --> D{Is Timing Determinism Required?} D -- No, throughput first --> E[General Purpose OS] D -- Yes --> F[Real Time OS] E --> E1[Windows IoT] E --> E2[Desktop Linux] E --> E3[Android Things] F --> G{Deadline Miss Consequence?} G -- Catastrophic --> H[Hard Real Time OS] G -- Quality Degradation --> I[Soft Real Time OS] G -- Late data useless --> J[Firm Real Time OS] H --> H1[VxWorks] H --> H2[QNX Neutrino] H --> H3[RTEMS] H --> H4[LynxOS-178] I --> I1[FreeRTOS] I --> I2[Embedded Linux with PREEMPT_RT] I --> I3[Windows CE] J --> J1[Certain multimedia streaming stacks] C --> C1[Arduino / STM32 HAL projects] C --> C2[BLDC motor control firmware] ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RTOS Kernel ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Application Tasks] --> B[POSIX / RTOS API Layer] B --> C[Kernel Services] C --> C1[Scheduler
Rate Monotonic / EDF] C --> C2[IPC
Queues / Semaphores / Mutex] C --> C3[Memory Manager
Static Partitions / Heap] C --> C4[Time Services
Tick Timer / Delays] C --> C5[Interrupt Controller] C1 --> D[Hardware Abstraction Layer HAL] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D C5 --> D D --> E[CPU + Peripherals
ARM Cortex-M / RISC-V / x86] ``` --- ## 4.3 Task State Transition Diagram (RTOS Perspective) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Ready: Task Created Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatch Running --> Ready: Preempted by Higher Priority Task Running --> Blocked: Wait on Semaphore / Queue / Delay Blocked --> Ready: Event Signaled / Timeout Running --> Suspended: vTaskSuspend API Suspended --> Ready: vTaskResume API Running --> [*: Task Deleted ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Visualization Tip:** Always include a task state diagram when explaining an RTOS scheduler. Examiners reward visual clarity with bonus marks in the 14-mark questions. --- ## 4.4 Timing Diagram — Hard vs Soft Real-Time Response ```mermaid gantt title Deadline Compliance Comparison dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Hard RTOS Task A execution :a1, 0, 3 Idle :a2, 3, 5 Task B execution :a3, 5, 8 section Soft RTOS Task C execution :b1, 0, 6 Task D execution :b2, 6, 11 Task E execution :b3, 11, 17 section Missed Deadline Task F execution :crit, 12, 20 ``` > *Interpretation:* The Hard RTOS section always completes each task well before the 5 ms deadline. The Soft RTOS allows Task F to overrun its implied 5 ms deadline because missing it only causes a quality glitch, not a safety hazard. --- ## 4.5 GPOS vs RTOS: Resource Management Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph GPOSLayer[General Purpose OS Architecture] G1[Application Layer] --> G2[System Libraries glibc / Win32] G2 --> G3[Monolithic Kernel] G3 --> G3a[Scheduler CFS] G3 --> G3b[Virtual File System] G3 --> G3c[Network Stack TCP IP] G3 --> G3d[Device Drivers] G3a --> GH[Hardware] G3b --> GH G3c --> GH G3d --> GH end subgraph RTOSLayer[RTOS Architecture] R1[Application Tasks] --> R2[POSIX RTOS API] R2 --> R3[Microkernel or Small Monolithic Kernel] R3 --> R3a[Priority Scheduler RMS EDF] R3 --> R3b[IPC Primitives] R3 --> R3c[Minimal HAL] R3a --> RH[Hardware] R3b --> RH R3c --> RH end ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** *List any three key differences between a GPOS and an RTOS.* **Model Answer:** | # | GPOS | RTOS | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Optimized for **average throughput**. | Optimized for **worst-case latency**. | | 2 | Scheduling latency is **unbounded** (can be 10s of ms). | Scheduling latency is **bounded and deterministic** (µs to low ms). | | 3 | Heavy footprint (**hundreds of MB**). | Small footprint (**few KB to few hundred KB**). | | 4 | No strict deadline guarantees. | Hard or soft deadline guarantees. | | 5 | Examples: Windows, Linux. | Examples: VxWorks, FreeRTOS, QNX. | **(3 marks)** ✓ --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** *Define "determinism" in the context of an RTOS. Why is it important for safety-critical embedded systems?* **Model Answer:** **Determinism** is the property of a system that guarantees a **bounded, predictable worst-case response time** to any external event, regardless of system load. **Importance in safety-critical systems:** In systems such as automotive ABS, pacemakers, or aircraft fly-by-wire, missing a deadline can lead to **loss of life or catastrophic equipment damage**. Determinism ensures the system always responds within the validated time window, enabling certification under standards like **IEC 61508, ISO 26262, and DO-178C**. **(3 marks)** ✓ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern:** Module 4 long answer question typically carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** *With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a typical RTOS kernel. List any four essential services provided by the kernel. (7 marks)* **(b)** *Explain the Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) algorithm. Given three periodic tasks with $C = \{2, 1, 1\}$ ms and $T = \{6, 4, 8\}$ ms respectively, compute the total processor utilization and verify whether the task set is schedulable under RMS. (7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **RTOS Kernel Architecture Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Application Tasks] --> B[System Calls API] B --> C[Kernel Core] C --> C1[Task Scheduler] C --> C2[IPC Manager] C --> C3[Memory Manager] C --> C4[Time and Timer Manager] C --> C5[Interrupt Handler] C1 --> D[BSP / HAL] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D C5 --> D D --> E[Target Hardware] ``` **Four essential kernel services:** 1. **Task Scheduler** — implements fixed-priority preemptive (RMS) or dynamic-priority (EDF) scheduling. 2. **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** — provides semaphores, mutexes, message queues, and event flags. 3. **Memory Management** — partitions RAM into static blocks or implements a deterministic heap allocator (e.g., TLSF). 4. **Interrupt and Time Management** — nested interrupt handling, tick timers, and software timers. **(7 marks: 4 for diagram + 3 for explanation)** ✓ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):** RMS is a **fixed-priority preemptive** scheduling algorithm where: - Every periodic task $\tau_i$ is assigned a priority inversely proportional to its period: **shorter period → higher priority**. - It is **optimal** among all fixed-priority algorithms for independent, preemptable periodic tasks with $D_i = T_i$. - Liu & Layland proved the sufficient utilization bound: $$ U_{bound} \;=\; n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) $$ **Step 1 — Compute individual utilizations:** $$ U_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{2}{6} = 0.333 $$ $$ U_2 = \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250 $$ $$ U_3 = \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 $$ **Step 2 — Total utilization:** $$ U_{total} = 0.333 + 0.250 + 0.125 = 0.708 $$ **Step 3 — Sufficient bound for $n = 3$ tasks:** $$ U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.780 $$ **Step 4 — Verdict:** $$ U_{total} = 0.708 \;\le\; 0.780 = U_{bound} $$ The task set **passes the Liu & Layland sufficient test** and is therefore **guaranteed to be schedulable under RMS**. **(7 marks: 2 for algorithm explanation + 5 for numerical evaluation)** ✓ --- ### Question B (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Internal Choice]** **(a)** *Compare and contrast the **microkernel** and **monolithic kernel** architectures used in RTOS design. Mention one commercial RTOS for each type. (7 marks)* **(b)** *What is priority inversion? Explain the Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) with a suitable example. Why is the Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP) considered safer than PIP? (7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) | Feature | **Microkernel** | **Monolithic Kernel** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition** | Only essential services (scheduler, IPC, low-level drivers) run in kernel space. | All services (FS, drivers, network, scheduler) run in a single kernel address space. | | **Size** | Small (a few thousand lines of code). | Large (millions of lines, e.g., Linux). | | **Fault Isolation** | High — driver crash does not crash the kernel. | Low — a faulty driver can crash the whole system. | | **Performance** | Lower (more user-kernel mode switches via IPC). | Higher (direct function calls within kernel). | | **Security** | Higher (less privileged code in kernel). | Lower (more attack surface). | | **Customization** | Modular, easy to configure for embedded targets. | Difficult to slim down. | | **Example RTOS** | **QNX Neutrino, MINIX 3**. | **VxWorks, FreeRTOS (small monolithic), Embedded Linux**. | **Verdict for embedded use:** Microkernels dominate **safety-critical aerospace and automotive** (QNX in millions of cars), while monolithic kernels dominate **high-throughput consumer and gateway** products (Embedded Linux in routers, set-top boxes). **(7 marks: 6 for table + 1 for verdict)** ✓ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Priority Inversion:** Priority inversion is an **anomalous scheduling situation** in which a higher-priority task is **indirectly preempted by a lower-priority task**, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities of the two tasks. It occurs when: 1. Low-priority task $\tau_L$ acquires a shared resource (lock). 2. High-priority task $\tau_H$ tries to acquire the same resource and **blocks**. 3. Medium-priority task $\tau_M$, unrelated to the resource, preempts $\tau_L$ and runs to completion, **delaying $\tau_H$ even further**. **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** When a high-priority task blocks on a resource held by a lower-priority task, the kernel **temporarily raises the priority of the holding task** to that of the highest-priority waiter. This prevents medium-priority tasks from preempting the critical section. **Example (Mars Pathfinder bug, 1997):** - A bus-management task $\tau_L$ held a shared semaphore. - A meteorological task $\tau_H$ blocked on the same semaphore. - Without PIP, communication tasks $\tau_M$ could preempt $\tau_L$, causing watchdog resets on $\tau_H$. - Implementing PIP raised $\tau_L$ to $\tau_H$'s priority, completing the critical section quickly. This famously fixed the Mars Pathfinder system. **Why Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP) is safer:** | Aspect | PIP | PCP | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Deadlock Prevention** | Cannot guarantee (transitive chains still possible). | **Prevents deadlock** by static analysis. | | **Chained Blocking** | A task may block on multiple resources held by different tasks. | **A task can block on at most one resource.** | | **Bound on Blocking** | $O(n)$ in worst case. | $O(1)$ — bounded by the duration of one critical section. | | **Implementation** | Dynamic, runs at runtime. | Static priority ceilings assigned at design time. | | **Predictability** | Lower (runtime overhead). | Higher (analytically provable bounds). | PCP therefore provides **provable bounded blocking**, which is essential for hard real-time certification. **(7 marks: 2 for definition + 3 for PIP + 2 for PCP comparison)** ✓ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Module 4:** > 1. **Confusing GPOS with RTOS scheduling objectives** — never say "RTOS is faster"; say "RTOS has bounded worst-case latency". Speed is not the metric; **determinism** is. > 2. **Forgetting units in numericals** — if the Liu & Layland bound gives $0.780$, explicitly state "78.0 %". Losing 1 mark on unit omission is routine. > 3. **Mixing up microkernel and monolithic** — QNX is a microkernel; VxWorks is a monolithic RTOS. Linux is a monolithic GPOS. Examiner instantly deducts 2 marks for confusing these. > 4. **Ignoring the $n$ in the RMS bound** — the bound $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ depends on the number of tasks. Do not quote $\ln 2$ as a generic bound. > 5. **Skipping the diagram in kernel architecture questions** — a 7-mark sub-question without a diagram typically yields only 4–5 marks. Always draw. > 6. **Saying "FreeRTOS is a Hard RTOS"** — FreeRTOS is **soft real-time** by default. Hard real-time requires additional configuration (e.g., FreeRTOS with hardware-assisted tick suppression or custom verification). > 7. **Priority Inversion vs Deadlock confusion** — priority inversion is *bounded by design*; deadlock is *unrecoverable*. Examiners specifically test this distinction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Types of Operating Systems** - ☐ **Bare-Metal** = no kernel, smallest footprint, sequential super-loop, used in simple MCUs. - ☐ **GPOS** = throughput-optimized, non-deterministic, large footprint, e.g., Desktop Linux, Windows. - ☐ **Hard RTOS** = deadline miss is **catastrophic**, e.g., VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS. - ☐ **Soft RTOS** = deadline miss degrades quality, e.g., FreeRTOS, Embedded Linux. - ☐ **Firm RTOS** = late result is useless, no catastrophic failure, e.g., streaming. - ☐ **Monolithic kernel** = faster, single address space, e.g., VxWorks, Embedded Linux. - ☐ **Microkernel** = more reliable, modular, IPC-heavy, e.g., QNX Neutrino. - ☐ **Determinism** = bounded worst-case response time, *not* speed. - ☐ **Jitter** = max − min response time, lower is better for real-time. - ☐ **Liu & Layland bound** for RMS: $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, sufficient but not necessary. - ☐ **Exact feasibility test** = Response Time Analysis (RTA) with the iterative equation $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$. - ☐ **Priority Inversion** = high-priority task blocked by lower-priority via shared resource. - ☐ **PIP** = dynamic priority raise; **PCP** = static ceiling, prevents deadlock, $O(1)$ blocking. - ☐ **FreeRTOS footprint** ≈ 4–10 KB; **VxWorks** typically 100 KB – 1 MB. - ☐ **Interrupt latency** in well-tuned RTOS: **< 5 µs**; in GPOS: **> 100 µs**. - ☐ **Typical kernel tick** = 1 kHz to 10 kHz depending on resolution needed. - ☐ **Mars Pathfinder (1997)** = classic real-world example of priority inversion and PIP fix. - ☐ **Certification pedigree matters**: DO-178C (aerospace), IEC 61508 (industrial), ISO 26262 (automotive). - ☐ **Always draw** a kernel block diagram and a task state transition diagram in long answers. ---

topics.title: Introduction to Real Time Operating Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Real Time Operating Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** is a specialized class of operating system designed to serve real-time applications that process data and events within strictly defined time constraints. Unlike a General-Purpose Operating System (GPOS), an RTOS guarantees deterministic, bounded response times to external stimuli, ensuring that critical tasks complete execution within their prescribed deadlines.

Mathematically, the correctness of a real-time system depends not only on the logical output of the computation but also on the **time at which the output is produced**:

$$ \text{Correctness} = f(\text{Logical Result}, \text{Time Constraint}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** An RTOS is *not* about executing tasks "as fast as possible" — it is about executing tasks "**predictably within deadlines**." Determinism is the central design philosophy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Air Traffic Controller

Imagine an **Air Traffic Controller (ATC)** managing multiple aircraft approaching a runway:

- The controller does **not** just say "land whenever you can."
- Every aircraft has a **strict landing slot** (deadline).
- Some aircraft (emergency medical flights) are **hard real-time** — they MUST land within minutes, or lives are lost.
- Some aircraft (commercial flights) are **soft real-time** — a small delay is inconvenient but not catastrophic.
- The controller must **prioritize and schedule** constantly.

In this analogy, the **Air Traffic Controller is the RTOS kernel**, the **aircraft are tasks/threads**, and the **landing slots are deadlines**.

### 1.3 Real-World RTOS Examples

| Domain | RTOS Used | Deadline Class |
|---|---|---|
| Mars Rover (NASA) | **VxWorks** | Hard Real-Time |
| Aircraft Flight Control | **RTEMS**, **Integrity DO-178B** | Hard Real-Time |
| Automotive ECU (ABS, Airbag) | **OSEK/VDX**, **AUTOSAR OS** | Hard Real-Time |
| Medical Pacemaker | Custom RTOS | Hard Real-Time |
| Mobile Phones (UI) | **FreeRTOS** (lightweight parts) | Soft Real-Time |
| IoT Sensor Hubs | **Contiki**, **Zephyr** | Soft/Firm Real-Time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Metric to Remember:** The **Interrupt Latency** of a typical commercial RTOS (e.g., VxWorks) is on the order of **< 1 µs to 5 µs**, whereas a GPOS like Linux may exhibit interrupt latencies of **100 µs to 10 ms** under load. This is a frequently tested comparison in KTU exams.

### 1.4 Classification of Real-Time Systems

Real-time systems are categorized based on the consequence of missing a deadline:

1. **Hard Real-Time System** — Missing a deadline is a **total system failure** (catastrophic). Example: Airbag deployment system.
2. **Firm Real-Time System** — Missing a deadline results in **degraded quality**, but no further use of the late result. Example: Video frame rendering (dropped frame is discarded).
3. **Soft Real-Time System** — Missing a deadline **reduces system value** but the system continues operating. Example: Online transaction processing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Key Characteristics of an RTOS

An RTOS is engineered around the following six pillars. KTU examiners frequently ask "List and explain the characteristics of an RTOS" — memorizing these is non-negotiable:

1. **Determinism** — The system responds to inputs within a **bounded and predictable time**, regardless of system load.
2. **Responsiveness** — The time taken to acknowledge and start servicing an interrupt (related to interrupt latency).
3. **Reliability** — Zero or near-zero failure rate, often using **Memory Management Units (MMU)** and **Watchdog Timers**.
4. **Pre-emptiveness** — A higher-priority task can interrupt a lower-priority task mid-execution.
5. **Small Footprint** — RTOS kernels are minimal (often **< 10 KB ROM**, **< 4 KB RAM**). e.g., FreeRTOS kernel is ~10 KB.
6. **Task Scheduling** — Uses priority-based preemptive algorithms like **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**, **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**, etc.

### 2.2 Core RTOS Architecture

An RTOS is structured as a thin software layer sitting directly on the hardware abstraction layer (HAL):

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|              APPLICATION TASKS                   |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|   SCHEDULER  |  DISPATCHER  |  SYNC & MUTEX     |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|                RTOS KERNEL                      |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|   Memory Mgmt | Timer Services | I/O Subsystem  |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|         HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER (BSP)        |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|              TARGET HARDWARE (MCU/CPU)          |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

The **Kernel** is the heart of the RTOS and provides:
- **Task Management** (creation, deletion, state transitions)
- **Task Scheduling** (deciding which task runs next)
- **Inter-Task Communication** (queues, semaphores, mailboxes)
- **Memory Management** (static allocation preferred)
- **Time Management** (system ticks, delays)
- **Interrupt Handling**

### 2.3 The Task State Model

A task in an RTOS cycles through **five well-defined states** (this is the classic **Five-State Task Model**):

| State | Description |
|---|---|
| **Ready** | Task is loaded in memory and waiting for CPU allocation |
| **Running** | Task is currently executing on the CPU |
| **Blocked / Waiting** | Task is waiting for an event (I/O, semaphore, delay) |
| **Suspended** | Task is explicitly put to sleep by another task or itself |
| **Terminated / Deleted** | Task is removed from the system |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Essential RTOS Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **must-know** for KTU University Exam numerical/derivation questions. Memorize the variables and units.

| # | Metric | Formula | Variable Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization (U)** | $U = \dfrac{C}{T}$ | $C$ = Worst-case execution time, $T$ = Task period | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | **System CPU Utilization (Uₛ)** | $U_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Sum of utilizations of $n$ tasks | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Schedulability Test (Liu & Layland)** | $U_s \leq n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Upper bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **Response Time (R)** | $R = C + I$ | $I$ = Worst-case interference from higher-priority tasks | Time units (µs, ms) |
| 5 | **Worst-Case Interference (I)** | $I = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$ | Sum over all higher-priority tasks $j$ | Time units |
| 6 | **Interrupt Latency (Lᵢ)** | $L_i = t_{ISR\_entry} - t_{event}$ | Time from event occurrence to first ISR instruction | Time units |
| 7 | **Context Switch Time (T_cs)** | $T_{cs} = t_{save} + t_{restore} + t_{schedule}$ | Time to save old task and load new task | Time units |
| 8 | **Deadline Monotonic Relation** | $D \leq T$ | Deadline must not exceed period | Time units |
| 9 | **Hard Real-Time Determinism Constraint** | $R_{max} \leq D$ | Maximum response time must satisfy deadline | Time units |
| 10 | **Task Switch Overhead Bound** | $T_{total} = T_{useful} + n \cdot T_{cs}$ | Total time = useful work + switch overhead | Time units |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are the foundation of **WCET (Worst-Case Execution Time) Analysis** in safety-critical industries (DO-178C for aviation, ISO 26262 for automotive). Before deploying any RTOS, engineers must prove analytically that the **worst-case response time never exceeds the deadline**.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

The principles learned here directly translate to:
- **AUTOSAR** in modern cars (BMW, Tesla ECUs run RTOS-based software)
- **FDA Class III medical devices** (pacemakers, infusion pumps)
- **Industrial Robotics** (KUKA, FANUC controllers)
- **Spacecraft** (NASA's cFS core flight system)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Liu & Layland Schedulability Bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)

**Problem Statement:** Given *n* independent, preemptable periodic tasks scheduled under Rate Monotonic Scheduling, derive the **upper bound of CPU utilization** below which the system is guaranteed to be schedulable.

**Given:**
- $n$ tasks
- $C_i$ = Worst-case execution time of task $i$
- $T_i$ = Period of task $i$
- Higher rate (smaller period) = Higher priority under RMS

**Step 1: Define Worst-Case Response Time for Task $i$**

The worst-case response time $R_i$ of task $i$ is its own execution time plus the maximum interference from all higher-priority tasks $j$:

$$ R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j $$

This is a **fixed-point equation** since $R_i$ appears on both sides.

**Step 2: Identify the Critical Instant**

The worst-case scenario occurs at the **critical instant** — the moment when:
- All higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with task $i$.
- This maximizes queueing delay.

**Step 3: Apply the Utilization Bound Theorem (Liu & Layland, 1973)**

The classical bound states that a set of $n$ periodic tasks scheduled by RMS is **guaranteed schedulable** if:

$$ U_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq U_{bound}(n) $$

where the bound is:

$$ U_{bound}(n) = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1) $$

**Step 4: Derivation Outline of the Bound**

For two tasks ($n = 2$) with periods $T_1 < T_2$ and the higher-priority task (Task 1) executing at the worst instant, the schedulable utilization bound is:

$$ \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} \leq 2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) \approx 0.828 $$

The general proof uses the fact that the worst-case demand from higher-priority tasks over interval $[0, R_i]$ is at most:

$$ \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j \leq \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left( \frac{R_i}{T_j} + 1 \right) \cdot C_j $$

By careful analysis of the ceiling function and the assumption that $T_i$ is the minimum period, Liu & Layland showed the bound reduces to $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.

**Step 5: Asymptotic Behavior**

As $n \to \infty$:

$$ \lim_{n \to \infty} n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693 $$

This means that for large task sets, only about **69.3%** of CPU capacity can be guaranteed schedulable under pure RMS.

| Number of Tasks (n) | RMS Utilization Bound |
|---|---|
| 1 | 1.000 (100%) |
| 2 | 0.828 (82.8%) |
| 3 | 0.780 (78.0%) |
| 5 | 0.743 (74.3%) |
| 10 | 0.718 (71.8%) |
| $\infty$ | 0.693 (69.3%) |

> [!NOTE]
> This is why real-time engineers often use **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** scheduling, which has a utilization bound of **100%** (i.e., $U_s \leq 1.0$ is sufficient for schedulability). However, RMS is preferred when implementation simplicity matters more than full utilization.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Schedulability Check

**Given Three Tasks:**

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | Priority (RMS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | Highest |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 | Medium |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 10 | Lowest |

**Step 1: Compute Individual Utilizations**

$$ U_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 $$

$$ U_2 = \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{6} \approx 0.333 $$

$$ U_3 = \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.30 $$

**Step 2: Compute Total Utilization**

$$ U_s = U_1 + U_2 + U_3 = 0.25 + 0.333 + 0.30 = 0.883 $$

**Step 3: Compute the RMS Bound for n = 3**

$$ U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798 $$

**Step 4: Compare**

$$ U_s = 0.883 \nleq 0.780 = U_{bound} $$

**Conclusion:** The task set **fails the RMS sufficient test**. However, this is only a sufficient condition, not a necessary one. We must perform **exact response time analysis** (Step 5) to confirm.

**Step 5: Exact Response Time Analysis for Task 3**

$$ R_3 = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2 $$

Iterate starting with $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3$:

$$ R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 $$

$$ R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 $$

$$ R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9 $$

$$ R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 $$

$$ R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 $$

Converged: $R_3 = 10$ ms. Since $R_3 = T_3 = 10$ ms and $D_3 \leq T_3$, the task is **just schedulable**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: RTOS vs GPOS Comparison

| Parameter | RTOS | GPOS (e.g., Linux/Windows) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Design Goal** | Deterministic timing | Maximize throughput |
| **Scheduling** | Priority-based, preemptive | Fair-share, time-sliced |
| **Interrupt Latency** | **< 5 µs** (bounded) | **100 µs – 10 ms** (variable) |
| **Kernel Size** | **< 32 KB** (often 4–10 KB) | **> 10 MB** |
| **Memory Allocation** | Static (pre-allocated pools) | Dynamic (heap, paged) |
| **Process Priority Levels** | Many (255 in VxWorks) | Few (interactive vs background) |
| **Latency on Event** | **Guaranteed bound** | Best-effort |
| **Examples** | FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RTEMS | Windows, Linux, macOS |
| **Application** | Embedded, safety-critical | Desktop, server, mobile |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RTOS Kernel Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app1[APPLICATION TASK 1]
    app2[APPLICATION TASK 2]
    app3[APPLICATION TASK 3]
    app4[ISR HANDLERS]
    
    sched[SCHEDULER MODULE]
    disp[DISPATCHER MODULE]
    ipc[IPC: QUEUES, SEMAPHORES, MAILBOXES]
    time[TIMER AND TICK SERVICES]
    mem[MEMORY MANAGER]
    
    kernel[RTOS KERNEL CORE]
    bsp[BOARD SUPPORT PACKAGE HAL]
    hw[TARGET HARDWARE MCU]
    
    app1 --> sched
    app2 --> sched
    app3 --> sched
    app4 --> disp
    
    sched --> kernel
    disp --> kernel
    ipc --> kernel
    time --> kernel
    mem --> kernel
    
    kernel --> bsp
    bsp --> hw
    
    time -.tick.-> sched
    ipc -.sync.-> app1
    ipc -.sync.-> app2
```

### 4.2 Five-State Task Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created
    Created --> Ready : Task Activate
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready : Preempted by higher priority
    Running --> Blocked : Wait for event or resource
    Blocked --> Ready : Event occurred or timeout
    Running --> Suspended : Suspend call
    Suspended --> Ready : Resume call
    Running --> Terminated : Task deletes itself
    Terminated --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Hard vs Soft vs Firm Real-Time Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[REAL TIME SYSTEMS] --> B[HARD RT]
    A --> C[FIRM RT]
    A --> D[SOFT RT]
    
    B --> B1[Airbag Deployment]
    B --> B2[Aircraft Flight Control]
    B --> B3[Pacemaker]
    
    C --> C1[Video Frame Rendering]
    C --> C2[Stock Trading]
    
    D --> D1[Online Streaming]
    D --> D2[Web Page Loading]
    D --> D3[VoIP]
```

### 4.4 Preemptive Scheduling — Context Switch Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU
    participant Sched as Scheduler
    participant T1 as Task 1 LP
    participant T2 as Task 2 HP
    participant Tick as System Tick
    
    CPU->>T1: Execute Task 1
    Tick->>Sched: Tick Interrupt fires
    Sched->>Sched: Evaluate priorities
    Sched->>T1: Save context of Task 1
    Sched->>T2: Restore context of Task 2
    Sched->>CPU: Dispatch Task 2
    CPU->>T2: Execute Task 2
    T2->>Sched: Task 2 blocks on semaphore
    Sched->>T1: Restore context of Task 1
    CPU->>T1: Resume Task 1
```

### 4.5 GPOS vs RTOS — Visual Comparison of Scheduling Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GPOS[GPOS BEHAVIOR]
        g1[Best Effort]
        g2[Variable Latency]
        g3[No Deadline Guarantee]
    end
    
    subgraph RTOS[RTOS BEHAVIOR]
        r1[Deterministic]
        r2[Bounded Latency]
        r3[Hard Deadline Guarantee]
    end
    
    g1 -.VS. Contrast.-> r1
    g2 -.VS. Contrast.-> r2
    g3 -.VS. Contrast.-> r3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for Students:** When drawing these for KTU exams, the **state diagram** and **GPOS vs RTOS comparison table** are the two most frequently demanded visuals. Practice drawing them by hand before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** 
Define a Real-Time Operating System. Differentiate between hard real-time and soft real-time systems with examples.

**Model Answer:**
A Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) is an operating system designed to handle tasks with strict timing constraints, where the correctness of the system depends not only on the logical result but also on the time at which the result is delivered. The response time to external events is bounded and deterministic.

| Feature | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|---|---|---|
| Deadline | **Must be met**; missing it is system failure | **Should be met**; missing degrades quality |
| Consequence of miss | Catastrophic (loss of life/data) | Reduced user experience |
| Example | Airbag deployment, pacemakers | Video streaming, audio playback |
| Design priority | Determinism over throughput | Throughput acceptable |

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
List any six characteristics of an RTOS and briefly explain **determinism**.

**Model Answer:**
The six key characteristics of an RTOS are:
1. **Determinism** — Same input always produces the same response within a bounded time.
2. **Responsiveness** — Quickly acknowledges and services interrupts.
3. **Reliability** — Operates correctly for long durations without failure.
4. **Pre-emptiveness** — Higher-priority tasks can interrupt lower-priority tasks.
5. **Small Footprint** — Minimal memory and power requirements.
6. **Priority-Based Scheduling** — Tasks are executed based on assigned priority.

**Determinism** means that the maximum time taken to respond to an external event is **guaranteed, bounded, and known at design time**. For instance, in an automotive brake-by-wire system, the RTOS guarantees the brake actuator responds within 5 ms — not "as fast as possible" but "within 5 ms, always."

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **confuse RTOS with "fast" OS**. Writing "RTOS executes tasks quickly" earns **0 marks**. Write "**deterministic and bounded response**" instead.
> - In schedulability problems, students **forget the ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in the interference equation. This is a **2-mark loss** every time.
> - Don't mix up **GPOS and RTOS characteristics**. Examiners allocate **1 mark per correct contrast point**.
> - For numericals, **always state units** (ms, µs) in the final answer. Omission = 0.5 mark deduction.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[a] [7 Marks]** 
Explain the architecture of an RTOS with a neat block diagram. Describe the functions of the **Scheduler**, **Dispatcher**, and **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs)**.

**Model Solution:**

An RTOS architecture consists of the following layers (from top to bottom):

**(i) Application Layer:** Contains user-written tasks/threads that perform the actual work.

**(ii) RTOS Kernel Layer:** The core software that provides:
- **Scheduler** — Decides which task gets the CPU next using a priority-based algorithm (RMS, EDF, etc.). Runs at every system tick or when a task state change occurs.
- **Dispatcher** — The mechanism that actually performs the context switch: saving the current task's registers (Program Counter, Stack Pointer, General Purpose Registers) into its TCB (Task Control Block) and restoring the new task's context.
- **IPC Services** — Queues, semaphores, mutexes, mailboxes for inter-task communication.
- **Timer Services** — System tick generation, delays, timeouts.

**(iii) BSP/HAL Layer:** Hardware abstraction that hides MCU-specific details.

**(iv) Hardware Layer:** The physical MCU.

**Functions in Detail:**

- **Scheduler:** Invoked whenever a scheduling decision is needed (tick interrupt, task creation, task unblock, etc.). Examines the ready queue and selects the highest-priority ready task. Common algorithms include Rate Monotonic (fixed priority by period), Deadline Monotonic (fixed priority by deadline), and EDF (dynamic by closest deadline).
- **Dispatcher:** Performs the actual CPU context switch. The time taken is the **context switch time** $T_{cs}$ — kept minimal in RTOS designs (typically 1–10 µs).
- **ISR:** Special routines triggered by hardware interrupts. In an RTOS, ISRs are kept **short and fast**, often deferring longer work to a **Deferred Procedure Call (DPC)** or a high-priority task to avoid blocking the kernel.

**[b] [7 Marks]** 
A real-time system has three periodic tasks with the following parameters. Determine if the task set is schedulable under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling**. Use exact response time analysis.

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 1 | 5 |
| $\tau_3$ | 2 | 10 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Assign Priorities (RMS — shorter period = higher priority)**
- $\tau_1$: Highest ($T_1 = 4$)
- $\tau_2$: Medium ($T_2 = 5$)
- $\tau_3$: Lowest ($T_3 = 10$)

**Step 2: Compute Utilizations**

$$ U_1 = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 $$

$$ U_2 = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20 $$

$$ U_3 = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20 $$

$$ U_s = 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.65 $$

**Step 3: Apply the Sufficient Test**

For $n = 3$:

$$ U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 0.7798 $$

Since $0.65 \leq 0.7798$, the sufficient test **passes**, but we verify with exact analysis.

**Step 4: Exact Response Time Analysis for $\tau_3$**

$$ R_3 = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2 = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 $$

**Iteration 1:** $R_3^{(0)} = 2$

$$ R_3^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 $$

**Iteration 2:**

$$ R_3^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{4} \right\rceil + \left\lceil \frac{4}{5} \right\rceil = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 $$

Converged: $R_3 = 4$ ms.

**Step 5: Check Schedulability**

Since $R_3 = 4 \text{ ms} \leq T_3 = 10 \text{ ms}$ and assuming $D_3 = T_3$, the task is **schedulable**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[a] [7 Marks]**
With a neat diagram, explain the **five-state task model** of an RTOS. Discuss the role of the **Task Control Block (TCB)**.

**Model Solution:**

The five states of a task in an RTOS are:
1. **Created** — Task object is initialized but not yet in the ready queue.
2. **Ready** — Task is eligible to run but CPU is held by another task.
3. **Running** — Task currently has the CPU.
4. **Blocked/Waiting** — Task suspended until an event occurs (e.g., semaphore give, delay elapsed).
5. **Suspended/Terminated** — Task explicitly put to sleep or removed.

**State Transitions:**

- Created $\to$ Ready: via `task_activate()`
- Ready $\to$ Running: via Scheduler + Dispatcher
- Running $\to$ Ready: preemption by higher-priority task
- Running $\to$ Blocked: wait on resource, delay, or queue
- Blocked $\to$ Ready: event signaled or timeout
- Running $\to$ Suspended: explicit suspend call
- Suspended $\to$ Ready: resume call
- Running $\to$ Terminated: task deletes itself

**Task Control Block (TCB):** A data structure in RAM that stores all the per-task information required by the kernel to manage the task, including:
- **Task ID** (unique identifier)
- **Stack Pointer** (top of task's stack)
- **Program Counter** (saved during context switch)
- **Task State** (one of the five states)
- **Priority** (used by scheduler)
- **Task Function Pointer** (entry point)
- **Local Storage / Parameters**
- **Next/Prev Pointers** (for ready/blocked lists)

When a context switch occurs, the CPU registers of the outgoing task are saved into its TCB, and the incoming task's TCB values are loaded into the CPU.

**[b] [7 Marks]**
Compare **RTOS** and **GPOS** in terms of scheduling, interrupt latency, kernel size, and determinism. Why is a GPOS unsuitable for hard real-time systems?

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | RTOS | GPOS |
|---|---|---|
| **Scheduling** | Priority-based, preemptive, deterministic | Time-shared, fair-share, non-deterministic |
| **Interrupt Latency** | Bounded, typically **< 5 µs** | Unbounded, can be **> 10 ms** under load |
| **Kernel Size** | Small (**4 KB – 100 KB**) | Large (**> 10 MB**) |
| **Determinism** | Guaranteed by design | Best-effort, not guaranteed |
| **Memory Management** | Static allocation, no virtual memory | Virtual memory, paging, swapping |
| **Examples** | FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RTEMS | Linux, Windows, macOS |

**Why GPOS is Unsuitable for Hard Real-Time Systems:**

1. **Non-deterministic interrupt handling** — A GPOS may disable interrupts for long durations (e.g., during memory defragmentation or page fault handling), causing **unbounded interrupt latency**.
2. **Paging and swapping** — Virtual memory may move a critical task's code/page to disk, causing **page fault delays of milliseconds to seconds**, violating hard deadlines.
3. **Priority inversion** — A low-priority process holding a lock needed by a high-priority process can block it indefinitely. While RTOS solutions like **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solve this, GPOS does not guarantee it.
4. **Time-sliced fairness** — GPOS allocates CPU "fairly" rather than by urgency, which is antithetical to deadline-driven execution.
5. **No formal schedulability analysis** — GPOS does not provide analytical bounds on worst-case response time, making **WCET certification impossible** for safety-critical standards like DO-178C or ISO 26262.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "GPOS is slow, RTOS is fast" — this is **not the correct reason**. The real reason is **unpredictability**, not slowness. A 1 GHz GPOS could be "faster" than a 100 MHz RTOS in raw throughput, but the RTOS is still the correct choice due to **determinism**.

---

### 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RTOS = Real-Time Operating System** = Deterministic, bounded-latency OS for time-critical applications.
- **Three classes of RT systems:** Hard (no deadline miss tolerated), Firm (late result discarded), Soft (degraded performance on miss).
- **Six core characteristics:** Determinism, Responsiveness, Reliability, Pre-emptiveness, Small Footprint, Priority Scheduling.
- **Five task states:** Created, Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended (or Terminated).
- **Two key components:** **Scheduler** (decides *what* runs) and **Dispatcher** (performs the actual *context switch*).
- **Liu & Layland RMS bound:** $U_s \leq n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$, asymptotic limit = $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- **EDF bound:** $U_s \leq 1.0$ (full utilization possible).
- **Exact response time equation:** $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$ — solved iteratively.
- **Schedulability condition:** $R_i \leq D_i \leq T_i$ for every task.
- **Interrupt latency** in RTOS: typically **< 5 µs** (bounded); in GPOS: variable, often **> 100 µs**.
- **Context switch time** $T_{cs}$ is overhead — minimizes in RTOS via small register sets and efficient TCB structures.
- **RTOS examples to memorize:** FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RTEMS, QNX, INTEGRITY, OSEK/VDX, Zephyr.
- **GPOS examples:** Windows, Linux, macOS — **never** used for hard real-time safety-critical applications.
- **Industry standards:** DO-178C (aviation), ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 61508 (industrial), IEC 62304 (medical) — all require deterministic, certified RTOS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tasks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of an **Embedded System** executing under a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)**, a **Task** (also called a *thread* of execution) is defined as an independent, sequential program unit that the kernel can schedule, dispatch, and execute concurrently with other tasks on a single processor using preemptive time-slicing or priority-based preemption.

Each task is characterized by:
- A unique **Task ID / Task Control Block (TCB)**
- A **Priority Level** (static or dynamic)
- A private **Stack** for context storage
- A set of **CPU registers** forming its execution context
- Its current **State** within the RTOS state machine

> [!IMPORTANT]
