> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A task is the basic unit of work scheduled by the RTOS kernel. It is *not* the same as a process — embedded RTOS tasks share a common address space (no memory protection in most embedded kernels like µC/OS-II, FreeRTOS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Emergency Ward"

Imagine a hospital with **one senior doctor** (the single CPU core) but **many patients** (tasks) needing attention simultaneously:

| Hospital Element | RTOS Equivalent |
|------------------|-----------------|
| Patients in waiting room | Tasks in Ready Queue |
| Patient currently being treated | Running Task |
| Patient inside operation theatre (anaesthetized, waiting) | Blocked / Waiting Task |
| Patient just arrived, not yet registered | Dormant Task |
| Senior Doctor | CPU |
| Triage Nurse | Scheduler / Dispatcher |
| Patient's medical file | Task Control Block (TCB) |

Just as the doctor rapidly switches between critical patients based on urgency, the RTOS scheduler rapidly context-switches between tasks based on priority. The "medical file" of every patient (TCB) holds vital signs, treatment history, and current status — exactly what the kernel stores in the TCB.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Highlights

> [!NOTE]
> **Stack Size:** Typically 256 bytes – 8 KB per task in 32-bit embedded MCUs.
> **Context Switch Time:** Must be **< 10 µs** in hard real-time systems (KTU benchmark).
> **Number of Tasks:** µC/OS-II supports up to **255** tasks; FreeRTOS supports practically unlimited (configMAX_TASKS).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Task States — The Five Canonical States

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, every task in an embedded RTOS exists in exactly one of the following **five states** at any given instant:

1. **Dormant State** — Task resides in memory (ROM/RAM) but is not yet created or has been deleted. The kernel does not schedule it.
2. **Ready State** — Task has all resources except the CPU. It sits in the Ready Queue awaiting dispatch.
3. **Running State** — Task currently owns the CPU and its instructions are being executed. Only **one task per core** can be in this state.
4. **Blocked / Waiting State** — Task cannot proceed until an external event occurs (I/O completion, semaphore release, delay expiry, message arrival).
5. **Suspended State** (Optional, in some kernels) — Task is administratively paused and not part of scheduling decisions.

### 2.2 Task State Transition Triggers — The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Transition | Trigger Event | Initiated By |
|------------|---------------|--------------|
| Dormant → Ready | TaskCreate() / OSTaskCreate() | Application / Kernel |
| Ready → Running | Scheduler dispatch | Scheduler |
| Running → Ready | Time-slice expiry OR higher-priority task becomes ready | Tick interrupt / ISR |
| Running → Blocked | Task waits on semaphore, mailbox, queue, or delay | Task itself |
| Blocked → Ready | Event signaled (semaphore given, delay expired) | ISR or another task |
| Running → Suspended | OSTaskSuspend() | Another task or itself |
| Suspended → Ready | OSTaskResume() | Another task |
| Any → Dormant | OSTaskDel() | Another task |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The *Running → Ready* transition on preemption is the heart of **preemptive priority scheduling** — the most commonly tested topic in KTU ESE Module 4 questions.

### 2.3 Task Control Block (TCB) — The Task's Identity Card

The TCB is a data structure (typically a `struct` in C) that the kernel maintains for every task. It contains:

- `*OSTCBStkPtr` — Pointer to top of task's private stack
- `OSTCBStkSize` — Stack size in words/bytes
- `OSTCBId` — Task Identifier (1–255)
- `OSTCBPrio` — Task priority (0 = highest in µC/OS-II)
- `OSTCBStat` — Task state flags
- `OSTCBDly` — Ticks remaining for delay/block timeout
- `*OSTCBNext`, `*OSTCBPrev` — Doubly linked list pointers for ready queue
- `*OSTCBEventPtr` — Pointer to event control block the task is waiting on
- `*OSTCBMsg` — Message received (for mailbox/queue tasks)

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are recurring in KTU Board Exams for the past 5 years (2019–2024).

| Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|-----------|---------|---------------|
| CPU Utilization (U) | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Unitless ratio (0 ≤ U ≤ 1) |
| Schedulability Test (Rate Monotonic) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | Liu & Layland bound |
| Response Time (Worst Case) | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Iterative solution |
| Context Switch Time | $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{restore} + T_{schedule}$ | Microseconds |
| Stack Size Minimum | $S_{min} = S_{ctx} + S_{local} + S_{nested} + S_{ISR}$ | Bytes / Words |
| Task Period vs. Deadline | $T_i \leq D_i$ for hard real-time | $D_i$ = deadline |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** In the above table, the absolute-value-style notation $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ has been deliberately written as $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ (ceiling) to avoid the markdown pipe conflict. When writing ceiling/floor in your own exam sheets, write them as `\lceil \rceil` and `\lfloor \rfloor` to be safe.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Task management forms the foundation of:
- **Automotive ECUs** (engine control, ABS, airbag deployment — each as separate task)
- **IoT Wearables** (sensor polling task, BLE task, display task, battery monitor)
- **Industrial PLCs** (PID loop task running at 1 kHz, HMI task at 10 Hz)
- **Drone Flight Controllers** (attitude control @ 1 kHz, GPS @ 10 Hz, telemetry @ 50 Hz)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Schedulability Bound

The **Liu & Layland (1973)** theorem gives an upper bound on the achievable CPU utilization for a set of $n$ periodic tasks under **Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**, where task priority is assigned inversely to the period (shorter period → higher priority).

**Step 1: Define Utilization.**
For $n$ tasks with execution time $C_i$ and period $T_i$:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}
$$

**Step 2: State the Theorem.**
A set of $n$ independent, preemptable periodic tasks scheduled by RMS is **guaranteed to meet all deadlines** if:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 3: Derive the Bound for $n = 1$.**
For a single task, the condition reduces to $C_1 / T_1 \leq 1$, which is trivially true.

**Step 4: Show Convergence to $\ln(2)$.**
As $n \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
$$

Therefore, RMS can theoretically guarantee schedulability only up to **~69.31 %** CPU utilization. Practical systems are kept below **~60 %** for safety margin.

**Step 5: Numerical Verification for $n = 3$.**

$$
3 \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) = 3 \left( 1.2599 - 1 \right) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7797
$$

So for 3 tasks, the bound is **77.97 %**.

**Step 6: Numerical Example.**
Given 3 tasks: $C_1 = 1, T_1 = 4$; $C_2 = 2, T_2 = 6$; $C_3 = 3, T_3 = 10$.

$$
U = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 = 0.883
$$

But the bound is $0.7797$. Since $0.883 > 0.7797$, the test **fails** (though the task set may still be schedulable — the bound is *sufficient but not necessary*).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Task Scheduling Algorithms

| Algorithm | Priority Assignment | Preemptive? | CPU Utilization Bound | Overhead |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------|----------------------|----------|
| Rate Monotonic (RM) | Static, based on period | Yes | $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Low |
| Deadline Monotonic (DM) | Static, based on deadline | Yes | Same as RM (transform) | Low |
| Earliest Deadline First (EDF) | Dynamic, based on absolute deadline | Yes | 1 (100 %) | Medium |
| Least Laxity First (LLF) | Dynamic, based on laxity | Yes | 1 (100 %) | High |
| Round Robin (RR) | Equal for all | Yes (time-slice) | 1 (100 %) | Medium |
| Cooperative | None | No (task yields) | N/A (not analyzable) | Lowest |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worst-Case Response Time (RCRT) Iteration

Given: Task 1 has $C_1 = 2$ ms, $T_1 = 10$ ms. Task 2 (higher priority) has $C_2 = 1$ ms, $T_2 = 5$ ms. Task 3 (highest priority) has $C_3 = 0.5$ ms, $T_3 = 4$ ms. Find worst-case response time of Task 1.

**Step 1: Initialize $R_1^{(0)} = C_1 = 2$ ms.**

**Step 2: Iteration 1.**

$$
R_1^{(1)} = C_1 + \sum_{j \in \{2,3\}} \left\lceil \frac{R_1^{(0)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

$$
R_1^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \times 1 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \times 0.5
$$

$$
R_1^{(1)} = 2 + 1 \times 1 + 1 \times 0.5 = 3.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Iteration 2.**

$$
R_1^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3.5}{5} \right\rceil \times 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3.5}{4} \right\rceil \times 0.5
$$

$$
R_1^{(2)} = 2 + 1 \times 1 + 1 \times 0.5 = 3.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4: Convergence Reached.** $R_1 = 3.5$ ms. Since $R_1 = 3.5 < T_1 = 10$ ms, the task is **schedulable**. ✅

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Task State Transition Diagram (KTU Most-Asked Diagram)

This is the single most important diagram for Module 4. Memorize the transitions and the trigger labels.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    dormantA : DORMANT\nTask not created or deleted
    readyB : READY\nAwaits CPU dispatch
    runningC : RUNNING\nOwns the CPU
    blockedD : BLOCKED\nWaits for event or delay
    suspendedE : SUSPENDED\nAdministratively paused
    
    dormantA --> readyB : OSTaskCreate()\n[Application Init]
    readyB --> runningC : Scheduler Dispatch\n[Highest Priority]
    runningC --> readyB : Time Slice Expiry\nor Preemption
    runningC --> blockedD : OS pend()\nSemaphore Queue Delay
    blockedD --> readyB : Event Occurs\nISR or task post()
    runningC --> suspendedE : OSTaskSuspend()
    suspendedE --> readyB : OSTaskResume()
    runningC --> dormantA : OSTaskDel()
    blockedD --> dormantA : Force delete\n(unsafe)
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Task Scheduling Pipeline

For complex physical drawings (such as the internal CPU pipeline during a context switch), the following Mermaid block-flow represents the architectural interactions:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Interrupt / SysTick Fires] --> B[Save Current Task Context\nto TCB on its Stack]
    B --> C[Invoke Scheduler\nOS_Sched()]
    C --> D{Priority Comparison\nin Ready Queue}
    D -->|New Task Higher| E[Pick Highest Priority\nReady TCB]
    D -->|No Change| F[Resume Current Task]
    E --> G[Restore New Task Context\nfrom its TCB Stack]
    G --> H[Set Stack Pointer SP\nto New TCB Stack Top]
    H --> I[RETI / Return from Interrupt\nCPU resumes New Task]
    F --> I
    I --> J[New Task Executes\nUser Code Resumes]
```

### 4.3 Task Priority Inversion Scenario (Classic KTU Diagram)

This is the *priority inversion* problem that the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solves:

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant H as High Priority Task
    participant M as Medium Priority Task
    participant L as Low Priority Task (Holds Lock)
    
    Note over L: L acquires Mutex
    L->>L: L starts critical section
    H->>H: H becomes Ready (preempts L)
    H->>L: H tries to lock Mutex - BLOCKED
    Note over M: M becomes Ready (preempts L)
    M->>M: M runs to completion
    Note over L: L resumes, finishes critical section
    L-->>H: L releases Mutex (Priority Boosts to H level)
    H->>H: H acquires Mutex, runs to completion
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always mention the **two conditions for priority inversion to occur** in your answer: (1) A low-priority task holds a shared resource, (2) A high-priority task tries to acquire the same resource.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a task in the context of an embedded RTOS. List any four fields stored in a Task Control Block.**

**Model Answer:**
A *task* is an independent, sequential unit of execution that the RTOS kernel can schedule and dispatch to the CPU. It is the fundamental schedulable entity, lighter than a process (shares address space).

Four fields of TCB:
1. Stack pointer (`OSTCBStkPtr`) – points to the top of the task's private stack.
2. Task priority (`OSTCBPrio`) – numeric priority used by the scheduler.
3. Task state (`OSTCBStat`) – current state flags (READY / WAITING / etc.).
4. Delay counter (`OSTCBDly`) – number of ticks remaining for the task's delay/block timeout.

*(Other acceptable: Task ID, next/prev pointers, event pointer, message pointer.)* **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between preemptive and non-preemptive (cooperative) task scheduling. State one disadvantage of cooperative scheduling.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Preemptive | Non-Preemptive (Cooperative) |
|--------|-----------|------------------------------|
| Who yields CPU? | Scheduler (hardware timer / ISR) | Task voluntarily calls `OS_Sched()` |
| Response to high-priority task | Immediate preemption | Must wait for current task to yield |
| Suitability for hard real-time | Suitable | Not suitable |
| Overhead | Higher (context switch cost) | Lower |
| Predictability | High (with RM/EDF) | Low |

**Disadvantage:** A single misbehaving (runaway) lower-priority task can starve the entire system, as it will never release the CPU. This makes deadlines impossible to guarantee — **unsuitable for hard real-time systems**. **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat state transition diagram, explain the **five states of a task** in an embedded RTOS. Clearly label the events that cause each transition. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The five states of an RTOS task (as per µC/OS-II / FreeRTOS nomenclature accepted by KTU):

1. **Dormant** – Task code is in memory but kernel has no TCB for it. Not in any queue.
2. **Ready** – Task is in the Ready Queue, eligible to run, but CPU is currently held by another task.
3. **Running** – Task owns the CPU. At any instant, only one task per core is in this state.
4. **Blocked / Waiting** – Task has voluntarily relinquished the CPU (e.g., waiting on a semaphore, message queue, or delay timer).
5. **Suspended** – Task has been administratively suspended (cannot be scheduled) until explicitly resumed.

**Transitions and Triggers:**

- Dormant → Ready: `OSTaskCreate()` is called by application during initialization.
- Ready → Running: The scheduler dispatches the highest-priority ready task on every tick or event.
- Running → Ready: (i) Time slice expires in Round-Robin mode, **OR** (ii) A higher-priority task wakes up and preempts the current task.
- Running → Blocked: Task calls `OSSemPend()`, `OSQPend()`, `OSMboxPend()`, or `OSTimeDly()`.
- Blocked → Ready: The awaited event occurs (semaphore posted, message arrives, delay timer expires). Often triggered from an ISR.
- Running ↔ Suspended: `OSTaskSuspend()` / `OSTaskResume()` called by another task.
- Any → Dormant: `OSTaskDel()` invoked (caller must be careful about ownership of shared resources).

**Diagram (Reference):** Refer to the Mermaid state diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. Hand-draw a clean version with 5 ovals and labelled directional arrows. **[7 Marks — 2 for diagram, 3 for transition description, 2 for trigger examples]**

---

**(b)** A system has 3 periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling: $T_1 = 20$ ms, $C_1 = 4$ ms; $T_2 = 40$ ms, $C_2 = 8$ ms; $T_3 = 80$ ms, $C_3 = 16$ ms. Using Liu & Layland's bound, determine whether the task set is schedulable. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Assign priorities by RMS** (smaller period → higher priority).
- Task 1: highest priority (T = 20 ms)
- Task 2: medium priority (T = 40 ms)
- Task 3: lowest priority (T = 80 ms)

**Step 2: Calculate actual CPU utilization.**

$$
U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3}
$$

$$
U = \frac{4}{20} + \frac{8}{40} + \frac{16}{80}
$$

$$
U = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.60
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Liu & Layland bound for $n = 3$.**

$$
U_{bound} = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

**Step 4: Compare.**

$$
U = 0.60 \leq U_{bound} = 0.7798 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **schedulable under RMS** with a comfortable safety margin of 17.98 %. **[7 Marks — 2 for formula, 2 for calculation, 1 for bound, 2 for conclusion]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Task Control Block (TCB)** in detail. Describe its role during a **context switch** between two tasks. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The **Task Control Block (TCB)** is a kernel-maintained data structure (a `struct` in C) created for each task. It serves as the "identity card" of the task across all scheduling and context-switching operations.

**Fields of TCB (with their role):**

| Field | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `*StkPtr` | Pointer to the current top of the task's private stack — used to save/restore CPU context |
| `StkSize` | Size of the stack in CPU words |
| `TaskID` | Unique task identifier (0–254 in µC/OS-II) |
| `Prio` | Static or dynamic priority of the task |
| `State` | Bitmask of current state flags (READY / WAIT / SUSPEND / DELAY) |
| `*EventPtr` | Pointer to the event control block (semaphore / mailbox / queue) the task is blocked on |
| `Dly` | Remaining ticks for delay/timeout |
| `*Next, *Prev` | Doubly-linked list pointers for insertion into the ready queue or wait list |
| `*MsgPtr` | Pointer to message received via mailbox/queue |

**Role during Context Switch:**

A context switch is the act of saving the state of the currently running task and loading the state of the new task. The sequence is:

1. **Trigger**: SysTick interrupt (time-slice expiry) OR preemption by a higher-priority task.
2. **Save context of Task A**:
   - CPU registers (R0–R15 on ARM Cortex-M, or equivalent) are pushed onto Task A's stack.
   - The updated `StkPtr` of Task A is written into Task A's TCB.
3. **Scheduler decision**: Kernel walks the ready queue and picks the highest-priority ready TCB (Task B).
4. **Restore context of Task B**:
   - The new stack pointer is loaded from Task B's TCB (`StkPtr` field).
   - CPU registers are popped from Task B's stack.
5. **RETI / RFE**: The interrupt return instruction atomically restores PC, PSW, and resumes Task B exactly where it left off.

The entire context switch is typically **atomic** (interrupts disabled) and executes in **5–20 µs** on a 100 MHz Cortex-M3. **[7 Marks — 3 for TCB structure, 4 for context switch steps]**

---

**(b)** What is **priority inversion**? Explain how the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solves it. Give one real-world automotive example. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
Priority inversion is a scheduling anomaly where a higher-priority task is *indirectly* preempted by a lower-priority task, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities. This occurs when:
- (i) A low-priority task $L$ holds a shared resource (e.g., mutex).
- (ii) A high-priority task $H$ attempts to acquire the same resource and is blocked.
- (iii) A medium-priority task $M$ preempts $L$ and runs *unrelated* code, further delaying $H$.

**Effect:** The high-priority task $H$ is forced to wait not just for $L$, but for $M$ as well — the worst-case blocking time becomes unbounded.

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) Solution:**

When task $H$ blocks on a mutex held by $L$, the kernel **temporarily raises** the priority of $L$ to that of $H$ (and to the highest priority of any other task blocked on the same mutex). $L$ then runs at this boosted priority, completing its critical section quickly, and releasing the mutex. After release, $L$'s original priority is restored.

**Net effect:** Medium-priority task $M$ **cannot preempt $L$** during the critical section, because $L$ is now running at $H$'s elevated priority. The blocking time of $H$ is bounded by the critical section length of $L$, not by the duration of $M$.

**Automotive Real-World Example — Mars Pathfinder (1997):**
The Mars Pathfinder lander experienced system resets due to priority inversion in its VxWorks RTOS controlling the bus management task. The meteorological task (low priority) held a shared semaphore; the bus task (high priority) blocked on it; the communication task (medium priority) preempted. The watchdog detected the bus task missing deadlines and reset the system. NASA diagnosed it remotely and *enabled priority inheritance* — the bug was fixed via a software patch from Earth, ~200 million km away.

**Other automotive example:** Engine control unit (ECU) — high-priority airbag deployment task blocked on a CAN bus mutex held by a low-priority diagnostic task; PIP prevents a misfire-detection (medium priority) task from inflating the delay. **[7 Marks — 2 for definition, 3 for PIP mechanism, 2 for example]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Traps (compiled from KTU Examiner Reports 2019–2024):**
> 1. **Forgetting to label diagram transitions** — Students draw the state diagram but forget to write the *triggering event* on the arrows. Costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing "task" with "process"** — In KTU's µC/OS-II/FreeRTOS context, tasks **share address space**; do NOT say "each task has its own virtual memory."
> 3. **Wrong bound formula** — Writing $U \leq 1$ for RMS instead of $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ will fetch **0/7** on RMS questions.
> 4. **Skipping iterations in RCRT** — Examiners want to see the iteration table for the worst-case response time; do not stop at iteration 1.
> 5. **PIP vs. Priority Ceiling** — Don't interchange. PIP raises the holder's priority *dynamically*; Priority Ceiling Protocol assigns a *static ceiling* at the start.
> 6. **No units in numerical answers** — Always write ms, µs, % — even if the question doesn't explicitly ask.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Punchlist — Module 4, Topic: Tasks**

- [ ] **Task** = basic schedulable unit; **Process** = heavier, with own address space (don't confuse).
- [ ] **Five states:** Dormant, Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended. *Only one task per core can be Running.*
- [ ] **TCB** holds: Stack pointer, priority, state, delay counter, event pointer, list pointers.
- [ ] **Context switch** = Save (push regs to current task's stack → update TCB) + Restore (load new SP from new TCB → pop regs).
- [ ] **RMS priority rule:** Shorter period → Higher priority. Static assignment.
- [ ] **Liu & Layland bound:** $\sum C_i/T_i \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; converges to $\ln 2 \approx 69.3\%$.
- [ ] **EDF bound:** 100 % (theoretically), but not predictable under overload.
- [ ] **Worst-case response time** is solved *iteratively* — initialize $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ and iterate until convergence.
- [ ] **Priority inversion** requires all three: L holds resource, H blocked on it, M preempts L.
- [ ] **PIP solution:** Boost L's priority to H's while L holds the resource; restore after release.
- [ ] **Real-world KTU favorites:** Mars Pathfinder (1997), Toyota ECU throttle control (2009–2010), Airbus A330 fuel sensor task scheduling.
- [ ] **Typical context switch time benchmark:** 5–20 µs on a 100 MHz Cortex-M3.
- [ ] **Stack sizing rule of thumb:** $S = S_{ctx} + S_{local} + S_{ISR} + S_{nested\;IRQ} + S_{safety\;margin}$.
- [ ] **Number of tasks supported:** µC/OS-II → 255, FreeRTOS → configurable up to **unlimited** (RAM permitting).
- [ ] **Preemptive vs Cooperative:** Preemptive is hard real-time capable; cooperative is not analyzable.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Process

In the context of **Embedded System Design** and **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)**, a **Process** is formally defined as a **program in execution**. It is the basic unit of work scheduled by the operating system kernel and represents an independent, sequential flow of control with its own dedicated memory address space, system resources, and execution state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> A *process* is a program in its execution state. It is not the same as a program. A program is a passive entity (a file containing instructions), whereas a process is an active entity with a program counter, register set, stack, and data section that represents the current activity of execution.

For KTU Board Examinations, the following terminological precision is mandatory:

| Term | Meaning (Examiner Expectation) |
|---|---|
| **Program** | Passive. A binary file stored on disk (e.g., `.elf`, `.hex`). |
| **Process** | Active. An instance of a program loaded into RAM with a live execution context. |
| **Thread** | Lightweight sub-unit of a process. Shares memory with parent process. |
| **Task** | In RTOS terminology, often used synonymously with thread/process. |

A process is uniquely identified by the kernel using a **Process Identifier (PID)**, an integer value assigned by the OS at the time of creation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a large commercial restaurant kitchen:

- **The Recipe Book** stored on a shelf is the *Program* (passive, just instructions).
- **A Chef currently following that recipe, with a cutting board, a pan on the stove, and a half-chopped onion** is the *Process* (active, has state and resources).
- If the head chef pauses, writes down exactly where he left off (which step, what temperature the pan is at), and starts cooking a different dish, then comes back later to resume — this **written note** is analogous to the **Process Control Block (PCB)**.
- If two chefs share the same kitchen counter, stove, and ingredients to make *one* dish collaboratively, they are **Threads** of the same process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Process" matters in Embedded Systems:**
> Modern embedded applications (automotive ECUs, IoT gateways, drones) execute **multiple concurrent processes** — sensor polling, motor control, wireless communication, user-interface updates — all on a single microcontroller. The RTOS kernel's job is to manage these processes so that hard real-time deadlines are never missed.

## 1.3 Components of a Process — The Process Image

A process is composed of three logical segments in memory:

1. **Text Section (Code Segment):** Read-only, fixed-size region containing the compiled executable instructions (`.text`).
2. **Data Section:** Contains initialized global and static variables (`.data`) and uninitialized variables (`.bss`).
3. **Stack Section:** Dynamic region used for function call frames, local (automatic) variables, and return addresses. Grows downward in memory.
4. **Heap Section:** Dynamic memory region used for runtime allocations via `malloc()` / `free()` in C. Grows upward.

The **Program Counter (PC)**, **Stack Pointer (SP)**, and **CPU Register Set** together form the *execution context* of the process.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Context Switch Time:** Typically **1 µs – 10 µs** in a commercial RTOS.
- **PID Range:** 16-bit (0 to 65535) for embedded RTOS like FreeRTOS uses handles instead of PIDs.
- **Minimum Stack Size per Process:** Usually **256 bytes – 4 KB** in microcontroller-class RTOS.
- **Quantum / Time Slice:** **1 ms – 10 ms** typical for round-robin scheduling.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Process Control Block (PCB) — Kernel's Data Structure

The **Process Control Block (PCB)** is the most important data structure in any OS/RTOS. It is a kernel-managed record (usually a `struct` in C) that stores *everything the kernel needs to know* about a process to manage and control its execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Alert:** "Explain the structure of a PCB" is a frequently asked 7-mark question. Memorize the fields below.

### Standard PCB Fields (Examiner's Checklist)

- **Process ID (PID):** Unique integer identifier.
- **Process State:** Current state (New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated).
- **Program Counter (PC):** Address of the next instruction to execute.
- **CPU Registers:** Accumulator, index registers, general-purpose registers, status flags.
- **Memory Management Info:** Base/limit registers, page tables, segment tables.
- **Scheduling Info:** Priority, accounting data (CPU time used, time limits).
- **List of Open Files:** File descriptors, socket handles.
- **List of I/O Devices / Resources:** Allocated peripherals, semaphores held.
- **Pointer to Parent Process:** For process tree hierarchy.
- **Pointer to Children Processes.**

### 2.1.1 Why PCB is Critical

When the kernel performs a **context switch**, it:
1. Saves the current process's CPU state (registers, PC, SP) **into its PCB**.
2. Loads the next process's state **from its PCB** into the CPU.

Without the PCB, the kernel would have no memory of suspended processes — they would essentially "die" the moment they were preempted.

## 2.2 The Five Process States — State Transition Model

Every textbook and KTU question bank uses the **5-state process model**:

| State | Description | Transition Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **New** | Process is being created. PCB is allocated. | Admitted → Ready |
| **Ready** | Process is waiting in the ready queue for CPU allocation. | Scheduler Dispatch → Running |
| **Running** | Instructions are being executed on the CPU. | Interrupt / Yield → Ready; I/O wait → Waiting |
| **Waiting / Blocked** | Process cannot run until some event occurs (I/O completion, semaphore signal, timer expiry). | Event Occurs → Ready |
| **Terminated** | Process execution finished. PCB and resources being reclaimed. | Kernel cleanup completes. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Only one process per CPU core can be in the Running state at any instant.** All other "active" processes sit in Ready or Waiting queues.

## 2.3 Process Scheduling in Embedded Context

The **Scheduler** is the kernel module that decides *which* Ready process gets the CPU next. Three broad classes:

1. **Preemptive Priority Scheduling** — Used in hard real-time systems (VxWorks, FreeRTOS). Higher-priority process preempts lower one immediately.
2. **Round-Robin (Time-Sliced)** — Each Ready process gets a fixed *quantum* in cyclic order. Fair but not deterministic.
3. **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** — Dynamic priority; process with nearest deadline runs first. Optimal for uniprocessor real-time.

## 2.4 Process Creation — `fork()` / `exec()` in POSIX

In Linux/POSIX (used in embedded Linux on Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone, etc.):

- **`fork()`:** Creates a child process that is a *duplicate* of the parent. Returns child PID to parent, 0 to child.
- **`exec()`:** Replaces the current process image with a new program. Used after `fork()` to run a different program in the child.
- **`wait()`:** Parent blocks until child terminates (used for synchronization).
- **`exit()`:** Terminates the calling process, returns status to parent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In bare-metal RTOS like **FreeRTOS**, there is no `fork()`. The equivalent is `xTaskCreate()` which spawns a *task* (thread) that begins execution at a specified function. This is a common KTU conceptual question.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Utilization** | $U = 1 - P^{n}$ | $0 \le U \le 1$ (dimensionless) | $n$ = number of processes, $P$ = idle probability |
| **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Processes Completed}}{\text{Time Unit}}$ | processes per second | Higher is better |
| **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $TAT = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$ | seconds | Total time in system |
| **Waiting Time (WT)** | $WT = TAT - T_{burst}$ | seconds | Time spent in Ready queue |
| **Response Time (RT)** | $RT = T_{first\_run} - T_{arrival}$ | seconds | Time to first execution |
| **CPU Burst Estimate (Exponential Avg.)** | $\tau_{n+1} = \alpha \cdot t_{n} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \tau_{n}$ | seconds | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ |
| **Context Switch Overhead** | $T_{cs} \le 0.01 \times T_{deadline}$ | seconds | Hard real-time constraint |
| **Rate Monotonic Priority Bound** | $U_{bound} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | dimensionless | Liu & Layland, 1973 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule:** All absolute values in formulas use `\vert` not `|` to preserve markdown table integrity. For example, the bound formula above must NOT contain the raw `|` character inside a row.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU (Engine Control Unit):** A 32-bit microcontroller runs 5–20 processes (crank angle sensing, fuel injection timing, knock detection, OBD-II communication, limp-home mode). The RTOS guarantees the fuel-injection process executes every 5 ms with **zero deadline miss**.
- **Drone Flight Controller (Pixhawk):** Runs processes for IMU fusion, PID control loop, GPS parsing, telemetry. The 1 kHz attitude control loop must preempt everything else.
- **IoT Edge Device:** MQTT publisher process, sensor sampling process, OTA update process — all share an MCU via an RTOS scheduler.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — Liu & Layland Bound

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 (Real-Time Scheduling). It is frequently asked for 7 marks.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Let there be $n$ periodic real-time processes $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ with:
- Period $T_i$ (equal to deadline $D_i$)
- Worst-case CPU execution time $C_i$
- Priority assigned inversely proportional to period (shorter period → higher priority)

The **schedulability condition** (sufficient, not necessary) states that a process set is guaranteed to meet all deadlines if:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq U_{bound}(n) = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)
$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the worst-case response time scenario:**

For a given process $P_k$, the worst-case blocking time occurs when a higher-priority process is released *just after* $P_k$ starts executing. The higher-priority process will then preempt $P_k$ as soon as possible.

**Step 2 — Interference from higher-priority processes:**

The total CPU demand from higher-priority processes $P_j$ (where $j < k$) in the interval $[0, t]$ is the number of jobs of $P_j$ released in that interval, multiplied by $C_j$:

$$
I_j(t) = \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

**Step 3 — Worst-case finish time of $P_k$:**

The worst-case finish time $W_k(t)$ is the time at which $P_k$ would complete if it were released at $t = 0$ under maximum preemption:

$$
W_k(t) = C_k + \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

**Step 4 — Fixed-point iteration to find $W_k$:**

Solve $W_k(t) = t$ by iteration. Start with $t^{(0)} = C_k$, then iterate:

$$
t^{(m+1)} = C_k + \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \left\lceil \frac{t^{(m)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

The smallest fixed point $W_k^*$ is the worst-case response time. $P_k$ is schedulable if and only if $W_k^* \le T_k$.

**Step 5 — Derivation of the RMS utilization bound (Liu & Layland, 1973):**

For the special case where deadlines equal periods, the critical instant for $P_i$ is when all higher-priority processes are released simultaneously at $t=0$. The total time consumed by these $i-1$ higher-priority jobs in the longest interval $[0, T_i]$ is:

$$
\text{Total Higher Priority Load} = \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{T_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

Since periods are not necessarily harmonic, the worst-case ceiling effect gives the **upper bound**. Liu and Layland showed that for the worst-case period configuration, the maximum utilization bound is:

$$
U_{bound}(n) = n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Limit as $n \to \infty$:**

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} - 1}{1/n}
$$

Applying L'Hôpital's rule (let $u = 1/n$, so $u \to 0$):

$$
\lim_{u \to 0} \frac{2^{u} - 1}{u} = \lim_{u \to 0} \frac{2^{u} \ln 2}{1} = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
$$

**Step 7 — Final schedulability test:**

A real-time system with $n$ RMS-scheduled processes is **guaranteed schedulable** if:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** Three real-time processes are scheduled using Rate Monotonic Scheduling. Their parameters are:

- $P_1$: $C_1 = 1$ ms, $T_1 = 4$ ms
- $P_2$: $C_2 = 2$ ms, $T_2 = 6$ ms
- $P_3$: $C_3 = 3$ ms, $T_3 = 10$ ms

**Test for schedulability:**

$$
U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10}
$$

$$
U = 0.2500 + 0.3333 + 0.3000 = 0.8833
$$

**RMS utilization bound for $n=3$:**

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
U = 0.8833 \quad \text{vs.} \quad U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798
$$

Since $U > U_{bound}$, the **sufficient test fails**. However, this does not mean the system is unschedulable — RMS bound is *sufficient but not necessary*. We must perform the **exact response time analysis** (Step 4) to confirm.

**Exact analysis for $P_3$ (lowest priority):**

Iteration 1: $t^{(0)} = 3$ (just $C_3$)
Iteration 2: $t^{(1)} = 3 + \lceil 3/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 3/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$
Iteration 3: $t^{(2)} = 3 + \lceil 6/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 6/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7$
Iteration 4: $t^{(3)} = 3 + \lceil 7/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 7/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9$
Iteration 5: $t^{(4)} = 3 + \lceil 9/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 9/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$
Iteration 6: $t^{(5)} = 3 + \lceil 10/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 10/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$

**Convergence:** $W_3^* = 10$ ms. Since $W_3^* = T_3 = 10$ ms, the deadline is **just met**. The system is schedulable despite failing the sufficient test.

## 3.2 Derivation: CPU Utilization for n Independent Processes

Let each of $n$ processes spend a fraction $p$ of its time waiting for I/O. The probability that **all $n$ processes are simultaneously waiting** is $p^n$. Therefore, the probability that **at least one process is ready** (CPU is not idle) is:

$$
P_{CPU\_busy} = 1 - p^{n}
$$

Hence the **CPU utilization** is:

$$
U = 1 - p^{n}
$$

This is the classic textbook formula demonstrating that even with high per-process I/O wait, an embedded system can keep the CPU nearly fully utilized by adding more processes (multiprogramming).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Five-State Process Transition Diagram (Mermaid State Machine)

The following Mermaid block is engineered to be fully compliant with the KTU Engine safeguards: every node ID is alphanumeric with letter prefix, and all labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    sNew : NEW
    sReady : READY
    sRun : RUNNING
    sWait : WAITING OR BLOCKED
    sTerm : TERMINATED

    sNew --> sReady : ADMIT
    sReady --> sRun : SCHEDULER DISPATCH
    sRun --> sReady : PREEMPT OR INTERRUPT OR YIELD
    sRun --> sWait : I/O REQUEST OR EVENT WAIT
    sWait --> sReady : I/O COMPLETION OR EVENT SIGNAL
    sRun --> sTerm : EXIT OR ABORT
    sReady --> sTerm : KILLED BY KERNEL
```

**Interpretation for students:**
- The **horizontal arrows** between READY and RUNNING form the *dispatch loop* — the core scheduling cycle.
- The **vertical-ish arrows** to/from WAITING represent *event-driven* transitions, the heart of RTOS behavior.
- Only one process can sit in the **RUNNING** node at any moment per CPU core.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: PCB and Context Switch

This block diagram illustrates how the kernel uses the PCB to perform a context switch between Process A and Process B.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Core Registers PC SP R0 R1 Status] --> B{KERNEL SCHEDULER}
    B --> C[Save State of Process A into PCBA]
    B --> D[Load State of Process B from PCBB]
    C --> E[PCBA Structure PID State Regs MemInfo]
    D --> F[PCBB Structure PID State Regs MemInfo]
    E --> G[Process A Queued in READY list]
    F --> H[Process B Resumes Execution on CPU]

    classDef kernel fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pcb fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cpu fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class B kernel
    class E,F pcb
    class A cpu
```

**Block-by-block interpretation:**

1. **CPU Core Registers (Green Block):** Active hardware registers of the processor. Only one set exists; this is the "current" execution context.
2. **Kernel Scheduler (Blue Block):** Triggered by a timer interrupt, syscall, or `yield()`. Decides next process to run.
3. **PCB-A and PCB-B (Orange Blocks):** Per-process storage in kernel memory. PCB-A holds the snapshot of Process A; PCB-B holds the snapshot of Process B.
4. **READY List:** Doubly-linked list of all Ready PCBs, ordered by priority or arrival.
5. **Context Restore:** Scheduler copies PCB-B's register values back into the CPU and sets the PC — Process B continues from exactly where it left off.

## 4.3 Process Memory Layout (RAM Partitioning)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HIGH["HIGH MEMORY ADDRESS 0xFFFFFFFF"]
        KRN["KERNEL SPACE / ISR VECTORS"]
    end
    subgraph USR3["PROCESS D STACK"]
        S3["Stack grows downward"]
    end
    subgraph USR2["PROCESS C STACK"]
        S2["Stack grows downward"]
    end
    subgraph USR1["PROCESS B STACK"]
        S1["Stack grows downward"]
    end
    subgraph LOW["LOW MEMORY ADDRESS 0x00000000"]
        H["HEAP shared or per process"]
        D["DATA Segment .data .bss"]
        T["TEXT Segment .text code"]
    end
    KRN --> USR3 --> USR2 --> USR1 --> LOW
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each process has its own private stack; the text and data segments may be shared (for the same program) or distinct (for different programs). The kernel space at the top is protected and inaccessible in user mode.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define a Process. Differentiate between a Process and a Thread. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Process** is a program in execution. It is an active entity consisting of a program counter, register set, stack, data section, and associated kernel resources managed via a Process Control Block (PCB).

| Aspect | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Space | Has its own separate address space | Shares address space with peer threads |
| Creation Cost | Heavy (requires PCB, page tables, memory maps) | Light (often just stack + TCB) |
| Communication | Requires IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory) | Direct (reads/writes same variables) |
| Context Switch | Slower (TLB/MMU flush) | Faster (less state to save) |
| Failure Isolation | Crash of one does not affect others | Crash of one can kill entire process |

> (3 marks: 1 mark definition, 2 marks for the comparison table.)

---

### Question 2. List any five fields of a Process Control Block (PCB) and explain the role of the Program Counter field. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Five fields of a PCB:
1. **Process ID (PID)** – unique numeric identifier.
2. **Process State** – current state in the 5-state model.
3. **CPU Registers** – snapshot of all general-purpose and special registers.
4. **Memory Management Info** – base/limit registers or page table pointer.
5. **Scheduling Information** – priority, quantum remaining, accounting data.

**Role of the Program Counter (PC):** The PC field stores the address of the **next instruction** the process must execute when it is next dispatched to the CPU. During a context switch, the kernel saves the running process's current PC into its PCB and later restores it so the process resumes *exactly* at the instruction where it was suspended — producing the illusion of continuous execution. Without saving the PC, preempted processes would restart from the beginning, which is incorrect.

> (3 marks: 2 marks for listing fields, 1 mark for explaining PC role.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Draw and explain the five-state process model with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the Process Control Block (PCB) in process management. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

Refer to the **5-state diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes. Explanation of each state and transition:

- **New → Ready (Admit):** When the kernel finishes allocating the PCB, memory, and resources, the process moves to the Ready queue. It is now eligible to compete for CPU time.
- **Ready → Running (Dispatch):** The scheduler selects this process from the Ready queue and loads its context into the CPU. Only one process per core is Running.
- **Running → Ready (Preempt/Interrupt/Yield):** Either the time quantum expires (round-robin), a higher-priority process becomes Ready (preemptive priority), or the process voluntarily yields. The current state is saved in the PCB.
- **Running → Waiting (I/O or Event):** The process issues an I/O request, calls `wait()`, or blocks on a semaphore/mutex. It cannot make progress until the external event occurs.
- **Waiting → Ready (Event Occurs):** The awaited I/O completes, timer expires, or semaphore is signaled. The process returns to the Ready queue to await re-dispatch.
- **Running → Terminated (Exit):** The process calls `exit()` or returns from `main()`. The kernel reclaims its resources, but the PCB is retained until the parent collects the exit status (zombie process).

**Role of PCB (7-Mark Detail):**

The PCB is the kernel's master record of every process. It enables four critical OS functions:

1. **Context Preservation:** Stores register values, PC, SP, and status flags so a process can be frozen and later resumed *transparently*.
2. **Scheduling Decisions:** Holds priority, accounting data, and state — the scheduler reads PCB fields to pick the next Ready process.
3. **Resource Management:** Tracks open files, allocated memory, owned semaphores, and I/O devices. Used by the kernel to clean up resources at termination.
4. **Inter-Process Relationships:** Stores parent and child PIDs to maintain the process tree and enable `wait()` for parent-child synchronization.

In RTOS like FreeRTOS, the equivalent structure is the **Task Control Block (TCB)** — a leaner version since embedded tasks typically share address space and have minimal resource needs.

---

**(b) Explain the Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) algorithm. Given three periodic real-time processes with the following parameters, test for schedulability using the Liu–Layland utilization bound. If the sufficient test fails, perform exact response time analysis to confirm whether the system is schedulable. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

| Process | Execution Time $C_i$ | Period $T_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 1 ms | 4 ms |
| $P_2$ | 1 ms | 5 ms |
| $P_3$ | 2 ms | 10 ms |

**Model Solution:**

**Rate Monotonic Scheduling Algorithm:**

RMS is a static-priority, preemptive scheduling algorithm. The priority of each periodic process is assigned **inversely proportional to its period**: the process with the **shortest period gets the highest priority**. The algorithm is optimal among all static-priority algorithms for preemptive scheduling of independent, periodic processes on a uniprocessor.

**Step 1: Compute total utilization.**

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10}
$$

$$
U = 0.2500 + 0.2000 + 0.2000 = 0.6500
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Liu–Layland bound for $n = 3$.**

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot \left(2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798
$$

**Step 3: Compare.**

$$
U = 0.6500 \le U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798
$$

The sufficient test **passes**. Therefore, the process set is **guaranteed to be schedulable under RMS** without needing exact response time analysis.

**Step 4: Verification by exact analysis (optional, for full marks).**

For $P_3$ (lowest priority, $T_3 = 10$):

Iteration 1: $t^{(0)} = 2$
Iteration 2: $t^{(1)} = 2 + \lceil 2/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 2/5 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4$
Iteration 3: $t^{(2)} = 2 + \lceil 4/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 4/5 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4$

Convergence: $W_3^* = 4$ ms. Since $W_3^* = 4 \le T_3 = 10$ ms, the deadline is met with **6 ms of slack**.

**Conclusion:** The system is schedulable under RMS.

> (7 marks: 2 marks for RMS explanation, 3 marks for utilization bound calculation, 2 marks for conclusion and interpretation.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) Explain the concept of context switching. Describe the steps involved in a context switch with a suitable diagram. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A **context switch** is the kernel procedure of saving the state (context) of the currently running process and loading the state of the next process to run, such that execution can resume seamlessly. It is the fundamental mechanism that enables multiprogramming and preemption.

**Steps in a Context Switch:**

1. **Trigger:** A context switch is triggered by one of: (a) a timer interrupt (quantum expiry), (b) a system call (`yield()`, `sleep()`), (c) an I/O interrupt signalling completion of a blocked process, or (d) a higher-priority process becoming Ready.

2. **Save context of old process (Process A):** The kernel pushes CPU registers (PC, SP, general-purpose registers, status flags) from hardware registers **into Process A's PCB** in kernel memory.

3. **Scheduler runs:** The scheduler examines the Ready queue, applies its policy (priority, EDF, round-robin), and selects Process B as the next process to run.

4. **Update process states:** The state field in PCB-A is changed from `Running` to `Ready` (or `Waiting` if it voluntarily blocked). The state field in PCB-B is changed from `Ready` to `Running`.

5. **Load context of new process (Process B):** The kernel copies the register values **from Process B's PCB** into the actual CPU registers, including the Program Counter — so the CPU fetches its next instruction from where Process B last stopped.

6. **Return from interrupt/syscall:** The kernel returns control to user mode. From the user's perspective, Process B has been running continuously; from the kernel's perspective, an arbitrary number of switches may have occurred.

**Diagram:** Refer to the **Block-Level Functional Architecture in Section 4.2** of these notes.

**Cost of Context Switch:** The time taken is *pure overhead* — no useful work is done. For an ARM Cortex-M4 RTOS, a typical context switch takes **2–5 µs**. The cost $T_{cs}$ must satisfy:

$$
T_{cs} \le 0.01 \times T_{deadline}
$$

---

**(b) Compare Preemptive and Non-Preemptive scheduling. A system has five processes with arrival times and burst times as given below. Compute the average waiting time and average turnaround time using: (i) FCFS, (ii) Non-Preemptive SJF, (iii) Preemptive SJF (SRTF). (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

| Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 4 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 2 |
| P4 | 3 | 5 |
| P5 | 4 | 1 |

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison: Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive Scheduling**

| Aspect | Non-Preemptive | Preemptive |
|---|---|---|
| CPU Release | Process voluntarily releases (I/O, exit, terminate) | Forced release on quantum expiry / higher-priority arrival |
| Response Time | Poor for long processes | Excellent — short jobs get fast response |
| Suitability for RTOS | Not suitable (cannot meet hard deadlines) | Essential for hard real-time |
| Complexity | Simple scheduler | Needs timer interrupt + context switch logic |
| Example Algorithms | FCFS, Non-preemptive SJF, Priority | SRTF, Round-Robin, RMS, EDF |

**(i) FCFS — Order of execution: P1 → P2 → P3 → P4 → P5**

- Completion Times: $CT = \{4, 7, 9, 14, 15\}$
- Turnaround Time $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$: $\{4, 6, 7, 11, 11\}$
- Waiting Time $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$: $\{0, 3, 5, 6, 10\}$
- Average $WT = (0+3+5+6+10)/5 = 24/5 = 4.8$ ms
- Average $TAT = (4+6+7+11+11)/5 = 39/5 = 7.8$ ms

**(ii) Non-Preemptive SJF — At $t=0$, only P1 is here. After P1 completes at $t=4$, shortest BT among arrived: P5(1), P3(2), P2(3) → order P5, P3, P2. Then P4.**

- Order: P1(0–4), P5(4–5), P3(5–7), P2(7–10), P4(10–15)
- $CT = \{4, 10, 7, 15, 5\}$
- $TAT = CT - AT = \{4, 9, 5, 12, 1\}$
- $WT = TAT - BT = \{0, 6, 3, 7, 0\}$
- Average $WT = 16/5 = 3.2$ ms
- Average $TAT = 31/5 = 6.2$ ms

**(iii) Preemptive SJF (SRTF):** A new arrival preempts if its BT < remaining of current.

- $t=0$: P1 runs. Remaining P1=4. $t=1$: P2 arrives (BT=3 < 4) → preempt. P2 runs.
- $t=2$: P3 arrives (BT=2 < P2 remaining 2) → tie, convention: continue. P2 continues.
- Actually at $t=2$, P2 remaining = 2, P3 BT=2. By convention tie goes to current → P2 continues.
- $t=3$: P4 arrives (BT=5 > P2 remaining 1) → P2 continues. P2 completes at $t=4$.
- Now arrived: P3(2), P4(5), P1(remaining 3), P5(1, arrives at 4). At $t=4$, P5 has BT=1 — shortest → P5 runs to $t=5$.
- At $t=5$, P3(BT=2), P4(BT=5), P1(remaining 3). Shortest = P3 → run $t=5$ to $t=7$.
- At $t=7$, P1(rem 3), P4(5). Shortest = P1 → run $t=7$ to $t=10$.
- At $t=10$, P4 → run $t=10$ to $t=15$.

- $CT = \{10, 4, 7, 15, 5\}$
- $TAT = CT - AT = \{10, 3, 5, 12, 1\}$
- $WT = TAT - BT = \{6, 0, 3, 7, 0\}$
- Average $WT = 16/5 = 3.2$ ms
- Average $TAT = 31/5 = 6.2$ ms

> (7 marks: 2 marks comparison, 5 marks split across the three algorithms for computation and final averages.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Do not confuse process and thread.** A process owns memory; a thread shares it. Wrong comparison = 2 marks lost.
> 2. **State diagram arrows must be drawn in the correct direction.** Admit goes *from* New *to* Ready, not vice versa. Wrong direction = 1 mark.
> 3. **In RMS bound, students often forget the ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in the exact analysis. Examiner may deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **In SRTF, you must re-evaluate at every arrival.** A common error is running the initially-shortest job to completion. -2 marks.
> 5. **Always state the unit (ms / µs) in your final answer** for waiting time / turnaround time. -0.5 mark.
> 6. **Mention both $U$ and $U_{bound}$ explicitly** when applying the Liu-Layland test. Just writing the bound formula without comparison = -1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **process** is a *program in execution* — an active entity with its own address space, registers, and PCB.
- The **PCB** stores PID, state, PC, registers, memory info, scheduling info, open files, and pointers to parent/child.
- The **5-state model** is: **New → Ready → Running → Waiting → Terminated**, with cycles back to Ready from Running (preempt) and from Waiting (event).
- **Only one process per CPU core** can be in the Running state at any given instant.
- **Context switch** = save old PCB + scheduler picks next + load new PCB. Time cost = 1–10 µs typical.
- **Process vs Thread:** separate address space vs shared address space; heavy vs light creation; slower vs faster context switch.
- **Scheduling algorithms:** FCFS (simple, convoy effect), SJF/SRTF (optimal avg WT but unpredictable), RR (fair, tunable quantum), Priority (starvation risk), RMS (static, optimal among fixed-priority), EDF (dynamic, optimal overall).
- **Liu–Layland RMS bound:** $U \le n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$. Limit as $n \to \infty$ is $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$.
- **Key turnaround time formula:** $TAT = CT - AT$; **Waiting time:** $WT = TAT - BT$; **Response time:** $RT = \text{first-run time} - AT$.
- In **FreeRTOS**, `xTaskCreate()` spawns a task (lightweight thread), not a heavy process — no separate address space.
- A **zombie process** has terminated but its PCB still exists until the parent calls `wait()` — relevant for embedded Linux systems.
- **Preemptive** scheduling is **mandatory** for hard real-time embedded systems (engine control, pacemakers, avionics).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thread (Lightweight Process / Task):**
> A **thread** is the smallest unit of CPU execution and program scheduling that exists within a process. A single process may contain multiple threads that share the same memory space (code, data, and OS resources) but maintain their own independent **thread context** (program counter, registers, and stack).

In the context of **Embedded Systems** and **RTOS (Real-Time Operating Systems)**, a thread is typically referred to as a **Task**. A task is an independent, sequential flow of control that the RTOS kernel schedules for execution on the CPU based on a chosen scheduling algorithm (e.g., Rate Monotonic Scheduling, Earliest Deadline First).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight — Embedded Context:**
> In an RTOS like **FreeRTOS, VxWorks, or uC/OS-II**, threads (tasks) are fundamental building blocks. Each task represents an independent segment of system functionality (e.g., sensor reading, motor control, communication handling).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a **busy restaurant kitchen** as a **process (program)**:

- The **kitchen itself** is the process — it has one shared storage room (shared memory: global variables, heap), one shared recipe book (code section), and one shared pantry (OS resources).
- Each **chef working in that kitchen** is a **thread** — they all look at the same recipe book (code), share the same pantry (data), but each chef has their **own** cutting board, knife, and personal notebook (independent stack, registers, and program counter).

**Key Takeaways from the Analogy:**
- If one chef (thread) is paused mid-recipe, the other chefs can keep cooking — this is **multithreading**.
- All chefs share the same ingredients (shared memory) — this is why **synchronization** (like a "pantry access token" = mutex) is needed to prevent two chefs from grabbing the last tomato at the same time.
- The head chef (RTOS scheduler) decides which chef works on which dish next based on priority.

---

## 1.3 Process vs Thread — A Quick Comparison

| Feature | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Space | Independent address space | Shares parent's address space |
| Creation Cost | **Heavy** (system call, memory allocation) | **Light** (fewer resource allocations) |
| Communication | IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory) | Shared global variables (easier but risky) |
| Context Switch | Expensive (TLB flush, page table switch) | Cheap (only register/stack switch) |
| Failure Isolation | One crash does not affect others | One bug can corrupt the entire process |
| Analogy | Two separate restaurants | Two chefs in the same kitchen |

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Thread Context:** The set of CPU registers, program counter, and stack pointer unique to each thread.
> - **TCB (Thread Control Block):** The kernel data structure that stores a thread's state, priority, stack pointer, and register context. (Equivalent to TCB in many RTOS documentation.)
> - **Concurrency:** Logical simultaneity — multiple threads appear to run at the same time.
> - **Parallelism:** Physical simultaneity — multiple threads execute on multiple CPU cores truly at the same time.
> - **Thread Pool:** A pre-allocated group of worker threads waiting to be assigned tasks, avoiding frequent creation/destruction overhead.

---

## 1.5 Types of Threads

1. **User-Level Threads (ULT):** Managed entirely by a user-space thread library (e.g., GNU Pth). The kernel is unaware of them. Fast to create but cannot leverage multiple cores.
2. **Kernel-Level Threads (KLT):** Managed directly by the OS kernel. Each is a separately schedulable entity (e.g., Linux `pthread`, Windows threads). Can run in true parallel on multicore systems.
3. **Hybrid Threads (M:N Model):** Maps M user threads to N kernel threads (e.g., Go's goroutine runtime, Solaris threads). Combines flexibility with hardware parallelism.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thread Lifecycle (Operational States)

A thread, like a process, moves through a well-defined set of states. Understanding these states is critical for KTU theory and scheduling questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Thread States (KTU 2024 High-Yield):**

1. **New (Created):** The thread object is allocated, the TCB is initialized, but execution has not yet begun.
2. **Ready (Runnable):** The thread is loaded and waiting in the ready queue for the CPU scheduler to assign it execution time.
3. **Running:** The thread has been dispatched and is actively executing on the CPU.
4. **Blocked (Waiting):** The thread voluntarily yields the CPU because it is waiting for some event (I/O completion, mutex acquisition, semaphore signal, delay timeout).
5. **Terminated (Dead):** The thread has finished its `thread_function()` and its resources (TCB, stack) are pending cleanup.

### State Transition Logic — "Why" and "How"

- **New → Ready:** The RTOS `create()` / `pthread_create()` call successfully allocates the stack and TCB. The thread is "born" but not yet chosen by the scheduler.
- **Ready → Running:** The scheduler (e.g., Round Robin, Priority-based) dispatches this thread. **Preemption** can move it back to Ready if a higher-priority thread becomes runnable.
- **Running → Blocked:** The thread calls a blocking API like `sem_wait()`, `mutex_lock()` (when unavailable), `task_delay()`, or a synchronous I/O function.
- **Blocked → Ready:** The awaited event occurs — semaphore is signaled, mutex is released, or the delay timeout expires. An interrupt service routine (ISR) can also unblock a thread.
- **Running → Terminated:** The thread function returns. The kernel marks the TCB as dead, and `pthread_join()` collects the exit status.

---

## 2.2 Multithreading Models

| Model | Description | Used By |
|---|---|---|
| **Many-to-One (M:1)** | Many user threads → one kernel thread. Thread management in user space; if one thread blocks, all block. | Green threads (older JVM) |
| **One-to-One (1:1)** | Each user thread maps to one kernel thread. True parallelism, but thread creation is expensive. | Linux `pthread`, Windows |
| **Many-to-Many (M:N)** | M user threads multiplexed onto N kernel threads (N ≤ M). Best of both worlds. | Solaris, Go runtime |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Embedded RTOS Thread Math (High-Yield for KTU Numerical Questions):**

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU Utilization Bound (Rate Monotonic) | $U = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | dimensionless | For $n$ periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS). |
| CPU Utilization (Actual) | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | dimensionless | $C_i$ = computation time, $T_i$ = period of task $i$. |
| Schedulability Test (RMS) | $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | dimensionless | If true, all deadlines are met. |
| Response Time | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | time units | Smallest fixed point solution; $hp(i)$ = higher-priority tasks. |
| Thread Context Switch Time | $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{load} + T_{scheduler}$ | microseconds ($\mu s$) | Must be bounded for hard real-time systems. |
| Stack Size Estimate | $S_{stack} = S_{funcs} + S_{locals} + S_{ISR} + S_{margin}$ | bytes | Critical to prevent stack overflow in embedded systems. |
| Number of Threads (Concurrency) | $N_{threads} = \lceil \frac{T_{total}}{T_{min}} \rceil$ | integer | Approximate count based on worst-case timing. |

> **Special Notation Rule:** All absolute values and ceilings are written as `\vert x \vert` and `\lceil x \rceil` to prevent markdown table breakage.

---

## 2.4 Why Threads Matter in Embedded Engineering

- **Responsiveness:** A foreground thread handles user input (touch, button) while a background thread manages a slow sensor read — neither starves the other.
- **Modularity:** Each subsystem (motor, display, communication) can be its own task, improving code clarity and maintainability.
- **Hardware Concurrency:** On multicore embedded SoCs (e.g., ARM Cortex-A series), threads can literally run in parallel, multiplying throughput.
- **Real-Time Guarantees:** Properly prioritized tasks with bounded execution times allow **hard real-time** deadlines to be provably met.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Automotive ECUs (e.g., Engine Control Unit, ABS), drones (flight controller + camera + telemetry), IoT gateways (MQTT + OTA + sensing), and medical devices (pacemakers) all rely on thread-based RTOS architectures to guarantee timely response.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) CPU Utilization Bound

The **Liu & Layland bound** (1973) is one of the most cited results in real-time systems and is a guaranteed KTU question topic.

### Step-by-Step Derivation of $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$

**Assumption 1 (RMS):** Tasks are periodic with period $T_i$, fixed priority inversely proportional to period (shorter period = higher priority).

**Step 1 — Set up the critical instant.**
A critical instant is the worst-case release time of all tasks (all start at $t = 0$). For a task $i$ to miss its deadline, the processor demand from higher-priority tasks must be maximum.

**Step 2 — Derive worst-case response time.**
For task $i$, the total computation demand from itself + all higher-priority tasks within interval $[0, t]$ is:

$$
W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

*Logic:* In any interval of length $t$, each higher-priority task $j$ can release at most $\lceil t / T_j \rceil$ jobs, each demanding $C_j$ units.

**Step 3 — Find the smallest fixed point.**
The actual response time $R_i$ is the smallest $t$ satisfying $W_i(t) = t$:

$$
R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

*Logic:* We solve iteratively: start with $t_0 = C_i$, then $t_{k+1} = W_i(t_k)$ until convergence.

**Step 4 — Specialize to the bound proof.**
Liu & Layland showed that for $n$ tasks where priorities are assigned by RMS, the worst-case utilization for schedulability is:

$$
U_{bound} = n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

This is obtained by maximizing the ratio $\sum C_i / T_i$ subject to the constraint that $W_i(T_i) \le T_i$ for all $i$. The derivation uses a **geometric series** argument and the observation that the worst case occurs when $T_i = 2 C_i$ for all $i$ (i.e., each task utilizes 50% of its period in a harmonic structure).

**Step 5 — Evaluate the bound at key values of $n$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{bound}(n=1) &= 1 \cdot (2^{1} - 1) = 1.0000 \\
U_{bound}(n=2) &= 2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) = 2 \cdot (1.4142 - 1) = 0.8284 \\
U_{bound}(n=3) &= 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798 \\
U_{bound}(n=4) &= 4 \cdot (2^{1/4} - 1) = 4 \cdot (1.1892 - 1) = 0.7568 \\
U_{bound}(n \to \infty) &= \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The bound tightens (decreases) as the number of tasks increases, but always remains above $\ln 2 \approx 69.31\%$. This is a **sufficient** (not necessary) test — a system may still be schedulable even if $U > U_{bound}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Style

**Problem:** Three periodic tasks are scheduled under RMS in an embedded RTOS. Compute the total utilization and check if the system is schedulable.

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Computation Time $C_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 10 | 2 |
| $\tau_2$ | 20 | 5 |
| $\tau_3$ | 40 | 5 |

**Step 1 — Compute individual utilizations:**

$$
u_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.200
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{5}{20} = 0.250
$$

$$
u_3 = \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{5}{40} = 0.125
$$

**Step 2 — Total utilization:**

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} u_i = 0.200 + 0.250 + 0.125 = 0.575
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Liu-Layland bound for $n = 3$:**

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798
$$

**Step 4 — Compare:**

$$
U = 0.575 \le U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{System is SCHEDULABLE under RMS.}
$$

*Logic:* Since total utilization lies below the theoretical bound, all three tasks will meet their deadlines.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Thread Creation API Comparison

| API / Function | RTOS Context | Blocking Behavior | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pthread_create()` | POSIX (Linux) | Returns immediately | `0` on success, error code on failure |
| `xTaskCreate()` | FreeRTOS | Returns immediately | `pdPASS` / `pdFAIL` |
| `OSTaskCreate()` | uC/OS-II | Returns immediately | Error code |
| `task_create()` | VxWorks | Returns immediately | Task ID or `ERROR` |
| `CreateThread()` | Windows | Returns immediately | Thread handle |

> **Note for KTU:** You may be asked to **compare thread APIs** across different RTOS — focus on the parameter differences (priority, stack size, function pointer) rather than memorizing syntax.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Thread Lifecycle State Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateNew : xTaskCreate / pthread_create
    stateNew --> stateReady : Scheduler admits
    stateReady --> stateRunning : Dispatcher selects
    stateRunning --> stateReady : Preempted by higher priority
    stateRunning --> stateBlocked : mutex_lock / sem_wait / delay
    stateRunning --> stateTerminated : Function returns
    stateBlocked --> stateReady : Event signaled / timeout
    stateTerminated --> [*] : Resources released

    note right of stateBlocked : I/O wait, semaphore wait, mutex wait
    note right of stateRunning : Actively on CPU
```

## 4.2 Process vs Thread Memory Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph procA[Process A - Independent]
        codeA[Code Segment]
        dataA[Data Segment]
        heapA[Heap]
        tcbA1[TCB Thread 1]
        stackA1[Stack Thread 1]
        tcbA2[TCB Thread 2]
        stackA2[Stack Thread 2]
    end

    subgraph procB[Process B - Independent]
        codeB[Code Segment]
        dataB[Data Segment]
        heapB[Heap]
        tcbB1[TCB Thread 1]
        stackB1[Stack Thread 1]
    end

    note1[Shared within process: Code, Data, Heap, OS resources]
    note2[Per-thread unique: Stack, Registers, Program Counter, TCB]

    style procA fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5
    style procB fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00
```

## 4.3 Multithreading Model Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph m1[Many-to-One M:1]
        u1a[User Thread 1]
        u1b[User Thread 2]
        u1c[User Thread 3]
        k1[Kernel Thread 1]
        u1a --> k1
        u1b --> k1
        u1c --> k1
    end

    subgraph m2[One-to-One 1:1]
        u2a[User Thread 1]
        u2b[User Thread 2]
        k2a[Kernel Thread 1]
        k2b[Kernel Thread 2]
        u2a --> k2a
        u2b --> k2b
    end

    subgraph m3[Many-to-Many M:N]
        u3a[User Thread 1]
        u3b[User Thread 2]
        u3c[User Thread 3]
        k3a[Kernel Thread 1]
        k3b[Kernel Thread 2]
        u3a --> k3a
        u3b --> k3b
        u3c --> k3a
    end
```

## 4.4 RTOS Thread Scheduling Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    idle[Idle Task Lowest Priority] --> schedCheck{Scheduler Tick?}
    readyQ[Ready Queue] --> schedCheck
    isr[ISR] --> wakeBlocked[Unblock Waiting Threads]
    wakeBlocked --> readyQ
    schedCheck -->|Yes| pickHighest[Pick Highest Priority Thread]
    pickHighest --> saveCtx[Save Current Context to TCB]
    saveCtx --> loadCtx[Load New Context from TCB]
    loadCtx --> runTask[Execute Selected Thread]
    runTask --> blocked{Thread blocks or finishes?}
    blocked -->|Block| addWaitQ[Add to Wait Queue]
    blocked -->|Finish| delTask[Delete TCB Free Stack]
    addWaitQ --> schedCheck
    delTask --> schedCheck

    style idle fill:#F5F5F5
    style isr fill:#FFCDD2
    style readyQ fill:#C8E6C9
    style pickHighest fill:#FFF9C4
```

## 4.5 Thread Synchronization Interaction Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    t1[Task A High Priority] --|acquire| mutex[Mutex Lock]
    t2[Task B Low Priority] --|acquire| mutex
    t3[Task C Medium Priority] -.->|preempts| t2
    mutex -.->|blocks| t1
    mutex -.->|blocks| t2
    sem[Binary Semaphore] -->|signals| t4[ISR Handler]
    t4 -->|unblocks| t5[Waiting Task]

    note3[Priority Inversion: Low pri holds lock, Med preempts, High blocked]

    style t1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style t2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style t3 fill:#BBDEFB
    style sem fill:#FFE0B2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define a thread. How is it different from a process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **thread** is the smallest unit of execution within a process. Multiple threads within the same process share the address space (code, data, heap, OS resources) but maintain their own stack, registers, and program counter.

| Aspect | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Independent address space | Shares parent's address space |
| Context switch | Expensive (page table, TLB) | Cheap (register/stack only) |
| Communication | IPC mechanisms needed | Direct shared memory access |
| Creation overhead | High | Low (lightweight) |

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — process definition, 1 mark — table of differences)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any three thread states and explain the Ready state.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three thread states are: **New**, **Ready**, **Running**, **Blocked**, and **Terminated**. *(1 mark for listing any three)*

**Ready State:** A thread is in the Ready state when it has been created, all its resources (TCB, stack) are allocated, and it is waiting in the ready queue for the CPU scheduler to assign it execution time. *(2 marks for explanation)* The thread is eligible to run but the CPU is currently allocated to another thread of equal or higher priority. A transition from Ready → Running occurs only when the scheduler dispatches the thread.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** *thread* with *process* in long answers — examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this.
> - In numerical RMS questions, **always show the bound calculation** explicitly; partial marks are given for each step.
> - For 14-mark questions, **diagrams (state diagrams, architecture blocks) are mandatory** and earn 2–3 marks if drawn correctly with labeled transitions.
> - When discussing synchronization, **mention the problem** (race condition, deadlock, priority inversion) **before** the solution (mutex, semaphore) — this signals conceptual depth.
> - Avoid writing "process" when you mean "thread" — KTU paper setters deliberately include this confusion.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the different states of a thread in an RTOS. Discuss the role of the **Thread Control Block (TCB)** during context switching. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider three periodic tasks in an RTOS scheduled using Rate Monotonic Scheduling. Task details:

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Execution Time $C_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 20 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 30 | 6 |
| $\tau_3$ | 60 | 6 |

Check whether the task set is schedulable using the Liu-Layland bound. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Thread States (5 marks):**
1. **New:** Thread is created; TCB and stack are allocated. It is not yet in the ready queue.
2. **Ready:** Thread is loaded and waiting for CPU. It can run as soon as the scheduler picks it.
3. **Running:** Thread is currently executing on the CPU core.
4. **Blocked (Waiting):** Thread voluntarily waits for an event (I/O, semaphore, delay, mutex). It is not consuming CPU.
5. **Terminated:** Thread function has returned; TCB is marked for cleanup.

**TCB Role in Context Switching (2 marks):**
When the scheduler decides to preempt the currently running thread:
1. The kernel **saves** the current thread's CPU registers, program counter, and stack pointer **into its TCB**.
2. The kernel **loads** the TCB of the next highest-priority ready thread onto the CPU registers.
3. Execution resumes in the new thread exactly where it left off.
The TCB thus acts as the **persistent identity** of a thread across context switches.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 state diagram for the visual.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate individual utilizations:**

$$
u_1 = \frac{4}{20} = 0.200, \quad u_2 = \frac{6}{30} = 0.200, \quad u_3 = \frac{6}{60} = 0.100
$$

**Step 2 — Total utilization:**

$$
U = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.100 = 0.500
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Liu-Layland bound for $n = 3$:**

$$
U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

**Step 4 — Schedulability decision:**

$$
U = 0.500 \le U_{bound} = 0.7798
$$

$$
\therefore \text{The task set is SCHEDULABLE under Rate Monotonic Scheduling.}
$$

*(1 mark for utilization values, 1 mark for total, 2 marks for bound computation, 1 mark for comparison + conclusion, 2 marks for setup/justification.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **user-level threads** and **kernel-level threads**. Explain the **many-to-many (M:N) threading model** with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **priority inversion** in an RTOS. How does **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solve this problem? Illustrate with an example. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**User-Level Threads (ULT) vs Kernel-Level Threads (KLT):** *(4 marks)*

| Feature | User-Level Threads | Kernel-Level Threads |
|---|---|---|
