Now $T_L$ runs at priority 5, **preempting $T_M$** (prio 3). $T_L$ finishes critical section faster, releases $M$, restores its original priority. $T_H$ acquires $M$ immediately.

**Bounded inversion time with PIP:**

$$
T_{\text{inversion}}^{\max} = C_{\text{critical\_section}}(T_L)
$$

This is **bounded by the length of the low-priority critical section**, not by medium-priority tasks.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Primitive Selection Guide

| Requirement | Best Primitive | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Protect single shared variable | **Mutex** | Ownership, PIP |
| Coordinate task + ISR | **Binary Semaphore** | Lightweight, no ownership |
| Pool of N identical resources | **Counting Semaphore** | Native count |
| Pass structured data task-to-task | **Message Queue** | FIFO, typed, sized |
| Pass single event/state flag | **Mailbox / Event Flag** | One slot, low overhead |
| Stream of bytes (like a file) | **Pipe** | Byte-oriented, POSIX |
| Wait for ANY of N events | **Event Group (OR)** | Bitwise wait |
| Wait for ALL of N events | **Event Group (AND)** | Bitwise AND wait |
| Producer > Consumer rate, fixed buffer | **Queue** | Decouples timing |
| Cross-core / cross-processor | **Message Queue / Mailbox** | No shared memory assumed |

## 3.5 Deadlock Avoidance — Banker's Algorithm (Simplified)

For $n$ tasks and $m$ resource types, a state is **safe** if there exists a sequence of task completions that allows all tasks to finish. The Banker's algorithm is rarely implemented in embedded firmware but is a **favorite KTU theory question**.

**Safe state condition:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Alloc}_i + \text{Available} \geq \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Max}_i
$$

where $\text{Alloc}_i$ is the resources held by task $i$, $\text{Available}$ is the free pool, and $\text{Max}_i$ is the maximum task $i$ may ever request.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RTOS Task Communication Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ISR["HW Interrupt Service Routine"]:::isr
    T1["Task T1: Sensor Acquisition\nPriority: HIGH"]:::task
    T2["Task T2: Data Processing\nPriority: MEDIUM"]:::task
    T3["Task T3: Actuator Control\nPriority: LOW"]:::task
    T4["Task T4: Logging / Comms\nPriority: BACKGROUND"]:::task

    Q1["Message Queue\nQ1: Sensor Data\nDepth: 10 msgs"]:::queue
    Q2["Message Queue\nQ2: Control Cmds\nDepth: 5 msgs"]:::queue
    MB["Mailbox\nMB: System State\nSize: 1 msg"]:::mailbox
    EG["Event Group\nEG: Flags\nBit-mask: 0xFF"]:::event

    MUT["Mutex M1\nShared Buffer"]:::mutex
    SEM1["Binary Sem\nSEM_SENSOR"]:::sem
    SEM2["Counting Sem\nSEM_BUF Pool=3"]:::sem

    SHM[/"Shared Memory Region\nstruct sensor_data"/]:::shm

    ISR -- "Signal SEM_SENSOR" --> SEM1
    SEM1 -- "Wake on release" --> T1
    T1 -- "Post msg" --> Q1
    T1 -- "Acquire M1" --> MUT
    MUT -- "Lock guard" --> SHM
    T1 -- "Release M1" --> MUT
    T1 -- "Set bit" --> EG

    Q1 -- "Pend msg" --> T2
    T2 -- "Post msg" --> Q2
    T2 -- "Pend flags" --> EG
    T2 -- "Wait SEM_BUF" --> SEM2
    SEM2 -- "Buffer available" --> T2

    Q2 -- "Pend msg" --> T3
    T3 -- "Post state" --> MB
    T3 -- "Acquire M1" --> MUT
    T3 -- "Release M1" --> MUT

    MB -- "Read state" --> T4

    classDef isr fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef task fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef queue fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mailbox fill:#e5ccff,stroke:#4b0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef event fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mutex fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sem fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef shm fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mutual Exclusion State Diagram (Mutex Lifecycle)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unlocked: MutexCreate()
    Unlocked --> Locked: Task T1 calls Wait Mutex\n(MUTEX.owner = T1)
    Locked --> Unlocked: Task T1 calls Post Mutex\n(MUTEX.owner = NONE)
    Locked --> Locked: Task T2 calls Wait Mutex\nT2 added to wait queue
    Unlocked --> Locked: T2 promoted to owner\nT2 removed from wait queue
    Locked --> [*]: MutexDelete()
    note right of Locked
        Priority Inheritance active
        if higher prio task waits
    end note
```

## 4.3 Producer–Consumer with Two Counting Semaphores

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Producer Task
    participant SF as Sem Free (init=5)
    participant BUF as Shared Buffer (N=5)
    participant SO as Sem Occupied (init=0)
    participant C as Consumer Task

    P->>SF: P(S_free) [block if S_free=0]
    SF-->>P: granted (S_free=4)
    P->>BUF: write item i
    P->>SO: V(S_occ) (S_occ=1)
    Note over P: continues immediately

    C->>SO: P(S_occ) [granted S_occ=0]
    C->>BUF: read item i
    C->>SF: V(S_free) (S_free=5)
    Note over C: continues with item i
```

## 4.4 Priority Inversion Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Priority Inversion Without PIP
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S
    section Task H (prio 5)
    Waiting on M       :crit, h1, 0, 9
    section Task M (prio 3)
    Running independently :m1, 1, 8
    section Task L (prio 1)
    Holds M, then preempted :active, l1, 0, 1
    Resumes, holds M, blocked by H :active, l2, 9, 10
    Releases M            :l3, 10, 11
```

```mermaid
gantt
    title With Priority Inheritance Protocol
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S
    section Task H (prio 5)
    Waiting on M       :crit, h1, 1, 3
    section Task M (prio 3)
    Preempted by L     :done, m1, 1, 3
    section Task L (prio 1)
    Holds M, preempted by H, then inherits H prio :active, l1, 1, 3
    Releases M            :l2, 3, 4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between Binary Semaphore and Mutex. Why is a Mutex preferred for protecting a critical section?  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Binary Semaphore | Mutex |
|---|---|---|
| Ownership | None — any task can signal | Strict — only owner can release |
| Priority Inheritance | Not supported | Supported (PIP) |
| Suitable Use | Signaling / event indication | Critical section protection |

> A Mutex is preferred for critical sections because it has **ownership semantics** (the same task that locks it must unlock it) and supports **Priority Inheritance Protocol**, which prevents unbounded priority inversion — a serious problem in real-time systems like automotive ECUs and flight controllers. A binary semaphore used for the same purpose risks a task unlocking a lock it never acquired, leading to corrupted mutual exclusion.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark table, 1 mark PIP reasoning — full marks.)*

### Q2. Define a Message Queue. List any four operations supported on a message queue. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Message Queue** is a kernel-managed FIFO buffer that allows tasks to exchange fixed-size messages asynchronously, decoupling producer and consumer timing.

Four operations:
1. `OSQCreate()` — Create a queue of given depth & message size.
2. `OSQPost()` / `OSQSend()` — Append a message at the tail.
3. `OSQPend()` / `OSQReceive()` — Dequeue a message from the head (blocking).
4. `OSQAccept()` / `OSQFlush()` — Non-blocking peek / clear queue.

*(1 mark definition + 4 ops × 0.5 = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Track 1

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the **producer–consumer problem** and solve it using **two counting semaphores** and a **mutex**. Write the pseudo-code and prove the correctness using an invariant. **\[7 Marks\]**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Model Solution:**

The producer–consumer problem is the canonical inter-task communication problem: a **producer** task generates data items and a **consumer** task processes them, sharing a bounded buffer of size $N$. Direct access leads to **race conditions** and **buffer overflow/underflow**.

**Shared variables & primitives:**

- Buffer `B[N]`
- Index `in = 0` (producer write pointer)
- Index `out = 0` (consumer read pointer)
- `Semaphore full = 0` (count of occupied slots)
- `Semaphore empty = N` (count of free slots)
- `Mutex m = 1` (protect access to B, in, out)

**Pseudo-code:**

```
Producer Task:                       Consumer Task:
  while (1) {                          while (1) {
    produce_item(item);                  P(full);
    P(empty);                            P(m);
    P(m);                                item = B[out];
    B[in] = item;                        out = (out + 1) % N;
    in = (in + 1) % N;                   V(m);
    V(m);                                V(empty);
    V(full);                             consume_item(item);
  }                                     }
```

**Correctness via invariant:**

$$
\text{INVARIANT:} \quad 0 \le \text{full} + \text{empty} \le N \quad \land \quad \text{full} = \#\text{items in B}
$$

- **Initialization:** full=0, empty=N. Invariant holds: $0+0 \le N$ ✓, full=0, empty=N ✓.
- **Producer step:** $P(\text{empty})$ decrements empty only if empty > 0; $V(\text{full})$ increments full. Sum preserved ✓.
- **Consumer step:** $P(\text{full})$ decrements full only if full > 0; $V(\text{empty})$ increments empty. Sum preserved ✓.
- **Mutual exclusion** of buffer indices is guaranteed by mutex $m$.

**No deadlock:** If a task is blocked, the corresponding counter is 0, so the other counter is N, ensuring the other side can always run.

**No starvation (bounded wait):** Standard FIFO wait queue inside semaphore implementation. *(Full marks = 7: 1 problem, 1 vars, 2 code, 2 invariant, 1 conclusion.)*

---

**Q.A. (b)** With a neat diagram, explain **priority inversion** and how the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solves it. Mention a real-world incident caused by this bug. **\[7 Marks\]**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Priority Inversion** is a scheduling anomaly where a high-priority task is **indirectly preempted by a lower-priority task** — effectively "inverting" the relative priorities — due to a shared mutex.

**Scenario (without PIP):** Tasks $T_H$ (prio 5), $T_M$ (prio 3), $T_L$ (prio 1) share mutex $M$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_0 &: T_L \text{ acquires } M \\
t_1 &: T_H \text{ preempts, runs, blocks on } M \\
t_2 &: T_M \text{ preempts } T_H \text{ (prio 3 > prio 1)} \\
t_3\text{–}t_{10} &: T_M \text{ runs for 8 ms (unrelated to } M) \\
t_{11} &: T_M \text{ finishes; } T_L \text{ resumes, releases } M \\
t_{12} &: T_H \text{ finally acquires } M
\end{aligned}
$$

Total inversion time = 8 ms, **unbounded** because $T_M$ could have been any length.

**Real-world incident:** **NASA Mars Pathfinder (1997)** — the spacecraft experienced unexplained system resets on Mars. Root cause: priority inversion in the bus-management task. Fix: enable PIP in VxWorks (already in code, just needed to be turned on remotely).

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):**

- When task $T_i$ blocks on a mutex held by $T_j$, **temporarily raise $T_j$'s priority to $\text{Priority}(T_i)$** (if higher).
- $T_j$ then runs at the inherited high priority, **preempting any medium-priority tasks**, finishes its critical section, releases the mutex, and **restores its original priority**.

**With PIP**, in the above scenario, at $t_1$, $T_L$'s priority is raised to 5, preempting $T_M$. $T_L$ releases $M$ in $\sim 1$ ms. $T_H$ then runs. Inversion time bounded by $T_L$'s critical section length.

**Bounded inversion with PIP:**

$$
T_{\text{inversion}}^{\max} = C_{\text{crit}}(T_L) \le T_{\text{deadline}}(T_H)
$$

*(1 mark scenario, 1 mark timeline, 1 mark real incident, 2 marks PIP mechanics, 1 mark diagram reference, 1 mark bound.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Track 2

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the **classification of task communication primitives** in RTOS. Compare **shared memory** vs **message passing** approaches with examples. **\[7 Marks\]**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

Task communication primitives in an RTOS are classified into two broad paradigms:

**1. Shared Memory Communication**
Tasks read/write a common global memory region. The kernel does NOT copy data — tasks access the same physical bytes. **Fastest** method but requires explicit synchronization.

Examples: global `struct sensor_data`, hardware registers mapped in memory, DMA buffers.

**2. Message Passing Communication**
The kernel acts as an intermediary, copying a message from sender's address space to a kernel buffer (queue/mailbox/pipe) and then to receiver's address space. **Safer**, no race conditions on data payload, works across processor cores.

Examples: FreeRTOS `xQueueSend()`, POSIX pipes, µC/OS-II `OSMboxPost()`.

**Comparative table:**

| Criterion | Shared Memory | Message Passing |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Fastest (no copy) | Slower (kernel copy overhead) |
| Safety | Error-prone (race conditions) | Safer (atomic transfer) |
| Cross-processor | No (needs shared RAM) | Yes |
| Synchronization | Manual (mutex, critical section) | Built-in (blocking) |
| Code complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Real-time latency | Predictable, minimal | $t_{\text{copy}} + t_{\text{schedule}}$ |
| Typical use | High-speed DSP buffers, ISR↔task | Inter-task command passing, IPC |

**Conclusion:** For latency-critical paths (e.g., ISR to control task), **shared memory with mutex** is preferred. For modular, decoupled, multi-core systems, **message passing** is preferred.

*(2 marks classification, 3 marks comparison table, 2 marks example + conclusion.)*

---

**Q.B. (b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **architecture of a message queue** in an RTOS. Explain the `OSQPost()` and `OSQPend()` operations and discuss what happens when the queue is **full** or **empty**. **\[7 Marks\]**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**Model Solution:**

A **Message Queue** is a kernel object comprising a circular FIFO buffer of fixed-size message slots, plus a **head pointer** (read index), a **tail pointer** (write index), a **count** semaphore, and a **free-slot** semaphore.

**Architecture diagram (logical):**

```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|                   MESSAGE QUEUE OBJECT                       |
|                                                              |
|   in (tail)  --->  [ msg0 ][ msg1 ][ msg2 ][ ... ][ msgN-1 ] |
|                      ^                                       |
|   out (head) -------+                                       |
|                                                              |
|   count_sem  : items currently in queue                      |
|   free_sem   : free slots available                          |
|   msg_size   : bytes per slot                                |
|   max_msgs   : N                                             |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**`OSQPost(queue, msg)` (Producer side):**

1. Atomically `P(free_sem)`. If `free_sem == 0` and `OSQPost()` is **blocking**, the task is suspended and added to the queue's send-wait list. If **non-blocking**, returns error `OS_ERR_QUEUE_FULL`.
2. Disable interrupts briefly.
3. Copy `msg` into the buffer slot at index `in`.
4. Update `in = (in + 1) mod max_msgs`.
5. Re-enable interrupts.
6. `V(count_sem)` — wake one receiver if any are waiting.

**`OSQPend(queue, timeout, err)` (Consumer side):**

1. If `timeout = 0` (wait forever): block on `count_sem`.
2. If `count_sem == 0` and timeout > 0, task suspended on receive-wait list; OS decrements a tick timer.
3. On semaphore grant, disable interrupts briefly.
4. Copy buffer slot at index `out` into caller's `msg` buffer.
5. Update `out = (out + 1) mod max_msgs`.
6. Re-enable interrupts.
7. `V(free_sem)` — wake one sender if any waiting.
8. Return `OS_ERR_NONE` or timeout error.

**Behavior summary:**

| Condition | OSQPost() behavior | OSQPend() behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Queue has free slot | Appends, returns OK | — |
| Queue full, blocking | Suspends sender task | — |
| Queue full, non-blocking | Returns `OS_ERR_QUEUE_FULL` | — |
| Queue empty | — | Blocks (or returns `OS_ERR_TIMEOUT`) |
| Queue has messages | — | Dequeues, returns OK |

**Why this matters in real-time:** Choosing the right **queue depth** $N$ trades off between **memory cost** and **blocking probability**. If producer rate $r_p$ exceeds consumer rate $r_c$ for sustained periods, the queue will fill and the producer will block — defeating the asynchronous decoupling purpose. Rule of thumb: $N \ge (r_p - r_c) \cdot t_{\text{burst}}$.

*(1 mark diagram, 2 marks post op, 2 marks pend op, 2 marks full/empty table.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in PBEVT504 Task Communication Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Mutex and Binary Semaphore** — they are NOT the same. If the question says "protect critical section," write **Mutex** + PIP. If the question says "signal between ISR and task," write **Binary Semaphore**. Wrong choice = 2 marks lost.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention atomicity** of `P()` and `V()` operations. The OS guarantees these are uninterruptible. Without this, the entire argument collapses (the increment/decrement itself can race).
>
> 3. **In race-condition questions**, students often say "use a mutex" but **do not state the invariant** (`0 ≤ count ≤ N` or `mutex.owner = ?`). Examiners specifically look for invariants and proof of correctness.
>
> 4. **Priority Inversion** questions: students often **forget to name the real-world incident** (Mars Pathfinder). This is worth 1 mark and is asked frequently.
>
> 5. **In producer–consumer code**, students forget to wrap buffer-index access inside the mutex (only protecting the data item, not `in`/`out`). This is a textbook bug.
>
> 6. **Notation:** KTU expects Dijkstra's `P` and `V`, OR `Wait`/`Signal`. Avoid mixing with `down`/`up` and `acquire`/`release` in the same answer (use one consistent pair).
>
> 7. **Deadlock vs Starvation:** These are different. Deadlock = circular wait, all blocked. Starvation = some task never gets CPU/mutex. Examiners deduct marks for using the terms interchangeably.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Race Condition** = outcome depends on interleaving; caused by unprotected shared data.
- **Critical Section** = code accessing shared resource; must run atomically.
- **Semaphore = counter + P()/V()**, atomic; non-negative.
  - **Binary** (0/1) → signaling.
  - **Counting** (≥0) → resource pool.
  - **Mutex** (1, with ownership + PIP) → critical section.
- **P (Wait/Proberen)**: blocks if counter=0, else decrements.
- **V (Signal/Verhogen)**: increments, wakes a waiter.
- **Message Queue** = FIFO buffer, asynchronous decoupling; ops: Create, Post, Pend, Accept, Flush.
- **Mailbox** = single-slot queue; lightweight event passing.
- **Pipe** = byte-stream oriented (POSIX style).
- **Event Group / Flags** = bitwise waiting (AND/OR conditions).
- **Shared Memory** = fastest but error-prone; needs Mutex.
- **Message Passing** = safer, kernel copies; cross-core friendly.
- **Priority Inversion** = H blocked behind L, M preempts → unbounded delay.
- **PIP** = inheritor runs at waiter's priority → bounds inversion.
- **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** = alternative: each mutex has a ceiling = highest prio of any task using it; task enters CS at ceiling, blocks all others.
- **Deadlock** needs 4 Coffman conditions: Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait.
- **Banker's Algorithm** = safe-state check; rarely in firmware, common in exams.
- **Mars Pathfinder (1997)** = the canonical priority-inversion real-world case.
- **ISR ↔ Task communication** → use `fromISR()` variants (FreeRTOS) and give semaphore **from ISR**, not mutex.
- **Queue depth $N$** must be sized to absorb producer-consumer rate mismatch: $N \ge (r_p - r_c) \cdot t_{\text{burst}}$.
- **Atomicity** of P/V is the foundation; never question it.
- **Mutual Exclusion ≠ Bounded Waiting ≠ Progress** — three distinct correctness conditions (Dijkstra).
- **OSQPend timeout** = 0 means wait forever; nonzero = tick-based timeout.
- **Disabling interrupts** is the cheapest critical-section mechanism but **must be brief** (≤ a few microseconds) or it kills interrupt latency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Choosing an RTOS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Choosing an RTOS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Choosing an RTOS (Real-Time Operating System)** is the systematic engineering decision-making process of evaluating, comparing, and selecting a real-time kernel that best satisfies the functional, temporal, resource, and economic constraints of a target embedded application. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this decision is treated as a **trade-off optimization problem** between deterministic behavior, footprint, performance, middleware availability, tooling, licensing, and long-term maintainability.

An RTOS is fundamentally defined (per the standard KTU textbook terminology aligned with Liu & Layland and Tanenbaum's definitions) as an operating system whose correctness depends not only on the **logical result** of a computation but also on the **time at which the result is delivered**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Under Module 4, the topic "Choosing an RTOS" requires the student to:
> 1. Enumerate the *features* an ideal RTOS must expose to the application.
> 2. Compare *commercial* vs *open-source* RTOS offerings.
> 3. Justify the *selection* of a specific RTOS for a given embedded scenario.
> 4. Understand the role of *middleware, scheduling, footprint*, and *standards* (POSIX, OSEK/VDX, AUTOSAR) in selection.

## 1.2 The Three Classes of Real-Time Systems

Before choosing an RTOS, the designer must classify the target system into one of three temporal classes — this classification **directly dictates** the class of RTOS that may be used.

| Class | Definition | Deadline Consequence | Example Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hard Real-Time** | Missing a deadline is a **total system failure** (catastrophic) | Loss of life / equipment | Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), Pacemaker, Aircraft Fly-by-Wire |
| **Firm Real-Time** | Late results are **useless**, but no catastrophic damage occurs | Result is discarded, no penalty | Video frame processing where late frames are dropped |
| **Soft Real-Time** | Late results **degrade utility** but are still accepted | Reduced Quality of Service (QoS) | Audio playback, Web servers, User-interface rendering |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Pointer:** A student who *does not classify the system into Hard/Firm/Soft first* loses 2 marks immediately on any 14-mark design question on RTOS selection.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Imagine you are running a **specialized restaurant kitchen**:

- **The Head Chef** = The **Scheduler** of the RTOS. It decides which dish (task) is cooked next.
- **The Kitchen Stations** = **CPU Cores / Hardware Resources**.
- **The Dishes on Order** = **Tasks/Threads** with priorities.
- **The Waiter** = The **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** that preempts the Chef for urgent orders (e.g., a VIP guest arrived).
- **The Recipe Book** = The **Kernel code** (VxWorks, FreeRTOS, QNX, RTLinux).

Now, choosing an RTOS is like asking: *"Which kitchen management system (Head Chef) gives us the least burnt food (deadline misses) given our customer mix, kitchen size, and budget?"* A Hard Real-Time kitchen (an ICU's ventilator controller) **cannot tolerate even one burnt dish**. A Soft Real-Time kitchen (a coffee shop app) tolerates a small delay.

## 1.4 What an "Ideal" RTOS Provides (KTU Standard Checklist)

A textbook-grade RTOS, irrespective of vendor, is expected to expose the following primitives:

1. **Multitasking** with preemptive, priority-based scheduling.
2. **Deterministic interrupt latency** and **task switching latency** (typically **< 10 µs** for premium RTOS).
3. **Small footprint** (often **< 10 KB** ROM for the kernel alone in microcontrollers).
4. **Scalability** — the kernel scales from minimal to full-featured builds.
5. **Portability** — abstract hardware layer (BSP — Board Support Package).
6. **Standard API** (POSIX 1003.1b, µITRON, OSEK/VDX, AUTOSAR OS).
7. **Inter-process communication (IPC)** — semaphores, mutexes, message queues, event flags, mailboxes.
8. **Memory management** — fixed/block memory pools to avoid fragmentation.
9. **Support for networking stacks and middleware** (TCP/IP, USB, CAN, File Systems).
10. **Tooling** — debugger, profiler, trace, performance analyzer.
11. **Certification evidence** — DO-178C (avionics), IEC 61508 (industrial), ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 62304 (medical).

## 1.5 Why a Bare-Metal Super-Loop Is *Not* an RTOS

A common KTU pitfall is conflating "real-time" with "fast." An RTOS is **not defined by speed** but by **determinism (bounded latency)**.

$$L_{max} = \max_{i} (t_{finish,i} - t_{deadline,i})$$

- For a **hard real-time system**, $L_{max} \le 0$ for **all** valid execution paths.
- For a **soft real-time system**, a small positive $L_{max}$ is statistically acceptable.

A bare-metal super-loop cannot guarantee $L_{max} \le 0$ under task interference, hence it fails the formal definition.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven-Pillar Selection Framework (KTU High-Yield)

The selection of an RTOS is not a single-criterion optimization. The KTU 2024 scheme (and standard embedded texts such as *Simon, "An Embedded Software Primer"*, and *Labrosse, "MicroC/OS-II"*) recommends evaluating the candidate kernel against these **seven pillars**. Each pillar contains a set of measurable metrics.

### Pillar 1 — Functional Requirements
- Number of concurrent tasks (typically 16 – 256+).
- IPC mechanisms required (queues, semaphores, mutex with priority inheritance, event groups).
- Middleware: TCP/IP, USB Host, CAN, File system (FAT, NAND).
- Graphics: LVGL, emWin, Qt for MCUs.
- Required language bindings: C, C++, Python (MicroPython), Rust.

### Pillar 2 — Temporal Requirements (The "R" in RTOS)
- **Hard** or **Soft** real-time?
- **Maximum acceptable interrupt latency** $L_{IRQ}^{max}$ (ns/µs).
- **Maximum acceptable task switch time** $T_{ctx}$ (ns/µs).
- **Jitter tolerance** — variation in $T_{ctx}$.
- **Scheduling policy** needed: Fixed Priority Preemptive (FPP), Rate Monotonic (RMS), Earliest Deadline First (EDF), or Round Robin.

### Pillar 3 — Resource Footprint
- **ROM footprint** $M_{ROM}$ (kernel code size, KB).
- **RAM footprint** $M_{RAM}$ (kernel data + per-task TCB + stacks, KB).
- **Per-task TCB (Task Control Block) size** — typically 64 B – 1 KB.
- **Minimum tick resolution** $\Delta t$ (1 ms, 100 µs, 10 µs).

### Pillar 4 — Hardware & Toolchain Support
- **Processor families** supported: ARM Cortex-M/A/R, RISC-V, AVR, PIC, x86, PowerPC.
- **MPU / MMU support** (critical for safety/security isolation).
- **Toolchains**: GCC, IAR, Keil, LLVM, vendor IDE.
- **Debug support**: JTAG, SWD, ETM trace, hardware semihosting.
- **Availability of a BSP** for the target development board.

### Pillar 5 — Standards Compliance
| Standard | Domain | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **POSIX 1003.1b** | General / Unix-like embedded | Portability of code across RTOSes |
| **µITRON** | Japanese embedded market | Mature, lightweight spec |
| **OSEK/VDX** | European automotive (legacy) | Foundation for AUTOSAR |
| **AUTOSAR OS / Classic** | Modern automotive | Scalability classes 1, 2, 3, 4 |
| **ARINC 653** | Avionics | Time & space partitioning |
| **DO-178C** | Avionics certification | Software assurance level A–E |
| **ISO 26262** | Automotive functional safety | ASIL A–D |
| **IEC 61508** | Industrial SIL 1–4 | SIL mapping |
| **IEC 62304 / FDA** | Medical devices | Class A, B, C software |

### Pillar 6 — Commercial & Licensing Factors
- **License model**: royalty per unit, one-time fee, GPL/LGPL, BSD/MIT, dual-license.
- **Royalty cost** $C_{roy}$ (USD per shipped device).
- **NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering)** cost = training + integration.
- **Long-term support and roadmap** of the vendor.
- **Vendor longevity risk** — will the company exist in 10 years?
- **Source code availability** (for certification audits).

### Pillar 7 — Ecosystem, Support & Documentation
- Quality of the **user manual**, **kernel awareness** plugins.
- **Community size** (Stack Overflow, GitHub stars, forums).
- **Vendor support tier** (24×7, business hours, community-only).
- **Training** availability and cost.
- **Third-party middleware vendors** that certify against the RTOS.

## 2.2 The RTOS Feature Checklist Table (Mandatory for KTU Answers)

The KTU board often awards 3–4 marks for a *feature table*. Use the following structure:

| Feature | Why it matters | Hard RTOS Example | Soft RTOS Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Preemptive scheduling** | Guarantees high-priority task runs first | VxWorks, QNX, RTX | FreeRTOS, Linux (PREEMPT_RT) |
| **Priority levels** | Number of distinct priorities | 256 (QNX) | Unlimited (Linux) |
| **Mutex with priority inheritance** | Avoids priority inversion | Yes (almost all hard RTOS) | Often optional |
| **Memory model** | Flat vs. process-isolated | Flat (most MCUs) | MMU-based (Linux) |
| **Tickless idle** | Power saving | Yes (FreeRTOS) | Yes (Linux) |
| **Tick rate** | Granularity of timing | 1 kHz–10 kHz | 100 Hz–1 kHz |
| **IPC: Message queues** | Task-to-task data | Yes | Yes |
| **IPC: Mailboxes** | Fixed-size messages | Yes | Limited |
| **Software timers** | Deferred work | Yes | Yes |
| **Interrupt nesting** | Allows ISR within ISR | Configurable depth | OS-dependent |

## 2.3 The Performance Metrics — The Formulas

The following five formulas are the *high-yield* equations the KTU examiner may ask in a 3-mark or 14-mark question.

### 2.3.1 Interrupt Latency

$$L_{IRQ} = t_{IRQ,start} - t_{IRQ,occur}$$

where $t_{IRQ,occur}$ is the instant the physical interrupt signal arrives, and $t_{IRQ,start}$ is the instant the first instruction of the ISR executes.

**KTU Benchmark:** $L_{IRQ}^{max} < 1\,\mu s$ for premium hard RTOS, $5$–$20\,\mu s$ for mid-range.

### 2.3.2 Context Switch Time

$$T_{ctx} = t_{save} + t_{restore} + t_{sched}$$

where $t_{save}$ is the time to push the current task's registers onto its stack, $t_{restore}$ is the time to pop the new task's registers, and $t_{sched}$ is the time the scheduler needs to pick the next task.

### 2.3.3 Utilization Bound (Liu & Layland Rate Monotonic Analysis)

For a set of $n$ periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), the schedulability bound is:

$$U_{RMS} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

As $n \to \infty$, $U_{RMS} \le \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.

### 2.3.4 Memory Footprint (Total Kernel Cost)

$$M_{kernel} = M_{ROM} + M_{RAM} = M_{code} + \sum_{k=1}^{n_{tasks}} M_{TCB,k} + M_{kernel\_data}$$

### 2.3.5 Worst-Case Response Time (under FPP / Deadline Monotonic)

$$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

The task $i$ is **schedulable** if $R_i \le D_i$, where $D_i$ is its deadline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet**
>
> | # | Formula | Meaning | When Used |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | $L_{IRQ} = t_{IRQ,start} - t_{IRQ,occur}$ | Interrupt latency | Benchmarking |
> | 2 | $T_{ctx} = t_{save} + t_{restore} + t_{sched}$ | Switch overhead | Benchmarking |
> | 3 | $U_{RMS} \le n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | RMS schedulability test | Design phase |
> | 4 | $M_{kernel} = M_{ROM} + M_{RAM}$ | Footprint estimation | Vendor comparison |
> | 5 | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i/T_j \rceil C_j$ | Worst-case response time | Hard RT analysis |

## 2.4 Commercial vs. Open-Source RTOS — The Core Comparison

This is the single most-frequently asked 14-mark topic in Module 4.

| Dimension | **Commercial RTOS** (e.g., VxWorks, QNX, ThreadX, Integrity) | **Open-Source RTOS** (e.g., FreeRTOS, Zephyr, RT-Thread, Contiki, NuttX) |
|---|---|---|
| **License Cost** | Per-seat + per-unit royalty; NRE may exceed \$100k | Free (BSD/MIT) or copyleft (GPL) |
| **Source Code** | Available under NDA; certified builds available | Public on GitHub |
| **Certification Evidence** | DO-178C, IEC 61508, ISO 26262 pre-certified | Self-certification burden on the user |
| **Support** | Vendor-backed, often 24×7, with defined SLAs | Community forums; paid third-party support available |
| **Determinism** | Typically sub-µs, validated | Good (e.g., Zephyr), but unvalidated out of the box |
| **Footprint** | Tunable, scalable | Often very small (FreeRTOS ≈ 4–10 KB) |
| **Longevity Risk** | Tied to vendor's roadmap | Tied to community / foundation (e.g., Zephyr Project) |
| **Time-to-Market** | Faster for safety-critical; slower for hobbyist | Fastest for prototyping and low-risk products |
| **Examples** | VxWorks, QNX Neutrino, Green Hills INTEGRITY, ThreadX, SafeRTOS, PikeOS | FreeRTOS, Zephyr, RT-Thread, NuttX, eCos, RTEMS, Contiki-NG, TinyOS |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hybrid Path:** Many production systems use an open-source RTOS (FreeRTOS or Zephyr) for the **application layer** and a commercial safety-certified RTOS (SafeRTOS or AUTOSAR OS) only for the **safety island** of the system. This is a common, KTU-relevant compromise.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice of RTOS permeates the *entire lifecycle* of a product:

1. **Concept phase:** Cost and licensing are early gates.
2. **Design phase:** Schedulability analysis (RMS / response-time) requires the chosen scheduler.
3. **Implementation phase:** API familiarity (POSIX vs. proprietary) drives developer productivity.
4. **Verification phase:** Certification evidence is non-negotiable in avionics, automotive, medical.
5. **Production phase:** Royalty cost and long-term support dominate.
6. **End-of-life phase:** Vendor exit (e.g., the controversial *BlackBerry QNX licensing changes*) can force costly porting.

Hence, choosing an RTOS is *the* architectural decision that constrains the product from cradle to grave.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Applying the RMS Utilization Bound

**Problem (3-mark style):**
Three periodic tasks are to be scheduled on a Rate Monotonic kernel. Compute and comment on their schedulability under RMS.

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Worst-case Computation $C_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 10 | 2 |
| $\tau_2$ | 20 | 4 |
| $\tau_3$ | 50 | 10 |

### Step 1 — Assign priorities by period (shorter period = higher priority in RMS)

By the Rate Monotonic rule:
- $\tau_1$ (period 10 ms) — **highest priority**
- $\tau_2$ (period 20 ms) — **middle priority**
- $\tau_3$ (period 50 ms) — **lowest priority**

### Step 2 — Compute the actual CPU utilization

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{actual} &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2}{10} + \frac{4}{20} + \frac{10}{50} \\[4pt]
&= 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.200 \\[4pt]
&= 0.600
\end{aligned}
$$

So $U_{actual} = 0.600 = 60\%$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Liu & Layland theoretical bound for $n = 3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{RMS,bound}(n=3) &= n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) \\[4pt]
&= 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) \\[4pt]
&= 3 \left( 1.2599 - 1 \right) \\[4pt]
&= 3 \times 0.2599 \\[4pt]
&= 0.7798
\end{aligned}
$$

So $U_{RMS,bound} \approx 0.7798 \approx 78\%$.

### Step 4 — Compare and conclude

$$
0.600 \le 0.7798 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{The task set IS schedulable under RMS.}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** A utilization of 60% is *not* 100% — this is the **deadline-monotonic insight**: RMS leaves ~22% headroom for $n=3$ because worst-case phasing is rare. Engineers can sometimes push utilization higher by switching to **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**, which is provably optimal with $U_{EDF} \le 1.0$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Worst-Case Response Time (Deadline-Monotonic)

**Problem (7-mark style):**
Given three tasks scheduled under Fixed-Priority Preemptive, compute the worst-case response time of the lowest-priority task $\tau_3$ and check schedulability.

| Task | $T_i$ (ms) | $C_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 6 | 1 | 6 | High |
| $\tau_2$ | 10 | 2 | 10 | Mid |
| $\tau_3$ | 15 | 3 | 15 | Low |

### Step 1 — List the higher-priority set $hp(3)$

Higher-priority tasks that can preempt $\tau_3$ are: $hp(3) = \{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$.

### Step 2 — Apply the iterative response-time equation

$$
R_3 = C_3 + \sum_{j \in hp(3)} \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

Substitute:

$$
R_3 = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{6} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{10} \right\rceil (2)
$$

### Step 3 — Iterate

**Iteration 0:** Guess $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3$ ms.

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \lceil 3/6 \rceil (1) + \lceil 3/10 \rceil (2) = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

**Iteration 1:** Now use $R_3^{(1)} = 6$.

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \lceil 6/6 \rceil (1) + \lceil 6/10 \rceil (2) = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

The value has converged: $R_3 = 6$ ms.

### Step 4 — Schedulability check

$$
R_3 = 6 \text{ ms} \quad \le \quad D_3 = 15 \text{ ms}
$$

Therefore, **task $\tau_3$ is schedulable**. The system passes the worst-case response time test.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Footprint Comparison Table

**Problem (3-mark style):**
For a candidate Cortex-M4 design, a designer shortlists two RTOSes. Compare their footprints.

| Metric | FreeRTOS Kernel | ThreadX (Express Logic) |
|---|---|---|
| ROM (kernel code) | 4 – 10 KB | 8 – 12 KB |
| RAM (kernel data) | 0.5 – 1 KB | 1 – 2 KB |
| Per-task TCB | 76 bytes | 96 bytes |
| Min tick rate | 1 ms | 1 µs (configurable) |
| Tickless idle | Yes | Yes |

For 20 tasks, the per-task TCB cost is:

**FreeRTOS:** $20 \times 76 = 1520$ bytes = **1.48 KB**
**ThreadX:** $20 \times 96 = 1920$ bytes = **1.88 KB**

Total RAM estimate (kernel + TCBs):

**FreeRTOS:** $\approx 1 \text{ KB} + 1.48 \text{ KB} = 2.48 \text{ KB}$
**ThreadX:** $\approx 2 \text{ KB} + 1.88 \text{ KB} = 3.88 \text{ KB}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For ultra-low-cost MCUs (Cortex-M0+, 16 KB flash, 4 KB RAM), the FreeRTOS footprint is often the *only* viable option. For safety-critical or high-performance parts, the additional ~1.4 KB of ThreadX is a worthwhile trade.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — A Selection Workflow

The following pseudo-procedure (which the KTU examiner may ask to "describe" in 7 marks) is the **standard industry workflow** for choosing an RTOS:

**Step 1:** Classify the system: **Hard / Firm / Soft** real-time. (Rejection of bare-metal if hard.)

**Step 2:** Quantify the timing budget. Compute $L_{IRQ}^{max}$, $T_{ctx}^{max}$, deadline $D_i$ per task.

**Step 3:** Quantify the resource budget. Determine $M_{ROM}^{budget}$, $M_{RAM}^{budget}$, clock speed, peripherals.

**Step 4:** Identify the **standards** required (DO-178C, ISO 26262, IEC 62304, POSIX).

**Step 5:** Identify the **middleware** required (TCP/IP, USB, CAN, GUI, FS).

**Step 6:** Apply a **weighted scoring matrix** to all candidate RTOSes. A typical weighting is:

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ |
|---|---|
| Determinism (worst-case latency) | 0.25 |
| Footprint | 0.10 |
| Middleware availability | 0.15 |
| Standards / certification | 0.20 |
| Cost (license + NRE) | 0.10 |
| Tooling | 0.10 |
| Vendor longevity & support | 0.10 |

**Step 7:** Score each RTOS (0–10) on each criterion, compute $\text{Total Score} = \sum w_i s_i$, and pick the winner.

**Step 8:** Build a **proof-of-concept** with the top-2 candidates on the actual hardware. Run real benchmark workloads. Verify $L_{IRQ}$, $T_{ctx}$, RAM, ROM against Step 3 budget.

**Step 9:** Lock-in decision; document the rationale in the **Architecture Decision Record (ADR)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Many students *skip Step 1 and Step 2* and jump directly to "FreeRTOS is good / VxWorks is good." That answer will lose 4–5 marks. The examiner wants the **structured decision flow**, not opinions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Seven-Pillar Selection Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need to Choose an RTOS] --> B[Classify Hard / Firm / Soft Real-Time]
    B --> C[Quantify Timing Budget: L_IRQ, T_ctx, Deadlines]
    C --> D[Quantify Resource Budget: ROM, RAM, Tick Rate]
    D --> E[Identify Standards: DO-178C, ISO 26262, POSIX, AUTOSAR]
    E --> F[Identify Middleware: TCP/IP, USB, CAN, FS, GUI]
    F --> G[Build Weighted Scoring Matrix]
    G --> H[Shortlist 2-3 Candidate RTOSes]
    H --> I[Run Proof-of-Concept on Target Hardware]
    I --> J{Meets All Constraints?}
    J -- No --> H
    J -- Yes --> K[Lock-In Decision + Document ADR]
    K --> L[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Commercial vs. Open-Source RTOS Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Application Profile] --> q1{Safety-Critical Domain?}
    q1 -- Yes Aviation or Defense --> C1[Use DO-178C Certified Commercial RTOS]
    q1 -- Yes Automotive --> C2[Use AUTOSAR OS or ISO 26262 Certified RTOS]
    q1 -- Yes Medical --> C3[Use IEC 62304 Certified RTOS]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Hard Real-Time Required?}
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Royalty Budget Available?}
    q3 -- Yes --> C4[Commercial: VxWorks, QNX, ThreadX]
    q3 -- No --> C5[Open Source: Zephyr, FreeRTOS, NuttX with validation]
    q2 -- No Soft Real-Time --> q4{Connectivity Heavy?}
    q4 -- Yes --> O1[Linux PREEMPT_RT or OpenWrt]
    q4 -- No --> O2[FreeRTOS or RT-Thread]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block-Level Architecture of an RTOS-Based System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph app[Application Layer]
        T1[Task 1 High Priority]
        T2[Task 2 Mid Priority]
        T3[Task 3 Low Priority]
    end
    subgraph mid[Middleware Layer]
        FS[File System FAT]
        NET[TCP/IP Stack]
        GUI[GUI Library LVGL]
    end
    subgraph kern[RTOS Kernel]
        SCH[Scheduler Fixed Priority RMS EDF]
        IPC[IPC: Queues, Semaphores, Mutex]
        TIM[Software Timers and Tick]
        MM[Memory Manager Pools]
    end
    subgraph bsp[Board Support Package BSP]
        DRV1[GPIO Driver]
        DRV2[UART Driver]
        DRV3[ADC Driver]
    end
    subgraph hw[Hardware]
        CPU[ARM Cortex-M4 Core]
        MEM[Flash + RAM]
    end
    T1 --> IPC
    T2 --> IPC
    T3 --> IPC
    FS --> IPC
    NET --> IPC
    GUI --> IPC
    SCH --> CPU
    IPC --> SCH
    TIM --> SCH
    MM --> MEM
    DRV1 --> CPU
    DRV2 --> CPU
    DRV3 --> CPU
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — RTOS Selection Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Concept] --> B[Design Schedulability]
    B --> C[Implementation]
    C --> D[Verification and Certification]
    D --> E[Production and Deployment]
    E --> F[Maintenance and Updates]
    F --> G[End of Life or Migration]
    G --> A
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#e1ffe1
    style D fill:#ffe1e1
    style E fill:#f0e1ff
    style F fill:#e1f0ff
    style G fill:#ffe1f0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - **Diagram 1** is the *canonical selection workflow* — reproduce this in long-answer questions for full marks.
> - **Diagram 2** demonstrates the **domain-driven** nature of RTOS choice (aviation vs. automotive vs. consumer).
> - **Diagram 3** shows how the **kernel, middleware, BSP, and hardware** are layered. The *scheduler* sits at the heart and must be chosen early.
> - **Diagram 4** emphasizes that RTOS choice is a *lifecycle-long* commitment, not a one-time decision.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three key features that you would look for while selecting an RTOS for an embedded automotive ECU.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Determinism with bounded latency:** The RTOS must guarantee worst-case interrupt latency and task switch time (e.g., < 10 µs). This is critical because an ECU controlling engine timing or anti-lock brakes cannot tolerate deadline misses. *(1 mark)*

2. **Standards compliance (ISO 26262 / AUTOSAR):** For automotive ECUs, the RTOS must be certifiable up to ASIL-D. AUTOSAR OS provides pre-certified building blocks. *(1 mark)*

3. **Priority inheritance mutex / deadlock detection:** To avoid priority inversion in mixed-criticality tasks, the RTOS should support mutexes with priority inheritance protocol. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a commercial RTOS and an open-source RTOS. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **commercial RTOS** is licensed under proprietary terms, often with per-unit royalties, vendor-backed support, and pre-built certification evidence. Examples: **VxWorks (Wind River), QNX Neutrino (BlackBerry)**. *(1.5 marks)*

An **open-source RTOS** is distributed under licenses such as MIT, BSD, or Apache 2.0 (permissive) or GPL (copyleft), with community-based support and no royalty cost. Examples: **FreeRTOS, Zephyr, RT-Thread**. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`
**Your team is developing a portable infusion pump for ICU use. The pump must deliver medication with a precision of ±2% and must trigger an alarm within 100 ms if a fault is detected. The MCU is an ARM Cortex-M4 with 256 KB flash and 64 KB RAM. As the lead embedded architect:**

**(a)** Propose the *classification* of the system (Hard / Firm / Soft) and justify your choice. Discuss the implications for the choice of RTOS. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **three candidate RTOSes** (one commercial, two open-source) using a structured criteria table. Recommend one and justify with a clear decision rationale. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a)

**Classification: HARD REAL-TIME SYSTEM.** *(1 mark)*

**Justification:** The system performs a *life-sustaining* function. A missed alarm deadline (alarm must fire within 100 ms) can lead to over-infusion of medication, which is a *catastrophic* patient safety failure. Under the Hard/Firm/Soft taxonomy:
- Missing a deadline = total system failure ⇒ **Hard Real-Time**.
- Also, the device falls under **IEC 62304 Class C** software (life-supporting) and likely FDA Class II/III. *(2 marks)*

**Implications for RTOS choice:**
1. The kernel must offer **provable, bounded worst-case latency** (Worst-Case Execution Time analysis, response-time analysis, or RMS schedulability).
2. **Pre-certified evidence** (IEC 62304 / IEC 61508 SIL 3) is strongly preferred to reduce certification effort. *(1 mark)*
3. The kernel must support **memory partitioning** (MPU) so a fault in the UI task cannot corrupt the dosing task. *(1 mark)*
4. **Watchdog timer** integration and **stack overflow detection** are mandatory. *(1 mark)*
5. A **bare-metal super-loop** is unacceptable — it cannot bound latency under task interference. *(1 mark)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the constraints** *(carried forward from part a)*:
- Hard real-time, IEC 62304 Class C, Cortex-M4, 256 KB flash, 64 KB RAM.
- Required: TCP/IP (for hospital network), GUI (touchscreen), alarm subsystem, RTC, motor control.

**Step 2 — Build the comparison table** *(5 marks for the table + discussion)*:

| Criterion | **SafeRTOS (Commercial)** | **FreeRTOS (Open-Source)** | **Zephyr (Open-Source)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **License** | Per-unit royalty + NRE | MIT (free) | Apache 2.0 (free) |
| **Certification** | IEC 61508 SIL 3 pre-certified | Self-certify (user burden) | Self-certify, in-progress evidence |
| **ROM footprint** | ~10 KB | ~4–10 KB | ~8 KB – 50 KB |
| **RAM footprint** | ~2 KB + TCBs | ~1 KB + TCBs | ~2–8 KB |
| **Priority levels** | Configurable (e.g., 32) | Unlimited | 32 / 64 |
| **Mutex w/ priority inheritance** | Yes | Yes (configurable) | Yes |
| **MPU support** | Yes (Cortex-M) | Yes (Cortex-M port) | Yes (advanced) |
| **TCP/IP** | Optional (CycloneTCP, lwIP) | lwIP, FreeRTOS+TCP | Native Zephyr NET |
| **GUI** | Third-party (emWin, Storyboard) | LVGL via port | LVGL via port |
| **Long-term support** | Vendor SLAs, multi-year | Amazon-maintained, MIT governance | Linux Foundation, multi-vendor |
| **Cost @ 10,000 units/yr** | ~\$10/unit + \$50k NRE | \$0 + training cost | \$0 + training cost |
| **Time to certification** | Months (vendor helps) | 12–24 months (in-house) | 12–24 months (in-house) |

**Step 3 — Recommendation:** **SafeRTOS (by Wittenstein High Integrity Systems / WHIS).** *(2 marks)*

**Rationale:**
- The infusion pump is a **Class C medical device**. The pre-certified IEC 61508 SIL 3 / IEC 62304 evidence of SafeRTOS drastically **reduces the certification timeline and cost** (saving potentially millions in audit cost and 6–12 months in time-to-market).
- The footprint fits comfortably in 256 KB flash / 64 KB RAM.
- The royalty of ~\$10/unit is acceptable for a \$2,000+ medical device.
- FreeRTOS / Zephyr are excellent but would require the in-house team to build, document, and audit the safety case themselves, which is feasible only for very large teams.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a)** With neat diagrams and equations, explain the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** algorithm. Derive the **Liu & Layland utilization bound** and state its significance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A satellite attitude-control system uses an RTOS with three periodic tasks as shown below. Using the **worst-case response time analysis**, determine if the task set is schedulable under Fixed Priority Preemptive scheduling. Assume the deadline equals the period. *(7 marks)*

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Computation $C_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 8 | 2 |
| $\tau_2$ | 16 | 4 |
| $\tau_3$ | 32 | 6 |

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a)

**Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):** *(2 marks)*
- A **fixed-priority** preemptive scheduler for **periodic, independent** tasks.
- Rule: *shorter period $\Rightarrow$ higher priority*. The task with the smallest period gets the highest priority.
- RMS is *optimal* among fixed-priority algorithms — if any fixed-priority scheme can schedule a task set, RMS can.

**Diagrammatic depiction (Mermaid Gantt):**

```mermaid
gantt
    title Rate Monotonic Scheduling Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section tau1 period 8
    tau1_instance_1 :a1, 0, 2
    tau1_instance_2 :a2, 8, 2
    tau1_instance_3 :a3, 16, 2
    section tau2 period 16
    tau2_instance_1 :b1, 2, 4
    section tau3 period 32
    tau3_instance_1 :c1, 6, 6
```

**Derivation of Liu & Layland Bound:** *(4 marks)*

For $n$ periodic tasks under RMS, the **sufficient (but not necessary) schedulability condition** is:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

The bound is obtained via the **critical instant** argument — the worst-case response time of any task occurs when *all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously* with it. By analyzing the response-time of task $\tau_i$ when it is interfered with by $i-1$ higher-priority tasks at the critical instant, one derives the recurrence:

$$
R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

and the closed-form bound $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ is the asymptotic solution of this recurrence.

**Significance:** As $n \to \infty$, the bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$. Thus, RMS can guarantee schedulability up to ~69.3% utilization for any $n$. EDF, by contrast, can schedule up to 100% utilization but is dynamic-priority. *(1 mark)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b)

**Step 1 — Assign priorities (RMS rule):** *(1 mark)*
- $\tau_1$: $T_1 = 8$ ms → **highest priority**
- $\tau_2$: $T_2 = 16$ ms → **mid priority**
- $\tau_3$: $T_3 = 32$ ms → **lowest priority**

**Step 2 — Response-time equation for $\tau_3$:** *(1 mark)*

$$
R_3 = C_3 + \sum_{j \in hp(3)} \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

$$
R_3 = 6 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{8} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{16} \right\rceil (4)
$$

**Step 3 — Iterate:** *(4 marks)*

**Iteration 0:** Start with $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 6$ ms.

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 6 + \lceil 6/8 \rceil (2) + \lceil 6/16 \rceil (4) = 6 + 1(2) + 1(4) = 12 \text{ ms}
$$

**Iteration 1:** Use $R_3^{(1)} = 12$ ms.

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 6 + \lceil 12/8 \rceil (2) + \lceil 12/16 \rceil (4) = 6 + 2(2) + 1(4) = 14 \text{ ms}
$$

**Iteration 2:** Use $R_3^{(2)} = 14$ ms.

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 6 + \lceil 14/8 \rceil (2) + \lceil 14/16 \rceil (4) = 6 + 2(2) + 1(4) = 14 \text{ ms}
$$

Converged at **$R_3 = 14$ ms**.

**Step 4 — Schedulability check:** *(1 mark)*

$$
R_3 = 14 \text{ ms} \quad \le \quad D_3 = T_3 = 32 \text{ ms}
$$

**Conclusion:** The task set **IS schedulable** under FPP / RMS. Task $\tau_3$ has a slack of $32 - 14 = 18$ ms.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks on "Choosing an RTOS" questions:**
>
> 1. **Skipping system classification.** A student who does not begin by stating "Hard / Firm / Soft real-time" and justifying it forfeits 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Vague adjectives, no numbers.** Phrases like "very fast" or "very small" are *worthless* in 14-mark answers. Use specific numbers: "$L_{IRQ} < 1 \mu s$", "ROM $< 10$ KB".
> 3. **No structured decision matrix.** A bullet list of "FreeRTOS is good because it is free" loses to a *weighted scoring matrix with numbers*.
> 4. **Ignoring the standards dimension.** For safety domains (medical, automotive, avionics), the certification evidence (IEC 62304, ISO 26262, DO-178C) is the *single most important* factor — not cost.
> 5. **No response-time or schedulability calculation.** When the question provides task periods and computation times, the KTU examiner *expects* the student to apply the Liu & Layland bound or the worst-case response time equation. A qualitative answer gets at most 8/14 marks.
> 6. **Confusing RTOS with GPOS.** A "Real-Time Linux" is *not* a hard RTOS in its stock form. It becomes one only with the PREEMPT_RT patch, and even then, it is widely considered *firm* real-time, not hard.
> 7. **Omitting the BSP/portability angle.** Most students forget that the BSP determines the time-to-port on new hardware.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Choosing an RTOS"**
>
> - **First Step is always classification:** Hard / Firm / Soft real-time. A hard real-time choice rules out a bare-metal super-loop.
> - **The Seven Pillars of Selection:** Functional, Temporal, Resource Footprint, Hardware/Toolchain, Standards, Commercial, Ecosystem/Support.
> - **Key Performance Metrics:** $L_{IRQ}$ (interrupt latency), $T_{ctx}$ (context switch), $U_{RMS}$ (utilization), $R_i$ (worst-case response time).
> - **Liu & Layland Bound:** $U_{RMS} \le n(2^{1/n} - 1) \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$. EDF is provably optimal at $U \le 1.0$ but is dynamic-priority.
> - **Worst-Case Response Time:** Solve $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i/T_j \rceil C_j$ iteratively; check $R_i \le D_i$.
> - **Commercial RTOS:** Per-unit royalty, NDA source, pre-certified (VxWorks, QNX, ThreadX, SafeRTOS, INTEGRITY, PikeOS).
> - **Open-Source RTOS:** BSD/MIT (FreeRTOS, Zephyr, RT-Thread) — faster TTM but the *user* bears the certification burden.
> - **Domain-specific standards:** DO-178C (avionics), ISO 26262 / AUTOSAR (automotive), IEC 61508 (industrial), IEC 62304 / FDA (medical), ARINC 653 (partitioned avionics), POSIX 1003.1b (portability), µITRON (Japanese market).
> - **Footprint budget is a hard gate:** FreeRTOS ≈ 4–10 KB; Zephyr ≈ 8–50 KB; VxWorks ≈ 100 KB+; QNX ≈ 100 KB+. Smaller than 4 KB ⇒ use SafeRTOS or custom kernel.
> - **Middleware is a forcing function:** Need TCP/IP, USB, CAN, GUI, FS? Check that the RTOS *ecosystem* supports them — don't reinvent.
> - **Long-term vendor risk matters:** Open-source with a foundation (Zephyr, Apache) tends to be more durable than a single-vendor open-source.
> - **Always finish with a documented Architecture Decision Record (ADR).** The KTU examiner rewards *process*, not just the final pick.
> - **Never say "Linux is an RTOS" without the PREEMPT_RT patch.** Even with the patch, it is *firm*, not *hard* real-time.
> - **The selection is a lifecycle decision** — it constrains design, certification, production, and end-of-life phases for the product.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Implementation of ARM Cortex Based Motor Speed Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT504 - EMBEDDED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design and Implementation of ARM Cortex Based Motor Speed Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ARM Cortex Based Motor Speed Control** is an embedded closed-loop control system that uses an ARM Cortex-M series microcontroller as the central processing and decision-making unit to regulate the rotational speed of a DC motor (or stepper / BLDC motor) by generating a Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) drive signal whose duty cycle is dynamically adjusted in response to real-time speed feedback from a sensor.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBEVT504 – Embedded System Design)**, this design problem brings together three foundational pillars studied in Module 4:

1. **ARM Cortex-M architecture** (Cortex-M0/M3/M4 registers, timers, NVIC, ADC).
2. **Peripheral interfacing** (GPIO, PWM modules, timers, interrupt-driven I/O).
3. **Control theory implementation** (open-loop vs closed-loop, PID feedback, sensor signal conditioning).

The formal performance objective is to maintain a **user-defined reference speed** $\omega_{ref}$ such that the steady-state error

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} \left[ \omega_{ref}(t) - \omega_{actual}(t) \right]$$

is minimised, typically below **5 %** of full scale in a well-tuned industrial implementation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The design of motor speed control systems is a direct application of *Timers/Counters*, *PWM*, *ADC*, *Interrupts*, and *Feedback Control* — all high-weightage topics in the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cruise Control of a Car"

Imagine you are driving a car on a hilly road and you set the cruise control to **80 km/h**:

- **The accelerator pedal** = PWM duty cycle (more pedal = more power).
- **The speedometer reading** = feedback from the speed sensor.
- **The driver / cruise computer** = ARM Cortex-M processor running a PID algorithm.
- **The hill (slope)** = load disturbance on the motor.
- **The engine (ECU adjustments)** = power electronics driving the motor.

When the car climbs a hill, speed drops, the driver "sees" the error and presses the accelerator harder (increases PWM duty). On a downhill, speed rises, the driver eases off. This is **exactly** the role the ARM Cortex MCU plays — it samples the actual speed, computes the error $e(t)$, and modulates the PWM duty cycle every few milliseconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** The ARM Cortex chip is the "tiny brain" that constantly compares *how fast the motor is going* with *how fast you want it to go*, and pushes the motor *harder or softer* to make them match.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities

The following engineering metrics are standardly used in motor speed control and must be memorised for KTU exams:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity / Speed | **rad/s** | 0 – 3000 rpm |
| $N$ | Rotational speed | **rpm** | 0 – 5000 |
| $D$ | PWM Duty Cycle | **%** (unitless ratio) | 0 – 100 |
| $f_{PWM}$ | PWM switching frequency | **Hz / kHz** | 1 kHz – 25 kHz |
| $V_{avg}$ | Average DC voltage to motor | **Volt (V)** | 0 – 24 V |
| $K_{p}, K_{i}, K_{d}$ | PID controller gains | dimensionless | Application-specific |
| $T_{s}$ | Sampling period | **seconds (s)** | 1 ms – 10 ms |
| $R_{a}$ | Armature resistance | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** | 1 – 10 |
| $L_{a}$ | Armature inductance | **Henry (H)** | 1 mH – 100 mH |
| $K_{b}$ | Back-EMF constant | **V·s/rad** | 0.01 – 0.1 |

---

## 1.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Text Overview)

The complete ARM-Cortex based motor speed control system contains five major functional blocks:

1. **Reference Input Block** — Keypad / Potentiometer / UART command for $\omega_{ref}$.
2. **ARM Cortex-M Controller Block** — Runs PID algorithm, generates PWM, reads ADC / encoder.
3. **Power Driver Block** — H-bridge IC (e.g., **L298N**, **BTS7960**) or MOSFET half-bridge.
4. **Actuator Block** — DC motor / BLDC / Stepper.
5. **Feedback Sensor Block** — Optical encoder, Hall-effect sensor, or back-EMF sensing.

These blocks are arranged in a **closed negative-feedback loop**, which we will analyse mathematically in Section 2.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2024 Board Exams:** Always draw a *closed-loop block diagram* showing **Reference → Comparator → Controller → Driver → Plant → Sensor → Feedback** — examiners award 2–3 marks purely for a clean labelled diagram.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step by Step

The ARM-Cortex based speed control system operates through the following structured logic chain. Each step is the **"why"** and **"how"** behind a real hardware event.

1. **Step 1 — Reference Acquisition:** The user inputs the desired speed $\omega_{ref}$ via a potentiometer (read by the ARM's ADC, e.g., 10-bit ADC giving 0–1023 counts corresponding to 0–1024 discrete speed levels) or via a keypad / UART command.

2. **Step 2 — Feedback Measurement:** An optical encoder disc with $N_{slots}$ slots produces $N_{pulses}$ per revolution. The ARM's **Timer/Counter peripheral** counts pulses over a fixed window $T_{w}$ to compute actual speed.

3. **Step 3 — Error Computation:** The Cortex MCU subtracts measured from reference:
   
   $$e(n) = \omega_{ref} - \omega_{actual}(n)$$

4. **Step 4 — PID Control Law Execution:** The MCU computes a control signal $u(n)$ using the discrete PID equation.

5. **Step 5 — PWM Duty Cycle Update:** The control signal $u(n)$ is mapped to a duty cycle $D(n)$ within 0–100 % and written to the PWM Compare Register.

6. **Step 6 — Power Delivery:** The PWM signal drives the gate of a MOSFET / IGBT in the H-bridge, which delivers an average voltage $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ to the motor armature.

7. **Step 7 — Speed Response:** Motor speed changes, which is sensed in the next sampling instant, and the loop repeats at frequency $f_s = 1/T_s$.

---

## 2.2 Why Closed-Loop Control?

An **open-loop** system (PWM duty set manually) cannot compensate for:
- Load torque disturbances
- Supply voltage fluctuations
- Temperature-induced resistance changes
- Mechanical wear and friction variation

A **closed-loop** system uses feedback to **reject disturbances automatically** and achieve a small steady-state error. KTU examiners love this comparison — it is a guaranteed **2–3 mark** sub-question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Criterion:** A well-designed ARM-Cortex based closed-loop system should achieve:
> - **Zero steady-state error** for step input (Type-1 system with integral action).
> - **Settling time** $t_s < 5 \cdot T_s$ samples.
> - **Overshoot** $M_p < 10\%$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete formula compendium** required to solve any numerical problem on ARM-Cortex based motor speed control. All formulas are mathematically grounded and appear frequently in past KTU papers.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{PWM}}$ | $D$ = unitless (0–1), $T_{on}$ = s, $T_{PWM}$ = s | Defines % of ON time in one PWM period |
| 2 | Average Motor Voltage | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ | $V$ in Volts | Average DC voltage delivered to motor |
| 3 | Back-EMF Equation | $E_{b} = K_{b} \cdot \omega$ | $E_{b}$ in V, $K_{b}$ in V·s/rad, $\omega$ in rad/s | Opposing voltage generated by spinning motor |
| 4 | Armature Voltage Eq. | $V_{a} = E_{b} + I_{a} R_{a} + L_{a} \dfrac{dI_{a}}{dt}$ | $V_{a}$ in V, $I_{a}$ in A | KVL around motor armature |
| 5 | Motor Torque Eq. | $T_{m} = K_{t} \cdot I_{a}$ | $T_{m}$ in N·m, $K_{t}$ in N·m/A | Electromagnetic torque produced |
| 6 | Mechanical Load Eq. | $T_{m} - T_{L} = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega$ | $J$ in kg·m², $B$ in N·m·s/rad | Newton's 2nd law for rotation |
| 7 | Speed from Encoder | $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{f_{pulse} \cdot 60}{N_{slots}}$ | $N_{rpm}$ in rpm, $f_{pulse}$ in Hz, $N_{slots}$ = integer | Converting pulse frequency to mechanical speed |
| 8 | Speed (Pulse Count) | $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{C \cdot 60}{N_{slots} \cdot T_{w}}$ | $C$ = pulse count, $T_{w}$ = window in s | Alternative M-method speed measurement |
| 9 | Discrete PID Law | $u(n) = K_{p} e(n) + K_{i} \sum e(k) + K_{d} [e(n)-e(n-1)]$ | All dimensionless | Digital control update rule |
| 10 | PID Incremental (Velocity) Form | $\Delta u(n) = K_{p}[e(n)-e(n-1)] + K_{i} e(n) + K_{d}[e(n)-2e(n-1)+e(n-2)]$ | dimensionless | Avoids integral windup, easier to implement |
| 11 | Sampling Theorem | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Nyquist criterion — minimum sample rate |
| 12 | Ziegler-Nichols $K_{u}$ | $K_{p} = 0.6 K_{u}$ | dimensionless | Empirical PID tuning rule |
| 13 | Ziegler-Nichols $T_{u}$ | $T_{i} = 0.5 T_{u}, \; T_{d} = 0.125 T_{u}$ | seconds | Empirical integral and derivative times |
| 14 | Steady-State Error | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_{p} K_{plant}}$ | dimensionless | For P-only control of a Type-0 plant |
| 15 | Transfer Function (Motor) | $G(s) = \dfrac{K_{t}}{(R_{a}+L_{a}s)(Js+B) + K_{t}K_{b}}$ | dimensionless | Laplace-domain model of DC motor |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ARM-Cortex based motor speed control is deployed in:

- **Robotics**: Wheel odometry and manipulator joint control.
- **CNC Machines**: Spindle speed regulation for cutting tools.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs)**: Traction motor torque/speed management.
- **Drones (UAVs)**: BLDC motor ESC (Electronic Speed Controller) for propellers.
- **HVAC Systems**: Variable-speed fans and blowers for energy efficiency.
- **Industrial Conveyor Belts**: Synchronised line speed control.
- **Medical Devices**: Infusion pumps, centrifuges requiring precise RPM.

> [!TIP]
> **Energy Saving Fact:** Replacing fixed-speed industrial drives with ARM-based variable-speed drives typically saves **30 % – 60 %** of electrical energy — a fact examiners occasionally quote in long-answer questions.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Pulse Count to RPM (M-Method)

**Problem Setup:** An optical encoder with $N_{slots} = 60$ slots/revolution is mounted on a DC motor. In a measurement window of $T_w = 0.1$ s, the ARM Cortex Timer counts $C = 120$ pulses. Compute the motor speed in rpm.

**Step 1 — Pulses per Second (Frequency):**

$$f_{pulse} = \frac{C}{T_{w}} = \frac{120}{0.1} = 1200 \text{ Hz}$$

> *Conversion logic:* Pulse count $C$ divided by the time window $T_w$ gives the pulse frequency in Hz (pulses per second).

**Step 2 — Revolutions per Second:**

$$f_{rev} = \frac{f_{pulse}}{N_{slots}} = \frac{1200}{60} = 20 \text{ rev/s}$$

> *Conversion logic:* Each revolution produces exactly $N_{slots}$ pulses, so dividing total pulses by $N_{slots}$ gives the number of revolutions.

**Step 3 — Revolutions per Minute (RPM):**

$$N_{rpm} = f_{rev} \times 60 = 20 \times 60 = 1200 \text{ rpm}$$

> *Conversion logic:* 1 minute = 60 seconds, so multiply rev/s by 60 to get rev/min.

**Final Answer:** $N_{rpm} = 1200$ **rpm**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Voltage and Armature Current for Given Duty Cycle

**Given:** $V_{supply} = 24$ V, $D = 0.6$ (60 % duty), $R_a = 4 \; \Omega$, $K_b = 0.05$ V·s/rad, motor running at $\omega = 200$ rad/s. Find armature current $I_a$ (neglecting $L_a$ and armature reaction).

**Step 1 — Average Terminal Voltage:**

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply} = 0.6 \times 24 = 14.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Back-EMF Generated:**

$$E_{b} = K_{b} \cdot \omega = 0.05 \times 200 = 10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KVL on Armature (Steady State, $dI_a/dt = 0$):**

$$V_{avg} = E_{b} + I_{a} R_{a}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Armature Current:**

$$I_{a} = \frac{V_{avg} - E_{b}}{R_{a}} = \frac{14.4 - 10}{4} = \frac{4.4}{4} = 1.1 \text{ A}$$

**Final Answer:** $I_a = 1.1$ **A**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Discrete PID Control Law (Positional Form)

The continuous-time PID controller is:

$$u(t) = K_{p} e(t) + K_{i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_{d} \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Discretise the Integral Term (Backward Rectangular Approximation):**

$$\int_{0}^{nT_s} e(\tau) d\tau \approx T_{s} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e(k)$$

**Step 2 — Discretise the Derivative Term (Backward Difference):**

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \approx \frac{e(n) - e(n-1)}{T_{s}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Into the Continuous Law:**

$$u(n) = K_{p} e(n) + K_{i} T_{s} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e(k) + K_{d} \frac{e(n) - e(n-1)}{T_{s}}$$

**Step 4 — Define Discrete Gains for Simplicity:**

$$K_{i}^{'} = K_{i} T_{s}, \quad K_{d}^{'} = \frac{K_{d}}{T_{s}}$$

**Step 5 — Final Discrete Position PID Equation:**

$$u(n) = K_{p} e(n) + K_{i}^{'} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e(k) + K_{d}^{'} [e(n) - e(n-1)]$$

> *Why use this in ARM Cortex?* The summation $\sum e(k)$ is easily implemented as a running accumulator variable in C code. The derivative uses only the current and previous error stored in two RAM locations.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Speed from Timer Capture in ARM Cortex-M3

**Setup:** ARM Cortex-M3 Timer 0 is configured in **Capture Mode** on a rising edge. System clock $f_{clk} = 72$ MHz, prescaler = 71, so timer tick = $1 \mu$s. Two successive captures give $CCR1 = 5000$ and $CCR2 = 5500$. Encoder has $N_{slots} = 100$.

**Step 1 — Time Between Pulses:**

$$\Delta t = (CCR2 - CCR1) \times T_{tick} = (5500 - 5000) \times 1 \mu s = 500 \; \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Pulse Frequency:**

$$f_{pulse} = \frac{1}{\Delta t} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^{-6}} = 2000 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Motor Speed in RPM:**

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{f_{pulse} \times 60}{N_{slots}} = \frac{2000 \times 60}{100} = 1200 \text{ rpm}$$

**Final Answer:** $N_{rpm} = 1200$ **rpm**.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Steady-State Error with P-Controller

**Given:** Plant transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+5)}$, controller $C(s) = K_p$ (proportional only). Find $K_p$ such that $e_{ss} \le 0.05$ for a unit step input.

**Step 1 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)} = \frac{10K_{p}}{s^{2} + 5s + 10K_{p}}$$

**Step 2 — Error Transfer Function:**

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)} = \frac{s(s+5)}{s^{2} + 5s + 10K_{p}} \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Final Value Theorem for Step Input $R(s) = 1/s$:**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{s(s+5)}{s^{2} + 5s + 10K_{p}} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Limit:**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s(s+5)}{s^{2} + 5s + 10K_{p}} = \frac{0}{10K_{p}} = 0$$

**Result:** For a Type-1 system (one pole at origin in $G(s)$), the steady-state error to a **step input is zero for any finite $K_p > 0$**. The integral term is not strictly required for step inputs in this plant topology.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Method | Input Type | Steady-State Error $e_{ss}$ | Best Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **P-only** | Step | $\dfrac{1}{1+K_p K_{plant}}$ (Type-0) | Simple, fast but offset exists |
| **PI** | Step | $0$ | Most industrial motor drives |
| **PID** | Ramp | $0$ | Servo applications with varying reference |
| **PD** | Step | Non-zero | Derivative kick problematic for step |
| **PWM only (open-loop)** | Step | Large and variable | Fan speed, low-cost toys |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"which controller is best for motor speed control?"*, the textbook-correct answer is **PI controller** (or PID) because the integral term eliminates steady-state error and motor drives typically see step-type reference changes.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**Define Pulse Width Modulation (PWM). How is it used for DC motor speed control in an ARM Cortex based embedded system?**

**Model Answer:**

> Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) is a technique in which the width of digital pulses in a periodic train is varied while keeping the period (and hence frequency) constant. The **duty cycle** $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}}$ determines the average DC voltage applied to the motor.
>
> In an ARM Cortex based system, a hardware **Timer/Counter peripheral** generates the PWM waveform autonomously once configured via its **Match Register** and **PWM Control Register**. By writing a value to the **Match Register (MR0)**, the duty cycle is changed, which proportionally varies the average voltage $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ to the DC motor armature. Since motor speed is approximately proportional to armature voltage, varying $D$ controls the speed. The ARM Cortex offloads the PWM toggling to hardware, freeing the CPU to run the PID algorithm concurrently. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**
**List any three speed measurement techniques used with optical encoders in ARM Cortex based motor control systems.**

**Model Answer:**

The three standard techniques for measuring motor speed from an encoder are:

1. **M-Method (Pulse Counting):** Count the number of pulses $C$ in a fixed time window $T_w$. Speed $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{C \cdot 60}{N_{slots} \cdot T_w}$. Best for **medium-to-high speeds**.

2. **T-Method (Pulse Timing):** Measure the time interval $\Delta t$ between two consecutive encoder pulses using a high-frequency timer. Speed $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{60}{N_{slots} \cdot \Delta t}$. Best for **low speeds** where the M-method gives few pulses.

3. **M/T-Method (Combined):** Simultaneously count pulses $C$ over a reference time $T_w$ AND measure the exact time $\Delta t$ of the last pulse. Provides good accuracy over the **entire speed range**, which is why it is preferred in industrial ARM-based drives. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the design of an **ARM Cortex-M3 based closed-loop DC motor speed control system**. Identify the major hardware blocks and the role of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **PWM is generated using the ARM Cortex-M3 Timer peripheral** for controlling the speed of a DC motor. Include the relevant registers and duty cycle formula. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The block diagram (drawn exactly as in **Section 4.1 of these notes**) consists of:

1. **Reference Input Block:** A potentiometer connected to an ADC channel of the ARM Cortex-M3 generates a voltage 0–3.3 V, which is digitised to set the reference speed $\omega_{ref}$. Alternative inputs include a 4×4 keypad matrix or a UART command from a PC.

2. **ARM Cortex-M3 Controller Block:** Acts as the brain of the system. It runs the **PID control algorithm** at a fixed sampling rate (e.g., 1 kHz triggered by a Timer interrupt), reads the ADC value, computes the error, executes the PID law, and updates the PWM duty cycle by writing to the **Match Register** of the PWM-generating timer.

3. **Power Driver Block:** The PWM output (3.3 V logic level) cannot directly drive a 24 V / 5 A DC motor. Hence a **MOSFET-based H-bridge driver** (e.g., **L298N** or **BTS7960**) is used. The H-bridge allows bidirectional current flow and PWM modulates the average voltage applied to the armature.

4. **DC Motor (Plant):** A 24 V permanent-magnet DC motor rated at 100 W. Its electrical and mechanical dynamics are described by the equations listed in Section 2.3 (formulas 4, 5, 6).

5. **Feedback Sensor Block:** An **incremental optical encoder** (e.g., 100–360 slots/rev) is mounted on the motor shaft. The quadrature outputs (A, B channels) feed into the ARM's **Timer Capture inputs**, allowing pulse counting and direction detection.

6. **Display / HMI Block:** A **16×2 character LCD** (HD44780) and a **UART link to a PC** show the real-time RPM, PWM duty cycle, and error.

7. **Power Supply Block:** A **regulated 24 V SMPS** for the motor and a **3.3 V LDO** (e.g., LM1117) derived from the 24 V for the ARM Cortex-M3 logic supply.

The **closed negative-feedback loop** ensures that any deviation between $\omega_{ref}$ and $\omega_{actual}$ is automatically corrected within one sampling period, achieving zero steady-state error for step inputs (due to integral action). **(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 2 for block identification, 2 for working principle)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

The ARM Cortex-M3 Timer peripheral has the following key registers for PWM generation:

| Register | Full Name | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **T0TCR** | Timer Control Register | Enable / disable / reset the timer |
| **T0PR** | Prescale Register | Divides PCLK to get timer tick |
| **T0MR0** | Match Register 0 | Defines PWM period (or duty, depending on mode) |
| **T0MR1** | Match Register 1 | Defines the second edge (duty cycle) |
| **T0EMR** | External Match Register | Routes match event to external pin (PWM output) |
| **T0MCR** | Match Control Register | Configures reset / interrupt on match |
| **T0PWMC** | PWM Control Register | Enables PWM mode on selected outputs |

**Generation Steps:**

1. **Step 1 — Configure PCLK:** Suppose $PCLK = 60$ MHz. Set prescaler $PR = 0$ (no division), so timer tick $T_{tick} = \dfrac{PR+1}{PCLK} = \dfrac{1}{60 \text{ MHz}} = 16.67$ ns.

2. **Step 2 — Set PWM Frequency:** Choose $f_{PWM} = 25$ kHz (above audible range). Period $T_{PWM} = 1/f_{PWM} = 40 \; \mu s$. Number of timer counts per period:
   
   $$N_{period} = \frac{T_{PWM}}{T_{tick}} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{16.67 \times 10^{-9}} = 2400$$
   
   Hence **MR0 = 2400** defines the PWM period.

3. **Step 3 — Set Duty Cycle:** For duty cycle $D = 60\%$, the ON time counts are:
   
   $$N_{on} = D \cdot N_{period} = 0.6 \times 2400 = 1440$$
   
   Hence **MR1 = 1440**.

4. **Step 4 — General Duty Cycle Formula:**
   
   $$D(\%) = \frac{MR1}{MR0} \times 100$$
   
   And the dynamic update equation is:
   
   $$MR1(n) = D(n) \cdot MR0$$
